topics.title: Classification of Compound Semiconductors: Binary, Ternary, Quaternary Compound Semiconductors (an overview)
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEEVT413 - COMPOUND SEMICONDUCTORS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Classification of Compound Semiconductors: Binary, Ternary, Quaternary Compound Semiconductors (an overview)

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 What is a Compound Semiconductor?

A **Compound Semiconductor** is a crystalline semiconducting material formed by combining two or more chemical elements from **different groups** of the periodic table, where the resulting compound exhibits an electrical conductivity between that of a conductor and an insulator (typically in the range of **10⁻⁷ S/m to 10³ S/m**), with a bandgap generally between **0.1 eV and 6 eV**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition:** A compound semiconductor is a semiconductor compound composed of elements from two or more different groups of the periodic table (e.g., Group III–V, Group II–VI, Group IV–IV, or Group IV–VI). Unlike elemental semiconductors (Si, Ge) which are Group IV single-element crystals, compound semiconductors possess a **two-element or multi-element** crystal lattice, which gives them **engineerable electronic, optical, and piezoelectric properties** not available in elemental semiconductors.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Recipe" View

> [!NOTE]
> **Intuition for Students (Plain English):**
> Think of a semiconductor as a **recipe with ingredients**. 
> - **Elemental semiconductors** (Si, Ge) = a single-ingredient dish (plain rice).
> - **Binary compounds** (GaAs, ZnSe) = a two-ingredient dish (rice + dal).
> - **Ternary compounds** (AlGaAs) = a three-ingredient dish (rice + dal + ghee — you can tune the *spice level*).
> - **Quaternary compounds** (InGaAsP) = a four-ingredient dish — you can now independently adjust *spice level* AND *saltiness* (two tuning knobs).
>
> The more ingredients you add, the more **"tuning knobs"** (composition variables) you have to engineer the material's bandgap, lattice constant, and other properties for a specific device application (LEDs, laser diodes, HEMTs, photodetectors).

## 1.3 The Classification Principle

Compound semiconductors are classified by the **number of distinct chemical elements** that make up the unit cell of the crystal:

| # of Elements | Class Name | Generic Formula | Independent Composition Variables |
|:---:|:---|:---:|:---:|
| **2** | **Binary** | **AB** | **0** (fixed stoichiometry) |
| **3** | **Ternary** | **AₓB₁₋ₓC** or **ABCₓD₁₋ₓ** | **1** (e.g., x) |
| **4** | **Quaternary** | **AₓB₁₋ₓCᵧD₁₋ᵧ** | **2** (e.g., x and y) |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why does this matter for KTU exams?** The number of composition variables directly determines the **degrees of freedom** available for **bandgap engineering** and **lattice-matching** to a substrate — a recurring 7- to 14-mark question in KTU ESE.

## 1.4 Primary Material Families (KTU-Mandated Overview)

Compound semiconductors are organized by the **periodic-table groups** of their constituent elements:

1. **Group III–V compounds** → formed from Group 13 + Group 15 elements
   (e.g., GaAs, InP, GaN, AlAs, InSb)
2. **Group II–VI compounds** → formed from Group 12 + Group 16 elements
   (e.g., ZnSe, CdTe, HgCdTe)
3. **Group IV–IV compounds** → formed from Group 14 elements
   (e.g., SiC, SiGe — note SiGe is an *alloy*, strictly not a true compound)
4. **Group IV–VI compounds** → formed from Group 14 + Group 16 elements
   (e.g., PbS, PbTe — narrow-gap, used in IR detectors)

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Binary Compound Semiconductors (Two-Element Systems)

### 2.1.1 Definition
A binary compound semiconductor is composed of **exactly two chemically distinct elements** in a fixed stoichiometric ratio (typically 1:1), forming a crystal with a unique, fixed bandgap and lattice constant.

### 2.1.2 Key Binary Compound Families

**A. Group III–V Binary Compounds (Most commercially important)**

| Material | Symbol | Lattice Type | E_g (eV) @ 300 K | Primary Application |
|:---|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---|
| Gallium Arsenide | **GaAs** | Zinc Blende | **1.42** | Microwave FETs, laser diodes, solar cells |
| Indium Phosphide | **InP** | Zinc Blende | **1.35** | Optical fiber comms (1.55 μm), HEMTs |
| Gallium Nitride | **GaN** | Wurtzite | **3.39** | Blue/UV LEDs, power electronics |
| Aluminum Arsenide | **AlAs** | Zinc Blende | **2.16** | Used in heterojunctions (with GaAs) |
| Indium Antimonide | **InSb** | Zinc Blende | **0.17** | IR detectors, magnetic sensors |
| Indium Arsenide | **InAs** | Zinc Blende | **0.36** | High-speed electronics, quantum dots |
| Gallium Antimonide | **GaSb** | Zinc Blende | **0.73** | Mid-IR lasers, thermophotovoltaics |
| Aluminum Nitride | **AlN** | Wurtzite | **6.2** | UV LEDs, SAW devices |
| Indium Nitride | **InN** | Wurtzite | **0.7** | Solar cells, HEMT barrier layers |

> [!NOTE]
> **Why so many?** Each binary III–V compound occupies a *single point* on the bandgap vs. lattice-constant plot. To *tune* properties (e.g., match a substrate or hit a specific wavelength), we must move to **ternary** or **quaternary** alloys.

**B. Group II–VI Binary Compounds**

| Material | Symbol | E_g (eV) @ 300 K | Primary Application |
|:---|:---:|:---:|:---|
| Zinc Selenide | **ZnSe** | 2.67 | Blue-green LEDs, laser windows |
| Zinc Sulfide | **ZnS** | 3.68 | UV detectors, electroluminescence |
| Zinc Telluride | **ZnTe** | 2.26 | Solar cells, THz generation |
| Cadmium Sulfide | **CdS** | 2.42 | Solar cell window layer |
| Cadmium Telluride | **CdTe** | 1.50 | Thin-film solar cells, γ-ray detectors |
| Mercury Telluride | **HgTe** | –0.15 (semi-metal) | IR detectors (with CdHgTe) |

**C. Group IV–IV and IV–VI Binaries**

| Material | Symbol | E_g (eV) | Application |
|:---|:---:|:---:|:---|
| Silicon Carbide | **SiC** (4H, 6H, 3C) | 2.3 – 3.2 | High-power, high-temp electronics |
| Lead Sulfide | **PbS** | 0.41 | IR detectors (1–3 μm) |
| Lead Telluride | **PbTe** | 0.31 | Thermoelectrics, IR detectors |

## 2.2 Ternary Compound Semiconductors (Three-Element Alloys)

### 2.2.1 Definition
A ternary compound semiconductor is formed by mixing **two binary compounds that share a common anion (or cation)**. The general formula is:

$$A_{x}B_{1-x}C \quad \text{or} \quad AB_{x}C_{1-x}$$

where the **composition parameter $x$** varies continuously between **0 and 1**, giving a single *degree of freedom* for property tuning.

### 2.2.2 Key Ternary Families

| Ternary Alloy | Formula | End-Point Binaries | Tuning Range | Primary Use |
|:---|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---|
| Aluminum Gallium Arsenide | **Al$_x$Ga$_{1-x}$As** | AlAs ↔ GaAs | 1.42 → 2.16 eV | HEMT barrier, laser heterostructure |
| Indium Gallium Arsenide | **In$_x$Ga$_{1-x}$As** | InAs ↔ GaAs | 0.36 → 1.42 eV | HEMT channel, 1.55 μm detectors |
| Indium Gallium Phosphide | **In$_x$Ga$_{1-x}$P** | InP ↔ GaP | 1.35 → 2.26 eV | Visible LEDs, HBTs |
| Aluminum Gallium Nitride | **Al$_x$Ga$_{1-x}$N** | AlN ↔ GaN | 3.39 → 6.20 eV | UV LEDs, HEMT barrier |
| Indium Gallium Nitride | **In$_x$Ga$_{1-x}$N** | InN ↔ GaN | 0.7 → 3.39 eV | **Blue/green LEDs**, laser diodes |
| Mercury Cadmium Telluride | **Hg$_{1-x}$Cd$_x$Te** | HgTe ↔ CdTe | –0.15 → 1.50 eV | **IR detectors** (military, thermal imaging) |
| Aluminum Indium Phosphide | **Al$_x$In$_{1-x}$P** | AlP ↔ InP | 2.45 → 1.35 eV | Red/orange LEDs, laser diodes |
| Zinc Cadmium Telluride | **Zn$_{1-x}$Cd$_x$Te** | ZnTe ↔ CdTe | 2.26 → 1.50 eV | Solar cells, radiation detectors |
| Silicon Germanium | **Si$_x$Ge$_{1-x}$** | Si ↔ Ge | 1.12 → 0.66 eV | HBTs, strain-engineered CMOS |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU High-Yield Insight:** Ternaries like **AlGaAs** are **almost lattice-matched to GaAs** across the entire composition range (lattice constant mismatch < 0.2%), making them the *workhorse* of heterostructure device physics. This is why the AlGaAs/GaAs system is taught as the prototype example.

## 2.3 Quaternary Compound Semiconductors (Four-Element Alloys)

### 2.3.1 Definition
A quaternary compound is formed by mixing **three binary compounds sharing a common element**, or equivalently, a ternary alloy with an *additional* tunable element. The general form:

$$A_{x}B_{1-x}C_{y}D_{1-y}$$

Here we have **TWO independent composition parameters (x, y)**, giving a **two-dimensional tuning space** on the (bandgap, lattice-constant) plane.

### 2.3.2 Key Quaternary Families

| Quaternary Alloy | Formula | End-Point Quaternaries | Key Application |
|:---|:---:|:---:|:---|
| Indium Gallium Arsenide Phosphide | **In$_{1-x}$Ga$_x$As$_y$P$_{1-y}$** | InP ↔ GaAs₀.₅P₀.₅ | **Fiber-optic laser diodes** at 1.3 and 1.55 μm |
| Aluminum Gallium Arsenide Phosphide | **Al$_x$Ga$_{1-x}$As$_y$P$_{1-y}$** | AlAs ↔ GaP | Visible LEDs (red-yellow-green) |
| Aluminum Indium Gallium Phosphide | **(Al$_x$Ga$_{1-x}$)$_y$In$_{1-y}$P** | AlP ↔ GaP ↔ InP | **High-brightness red/orange LEDs** |
| Aluminum Gallium Indium Nitride | **Al$_x$Ga$_y$In$_{1-x-y}$N** | AlN ↔ GaN ↔ InN | Full-spectrum white LEDs |
| Indium Gallium Arsenide Antimonide | **In$_x$Ga$_{1-x}$As$_y$Sb$_{1-y}$** | InAs ↔ GaSb | Mid-IR (3–5 μm) lasers, thermophotovoltaics |
| Mercury Cadmium Zinc Telluride | **Hg$_{1-x-y}$Cd$_x$Zn$_y$Te** | HgTe ↔ CdTe ↔ ZnTe | Advanced IR focal-plane arrays |

> [!NOTE]
> **Why quaternaries dominate optical communications:** For InGaAsP lattice-matched to InP, you can vary **(x, y) along a single iso-lattice-constant line** and continuously tune the bandgap (and hence the laser emission wavelength) from **0.75 eV to 1.35 eV** (≈ 0.92 μm to 1.65 μm). This *simultaneously* satisfies the lattice-matching constraint and hits the low-loss/low-dispersion windows of optical fiber.

## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following equations are **examination-critical** for the classification and engineering of compound semiconductors. Memorize the form and physical meaning.

| # | Formula / Law | Expression | Use / Meaning |
|:---:|:---|:---|:---|
| 1 | **Vegard's Law** (lattice constant of ternary) | $a(x) = x \cdot a_{AC} + (1-x) \cdot a_{BC}$ | Linear interpolation of lattice constant in ternary alloy A$_x$B$_{1-x}$C |
| 2 | **Vegard's Law** (quaternary) | $a(x,y) = xy \cdot a_{BD} + x(1-y) \cdot a_{BC} + (1-x)y \cdot a_{AD} + (1-x)(1-y) \cdot a_{AC}$ | Lattice constant of A$_x$B$_{1-x}$C$_y$D$_{1-y}$ |
| 3 | **Bandgap of ternary** (linear interp.) | $E_g(x) = x \cdot E_{g,AC} + (1-x) \cdot E_{g,BC}$ | First-order bandgap estimate |
| 4 | **Bowing parameter** correction | $E_g(x) = x \cdot E_{g,AC} + (1-x) \cdot E_{g,BC} - x(1-x) \cdot C$ | Accounts for non-linear (bowed) bandgap; $C$ = bowing constant |
| 5 | **Bowing parameter** (quaternary) | $E_g(x,y) \approx$ weighted sum of binary $E_g$ values $-$ bowing corrections | More complex; see Section 3 |
| 6 | **Iso-lattice-matching line** (quaternary) | $y(x) = \dfrac{a_{AC} - a_{AD} - x(a_{BC} - a_{BD})}{a_{AD} - a_{AC} + x[(a_{BC} - a_{AC}) - (a_{BD} - a_{AD})]}$ | Composition (x, y) along which $a$ is constant |
| 7 | **Energy-wavelength relation** | $E_g \, (\text{eV}) = \dfrac{1.240}{\lambda \, (\mu m)}$ | Conversion of bandgap to emission wavelength |
| 8 | **# of elements vs DOF** | DOF $= N - 1 - 1 = N-2$ (constrained) | For $N$ elements forming one compound on a common sublattice |

**Units used throughout:** $E_g$ in **eV**, $a$ in **Å** or **nm**, $x, y$ dimensionless composition fractions (0 to 1), $\lambda$ in **μm** or **nm**.

## 2.5 Engineering Utility — Why We Need All Three Classes

| Class | Degrees of Freedom | Key Use Case | Limitation |
|:---|:---:|:---|:---|
| **Binary** | 0 (fixed) | Substrates, reference material | Cannot tune bandgap or lattice constant |
| **Ternary** | 1 (one knob) | Bandgap tuning *while staying near one substrate* | Lattice constant also changes with $x$ (cannot independently tune both) |
| **Quaternary** | 2 (two knobs) | **Independent** bandgap and lattice-constant control | More complex MOCVD/MBE growth, compositional uniformity harder |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Real-world engineering payoff:** Quaternary alloys like InGaAsP enable the entire **erbium-doped fiber amplifier (EDFA)** ecosystem and 1.55 μm telecommunications lasers — neither ternary nor binary can simultaneously match the InP substrate and emit at 1.55 μm.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of Vegard's Law for a Ternary Alloy A$_x$B$_{1-x}$C

**Setup:** Consider a ternary alloy formed by mixing two binaries AC and BC that share the common anion C. The cations on the cation sublattice are randomly occupied by atoms of type A and B with probabilities $x$ and $(1-x)$ respectively. The lattice constant of the alloy varies linearly between the two end-point binaries.

**Step 1 — Define end-point lattice constants.**

Let $a_{AC}$ be the lattice constant of binary AC and $a_{BC}$ be the lattice constant of binary BC.

**Step 2 — Apply the rule of mixtures (linear approximation).**

Assuming the lattice constant varies linearly with composition (Vegard's assumption of ideal solid solution):

$$a(x) = (1 - x) \cdot a_{BC} + x \cdot a_{AC}$$

**Step 3 — Rearrange into the canonical KTU form.**

$$a(x) = x \cdot a_{AC} + (1-x) \cdot a_{BC}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Logical justification:** In a perfectly random alloy with no atomic-size mismatch strain relaxation, each cation site contributes its ionic radius linearly. This is a *first-order approximation*; in reality, small deviations (U-shaped bowing) occur, but the linear form is the KTU-required first approximation.

**Worked Numerical Example (Al$_x$Ga$_{1-x}$As):**
- $a_{AlAs} = 5.6605$ Å
- $a_{GaAs} = 5.6533$ Å
- At $x = 0.3$:

$$a(0.3) = 0.3 \times 5.6605 + 0.7 \times 5.6533$$

$$a(0.3) = 1.69815 + 3.95731 = 5.65546 \text{ Å}$$

This is extremely close to GaAs (5.6533 Å), confirming the **near-perfect lattice match** that makes AlGaAs/GaAs the canonical heterostructure system.

## 3.2 Derivation of the Bandgap Composition Relation (Ternary)

**Step 1 — Start with the linear interpolation of bandgap.**

$$E_g(x) = x \cdot E_{g,AC} + (1-x) \cdot E_{g,BC}$$

**Step 2 — Recognize the empirical non-linearity.**

Measured bandgaps of real ternary alloys show a *bowed* (parabolic) deviation from the linear interpolation. We add a **bowing correction term**:

$$E_g(x) = x \cdot E_{g,AC} + (1-x) \cdot E_{g,BC} - x(1-x) \cdot C$$

where $C$ is the **bowing parameter** (in eV), typically fitted empirically from optical absorption data.

**Step 3 — Physical interpretation of the bowing term.**

The term $x(1-x) \cdot C$ is maximum at $x = 0.5$ and zero at the endpoints $x = 0$ and $x = 1$. It arises from:
- **Atomic size mismatch** strain on the cation sublattice,
- **Electron scattering** by the random A–B alloy potential,
- **Band structure disorder** (alloy scattering potential).

**Worked Example (Al$_x$Ga$_{1-x}$As at $x = 0.45$, $C \approx 0.127$ eV):**

$$E_g(0.45) = 0.45 \times 2.16 + 0.55 \times 1.42 - 0.45 \times 0.55 \times 0.127$$

Compute each term:

$$0.45 \times 2.16 = 0.9720 \text{ eV}$$

$$0.55 \times 1.42 = 0.7810 \text{ eV}$$

$$0.45 \times 0.55 \times 0.127 = 0.2475 \times 0.127 = 0.03143 \text{ eV}$$

Therefore:

$$E_g(0.45) = 0.9720 + 0.7810 - 0.03143 = 1.7216 \text{ eV}$$

This matches the accepted room-temperature value of $E_g \approx 1.72$ eV for Al$_{0.45}$Ga$_{0.55}$As.

## 3.3 Derivation for a Quaternary: In$_{1-x}$Ga$_x$As$_y$P$_{1-y}$

**Step 1 — Identify the four end-point binaries.**

- $a$ (InP) with $E_g = 1.35$ eV, $a = 5.8687$ Å
- $b$ (GaP) with $E_g = 2.26$ eV, $a = 5.4505$ Å
- $c$ (InAs) with $E_g = 0.36$ eV, $a = 6.0584$ Å
- $d$ (GaAs) with $E_g = 1.42$ eV, $a = 5.6533$ Å

The quaternary In$_{1-x}$Ga$_x$As$_y$P$_{1-y}$ is a *mixture* of all four, with two independent composition variables $x$ and $y$.

**Step 2 — Apply the generalized Vegard's law for a quaternary.**

$$a(x, y) = xy \cdot a_{GaAs} + x(1-y) \cdot a_{GaP} + (1-x)y \cdot a_{InAs} + (1-x)(1-y) \cdot a_{InP}$$

**Step 3 — Apply lattice-matching constraint $a(x, y) = a_{InP} = 5.8687$ Å.**

This gives the **iso-lattice-constant curve** in the $(x, y)$ plane. Substituting and solving for $y(x)$:

$$5.8687 = xy(5.6533) + x(1-y)(5.4505) + (1-x)y(6.0584) + (1-x)(1-y)(5.8687)$$

Expanding and isolating $y$:

$$y(x) = \dfrac{5.8687 - x(5.4505) - (1-x)(5.8687)}{x(5.6533 - 5.4505) + (1-x)(6.0584 - 5.8687) - (5.8687 - 5.4505)}$$

Simplifying the numerator:

$$\text{Num} = 5.8687 - 5.4505x - 5.8687 + 5.8687x = x(5.8687 - 5.4505) = 0.4182x$$

Simplifying the denominator:

$$\text{Den} = x(0.2028) + (1-x)(0.1897) - 0.4182$$
$$= 0.2028x + 0.1897 - 0.1897x - 0.4182$$
$$= 0.0131x - 0.2285$$

Therefore:

$$y(x) = \dfrac{0.4182 \, x}{0.0131 \, x - 0.2285}$$

**Step 4 — Bandgap along the iso-lattice-matched line.**

Using the bowing-parameter form (with $C \approx 0.10$ eV approximate):

$$E_g(x, y) = (1-x)y(0.36) + (1-x)(1-y)(1.35) + xy(1.42) + x(1-y)(2.26) - \text{bowing}$$

For the standard lattice-matched composition used in 1.55 μm laser diodes: $x \approx 0.42$, $y \approx 0.90$, giving $E_g \approx 0.81$ eV.

Then the emission wavelength is:

$$\lambda = \dfrac{1.240}{E_g} = \dfrac{1.240}{0.81} \approx 1.53 \text{ μm}$$

This precisely matches the **low-loss window of standard single-mode optical fiber** — the engineering reason for InGaAsP's commercial dominance.

## 3.4 Analytical Comparison Matrix

| Property | Binary | Ternary | Quaternary |
|:---|:---:|:---:|:---:|
| # of elements | 2 | 3 | 4 |
| # of independent composition vars | 0 | 1 | 2 |
| Degrees of freedom in (Eg, a) plane | 1 point | 1 line | 2-D region |
| Lattice match + Eg tuning? | ❌ | ❌ (one constraint remains) | ✅ |
| Bowing effects | N/A | Single bowing constant $C$ | Multiple bowing terms |
| Growth complexity (MOCVD/MBE) | Lowest | Moderate | Highest |
| Example | GaAs, InP | AlGaAs, InGaN | InGaAsP, AlGaInP |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Hierarchical Classification of Compound Semiconductors

The following Mermaid block diagram presents a complete taxonomy of compound semiconductors by **number of constituent elements** and by **periodic-table group family**.

```mermaid
graph TD
    A[Compound Semiconductors] --> B[Binary AB]
    A --> C[Ternary A_xB_1-xC]
    A --> D[Quaternary A_xB_1-xC_yD_1-y]

    B --> B1[Group III-V]
    B --> B2[Group II-VI]
    B --> B3[Group IV-IV]
    B --> B4[Group IV-VI]

    B1 --> B1a[GaAs 1.42 eV]
    B1 --> B1b[InP 1.35 eV]
    B1 --> B1c[GaN 3.39 eV]
    B1 --> B1d[InSb 0.17 eV]
    B1 --> B1e[AlAs 2.16 eV]
    B1 --> B1f[InAs 0.36 eV]

    B2 --> B2a[ZnSe 2.67 eV]
    B2 --> B2b[CdTe 1.50 eV]
    B2 --> B2c[ZnS 3.68 eV]
    B2 --> B2d[HgTe -0.15 eV]

    B3 --> B3a[SiC 2.3 to 3.2 eV]
    B4 --> B4a[PbS 0.41 eV]
    B4 --> B4b[PbTe 0.31 eV]

    C --> C1[III-III-V Type]
    C --> C2[II-II-VI Type]
    C --> C1a[Al_xGa_1-xAs]
    C --> C1b[In_xGa_1-xAs]
    C --> C1c[In_xGa_1-xN]
    C --> C1d[Al_xGa_1-xN]
    C --> C1e[Al_xIn_1-xP]
    C --> C2a[Hg_1-xCd_xTe]
    C --> C2b[Zn_1-xCd_xTe]

    D --> D1[III-III-V-V Type]
    D --> D2[II-II-VI-VI Type]
    D --> D1a[In_1-xGa_xAs_yP_1-y]
    D --> D1b[Al_xGa_1-xAs_yP_1-y]
    D --> D1c[Al_xGa_yIn_1-x-yN]
    D --> D1d[In_xGa_1-xAs_ySb_1-y]
    D --> D1e[Al_xGa_1-x_yIn_yP]
    D --> D2a[Hg_1-x-yCd_xZn_yTe]
```

## 4.2 Bandgap vs. Lattice-Constant "Tuning Knobs" Schematic

This block diagram illustrates how the **number of elements** translates into **degrees of freedom** on the (Eg, a) design plane — the central engineering concept of compound semiconductor classification.

```mermaid
graph LR
    P1[Binary: 1 fixed point in Eg-a plane] --> P2[Ternary: 1 line segment connecting 2 binaries]
    P2 --> P3[Quaternary: 2-D region bounded by 4 ternary lines]
    P3 --> P4[Quaternary iso-lattice line: a equals substrate constraint]
    P4 --> P5[Continuously tunable Eg while staying lattice-matched]

    P1 -. Example .-> EX1[GaAs at 1.42 eV, 5.653 A]
    P2 -. Example .-> EX2[AlGaAs from 1.42 to 2.16 eV, near 5.65 A]
    P3 -. Example .-> EX3[InGaAsP region, 0.75 to 1.35 eV, 5.87 A]
    P4 -. Example .-> EX4[1.55 um laser on InP substrate]
```

## 4.3 Composition-Tuning Block Diagram (for AlGaAs/GaAs Heterostructure)

```mermaid
graph TD
    S[Substrate: GaAs a equals 5.6533 A, Eg equals 1.42 eV] --> L1[MBE or MOCVD Growth Chamber]
    L1 --> A1[Al_xGa_1-xAs Layer x equals 0 to 0.45]
    A1 --> P1[E_g linearly tunable from 1.42 to 1.72 eV]
    A1 --> P2[Lattice mismatch less than 0.1 percent]
    P2 --> R[Result: pseudomorphic heterostructure]
    P1 --> D1[Device: HEMT, laser diode, LED]
    R --> D1
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading aid for the diagrams:** Each block maps a *classification category* (left side) to its *engineering consequence* (right side). In KTU exams, a diagram of this kind drawn cleanly on the answer sheet is worth 2–3 bonus marks for clarity.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Paper]
**Define a ternary compound semiconductor with a suitable example. State the role of the composition parameter.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

A **ternary compound semiconductor** is a semiconducting alloy formed by combining **three chemical elements**, typically obtained by mixing two binary compounds that share a common element (cation or anion). Its general formula is **A$_x$B$_{1-x}$C** (or **AB$_x$C$_{1-x}$**), where $x$ is the **molar composition parameter** that varies continuously between 0 and 1.

The composition parameter $x$ acts as a **tuning knob** for the material's electronic and structural properties. As $x$ is varied:
- The **bandgap** $E_g(x)$ changes (usually monotonically) from the bandgap of one binary end-point to the other.
- The **lattice constant** $a(x)$ varies linearly according to Vegard's law.
- Other properties (effective mass, mobility, refractive index) are also composition-dependent.

**Example:** Al$_x$Ga$_{1-x}$As is the most common ternary. When $x$ goes from 0 → 1, the bandgap varies from 1.42 eV (GaAs) to 2.16 eV (AlAs), and it remains nearly lattice-matched to GaAs throughout — making it ideal for GaAs-based heterostructure devices like HEMTs and laser diodes.

*(Full 3 marks requires: definition + formula + role of x + one worked example.)*

---

### Q2. [KTU University Exam – June 2024, Supplementary Model]
**Differentiate between ternary and quaternary compound semiconductors with one example each.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Feature | Ternary | Quaternary |
|:---|:---|:---|
| # of elements | 3 | 4 |
| General formula | A$_x$B$_{1-x}$C | A$_x$B$_{1-x}$C$_y$D$_{1-y}$ |
| # of independent composition parameters | 1 (e.g., $x$) | 2 (e.g., $x$ and $y$) |
| Degrees of freedom on (E_g, a) plane | 1 (line) | 2 (area) |
| Independent control of E_g and $a$ | Not possible (changing $x$ moves BOTH) | Possible (choose x AND y to fix $a$ while varying E_g) |
| Example | Al$_x$Ga$_{1-x}$As (GaAs-based) | In$_{1-x}$Ga$_x$As$_y$P$_{1-y}$ (InP-based) |
| Application | HEMT barrier layer, visible LED | 1.55 μm fiber-optic laser diode |

*(Full 3 marks: 1.5 marks for ternary explanation + 1.5 marks for quaternary contrast.)*

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long-Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks) — Choice Option 1

**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Q2(b) Adapted]**

**(a)** Classify compound semiconductors with suitable examples. Explain **binary, ternary, and quaternary** compound semiconductors in terms of their general formula and the **number of independent composition variables**. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** For the ternary alloy **Al$_x$Ga$_{1-x}$As**, calculate the lattice constant and the bandgap at $x = 0.30$ using Vegard's law and the linear (uncorrected) bandgap interpolation. Given: $a_{AlAs} = 5.6605$ Å, $a_{GaAs} = 5.6533$ Å, $E_{g,AlAs} = 2.16$ eV, $E_{g,GaAs} = 1.42$ eV. Comment on the suitability of this alloy for heterostructure devices on a GaAs substrate. **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution for Q-A(a) — 7 marks:**

**Step 1 (1 mark):** Compound semiconductors are classified by the **number of distinct elements** in their unit cell.

**Step 2 (2 marks):** 
- **Binary compounds (2 elements):** General formula AB. No composition degrees of freedom. Examples: GaAs, InP, ZnSe, SiC, GaN.
- **Ternary compounds (3 elements):** General formula A$_x$B$_{1-x}$C (or AB$_x$C$_{1-x}$). **One** independent composition variable $x$. Example: Al$_x$Ga$_{1-x}$As, In$_x$Ga$_{1-x}$N.
- **Quaternary compounds (4 elements):** General formula A$_x$B$_{1-x}$C$_y$D$_{1-y}$. **Two** independent composition variables $x, y$. Example: In$_{1-x}$Ga$_x$As$_y$P$_{1-y}$, (Al$_x$Ga$_{1-x}$)$_y$In$_{1-y}$P.

**Step 3 (2 marks):** The number of independent composition variables equals the **degrees of freedom** for tuning the bandgap $E_g$ and lattice constant $a$.

**Step 4 (2 marks):** 
- Binary → single point on (E_g, a) plane
- Ternary → line segment connecting two binary endpoints
- Quaternary → 2-D region; *iso-lattice-constant* sub-lines within it allow independent bandgap tuning while staying matched to a chosen substrate (key for 1.55 μm InP-based lasers).

---

**Model Solution for Q-A(b) — 7 marks:**

**Step 1 (1.5 marks):** Vegard's law for lattice constant:

$$a(x) = x \cdot a_{AlAs} + (1-x) \cdot a_{GaAs}$$

**Step 2 (1.5 marks):** Substituting $x = 0.30$:

$$a(0.30) = 0.30 \times 5.6605 + 0.70 \times 5.6533$$

$$= 1.69815 + 3.95731 = 5.65546 \text{ Å}$$

**Step 3 (1.5 marks):** Linear bandgap interpolation:

$$E_g(0.30) = 0.30 \times 2.16 + 0.70 \times 1.42 = 0.648 + 0.994 = 1.642 \text{ eV}$$

**Step 4 (1.5 marks):** Lattice mismatch with GaAs substrate:

$$\dfrac{\Delta a}{a} = \dfrac{5.65546 - 5.6533}{5.6533} = 3.8 \times 10^{-4} = 0.038\%$$

**Step 5 (1 mark):** **Conclusion:** Since the lattice mismatch is **< 0.1%**, the Al$_{0.3}$Ga$_{0.7}$As layer is **pseudomorphic** (essentially lattice-matched) to GaAs, and the bandgap is significantly larger than GaAs (1.642 eV vs 1.42 eV). This makes it **excellent** for use as a **barrier layer in HEMTs, a cladding/window layer in laser diodes, and as the wide-bandgap partner in GaAs-based heterostructure devices**.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Choice Option 2

**[KTU University Exam – June 2024, Q1(b) Adapted]**

**(a)** With a **neat block diagram**, explain the classification of compound semiconductors into **binary, ternary, and quaternary** types. Give **at least two examples** of each and state one key application of each class. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** The quaternary alloy **In$_{1-x}$Ga$_x$As$_y$P$_{1-y}$** is lattice-matched to InP substrate. Using the four end-point binaries (InP, GaP, InAs, GaAs), derive the general expression for the lattice constant $a(x, y)$. If $a_{InP} = 5.8687$ Å is required, find the **relation** $y(x)$ along which the alloy is lattice-matched to InP. Use the following data: $a_{InP} = 5.8687$ Å, $a_{GaP} = 5.4505$ Å, $a_{InAs} = 6.0584$ Å, $a_{GaAs} = 5.6533$ Å. **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution for Q-B(a) — 7 marks:**

**Step 1 (2 marks):** A clean **block diagram** is expected (see Section 4.1 Mermaid). The classification head shows: Compound Semiconductors → Binary / Ternary / Quaternary.

**Step 2 (2 marks):** Binary examples and applications:
- GaAs (zinc blende) — microwave FETs, integrated circuits.
- InP — substrate for 1.55 μm optical communication devices.
- GaN — blue/UV LEDs, power electronics.

**Step 3 (1.5 marks):** Ternary examples and applications:
- Al$_x$Ga$_{1-x}$As — HEMT barrier, heterostructure laser.
- In$_x$Ga$_{1-x}$N — **blue/green LED active region** (Nobel Prize, 2014).

**Step 4 (1.5 marks):** Quaternary examples and applications:
- In$_{1-x}$Ga$_x$As$_y$P$_{1-y}$ — **1.55 μm telecom laser diode**, photodetector.
- (Al$_x$Ga$_{1-x}$)$_y$In$_{1-y}$P — high-brightness red/orange LED.

---

**Model Solution for Q-B(b) — 7 marks:**

**Step 1 (2 marks):** Generalized Vegard's law for a quaternary (A=In, B=Ga, C=As, D=P):

$$a(x,y) = xy \cdot a_{GaAs} + x(1-y) \cdot a_{GaP} + (1-x)y \cdot a_{InAs} + (1-x)(1-y) \cdot a_{InP}$$

**Step 2 (1 mark):** Substitute the numerical values:

$$a(x,y) = 5.6533xy + 5.4505x(1-y) + 6.0584(1-x)y + 5.8687(1-x)(1-y)$$

**Step 3 (2 marks):** Set $a(x,y) = a_{InP} = 5.8687$ Å and simplify. Expanding:

$$5.8687 = xy(5.6533 - 5.4505 - 6.0584 + 5.8687) + x(5.4505 - 5.8687) + y(6.0584 - 5.8687) + 5.8687$$

$$5.8687 = xy(0.0131) - 0.4182x + 0.1897y + 5.8687$$

Cancelling $5.8687$ on both sides:

$$0 = 0.0131xy - 0.4182x + 0.1897y$$

**Step 4 (2 marks):** Solve for $y(x)$. Group terms:

$$0.1897y = 0.4182x - 0.0131xy = x(0.4182 - 0.0131y)$$

$$y(0.1897 + 0.0131x) = 0.4182x$$

$$\boxed{\,y(x) = \dfrac{0.4182 \, x}{0.1897 + 0.0131 \, x}\,}$$

This is the **iso-lattice-matching curve** for InGaAsP on InP. For $x = 0.42$ (typical 1.55 μm laser composition), $y \approx 0.90$, confirming the standard textbook value.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Mark-Loss Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Confusing "ternary" and "quaternary" examples** — students often write "AlGaAs" as a quaternary. It is a TERNARY (3 elements: Al, Ga, As).
> 2. **Omitting the bowing parameter $C$** — full-marks answers for bandgap should mention that linear interpolation is a first approximation and that the bowing correction $x(1-x)C$ exists.
> 3. **Mixing up common-anion vs common-cation ternaries** — AlGaAs is a *common-anion* ternary (anion As is shared); InGaP is also common-anion. Mercuric Cadmium Telluride (HgCdTe) is a *common-cation* ternary (cation Te is shared).
> 4. **Forgetting the second variable in quaternaries** — InGaAsP has TWO composition parameters $x$ and $y$; writing only one is incomplete.
> 5. **Wrong end-point binary mapping** — for InGaAsP, the end-points are InP, GaP, InAs, GaAs; do NOT write InAsP or InGaAs (those are ternaries).
> 6. **Sign error in the bowing term** — it is *subtracted* from the linear interpolation: $E_g = $ linear $-$ bowing.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist (print this before the exam):**

- **Compound semiconductor** = ≥ 2 elements from different periodic-table groups forming a crystal with bandgap ~ 0.1–6 eV.
- **Classification axis:** number of elements → binary (2), ternary (3), quaternary (4).
- **Binary examples:** GaAs, InP, GaN, ZnSe, CdTe, SiC, PbS.
- **Ternary examples:** AlGaAs, InGaAs, InGaN, HgCdTe, AlGaN.
- **Quaternary examples:** InGaAsP, AlGaInP, AlGaInN, InGaAsSb.
- **General formula:**
  - Binary: AB (no tuning)
  - Ternary: A$_x$B$_{1-x}$C (1 tuning knob → $x$)
  - Quaternary: A$_x$B$_{1-x}$C$_y$D$_{1-y}$ (2 tuning knobs → $x$ and $y$)
- **Vegard's law (lattice):** $a(x) = x \cdot a_{AC} + (1-x) \cdot a_{BC}$ (linear).
- **Bandgap:** $E_g(x) = x E_{g,AC} + (1-x) E_{g,BC} - x(1-x) C$ (linear − bowing).
- **Key engineering fact:** Quaternaries allow **independent** control of bandgap and lattice constant → enables 1.55 μm InP-based laser diodes (InGaAsP), EDFA-pumped optical fiber networks, and high-brightness visible LEDs (AlGaInP).
- **Bowing parameter $C$:** empirical, material-specific; arises from alloy disorder, atomic-size mismatch, and band-tail effects.
- **Number of degrees of freedom in (E_g, a) plane:** Binary = 0, Ternary = 1, Quaternary = 2.
- **Bandgap–wavelength conversion:** $\lambda (\mu m) = 1.240 / E_g (eV)$.
- **Group family map:**
  - III–V → majority of optoelectronic + high-speed devices
  - II–VI → IR detectors, blue-green emitters
  - IV–IV (SiC) → high-power, high-temperature
  - IV–VI (PbS, PbTe) → mid-IR detectors, thermoelectrics
- **Why binaries cannot do the job alone:** They are isolated points in property space; only by alloying (ternary / quaternary) can we *engineer* a material to match a substrate *and* hit a target wavelength *simultaneously*.
- **Master equation to memorize first:** $a(x) = x \cdot a_{AC} + (1-x) \cdot a_{BC}$ — the foundation of every KTU numerical.
- **Master fact to memorize first:** "Quaternary = 2 degrees of freedom = independent control of E_g and $a$."

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Material Properties of Compound Semiconductors: Properties of III-V Compound Semiconductors (GaAs, GaN, InP, AlGaAs, AlGaN, InGaAs, InAlGaN)
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEEVT413 - COMPOUND SEMICONDUCTORS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Material Properties of Compound Semiconductors: Properties of III-V Compound Semiconductors (GaAs, GaN, InP, AlGaAs, AlGaN, InGaAs, InAlGaN)

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition of III-V Compound Semiconductors

**III-V Compound Semiconductors** are binary, ternary, or quaternary compound materials formed by combining elements from **Group III (B, Al, Ga, In)** and **Group V (N, P, As, Sb)** of the periodic table. They possess a defined crystal structure (typically **Zincblende** or **Wurtzite**), a tuneable direct energy bandgap, and superior high-frequency, optoelectronic, and high-power electronic properties compared to elemental semiconductors like **Silicon (Si)** and **Germanium (Ge)**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** The course PEEVT413 focuses heavily on the **material parameters** of these compounds, because the choice of semiconductor directly dictates the operating wavelength of an optoelectronic device, the cut-off frequency of an HEMT, or the breakdown voltage of a power device.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine building a house. **Silicon (Si)** is like standard concrete — abundant, cheap, and decent for a normal house (general electronics). But if you need a *smart window* that changes opacity, a *high-speed bullet train track*, or a *UV sterilizer light*, you cannot use standard concrete. You need *specialized alloys*.

**III-V compounds are these specialized alloys** for electronics. By mixing Group III and Group V elements (like a chef mixing ingredients), engineers "dial in" specific properties:

- **GaAs** → Microwave ovens & satellite communication (high speed).
- **GaN** → LED bulbs & 5G base stations (high power, blue/UV light).
- **InGaAs** → Fibre optic internet receivers (detects the exact infrared wavelength used in optical fibres).

> [!NOTE]
> **Fundamental Design Rule:** The **bandgap energy ($E_g$)** increases as you move from heavier to lighter elements (e.g., $E_g$ of $\text{InSb} < \text{InAs} < \text{GaAs} < \text{GaN}$). Heavier atoms = weaker bonds = smaller bandgap.

## 1.3 Key Physical Constants of Major III-V Semiconductors

| Property | Symbol | Unit |
|----------|--------|------|
| Electron effective mass | $m_e^*$ | $kg$ or $m_0$ (free electron mass) |
| Hole effective mass | $m_h^*$ | $kg$ or $m_0$ |
| Electron mobility | $\mu_e$ | $\text{cm}^2/\text{V}\cdot\text{s}$ |
| Hole mobility | $\mu_h$ | $\text{cm}^2/\text{V}\cdot\text{s}$ |
| Energy bandgap | $E_g$ | $\text{eV}$ |
| Relative permittivity | $\varepsilon_r$ | dimensionless |
| Lattice constant | $a$ | $\text{Å}$ (Angstrom) |
| Saturation velocity | $v_{sat}$ | $\text{cm/s}$ or $10^7 \text{ cm/s}$ |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Reference Constants for all subsequent derivations:**
> - **Boltzmann constant:** $k_B = 1.38 \times 10^{-23} \text{ J/K} = 8.617 \times 10^{-5} \text{ eV/K}$
> - **Free electron mass:** $m_0 = 9.11 \times 10^{-31} \text{ kg}$
> - **Planck's constant:** $h = 6.626 \times 10^{-34} \text{ J}\cdot\text{s}$, $\hbar = h/2\pi$
> - **Elementary charge:** $q = 1.6 \times 10^{-19} \text{ C}$

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Why III-V Semiconductors are Superior to Silicon

The advantages stem from fundamental **material physics**:

1. **Direct Bandgap Nature:** Unlike Si (which has an *indirect* bandgap), most III-V compounds (GaAs, InP, GaN) have a **direct bandgap**. This means an electron dropping from the conduction band minimum (CBM) to the valence band maximum (VBM) does not require a phonon (lattice vibration) to conserve momentum. Result: **Highly efficient light emission** — essential for LEDs, LASERs, and photodetectors.
2. **Higher Electron Mobility:** The lighter effective mass in III-Vs means electrons accelerate faster under an electric field.
3. **Wider Bandgap Availability:** GaN ($E_g \approx 3.4 \text{ eV}$) and AlN ($E_g \approx 6.2 \text{ eV}$) enable high-temperature, high-power, and UV optoelectronics impossible with Si ($E_g = 1.12 \text{ eV}$).
4. **Heterostructure Engineering:** The lattice constants of ternary alloys (AlGaAs, InGaAs) can be matched to binary substrates, enabling **bandgap engineering** (modifying $E_g$ continuously).

## 2.2 The Binary III-V Compounds

### 2.2.1 Gallium Arsenide (GaAs)
- **Crystal Structure:** Zincblende (cubic)
- **Lattice constant:** $a = 5.653 \text{ Å}$
- **Bandgap:** $E_g = 1.42 \text{ eV}$ (at 300 K), **Direct**
- **Electron mobility:** $\mu_e \approx 8500 \text{ cm}^2/\text{V}\cdot\text{s}$ (5x higher than Si)
- **Application:** Microwave integrated circuits, Gunn diodes, HEMTs, infrared LEDs.

### 2.2.2 Gallium Nitride (GaN)
- **Crystal Structure:** Wurtzite (hexagonal, stable phase)
- **Lattice constants:** $a = 3.189 \text{ Å}$, $c = 5.185 \text{ Å}$
- **Bandgap:** $E_g = 3.4 \text{ eV}$ (at 300 K), **Direct**
- **Electron mobility:** $\mu_e \approx 1000\text{-}2000 \text{ cm}^2/\text{V}\cdot\text{s}$ (in bulk); $>2000$ in 2DEG
- **Application:** Blue/UV LEDs, 5G RF power amplifiers, power electronics.

### 2.2.3 Indium Phosphide (InP)
- **Crystal Structure:** Zincblende
- **Lattice constant:** $a = 5.868 \text{ Å}$
- **Bandgap:** $E_g = 1.35 \text{ eV}$, **Direct**
- **Electron mobility:** $\mu_e \approx 5400 \text{ cm}^2/\text{V}\cdot\text{s}$
- **Peak electron velocity** is extremely high $\rightarrow$ ideal for ultra-high-speed transistors (HEMTs for $>100 \text{ GHz}$ operation).
- **Application:** Optical fibre communication (1.3 µm and 1.55 µm wavelength), high-speed HEMTs.

## 2.3 The Ternary & Quaternary Alloys

Ternary alloys are formed by mixing two binary compounds with a common Group V or Group III element. The composition is denoted by a **mole fraction** $x$ (0 ≤ x ≤ 1).

### 2.3.1 Aluminium Gallium Arsenide — $\text{Al}_x\text{Ga}_{1-x}\text{As}$
- **Direct bandgap range:** $1.42 \text{ eV}$ (at $x=0$, GaAs) to $\approx 3.0 \text{ eV}$ (at $x \approx 0.45$).
- For $x > 0.45$, it becomes **indirect**.
- **Lattice constant:** Nearly constant ($a \approx 5.653\text{-}5.661 \text{ Å}$) over the entire range → **lattice-matched to GaAs**, the single most important property of this alloy.
- **Application:** Quantum wells in HEMTs, laser diodes, and red LEDs.

### 2.3.2 Aluminium Gallium Nitride — $\text{Al}_x\text{Ga}_{1-x}\text{N}$
- **Bandgap range:** $3.4 \text{ eV}$ (GaN) to $6.2 \text{ eV}$ (AlN).
- **Direct bandgap** across the entire range.
- **Application:** UV LEDs, deep-UV photodetectors, high electron mobility transistors (HEMTs) for 5G base stations.

### 2.3.3 Indium Gallium Arsenide — $\text{In}_x\text{Ga}_{1-x}\text{As}$
- **Bandgap range:** $0.36 \text{ eV}$ (InAs) to $1.42 \text{ eV}$ (GaAs).
- **Lattice constant** varies significantly with $x$ ($5.653 \text{ Å}$ to $6.058 \text{ Å}$).
- **Application:** PIN photodetectors for optical fibre (1.55 µm region), high-speed channel material in advanced CMOS/HEMTs.

### 2.3.4 Indium Aluminium Gallium Nitride — $\text{In}_x\text{Al}_y\text{Ga}_{1-x-y}\text{N}$ (Quaternary)
- A single quaternary alloy that can be **lattice-matched to GaN** while independently varying the bandgap.
- Enables strain-free heterostructures and high-quality quantum wells.
- **Application:** Full-spectrum white LEDs, high-efficiency laser diodes for projectors and Blu-ray.

## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> **CRITICAL INSTRUCTION FOR THE TABLE:** The vertical pipe symbol `|` has been replaced with `\vert` or `\mid` in all mathematical expressions to prevent markdown table corruption.

| # | Formula / Property | Mathematical Expression | Engineering Use |
|---|-------------------|--------------------------|-----------------|
| 1 | Bandgap of ternary alloy (Vegard's approximation extended) | $E_g(x) = x E_{g,1} + (1-x) E_{g,2} + C \cdot x(1-x)$ | Find bandgap of AlGaAs, InGaAs, AlGaN; $C$ is the bowing parameter |
| 2 | Vegard's Law (Lattice constant) | $a(x) = x \cdot a_1 + (1-x) \cdot a_2$ | Determine if heterostructure is lattice-matched |
| 3 | Effective mass scaling (linear approx) | $m^*(x) = x \cdot m_1^* + (1-x) \cdot m_2^*$ | Estimate density of states effective mass in ternary |
| 4 | Intrinsic carrier concentration | $n_i^2 = N_C N_V \exp\!\left(\dfrac{-E_g}{k_B T}\right)$ | Predict thermal carrier generation |
| 5 | Effective Density of States (CB) | $N_C = 2\!\left(\dfrac{2\pi m_e^* k_B T}{h^2}\right)^{3/2}$ | Number of available states in conduction band |
| 6 | Effective Density of States (VB) | $N_V = 2\!\left(\dfrac{2\pi m_h^* k_B T}{h^2}\right)^{3/2}$ | Number of available states in valence band |
| 7 | Conductivity | $\sigma = q (n \mu_e + p \mu_h)$ | Calculate sheet resistance and contact resistance |
| 8 | Mobility–Conductivity mass (Drude) | $v_d = \mu \mathcal{E}$ | Drift velocity under electric field $\mathcal{E}$ |
| 9 | Saturation velocity (GaN) | $v_{sat} \approx 2.5 \times 10^7 \text{ cm/s}$ | High-field transport limit |
| 10 | Critical field (GaN) | $\mathcal{E}_{crit} \approx 3.3 \text{ MV/cm}$ | Determines breakdown voltage in power devices |
| 11 | Johnson's Figure of Merit | $JFoM = \dfrac{\mathcal{E}_{crit} \cdot v_{sat}}{2\pi}$ | Compares high-frequency power capability |
| 12 | Baliga's Figure of Merit | $BFoM = \varepsilon_r \mu_e \mathcal{E}_{crit}^3$ | Compares low-frequency power switching loss |
| 13 | 2DEG Density (HEMT) | $n_s = \dfrac{\varepsilon}{q(d + \Delta d)}(\Phi_b - \Delta E_C)$ | Sheet carrier density in AlGaN/GaN HEMT |
| 14 | Photon emission wavelength | $\lambda(\mu m) = \dfrac{1.24}{E_g(\text{eV})}$ | Wavelength of light emitted by LED/LASER |

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **5G Base Stations:** AlGaN/GaN HEMTs exploit the high **critical electric field** of GaN ($\mathcal{E}_{crit} = 3.3 \text{ MV/cm}$) to deliver tens of watts of RF power at frequencies up to 40 GHz.
- **Optical Fibre Communication:** InGaAs PIN photodiodes detect the **1.55 µm** wavelength (the silica-fibre loss minimum) with extremely low dark current.
- **Blu-ray Players:** InGaN blue laser diodes emit at 405 nm — derived directly from the bandgap of In$_x$Ga$_{1-x}$N.
- **Satellite TV LNBs (Low Noise Block converters):** InP HEMTs operate at 12 GHz–77 GHz with noise figures under 1 dB.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Vegard's Law and the Bowing Parameter for Ternary Alloys

We wish to derive the **bandgap of a ternary alloy** $A_x B_{1-x} C$ (e.g., Al$_x$Ga$_{1-x}$As) as a function of its mole fraction $x$.

### 3.1.1 Stage 1 — Linear Interpolation Assumption

If the lattice was a perfectly linear combination of the two binary endpoint compounds $AC$ and $BC$, the bandgap would be a straight line:

$$
E_g^{\text{linear}}(x) = x E_g(AC) + (1-x) E_g(BC)
$$

This is the **Vegard linear approximation** for energy.

### 3.1.2 Stage 2 — Introduction of the Bowing Parameter

In reality, the unit cell of the ternary alloy is not a simple arithmetic mean. The bond lengths, bond angles, and local chemical disorder introduce a **non-linear correction** that is a symmetric quadratic in $x$:

$$
E_g^{\text{ternary}}(x) = E_g^{\text{linear}}(x) - b \cdot x(1-x)
$$

where $b$ is the **bowing parameter** (in eV). Expanding the linear term:

$$
E_g^{\text{ternary}}(x) = x E_g(AC) + (1-x) E_g(BC) - b \cdot x(1-x)
$$

### 3.1.3 Stage 3 — Worked Example: $\text{Al}_x\text{Ga}_{1-x}\text{As}$

Given the endpoint binary values:

$$
E_g(\text{GaAs}) = 1.42 \text{ eV}, \quad E_g(\text{AlAs}) = 2.16 \text{ eV}, \quad b \approx 0.127 \text{ eV}
$$

Substitute into the general equation:

$$
E_g(x) = x(2.16) + (1-x)(1.42) - 0.127 \cdot x(1-x) \text{ eV}
$$

Simplify the linear combination:

$$
x(2.16) + (1-x)(1.42) = 2.16x + 1.42 - 1.42x = 0.74x + 1.42
$$

Therefore the closed-form result is:

$$
E_g(\text{AlGaAs}) = 1.42 + 0.74x - 0.127 x(1-x) \text{ eV}
$$

**Numerical verification at $x = 0.3$:**

$$
E_g = 1.42 + 0.74(0.3) - 0.127(0.3)(0.7) = 1.42 + 0.222 - 0.0267 \approx 1.615 \text{ eV}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Direct vs Indirect transition:** For $x < 0.45$, the bandgap remains **direct**; beyond $x > 0.45$, the $\Gamma$-valley minimum rises faster than the $X$-valley, so the alloy becomes **indirect**. This is critical for laser design — an indirect bandgap material is **inefficient at light emission**.

## 3.2 Derivation: Intrinsic Carrier Concentration in a Direct Bandgap Semiconductor

We now derive $n_i$, the most fundamental semiconductor property after $E_g$.

### 3.2.1 Stage 1 — Free Electron Density in the Conduction Band

For a parabolic conduction band, the density of states per unit volume per unit energy is:

$$
g_C(E) = \frac{1}{2\pi^2} \left( \frac{2m_e^*}{\hbar^2} \right)^{3/2} \sqrt{E - E_C} \quad \text{for } E > E_C
$$

The number of occupied states is governed by the Fermi–Dirac distribution. In the **non-degenerate case** (Boltzmann approximation), where $E_C - E_F \gg k_B T$:

$$
f(E) \approx \exp\!\left( -\frac{E - E_F}{k_B T} \right)
$$

Multiplying $g_C(E)$ by $f(E)$ and integrating from $E_C$ to $\infty$:

$$
n = \int_{E_C}^{\infty} g_C(E) \, f(E) \, dE
$$

### 3.2.2 Stage 2 — Perform the Integration

Substituting the expressions:

$$
n = \int_{E_C}^{\infty} \frac{1}{2\pi^2} \left( \frac{2m_e^*}{\hbar^2} \right)^{3/2} \sqrt{E - E_C} \exp\!\left( -\frac{E - E_F}{k_B T} \right) dE
$$

Apply the standard integral identity $\int_0^\infty \sqrt{u} \exp(-u/k_B T) du = \frac{\sqrt{\pi}}{2}(k_B T)^{3/2}$:

$$
n = 2 \left( \frac{2\pi m_e^* k_B T}{h^2} \right)^{3/2} \exp\!\left( -\frac{E_C - E_F}{k_B T} \right)
$$

Define the **effective density of states** in the conduction band:

$$
N_C \equiv 2 \left( \frac{2\pi m_e^* k_B T}{h^2} \right)^{3/2}
$$

So the result simplifies to:

$$
n = N_C \exp\!\left( -\frac{E_C - E_F}{k_B T} \right)
$$

### 3.2.3 Stage 3 — Mirror Result for the Valence Band

By symmetry, for holes in a parabolic valence band:

$$
p = N_V \exp\!\left( -\frac{E_F - E_V}{k_B T} \right)
$$

where:

$$
N_V = 2 \left( \frac{2\pi m_h^* k_B T}{h^2} \right)^{3/2}
$$

### 3.2.4 Stage 4 — The Law of Mass Action

Multiplying $n \cdot p$ eliminates the unknown Fermi level $E_F$:

$$
n \cdot p = N_C N_V \exp\!\left( -\frac{E_C - E_V}{k_B T} \right) = N_C N_V \exp\!\left( -\frac{E_g}{k_B T} \right)
$$

For an **intrinsic** semiconductor, $n = p = n_i$, hence:

$$
\boxed{n_i^2 = N_C N_V \exp\!\left( -\frac{E_g}{k_B T} \right)}
$$

## 3.3 Derivation: Why GaN is the King of Power Electronics — Baliga's Figure of Merit

The key figure of merit for a power-switching transistor is Baliga's FoM. We derive why a wide bandgap directly enables lower power loss.

### 3.3.1 Stage 1 — Conduction Loss in the On-State

A power switch in the ON state has a small on-resistance $R_{on}$. For a given breakdown voltage $V_{br}$, the on-resistance scales as:

$$
R_{on} = \frac{4 V_{br}^2}{\varepsilon_r \varepsilon_0 \mu_e \mathcal{E}_{crit}^3}
$$

The conduction power loss in a switching converter is proportional to $R_{on}$:

$$
P_{\text{loss}} \propto \frac{1}{\varepsilon_r \mu_e \mathcal{E}_{crit}^3}
$$

### 3.3.2 Stage 2 — The Wider the Bandgap, the Higher the Critical Field

The critical electric field $\mathcal{E}_{crit}$ is empirically related to the bandgap as:

$$
\mathcal{E}_{crit} \propto E_g^{3/2}
$$

Plugging this in:

$$
P_{\text{loss}} \propto \frac{1}{\varepsilon_r \mu_e E_g^{9/2}}
$$

### 3.3.3 Stage 3 — The Final Form of Baliga's FoM

For the same breakdown voltage, lower conduction loss is achieved by a *larger* value of:

$$
\boxed{BFoM = \varepsilon_r \mu_e \mathcal{E}_{crit}^3}
$$

Comparing GaN vs Si (at room temperature):

$$
\frac{BFoM_{\text{GaN}}}{BFoM_{\text{Si}}} = \frac{9 \times 2000 \times (3.3 \times 10^6)^3}{11.7 \times 1350 \times (0.3 \times 10^6)^3} \approx 1780
$$

This means a GaN power transistor has **~1800x lower conduction loss** than a silicon MOSFET of the same breakdown voltage — a revolutionary number.

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Comparative Material Properties Table

> [!NOTE]
> All values at **300 K**. Effective masses given in units of free-electron mass $m_0$.

| Material | $E_g$ (eV) | Direct? | $a$ (Å) | $m_e^*$ (× $m_0$) | $\mu_e$ ($\text{cm}^2/\text{Vs}$) | $\varepsilon_r$ | Key Use |
|----------|-----------|---------|--------|------------------|------------------------------|----------------|---------|
| Si | 1.12 | No | 5.431 | 0.26 | 1350 | 11.7 | Baseline CMOS |
| GaAs | 1.42 | Yes | 5.653 | 0.067 | 8500 | 13.1 | Microwave, IR LED |
| InP | 1.35 | Yes | 5.868 | 0.077 | 5400 | 12.5 | 1.55 µm fibre optics |
| GaN (wz) | 3.39 | Yes | 3.189 | 0.20 | 2000 (bulk) | 9.0 | 5G, Power, Blue LED |
| InAs | 0.36 | Yes | 6.058 | 0.023 | 40000 | 14.6 | IR detectors |
| AlAs | 2.16 | Indirect | 5.661 | 0.15 | 200 | 10.1 | Alloy component |
| InN | 0.70 | Yes | 3.538 | 0.07 | 3200 | 9.5 | Solar cells |

| Ternary Alloy | $E_g$ range (eV) | Bowing $b$ (eV) | Lattice match to | Application |
|--------------|-----------------|-----------------|------------------|-------------|
| $\text{Al}_x\text{Ga}_{1-x}\text{As}$ | 1.42 – 3.0 | 0.127 | GaAs (perfect) | HEMT barrier, laser |
| $\text{Al}_x\text{Ga}_{1-x}\text{N}$ | 3.4 – 6.2 | ~1.3 | GaN | UV LED, UV HEMT |

| $\text{In}_x\text{Ga}_{1-x}\text{N}$ | 0.7 – 3.4 | ~1.4 | GaN (at low $x$) | Blue/green LED |
| $\text{In}_x\text{Al}_{1-x}\text{As}$ | 0.36 – 3.0 | ~0.7 | InP at $x=0.52$ | HEMT barrier on InP |

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Block 1 — III-V Compound Semiconductor Family Tree

This diagram maps the relationship between binary, ternary, and quaternary III-V alloys and indicates their primary application domain.

```mermaid
graph TD
    N1[Group III Elements] --> N2[Aluminium Al]
    N1 --> N3[Gallium Ga]
    N1 --> N4[Indium In]
    N5[Group V Elements] --> N6[Nitrogen N]
    N5 --> N7[Phosphorus P]
    N5 --> N8[Arsenic As]
    N5 --> N9[Antimony Sb]
    N3 --> N10[GaAs Binary]
    N3 --> N11[GaN Binary]
    N4 --> N12[InP Binary]
    N10 --> N13[AlGaAs Ternary]
    N10 --> N14[InGaAs Ternary]
    N11 --> N15[AlGaN Ternary]
    N11 --> N16[InGaN Ternary]
    N12 --> N17[InAlAs Ternary]
    N13 --> N18[InAlGaAs Quaternary]
    N15 --> N19[InAlGaN Quaternary]
    N11 --> N20[White LED]
    N10 --> N21[Microwave HEMT]
    N12 --> N22[Fibre Optic Detector]
    N15 --> N23[5G Power Amplifier]
    N18 --> N24[1.55 um LASER]
    N19 --> N25[Full Spectrum LED]
```

## 4.2 Mermaid Block 2 — Bandgap Engineering Flow for AlGaAs

A flowchart showing how the bandgap of a ternary alloy can be continuously tuned by adjusting composition, including the critical direct-to-indirect transition.

```mermaid
graph TD
    S0[Start: AlAs + GaAs Mixture] --> S1{Choose mole fraction x}
    S1 --> S2a[x equals 0.0 - Pure GaAs]
    S1 --> S2b[x between 0 and 0.45]
    S1 --> S2c[x between 0.45 and 1.0]
    S1 --> S2d[x equals 1.0 - Pure AlAs]
    S2a --> S3a[Eg equals 1.42 eV, Direct]
    S2b --> S3b[Eg ranges 1.42 to 2.0 eV, Direct, Lattice Matched to GaAs]
    S2c --> S3c[Eg ranges 2.0 to 2.16 eV, Indirect]
    S2d --> S3d[Eg equals 2.16 eV, Indirect]
    S3b --> S4a[Application: HEMT Barrier, Quantum Well LASER]
    S3c --> S4b[Application: Wide Window Layer, Bragg Reflector]
    S3d --> S4c[Application: Insulating Barrier, Oxidation Layer]
```

## 4.3 Mermaid Block 3 — Block-Level Functional Architecture of an AlGaAs/GaAs HEMT

A physical cross-section is impossible in Mermaid, so the block diagram below maps the functional cross-section: how a Two-Dimensional Electron Gas (2DEG) is formed at the heterointerface.

```mermaid
graph TD
    L1[Gate Metal Schottky Contact] --> L2[AlGaAs Barrier Layer wide bandgap]
    L2 --> L3[Heterointerface 2DEG Channel]
    L3 --> L4[GaAs Buffer Layer narrow bandgap]
    L4 --> L5[Semi-Insulating GaAs Substrate]
    L6[Source Ohmic Contact] --> L3
    L3 --> L7[Drain Ohmic Contact]
    L8[Spontaneous Polarization Charge] --> L3
    L9[Piezolelectric Polarization Charge] --> L3
    L2 --> L8
    L4 --> L9
    L1 --> L10[Apply Negative VGS to Deplete 2DEG]
    L10 --> L3
    L7 --> L11[Drain Current ID Flows through 2DEG]
    L3 --> L11
```

## 4.4 Mermaid Block 4 — Crystal Structure Comparison

A schematic mapping of how Zincblende (GaAs, InP) and Wurtzite (GaN, AlN) structures arise from different stackings of Group III–V atomic bilayers.

```mermaid
graph LR
    S1[Zincblende Structure] --> S2[ABCABC Stacking]
    S1 --> S3[Cubic Unit Cell]
    S1 --> S4[Typical: GaAs, InP, InGaAs]
    S5[Wurtzite Structure] --> S6[ABABAB Stacking]
    S5 --> S7[Hexagonal Unit Cell]
    S5 --> S8[Typical: GaN, AlN, InGaN]
    S5 --> S9[Spontaneous Polarization Present]
    S1 --> S10[No Spontaneous Polarization]
    S3 --> S11[110 Cleavage Plane]
    S7 --> S12[Non Polar m Plane Available]
```

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1
**[KTU University Exam – Model Question, PEEVT413 Module 1]**
With neat diagrams and bandgap values, compare the **Zincblende** and **Wurtzite** crystal structures of GaAs and GaN. Why is GaN preferred for high-power and high-temperature applications?

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- **GaAs** has a **Zincblende** (cubic) structure, lattice constant $a = 5.653 \text{ Å}$, with an **ABCABC** stacking sequence of Ga–As bilayers. Its bandgap is $E_g = 1.42 \text{ eV}$ at 300 K.
- **GaN** has a **Wurtzite** (hexagonal) structure with $a = 3.189 \text{ Å}$ and $c = 5.185 \text{ Å}$, showing an **ABABAB** stacking sequence and a wider bandgap of $E_g = 3.39 \text{ eV}$.
- Because of the **wider bandgap**, GaN has a **much higher critical electric field** ($\mathcal{E}_{crit} = 3.3 \text{ MV/cm}$ vs $0.4 \text{ MV/cm}$ for GaAs) and operates at higher temperatures (>300 °C) without thermal runaway. (3 marks)

### Question 2
**[KTU University Exam – Model Question, PEEVT413 Module 1]**
Define the **bowing parameter** of a ternary semiconductor alloy. What is its physical origin, and what is the typical value of $b$ for $\text{Al}_x\text{Ga}_{1-x}\text{As}$?

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- The **bowing parameter** $b$ is the second-order coefficient that captures the **non-linear** deviation of a ternary alloy's bandgap from a linear interpolation of its two binary endpoints.
- It originates from **bond-length disorder**, **optical bowing** (band anticrossing), and **local chemical inhomogeneity** in the mixed sublattice.
- For $\text{Al}_x\text{Ga}_{1-x}\text{As}$, $b \approx 0.127 \text{ eV}$, which is small because Al and Ga have nearly identical atomic radii. (3 marks)

## 5.2 Part B — Full-Length Questions (14 Marks, Internal Choice)

### ⭐ Question A (Choice Option 1)

**[KTU University Exam – Model Question, PEEVT413 Module 1, 14 Marks]**
**(a)** Derive the expression for the **intrinsic carrier concentration** $n_i$ in a direct bandgap III-V semiconductor, clearly defining the effective density of states in the conduction and valence bands. (7 marks)

**(b)** A GaAs sample at 300 K is doped with $N_D = 10^{16} \text{ cm}^{-3}$ donors. Given $E_g = 1.42 \text{ eV}$, $m_e^* = 0.067 m_0$, $m_h^* = 0.50 m_0$, calculate (i) $N_C$, (ii) $N_V$, and (iii) the **minority hole concentration** $p$ using the law of mass action. (7 marks)

#### Model Solution — Part (a) [7 marks]

**Step 1 (1 mark):** Start with the density of states in a parabolic conduction band:
$$
g_C(E) = \frac{1}{2\pi^2} \left( \frac{2 m_e^*}{\hbar^2} \right)^{3/2} \sqrt{E - E_C}
$$

**Step 2 (2 marks):** In the non-degenerate (Boltzmann) limit, the electron density is:
$$
n = \int_{E_C}^{\infty} g_C(E) \exp\!\left( -\frac{E - E_F}{k_B T} \right) dE
$$

**Step 3 (2 marks):** Carry out the integration using $\int_0^\infty \sqrt{u} e^{-u/k_B T} du = \frac{\sqrt{\pi}}{2}(k_B T)^{3/2}$:
$$
n = 2 \left( \frac{2\pi m_e^* k_B T}{h^2} \right)^{3/2} \exp\!\left( -\frac{E_C - E_F}{k_B T} \right) = N_C \exp\!\left( -\frac{E_C - E_F}{k_B T} \right)
$$

**Step 4 (1 mark):** Similarly for holes:
$$
p = N_V \exp\!\left( -\frac{E_F - E_V}{k_B T} \right)
$$

**Step 5 (1 mark):** Multiplying and using $n = p = n_i$ in an intrinsic material:
$$
\boxed{n_i^2 = N_C N_V \exp\!\left( -\frac{E_g}{k_B T} \right)}
$$

#### Model Solution — Part (b) [7 marks]

**Step 1 — Compute $N_C$ (3 marks):** Using $T = 300 \text{ K}$, $k_B T = 0.02585 \text{ eV}$, and $m_e^* = 0.067 m_0$:

$$
N_C = 2 \left( \frac{2\pi \cdot 0.067 \cdot 9.11 \times 10^{-31} \cdot 1.38 \times 10^{-23} \cdot 300}{(6.626 \times 10^{-34})^2} \right)^{3/2}
$$

Calculating the inner term:

$$
\frac{2\pi \cdot 0.067 \cdot 9.11 \times 10^{-31} \cdot 1.38 \times 10^{-23} \cdot 300}{(6.626 \times 10^{-34})^2} \approx 1.93 \times 10^{20} \text{ m}^{-2}
$$

Raise to the power $3/2$:

$$
(1.93 \times 10^{20})^{1.5} \approx 8.47 \times 10^{30}
$$

Multiply by 2:

$$
N_C \approx 1.69 \times 10^{31} \text{ m}^{-3} = 4.7 \times 10^{17} \text{ cm}^{-3}
$$

**Step 2 — Compute $N_V$ (1 mark):** Using $m_h^* = 0.50 m_0$:

$$
N_V = 4.7 \times 10^{17} \times \left( \frac{0.50}{0.067} \right)^{3/2} \approx 4.7 \times 10^{17} \times 15.6 \approx 7.3 \times 10^{18} \text{ cm}^{-3}
$$

**Step 3 — Compute $n_i$ (1 mark):**

$$
n_i^2 = (4.7 \times 10^{17})(7.3 \times 10^{18}) \exp\!\left( -\frac{1.42}{0.02585} \right) \approx 3.43 \times 10^{36} \cdot e^{-54.93}
$$

Since $e^{-54.93} \approx 1.04 \times 10^{-24}$:

$$
n_i^2 \approx 3.57 \times 10^{12} \implies n_i \approx 1.89 \times 10^{6} \text{ cm}^{-3}
$$

**Step 4 — Compute minority hole concentration $p$ (2 marks):** Apply the law of mass action:

$$
n \cdot p = n_i^2 \implies p = \frac{n_i^2}{n} = \frac{(1.89 \times 10^{6})^2}{10^{16}} \approx \frac{3.57 \times 10^{12}}{10^{16}} \approx 3.57 \times 10^{-4} \text{ cm}^{-3}
$$

This extremely small value confirms that GaAs is effectively free of intrinsic carriers at room temperature — a major advantage over narrow-bandgap semiconductors. **(Total: 7 marks)**

---

### ⭐ Question B (Choice Option 2)

**[KTU University Exam – Model Question, PEEVT413 Module 1, 14 Marks]**
**(a)** Explain the **Vegard's law** and the **bowing parameter** for ternary alloys. Derive the bandgap equation for $\text{Al}_x\text{Ga}_{1-x}\text{As}$ and find the composition at which the direct-to-indirect crossover occurs. (7 marks)

**(b)** With a clear **energy band diagram**, explain the working of an **$\text{Al}_x\text{Ga}_{1-x}\text{As/GaAs}$ HEMT**. Why does the 2DEG have very high mobility? Mention two real-world applications. (7 marks)

#### Model Solution — Part (a) [7 marks]

**Step 1 (1 mark):** **Vegard's law** states that the lattice constant of a ternary alloy is a linear interpolation of its two binary endpoints:
$$
a(x) = x \cdot a_1 + (1-x) \cdot a_2
$$

**Step 2 (1 mark):** The bandgap deviates from linearity because of **optical bowing**. The general form is:
$$
E_g(x) = x E_{g,1} + (1-x) E_{g,2} - b \cdot x(1-x)
$$

**Step 3 (2 marks):** For AlGaAs with $E_g(\text{GaAs}) = 1.42 \text{ eV}$, $E_g(\text{AlAs}) = 2.16 \text{ eV}$, $b = 0.127 \text{ eV}$:

$$
\boxed{E_g(x) = 1.42 + 0.74x - 0.127 x(1-x) \text{ eV}}
$$

**Step 4 (1 mark):** The **direct** gap is associated with the $\Gamma$ valley, and the **indirect** gap with the $X$ valley. The direct gap grows as $E_g^\Gamma(x) = 1.42 + 1.247x$ eV; the indirect gap is roughly $E_g^X(x) \approx 1.90 + 0.21x$ eV.

**Step 5 (2 marks):** Setting $E_g^\Gamma = E_g^X$ to find the crossover composition $x_c$:

$$
1.42 + 1.247 x_c = 1.90 + 0.21 x_c
$$

$$
1.037 x_c = 0.48 \implies x_c \approx 0.46
$$

Beyond $x > 0.46$, AlGaAs becomes **indirect**, severely reducing its light-emission efficiency. **(Total: 7 marks)**

#### Model Solution — Part (b) [7 marks]

**Step 1 — HEMT Structure (3 marks):**
An HEMT (High Electron Mobility Transistor) is built on an undoped GaAs buffer, topped by an **n-doped AlGaAs** donor layer, then a Schottky gate metal. The conduction band of AlGaAs is higher than that of GaAs by the conduction-band offset $\Delta E_C \approx 0.30 \text{ eV}$ (for $x \approx 0.3$). Donors in AlGaAs release electrons that **fall into the lower-energy GaAs layer** and get trapped in the narrow triangular quantum well formed at the heterointerface — this is the **2-Dimensional Electron Gas (2DEG)**.

**Step 2 — Why 2DEG has high mobility (2 marks):** Because the GaAs channel is **undoped**, there are **no ionized impurity scatterers** in the conduction path. The only scattering is from lattice phonons, allowing mobilities as high as $\mu_e \approx 10^7 \text{ cm}^2/\text{Vs}$ at 77 K. Even at 300 K, 2DEG mobilities are $\sim 8000\text{-}10000 \text{ cm}^2/\text{Vs}$.

**Step 3 — Applications (2 marks):**
- **Direct Broadcast Satellite (DBS) LNBs** at 12 GHz.
- **mm-wave radar** for automotive and military applications.
- **High-speed digital logic** for fibre optic transceivers.

**(Total: 7 marks)**

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall — Read Carefully!**
> 1. **Do not** confuse $E_g$ of AlAs ($2.16$ eV, indirect) with the direct gap of AlAs at the $\Gamma$ point ($3.0$ eV). The **fundamental gap is $2.16$ eV** — that is the value KTU uses in Vegard's law.
> 2. **Do not** use $E_g(\text{AlAs}) = 3.0$ eV. If you do, your ternary bandgap will be **wrong by up to 0.5 eV**, and you lose full marks in part (a).
> 3. In HEMT questions, **always draw the conduction band bending** — the triangular well, the Fermi level, the donor level, and the 2DEG. A "flat band" diagram fetches zero marks.
> 4. For $n_i$ numericals, **always show the unit conversion** from $\text{m}^{-3}$ to $\text{cm}^{-3}$ explicitly. Skipping this step costs 1 mark.

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **III-V compounds** = Group III (Al, Ga, In) + Group V (N, P, As, Sb) combinations.
- **GaAs** is the workhorse for microwave electronics ($E_g = 1.42$ eV, $\mu_e = 8500 \text{ cm}^2/\text{Vs}$).
- **InP** dominates long-haul optical fibre communication ($1.55 \text{ μm}$).
- **GaN** (Wurtzite) is the choice for power electronics, 5G, and blue/UV LEDs ($E_g = 3.4$ eV, $\mathcal{E}_{crit} = 3.3$ MV/cm).
- **Ternary alloys** allow continuous tuning of $E_g$ via the mole fraction $x$.
- **Vegard's Law:** $a(x) = x a_1 + (1-x) a_2$.
- **Ternary bandgap:** $E_g(x) = x E_{g,1} + (1-x) E_{g,2} - b \cdot x(1-x)$.
- **AlGaAs** is nearly lattice-matched to GaAs over the **entire** $x$ range — its single biggest advantage.
- **InGaAs** is lattice-matched to InP at $x = 0.53$ — the foundation of 1.55 µm fibre-optic detectors.
- **AlGaN** is direct across the full range, enabling UV LEDs up to 6.2 eV (AlN endpoint).
- **Direct vs Indirect gap** is the deciding factor for light emission — only direct gap materials make efficient LEDs/LASERs.
- **Intrinsic carrier concentration:** $n_i^2 = N_C N_V \exp(-E_g/k_B T)$.
- **Effective density of states:** $N_C = 2(2\pi m_e^* k_B T/h^2)^{3/2}$.
- **Baliga's FoM** ($BFoM = \varepsilon_r \mu_e \mathcal{E}_{crit}^3$) explains why GaN has ~1800× lower power loss than Si.
- **Photon energy relation:** $\lambda(\mu\text{m}) = 1.24 / E_g(\text{eV})$ — use this to find LED emission wavelength.
- **HEMT = 2DEG at a heterointerface** — undoped channel gives extremely high mobility.
- **Bowing parameter $b$** is small for lattice-matched systems (AlGaAs: 0.127 eV) and large for highly mismatched systems (InGaN: ~1.4 eV).
- **Crystal structure rule:** AlGaAs is Zincblende (cubic); GaN, AlN, InN are Wurtzite (hexagonal).

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Group IV Compound Semiconductors — Formal Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Definition:**
> Group IV (IV–IV) compound semiconductors are binary or ternary alloys formed by combining two or more elements from Group IVA of the periodic table (C, Si, Ge, Sn). Unlike Group III–V or II–VI compounds, they consist of **non-polar covalent bonds** between chemically similar atoms, which gives them unique mechanical, thermal, and electronic properties.

The two most industrially significant Group IV compound semiconductors studied under this module are:

1. **Silicon Carbide (SiC)** — Si + C
2. **Silicon Germanium (SiGe)** — Si + Ge (and strained-Si on SiGe)

### Classification of SiC and SiGe within the IV family

| Compound | Type | Bond Nature | Polarity |
|----------|------|-------------|----------|
| Si | Elemental IV | Purely covalent | Non-polar |
| Ge | Elemental IV | Purely covalent | Non-polar |
| SiGe (Si₁₋ₓGeₓ) | **IV–IV alloy** | Covalent (mildly polar) | Quasi-non-polar |
| SiC | **IV–IV compound** | Partially ionic (~12%) | Weakly polar |

> [!NOTE]
> Although SiC is purely made of Group IV elements, the large electronegativity difference between Si (1.90) and C (2.55) introduces a **small ionic character (~12%)**, giving it some polar properties (e.g., weak piezoelectricity, larger LO–TO phonon splitting).

---

## 1.2 Intuitive Real-World Analogy

> [!TIP]
> **Analogy — "The Alloy Steel of Semiconductors":**
> Think of pure silicon as **standard steel** used in everyday nuts and bolts. Now imagine two ways of "upgrading" it for special jobs:
>
> 1. **SiC ≈ Tungsten Carbide (extreme-duty cutting tool):** You mix silicon with a very different, very stiff partner (carbon). The result is an ultra-hard, heat-proof, high-voltage-tolerant material — perfect for electric-vehicle power inverters and radar.
>
> 2. **SiGe ≈ Stainless Steel Alloy (tunable, versatile):** You blend silicon with a very close cousin (germanium). The result is a flexible alloy whose properties (bandgap, lattice constant, mobility) can be **tuned by changing the recipe (Ge fraction x)**. It is the "dial-a-material" of silicon-compatible electronics — perfect for high-speed transistors in mobile phones.

### Why IV Compounds Matter in KTU 2024 Context

- **SiC** enables the **"More Electric Aircraft" and EV revolution** because of its wide bandgap (2.3–3.26 eV), high breakdown field (~3 MV/cm), and ability to operate above 600 °C.
- **SiGe** extended the life of silicon technology by providing **bandgap engineering and strain engineering** for the Heterojunction Bipolar Transistor (HBT), the workhorse of RF front-ends (5G, Wi-Fi, satellite).

---

> [!IMPORTANT]

| Quantity | Si | Ge | SiC (4H) | SiGe (x = 0.2) |
|----------|-----|------|----------|----------------|
| **Bandgap (eV)** | **1.12** | 0.66 | **3.26** | ~1.00 |

| Thermal conductivity (W/cm·K) | 1.5 | 0.6 | **4.9** | ~1.4 |

| Saturation velocity (×10⁷ cm/s) | 1.0 | 0.6 | **2.7** | ~1.0 |

# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Silicon Carbide (SiC) — Deep Theoretical Breakdown

### 2.1.1 Crystal Structure and Polytypism

> [!NOTE]
> **Polytypism** is the property of a compound to crystallize into **multiple stacking variants** along the close-packed direction. SiC is the textbook example — it exhibits **over 200 known polytypes**, but only a few are commercially important.

The three most common SiC polytypes studied in KTU Module 1:

| Polytype | Stacking | Bandgap (eV) | Common Name |
|----------|----------|--------------|-------------|
| 3C-SiC | ABC (cubic, zincblende) | **2.36** | β-SiC |
| 4H-SiC | ABAC (hexagonal) | **3.26** | α-SiC (dominant in power) |
| 6H-SiC | ABCACB (hexagonal) | **3.02** | α-SiC (older devices) |

**Operational logic (How & Why):**

- **Why polytypes exist:** Si and C form strong, directional sp³ bonds. The (0001) basal plane of SiC can stack in many periodic ways without changing the local Si–C tetrahedral bonding.
- **Why it matters:** Different polytypes have **different bandgaps**, **different effective masses**, and **different breakdown fields**, even though they have identical local chemistry. A 4H-SiC device will block ~40 % more voltage than an identically sized 6H-SiC device.
- **Industry leader:** **4H-SiC dominates** power-device manufacturing (Wolfspeed, Infineon, STMicroelectronics) because of its highest bandgap among the practical polytypes.

### 2.1.2 Electronic Band Structure of SiC

- **Indirect bandgap** for all common polytypes (conduction-band minimum is away from the Γ point).
- Effective mass of electrons (4H-SiC): **m*ₑ ≈ 0.39 m₀** (perpendicular to c-axis) and **0.31 m₀** (parallel).
- Hole effective mass: highly anisotropic due to the hexagonal symmetry.

### 2.1.3 Key Engineering Properties of SiC

1. **High Breakdown Field (E_br ≈ 3 MV/cm):** Ten times that of Si. This is the **single most important property** for power electronics — it allows thinner drift regions for the same blocking voltage.
2. **High Thermal Conductivity (4.9 W/cm·K):** ~3× silicon. Allows SiC chips to dissipate enormous heat without exotic cooling.
3. **Wide Bandgap:** Low intrinsic carrier concentration even at high T → **junction leakage stays low up to 600 °C**.
4. **High Saturation Velocity (2.7 × 10⁷ cm/s):** Enables high-frequency RF operation.

### 2.1.4 Baliga's Figure of Merit (BFOM) — Why SiC Dominates Power Electronics

The **Baliga Figure of Merit** quantifies the conduction losses of a power device. For a unipolar device:

$$\text{BFOM} = \varepsilon \cdot \mu \cdot E_{br}^{3}$$

The figure below shows the **normalized** BFOM (Si = 1):

| Material | Normalized BFOM |
|----------|------------------|
| Si | 1 |
| GaAs | 16 |
| SiC (4H) | **≈ 500** |
| GaN | **≈ 1450** |

> [!TIP]
> **Real-world utility:** A SiC MOSFET in a Tesla Model 3 inverter replaces 6–8 silicon IGBTs and shrinks the inverter by ~70 % in volume while cutting switching losses by ~80 %.

### 2.1.5 Applications of SiC

- **Power MOSFETs and Schottky diodes** (EV traction inverters, solar inverters, SMPS)
- **High-temperature sensors** (jet engine, well-logging)
- **RF power amplifiers** (radar, 5G base stations)
- **UV photodetectors** and **blue LEDs** (early GaN-on-SiC substrate)
- **Nuclear radiation-hard detectors**

---

## 2.2 Silicon Germanium (SiGe) — Deep Theoretical Breakdown

### 2.2.1 Crystal Structure of SiGe

SiGe forms a **complete substitutional solid solution** Si₁₋ₓGeₓ, with 0 ≤ x ≤ 1. Both Si and Ge have the **diamond cubic structure** with very similar local bonding, so atoms substitute randomly on the same sublattice.

- **Lattice constant follows Vegard's Law** (nearly linear interpolation):

$$a_{SiGe}(x) = a_{Si} + x\,(a_{Ge} - a_{Si})$$

$$a_{SiGe}(x) = 5.431\,\text{Å} + 0.227\,x\,\text{Å}$$

- A small downward bowing (~0.02 Å) is observed experimentally, but for KTU problems Vegard's law is sufficient.

### 2.2.2 Bandgap of Strained and Unstrained SiGe

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **For unstrained (relaxed) Si₁₋ₓGeₓ at 300 K:**

$$E_{g}^{SiGe}(x) = 1.12 - 0.41\,x + 0.008\,x^{2}\,\,\text{eV}$$

This is the **canonical KTU formula** — memorize it.

- The bandgap **decreases** with Ge content because Ge has a smaller bandgap (0.66 eV) than Si (1.12 eV).
- For x < ~0.85, the alloy remains an **indirect bandgap** semiconductor. Above x ≈ 0.85, the conduction-band minimum crosses from the Δ valley to the L valley, but the **direct gap (Γ)** also decreases.

**Strained-SiGe / Strained-Si on SiGe (HBT/MOSFET context):**

When SiGe is grown epitaxially on a Si substrate, the lattice mismatch is accommodated by **strain**. Two cases:

1. **Strained SiGe on Si (compressively strained):** Used as the **base** of an SiGe HBT. The strain splits the valence band, reducing hole effective mass and increasing hole mobility.
2. **Strained Si on relaxed SiGe (tensile strained Si):** Used as the **channel** in advanced MOSFETs. The tensile strain splits the six-fold Δ conduction-band valleys, repopulating electrons into the two valleys with lower transverse mass, boosting electron mobility by **30–80 %**.

### 2.2.3 Effective Mass and Mobility in SiGe

- **Electron mobility** in unstrained SiGe generally **decreases** with x due to alloy scattering.
- **Hole mobility** in strained SiGe (the HBT base) **increases** dramatically with x due to strain-induced band splitting.
- **Strained Si on SiGe** with x ≈ 0.2–0.3 gives the optimal electron mobility enhancement.

### 2.2.4 SiGe Heterojunction Bipolar Transistor (HBT)

> [!NOTE]
> **The SiGe HBT** is one of the great commercial successes of bandgap engineering. By using a **Si₁₋ₓGeₓ base with x graded from ~0.05 at the emitter to ~0.20 at the collector**, the bandgap is **graded across the base**. This creates a **built-in quasi-electric field** that accelerates electrons across the base, reducing base-transit time by an order of magnitude.

**Key performance features of SiGe HBT:**

- Cut-off frequency **f_T > 500 GHz** in modern BiCMOS processes.
- Compatible with **standard CMOS fabrication** (no exotic tools needed).
- Power-efficient RF amplification for 5G, Wi-Fi 6/7, automotive radar (77 GHz).

### 2.2.5 Applications of SiGe

- **SiGe HBT** in BiCMOS RF front-ends (mobile phones, satellite)
- **SiGe photodetectors** for long-wavelength infrared (LWIR, 8–14 μm) imaging
- **Strained-Si/SiGe CMOS** for high-speed digital logic
- **Thermoelectric materials** (SiGe alloys used in RTGs of space probes — Voyager, Curiosity rover)
- **Bandgap-engineered solar cells**

---

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The following table consolidates every formula you will need for KTU 2024 Module 1 problems on SiC and SiGe. Note:** vertical bars `|` are written as `\vert` or `\mid` to keep the table safe.

| # | Formula / Relation | Description / Use | Units |
|---|--------------------|-------------------|-------|
| 1 | $a_{SiGe}(x) = 5.431 + 0.227\,x$ | Vegard's law for lattice constant of Si₁₋ₓGeₓ | Å |
| 2 | $E_{g}^{SiGe}(x) = 1.12 - 0.41\,x + 0.008\,x^{2}$ | Unstrained SiGe bandgap at 300 K | eV |
| 3 | $\varepsilon_{r}^{SiGe}(x) \approx 11.7 + 4.2\,x$ | Relative permittivity of Si₁₋ₓGeₓ | — |
| 4 | $E_{g}^{SiC}(4H) = 3.26\,\text{eV}$ | Bandgap of 4H-SiC | eV |
| 5 | $E_{g}^{SiC}(3C) = 2.36\,\text{eV}$ | Bandgap of 3C-SiC (β-SiC) | eV |
| 6 | $E_{g}^{SiC}(6H) = 3.02\,\text{eV}$ | Bandgap of 6H-SiC | eV |
| 7 | $\text{BFOM} = \varepsilon_{r}\cdot\varepsilon_{0}\cdot\mu\cdot E_{br}^{3}$ | Baliga figure of merit for unipolar power devices | — |
| 8 | $n_i \propto T^{3/2}\,\exp\!\left(-\dfrac{E_g}{2k_B T}\right)$ | Intrinsic carrier concentration | cm⁻³ |

| 11 | $\lambda_{c} = \dfrac{1.24}{E_g(\text{eV})}\,\mu\text{m}$ | Cut-off wavelength for absorption | μm |
| 12 | $E_{br} \propto E_g^{2.5}$ (empirical) | Breakdown field vs bandgap scaling | — |

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility of These Equations

- **Equation (1) — Vegard's law:** Used by process engineers to predict the **critical thickness** for pseudomorphic SiGe growth on Si.
- **Equation (2) — SiGe bandgap formula:** Used to design the **Ge profile** of an HBT base for the desired quasi-electric field.
- **Equation (7) — BFOM:** Used by device engineers to **rank materials** for power-device design — the single most important number behind the "SiC vs GaN vs Si" debate.
- **Equation (11) — Cut-off wavelength:** Used to design **SiGe infrared photodetectors** that can "see" thermal signatures through smoke (firefighting, military).
