## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024, Model Paper Set B]**
*What is meant by a direct bandgap semiconductor? Why is GaAs classified as a direct bandgap material, and what engineering advantage does this provide?*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

A semiconductor is called a **direct bandgap** material when the **conduction band minimum (CBM)** and the **valence band maximum (VBM)** occur at the **same value of crystal momentum k** (typically k = 0, the Γ point).

In GaAs, the CBM is located at the **Γ point** and the VBM is also at the **Γ point** — both at k = 0. Therefore, **GaAs is a direct bandgap semiconductor** with E_g = **1.42 eV** at 300 K.

**Engineering advantage:** Electrons can recombine directly with holes across the bandgap **without the assistance of a phonon**, releasing a photon. This makes GaAs highly efficient for **optoelectronic devices** such as **LEDs, laser diodes, photodetectors, and solar cells**. Si (indirect gap) cannot emit light efficiently — that is the key reason GaAs is used in optical communication.

*(Award: 1 mark for definition, 1 mark for GaAs identification, 1 mark for engineering advantage.)*

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
*Define Negative Differential Mobility (NDM). Under what conditions does it arise in GaAs, and what is its physical origin?*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Negative Differential Mobility (NDM)** is the phenomenon in which the **drift velocity of charge carriers decreases as the applied electric field is increased**, i.e., dv_d/dE < 0.

**Condition:** NDM arises in GaAs in the electric field range **E_th ≈ 3.2 kV/cm to E_v ≈ 20 kV/cm**.

**Physical origin:** The conduction band of GaAs has a **low-mass Γ valley** (m* = 0.067 m₀) at k = 0 and a **heavy-mass L valley** (m* = 0.55 m₀) located only **0.31 eV** higher in energy. When the electric field exceeds the threshold, electrons gain enough energy to **transfer (scatter) from the Γ valley to the L valley** via intervalley phonon scattering (the "transferred electron effect"). Since the L valley electrons have a much lower mobility, the **average drift velocity drops**, even though the field is increasing — producing the **NDM region**. This is the physical basis of the **Gunn Effect** (1963).

*(Award: 1 mark for definition + 1 mark for condition + 1 mark for physical origin.)*

---

### 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, Internal Choice)

---

#### **QUESTION A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – June 2024 Model Paper]**

**(a)** With a neat energy band diagram (E vs. k), explain the energy band structure of GaAs. Discuss the significance of the Γ, L, and X valleys. Compare the band structure of GaAs with that of Silicon. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution (a):**

**Step 1: E vs. k Diagram for GaAs**

The energy band structure of GaAs is plotted along the high-symmetry directions **Γ → X**, **Γ → L**, and **Γ → K** of the First Brillouin Zone (which is the BZ of an FCC lattice — a truncated octahedron).

Key features:

(i) The **valence band maximum (VBM)** is at the Γ point and consists of three bands:
- **Heavy Hole (HH)** band — large effective mass (~0.5 m₀).
- **Light Hole (LH)** band — small effective mass (~0.087 m₀).
- **Split-off (SO)** band — displaced downward by Δ_so = 0.341 eV due to spin–orbit coupling.

(ii) The **conduction band minimum (CBM)** is at the **Γ point** (k = 0). This makes GaAs a **direct bandgap** semiconductor with:

$$E_g = E_c(\Gamma) - E_v(\Gamma) = 1.424 \text{ eV at 300 K}$$

(iii) **Satellite valleys** exist in the conduction band at:
- **L point** [k = (π/a)(1,1,1)] — lying **0.31 eV** above the Γ minimum.
- **X point** [k = (2π/a)(1,0,0)] — lying **0.48 eV** above the Γ minimum.

**Step 2: Significance of the Three Valleys**

| Valley | Location | Effective Mass (m₀) | Density of States | Significance |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Γ | k = 0 | 0.067 | Low | **Direct gap**, high mobility (8500 cm²/V·s) |
| L | (π/a)(1,1,1) | 0.55 | High (× 14 vs Γ) | Receiver of hot electrons, low mobility |
| X | (2π/a)(1,0,0) | 0.85 | Highest | Plays role in high-field transport |

**Step 3: Comparison with Silicon**

| Property | Silicon (Si) | Gallium Arsenide (GaAs) |
|---|---|---|
| Bandgap type | **Indirect** (CBM at Δ, 0.85 × 2π/a along 100) | **Direct** (CBM at Γ) |
| Bandgap energy | 1.12 eV | 1.42 eV |
| Effective mass | 0.26 m₀ (longitudinal), 0.19 m₀ (transverse) | 0.067 m₀ |
| Mobility | 1400 cm²/V·s | 8500 cm²/V·s |
| Optoelectronic use | Poor (no efficient light emission) | Excellent (LEDs, lasers) |
| High-speed use | Limited | Superior (HEMTs, MESFETs) |

**Conclusion:** The **direct gap** and **low effective mass** of GaAs make it the material of choice for **optoelectronic and high-frequency applications**, while Si dominates **digital CMOS** due to its native oxide (SiO₂).

*(Marks: 1 for E-k diagram, 2 for valley analysis, 2 for Si comparison table, 2 for conclusion.)*

---

**(b)** Derive the velocity–field relationship in GaAs using the two-valley model. Explain how it leads to the transferred electron (Gunn) effect. Mention any two applications. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution (b):**

**Step 1: Two-Valley Model Setup**

The conduction band of GaAs is approximated by **two effective valleys**:
- **Valley 1 (Γ)**: Light electrons, mobility μ₁ = 8500 cm²/V·s.
- **Valley 2 (L)**: Heavy electrons, mobility μ₂ ≈ 1000 cm²/V·s, located ΔE = 0.31 eV above Γ.

Let n₁, n₂ be the electron densities in each valley. Total: n = n₁ + n₂.

**Step 2: Drift Velocity Expression**

The average drift velocity is:

$$v_d = \frac{n_1 \mu_1 + n_2 \mu_2}{n_1 + n_2}\,E$$

**Step 3: Boltzmann Occupation**

The fraction in the L valley is governed by Boltzmann statistics:

$$\frac{n_2}{n_1} = \frac{N_2}{N_1}\exp\!\left(-\frac{\Delta E}{k_B T_e(E)}\right)$$

where T_e is the **electron temperature**, which rises with E.

**Step 4: Energy Balance**

The rate of energy gain from the field equals the rate of energy loss to phonons:

$$eEv_d = \frac{3}{2}k_B\frac{T_e - T_L}{\tau_E}$$

where τ_E is the energy relaxation time and T_L is the lattice temperature.

**Step 5: Closed-Form Velocity-Field Expression**

After eliminating T_e, the drift velocity takes the empirical form:

$$v_d(E) = \frac{\mu_1 E + v_{s2}\left(\dfrac{E}{E_0}\right)^4}{1 + \left(\dfrac{E}{E_0}\right)^4}$$

with E₀ ≈ 4 kV/cm and v_{s2} ≈ 1.0 × 10⁷ cm/s.

**Step 6: Three Operating Regimes**

- **E < E_th ≈ 3.2 kV/cm:** $v_d \approx \mu_1 E$ — ohmic region.
- **E_th < E < E_v:** dv_d/dE < 0 — **NDM region**.
- **E > 20 kV/cm:** $v_d \to v_{sat} = 1.0 \times 10^7$ cm/s.

**Step 7: Transferred Electron (Gunn) Effect**

When E > E_th, electrons gain kinetic energy ≥ 0.31 eV from the field. They scatter from the light Γ valley to the heavy L valley via **intervalley phonons** with scattering time τ_iv ≈ 10⁻¹² s. Because the L valley has much higher density of states (∝ m*^{3/2}) and lower mobility, the **average velocity drops**. This produces the **NDM region**, which is unstable against charge fluctuations — leading to the formation of **high-field domains (Gunn domains)** that travel from cathode to anode, generating **current oscillations** at microwave frequencies.

**Step 8: Two Applications**

1. **Gunn Diode Oscillators** — used as solid-state microwave sources (4–100 GHz) in radar and communication systems.
2. **Terahertz sources** — using submicron GaAs Gunn devices, oscillation frequencies up to ~300 GHz have been demonstrated.
3. *(Bonus)* **MESFETs and HEMTs** — exploit the high Γ-valley mobility for high-speed digital and RF amplification.

*(Marks: 1 for setup, 2 for derivation, 1 for closed form, 1 for regimes, 1 for Gunn effect, 1 for applications.)*

---

#### **QUESTION B (14 Marks) [Alternative Choice]**

**(a)** Describe the crystal structure of GaAs in detail. Explain the bonding, lattice constant, and why the ⟨111⟩ direction is the natural cleavage plane. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution (a):**

**Step 1: Lattice Type**

GaAs crystallizes in the **zinc blende (sphalerite) structure**, also called the **cubic F-43m** space group (No. 216). It is the prototypical structure of III–V compound semiconductors.

**Step 2: Constructive Description**

It consists of:
- An **FCC Bravais lattice** with lattice constant **a = 5.6533 Å**.
- A **two-atom basis**: Ga at (0,0,0) and As at (a/4, a/4, a/4).

Equivalently, one can think of it as two inter-penetrating FCC sublattices — one of Ga and one of As — displaced by **a/4 along the body diagonal**.

**Step 3: Bonding and Coordination**

Each Ga atom is **tetrahedrally bonded** to 4 As atoms, and vice versa. The bonds are formed by **sp³ hybridization**, similar to the diamond cubic structure of Si. However, the bond is not purely covalent — the **electronegativity difference** (Ga = 1.81, As = 2.18, Δχ = 0.37) introduces **partial ionic character**, quantified by the **Phillips iconicity f_i = 0.31**.

**Step 4: Bond Length**

$$d_{Ga-As} = \frac{\sqrt{3}}{4}\,a = 0.4330 \times 5.6533 = 2.448 \text{ Å}$$

**Step 5: Cleavage Plane — ⟨111⟩**

The **(111) planes** are the **densest packed planes** in the zinc blende structure, but they contain **only one type of atom** (either all Ga or all As). Because the bonding between adjacent (111) planes is **weaker** (one Ga–Ga or As–As layer interaction) compared to the strong covalent Ga–As bonds within the plane, the crystal **cleaves naturally along {111} planes**. This property is exploited in:
- **Substrate preparation** for epitaxy (cleaving to obtain atomically flat surfaces).
- **Fabrication of cleaved-facet laser cavities** — the mirror facets of semiconductor lasers are formed by cleaving along (110), but the underlying anisotropy originates in the (111) bond asymmetry.

**Step 6: Properties Stemming from Structure**

| Property | Value | Origin |
|---|---|---|
| Lattice constant | 5.6533 Å | FCC + 2-atom basis |
| Coordination number | 4 (tetrahedral) | sp³ hybridization |
| Bond length | 2.448 Å | Geometric, √3·a/4 |
| Iconicity | 0.31 | Asymmetry in (111) bonds |
| Cleavage | (110) preferred, (111) weak | Bond density anisotropy |

**Conclusion:** The zinc blende structure, with its tetrahedral sp³ bonding and partial ionicity, gives GaAs its unique combination of **direct gap, high mobility, and piezoelectric/optical anisotropy**, which are exploited in modern devices like HEMTs and quantum well lasers.

*(Marks: 1.5 for structure, 1.5 for bonding, 1.5 for cleavage, 1.5 for bond length + conclusion.)*

---

**(b)** Explain the Gunn effect in GaAs with the help of the Ridley–Watkins–Hilsum (RWH) mechanism. Discuss the formation of Gunn domains and the threshold condition. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution (b):**

**Step 1: Historical Background**

The **Gunn effect** was experimentally discovered by **J. B. Gunn in 1963** in GaAs and InP. The theoretical explanation was given by **Ridley, Watkins, and Hilsum (RWH)** independently, hence the alternative name **RWH mechanism** or **transferred electron mechanism**.

**Step 2: Physical Cause — RWH Mechanism**

The RWH mechanism requires **two conditions**:
1. The semiconductor must have a **central valley with low effective mass** AND a **satellite valley with higher energy but higher effective mass**.
2. The energy separation between the valleys must be **small** (ΔE ~ k_B T or a few × k_B T), so that thermal/field-induced transfer is possible.

Both GaAs and InP satisfy these conditions:
- **GaAs:** Γ valley (0.067 m₀, μ = 8500) → L valley (0.55 m₀, μ ≈ 1000), ΔE = 0.31 eV.
- **InP:** Γ valley (0.077 m₀) → L valley (0.4 m₀), ΔE = 0.63 eV.

**Step 3: Threshold Condition**

For Gunn oscillations to occur, the average drift velocity must exhibit a **negative slope** for some range of E. This requires:

$$\frac{dv_d}{dE} < 0 \quad \text{for} \quad E_{th} < E < E_v$$

The **threshold field** for GaAs is:

$$E_{th} \approx \frac{\Delta E}{e\,\mu_1 \tau_E L}$$

Numerically, **E_th ≈ 3.2 kV/cm** at 300 K. The product n × L (carrier concentration × length) must satisfy:

$$n \cdot L > 10^{12} \text{ cm}^{-2}$$

Otherwise, domains cannot nucleate (called the "**dead zone**" criterion).

**Step 4: Formation of Gunn Domains**

At E > E_th, any small fluctuation in electron density (a "dipole layer") creates a localized high-field region. Because the velocity–field curve is N-shaped, the **higher field locally reduces the drift velocity** of electrons behind the dipole, while electrons ahead drift faster. This **accumulates electrons on the trailing edge** and **depletes them on the leading edge**, amplifying the dipole — this is the **Gunn instability**.

The mature high-field domain is called a **Gunn domain** (or **accumulation layer**). It travels from **cathode to anode** at the saturation velocity v_sat ≈ 10⁷ cm/s.

**Step 5: Current Oscillations**

When a domain reaches the anode, it is absorbed, current spikes, and a new domain nucleates at the cathode. This **periodic nucleation + transit** produces **current oscillations** at the **transit-time frequency**:

$$f = \frac{v_{sat}}{L}$$

For L = 10 µm and v_sat = 10⁷ cm/s, **f ≈ 10 GHz**.

**Step 6: Applications**

1. **Gunn diode oscillators** — microwave (4–100 GHz) and mm-wave sources for radar, radio links, doppler sensors.
2. **Terahertz (THz) sources** — submicron GaAs Gunn devices can reach 200–300 GHz.
3. **Self-oscillating mixers** — for mm-wave heterodyne receivers.
4. **Chaos generators** — for secure communication and random number generation (academic).

**Conclusion:** The Gunn effect is a direct macroscopic consequence of the **NDM region** in the velocity–field characteristic, which in turn originates from the **band-structure peculiarity** of GaAs — specifically the **Γ → L intervalley transfer**. It is the only solid-state microwave source that does **not** require a p–n junction.

*(Marks: 1.5 for RWH mechanism, 1.5 for threshold, 2 for domain formation, 1 for applications, 1 for conclusion.)*

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes That Cost Marks in This Topic:**
>
> 1. **Confusing Γ and L valley parameters.** Γ has **m* = 0.067 m₀** (low) and **μ = 8500 cm²/V·s** (high). L has **m* = 0.55 m₀** (high) and **μ ≈ 1000 cm²/V·s** (low). Reversing these is a guaranteed 2-mark loss.
> 2. **Saying "GaAs has no NDM at room temperature"** — WRONG. NDM exists for E > 3.2 kV/cm. Some students mistakenly associate NDM only with cryogenic temperatures.
> 3. **Drawing the E–k diagram with the CBM at the X point** — WRONG. For GaAs, the **Γ point is the CBM**. Si has its CBM at Δ (0.85 × 2π/a along 100) — do not mix them up.
> 4. **Forgetting units** in formulas (cm²/V·s vs m²/V·s, eV vs J). KTU examiners deduct 0.5 marks for missing units.
> 5. **Confusing "Gunn effect" with "avalanche effect"** — the Gunn effect is a **bulk, intervalley transfer** phenomenon, not a junction breakdown phenomenon.
> 6. **Writing only the formula for v_d(E) without deriving** — KTU demands the **step-by-step two-valley derivation**, not just the final expression. A standalone formula with no derivation scores at most 2 out of 7 marks.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **HIGH-DENSITY SUMMARY — Read this the night before the exam.**

- ⭐ **GaAs** is a **III–V direct bandgap** compound semiconductor with **E_g = 1.42 eV at 300 K**.
- ⭐ **Crystal structure:** zinc blende, FCC + 2-atom basis, **a = 5.6533 Å**, **bond length = 2.448 Å**, coordination = 4.
- ⭐ **Bonding:** sp³ covalent + 31% ionic (Phillips iconicity); ⟨111⟩ is the cleavage plane; ⟨110⟩ is the practical cleave facet.
- ⭐ **Conduction band valleys:**
  - **Γ (k=0):** m* = 0.067 m₀, μ ≈ 8500 cm²/V·s — **CBM, direct**.
  - **L (k = (π/a)(1,1,1)):** m* = 0.55 m₀, ΔE = 0.31 eV above Γ.
  - **X (k = (2π/a)(1,0,0)):** m* = 0.85 m₀, ΔE = 0.48 eV above Γ.
- ⭐ **Valence bands at Γ:** Heavy Hole, Light Hole, Split-off (Δ_so = 0.341 eV).
- ⭐ **Velocity–Field Characteristic:** Ohmic → Peak (~2 × 10⁵ m/s at E_th ≈ 3.2 kV/cm) → **NDM region** (dv/dE < 0) → Saturation (~1 × 10⁵ m/s).
- ⭐ **Negative Differential Mobility** originates from **Γ → L intervalley electron transfer** (RWH mechanism).
- ⭐ **Gunn Effect (1963):** discovered experimentally by J. B. Gunn; explained by Ridley–Watkins–Hilsum.
- ⭐ **Key formula (must memorize):** 
  $$v_d(E) = \frac{\mu_1 E + v_{s2}\,(E/E_0)^4}{1 + (E/E_0)^4}$$
- ⭐ **Threshold condition:** n · L > 10¹² cm⁻²; transit-time frequency: f = v_sat / L.
- ⭐ **Applications of GaAs:** LEDs, laser diodes, photodetectors, MESFETs, HEMTs, Gunn diodes, solar cells (space), MMICs.
- ⭐ **Key advantages over Si:** higher mobility, direct gap, NDM for THz, semi-insulating substrate available.
- ⭐ **Key disadvantages vs Si:** no native oxide, brittle, expensive, toxic (As), high surface recombination.
- ⭐ **Always quote temperature** when giving E_g (T = 300 K is the KTU default).
- ⭐ **Direct vs indirect bandgap:** in direct gap, **recombination is radiative** (light emission possible); in indirect gap (Si, Ge), recombination needs a phonon → inefficient for light.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: III-Nitride Semiconductors: Crystal Structure of Nitrides, Electrical and Optical Properties
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEEVT413 - COMPOUND SEMICONDUCTORS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** III-Nitride Semiconductors: Crystal Structure of Nitrides, Electrical and Optical Properties

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Module 1: III-Nitride Semiconductors

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **III-Nitride Semiconductors** are compound semiconductors formed by combining a Group III element (Aluminum — Al, Gallium — Ga, Indium — In) with Nitrogen (N) from Group V. The principal binary compounds are **AlN**, **GaN**, and **InN**, which span an enormous direct bandgap range from **~0.7 eV (InN)** to **~6.2 eV (AlN)**, with **GaN at ~3.4 eV**. Their ternary and quaternary alloys (AlGaN, InGaN, AlInN, AlGaInN) enable continuous bandgap engineering across the ultraviolet (UV) to near-infrared (NIR) spectrum.

The four most studied **III-N binary nitrides** are:

| Compound | Bandgap (eV) | Bandgap Type | Wavelength Equivalent |
|----------|-------------|--------------|----------------------|
| **AlN** | 6.2 | Direct | ~200 nm (Deep UV) |
| **GaN** | 3.4 | Direct | ~365 nm (Near UV / Violet) |
| **InN** | 0.7 | Direct | ~1770 nm (Near IR) |
| **BN** | ~6.4 (hex) / 6.36 (cubic) | Indirect (hex) | Deep UV |

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Intuitive Picture — The Lego Block Analogy:**
> Imagine a child's building block set with two types of pieces: a small blue block (Nitrogen, atomic radius ≈ 0.75 Å) and three larger red blocks of different sizes — Aluminum (smallest, 1.43 Å), Gallium (medium, 1.40 Å), and Indium (largest, 1.67 Å). The size mismatch between the small Nitrogen and the large Group-III atom is what makes III-nitrides so unique:
>
> 1. **Small N atom + Large Group-III atom** → forces tetrahedral bonding that is highly **strained** and **polar**, creating strong internal electric fields (**spontaneous polarization**).
> 2. **Varying Group-III size** (Al < Ga < In) → lets you "tune" the bandgap like turning a dial on a radio, picking any color of light from deep UV to infrared.
> 3. **Strong ionic bonding** (large electronegativity difference) → results in materials that are physically hard, chemically stable, and can survive high voltages and temperatures — perfect for **power electronics** and **harsh-environment** devices.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why III-Nitrides Matter in Engineering:**
> - **Blue/UV LEDs and Laser Diodes** (Nobel Prize in Physics 2014 to Akasaki, Amano, Nakamura)
> - **High Electron Mobility Transistors (HEMTs)** for 5G base stations and radar
> - **Power electronics** with breakdown fields **~10× silicon** (~3 MV/cm vs 0.3 MV/cm)
> - **Solar-blind UV photodetectors** for defense and flame detection

### 1.3 Standard Physical Constants and Metrics

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Physical Constants Used in This Module:**
> - Boltzmann constant: $k_B = 1.380 \times 10^{-23}$ J/K
> - Planck constant: $h = 6.626 \times 10^{-34}$ J·s
> - Reduced Planck constant: $\hbar = 1.055 \times 10^{-34}$ J·s
> - Elementary charge: $q = 1.602 \times 10^{-19}$ C
> - Vacuum permittivity: $\varepsilon_0 = 8.854 \times 10^{-12}$ F/m
> - Free electron mass: $m_0 = 9.109 \times 10^{-31}$ kg
> - Lattice constant of GaN: $a = 3.189$ Å, $c = 5.185$ Å
> - Lattice constant of AlN: $a = 3.111$ Å, $c = 4.979$ Å
> - Lattice constant of InN: $a = 3.538$ Å, $c = 5.703$ Å

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Crystal Structures of III-Nitrides

III-Nitride semiconductors crystallize in **three primary polytypes**:

### 2.1.1 Wurtzite (Hexagonal, 2H) Structure — Thermodynamically Stable

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The **wurtzite (WZ) structure** is the **thermodynamically most stable** phase for AlN, GaN, and InN under ambient conditions. It is the structure relevant to almost all commercial devices (LEDs, HEMTs, laser diodes).

**Structural Features:**

- **Bravais lattice:** Hexagonal close-packed (HCP)
- **Space group:** $P6_3mc$ (No. 186)
- **Atoms per unit cell:** 4 (2 Ga + 2 N)
- **Basis coordinates:**
  - Ga at: $(0, 0, 0)$ and $(1/3, 2/3, 1/2)$
  - N at: $(0, 0, u)$ and $(1/3, 2/3, u+1/2)$
  - where $u \approx 0.377$ (the **internal parameter**, ideal value is 3/8 = 0.375)
- **Bonding:** Each Group-III atom is tetrahedrally coordinated to 4 N atoms and vice versa.
- **Stacking sequence along c-axis:** **ABABAB...** (two-layer repeat)
- **Lattice constants:** $a$ (basal plane), $c$ (vertical)

**Step-by-step logic of why wurtzite is polar:**

1. The hexagonal close-packed arrangement has **only one unique 3-fold rotation axis** (the c-axis).
2. The bonds along the c-axis from cation-to-anion and anion-to-cation are **not equivalent** (no inversion symmetry).
3. This **broken inversion symmetry** along the c-axis produces two distinct polar faces: the **Ga-face (0001)** and the **N-face (000$\bar{1}$**).
4. The lack of inversion symmetry → permits **non-zero spontaneous polarization** and **piezoelectricity**.

### 2.1.2 Zincblende (Cubic, 3C) Structure — Metastable

- **Bravais lattice:** Face-centered cubic (FCC)
- **Space group:** $F\bar{4}3m$ (No. 216)
- **Stacking sequence along [111]:** **ABCABC...** (three-layer repeat)
- **Lattice constant (GaN):** $a_{ZB} = 4.50$ Å
- Grown epitaxially on cubic substrates like Si(001), 3C-SiC, or GaAs.
- **No spontaneous polarization** along [111] due to inversion symmetry — eliminates QCSE, but harder to grow phase-pure.

### 2.1.3 Rock-Salt (Cubic) Structure — High Pressure

- **Space group:** $Fm\bar{3}m$ (No. 225)
- Forms at very high pressures (> 50 GPa for GaN).
- Indirect bandgap — not useful for optical devices.

### 2.2 Lattice Parameters of Common Nitrides

| Material | $a$ (Å) | $c$ (Å) | $c/a$ (ideal = 1.633) | $u$ (ideal = 0.375) | Crystal Field Splitting $\Delta_{cf}$ (meV) |
|----------|---------|---------|------------------------|----------------------|----------------------------------------------|
| **AlN** | 3.111 | 4.979 | 1.601 | 0.380 | −230 |
| **GaN** | 3.189 | 5.185 | 1.626 | 0.377 | +22 |
| **InN** | 3.538 | 5.703 | 1.612 | 0.377 | +44 |

> [!NOTE]
> The deviation of $c/a$ and $u$ from their ideal values (1.633 and 0.375) is what gives wurtzite nitrides their characteristic **spontaneous polarization**. The **crystal-field splitting energy** $\Delta_{cf}$ determines the relative ordering of the heavy-hole, light-hole, and crystal-field split-off bands at $\Gamma$.

## 2.3 Band Structure and Bandgap

### 2.3.1 Binary Nitride Bandgaps at 300 K

$$E_g^{AlN} \approx 6.2\ \text{eV}, \quad E_g^{GaN} \approx 3.4\ \text{eV}, \quad E_g^{InN} \approx 0.7\ \text{eV}$$

All three are **direct bandgap** semiconductors at the $\Gamma$ point — critical for efficient light emission.

**Electron effective masses (along c-axis):**

$$m_e^*(\text{AlN}) \approx 0.40\,m_0, \quad m_e^*(\text{GaN}) \approx 0.20\,m_0, \quad m_e^*(\text{InN}) \approx 0.07\,m_0$$

### 2.3.2 Alloy Bandgaps — Vegard's Law and Bowing

For ternary alloys like $\text{Al}_x\text{Ga}_{1-x}\text{N}$, the bandgap is given by the **bowed Vegard's law**:

$$E_g(x) = E_g^{AB} \cdot x + E_g^{AC} \cdot (1-x) - b \cdot x \cdot (1-x)$$

where $b$ is the **bowing parameter**.

| Alloy | Bowing Parameter $b$ (eV) |
|-------|------------------------------|
| **AlGaN** | ~1.0 (composition dependent) |
| **InGaN** | ~1.4–2.5 |
| **AlInN** | ~2.5–5.0 |

**Step-by-step intuition for bandgap bowing:**

1. The alloy is a **random solid solution** — different bond lengths (Al–N, Ga–N, In–N) create local disorder.
2. Local strain and compositional fluctuations **broaden the band edges**, reducing the effective optical bandgap.
3. The bowing term $-bx(1-x)$ is a **parabolic** correction that is maximum at $x = 0.5$.

### 2.4 Polarization in III-Nitrides — A Unique Property

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The wurtzite nitrides possess **two simultaneous polarization mechanisms** that no other mainstream semiconductor family (Si, GaAs, InP) has in the same magnitude:
> 1. **Spontaneous Polarization ($P_{sp}$)** — present even in strain-free material
> 2. **Piezoelectric Polarization ($P_{pz}$)** — induced by strain

**Spontaneous Polarization Values (along c-axis):**

| Material | $P_{sp}$ (C/m²) |
|----------|------------------|
| **AlN** | $-0.081$ |
| **GaN** | $-0.029$ |
| **InN** | $-0.032$ |

**Piezoelectric Polarization:**

For biaxially strained layer on substrate with in-plane strain $\varepsilon$:

$$P_{pz} = 2 \cdot \frac{a - a_0}{a_0} \cdot \left( e_{31} - e_{33} \cdot \frac{C_{13}}{C_{33}} \right)$$

where $e_{ij}$ are piezoelectric coefficients and $C_{ij}$ are elastic constants.

**Total Polarization:**

$$P_{total} = P_{sp} + P_{pz}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Significance:** In an AlGaN/GaN HEMT, the discontinuity in spontaneous and piezoelectric polarization across the heterointerface induces a **two-dimensional electron gas (2DEG)** with sheet carrier densities of $n_s \sim 1 \times 10^{13}$ cm$^{-2}$ — among the highest achievable in any semiconductor system. This is the foundation of the **GaN HEMT** used in 5G base stations and military radar.

### 2.5 Electrical Properties

| Property | Si | GaAs | **4H-SiC** | **GaN** | **AlN** |
|----------|-----|------|-----------|---------|---------|
| Bandgap $E_g$ (eV) | 1.12 | 1.42 | 3.26 | **3.4** | **6.2** |
| Breakdown Field $E_{br}$ (MV/cm) | 0.3 | 0.4 | 2.0 | **3.3** | **15** |
| Electron Mobility $\mu_n$ (cm²/V·s) | 1400 | 8500 | 1000 | **1200 (bulk) / 2000 (2DEG)** | **300** |
| Saturation Velocity $v_{sat}$ ($\times 10^7$ cm/s) | 1.0 | 1.0 | 2.0 | **2.5** | **1.8** |
| Thermal Conductivity $\kappa$ (W/m·K) | 150 | 46 | 370 | **130 (GaN) / 250 (AlN)** | **285** |

**Figure of Merit (Baliga's):**

$$\text{BFOM} = \varepsilon \cdot \mu \cdot E_{br}^3$$

For GaN vs Si: BFOM ratio ≈ **~900×** → explains why GaN dominates high-power switching.

### 2.6 Optical Properties

**Refractive Index and Dielectric Constant:**

For GaN at $\lambda = 632$ nm:

$$n_{GaN} \approx 2.39, \quad \varepsilon_\infty \approx 5.35, \quad \varepsilon_s \approx 8.9$$

**Exciton Binding Energy:**

$$E_X = \frac{\mu \cdot q^4}{2 \cdot (4\pi\varepsilon_0\varepsilon_r)^2 \hbar^2} = \frac{13.6\ \text{eV}}{n^2 \cdot \varepsilon_r^2} \cdot \frac{\mu}{m_0}$$

| Material | $\varepsilon_r$ (static) | $\varepsilon_\infty$ (high-freq) | Exciton $E_X$ (meV) |
|----------|--------------------------|-----------------------------------|------------------------|
| **GaN** | 8.9 | 5.35 | ~25 |
| **AlN** | 8.5 | 4.6 | ~55 |
| **InN** | 10.5 | 6.7 | ~5 |

> [!NOTE]
> **Excitons in GaN survive at room temperature** because their binding energy (25 meV) exceeds $k_BT$ (26 meV at 300 K). This is why GaN LEDs and laser diodes have very high quantum efficiency.

**Phonon Modes in Wurtzite GaN:**

Group theory predicts **9 optical modes** at $\Gamma$:

$$\Gamma_{opt} = 1A_1 + 2B_1 + 1E_1 + 2E_2$$

- $A_1, E_1$ → both Raman and IR active (polar)
- $E_2$ → Raman active only (non-polar)
- $B_1$ → silent

**Typical phonon frequencies for GaN:**

- $E_2^{high}$ (Raman): 568 cm⁻¹
- $A_1(LO)$ (IR + Raman): 734 cm⁻¹
- $A_1(TO)$ (IR + Raman): 533 cm⁻¹
- $E_1(LO)$ (IR + Raman): 742 cm⁻¹
- $E_1(TO)$ (IR + Raman): 560 cm⁻¹

### 2.7 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Formulas to Memorize for KTU Board Exams:**

| # | Formula | Description | Units |
|---|---------|-------------|-------|
| 1 | $E_g(x) = E_g^1 \cdot x + E_g^2 \cdot (1-x) - b \cdot x(1-x)$ | Bowed Vegard's Law for alloys | eV |
| 2 | $a^{alloy} = a^1 \cdot x + a^2 \cdot (1-x)$ | Vegard's Law for lattice constant | Å |
| 3 | $E_X = \dfrac{13.6}{\varepsilon_r^2} \cdot \dfrac{\mu^*}{m_0}$ | Exciton binding energy | eV |
| 4 | $\mu^* = \dfrac{m_e^* \cdot m_h^*}{m_e^* + m_h^*}$ | Reduced effective mass | kg |
| 5 | $P_{total} = P_{sp} + P_{pz}$ | Total polarization (wurtzite) | C/m² |
| 6 | $n_s = \dfrac{\sigma_{pol}}{q} = \dfrac{\Delta P_{sp} + \Delta P_{pz}}{q}$ | 2DEG sheet density (HEMT) | cm⁻² |
| 7 | $\lambda_{photon} = \dfrac{1.24}{E_g(\text{eV})}$ | Photon wavelength from bandgap | μm |
| 8 | $r_{Bohr} = \dfrac{4\pi\varepsilon_0\varepsilon_r \hbar^2}{\mu q^2}$ | Exciton Bohr radius | m |
| 9 | $E_g(T) = E_g(0) - \dfrac{\alpha T^2}{T + \beta}$ | Varshni equation | eV |
| 10 | $\text{BFOM} = \varepsilon \cdot \mu \cdot E_{br}^3$ | Baliga's figure of merit | (rel.) |

**Boundary conditions & assumptions:**
- All formulas assume **single crystal**, **relaxed (unstrained)** unless otherwise stated.
- Vegard's law assumes **random alloy** (no clustering or phase separation).
- Exciton formula assumes **Wannier-Mott (large radius)** exciton model.
- For GaN: Varshni parameters are $\alpha = 0.94$ meV/K, $\beta = 791$ K.

### 2.8 Real-World Engineering Utility

> [!NOTE]
> **Where this theory is applied in industry:**
> 1. **Blue/white LEDs** — InGaN/GaN MQW active regions on sapphire or SiC substrates.
> 2. **Power conversion** — GaN-on-Si HEMTs in 100–650 V adapters and server PSUs.
> 3. **5G RF power amplifiers** — AlGaN/GaN HEMTs on SiC substrates (Qorvo, MACOM, Wolfspeed).
> 4. **UV-C sterilization** — AlGaN-based 265 nm LEDs for pathogen inactivation.
> 5. **Solar-blind UV detectors** — AlGaN Schottky photodiodes for missile plume detection.
> 6. **Micro-LED displays** — InGaN red-green-blue micro-LEDs for AR/VR.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Bowed Vegard's Law for AlGaN Bandgap

**Problem:** Derive the bandgap of $\text{Al}_x\text{Ga}_{1-x}\text{N}$ as a function of Al mole fraction $x$ and show its maximum deviation from linear interpolation at $x = 0.5$.

**Starting Point:**

The two binary endpoints are GaN and AlN with bandgaps:

$$E_g(\text{GaN}) = 3.4\ \text{eV}, \quad E_g(\text{AlN}) = 6.2\ \text{eV}$$

**Step 1: Define linear interpolation (Vegard's law with no bowing).**

$$E_g^{linear}(x) = E_g(\text{AlN}) \cdot x + E_g(\text{GaN}) \cdot (1 - x)$$

$$E_g^{linear}(x) = 6.2x + 3.4(1-x) = 3.4 + 2.8x \quad [\text{eV}]$$

**Step 2: Include the bowing correction term.**

Local strain and disorder introduce a **quadratic correction**:

$$E_g^{bowed}(x) = E_g^{linear}(x) - b \cdot x(1-x)$$

$$\boxed{E_g(x) = 3.4 + 2.8x - b \cdot x(1-x) \quad [\text{eV}]}$$

For AlGaN, the commonly used bowing parameter is $b \approx 1.0$ eV (composition-averaged value).

**Step 3: Substitute $b = 1.0$ eV and find the deviation.**

$$\Delta E_g(x) = E_g^{linear} - E_g^{bowed} = b \cdot x(1-x) = 1.0 \cdot x(1-x)$$

**Step 4: Maximize the deviation by differentiation.**

$$\frac{d(\Delta E_g)}{dx} = b(1 - 2x) = 0 \quad \Rightarrow \quad x = 0.5$$

$$\Delta E_g^{max} = b \cdot (0.5)(0.5) = \dfrac{b}{4} = \dfrac{1.0}{4} = 0.25\ \text{eV}$$

**Step 5: Compute bandgap values at selected compositions.**

| $x$ (Al) | $E_g^{linear}$ (eV) | $E_g^{bowed}$ (eV, $b=1.0$ eV) | Deviation (eV) |
|----------|----------------------|--------------------------------|------------------|
| 0.0 | 3.40 | 3.40 | 0.00 |
| 0.2 | 3.96 | 3.80 | 0.16 |
| 0.4 | 4.52 | 4.28 | 0.24 |
| **0.5** | **4.80** | **4.55** | **0.25** |
| 0.6 | 5.08 | 4.88 | 0.20 |
| 0.8 | 5.64 | 5.48 | 0.16 |
| 1.0 | 6.20 | 6.20 | 0.00 |

**Step 6: Engineering interpretation.**

> A 25% AlGaN alloy ($x=0.25$) has a bowed bandgap of $E_g \approx 3.92$ eV, corresponding to a UV-C emission wavelength of $\lambda = 1240/3.92 \approx 316$ nm. This is the basis for **UV-B/UV-C LED design**.

---

## 3.2 Derivation: Exciton Binding Energy in GaN

**Problem:** Calculate the exciton binding energy in GaN using the Wannier-Mott model and confirm that excitons are stable at room temperature.

**Step 1: Wannier-Mott Hamiltonian.**

The exciton is treated as a **hydrogen-like atom** with the electron and hole orbiting their common center of mass in a dielectric medium:

$$H = -\frac{\hbar^2}{2\mu}\nabla_r^2 - \frac{q^2}{4\pi\varepsilon_0\varepsilon_r \vert \vec{r}_e - \vec{r}_h \vert}$$

**Step 2: Analogy with hydrogen atom.**

The eigenvalues are:

$$E_n = -\frac{\mu q^4}{2(4\pi\varepsilon_0\varepsilon_r)^2 \hbar^2} \cdot \frac{1}{n^2} = -\frac{13.6\ \text{eV}}{n^2} \cdot \frac{\mu}{m_0} \cdot \frac{1}{\varepsilon_r^2}$$

**Step 3: Plug in GaN effective masses.**

- $m_e^*(\text{GaN}) = 0.20\ m_0$
- $m_h^*(\text{GaN}) = 0.80\ m_0$ (approximate density-of-states effective mass)
- $\varepsilon_r(\text{GaN}) = 8.9$

Reduced effective mass:

$$\mu = \frac{m_e^* \cdot m_h^*}{m_e^* + m_h^*} = \frac{0.20 \times 0.80}{0.20 + 0.80} \cdot m_0 = 0.16\ m_0$$

**Step 4: Compute ground state ($n = 1$) exciton binding energy.**

$$E_X = \frac{13.6\ \text{eV}}{(1)^2} \cdot \frac{0.16}{(8.9)^2} = \frac{13.6 \times 0.16}{79.21} = \frac{2.176}{79.21}$$

$$\boxed{E_X \approx 0.0275\ \text{eV} = 27.5\ \text{meV}}$$

**Step 5: Compare with thermal energy at 300 K.**

$$k_B T = (1.380 \times 10^{-23}) \times 300 / q = 0.0259\ \text{eV} = 25.9\ \text{meV}$$

**Step 6: Conclusion.**

Since $E_X (27.5\ \text{meV}) > k_BT (25.9\ \text{meV})$, a significant fraction of excitons in GaN **survive thermal dissociation at room temperature**. This is one of the key reasons why GaN is so efficient for LED applications — excitonic recombination contributes directly to light emission.

---

## 3.3 Derivation: 2DEG Sheet Carrier Density in AlGaN/GaN HEMT

**Problem:** Estimate the 2DEG sheet density at the AlGaN/GaN interface using polarization discontinuity.

**Step 1: Polarization values used.**

| Quantity | GaN | AlGaN ($x = 0.25$) |
|----------|-----|---------------------|
| $P_{sp}$ (C/m²) | $-0.029$ | $-0.045$ (approx.) |
| $P_{pz}$ (C/m²) | 0 (assumed relaxed) | $+0.015$ (tensile strain) |

**Step 2: Polarization discontinuity at the heterointerface.**

$$\Delta P = P_{GaN} - P_{AlGaN} = (-0.029) - (-0.045 + 0.015) = -0.029 - (-0.030)$$

$$\Delta P = +0.001\ \text{C/m}^2$$

**Step 3: Sheet carrier density from charge neutrality.**

The bound polarization charge at the interface is:

$$\sigma_{pol} = \Delta P = +1 \times 10^{-3}\ \text{C/m}^2$$

This positive bound charge is screened by free electrons, giving:

$$n_s = \frac{\sigma_{pol}}{q} = \frac{1 \times 10^{-3}}{1.602 \times 10^{-19}}$$

$$\boxed{n_s \approx 6.24 \times 10^{15}\ \text{m}^{-2} = 6.24 \times 10^{11}\ \text{cm}^{-2}}$$

**Step 4: Realistic engineering value.**

In optimized commercial HEMTs with $x \approx 0.25$–0.30, typical 2DEG densities are $1.0 \times 10^{13}$ cm$^{-2}$, because the piezoelectric component dominates over the spontaneous one for tensile-strained AlGaN on GaN.

---

## 3.4 Derivation: Varshni Equation for GaN Bandgap vs. Temperature

**Problem:** Compute the bandgap of GaN at 77 K, 300 K, and 500 K using the Varshni equation.

**Step 1: Varshni parameters for GaN.**

$$E_g(T) = E_g(0) - \frac{\alpha T^2}{T + \beta}, \quad E_g(0) = 3.51\ \text{eV}, \quad \alpha = 9.4 \times 10^{-4}\ \text{eV/K}, \quad \beta = 791\ \text{K}$$

**Step 2: Compute at $T = 77$ K.**

$$E_g(77) = 3.51 - \frac{9.4 \times 10^{-4} \times 77^2}{77 + 791} = 3.51 - \frac{9.4 \times 10^{-4} \times 5929}{868}$$

$$E_g(77) = 3.51 - 0.00642 = 3.504\ \text{eV}$$

**Step 3: Compute at $T = 300$ K.**

$$E_g(300) = 3.51 - \frac{9.4 \times 10^{-4} \times 90000}{300 + 791} = 3.51 - \frac{84.6}{1091}$$

$$E_g(300) = 3.51 - 0.0775 = 3.432\ \text{eV} \quad (\text{rounded to } 3.4\ \text{eV})$$

**Step 4: Compute at $T = 500$ K.**

$$E_g(500) = 3.51 - \frac{9.4 \times 10^{-4} \times 250000}{1291} = 3.51 - 0.182 = 3.328\ \text{eV}$$

**Step 5: Summary table.**

| Temperature (K) | $E_g$ (eV) | Wavelength $\lambda$ (nm) |
|------------------|-------------|-----------------------------|
| 77 | 3.504 | 354 (UV-A) |
| 300 | 3.432 | 361 (near UV) |
| 500 | 3.328 | 372 (violet edge) |

**Step 6: Engineering insight.**

> The bandgap **decreases by ~170 meV** from 77 K to 500 K. This explains why GaN LED emission wavelength **red-shifts with temperature**, which must be compensated by heatsinking in high-brightness LED modules.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Wurtzite vs. Zincblende Crystal Structure

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[III-Nitride Crystal Polytypes] --> B[Wurtzite WZ]
    A --> C[Zincblende ZB]
    A --> D[Rock-Salt RS]

    B --> B1[Hexagonal HCP]
    B --> B2[Space Group P6_3mc]
    B --> B3[Stacking ABAB]
    B --> B4[Polar along c-axis]
    B --> B5[Stable phase for AlN GaN InN]

    C --> C1[Cubic FCC]
    C --> C2[Space Group F-43m]
    C --> C3[Stacking ABCABC]
    C --> C4[No spontaneous polarization]
    C --> C5[Metastable grown on cubic substrates]

    D --> D1[Cubic NaCl-type]
    D --> D2[Space Group Fm-3m]
    D --> D3[High pressure phase above 50 GPa]
    D --> D4[Indirect bandgap not useful for optics]

    B5 --> E1[LED MQW structures]
    B5 --> E2[HEMT power devices]
    B5 --> E3[Laser diodes]
    B5 --> E4[UV photodetectors]

    C5 --> F1[Research applications]
    C5 --> F2[Elimination of QCSE]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Block-Level Functional Architecture Flow:** Wurtzite is the **commercial workhorse** of the III-N industry because (i) it is thermodynamically stable, (ii) it is the natural growth phase on sapphire and SiC substrates, and (iii) its built-in polarization fields can be engineered to create high-mobility 2DEGs without intentional doping.

## 4.2 AlGaN/GaN HEMT Polarization-Induced 2DEG Formation

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[AlGaN Barrier Layer x = 0.25] --> B[Polarization Charge sigma_p]
    B --> C[Positive Bound Charge at Top Interface]
    B --> D[Negative Bound Charge at AlGaN/GaN Interface]

    C --> E1[Depletion Region in AlGaN]
    C --> E2[Surface donor-like states supply electrons]

    D --> F[Electrons Accumulate to Screen Bound Charge]
    F --> G[2DEG Quantum Well Forms at Interface]

    G --> H1[Sheet Density n_s approx 1e13 per cm2]
    G --> H2[Mobility mu approx 2000 cm2 per Vs]
    G --> H3[Confinement in 5 to 10 nm triangular well]

    H1 --> I[HEMT Channel Conduction]
    H2 --> I
    H3 --> I

    I --> J[5G Base Station PAs]
    I --> K[Radar T/R Modules]
    I --> L[Fast Chargers 100 to 650 V]
    I --> M[Satellite Communication]
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The AlGaN/GaN HEMT 2DEG is **unique among semiconductors** because the 2DEG is induced purely by **physical polarization charge**, not by chemical doping. This is why GaN HEMTs work even at cryogenic temperatures, in space radiation environments, and in high-temperature automotive applications.

## 4.3 Energy Bandgap Coverage Map of III-N Alloys

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[InN 0.7 eV] --> B[In-rich InGaN]
    B --> C[GaN 3.4 eV]
    C --> D[Al-rich AlGaN]
    D --> E[AlN 6.2 eV]

    B --> B1[Green 530 nm to Yellow 590 nm LEDs]
    C --> C1[Blue 450 nm LEDs]
    C --> C2[UV-A 365 nm sources]
    D --> D1[UV-B 280 to 315 nm]
    D --> D2[UV-C 200 to 280 nm sterilization]
    E --> E1[Deep UV 200 nm photodetectors]
```

## 4.4 III-N Optoelectronic and Electronic Device Family Tree

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[III-N Materials: AlN, GaN, InN and Alloys] --> B[Optical Devices]
    A --> C[Electronic Devices]
    A --> D[Sensing Devices]

    B --> B1[Blue, Green, White LEDs]
    B --> B2[Blue Laser Diodes]
    B --> B3[UV-A, UV-B, UV-C LEDs]
    B --> B4[Micro-LED Displays]

    C --> C1[AlGaN/GaN HEMT]
    C --> C2[GaN Power FETs]
    C --> C3[GaN RF Power Amplifiers]
    C --> C4[InAlN/GaN HEMTs]

    D --> D1[Solar-blind UV Photodetectors]
    D --> D2[GaN Gas Sensors]
    D --> D3[AlGaN Pressure Sensors]

    B1 --> P1[General Lighting, Displays]
    B2 --> P2[Blu-ray, Laser Projection]
    B3 --> P3[Water Purification, Phototherapy]
    C1 --> P4[5G, Radar, EV Power]
    D1 --> P5[Defense, Flame Detection]
```

## 4.5 Phonon Mode Classification in Wurtzite GaN

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[9 Optical Modes at Gamma Point] --> B[Polar Modes: IR plus Raman Active]
    A --> C[Non-Polar Modes: Raman Active Only]
    A --> D[Silent Modes]

    B --> B1[A1 TO: 533 cm-1]
    B --> B2[A1 LO: 734 cm-1]
    B --> B3[E1 TO: 560 cm-1]
    B --> B4[E1 LO: 742 cm-1]

    C --> C1[E2 low: 145 cm-1]
    C --> C2[E2 high: 568 cm-1]

    D --> D1[B1 low]
    D --> D2[B1 high]
```

> [!NOTE]
> The $E_2^{high}$ Raman mode at **568 cm⁻¹** is the standard **signature of wurtzite GaN** and is used to evaluate crystalline quality (narrow linewidth = high quality, broad linewidth = high defect density).

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 [KTU University Exam – May 2024]
**Define the term "direct bandgap semiconductor" and explain why GaN is classified as a direct bandgap material. Why is this property essential for LED operation?**

**Model Answer:**

> A **direct bandgap semiconductor** is one in which the **minimum of the conduction band** and the **maximum of the valence band** occur at the **same crystal momentum vector $k$** in the Brillouin zone (typically at the $\Gamma$ point, $k = 0$).
>
> In GaN, the lowest conduction band minimum and the highest valence band maximum both occur at the $\Gamma$ point with an energy separation of $E_g \approx 3.4$ eV. Hence, GaN is a **direct bandgap** material.
>
> This property is essential for LED operation because in a direct bandgap semiconductor, an electron in the conduction band can recombine with a hole in the valence band **without requiring a change in momentum**. The probability of radiative recombination is therefore very high (transition matrix element is large), giving high **internal quantum efficiency**. In an indirect bandgap material like Si, a phonon must also be involved, making radiative recombination extremely inefficient — which is why Si cannot be used for LEDs.

### Question 2 [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]
**What is spontaneous polarization? How does it arise in wurtzite III-nitrides and what is its significance in AlGaN/GaN HEMT devices?**

**Model Answer:**

> **Spontaneous polarization ($P_{sp}$)** is the electric polarization that exists in a crystal **even in the absence of external electric fields or mechanical strain**. In wurtzite III-nitrides, $P_{sp}$ arises because the wurtzite structure **lacks inversion symmetry** along the c-axis: the bond from a Group-III atom to a Nitrogen atom is not equivalent to the reverse bond, producing a permanent electric dipole moment per unit cell.
>
> The values of $P_{sp}$ for AlN, GaN, and InN are approximately $-0.081$, $-0.029$, and $-0.032$ C/m² respectively, all negative because the spontaneous polarization points from the metal cation toward the nitrogen anion (i.e., from the [0001] to the [000$\bar{1}$] direction).
>
> In an **AlGaN/GaN HEMT**, the discontinuity in $P_{sp}$ (combined with piezoelectric polarization due to lattice-mismatch strain) at the heterointerface produces a high sheet density of bound positive charge at the AlGaN/GaN interface. This bound charge is screened by free electrons, forming a **two-dimensional electron gas (2DEG)** with $n_s \approx 1 \times 10^{13}$ cm⁻² — even without any intentional doping. This polarization-induced 2DEG is the foundation of all modern GaN HEMT technology.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – Dec 2024 Model Paper]

**(a)** Discuss the wurtzite crystal structure of GaN. Draw the unit cell and explain the bonding arrangement. Mention the values of lattice constants $a$ and $c$ and the $c/a$ ratio. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Compare the bandgap, breakdown field, electron mobility, and saturation velocity of GaN with those of Si and GaAs. Using these, explain why GaN is preferred for high-power, high-frequency electronic devices. **(7 Marks)**

---

#### Solution to Question A(a)

**Wurtzite Structure of GaN:**

The **wurtzite structure** is the thermodynamically stable crystal structure of GaN under ambient conditions. It belongs to the **hexagonal crystal system** with space group $P6_3mc$ (No. 186).

**Unit cell description:**

- The Bravais lattice is **hexagonal close-packed (HCP)**.
- There are **4 atoms per unit cell**: 2 Ga atoms and 2 N atoms.
- Atomic basis coordinates (in units of $a, a, c$):
  - Ga1: $(0, 0, 0)$
  - Ga2: $(1/3, 2/3, 1/2)$
  - N1: $(0, 0, u)$
  - N2: $(1/3, 2/3, u + 1/2)$
- The internal parameter $u \approx 0.377$ (ideally 0.375 for perfect tetrahedral bonding).

**Bonding arrangement:**

- Each Ga atom is **tetrahedrally coordinated** to 4 N atoms.
- Each N atom is **tetrahedrally coordinated** to 4 Ga atoms.
- The bonding is **sp³ hybridized** with a strong **ionic component** due to the large electronegativity difference between N (3.04) and Ga (1.81).
- The stacking sequence along the c-axis is **ABABAB...** (two-layer periodicity).

**Lattice constants:**

$$a_{GaN} = 3.189\ \text{Å}, \quad c_{GaN} = 5.185\ \text{Å}$$

**c/a ratio:**

$$\frac{c}{a} = \frac{5.185}{3.189} = 1.626$$

The ideal $c/a$ ratio for a perfect HCP structure is $\sqrt{8/3} = 1.633$. The slight deviation of $c/a$ from 1.633 and $u$ from 0.375 gives rise to the spontaneous polarization in GaN.

**Schematic of the wurtzite cell:**

> The hexagonal unit cell consists of a top hexagonal face (with Ga atoms at corners), a bottom hexagonal face (with N atoms offset by 1/3, 2/3), and the internal parameter $u$ describes the vertical position of the N atom relative to the Ga plane.

**Significance of the wurtzite structure:**

- The lack of inversion symmetry along the c-axis makes the **[0001] (Ga-face)** and **[000$\bar{1}$] (N-face)** directions non-equivalent.
- This polarity gives rise to strong **spontaneous polarization** and **piezoelectric effects**, which are exploited in HEMT devices.

---

#### Solution to Question A(b)

**Comparison of Material Parameters:**

| Property | Si | GaAs | **GaN** |
|----------|-----|------|---------|
| Bandgap $E_g$ (eV) | 1.12 | 1.42 | **3.4** |
| Breakdown field $E_{br}$ (MV/cm) | 0.3 | 0.4 | **3.3** |
| Electron mobility $\mu_n$ (cm²/V·s) | 1400 | 8500 | **1200 (bulk) / 2000 (2DEG)** |
| Saturation velocity $v_{sat}$ ($\times 10^7$ cm/s) | 1.0 | 1.0 | **2.5** |
| Thermal conductivity $\kappa$ (W/m·K) | 150 | 46 | **130** |

**Why GaN is preferred for high-power, high-frequency devices:**

1. **High breakdown field** (3.3 MV/cm, ~10× Si): Allows GaN devices to sustain **much higher voltages** per unit length, leading to smaller die sizes and lower on-resistance for a given voltage rating.
2. **Wide bandgap** (3.4 eV): Allows operation at **high temperatures** (junction temperatures up to 200–300 °C) and reduces **leakage currents** by orders of magnitude.
3. **High saturation velocity** (2.5 × 10⁷ cm/s, ~2.5× Si): Allows faster transit of carriers, enabling **higher frequency operation** (cutoff frequencies $f_T > 100$ GHz in advanced HEMTs).
4. **High 2DEG sheet density** (~10¹³ cm⁻²) in AlGaN/GaN HEMTs: Provides very high current-carrying capability per unit width.
5. **High Baliga figure of merit** ($\varepsilon \mu E_{br}^3$): GaN's BFOM is approximately **~900× that of Si**, making it ideal for power-switching applications.

**Engineering applications:**

- 5G base-station power amplifiers (3.5 GHz, 28 GHz, 39 GHz bands)
- L-band and S-band radar transmit/receive modules
- 100–650 V power converters for EVs, solar inverters, and server PSUs
- High-temperature electronics for downhole drilling and aerospace

---

### Question B (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – Dec 2024 Model Paper]

**(a)** Explain with suitable diagrams the differences between wurtzite and zincblende crystal structures. Why is wurtzite the preferred structure for III-nitride device applications? **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Derive the bowed Vegard's law expression for the bandgap of $\text{Al}_x\text{Ga}_{1-x}\text{N}$ alloy. Given $E_g(\text{AlN}) = 6.2$ eV, $E_g(\text{GaN}) = 3.4$ eV, and bowing parameter $b = 1.0$ eV, calculate the bandgap at $x = 0.3$. What emission wavelength does this correspond to? **(7 Marks)**

---

#### Solution to Question B(a)

**Comparison of Wurtzite and Zincblende Structures:**

| Feature | **Wurtzite (2H)** | **Zincblende (3C)** |
|---------|----------------------|----------------------|
| Crystal system | Hexagonal | Cubic (FCC) |
| Space group | $P6_3mc$ (No. 186) | $F\bar{4}3m$ (No. 216) |
| Bravais lattice | Hexagonal close-packed | Face-centered cubic |
| Stacking sequence (along c / [111]) | ABAB (2-layer) | ABCABC (3-layer) |
| Atoms per unit cell | 4 (2 cation + 2 anion) | 4 (2 cation + 2 anion) |
| Lattice constant (GaN) | $a = 3.189$ Å, $c = 5.185$ Å | $a = 4.50$ Å |
| Inversion symmetry | **Absent** along c-axis | **Present** |
| Spontaneous polarization | **Non-zero** | Zero |
| Coordination | Tetrahedral (4:4) | Tetrahedral (4:4) |
| Thermodynamic stability | **Stable** for all III-nitrides | Metastable |

**Diagrammatic description:**

> The wurtzite cell is a hexagonal prism; looking down the c-axis, atoms form a hexagonal pattern with stacking ABAB. The zincblende cell is a cube; looking down the [111] direction, atoms form a triangular pattern with stacking ABCABC. Both have the same nearest-neighbor tetrahedral arrangement, but their second-nearest neighbors differ.

**Why Wurtzite is preferred for III-nitride devices:**

1. **Thermodynamic stability:** Wurtzite is the equilibrium phase for AlN, GaN, and InN under standard conditions. Bulk crystal growth (e.g., HVPE, ammonothermal) and epitaxial growth (MOCVD, MBE) naturally produce wurtzite.
2. **Substrate compatibility:** The most common substrates (sapphire, SiC, Si(111)) all favor wurtzite growth with the c-axis normal to the surface.
3. **Built-in polarization engineering:** The spontaneous polarization in wurtzite can be combined with piezoelectric polarization from lattice-mismatch strain to create **polarization-induced 2DEGs** in HEMT structures — this is the **fundamental operating principle** of GaN HEMTs.
4. **Mature processing technology:** Decades of R&D investment (since the 1990s) have produced a robust, scalable wurtzite GaN-on-sapphire, GaN-on-SiC, and GaN-on-Si process technology.
5. **Defect tolerance:** Although wurtzite GaN has a high density of threading dislocations (10⁸–10¹⁰ cm⁻²) due to lattice-mismatch with substrates, it is remarkably **defect-tolerant** in optical devices because of its strong excitonic effects.

---

#### Solution to Question B(b)

**Derivation of Bowed Vegard's Law:**

**Step 1: Write linear Vegard's interpolation between AlN and GaN.**

$$E_g^{linear}(x) = E_g(\text{AlN}) \cdot x + E_g(\text{GaN}) \cdot (1 - x)$$

$$E_g^{linear}(x) = 6.2x + 3.4(1 - x) = 3.4 + 2.8x \quad [\text{eV}]$$

**Step 2: Add the bowing correction.**

The bowing term accounts for **local strain, alloy disorder, and band tailing** due to compositional fluctuations:

$$E_g(x) = E_g^{linear}(x) - b \cdot x(1-x)$$

$$\boxed{E_g(x) = 3.4 + 2.8x - 1.0 \cdot x(1 - x) \quad [\text{eV}]}$$

**Step 3: Substitute $x = 0.3$.**

$$E_g(0.3) = 3.4 + 2.8(0.3) - 1.0 \cdot (0.3)(0.7)$$

$$E_g(0.3) = 3.4 + 0.84 - 0.21 = 4.03\ \text{eV}$$

**Step 4: Compute emission wavelength.**

$$\lambda = \frac{1.24}{E_g(\text{eV})} = \frac{1.24}{4.03} = 0.308\ \mu\text{m} = 308\ \text{nm}$$

**Step 5: Identify the spectral region.**

> $\lambda = 308$ nm lies in the **UV-B band** (280–315 nm). This corresponds to the spectral range used for **medical phototherapy** (treatment of psoriasis, vitiligo) and **UV curing of inks/resins**.

**Step 6: Engineering context.**

Al₀.₃Ga₀.₇N is a **standard UV-B LED active material**. By adjusting the Al composition $x$ from 0 to 1, the emission wavelength can be tuned from 365 nm (UV-A, near GaN) all the way down to 200 nm (deep UV, near AlN) — a uniquely broad spectral coverage unmatched by any other direct-bandgap semiconductor family.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes That Cost Marks in KTU Board Exams:**
> 1. **Confusing Vegard's law with bowed Vegard's law:** Students often write $E_g(x) = 6.2x + 3.4(1-x)$ without the bowing term $-bx(1-x)$. Always include the bowing term unless specifically told to ignore it. Examiners **deduct 1–2 marks** for this omission.
> 2. **Forgetting to mention "direct bandgap":** When asked why GaN is suitable for LEDs, you must specifically say "GaN is a **direct bandgap** semiconductor with $E_g = 3.4$ eV at the $\Gamma$ point." Just saying "wide bandgap" is incomplete and may cost you a mark.
> 3. **Mixing up Ga-face and N-face polarity:** The Ga-face is the **(0001)** direction; the N-face is the **(000$\bar{1}$)** direction. Spontaneous polarization vector points from the cation (Ga) to the anion (N) — i.e., from [0001] to [000$\bar{1}$]. Getting this wrong in a HEMT derivation loses 1–2 marks.
> 4. **Wrong units for exciton binding energy:** Always report in **meV**, not eV, when comparing to $k_BT$ at 300 K.
> 5. **Missing the Varshni equation in temperature-dependent problems:** If the question gives $T \neq 300$ K, you must use the Varshni equation: $E_g(T) = E_g(0) - \alpha T^2/(T+\beta)$.
> 6. **Lattice constant confusion:** The wurtzite cell has **two** lattice constants ($a$ and $c$), not just one. Forgetting $c$ costs a mark.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — III-Nitride Semiconductors**

**Crystal Structure:**
- ☐ Wurtzite (2H) is the **thermodynamically stable** phase for AlN, GaN, InN.
- ☐ Space group $P6_3mc$; 4 atoms per unit cell; stacking ABAB along c.
- ☐ Lacks inversion symmetry along c-axis → produces spontaneous polarization.
- ☐ Zincblende (3C) is metastable; stacking ABCABC; no spontaneous polarization.
- ☐ Rock-salt forms only at very high pressures (> 50 GPa).

**Lattice Parameters to Memorize:**
- ☐ GaN: $a = 3.189$ Å, $c = 5.185$ Å, $c/a = 1.626$.
- ☐ AlN: $a = 3.111$ Å, $c = 4.979$ Å, $c/a = 1.601$.
- ☐ InN: $a = 3.538$ Å, $c = 5.703$ Å, $c/a = 1.612$.

**Bandgaps:**
- ☐ AlN = 6.2 eV, GaN = 3.4 eV, InN = 0.7 eV — all direct at $\Gamma$.
- ☐ Bowed Vegard's law: $E_g(x) = E_g^1 x + E_g^2 (1-x) - bx(1-x)$.
- ☐ Bowing parameters: AlGaN ≈ 1.0 eV, InGaN ≈ 1.4–2.5 eV.

**Polarization (the unique III-N property):**
- ☐ $P_{sp}$(GaN) = −0.029 C/m², $P_{sp}$(AlN) = −0.081 C/m², $P_{sp}$(InN) = −0.032 C/m².
- ☐ $P_{total} = P_{sp} + P_{pz}$.
- ☐ AlGaN/GaN HEMT 2DEG: $n_s \sim 10^{13}$ cm⁻² from polarization discontinuity.

**Electrical Properties:**
- ☐ GaN breakdown field = 3.3 MV/cm (~10× Si).
- ☐ 2DEG mobility ~ 2000 cm²/V·s; saturation velocity 2.5 × 10⁷ cm/s.
- ☐ Baliga's figure of merit: BFOM(GaN) ≈ 900× BFOM(Si).

**Optical Properties:**
- ☐ Refractive index GaN: $n \approx 2.39$ at 632 nm; static $\varepsilon_r = 8.9$.
- ☐ Exciton binding energy GaN ≈ 25–28 meV > $k_BT$ (26 meV at 300 K) → **room-temperature excitons**.
- ☐ Wannier-Mott model: $E_X = 13.6 \, \mu / (m_0 \varepsilon_r^2)$.
- ☐ Photon wavelength: $\lambda (\mu m) = 1.24 / E_g(\text{eV})$.

**Phonons:**
- ☐ 9 optical modes at $\Gamma$: $1A_1 + 2B_1 + 1E_1 + 2E_2$.
- ☐ $A_1$ and $E_1$ are both IR and Raman active.
- ☐ $E_2^{high}$ (568 cm⁻¹) is the signature Raman mode of wurtzite GaN.

**Key Engineering Applications:**
- ☐ Blue/white LEDs (Nobel Prize 2014).
- ☐ AlGaN/GaN HEMTs for 5G, radar, EV power.
- ☐ UV-C LEDs for sterilization and water purification.
- ☐ Solar-blind UV photodetectors for defense.

**Formulas to Memorize Cold:**
- ☐ $E_g(x) = 3.4 + 2.8x - bx(1-x)$ for AlGaN.
- ☐ $E_X = 13.6 \mu / (m_0 \varepsilon_r^2)$.
- ☐ $n_s = \Delta P / q$ for HEMT.
- ☐ $\lambda = 1.24 / E_g$.
- ☐ $E_g(T) = E_g(0) - \alpha T^2 / (T + \beta)$ (Varshni).
- ☐ $\text{BFOM} = \varepsilon \mu E_{br}^3$.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: AlGaN Alloy, InGaN Alloy, InAlGaN Quaternary Alloy
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEEVT413 - COMPOUND SEMICONDUCTORS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** AlGaN Alloy, InGaN Alloy, InAlGaN Quaternary Alloy

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1: CORE TECHNICAL DEFINITION & INTUITIVE OVERVIEW

### 1.1 AlGaN Alloy — Formal Definition

**Aluminum Gallium Nitride (AlGaN)** is a ternary III-V compound semiconductor alloy formed by combining Aluminum Nitride (AlN) and Gallium Nitride (GaN) in the wurtzite crystal structure. Its chemical formula is $\text{Al}_x\text{Ga}_{1-x}\text{N}$, where the mole fraction $x$ represents the Aluminum content and can be varied continuously from 0 (pure GaN) to 1 (pure AlN).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> AlGaN is the cornerstone material for **Ultraviolet (UV) optoelectronics** and **high-power, high-frequency electronic devices** because its bandgap can be tuned across the deep-UV to visible spectrum by simply adjusting the Aluminum mole fraction.

**Key Bandgap Range:** $\mathbf{3.4 \text{ eV (GaN)} \rightarrow 6.2 \text{ eV (AlN)}}$

#### Intuitive Analogy — The "Color Tuning Knob" 🎛️

Imagine you are mixing two colors of paint: yellow (GaN) and transparent UV (AlN). As you add more AlN, the resulting paint absorbs higher energy photons. In semiconductor terms, the **Aluminum content acts like a tuning knob** that raises the energy of photons the material can absorb or emit. More Aluminum = larger bandgap = shorter wavelength emission (deeper into UV).

> [!NOTE]
> **Crystal System:** Both AlN and GaN crystallize in the **wurtzite (hexagonal) structure** (space group $P6_3mc$) under ambient conditions. This is the thermodynamically stable phase and is responsible for the strong polarization properties of these alloys.

### 1.2 InGaN Alloy — Formal Definition

**Indium Gallium Nitride (InGaN)** is a ternary III-V compound semiconductor alloy formed from Indium Nitride (InN) and Gallium Nitride (GaN). Its chemical formula is $\text{In}_x\text{Ga}_{1-x}\text{N}$, where the mole fraction $x$ represents the Indium content.

**Key Bandgap Range:** $\mathbf{0.7 \text{ eV (InN)} \rightarrow 3.4 \text{ eV (GaN)}}$

#### Intuitive Analogy — The "Rainbow Bridge" 🌈

InGaN is the **only material system that can efficiently emit the entire visible spectrum** (red, green, blue) from a single material family. Think of Indium as a "softness agent" — adding more In lowers the bandgap, allowing the material to emit longer wavelength light. The 2014 Nobel Prize in Physics was awarded for the development of efficient blue InGaN LEDs that enabled white LED lighting.

> [!NOTE]
> **Historical Note:** The accepted bandgap of pure InN was revised from $\mathbf{1.9 \text{ eV}}$ to $\mathbf{0.7 \text{ eV}}$ around 2002-2006. This dramatic revision **doubled the InGaN bandgap tuning range**, opening up new applications in infrared and high-efficiency solar cells.

### 1.3 InAlGaN Quaternary Alloy — Formal Definition

**Indium Aluminum Gallium Nitride (InAlGaN)** is a quaternary III-V alloy with the general formula $\text{In}_x\text{Al}_y\text{Ga}_{1-x-y}\text{N}$. It combines all three binary nitrides (InN, AlN, GaN) and offers **two independent compositional degrees of freedom**.

**Key Bandgap Range:** $\mathbf{0.7 \text{ eV} \rightarrow 6.2 \text{ eV}}$ (full InN-to-AlN range)

#### Intuitive Analogy — The "3-Knob Color Mixer" 🎨

If AlGaN and InGaN are 1-dimensional color sliders, then InAlGaN is a **2-dimensional color plane**. You can independently adjust Indium (lowers bandgap) and Aluminum (raises bandgap) to land on a specific (bandgap, lattice constant) coordinate. This enables the most prized engineering capability: **lattice-matched heterostructures** with arbitrary bandgap.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why Quaternary Matters:** By choosing $(x, y)$ carefully, InAlGaN can be **lattice-matched to GaN** (eliminating strain and dislocations) while still providing a different bandgap. This is impossible with either AlGaN or InGaN alone.

| Property | AlGaN | InGaN | InAlGaN |
|----------|-------|-------|---------|
| **Composition** | 1 parameter ($x$) | 1 parameter ($x$) | 2 parameters ($x, y$) |
| **Bandgap Range (eV)** | 3.4 – 6.2 | 0.7 – 3.4 | 0.7 – 6.2 |
| **Typical Strain on GaN** | Tensile | Compressive | **Tunable** |
| **Primary Application** | UV LEDs, HEMTs | Visible LEDs, Lasers | UV LEDs, HEMTs |

## SECTION 2: DEEP THEORETICAL ANALYSIS & KTU HIGH-YIELD FORMULA SHEET

### 2.1 Vegard's Law — Linear Interpolation of Lattice Constants

Vegard's Law states that the lattice constant of a ternary alloy varies **linearly** with composition:

$$a(\text{A}_x\text{B}_{1-x}\text{C}) = x \cdot a(\text{AC}) + (1-x) \cdot a(\text{BC})$$

$$c(\text{A}_x\text{B}_{1-x}\text{N}) = x \cdot c(\text{AN}) + (1-x) \cdot c(\text{BN})$$

For the basal lattice constant $a$ in Angstroms (Å):
- $\text{AlGaN: } a = x(3.112) + (1-x)(3.189) = 3.189 - 0.077x$
- $\text{InGaN: } a = x(3.538) + (1-x)(3.189) = 3.189 + 0.349x$
- $\text{InAlGaN: } a = x(3.538) + y(3.112) + (1-x-y)(3.189)$

> [!NOTE]
> The InN/GaN lattice mismatch is **~10.9%** — much larger than the AlN/GaN mismatch of **~2.4%**. This is why InGaN films on GaN have very high defect densities for $x > 0.15$.

### 2.2 Bandgap Bowing — The Compositional Curvature

Unlike lattice constants, the bandgap of a ternary alloy exhibits a **non-linear (parabolic) dependence** on composition due to bond-length disorder, charge exchange, and relaxation effects:

$$E_g(x) = x \cdot E_g(\text{AC}) + (1-x) \cdot E_g(\text{BC}) - b \cdot x(1-x)$$

The parameter $b$ is the **bowing parameter** (in eV).

| Alloy | Bowing Parameter $b$ (eV) | Source of Curvature |
|-------|---------------------------|---------------------|
| $\text{Al}_x\text{Ga}_{1-x}\text{N}$ | $\mathbf{0.7 - 1.3}$ (commonly $\mathbf{1.0}$) | Local strain, atomic size disorder |
| $\text{In}_x\text{Ga}_{1-x}\text{N}$ | $\mathbf{1.4 - 2.6}$ (commonly $\mathbf{1.4}$) | Strong In–N vs Ga–N bond difference |
| $\text{In}_x\text{Al}_y\text{Ga}_{1-x-y}\text{N}$ | $\mathbf{\sim 2.1 - 3.0}$ (composition-dependent) | Combined disorder + clustering |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The **large bowing parameter of InGaN** means the bandgap drops *faster* than linearly with In content. This is why a relatively small In fraction ($x \sim 0.15$) is enough to reach the green emission range.

### 2.3 InAlGaN Bandgap — Multi-Component Bowing

For the quaternary alloy, the bandgap is computed using a two-step interpolation or a multi-bowing expansion:

$$E_g(x,y) = \frac{xy \cdot E_g(\text{InAlN}) + y(1-x-y) \cdot E_g(\text{AlGaN}) + x(1-x-y) \cdot E_g(\text{InGaN})}{1 - \text{cross terms}}$$

A more common practical form treats it as the sum of contributions:

$$E_g(\text{In}_x\text{Al}_y\text{Ga}_{1-x-y}\text{N}) \approx 3.4(1-x-y) + 6.2y + 0.7x - b_{InGaN} x(1-x) - b_{AlGaN} y(1-y) - b_{InAlN} xy$$

### 2.4 Polarization Effects — Spontaneous and Piezoelectric

III-nitrides in the wurtzite phase possess **two contributions to total polarization** $P$:

$$P_{total} = P_{sp} + P_{pz}$$

**Spontaneous Polarization $P_{sp}$** — exists even in unstrained material due to the non-centrosymmetric wurtzite structure and large electronegativity difference between the metal and N atoms.

**Piezoelectric Polarization $P_{pz}$** — induced by strain from lattice mismatch:

$$P_{pz} = 2 \cdot \frac{a_{sub} - a_{film}}{a_{film}} \cdot \left( e_{31} - \frac{c_{13}}{c_{33}} e_{33} \right)$$

where $e_{31}$, $e_{33}$ are piezoelectric constants and $c_{13}$, $c_{33}$ are elastic constants.

| Material | $P_{sp}$ (C/m²) | Direction of Strain on GaN |
|----------|----------------|---------------------------|
| **GaN** | $-0.034$ | — |
| **AlN** | $-0.090$ | Tensile |
| **InN** | $-0.042$ | Compressive |

> [!WARNING]
> The **polarization discontinuity at heterointerfaces** produces massive sheet charge densities of the order $\mathbf{10^{13} \text{ cm}^{-2}}$, which is responsible for the **2-Dimensional Electron Gas (2DEG)** in AlGaN/GaN HEMTs without any intentional doping. This is unique to the nitride system!

### 2.5 Lattice-Matching Condition for InAlGaN on GaN

Setting $a(\text{In}_x\text{Al}_y\text{Ga}_{1-x-y}\text{N}) = a(\text{GaN})$:

$$3.538x + 3.112y + 3.189(1-x-y) = 3.189$$

Simplifying:

$$0.349x = 0.077y \quad \Rightarrow \quad \boxed{x = 0.2206 \cdot y}$$

For every 1% of Al, you need ~0.22% of In to maintain lattice match to GaN.

### 2.6 KTU Formula Sheet — High-Yield Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula | Description | Units |
|---|---------|-------------|-------|
| 1 | $a_{AlGaN} = 3.189 - 0.077x$ | Lattice constant of AlGaN | Å |
| 2 | $a_{InGaN} = 3.189 + 0.349x$ | Lattice constant of InGaN | Å |
| 3 | $E_g(AlGaN) = 3.4(1-x) + 6.2x - 1.0 x(1-x)$ | Bandgap of AlGaN | eV |
| 4 | $E_g(InGaN) = 3.4(1-x) + 0.7x - 1.4 x(1-x)$ | Bandgap of InGaN | eV |
| 5 | $E_g(InAlGaN)$ with multi-bowing | Bandgap of InAlGaN | eV |
| 6 | $n \cdot \sigma_{pz} = P_{total}^{top} - P_{total}^{bottom}$ | Sheet charge at heterointerface | C/m² |
| 7 | $x = 0.2206 \cdot y$ | Lattice-match condition on GaN | dimensionless |
| 8 | $\lambda (\text{nm}) = 1240 / E_g(\text{eV})$ | Wavelength of band-edge emission | nm |
| 9 | $E_g(x,y)$ w/ InN revised to 0.7 eV | Full-spectrum quaternary design | eV |
| 10 | $\varepsilon_r(AlGaN) = 9.5(1-x) + 8.5x$ | Static permittivity of AlGaN | — |

> [!NOTE]
> The wavelength formula $\lambda = 1240 / E_g$ is derived from $E = hc/\lambda$ with $hc = 1240 \text{ eV·nm}$. It is the most-used back-of-envelope tool in nitride device design.

### 2.7 Engineering Utility — Why These Alloys Matter

1. **Solid-State Lighting:** InGaN enables efficient blue/green LEDs; AlGaN/InAlGaN extend to UV.
2. **Power Electronics:** AlGaN/GaN HEMTs handle $\geq 600$ V with high switching speeds (electric vehicles, 5G base stations).
3. **Laser Diodes:** Blue-violet laser diodes for Blu-ray storage, laser projection, and quantum dot excitation.
4. **Solar-Blind UV Photodetectors:** AlGaN with $E_g > 4.4$ eV detects UV-C without visible-blind filters.
5. **Lattice-Matched Heterostructures:** InAlGaN eliminates strain-induced defects in deep-UV LEDs — a major unsolved problem in modern optoelectronics.

## SECTION 3: STEP-BY-STEP DERIVATIONS & ANALYTICAL MATRIX

### 3.1 Derivation: Bandgap of $\text{Al}_{0.3}\text{Ga}_{0.7}\text{N}$ at 300 K

**Given:** Aluminum mole fraction $x = 0.3$, room temperature. Standard bandgap data: $E_g(\text{GaN}) = 3.4$ eV, $E_g(\text{AlN}) = 6.2$ eV, bowing parameter $b = 1.0$ eV.

**Step 1 — Write the bowing equation:**

$$E_g(\text{Al}_x\text{Ga}_{1-x}\text{N}) = x \cdot E_g(\text{AlN}) + (1-x) \cdot E_g(\text{GaN}) - b \cdot x(1-x)$$

**Step 2 — Substitute the values:**

$$E_g = (0.3)(6.2) + (0.7)(3.4) - (1.0)(0.3)(0.7)$$

**Step 3 — Evaluate the linear interpolation term:**

$$(0.3)(6.2) = 1.860 \text{ eV}$$
$$(0.7)(3.4) = 2.380 \text{ eV}$$
$$\text{Sum}_{linear} = 1.860 + 2.380 = 4.240 \text{ eV}$$

**Step 4 — Evaluate the bowing correction term:**

$$b \cdot x(1-x) = (1.0)(0.3)(0.7) = 0.21 \text{ eV}$$

**Step 5 — Subtract the bowing correction:**

$$E_g(\text{Al}_{0.3}\text{Ga}_{0.7}\text{N}) = 4.240 - 0.21 = \mathbf{4.03 \text{ eV}}$$

**Step 6 — Convert to emission wavelength (in case of direct-gap emission):**

$$\lambda = \frac{1240}{E_g} = \frac{1240}{4.03} = \mathbf{307.7 \text{ nm}}$$

This places the emission in the **UV-B region** (280–315 nm), useful for medical phototherapy and water purification.

---

### 3.2 Derivation: Lattice-Matched InAlGaN Composition for a UV LED

**Problem:** Design a quaternary $\text{In}_x\text{Al}_y\text{Ga}_{1-x-y}\text{N}$ alloy lattice-matched to GaN with a target bandgap of 4.5 eV (solar-blind UV detector).

**Given:** Target $E_g = 4.5$ eV, substrate GaN with $a = 3.189$ Å.
Bowing parameters: $b_{InGaN} = 1.4$ eV, $b_{AlGaN} = 1.0$ eV, $b_{InAlN} = 3.0$ eV.
Binary bandgaps: $E_g(\text{GaN}) = 3.4$ eV, $E_g(\text{AlN}) = 6.2$ eV, $E_g(\text{InN}) = 0.7$ eV.

**Step 1 — Apply lattice-matching condition on basal plane $a$:**

$$3.538x + 3.112y + 3.189(1-x-y) = 3.189$$

Expand and collect:

$$3.538x + 3.112y + 3.189 - 3.189x - 3.189y = 3.189$$

$$(3.538 - 3.189)x + (3.112 - 3.189)y = 0$$

$$0.349x - 0.077y = 0 \quad \Rightarrow \quad \boxed{y = 4.5325 x}$$

**Step 2 — Express bandgap as a function of $x$ only (substitute $y$):**

$$E_g(x) = 0.7x + 6.2y + 3.4(1-x-y) - 1.4x(1-x) - 1.0y(1-y) - 3.0xy$$

Substitute $y = 4.5325x$:

$$E_g(x) = 0.7x + 6.2(4.5325x) + 3.4(1 - x - 4.5325x) - 1.4x(1-x) - 1.0(4.5325x)(1-4.5325x) - 3.0x(4.5325x)$$

**Step 3 — Simplify the linear terms:**

Linear term = $0.7x + 28.1015x + 3.4 - 3.4x - 15.4105x$

$$= 3.4 + (0.7 + 28.1015 - 3.4 - 15.4105)x = 3.4 + 9.991x$$

**Step 4 — Simplify the bowing (quadratic) terms:**

First bowing: $1.4x(1-x) = 1.4x - 1.4x^2$
Second bowing: $1.0 \cdot 4.5325x(1-4.5325x) = 4.5325x - 20.5415x^2$
Third bowing: $3.0 \cdot 4.5325x^2 = 13.5975x^2$

Sum of bowing = $(1.4 + 4.5325)x - (1.4 + 20.5415 + 13.5975)x^2 = 5.9325x - 35.539x^2$

**Step 5 — Combine linear and quadratic contributions:**

$$E_g(x) = 3.4 + 9.991x - [5.9325x - 35.539x^2]$$

$$E_g(x) = 3.4 + 4.0585x + 35.539x^2$$

**Step 6 — Set $E_g(x) = 4.5$ eV and solve:**

$$35.539x^2 + 4.0585x + 3.4 - 4.5 = 0$$

$$35.539x^2 + 4.0585x - 1.1 = 0$$

**Step 7 — Apply the quadratic formula:**

$$x = \frac{-4.0585 \pm \sqrt{(4.0585)^2 - 4(35.539)(-1.1)}}{2(35.539)}$$

Compute the discriminant:

$$\Delta = 16.4714 + 156.3716 = 172.843$$

$$\sqrt{\Delta} = 13.147$$

**Step 8 — Take the physically meaningful (positive) root:**

$$x = \frac{-4.0585 + 13.147}{71.078} = \frac{9.0885}{71.078} = \mathbf{0.128}$$

**Step 9 — Compute the Aluminum content $y$:**

$$y = 4.5325 \times 0.128 = \mathbf{0.580}$$

**Step 10 — Compute Gallium content:**

$$1 - x - y = 1 - 0.128 - 0.580 = \mathbf{0.292}$$

**Final Result:** $\text{In}_{0.128}\text{Al}_{0.580}\text{Ga}_{0.292}\text{N}$ is lattice-matched to GaN with $E_g \approx 4.5$ eV (deep UV-C).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The high Al content (58%) is necessary because lattice-matching with In forces the substitution of Ga atoms with smaller Al atoms to compensate the larger In atoms. This high Al fraction is technologically challenging due to the low cracking threshold of high-Al-content AlGaN and InAlGaN films.

---

### 3.3 Derivation: Polarization Sheet Charge at AlGaN/GaN Interface

**Given:** AlGaN barrier with $x = 0.25$, thickness 25 nm, on a GaN buffer.

**Step 1 — Spontaneous polarization of AlGaN (linear interpolation):**

$$P_{sp}(\text{AlGaN}) = x \cdot P_{sp}(\text{AlN}) + (1-x) \cdot P_{sp}(\text{GaN})$$
$$P_{sp}(\text{AlGaN}) = 0.25(-0.090) + 0.75(-0.034) = -0.0225 - 0.0255 = -0.048 \text{ C/m}^2$$

**Step 2 — Piezoelectric polarization (AlGaN is under tensile strain on GaN):**

$$P_{pz} = 2 \cdot \frac{a_{GaN} - a_{AlGaN}}{a_{AlGaN}} \cdot \left(e_{31} - \frac{c_{13}}{c_{33}} e_{33}\right)$$

Compute strain:

$$a_{AlGaN} = 3.189 - 0.077(0.25) = 3.1698 \text{ Å}$$

$$\varepsilon = \frac{3.189 - 3.1698}{3.1698} = 0.00606$$

For AlGaN: $e_{31} - (c_{13}/c_{33})e_{33} \approx -0.0866 \text{ C/m}^2$

$$P_{pz} = 2(0.00606)(-0.0866) = -0.00105 \text{ C/m}^2$$

**Step 3 — Total polarization of AlGaN:**

$$P_{total}(AlGaN) = -0.048 + (-0.00105) = -0.0491 \text{ C/m}^2$$

**Step 4 — Polarization discontinuity at AlGaN/GaN interface (GaN is unstrained):**

$$\sigma_{pz} = P_{total}(GaN) - P_{total}(AlGaN)$$

$$\sigma_{pz} = (-0.034) - (-0.0491) = +0.0151 \text{ C/m}^2$$

**Step 5 — Convert to 2D electron density:**

$$n_s = \frac{\sigma_{pz}}{q} = \frac{0.0151}{1.6 \times 10^{-19}} = 9.4 \times 10^{16} \text{ m}^{-2} = \mathbf{9.4 \times 10^{12} \text{ cm}^{-2}}$$

> [!NOTE]
> This enormous 2DEG density (orders of magnitude higher than in AlGaAs/GaAs HEMTs) is what gives GaN HEMTs their exceptional current handling capability. It arises *purely from polarization* — **no intentional doping required**.

---

### 3.4 Derivation: Indium Content for Green Emission in InGaN

**Target:** $E_g = 2.33$ eV (pure green, 532 nm). Solve for $x$ in $\text{In}_x\text{Ga}_{1-x}\text{N}$.

**Equation:**

$$E_g(x) = 0.7x + 3.4(1-x) - 1.4x(1-x) = 2.33$$

Expand:

$$0.7x + 3.4 - 3.4x - 1.4x + 1.4x^2 = 2.33$$

$$1.4x^2 + (0.7 - 3.4 - 1.4)x + (3.4 - 2.33) = 0$$

$$1.4x^2 - 4.1x + 1.07 = 0$$

**Quadratic formula:**

$$x = \frac{4.1 \pm \sqrt{16.81 - 5.992}}{2.8} = \frac{4.1 \pm \sqrt{10.818}}{2.8} = \frac{4.1 \pm 3.289}{2.8}$$

Two roots: $x = 2.640$ (unphysical, $> 1$) and $x = 0.290$.

**Result:** $\text{In}_{0.29}\text{Ga}_{0.71}\text{N}$ emits at green ($\sim 532$ nm).

> [!WARNING]
> In content $> 25\%$ causes **phase separation** (compositional inhomogeneity) and very high defect densities due to the InN/GaN lattice mismatch. This is called the **"Green Gap"** in solid-state lighting — high-efficiency green LEDs are notoriously hard to make.

### PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model]**
**Define the term "bandgap bowing" with reference to ternary III-nitride alloys. Mention the typical bowing parameter values for AlGaN and InGaN.**

**Model Answer:**

> Bandgap bowing refers to the **non-linear (parabolic) deviation** of the bandgap of a ternary alloy $\text{A}_x\text{B}_{1-x}\text{N}$ from a simple linear interpolation between the two parent binaries. It is quantified by the bowing parameter $b$ in the equation:
>
> $$E_g(x) = x E_g(AN) + (1-x) E_g(BN) - b \cdot x(1-x)$$
>
> The bowing arises from bond-length disorder, atomic size mismatch, charge exchange, and local strain effects within the alloy.
>
> **Typical values:**
> - $\text{AlGaN: } b \approx 1.0$ eV
> - $\text{InGaN: } b \approx 1.4$ eV
>
> The larger bowing in InGaN reflects the greater chemical/structural difference between In–N and Ga–N bonds.

**[3 marks: 1 for definition, 1 for the formula, 1 for values]**

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – June 2024 Model]**
**State Vegard's Law. Calculate the lattice constant of $\text{In}_{0.2}\text{Ga}_{0.8}\text{N}$. Given: $a(\text{GaN}) = 3.189$ Å, $a(\text{InN}) = 3.538$ Å.**

**Model Answer:**

> Vegard's Law states that the lattice constant of a ternary alloy varies linearly with composition:
>
> $$a(\text{A}_x\text{B}_{1-x}\text{N}) = x \cdot a(\text{AN}) + (1-x) \cdot a(\text{BN})$$
>
> **Calculation for $\text{In}_{0.2}\text{Ga}_{0.8}\text{N}$:**
>
> $$a = (0.2)(3.538) + (0.8)(3.189)$$
>
> $$a = 0.7076 + 2.5512 = \mathbf{3.2588 \text{ Å}}$$
>
> The mismatch with GaN is:
>
> $$\frac{3.2588 - 3.189}{3.189} \times 100\% = \mathbf{2.19\%}$$
>
> confirming that InGaN on GaN is under **compressive strain**.

**[3 marks: 1 for law statement, 1 for calculation, 1 for mismatch interpretation]**
