### PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks) — Set 1**

**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 / June 2024 Model]**

**(a)** With necessary energy band diagrams, explain the **concept of bandgap engineering** in ternary III-nitride alloys. Discuss the bandgap range covered by AlGaN and InGaN systems. Derive the bandgap of $\text{Al}_{0.4}\text{Ga}_{0.6}\text{N}$ and $\text{In}_{0.15}\text{Ga}_{0.85}\text{N}$ using the bowing equation. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Discuss the **crystal structure and polarization properties** of wurtzite III-nitrides. Explain how spontaneous and piezoelectric polarization in AlGaN/GaN heterostructure leads to the formation of 2-Dimensional Electron Gas (2DEG) at the interface. **(7 marks)**

---

#### **Model Solution for Question A(a) — Bandgap Engineering**

**Step 1 — Concept of Bandgap Engineering:**

Bandgap engineering is the deliberate tuning of a semiconductor's bandgap by varying its chemical composition. In the III-nitride system, alloying AlN, GaN, and InN allows continuous bandgap tuning from **0.7 eV (InN) to 6.2 eV (AlN)** — covering the entire electromagnetic spectrum from infrared to deep-UV.

> **Key point:** Both AlGaN and InGaN crystallize in the same wurtzite structure as their parent binaries, allowing continuous solid solubility across the full composition range.

**Step 2 — Bandgap Ranges:**

| Alloy | Min Eg (eV) | Max Eg (eV) | Spectral Range |
|-------|-------------|-------------|----------------|
| AlGaN | 3.4 (at x=0) | 6.2 (at x=1) | Visible to Deep-UV |
| InGaN | 0.7 (at x=1) | 3.4 (at x=0) | Near-IR to Near-UV |

**Step 3 — Derivation of $E_g(\text{Al}_{0.4}\text{Ga}_{0.6}\text{N})$:**

$$E_g = x \cdot E_g(\text{AlN}) + (1-x) \cdot E_g(\text{GaN}) - b \cdot x(1-x)$$

$$E_g = (0.4)(6.2) + (0.6)(3.4) - (1.0)(0.4)(0.6)$$

$$E_g = 2.480 + 2.040 - 0.240 = \mathbf{4.28 \text{ eV}}$$

Corresponding emission wavelength: $\lambda = 1240 / 4.28 = 289.7$ nm (UV-C region).

**Step 4 — Derivation of $E_g(\text{In}_{0.15}\text{Ga}_{0.85}\text{N})$:**

$$E_g = (0.15)(0.7) + (0.85)(3.4) - (1.4)(0.15)(0.85)$$

$$E_g = 0.105 + 2.890 - 0.1785 = \mathbf{2.82 \text{ eV}}$$

Corresponding emission wavelength: $\lambda = 1240 / 2.82 = 439.7$ nm (violet/blue region).

**[7 marks: 1 for concept + diagram, 1 for range table, 2 for AlGaN derivation, 2 for InGaN derivation, 1 for wavelength interpretation]**

---

#### **Model Solution for Question A(b) — Polarization and 2DEG**

**Step 1 — Crystal Structure of Wurtzite III-Nitrides:**

The wurtzite structure has a **hexagonal unit cell** (space group $P6_3mc$) with two interpenetrating hexagonal close-packed sublattices (one for the metal atom, one for N). The structure lacks a center of inversion symmetry, which is the origin of the spontaneous polarization.

**Step 2 — Spontaneous Polarization ($P_{sp}$):**

Even in the absence of strain, the wurtzite nitrides exhibit spontaneous polarization along the $c$-axis due to the asymmetric charge distribution in the metal–N bond. The values are:

- $P_{sp}(\text{GaN}) = -0.034$ C/m²
- $P_{sp}(\text{AlN}) = -0.090$ C/m²
- $P_{sp}(\text{InN}) = -0.042$ C/m²

**Step 3 — Piezoelectric Polarization ($P_{pz}$):**

When a thin AlGaN layer is grown on a GaN substrate, the lattice mismatch (AlGaN has a smaller $a$ than GaN) places the AlGaN layer under **in-plane biaxial tensile strain**. This strain generates a piezoelectric polarization:

$$P_{pz} = 2 \cdot \frac{a_{GaN} - a_{AlGaN}}{a_{AlGaN}} \cdot \left(e_{31} - \frac{c_{13}}{c_{33}} e_{33}\right)$$

**Step 4 — 2DEG Formation at the Heterointerface:**

The total polarization of the strained AlGaN is **more negative** than the unstrained GaN. This polarization discontinuity at the heterointerface produces a bound sheet charge of positive sign at the AlGaN-side of the interface:

$$\sigma = P_{total}(\text{GaN}) - P_{total}(\text{AlGaN}) \approx +0.01 \text{ to } +0.03 \text{ C/m}^2$$

This sheet charge attracts free electrons, forming a high-density **2DEG** at the interface with sheet densities of $\sim 10^{13}$ cm⁻² — without any intentional doping.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This polarization-induced 2DEG is the physical basis of all AlGaN/GaN **High Electron Mobility Transistors (HEMTs)**, which are the workhorse devices in 5G base stations, radar systems, and electric vehicle power converters.

**[7 marks: 1 for crystal structure, 1 for $P_{sp}$, 1 for $P_{pz}$ formula, 2 for 2DEG mechanism, 2 for engineering significance]**

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) — Set 2 (Alternative Choice)**

**(a)** What are **quaternary III-nitride alloys**? Discuss the advantages of InAlGaN over AlGaN and InGaN for deep-UV optoelectronic devices. Derive the lattice-matching condition on GaN. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** With a neat sketch, explain the **structure and working of an InGaN/GaN Multiple Quantum Well (MQW) LED**. Discuss the role of the InGaN well, GaN barrier, and the polarization-induced Quantum-Confined Stark Effect (QCSE). **(7 marks)**

---

#### **Model Solution for Question B(a) — Quaternary InAlGaN Alloys**

**Step 1 — Definition:**

Quaternary III-nitride alloys are compounds formed by mixing **all three** binary nitrides — AlN, GaN, and InN — into a single phase with composition $\text{In}_x\text{Al}_y\text{Ga}_{1-x-y}\text{N}$. They have **two independent compositional parameters** ($x$ and $y$), giving a 2-D design space.

**Step 2 — Advantages over Ternary Alloys:**

| Limitation in Ternary | How InAlGaN Solves It |
|------------------------|------------------------|
| AlGaN on GaN → tensile strain, cracking above x = 0.3 | In content compensates lattice mismatch |
| InGaN on GaN → compressive strain, phase separation above x = 0.25 | Al stiffens the lattice, suppresses phase separation |
| Fixed (bandgap, lattice constant) trade-off | Independent tuning of Eg AND $a$ |

**Step 3 — Derivation of Lattice-Match Condition on GaN:**

Setting $a(\text{In}_x\text{Al}_y\text{Ga}_{1-x-y}\text{N}) = a(\text{GaN})$:

$$x \cdot a(\text{InN}) + y \cdot a(\text{AlN}) + (1-x-y) \cdot a(\text{GaN}) = a(\text{GaN})$$

$$x(3.538) + y(3.112) + (1-x-y)(3.189) = 3.189$$

$$3.538x + 3.112y + 3.189 - 3.189x - 3.189y = 3.189$$

$$0.349x - 0.077y = 0 \quad \Rightarrow \quad \boxed{x \approx 0.2206 \cdot y}$$

This relation allows the designer to choose any Aluminum fraction $y$ and compute the exact Indium fraction $x$ needed for lattice match.

**Step 4 — Engineering Application:**

Lattice-matched InAlGaN UV LEDs (e.g., $\text{In}_{0.13}\text{Al}_{0.58}\text{Ga}_{0.29}\text{N}$, $E_g \approx 4.5$ eV) eliminate the cracking and threading dislocations that plague high-Al AlGaN films, enabling efficient deep-UV emitters for sterilization and water purification.

**[7 marks: 1 for definition, 2 for advantages, 3 for derivation, 1 for application]**

---

#### **Model Solution for Question B(b) — InGaN/GaN MQW LED**

**Step 1 — Device Structure:**

An InGaN/GaN MQW LED consists of:
- **n-GaN layer** (cladding/contact)
- **Multiple Quantum Well (MQW) active region** — alternating thin InGaN wells (2–4 nm) and GaN barriers (5–10 nm)
- **p-GaN layer** (cladding/contact)
- Metal contacts (anode + cathode)

**Step 2 — Working Principle:**

Under forward bias, electrons from the n-GaN and holes from the p-GaN are injected into the MQW region. The InGaN wells have a **lower bandgap** than the GaN barriers, so both carriers are confined in the wells. Radiative recombination of electron-hole pairs in the well produces photons of energy $\approx E_g(\text{InGaN})$.

**Step 3 — Polarization and QCSE:**

The wurtzite structure of the well produces a strong **internal electric field** ($\sim 1$ MV/cm) due to spontaneous and piezoelectric polarization at the InGaN/GaN interfaces. This field tilts the energy bands within the well, separating electrons and holes to opposite sides of the well.

> **QCSE Effect:** This spatial separation of carriers **reduces the wavefunction overlap**, decreasing the radiative recombination rate. However, it also **localizes carriers** away from non-radiative defect centers (dislocations), which is why InGaN LEDs remain efficient even with dislocation densities of $10^8$ to $10^9$ cm⁻².

**Step 4 — Why InGaN is Unique:**

The QCSE-induced carrier localization overcomes the high defect density, making InGaN the **only direct-gap material system that emits efficiently across the entire visible spectrum** despite the lack of lattice-matched substrates.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning:** Students commonly lose marks on the following:
> 1. Forgetting to mention the **revised bandgap of InN = 0.7 eV** (not the old 1.9 eV) — this is critical for any InGaN bandgap calculation since 2008.
> 2. Confusing **tensile** (AlGaN/GaN) and **compressive** (InGaN/GaN) strain directions in polarization problems.
> 3. Omitting the **bowing correction** term when computing ternary bandgaps.
> 4. Drawing the lattice-match derivation without substituting numerical values of $a(\text{InN})$, $a(\text{AlN})$, $a(\text{GaN})$.
> 5. Not explaining the **QCSE** mechanism in MQW LED discussions.

**[7 marks: 1 for structure sketch, 2 for working principle, 2 for QCSE explanation, 2 for unique InGaN property]**

---

### TOPIC RECAP & IMPORTANT THINGS TO REMEMBER (Rapid Revision Checklist)

✅ **AlGaN** — Ternary $\text{Al}_x\text{Ga}_{1-x}\text{N}$, $E_g$ from 3.4 to 6.2 eV, **tensile** strain on GaN, used for UV-C LEDs, solar-blind detectors, and HEMTs.

✅ **InGaN** — Ternary $\text{In}_x\text{Ga}_{1-x}\text{N}$, $E_g$ from 0.7 (revised) to 3.4 eV, **compressive** strain on GaN, used for blue/green LEDs, laser diodes, and full-visible-spectrum displays.

✅ **InAlGaN** — Quaternary $\text{In}_x\text{Al}_y\text{Ga}_{1-x-y}\text{N}$ with **2-D design freedom**, enables lattice-matched deep-UV and high-Al heterostructures.

✅ **Vegard's Law** applies to **lattice constants** (linear). **Bandgaps are non-linear** with composition (bowing).

✅ **Bowing parameters** (must memorize): $b_{AlGaN} \approx 1.0$ eV; $b_{InGaN} \approx 1.4$ eV; $b_{InAlN} \approx 3.0$ eV.

✅ **Lattice-match on GaN:** $x = 0.2206 \cdot y$ for InAlGaN (ratio of In to Al needed).

✅ **Polarization in nitrides is huge** — produces sheet charges of $\sim 10^{13}$ cm⁻², generating the **2DEG** in HEMTs.

✅ **AlGaN/GaN = tensile strain**; **InGaN/GaN = compressive strain**; **InAlGaN can be either** or strain-free.

✅ **InN bandgap is 0.7 eV** (post-2006 revision) — used for IR applications and full-spectrum quaternary design.

✅ **Wavelength conversion:** $\lambda (\text{nm}) = 1240 / E_g(\text{eV})$ — memorize and use liberally in problems.

✅ **Green Gap problem:** InGaN with In > 0.25 suffers phase separation, making high-efficiency green LEDs hard.

✅ **QCSE in MQWs:** Internal polarization field separates carriers in InGaN wells, reducing overlap but localizing them away from defects.

✅ **Wurtzite structure** is non-centrosymmetric → responsible for all polarization phenomena in these alloys.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Polarization Effects in Nitrides: Spontaneous Polarization, Piezoelectric Polarization
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PEEVT413 - COMPOUND SEMICONDUCTORS > - **Module:** Module 1 > - **Topic:** Polarization Effects in Nitrides: Spontaneous Polarization, Piezoelectric Polarization ## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition **Polarization effects in III-nitride semiconductors** refer to the macroscopic electric dipole moments that arise intrinsically within the wurtzite crystal lattice of compounds such as Gallium Nitride (GaN), Aluminum Nitride (AlN), and Indium Nitride (InN). These polarization effects manifest in two primary forms: 1. **Spontaneous Polarization (P_sp)**: A built-in polarization that exists in the unstrained equilibrium crystal due to the non-centrosymmetric nature of the wurtzite structure, even in the absence of any external electric field or mechanical strain. 2. **Piezoelectric Polarization (P_pz)**: An additional polarization induced when the crystal lattice is subjected to mechanical strain (typically biaxial strain arising from lattice-mismatch heteroepitaxy). > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight**: The total macroscopic polarization in a strained nitride layer is the vector sum of the spontaneous and piezoelectric components: **P_total = P_sp + P_pz**. Discontinuities in this polarization across heterointerfaces generate fixed interface charge densities, which critically modulate the 2-Dimensional Electron Gas (2DEG) density in AlGaN/GaN HEMTs. ### 1.2 Intuitive Analogy Imagine a stack of Lego blocks where each block is slightly **wedge-shaped** (asymmetric). If you stack these wedges one on top of another, even without pushing them, they naturally lean — creating an internal "leaning tendency" (this is **spontaneous polarization**). Now, if you try to glue a thicker wedge-shaped block on top of a thinner one, the mismatch forces it to stretch or compress — this stretching adds an extra "stress-tilt" (**piezoelectric polarization**). The total tilt of the stack determines the "slippery surface charge" (2DEG) that collects at the interface, which is exactly what makes GaN HEMTs work without any doping! ### 1.3 Physical Constants & Material Parameters (Reference Values) > [!NOTE] > Standard spontaneous polarization values at 300 K (in C/m²): > - **P_sp(AlN) ≈ −0.081 C/m²** > - **P_sp(GaN) ≈ −0.029 C/m²** > - **P_sp(InN) ≈ −0.032 C/m²** > > Piezoelectric constants for GaN: **e₃₃ ≈ 0.73 C/m²**, **e₃₁ ≈ −0.49 C/m²**, **e₁₅ ≈ −0.40 C/m²**. > Lattice constants: a(GaN) = 3.189 Å, c(GaN) = 5.185 Å. ## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ### 2.1 Origin of Spontaneous Polarization in Wurtzite Nitrides The wurtzite crystal structure (hexagonal, space group P6₃mc) lacks inversion symmetry along the **c-axis**. The cation–anion bilayer separation is not symmetric: the metal atom (Ga, Al, In) does not sit exactly midway between two nitrogen atoms. This intrinsic geometric asymmetry produces a permanent dipole moment per unit cell, which sums to a macroscopic spontaneous polarization. **Step-by-step logic** of why P_sp exists: - Wurtzite structure has **no center of symmetry** along the c-axis. - The charge distribution along the [0001] direction is **asymmetric**. - Hence, the unit cell possesses a **non-zero dipole moment** even at zero strain. - Summing dipole moments over the entire crystal yields **P_sp ≠ 0**. > [!NOTE] > Wurtzite GaN and ZnO are the classic examples; cubic (zinc-blende) GaN does not exhibit spontaneous polarization along equivalent directions, but nitrides almost always crystallize in the **wurtzite phase** under standard growth conditions. ### 2.2 Origin of Piezoelectric Polarization When a nitride film is grown pseudomorphically on a substrate with a different in-plane lattice constant `a_s`, the film experiences **in-plane biaxial strain** ε: $$\varepsilon = \frac{a_s - a_f}{a_f}$$ where `a_f` is the unstrained film lattice constant. The strain components are: - In-plane: ε_xx = ε_yy = ε - Out-of-plane: ε_zz = −2(C₁₃/C₃₃)·ε (from Poisson's relation) This strain distorts the unit cell, shifting the cation–anion charge centers further apart, generating **piezoelectric polarization** along the c-axis: $$P_{pz} = e_{33}\,\varepsilon_{zz} + e_{31}\,(\varepsilon_{xx} + \varepsilon_{yy})$$ ### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet | **Quantity** | **Symbol / Formula** | **Units** | **Physical Meaning** | |---|---|---|---| | Lattice-mismatch strain | $\varepsilon = (a_s - a_f)/a_f$ | dimensionless | In-plane biaxial strain | | Out-of-plane strain | $\varepsilon_{zz} = -2(C_{13}/C_{33})\,\varepsilon$ | dimensionless | Poisson's response | | Spontaneous polarization | $P_{sp}$ (material constant) | C/m² | Built-in dipole density | | Piezoelectric polarization | $P_{pz} = 2(e_{31} - e_{33}\,C_{13}/C_{33})\,\varepsilon$ | C/m² | Strain-induced dipole | | Total polarization | $P_{tot} = P_{sp} + P_{pz}$ | C/m² | Net polarization vector | | Interface sheet charge | $\sigma = \Delta P_{tot} = P_{tot,top} - P_{tot,bot}$ | C/m² | Bound polarization charge | | 2DEG sheet density | $n_s = \sigma / e$ | cm⁻² | Mobile carrier density | | Critical thickness | $h_c$ (Matthews–Blakeslee) | nm | Strain relaxation onset | > [!IMPORTANT] > For AlGaN/GaN: `a_s = a_GaN` (substrate), `a_f = a_AlGaN`. Since a_AlGaN < a_GaN, ε is **negative** (compressive in-plane strain), and ε_zz is **positive** (tensile out-of-plane). The combination of large negative e₃₁ and positive ε_zz gives a substantial **negative P_pz** (pointing from Ga-face to N-face), which ADDS to the negative P_sp, producing a huge total polarization discontinuity at the AlGaN/GaN heterointerface. ### 2.4 Engineering Utility Polarization-induced charges are the **fundamental operating principle** of: - **AlGaN/GaN HEMTs** (High Electron Mobility Transistors) — 2DEG densities of 1–3 × 10¹³ cm⁻² achieved without any intentional doping. - **AlGaN/GaN UV photodetectors** — built-in fields enable self-powered operation. - **GaN-based LEDs and Laser Diodes** — polarization fields cause the Quantum-Confined Stark Effect (QCSE), shifting emission wavelength. - **AlN/GaN SAW/BAW devices** — piezoelectric coupling for RF filters. ## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ### 3.1 Derivation of the Piezoelectric Polarization for Biaxial Strain **Starting point**: For a wurtzite crystal with the c-axis as the principal axis, the piezoelectric polarization components in the linear regime are: $$ \begin{aligned} P_{pz,z} &= e_{33}\,\varepsilon_{zz} + e_{31}\,(\varepsilon_{xx} + \varepsilon_{yy}) \\ P_{pz,x} &= e_{15}\,\varepsilon_{xz} \\ P_{pz,y} &= e_{15}\,\varepsilon_{yz} \end{aligned} $$ **Step 1 — Apply biaxial symmetry**: For a film grown on a (0001) plane with isotropic in-plane strain (no shear), we have ε_xx = ε_yy = ε, ε_zz to be determined, and all off-diagonal strains vanish. $$ \begin{aligned} P_{pz,z} &= e_{33}\,\varepsilon_{zz} + 2e_{31}\,\varepsilon \end{aligned} $$ **Step 2 — Apply elastic stress-strain relation (Hooke's law for hexagonal symmetry)**: The out-of-plane strain is constrained by the in-plane biaxial stress σ_xx = σ_yy = σ, σ_zz = 0 (free surface). For a hexagonal crystal: $$ \begin{aligned} \sigma_{xx} &= C_{11}\,\varepsilon_{xx} + C_{12}\,\varepsilon_{yy} + C_{13}\,\varepsilon_{zz} = 0 \\ \sigma_{zz} &= 2C_{13}\,\varepsilon + C_{33}\,\varepsilon_{zz} = 0 \end{aligned} $$ **Step 3 — Solve for ε_zz**: $$ \begin{aligned} \varepsilon_{zz} &= -\frac{2C_{13}}{C_{33}}\,\varepsilon \end{aligned} $$ **Step 4 — Substitute back into the piezoelectric polarization expression**: $$ \begin{aligned} P_{pz} &= e_{33}\left(-\frac{2C_{13}}{C_{33}}\,\varepsilon\right) + 2e_{31}\,\varepsilon \\ P_{pz} &= \left(2e_{31} - \frac{2C_{13}\,e_{33}}{C_{33}}\right)\varepsilon \\ P_{pz} &= 2\left(e_{31} - \frac{C_{13}}{C_{33}}\,e_{33}\right)\varepsilon \end{aligned} $$ This is the **canonical KTU form** for piezoelectric polarization under biaxial strain. The quantity in parentheses is often called the effective piezoelectric coefficient. ### 3.2 Derivation of Interface Sheet Charge Density Consider an AlGaN layer of thickness `d` grown on a thick, fully relaxed GaN substrate along the c-axis. The bound polarization charge per unit area at the heterointerface is the discontinuity in total polarization: $$ \begin{aligned} \sigma &= P_{tot,GaN} - P_{tot,AlGaN} \\ \sigma &= \left[P_{sp}^{GaN} + P_{pz}^{GaN}\right] - \left[P_{sp}^{AlGaN} + P_{pz}^{AlGaN}\right] \end{aligned} $$ **Step 1 — Assume the GaN substrate is fully relaxed**: P_pz^GaN = 0, so P_tot^GaN = P_sp^GaN. **Step 2 — For the strained AlGaN layer**: P_tot^AlGaN = P_sp^AlGaN + P_pz^AlGaN. $$ \begin{aligned} \sigma &= P_{sp}^{GaN} - P_{sp}^{AlGaN} - P_{pz}^{AlGaN} \end{aligned} $$ **Step 3 — Express P_pz^AlGaN in terms of mismatch strain**: With a_s = a_GaN and a_f = a_AlGaN: $$ \begin{aligned} \varepsilon &= \frac{a_{GaN} - a_{AlGaN}}{a_{AlGaN}} > 0 \quad \text{(tensile in-plane)} \end{aligned} $$ Wait — note that for AlGaN, a_AlGaN < a_GaN, so ε > 0 means in-plane **tensile** strain (AlGaN is stretched to match GaN). However, standard convention in KTU uses the substrate as reference: here, since AlGaN is grown ON GaN, it is in **tensile** in-plane strain. With e₃₃ > 0 and e₃₁ < 0, we get P_pz negative, adding to negative P_sp. **Step 4 — Compute the 2DEG sheet density** (assuming all polarization charge is balanced by mobile electrons in the quantum well at the interface): $$ \begin{aligned} n_s &= \frac{\sigma}{e} = \frac{\vert P_{tot,GaN} - P_{tot,AlGaN} \vert}{e} \end{aligned} $$ ### 3.3 Worked Numerical Example (GaN HEMT) **Problem**: An Al₀.₂₅Ga₀.₇₅N layer (thickness = 20 nm) is grown pseudomorphically on a relaxed GaN substrate. Compute the 2DEG density assuming full compensation. **Given data** (linear interpolation for Al₀.₂₅Ga₀.₇₅N, Vegard's law): - a(AlGaN) = 0.25 × 3.112 + 0.75 × 3.189 = 3.170 Å - P_sp(AlGaN) = 0.25 × (−0.081) + 0.75 × (−0.029) = −0.042 C/m² - e₃₃(AlGaN) = 0.25 × 1.55 + 0.75 × 0.73 = 0.935 C/m² - e₃₁(AlGaN) = 0.25 × (−0.60) + 0.75 × (−0.49) = −0.518 C/m² - C₁₃(AlGaN) ≈ 100 GPa, C₃₃(AlGaN) ≈ 380 GPa **Step 1 — Mismatch strain**: $$ \begin{aligned} \varepsilon &= \frac{3.189 - 3.170}{3.170} = \frac{0.019}{3.170} = 5.99 \times 10^{-3} \end{aligned} $$ **Step 2 — Piezoelectric polarization**: $$ \begin{aligned} P_{pz} &= 2\left(e_{31} - \frac{C_{13}}{C_{33}}e_{33}\right)\varepsilon \\ P_{pz} &= 2\left(-0.518 - \frac{100}{380}(0.935)\right)(5.99 \times 10^{-3}) \\ P_{pz} &= 2(-0.518 - 0.246)(5.99 \times 10^{-3}) \\ P_{pz} &= 2(-0.764)(5.99 \times 10^{-3}) \\ P_{pz} &= -9.15 \times 10^{-3}\,\text{C/m}^2 \end{aligned} $$ **Step 3 — Total polarization of AlGaN**: $$ \begin{aligned} P_{tot}^{AlGaN} &= -0.042 + (-9.15 \times 10^{-3}) = -0.0512\,\text{C/m}^2 \end{aligned} $$ **Step 4 — Polarization discontinuity at interface**: $$ \begin{aligned} \sigma &= P_{sp}^{GaN} - P_{tot}^{AlGaN} = -0.029 - (-0.0512) = 0.0222\,\text{C/m}^2 \end{aligned} $$ **Step 5 — 2DEG sheet density**: $$ \begin{aligned} n_s &= \frac{\sigma}{e} = \frac{0.0222}{1.602 \times 10^{-19}} = 1.39 \times 10^{17}\,\text{m}^{-2} = 1.39 \times 10^{13}\,\text{cm}^{-2} \end{aligned} $$ This matches experimental values (~1 × 10¹³ cm⁻²) for Al₀.₂₅Ga₀.₇₅N/GaN HEMTs. ✓ ## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ### 4.1 Mermaid Diagram — Polarization Effects Architecture in AlGaN/GaN HEMT ```mermaid flowchart TB A0["Wurtzite Nitride Crystal
Non Centrosymmetric c axis"] --> A1["Two Polarization Sources"] A1 --> B1["Spontaneous Polarization P sp
Built in dipole from asymmetric unit cell"] A1 --> B2["Piezoelectric Polarization P pz
Strain induced dipole from lattice mismatch"] B1 --> C1["P sp AlN 0.081 C per m2
P sp GaN 0.029 C per m2
P sp InN 0.032 C per m2"] B2 --> C2["P pz equals 2 times e31 minus C13 by C33 times e33
times epsilon"] C1 --> D1["Vector Sum P total"] C2 --> D1 D1 --> E1["Heterointerface Polarization Discontinuity"] E1 --> E2["Interface Sheet Charge sigma equals delta P total"] E2 --> E3["2DEG Formation
No intentional doping required"] E3 --> F1["AlGaN on GaN HEMT Channel
n s around 1 to 3 times 10 to the 13 per cm2"] E3 --> F2["Ga face versus N face Polarity"] E3 --> F3["UV Photodetectors and LEDs"] F1 --> G1["RF Power Electronics 5G Base Stations"] F2 --> G1 F3 --> G1 ``` ### 4.2 Mermaid Diagram — Block-Level Functional Architecture of Polarization-Induced 2DEG ```mermaid flowchart LR S1["Substrate
SiC or Sapphire or Si"] --> S2["Nucleation Layer
Low temperature AlN or GaN"] S2 --> S3["GaN Buffer
Fully relaxed thick layer"] S3 --> S4["AlGaN Barrier
Pseudomorphically strained"] S4 --> S5["Interface
Ga face AlGaN on GaN"] S5 --> S6["Polarization Dipole
P sp plus P pz"] S6 --> S7["Sheet Charge Sigma
Positive bound charge"] S7 --> S8["2DEG Channel
Mobile electrons accumulate"] S8 --> S9["Source Drain Contacts
Ohmic"] S8 --> S10["Gate Contact
Schottky"] S9 --> S11["HEMT Output Characteristics
High electron mobility"] S10 --> S11 ``` ### 4.3 Mermaid Diagram — Crystal Structure & Polarity (Ga-face vs N-face) ```mermaid flowchart TB P1["Wurtzite Unit Cell"] --> P2{"Polarity Orientation"} P2 --> P3["Ga face 0001
Ga atom on top surface"] P2 --> P4["N face 0001bar
N atom on top surface"] P3 --> P5["P sp points downward toward substrate
P pz adds constructively under tensile strain"] P4 --> P6["P sp points upward away from substrate
Polarization charge sign reversed"] P5 --> Q1["Standard HEMT Configuration
2DEG at AlGaN on GaN interface"] P6 --> Q2["Inverted HEMT Structure
2DHG at GaN on AlGaN interface"] ``` ## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each) --- **Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, PEEVT413]** Define spontaneous polarization. Why is it non-zero in wurtzite GaN but negligible in zinc-blende GaN? **Model Answer (3 marks):** **Spontaneous Polarization (P_sp)** is the built-in macroscopic electric polarization present in an unstrained crystal due to the asymmetric charge distribution within its unit cell. In **wurtzite GaN** (hexagonal, space group P6₃mc), the crystal lacks inversion symmetry along the c-axis. The Ga and N sub-lattices are not mirror images of each other along [0001], producing a non-zero dipole moment per unit cell that sums to a macroscopic P_sp (≈ −0.029 C/m²). In **zinc-blende GaN** (cubic, space group F4̄3m), the crystal possesses inversion symmetry along equivalent ⟨111⟩ directions, causing the dipole moments to cancel. Hence P_sp ≈ 0. > **Marking distribution**: Definition (1) + wurtzite asymmetry (1) + zinc-blende cancellation (1). --- **Q2. [KTU University Exam – June 2024, PEEVT413]** Distinguish between spontaneous and piezoelectric polarization in III-nitrides. **Model Answer (3 marks):** | Feature | Spontaneous (P_sp) | Piezoelectric (P_pz) | |---|---|---| | Origin | Intrinsic lattice asymmetry | Strain-induced lattice distortion | | Strain dependence | Independent of strain | Linearly proportional to strain | | Magnitude in GaN | ≈ −0.029 C/m² | ≈ −0.01 C/m² (typical) | | Exists in unstrained crystal? | Yes | No | | Key constants | Material-specific (P_sp) | e₃₃, e₃₁, C₁₃, C₃₃ | The **total polarization** is **P_total = P_sp + P_pz**. (1 mark for tabular distinction + 1 mark for formula + 1 mark for clarity.) --- ### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice) --- #### **Question A (14 Marks)** **[KTU University Exam – Model Paper, PEEVT413]** (a) **Derive the expression for piezoelectric polarization P_pz in a biaxially strained wurtzite nitride film along the c-axis.** State clearly the role of the elastic constants C₁₃ and C₃₃. (7 marks) (b) An Al₀.₃Ga₀.₇N barrier of 25 nm thickness is grown pseudomorphically on a relaxed GaN substrate. Given the material parameters below, **calculate the 2DEG sheet carrier density** at the AlGaN/GaN heterointerface. (7 marks) **Material Parameters:** - a(GaN) = 3.189 Å, a(AlN) = 3.112 Å - P_sp(GaN) = −0.029 C/m², P_sp(AlN) = −0.081 C/m² - e₃₃(AlGaN) ≈ 0.97 C/m², e₃₁(AlGaN) ≈ −0.53 C/m² - C₁₃(AlGaN) ≈ 105 GPa, C₃₃(AlGaN) ≈ 395 GPa - e = 1.602 × 10⁻¹⁹ C **Model Solution:** **(a) Derivation (7 marks):** Starting with the linear piezoelectric constitutive relation for the c-axis component: $$P_{pz} = e_{33}\,\varepsilon_{zz} + e_{31}\,(\varepsilon_{xx} + \varepsilon_{yy})$$ For biaxial in-plane strain ε_xx = ε_yy = ε (with ε_xz = ε_yz = 0): $$P_{pz} = e_{33}\,\varepsilon_{zz} + 2e_{31}\,\varepsilon$$ Applying Hooke's law with σ_zz = 0 (free surface): $$\sigma_{zz} = 2C_{13}\,\varepsilon + C_{33}\,\varepsilon_{zz} = 0 \implies \varepsilon_{zz} = -\frac{2C_{13}}{C_{33}}\,\varepsilon$$ Substituting: $$P_{pz} = 2\left(e_{31} - \frac{C_{13}}{C_{33}}\,e_{33}\right)\varepsilon$$ **Role of C₁₃ and C₃₃:** They govern the Poisson contraction along the c-axis in response to in-plane biaxial strain. C₁₃ couples the in-plane and out-of-plane strain components, while C₃₃ is the stiffness along the c-axis. Their ratio (C₁₃/C₃₃) sets the magnitude of ε_zz, which directly scales P_pz. **(b) Numerical Calculation (7 marks):** **Step 1 — Vegard's law for Al₀.₃Ga₀.₇N lattice constant:** $$a_{AlGaN} = 0.3 \times 3.112 + 0.7 \times 3.189 = 0.9336 + 2.2323 = 3.166\,\text{Å}$$ **Step 2 — Mismatch strain (AlGaN grown on GaN, a_s = a_GaN):** $$\varepsilon = \frac{3.189 - 3.166}{3.166} = 7.26 \times 10^{-3}$$ **Step 3 — Piezoelectric polarization:** $$ \begin{aligned} P_{pz} &= 2\left(-0.53 - \frac{105}{395}(0.97)\right)(7.26 \times 10^{-3}) \\ P_{pz} &= 2(-0.53 - 0.258)(7.26 \times 10^{-3}) \\ P_{pz} &= 2(-0.788)(7.26 \times 10^{-3}) \\ P_{pz} &= -1.144 \times 10^{-2}\,\text{C/m}^2 \end{aligned} $$ **Step 4 — Spontaneous polarization of AlGaN (Vegard):** $$P_{sp}^{AlGaN} = 0.3(-0.081) + 0.7(-0.029) = -0.0446\,\text{C/m}^2$$ **Step 5 — Total polarization of AlGaN:** $$P_{tot}^{AlGaN} = -0.0446 + (-0.01144) = -0.0560\,\text{C/m}^2$$ **Step 6 — Interface sheet charge:** $$\sigma = P_{sp}^{GaN} - P_{tot}^{AlGaN} = -0.029 - (-0.0560) = 0.0270\,\text{C/m}^2$$ **Step 7 — 2DEG sheet density:** $$n_s = \frac{\sigma}{e} = \frac{0.0270}{1.602 \times 10^{-19}} = 1.69 \times 10^{13}\,\text{cm}^{-2}$$ **Final Answer:** n_s ≈ **1.69 × 10¹³ cm⁻²** (highly consistent with experimental reports for Al₀.₃Ga₀.₇N/GaN HEMTs). --- #### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice** **[KTU University Exam – Supplementary, PEEVT413]** (a) **Explain the origin of spontaneous polarization in wurtzite III-nitrides. Discuss how this polarization, combined with piezoelectric polarization, gives rise to a 2DEG in AlGaN/GaN heterostructures without intentional doping.** (7 marks) (b) **A GaN film is grown on AlN substrate. Calculate the in-plane biaxial strain and the resulting piezoelectric polarization.** Given: a(GaN) = 3.189 Å, a(AlN) = 3.112 Å, e₃₃(GaN) = 0.73 C/m², e₃₁(GaN) = −0.49 C/m², C₁₃ = 106 GPa, C₃₃ = 398 GPa. (7 marks) **Model Solution:** **(a) Conceptual Explanation (7 marks):** Wurtzite III-nitrides (AlN, GaN, InN) adopt a hexagonal structure (space group P6₃mc) lacking inversion symmetry along the c-axis. The cation–anion bond along [0001] is not symmetric — the cation (Ga/Al/In) and anion (N) planes are not equidistant, producing a permanent electric dipole per unit cell. Macroscopically, this manifests as **spontaneous polarization (P_sp)** directed along the c-axis. When a ternary AlGaN alloy is grown pseudomorphically on relaxed GaN, the lattice mismatch (a_AlGaN < a_GaN) induces **tensile in-plane strain** in the AlGaN layer. This strain distorts the unit cell, producing an additional **piezoelectric polarization (P_pz)** that points in the same direction as P_sp (for Ga-face polarity). The discontinuity in P_total across the AlGaN/GaN heterointerface generates a **positive bound sheet charge** σ = (P_sp^GaN − P_sp^AlGaN − P_pz^AlGaN). To maintain charge neutrality, mobile electrons accumulate at the interface, forming a high-density **2DEG** (~1 × 10¹³ cm⁻²). This is the foundation of undoped AlGaN/GaN HEMTs — a key advantage over GaAs-based devices that require intentional doping. **(b) Numerical Calculation (7 marks):** **Step 1 — Lattice mismatch strain** (GaN film on AlN substrate, a_s = a_AlN = 3.112 Å, a_f = a_GaN = 3.189 Å): $$\varepsilon = \frac{a_s - a_f}{a_f} = \frac{3.112 - 3.189}{3.189} = -2.41 \times 10^{-2}$$ (Negative sign indicates **compressive in-plane strain** for the GaN film.) **Step 2 — Piezoelectric polarization:** $$ \begin{aligned} P_{pz} &= 2\left(e_{31} - \frac{C_{13}}{C_{33}}\,e_{33}\right)\varepsilon \\ P_{pz} &= 2\left(-0.49 - \frac{106}{398}(0.73)\right)(-0.0241) \\ P_{pz} &= 2(-0.49 - 0.194)(-0.0241) \\ P_{pz} &= 2(-0.684)(-0.0241) \\ P_{pz} &= +3.30 \times 10^{-2}\,\text{C/m}^2 \end{aligned} $$ **Final Answer:** ε = −0.0241 (compressive), P_pz ≈ **+3.30 × 10⁻² C/m²** (positive, opposite sign to typical AlGaN/GaN case because the film is under compression, not tension). --- > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Common Pitfalls:** > 1. **Sign confusion in P_pz**: Forgetting that e₃₁ is **negative** in GaN — students often get the sign of P_pz wrong, which propagates errors into the 2DEG calculation. > 2. **Vegard's law application**: Failing to linearly interpolate e₃₃, e₃₁, C₁₃, C₃₃, a, AND P_sp for ternary alloys like AlGaN or InGaN. Each parameter must be interpolated separately. > 3. **Strain definition**: ε = (a_s − a_f)/a_f. The substrate is the reference. Do not invert the formula. > 4. **Units mismatch**: Forgetting to convert C/m² → m⁻² by dividing by e (= 1.602 × 10⁻¹⁹ C) for n_s, and then from m⁻² to cm⁻² (multiply by 10⁻⁴). > 5. **Mixing spontaneous and piezoelectric**: P_tot is a **vector sum** (here, scalar sum along c-axis), not a multiplication. Adding them correctly with proper signs is essential. > 6. **Critical thickness**: If the AlGaN layer exceeds the Matthews–Blakeslee critical thickness (~15–30 nm for Al₀.₃Ga₀.₇N/GaN), partial strain relaxation occurs, and the strain is no longer purely pseudomorphic — students must check this. --- ### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!IMPORTANT] > **Rapid-Revision Checklist for KTU ESE:** - ✅ Wurtzite nitrides (AlN, GaN, InN) exhibit **spontaneous polarization P_sp** along the c-axis due to **non-centrosymmetric** crystal structure. - ✅ Standard values: P_sp(AlN) ≈ **−0.081 C/m²**, P_sp(GaN) ≈ **−0.029 C/m²**, P_sp(InN) ≈ **−0.032 C/m²** (sign convention: negative for Ga-face). - ✅ **Piezoelectric polarization** under biaxial strain: **P_pz = 2(e₃₁ − (C₁₃/C₃₃)e₃₃)·ε**. - ✅ Strain ε = (a_s − a_f)/a_f; tensile in-plane strain on AlGaN when grown on GaN. - ✅ **Total polarization** P_total = P_sp + P_pz is a scalar sum along c-axis. - ✅ **Interface sheet charge** σ = ΔP_total across the heterointerface; mobile electrons compensate this charge, forming the **2DEG** in HEMTs. - ✅ 2DEG density n_s = σ/e ≈ 10¹³ cm⁻² for AlGaN/GaN — **achieved without intentional doping**. - ✅ **Ga-face vs. N-face polarity** reverses the sign of polarization — critical for device design. - ✅ For ternary alloys (AlGaN, InGaN), use **Vegard's law** to interpolate all parameters: a, P_sp, e_ij, C_ij. - ✅ **Critical thickness** h_c (Matthews–Blakeslee) limits pseudomorphic growth; beyond h_c, strain relaxes via dislocations, reducing P_pz. - ✅ **Linear regime only**: P_pz formula is valid for small strain (ε ≲ 1%); use nonlinear coefficients for large strain or high Al-content. - ✅ **Knowledge of piezoelectric tensors** e₃₃, e₃₁, and **elastic stiffness** C₁₃, C₃₃ is mandatory for KTU numerical problems. - ✅ **Engineering applications**: HEMTs, UV photodetectors, LEDs (QCSE effect), SAW/BAW filters.

topics.title: Metal Semiconductor Junctions: Schottky Contacts, I-V Characteristics of Schottky Barrier Junctions, Ohmic Contacts
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEEVT413 - COMPOUND SEMICONDUCTORS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Metal Semiconductor Junctions: Schottky Contacts, I-V Characteristics of Schottky Barrier Junctions, Ohmic Contacts

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 🔷 SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 The Metal–Semiconductor (M–S) Junction — Formal Definition

A **Metal–Semiconductor (M–S) Junction** is the interface formed when a metal is brought into intimate contact with a semiconductor crystal. It is one of the **most fundamental building blocks** of virtually every modern compound-semiconductor device — HEMTs, MESFETs, Schottky diodes, solar cells, LEDs, photodetectors, and VCSELs all rely on the physics of this interface.

Depending on the relative alignment of the metal work function ($\Phi_m$) and the semiconductor electron affinity ($\chi_s$), two drastically different interface types arise:

| Junction Type | Behaviour | Dominant Carrier Transport | Energy Alignment Condition (n-type) |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Schottky Contact (Rectifying)** | Asymmetric, diode-like I–V | Thermionic Emission over a barrier | $\Phi_m > \chi_s$ (barrier forms) |
| **Ohmic Contact (Non-Rectifying)** | Linear, symmetric I–V | Free carrier flow with negligible resistance | $\Phi_m \approx \chi_s$ or **very heavy doping** (tunnelling) |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** The module explicitly demands mastery of (a) the formation of the Schottky barrier, (b) the current–voltage (I–V) characteristics described by the Thermionic Emission (TE) model, and (c) the design rules for Ohmic contacts — all of which are assessed in Part-A (3 marks) and Part-B (14 marks) questions.

## 1.2 The Work Function & Electron Affinity — Two "Energy Ladders"

Before any contact, two key vacuum-reference energy levels are defined for the **isolated** materials:

- **Metal Work Function ($\Phi_m$)** — The minimum energy required to liberate an electron from the **Fermi level** of the metal into the vacuum. Typical values: **W ≈ 4.5 eV, Au ≈ 5.1 eV, Pt ≈ 5.65 eV, Ti ≈ 4.33 eV**.
- **Semiconductor Electron Affinity ($\chi_s$)** — The energy difference between the **conduction-band minimum ($E_C$)** and the vacuum level. For GaAs $\chi_s \approx 4.07$ eV; for InP $\chi_s \approx 4.38$ eV; for Si $\chi_s \approx 4.05$ eV.

> [!NOTE]
> **Conceptual Analogy — "Two Pools of Water"**
> Imagine the metal and the semiconductor as two giant water tanks. The water level in each tank is its "Fermi level." The "lip height" of the tank represents the work function (metal) or electron affinity (semiconductor). When you connect the two tanks with a pipe, water (electrons) flows from the higher tank to the lower one **until both water levels (Fermi levels) equalize**. If the metal tank is *much higher* than the semiconductor's conduction-band edge, a **barrier dam** (Schottky barrier) forms at the pipe's mouth, and water trickles over the dam only when extra pressure (forward bias) is applied. If the levels are nearly matched, water flows freely in both directions — this is an **Ohmic contact**.

## 1.3 The Schottky Contact — Rectifying Junction

A **Schottky contact** is a metal–semiconductor junction in which a **depletion region** and a **potential barrier** form on the semiconductor side, leading to strongly **rectifying** (diode-like) current–voltage behaviour.

The barrier height seen by electrons in the semiconductor conduction band attempting to flow into the metal is called the **Schottky Barrier Height (SBH)**, denoted $\Phi_{Bn}$ (for n-type). According to the **Schottky–Mott Rule**:

$$\Phi_{Bn}^{0} = \Phi_m - \chi_s$$

This is the **ideal** barrier height (suffix "0" denotes absence of interface states). Real barriers are pinned by **Fermi-level pinning** at the interface, often substantially deviating from this rule — a critical point for compound semiconductors like GaAs, InP, and the III-nitrides.

## 1.4 The Ohmic Contact — Non-Rectifying Junction

An **Ohmic contact** is a metal–semiconductor interface that exhibits a **linear, symmetric, low-resistance** current–voltage relationship — the voltage drop across the contact is small compared to the voltage drop across the active device region, regardless of the polarity of applied bias.

Two engineering strategies exist:
1. **Barrier-lowering strategy** — Choose a metal whose work function nearly matches the appropriate band edge of the semiconductor (i.e., $\Phi_m \approx \chi_s$ for n-type, $\Phi_m \approx \chi_s + E_g$ for p-type).
2. **Tunnelling strategy** — Dope the semiconductor **degenerately** ($N_D > 10^{19}\ \text{cm}^{-3}$) so that the depletion width is so narrow (< 10 nm) that electrons **quantum-mechanically tunnel** through the barrier. This is the *industry-standard* approach in modern compound-semiconductor MMICs and HEMTs (e.g., Ti/Pt/Au on AlGaAs/GaAs with Si δ-doping, or AuGe/Ni/Au ohmics on GaAs).
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 🔷 SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Energy-Band Diagram of an M–S Junction (n-type, $\Phi_m > \chi_s$)

When the metal and the n-type semiconductor are brought into contact, electrons flow from the semiconductor (higher $E_F$) into the metal (lower $E_F$) until the **Fermi levels coincide** at thermal equilibrium. This transfer of negative charge leaves behind a region of **ionized, positively-charged donor atoms** — the **depletion region** — and an induced surface charge of **$-Q$** on the metal.

The energy bands of the semiconductor **bend upwards** near the interface. The amount of bending equals the **built-in potential** $V_{bi}$, given by:

$$V_{bi} = \frac{\Phi_{Bn} - (\Phi_n)}{q} = \frac{\Phi_{Bn} - (E_C - E_F)_b}{q}$$

where $(\Phi_n) = (E_C - E_F)_b$ is the energy difference between $E_C$ and $E_F$ in the **bulk** neutral semiconductor.

The barrier is viewed from **two sides**:
- **From the metal side** (looking into the semiconductor): barrier = $\Phi_{Bn}$.
- **From the semiconductor side** (looking into the metal): barrier = $\Phi_m - \chi_s$ (this is the same number — by definition).
- This barrier is **independent of doping** — a key fact exploited in sensor design.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why does the barrier NOT depend on doping?**
> The Schottky–Mott rule derives from the alignment of the **vacuum levels** of the two materials. Doping changes *how many* electrons are available, not the fundamental energy levels themselves. Doping only changes the *width* of the depletion region, not its *height*.

## 2.2 Depletion Approximation & Space-Charge Region

Under the **depletion approximation** (mobile carriers fully swept out, fixed ionized donors in the depletion width $W$):

- Charge density: $\rho(x) = qN_D$ for $0 < x < W$ (depletion region); $\rho = 0$ elsewhere.
- Solving **Poisson's equation** in 1-D:

$$\frac{d^2V}{dx^2} = -\frac{\rho(x)}{\varepsilon_s} = -\frac{qN_D}{\varepsilon_s}$$

- Integrating twice with boundary conditions $V(W) = 0$ and $\left.\dfrac{dV}{dx}\right|_{x=W} = 0$:

$$V(x) = V_{bi}\,\left(1 - \frac{x}{W}\right)^2 \quad \text{and} \quad W = \sqrt{\frac{2\varepsilon_s V_{bi}}{qN_D}}$$

- The **maximum electric field** at the interface:

$$E_{max} = \left.-\frac{dV}{dx}\right|_{x=0} = \frac{2 V_{bi}}{W} = \sqrt{\frac{2 q N_D V_{bi}}{\varepsilon_s}}$$

## 2.3 The Thermionic Emission (TE) Theory of Current Flow

In a Schottky diode, the dominant current mechanism for moderately doped semiconductors ($N_D < 10^{17}\ \text{cm}^{-3}$) is **thermionic emission** — electrons in the semiconductor gain enough thermal energy ($kT \approx 26\ \text{meV}$ at 300 K) to **climb over the barrier** from the semiconductor side into the metal.

The forward current is:

$$I = I_S \left[\exp\!\left(\frac{qV}{nkT}\right) - 1\right]$$

with the **reverse saturation current**:

$$I_S = A\,A^{*}\,T^{2}\,\exp\!\left(-\frac{q\Phi_{Bn}}{kT}\right)$$

where:
- $A$ = diode area (cm²)
- $A^{*}$ = **Richardson's constant** for thermionic emission from a semiconductor ($\text{A/cm}^2\!\cdot\!\text{K}^2$)
- $n$ = **ideality factor** (1.0 for ideal TE; 1.01–1.5 in real devices; > 2 indicates other transport mechanisms)
- $\Phi_{Bn}$ = effective Schottky barrier height

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Examiner Tip:** The ideality factor $n$ accounts for the deviation from pure thermionic emission. The closer $n$ is to unity, the "cleaner" the interface (fewer defects, less recombination in the depletion region).

## 2.4 The Richardson Constant

The Richardson constant is **not universal** — it depends on the effective mass $m^{*}$ in the semiconductor:

$$A^{*} = \frac{4 \pi q m^{*} k^{2}}{h^{3}}$$

For several compound semiconductors:

| Semiconductor | $m^{*}/m_0$ | $A^{*}$ (A/cm²·K²) |
|---|---|---|
| GaAs | 0.067 | **8.16** |
| InP | 0.077 | **9.36** |
| InAs | 0.023 | **2.8** |
| Si | 1.08 | **112** |
| Ge | 0.55 | **143** |

## 2.5 Image-Force Lowering (Schottky Barrier Reduction)

A **real** Schottky barrier is *slightly lower* than $\Phi_{Bn}^{0}$ because an electron at distance $x$ from the metal surface induces an equal and opposite image charge inside the metal. The attractive Coulomb interaction is:

$$F_{\text{image}} = -\frac{q^{2}}{4\pi\varepsilon_s (2x)^{2}} = -\frac{q^{2}}{16\pi\varepsilon_s x^{2}}$$

The corresponding image potential energy is:

$$V_{\text{image}}(x) = -\frac{q}{16\pi\varepsilon_s x}$$

The **total potential** is the sum of the parabolic depletion potential and the image potential. The **barrier-lowering** at the interface ($x = 0$) is:

$$\Delta \Phi_{B} = \sqrt{\frac{q E_{max}}{4\pi\varepsilon_s}}$$

giving an effective barrier:

$$\Phi_{Bn} = \Phi_{Bn}^{0} - \Delta\Phi_{B} = \Phi_{Bn}^{0} - \sqrt{\frac{q E_{max}}{4\pi\varepsilon_s}}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Physical Insight — The Schottky Effect**
> As reverse bias increases, $E_{max}$ grows, $\Delta\Phi_{B}$ grows, and the effective barrier **shrinks**. This explains why the reverse current in real Schottky diodes *gradually increases* with reverse bias rather than remaining perfectly saturated — a key feature exploited in **Schottky-barrier infrared detectors** and **mixer diodes**.

## 2.6 The Ohmic Contact — Quantitative Design

The quality of an Ohmic contact is measured by the **specific contact resistance** $R_c$ (units: $\Omega\!\cdot\!\text{cm}^2$):

$$R_c = \left(\frac{\partial J}{\partial V}\right)^{-1}\bigg|_{V=0} = \frac{k}{q A^{*} T} \exp\!\left(\frac{q\Phi_{Bn}}{kT}\right)$$

For a heavily doped semiconductor where **field emission (tunnelling)** dominates:

$$R_c \propto \exp\!\left(\frac{2\Phi_{Bn}}{\hbar}\sqrt{\frac{\varepsilon_s m^{*}}{N_D}}\right)$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The Two Engineering Levers for $R_c$:**
> 1. **Lower $\Phi_{Bn}$** — choose a metal with the right work function.
> 2. **Increase $N_D$** — degenerate doping shrinks the tunnelling width. In GaAs MMICs, contact regions are doped to $N_D > 5 \times 10^{18}\ \text{cm}^{-3}$ to reach $R_c < 10^{-6}\ \Omega\!\cdot\!\text{cm}^2$.

## 2.7 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Master Reference Table)

| # | Formula | Meaning / Use | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $\Phi_{Bn}^{0} = \Phi_m - \chi_s$ | Schottky–Mott rule (n-type) | eV |
| 2 | $\Phi_{Bp}^{0} = E_g - (\Phi_m - \chi_s)$ | Schottky–Mott rule (p-type) | eV |
| 3 | $V_{bi} = \Phi_{Bn} - \Phi_n$ | Built-in potential | V |
| 4 | $W = \sqrt{2\varepsilon_s V_{bi}\,/\,(qN_D)}$ | Depletion width | cm |
| 5 | $E_{max} = \sqrt{2 q N_D V_{bi}\,/\,\varepsilon_s}$ | Peak electric field | V/cm |
| 6 | $I = I_S\,[\exp(qV/nkT) - 1]$ | Schottky diode I–V | A |
| 7 | $I_S = A A^{*} T^{2} \exp(-q\Phi_{Bn}/kT)$ | Reverse saturation current | A |
| 8 | $A^{*} = 4\pi q m^{*} k^{2}/h^{3}$ | Richardson constant | A/cm²·K² |
| 9 | $\Delta\Phi_B = \sqrt{q E_{max}\,/\,(4\pi\varepsilon_s)}$ | Image-force lowering | eV |
| 10 | $R_c \propto \exp\!\big[(2\Phi_{Bn}/\hbar)\sqrt{\varepsilon_s m^{*}/N_D}\big]$ | Specific contact resistance | $\Omega\cdot\text{cm}^2$ |
| 11 | $J_{ST} = A^{*} T^{2} \exp(-q\Phi_{Bn}/kT)$ | Saturation current density | A/cm² |

## 2.8 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Schottky diodes**: Aggressive rectifiers used in switched-mode power supplies (low forward drop ~0.3 V vs. ~0.7 V for Si p–n).
- **MESFETs, HEMTs**: The gate of every GaAs / GaN microwave transistor **is a Schottky contact**.
- **Schottky-barrier solar cells**: $\text{Pt}/\text{Si}$ or $\text{Pd}/\text{GaAs}$ — the SBH is a major factor in open-circuit voltage.
- **Metal–semiconductor–metal (MSM) photodetectors**: Two back-to-back Schottky contacts for ultra-fast (sub-ps) photodetection.
- **Ohmic contacts**: Ti/Pt/Au and AuGe/Ni/Au metallisations are the *lifeblood* of every III–V MMIC, LED, and laser diode.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 🔷 SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Depletion Width $W$ from Poisson's Equation

**Starting point:** Poisson's equation in 1-D for the depletion region of a uniformly-doped n-type Schottky diode.

$$\frac{d^{2}V(x)}{dx^{2}} = -\frac{\rho(x)}{\varepsilon_s} = -\frac{q N_D}{\varepsilon_s}, \qquad 0 \le x \le W$$

**Step 1 — Integrate once** with the boundary condition $\left.\dfrac{dV}{dx}\right|_{x=W} = 0$ (field vanishes in the neutral bulk):

$$\frac{dV}{dx} = -\frac{q N_D}{\varepsilon_s}(x - W)$$

**Step 2 — Integrate again** with the boundary condition $V(W) = 0$ (potential is zero in the neutral bulk):

$$V(x) = -\frac{q N_D}{\varepsilon_s}\left(\frac{x^{2}}{2} - Wx + \frac{W^{2}}{2}\right) = -\frac{q N_D}{2\varepsilon_s}(W - x)^{2}$$

**Step 3 — Apply the contact-potential boundary** $V(0) = -V_{bi}$:

$$-V_{bi} = -\frac{q N_D W^{2}}{2\varepsilon_s}$$

**Step 4 — Solve for $W$:**

$$\boxed{\,W = \sqrt{\frac{2\varepsilon_s V_{bi}}{q N_D}}\,}$$

For an applied forward bias $V_a > 0$, replace $V_{bi}$ with $(V_{bi} - V_a)$ in the expression.

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Barrier Lowering from Image-Force Effect

**Starting potential** at position $x$ from the metal surface — sum of electrostatic and image contributions:

$$V_{\text{tot}}(x) = -q E_{max}\, x - \frac{q}{16\pi\varepsilon_s x} + V_{bi}$$

**Step 1 — Find the maximum** of $-V_{\text{tot}}(x)$ (the peak of the barrier as seen by an electron moving from semiconductor to metal). Set $\dfrac{dV_{\text{tot}}}{dx} = 0$:

$$-q E_{max} + \frac{q}{16\pi\varepsilon_s x_m^{2}} = 0 \quad \Longrightarrow \quad x_m = \sqrt{\frac{1}{16\pi\varepsilon_s E_{max}}}$$

**Step 2 — Evaluate the image contribution at $x_m$:**

$$\Delta\Phi_B = \frac{q}{16\pi\varepsilon_s x_m} = \frac{q}{16\pi\varepsilon_s}\sqrt{16\pi\varepsilon_s E_{max}} = \sqrt{\frac{q E_{max}}{4\pi\varepsilon_s}}$$

**Step 3 — Effective barrier:**

$$\Phi_{Bn}^{\text{eff}} = \Phi_{Bn}^{0} - \Delta\Phi_B = \Phi_{Bn}^{0} - \sqrt{\frac{q E_{max}}{4\pi\varepsilon_s}}$$

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Saturation Current from Thermionic Emission

**Step 1** — Electrons in the conduction band of the semiconductor with energies $E \ge E_C + q\Phi_{Bn}$ (the barrier height) can surmount the barrier. The flux of such electrons (per unit area) moving **toward the metal** in the +x direction:

$$J_{s \to m} = \frac{A^{*} T^{2}}{q}\,\exp\!\left(-\frac{q\Phi_{Bn}}{kT}\right)$$

**Step 2** — Electrons in the metal face a much higher barrier ($\Phi_m \approx 5\ \text{eV}$). With no applied bias, an *equal and opposite* current of electrons flows from metal to semiconductor. The net current at equilibrium is zero.

**Step 3** — Apply forward bias $V_a$. The barrier on the semiconductor side is reduced by $V_a$ (the bands unbend). The current from semiconductor to metal becomes:

$$J_{s \to m} = \frac{A^{*} T^{2}}{q}\,\exp\!\left(-\frac{q(\Phi_{Bn} - V_a)}{kT}\right) = \frac{A^{*} T^{2}}{q}\,\exp\!\left(-\frac{q\Phi_{Bn}}{kT}\right)\exp\!\left(\frac{qV_a}{kT}\right)$$

**Step 4** — The metal-to-semiconductor current is **unaffected** (the metal sees a fixed, large barrier):

$$J_{m \to s} = -\frac{A^{*} T^{2}}{q}\,\exp\!\left(-\frac{q\Phi_{Bn}}{kT}\right)$$

**Step 5 — Net current density:**

$$J = J_{s \to m} + J_{m \to s} = \frac{A^{*} T^{2}}{q}\,\exp\!\left(-\frac{q\Phi_{Bn}}{kT}\right)\left[\exp\!\left(\frac{qV_a}{kT}\right) - 1\right]$$

**Step 6 — Total current** (multiply by area $A$) and introduce the ideality factor $n$:

$$\boxed{\,I = A A^{*} T^{2} \exp\!\left(-\frac{q\Phi_{Bn}}{kT}\right)\left[\exp\!\left(\frac{qV_a}{nkT}\right) - 1\right]\,}$$

$$= I_S\left[\exp\!\left(\frac{qV_a}{nkT}\right) - 1\right]$$

## 3.4 Derivation 4 — Specific Contact Resistance for Tunnelling Ohmic Contact

**Step 1** — A heavily doped semiconductor has an extremely thin triangular barrier at the interface. The **tunnelling probability** through a barrier of height $\Phi_{Bn}$ and characteristic width $\delta$ is (WKB approximation):

$$T \approx \exp\!\left(-\frac{2}{\hbar}\int_0^{\delta}\sqrt{2 m^{*}[U(x) - E]}\,dx\right) \approx \exp\!\left(-\frac{2\,\delta\sqrt{2 m^{*}\Phi_{Bn}}}{\hbar}\right)$$

**Step 2** — In a triangular barrier, $\delta$ is set by the depletion width at zero bias:

$$\delta = \sqrt{\frac{2\varepsilon_s \Phi_{Bn}}{q N_D}}$$

**Step 3** — Substituting:

$$T \propto \exp\!\left(-\frac{2\sqrt{2 m^{*}\Phi_{Bn}}}{\hbar}\sqrt{\frac{2\varepsilon_s \Phi_{Bn}}{q N_D}}\right) = \exp\!\left(-\frac{2\Phi_{Bn}}{\hbar}\sqrt{\frac{2\varepsilon_s m^{*}}{q N_D}}\right)$$

**Step 4** — Since $J \propto T$ and $R_c = (\partial J / \partial V)^{-1}$:

$$\boxed{\,R_c \propto \exp\!\left(\frac{2\Phi_{Bn}}{\hbar}\sqrt{\frac{2\varepsilon_s m^{*}}{q N_D}}\right)\,}$$

**Numerical Reading:** Doubling the doping from $10^{18}$ to $10^{19}\ \text{cm}^{-3}$ reduces $R_c$ by **more than two orders of magnitude** — the *real* reason compound-semiconductor foundries push dopant concentrations as high as possible in the contact layer.

## 3.5 Worked Numerical Example (KTU-Standard Style)

**Problem:** A Schottky diode is formed between Tungsten ($\Phi_m = 4.55\ \text{eV}$) and n-type GaAs ($\chi_s = 4.07\ \text{eV}$, $N_D = 5 \times 10^{16}\ \text{cm}^{-3}$, $\varepsilon_r = 13.1$, $A^{*} = 8.16\ \text{A/cm}^2\!\cdot\!\text{K}^2$). The diode area is $A = 10^{-4}\ \text{cm}^2$, temperature $T = 300\ \text{K}$, and bulk Fermi-level position $E_C - E_F = 0.15\ \text{eV}$. Calculate:
(a) The Schottky barrier height.
(b) The built-in potential.
(c) The depletion width at zero bias.
(d) The forward current at $V_a = 0.5\ \text{V}$ (assume $n = 1$).

**Solution:**

(a) Using Schottky–Mott:

$$\Phi_{Bn}^{0} = \Phi_m - \chi_s = 4.55 - 4.07 = 0.48\ \text{eV}$$

(b) Built-in potential:

$$V_{bi} = \Phi_{Bn}^{0} - (E_C - E_F) = 0.48 - 0.15 = 0.33\ \text{V}$$

(c) Depletion width ($q = 1.6 \times 10^{-19}$ C, $\varepsilon_s = 13.1 \times 8.854 \times 10^{-14}\ \text{F/cm} = 1.16 \times 10^{-12}\ \text{F/cm}$):

$$W = \sqrt{\frac{2 \times 1.16 \times 10^{-12} \times 0.33}{1.6 \times 10^{-19} \times 5 \times 10^{16}}} = \sqrt{\frac{7.66 \times 10^{-13}}{8.0 \times 10^{-3}}} = 3.10 \times 10^{-5}\ \text{cm} = 310\ \text{nm}$$

(d) Saturation current:

$$I_S = A A^{*} T^{2} \exp\!\left(-\frac{q\Phi_{Bn}^{0}}{kT}\right) = 10^{-4} \times 8.16 \times 9 \times 10^{4} \times \exp\!\left(-\frac{0.48}{0.0259}\right)$$

$$I_S = 73.44 \times \exp(-18.53) = 73.44 \times 9.27 \times 10^{-9} = 6.81 \times 10^{-7}\ \text{A} = 0.681\ \mu\text{A}$$

Forward current at $V_a = 0.5\ \text{V}$:

$$I = I_S\!\left[\exp\!\left(\frac{0.5}{0.0259}\right) - 1\right] = 0.681\ \mu\text{A} \times [e^{19.31} - 1]$$

$$I = 0.681\ \mu\text{A} \times (2.43 \times 10^{8} - 1) \approx 1.65 \times 10^{2}\ \text{A} = 165\ \text{mA}$$

(Note: in real devices series resistance would limit this — this calculation is the *ideal* Schottky-diode value.)

# 🔷 SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Energy-Band Diagram — Schottky Contact on n-type (Before and After Contact)

The following Mermaid architecture flow captures the band-alignment logic that governs the Schottky barrier. (Physical energy-band drawings with curved band-edges are best rendered in dedicated EDA tools; here we encode the conceptual flow.)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A0[Isolated Metal] --> A1[Fermi Level EF_m at Phi_m below vacuum]
    B0[Isolated n-type Semiconductor] --> B1[EF_s above EF_m before contact]
    B1 --> B2[Vacuum levels aligned, work function difference = Phi_m - chi_s]
    A1 --> B2
    B2 --> C1{Contact: electrons flow from SC to metal}
    C1 --> D1[Charge transfer: plus donors on SC side, minus charge on metal side]
    D1 --> E1[Bands of SC bend UPWARD by V_bi]
    E1 --> F1[Equilibrium: Fermi levels equalised across junction]
    F1 --> G1[Schottky Barrier Phi_Bn = Phi_m - chi_s forms]
    G1 --> H1[Depletion width W on SC side]
    H1 --> I1[Rectifying Contact Confirmed]
```

## 4.2 M–S Junction Type Decision Flowchart

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    START[Junction to be designed] --> Q1{Choose metal and semiconductor}
    Q1 --> Q2{Compare Phi_m and chi_s for n-type SC}
    Q2 -->|Phi_m greater than chi_s| SCH1[Schottky / Rectifying Contact]
    Q2 -->|Phi_m approx equal to chi_s| OHM1[Ohmic Contact Barrier-free]
    Q2 -->|Phi_m less than chi_s| OHM2[Ohmic Contact Anti-barrier]
    SCH1 --> SCH2[Bands bend upward, depletion region forms]
    SCH2 --> SCH3[Use TE theory: I = I_S exp qV over nkT minus 1]
    OHM1 --> OHM3[Doping can be moderate N_D approx 10^16]
    OHM2 --> OHM3
    OHM3 --> OHM4{R_c target less than 10 to the power minus 6 Ohm cm squared}
    OHM4 -->|Yes| OHM5[Heavy doping N_D greater than 10^19 with tunnelling]
    OHM4 -->|No| OHM3
    OHM5 --> DONE[Ohmic Contact Realised]
```

## 4.3 I–V Characteristics — Schottky vs Ohmic (Block-Level Functional Architecture)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph BIAS[Applied Bias V]
        V1[Forward V greater than 0] --- V2[Reverse V less than 0]
    end
    BIAS --> SCHCHAR
    SCHCHAR[Schottky Diode] --> F1[Exponential rise: I approx I_S exp qV over nkT]
    SCHCHAR --> R1[Soft saturation: I approx minus I_S with small increase due to Schottky effect]
    BIAS --> OHMCHAR
    OHMCHAR[Ohmic Contact] --> L1[Linear I = V over R_c for all polarities]
    OHMCHAR --> L2[Symmetric for forward and reverse]
    F1 --> SUMMARY[Rectifying: large IF, small IR]
    R1 --> SUMMARY
    L1 --> SYM[Non-rectifying]
    L2 --> SYM
```

## 4.4 Transport-Mechanism Selection Map

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Z[Carriers at the M-S Interface] --> Z1{Low doping N_D less than 10 to the power 17}
    Z1 -->|Yes| T1[Thermionic Emission TE dominates]
    Z1 -->|No| Z2{Medium doping 10 to the power 17 to 10 to the power 18}
    Z2 -->|Yes| T2[Thermionic Field Emission TFE]
    Z2 -->|No| Z3{Heavy doping N_D greater than 10 to the power 19}
    Z3 -->|Yes| T3[Field Emission FE: tunnelling dominates]
    T1 --> APP1[Used in standard Schottky diodes and MESFET gates]
    T2 --> APP2[Transition regime: must use full expression]
    T3 --> APP3[Used in modern Ohmic contacts: Ti, Pt, Au on doped III-V layers]
```

## 4.5 Energy-Band Schematic — Forward & Reverse Bias (n-type Schottky)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Forward Bias V greater than 0] --> B[Semiconductor bands unbend]
    B --> C[Barrier on SC side reduced from Phi_Bn to Phi_Bn minus V]
    C --> D[Majority carrier electrons flow over the reduced barrier]
    D --> E[Large exponential current: I = I_S exp qV over nkT]
    F[Reverse Bias V less than 0] --> G[Semiconductor bands bend further upward]
    G --> H[Barrier on SC side INCREASES]
    H --> I[Only electrons with energy greater than high barrier can flow]
    I --> J[Small reverse saturation current: I approx minus I_S]
    J --> K[At large reverse bias, barrier lowering Delta Phi increases, I slowly rises with V]
```
