- **Dominant at low temperatures** and in heavily doped material.
- Temperature scaling: $\mu_{ii} \propto \dfrac{T^{3/2}}{N_I \cdot f(x)}$

where $f(x) = \ln(1 + x) - \dfrac{x}{1+x}$ with $x = \dfrac{24 m^* \epsilon_r \epsilon_0 (k_B T)^2}{\hbar^2 q^2 n}$.

### (d) Alloy Scattering
- Specific to **ternary and quaternary alloys** (e.g., $\text{Al}_x\text{Ga}_{1-x}\text{As}$, $\text{In}_{1-x}\text{Ga}_x\text{As}_y\text{P}_{1-y}$).
- Random distribution of the two cation/anion species creates local potential fluctuations.
- Independent of temperature (approximately) but depends on alloy composition $x$.

### (e) Neutral Impurity & Defect Scattering
- Important at very low temperatures when donors/acceptors are not ionized, or in highly defective materials (GaN with dislocations, for example).

## 2.3 Matthiessen's Rule — Combining Scattering Rates

Since each scattering mechanism independently randomizes momentum, the rates add linearly:

$$\frac{1}{\tau_{tot}} = \sum_i \frac{1}{\tau_i} \quad \Longrightarrow \quad \frac{1}{\mu_{tot}} = \sum_i \frac{1}{\mu_i}$$

> [!WARNING]
> Matthiessen's rule is an *approximation* — it assumes scattering processes are independent. In heavily doped polar semiconductors it can break down by 20–30%, but it is the standard tool for KTU numerical problems and qualitative mobility-vs-T plots.

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> **Master this table.** KTU frequently asks derivations or numerical substitution directly from these expressions.

| # | Formula | Meaning | Typical Use |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $\mu = \dfrac{q\tau}{m^*}$ | Fundamental Drude mobility | Base relation |
| 2 | $v_d = \mu E$ | Low-field drift velocity | Velocity calculation |
| 3 | $\sigma = q(n\mu_n + p\mu_p)$ | Conductivity | Resistivity problems |
| 4 | $\dfrac{1}{\mu_{tot}} = \sum \dfrac{1}{\mu_i}$ | Matthiessen's rule | Total mobility |
| 5 | $\mu_{ac} \propto T^{-3/2}$ | Acoustic phonon mobility | Lattice scattering |
| 6 | $\mu_{PO} \propto \left[e^{\hbar\omega_{LO}/k_BT}-1\right]$ | Polar optical mobility | III-V compounds at 300 K |
| 7 | $\mu_{ii} \propto \dfrac{T^{3/2}}{N_I \ln(\dots)}$ | Ionized impurity mobility | Doping dependence |
| 8 | $\mu_{alloy} = \dfrac{C}{x(1-x)}$ | Alloy mobility (bowing) | Ternary alloys |
| 9 | $\mu_H = r_H \cdot \mu_d$ | Hall vs drift mobility | Hall measurement |
| 10 | $R_H = \dfrac{r_H}{qn}$ | Hall coefficient | Carrier concentration |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> In the table above, the symbol `\vert` is *not* used in any formula — pure mathematical notation is preserved using parentheses, `exp`, and `ln` to avoid breaking markdown table syntax.

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **High Electron Mobility Transistors (HEMTs)** — exploit high $\mu_n$ in modulation-doped $\text{AlGaAs/GaAs}$ or $\text{AlGaN/GaN}$ heterostructures to push THz frequency operation.
- **HBTs (Heterojunction Bipolar Transistors)** — use high-mobility InGaAs bases for ultra-fast switching in 5G/6G RF front-ends.
- **Photodetectors and Solar Cells** — mobility determines carrier *collection* length $L = \sqrt{D\tau}$ where $D = \mu k_B T/q$.
- **Gunn Diodes** — exploit *negative differential mobility* in GaAs and InP for microwave oscillation.
- **LEDs and Laser Diodes** — mobility impacts the *series resistance* and the *modulation bandwidth* $f_{-3dB}$ of laser diodes.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of the Drude Mobility

**Starting point:** Newton's second law applied to a single electron drifting in an applied field $E$, with a frictional drag representing scattering collisions.

The equation of motion in the relaxation-time approximation is:

$$m^* \frac{dv_d}{dt} = qE - \frac{m^* v_d}{\tau}$$

**Step 1 — Set the derivative to zero** (steady-state, $dv_d/dt = 0$):

$$0 = qE - \frac{m^* v_d}{\tau}$$

**Step 2 — Solve algebraically for $v_d$:**

$$v_d = \frac{q\tau E}{m^*}$$

**Step 3 — Apply the definition of mobility** $\mu = v_d / E$:

$$\boxed{\mu = \frac{q\tau}{m^*}}$$

**Physical interpretation:** Mobility is large when (i) the carrier stays in flight for a long time between collisions (large $\tau$), and (ii) the carrier is intrinsically light (small $m^*$). The quantity $q\tau/m^*$ is the *Hall* drift mobility under the assumption of energy-independent scattering (often called the "Drude" picture).

## 3.2 Derivation of Matthiessen's Rule

**Step 1 — Suppose a carrier is subject to two independent scattering processes** with relaxation times $\tau_1$ and $\tau_2$.

**Step 2 — The total rate of momentum randomization is the sum of individual rates:**

$$\frac{1}{\tau_{tot}} = \frac{1}{\tau_1} + \frac{1}{\tau_2}$$

This is because each collision event is a *renewal* of the random walk of momentum.

**Step 3 — Substitute the Drude formula** $\mu_i = q\tau_i / m^*$ into the sum:

$$\frac{1}{\tau_{tot}} = \frac{m^*\mu_1}{q} \cdot \frac{1}{m^* \mu_1 \tau_1} + \frac{m^*\mu_2}{q} \cdot \frac{1}{m^*\mu_2 \tau_2}$$

Equivalently, divide both sides of the rate-sum equation by $q/m^*$:

$$\frac{q}{m^* \tau_{tot}} = \frac{q}{m^* \tau_1} + \frac{q}{m^* \tau_2}$$

**Step 4 — Identify each side as a mobility** using $\mu = q\tau/m^*$:

$$\boxed{\frac{1}{\mu_{tot}} = \frac{1}{\mu_1} + \frac{1}{\mu_2}}$$

This generalizes trivially to $N$ mechanisms. The *smallest* mobility dominates the total, just as the *largest* resistance dominates in a parallel resistor network — a beautiful analogy useful for KTU viva questions.

## 3.3 Temperature Dependence of Mobility — Analytical Reasoning

We examine each dominant mechanism:

### (a) Acoustic Phonon Scattering

The phonon population at temperature $T$ scales as $n_{ph} \propto T$ (high-$T$ equipartition), and the scattering cross-section depends on the *amplitude* of atomic vibration, which scales as $\sqrt{T}$. Combining:

$$\frac{1}{\tau_{ac}} \propto n_{ph} \cdot \sigma_{ac} \propto T \cdot \sqrt{T} = T^{3/2}$$

Therefore:

$$\mu_{ac} = \frac{q\tau_{ac}}{m^*} \propto T^{-3/2}$$

### (b) Polar Optical Phonon Scattering (Fröhlich)

The number of LO phonons follows Bose–Einstein statistics:

$$n_{LO} = \frac{1}{\exp(\hbar\omega_{LO}/k_BT) - 1}$$

The scattering rate depends linearly on the phonon population plus a *spontaneous emission* term, giving the canonical form:

$$\mu_{PO} \propto \frac{1}{n_{LO}} = \left[ \exp\!\left(\frac{\hbar\omega_{LO}}{k_BT}\right) - 1 \right]$$

As $T$ rises, the bracket *shrinks* — mobility drops sharply. This is why GaAs mobility plummets above 300 K.

### (c) Ionized Impurity Scattering (Brooks–Herring)

Higher temperature $\Rightarrow$ faster carrier $\Rightarrow$ less time near the ion $\Rightarrow$ weaker scattering. Screening of the ion's Coulomb field by free carriers also increases with $T$. Net result:

$$\mu_{ii} \propto \frac{T^{3/2}}{N_I}$$

### (d) Combined Temperature Plot (Analytical Behavior)

The total mobility $\mu_{tot}(T)$ is governed by the *minimum* of the contributing mechanisms. Three distinct regimes appear in a $\log\mu$ vs $\log T$ plot:

| Regime | Temperature | Dominant Mechanism | Slope on $\log\mu$–$\log T$ |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Low $T$ (< 77 K) | Ionized impurity | $+3/2$ |
| 2 | Mid $T$ (77–200 K) | Acoustic / Polar optical | $-1$ to $-3/2$ |
| 3 | High $T$ (> 250 K) | Polar optical (Fröhlich) | Strong negative (exponential) |

## 3.4 Numerical Worked Example (KTU-Style)

> [!NOTE]
> **Question:** In a sample of GaAs, the electron drift mobility is $\mu_n = 8500\ cm^2/Vs$ at 300 K. The electron concentration is $n = 10^{16}\ cm^{-3}$. Calculate (i) the conductivity, and (ii) the relaxation time, given $m_n^* = 0.067\ m_0$.

**Solution:**

**Part (i) — Conductivity:**

$$\sigma = q n \mu_n$$

$$\sigma = (1.6 \times 10^{-19})(10^{22})(8500 \times 10^{-4})$$

$$\sigma = (1.6 \times 10^{-19})(10^{22})(0.85)$$

$$\sigma = 1.36\ \Omega^{-1}m^{-1}$$

**Part (ii) — Relaxation time:**

First, convert the mobility to SI: $\mu_n = 8500 \times 10^{-4} = 0.85\ m^2/Vs$.

$$\tau_n = \frac{\mu_n m_n^*}{q} = \frac{(0.85)(0.067 \times 9.11 \times 10^{-31})}{1.6 \times 10^{-19}}$$

$$\tau_n = \frac{(0.85)(6.10 \times 10^{-32})}{1.6 \times 10^{-19}}$$

$$\boxed{\tau_n \approx 3.24 \times 10^{-13}\ s \quad (\text{i.e., } 324\ fs)}$$

**Interpretation:** The mean free time between collisions in GaAs at room temperature is only about 0.3 picoseconds — this is precisely why device engineers model GaAs transport using the **Boltzmann Transport Equation** rather than classical ballistic pictures.

## 3.5 Hall Mobility vs Drift Mobility

When a magnetic field is applied, the Hall voltage $V_H$ yields a measured "Hall mobility":

$$\mu_H = \frac{\sigma R_H}{q} = \frac{r_H}{qn}$$

where the **Hall factor** $r_H = \langle \tau^2 \rangle / \langle \tau \rangle^2$ depends on the *energy dependence* of $\tau$. For common scattering types:

| Scattering Type | $r_H$ Value |
|---|---|
| Acoustic deformation potential | $1.93$ |
| Ionized impurity (low $B$) | $1.93$ |
| Ionized impurity (high $B$) | $1.18$ |
| Polar optical | $\sim 1.0$ |

Thus $\mu_H$ and $\mu_d$ differ by 10–90% — KTU questions often test this distinction.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Functional Block Diagram: The Mobility Ecosystem

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Applied Electric Field E] --> B[Carrier Acceleration qE]
    B --> C{Momentum Balance}
    C --> D[Steady Drift Velocity vd]
    C --> E[Scattering Events]
    E --> F1[Acoustic Phonon]
    E --> F2[Polar Optical Phonon Frohlich]
    E --> F3[Ionized Impurity]
    E --> F4[Alloy Disorder]
    E --> F5[Neutral Defect]
    F1 --> G[Matthiessen Combination 1/mu_tot]
    F2 --> G
    F3 --> G
    F4 --> G
    F5 --> G
    G --> H[Total Relaxation Time tau]
    H --> I[Carrier Mobility mu = q tau / m star]
    I --> J[Device Performance]
    J --> J1[HEMT Speed]
    J --> J2[Gunn Oscillator]
    J --> J3[Laser Modulation BW]
    D --> I
```

## 4.2 Block Diagram: Mobility vs Temperature Regimes

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph LOWT[Low Temperature T less than 77 K]
        A1[Ionized Impurity Dominant] --> A2[mu rises as T to power 3/2]
    end
    subgraph MIDT[Mid Temperature 77 to 200 K]
        B1[Acoustic Phonon] --> B2[mu falls as T to power minus 3/2]
    end
    subgraph HIGHT[High Temperature greater than 250 K]
        C1[Polar Optical Phonon] --> C2[mu falls exponentially]
    end
    LOWT --> MIDT
    MIDT --> HIGHT
```

## 4.3 Block Diagram: Drift vs Hall Mobility Pipeline

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Sample under E and B] --> B[Measure Current I and Hall Voltage VH]
    B --> C[Compute Hall Coefficient RH]
    B --> D[Compute Conductivity sigma]
    C --> E[Hall Mobility muH = sigma RH]
    D --> E
    E --> F{Scattering Type}
    F --> F1[Acoustic: rH = 1.93]
    F --> F2[Ionized: rH between 1.18 and 1.93]
    F --> F3[Polar Optical: rH approx 1.0]
    F1 --> G[Drift Mobility mud = muH / rH]
    F2 --> G
    F3 --> G
```

## 4.4 Block Diagram: Compound-Semiconductor Mobility Hierarchy

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Compound Semiconductors] --> B[III V Binary GaAs InP GaN InSb]
    A --> C[III V Ternary AlGaAs InGaAs InGaP]
    A --> D[II VI ZnSe CdTe]
    A --> E[IV IV SiC]
    B --> B1[mu_n 8500 cm2Vs for GaAs]
    B --> B2[mu_n 77000 cm2Vs for InSb]
    C --> C1[Alloy Scattering Penalty]
    D --> D1[Polar LO Strong]
    E --> E1[High Field Mobility at High T]
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## Part A — 3 Mark Questions (Short Answer)

### Q1. `[KTU University Exam – May 2024, PEEVT413]`
**Define carrier mobility. Why is electron mobility higher than hole mobility in most compound semiconductors?**

**Model Answer:**
Carrier mobility is defined as the steady-state drift velocity acquired by a charge carrier per unit applied electric field, $\mu = v_d / E$. Its SI unit is $m^2/Vs$.

Electron mobility is higher than hole mobility because:
- The conduction band minimum in III-V compounds (e.g., GaAs) lies at the $\Gamma$-point, with a small effective mass ($m_n^* = 0.067\,m_0$ for GaAs).
- The valence band maximum involves heavy-hole, light-hole, and split-off bands, all with much larger effective masses (heavy hole: $\sim 0.5\,m_0$).
- Since $\mu \propto 1/m^*$, electrons are intrinsically more mobile.

### Q2. `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, PEEVT413]`
**What is Matthiessen's rule? Why is it considered an approximation?**

**Model Answer:**
Matthiessen's rule states that the reciprocal of total mobility is the sum of reciprocals of individual mobilities arising from each independent scattering mechanism:

$$\frac{1}{\mu_{tot}} = \sum_i \frac{1}{\mu_i}$$

It is an approximation because it assumes all scattering mechanisms are statistically independent. In heavily doped polar semiconductors and at high fields, mechanisms couple (e.g., impurity scattering is screened by mobile carriers, and phonon scattering depends on carrier energy), so the rule can deviate by 20–30% from experimental values.

---

## Part B — 14 Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2024, PEEVT413]`

**(a) Derive the expression for drift mobility in a semiconductor using the relaxation-time approximation. Show how Matthiessen's rule emerges when multiple scattering mechanisms are present.** *(7 marks)*

**(b) The electron drift mobility in undoped GaAs at 300 K is $8500\ cm^2/Vs$. Calculate the conductivity and mean free time, given $n = 5 \times 10^{15}\ cm^{-3}$ and $m_n^* = 0.067\ m_0$.** *(7 marks)*

#### Model Solution for Question A

**(a) Derivation:**

The equation of motion for a single electron under field $E$ in the relaxation-time approximation is:

$$m^* \frac{dv_d}{dt} + \frac{m^* v_d}{\tau} = qE$$

Setting $dv_d/dt = 0$ in steady state:

$$v_d = \frac{q\tau E}{m^*}$$

Hence:

$$\mu = \frac{v_d}{E} = \frac{q\tau}{m^*}$$

When $N$ independent scattering processes act simultaneously, the *rates* of momentum relaxation add:

$$\frac{1}{\tau_{tot}} = \sum_{i=1}^{N} \frac{1}{\tau_i}$$

Dividing by $q/m^*$ and using $\mu = q\tau/m^*$:

$$\frac{1}{\mu_{tot}} = \sum_{i=1}^{N} \frac{1}{\mu_i}$$

This is **Matthiessen's rule**. The mechanism with the *smallest* mobility dominates the total mobility.

**(b) Numerical solution:**

Convert all quantities to SI:
- $n = 5 \times 10^{15}\ cm^{-3} = 5 \times 10^{21}\ m^{-3}$
- $\mu_n = 8500\ cm^2/Vs = 0.85\ m^2/Vs$
- $m_n^* = 0.067 \times 9.11 \times 10^{-31} = 6.10 \times 10^{-32}\ kg$

**Conductivity:**

$$\sigma = q n \mu_n = (1.6 \times 10^{-19})(5 \times 10^{21})(0.85) = 0.68\ \Omega^{-1}m^{-1}$$

**Mean free time:**

$$\tau = \frac{\mu_n m_n^*}{q} = \frac{(0.85)(6.10 \times 10^{-32})}{1.6 \times 10^{-19}} = 3.24 \times 10^{-13}\ s$$

$$\boxed{\sigma = 0.68\ \Omega^{-1}m^{-1}, \qquad \tau = 3.24 \times 10^{-13}\ s}$$

---

### Question B (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2024, PEEVT413]`

**(a) Discuss the various scattering mechanisms that limit carrier mobility in III-V compound semiconductors. Give the temperature dependence of each mechanism.** *(7 marks)*

**(b) With the help of a sketch, explain the temperature dependence of mobility in a non-degenerate semiconductor. Identify the dominant mechanism in each regime.** *(7 marks)*

#### Model Solution for Question B

**(a) Scattering Mechanisms:**

1. **Acoustic Phonon Scattering** — arises from lattice vibrations modulating the band edge (deformation potential). Dominant near room temperature in non-polar materials. $\mu_{ac} \propto T^{-3/2}$.

2. **Polar Optical Phonon Scattering (Fröhlich)** — the dominant room-temperature mechanism in polar III-V compounds (GaAs, InP, GaN). Caused by long-range electrostatic potential from relative displacement of the two atoms in the unit cell. $\mu_{PO} \propto \left[\exp(\hbar\omega_{LO}/k_BT) - 1\right]$.

3. **Ionized Impurity Scattering (Brooks–Herring)** — Coulomb scattering from charged dopants. Dominant at low $T$ and in heavily doped samples. $\mu_{ii} \propto T^{3/2}/N_I$ (screened).

4. **Alloy Scattering** — present in ternary/quaternary alloys (AlGaAs, InGaAsP). Caused by random compositional disorder. $\mu_{alloy} \propto [x(1-x)]^{-1}$.

5. **Neutral Impurity and Defect Scattering** — important in compensated or highly defective material (e.g., GaN with high dislocation density).

6. **Carrier–Carrier Scattering** — significant only at very high carrier concentrations (degenerate regime).

The total mobility combines all via Matthiessen's rule.

**(b) Temperature Dependence Sketch (described in words for the answer sheet):**

On a graph of $\log \mu$ vs $\log T$, three regions appear:

- **Region I (Low T, < 77 K):** Mobility *increases* with $T$ as $T^{+3/2}$, dominated by ionized impurity scattering. As temperature rises, carriers move faster and spend less time near the ion, reducing scattering.
- **Region II (Mid T, 77–200 K):** Mobility peaks and then begins to *decrease* as $T^{-3/2}$, dominated by acoustic phonon scattering.
- **Region III (High T, > 250 K):** Mobility drops sharply (almost exponentially), dominated by polar optical phonon scattering in compound semiconductors.

**Identification of dominant mechanism:**

| Temperature | Dominant Mechanism | Sign of Slope |
|---|---|---|
| Low T | Ionized impurity | Positive (+3/2) |
| Mid T | Acoustic phonon | Negative (-3/2) |
| High T | Polar optical (Frohlich) | Strongly negative (exponential) |

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout — Where Students Commonly Lose Marks**
> 1. **Forgetting to convert** mobility from $cm^2/Vs$ to $m^2/Vs$ (factor $10^{-4}$) in numerical problems — costs 2 marks.
> 2. **Confusing Hall mobility with drift mobility** without applying the Hall factor $r_H$ — examiner deducts 1 mark per sub-question.
> 3. **Writing Matthiessen's rule as $\mu_{tot} = \sum \mu_i$** — this is WRONG. The rule is *reciprocal*. Many students get this backwards; expect a 2-mark penalty.
> 4. **Ignoring polar optical scattering** in GaAs/InP problems — at 300 K, it is the *dominant* mechanism, not acoustic. Examiners specifically test this.
> 5. **Not stating assumptions** (energy-independent $\tau$, non-degenerate statistics, low-field) — KTU mark schemes award 1 mark for explicitly listing these.

---

## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Definition:** $\mu = v_d / E = q\tau / m^*$ — the ratio of drift velocity to applied field, governed by scattering.
- **Drude relation:** $\mu = q\tau/m^*$ is the foundation; everything else is a refinement.
- **Matthiessen's rule:** $\dfrac{1}{\mu_{tot}} = \sum \dfrac{1}{\mu_i}$ — the *minimum* mobility dominates the total.
- **Five key scattering mechanisms** in III-V compounds: acoustic phonon ($T^{-3/2}$), polar optical Fröhlich (exponential in $T$), ionized impurity ($T^{3/2}/N_I$), alloy ($[x(1-x)]^{-1}$), and neutral impurity.
- **Polar optical scattering** is the *signature* of compound semiconductors — it is *absent* in pure elemental semiconductors like Si and Ge because those have homonuclear bonds with no built-in dipole.
- **Electron mobility > hole mobility** in compound semiconductors because of the lighter $\Gamma$-valley conduction band mass and heavier valence band mass.
- **Conductivity formula:** $\sigma = q(n\mu_n + p\mu_p)$ — both carrier *density* and *mobility* matter.
- **Hall vs drift mobility:** $\mu_H = r_H \mu_d$, with $r_H$ ranging from 1.0 (polar optical) to 1.93 (acoustic deformation potential).
- **High-field behavior:** mobility loses meaning; the velocity-field curve shows saturation and (in GaAs, InP) negative differential mobility → **Gunn effect**.
- **Practical tip:** For KTU 14-mark questions, always include (i) the physical origin of each mechanism, (ii) the temperature/doping scaling law, (iii) Matthiessen's combination, and (iv) at least one numerical example to earn full marks.
- **Memorize the formulas in the cheat sheet (Section 2.4)** — they appear in nearly every KTU university paper on this module.
- **Material comparison numbers to remember:** GaAs ($\mu_n \approx 8500\ cm^2/Vs$), InP ($\mu_n \approx 5400\ cm^2/Vs$), InSb ($\mu_n \approx 77000\ cm^2/Vs$), GaN ($\mu_n \approx 1000\ cm^2/Vs$, but with very high saturation velocity), Si ($\mu_n \approx 1400\ cm^2/Vs$).
- **Why HEMTs use AlGaAs/GaAs:** The modulation-doping technique spatially separates electrons from their parent donors, *eliminating ionized impurity scattering* in the channel and approaching the lattice-limited mobility.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Doping Concentration and Temperature
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEEVT413 - COMPOUND SEMICONDUCTORS
> - **Module:** Module Module 2
> - **Topic:** Doping Concentration and Temperature

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# 📘 SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

In compound semiconductors, **Doping Concentration** refers to the deliberate introduction of specific impurity atoms (donors or acceptors) into the host III-V or II-VI crystal lattice to control the type and density of free charge carriers, with concentrations typically ranging from **10¹⁴ cm⁻³ to 10¹⁹ cm⁻³** for device-grade material.

**Temperature** is a critical extrinsic variable that governs the statistical occupancy of dopant states, the generation of intrinsic carriers across the bandgap $E_g$, and the scattering mechanisms that limit carrier transport.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Anchor:** The carrier concentration $n(T)$ and $p(T)$ are **NOT** equal to the doping concentration $N_D$ and $N_A$ at all temperatures. Only in the *extrinsic saturation region* does $n \approx N_D$ (for n-type). Below and above this region, the relationship is governed by **Fermi-Dirac statistics** and the **charge neutrality condition (CNC)**.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Crowded Bus Stop" Intuition

Imagine a **bus stop in a small town** (the semiconductor crystal):

- **Bus Passengers** = Electrons (free carriers) waiting to travel
- **Bus Tickets (₹10 tickets)** = Dopant energy levels sitting just below the conduction band
- **Waiting Bench** = Valence band
- **Bus Fare Cost** = Energy gap $E_g$ between valence and conduction bands

At **very low temperature (winter, freeze-out region):** Only a few brave passengers are willing to pay the small ticket price; the rest huddle on the bench. Almost no buses run — carrier concentration is **tiny**.

At **room temperature (extrinsic region):** Most ticket-holders are comfortable paying the small fare, so the bus is **full** of electrons released from donors. The number of free electrons ≈ number of ticket holders ≈ $N_D$. The bus conductor says, "I've reached capacity!"

At **very high temperature (intrinsic region):** Passengers start **breaking the bench** (covalent bonds) to get on the bus directly, ignoring tickets altogether. Now the bus is overflowing with **thermally generated** electron-hole pairs, swamping the dopant signal. The original ticket system is irrelevant.

## 1.3 Three Canonical Temperature Regimes

| Regime | Temperature Range (Si / GaAs) | Dominant Mechanism | Carrier Density |
|:---|:---|:---|:---|
| **Freeze-out** | $T \ll T_D$ (e.g., < 50 K) | Dopants not fully ionized | $n \ll N_D$ |
| **Extrinsic (Saturation)** | $T \approx 100$–$450$ K | All dopants ionized; no intrinsic carriers | $n \approx N_D$ |
| **Intrinsic** | $T > 500$ K (Si); > 700 K (GaAs) | Thermal band-to-band generation dominates | $n = p = n_i$ |

> [!NOTE]
> **Why compound semiconductors are special:** Wide-bandgap materials (GaN: $E_g = 3.4$ eV, SiC: $E_g = 3.26$ eV) shift the **intrinsic region to much higher temperatures** (> 1000 K), making them ideal for **high-temperature electronics**. Narrow-gap materials (InSb: $E_g = 0.17$ eV) become intrinsic at cryogenic temperatures.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# 📗 SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Statistical Foundation — Fermi-Dirac Distribution

The probability that a state at energy $E$ is occupied by an electron is given by the **Fermi-Dirac distribution function**:

$$f(E) = \frac{1}{1 + \exp\left(\frac{E - E_F}{k_B T}\right)}$$

where $E_F$ is the **Fermi level** (chemical potential of electrons), $k_B = 8.617 \times 10^{-5}$ **eV/K** is Boltzmann's constant, and $T$ is absolute temperature.

For a donor level at energy $E_D$ just below $E_C$, the probability that the donor is **ionized** (i.e., has given up its electron) is:

$$f_D^+ = 1 - f(E_D) = \frac{1}{1 + \frac{1}{g_D}\exp\left(\frac{E_F - E_D}{k_B T}\right)}$$

where $g_D = 2$ is the **donor degeneracy factor** (spin degeneracy).

## 2.2 Charge Neutrality Condition (CNC)

The master equation governing carrier statistics in a non-degenerate, uniformly doped n-type semiconductor is:

$$n + N_A^- = p + N_D^+$$

For **n-type** (no intentional acceptors, $N_A = 0$):

$$n = p + N_D^+$$

For **p-type**:

$$p = n + N_A^-$$

## 2.3 Carrier Concentration in Each Regime

### 🔹 Regime I: Freeze-Out (Low T)
Dopants are only **partially ionized**; thermal energy $k_B T$ is insufficient to ionize all donors.

$$n = \sqrt{\frac{N_C N_D}{2}} \cdot \exp\left(-\frac{E_D}{2 k_B T}\right)$$

Here, $E_D = E_C - E_D$ is the **donor ionization energy** (typically 5–50 meV in III-V compounds).

### 🔹 Regime II: Extrinsic / Saturation (Mid T)
All donors fully ionized, intrinsic generation negligible.

$$\boxed{n \approx N_D \quad \text{(n-type)} \quad \text{or} \quad p \approx N_A \quad \text{(p-type)}}$$

The Fermi level sits very close to the band edge:
$$E_F = E_C - k_B T \ln\left(\frac{N_C}{N_D}\right)$$

### 🔹 Regime III: Intrinsic (High T)
Thermal generation across $E_g$ dominates.

$$n_i = \sqrt{N_C N_V} \cdot \exp\left(-\frac{E_g}{2 k_B T}\right)$$

$$n = p = n_i \gg N_D$$

## 2.4 Effective Density of States

$$N_C = 2\left(\frac{2\pi m_e^* k_B T}{h^2}\right)^{3/2}, \quad N_V = 2\left(\frac{2\pi m_h^* k_B T}{h^2}\right)^{3/2}$$

For GaAs at 300 K: $N_C = 4.7 \times 10^{17}$ **cm⁻³**, $N_V = 7.0 \times 10^{18}$ **cm⁻³**.

## 2.5 📊 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Symbol | Quantity | Formula | Units |
|:---:|:---|:---|:---:|
| $n$ | Electron concentration | $N_C \exp\left(-\frac{E_C - E_F}{k_B T}\right)$ | cm⁻³ |
| $p$ | Hole concentration | $N_V \exp\left(-\frac{E_F - E_V}{k_B T}\right)$ | cm⁻³ |
| $n_i$ | Intrinsic carrier density | $\sqrt{N_C N_V} \exp\left(-\frac{E_g}{2 k_B T}\right)$ | cm⁻³ |
| $N_C$ | Conduction band DOS | $2\left(\frac{2\pi m_e^* k_B T}{h^2}\right)^{3/2}$ | cm⁻³ |
| $N_V$ | Valence band DOS | $2\left(\frac{2\pi m_h^* k_B T}{h^2}\right)^{3/2}$ | cm⁻³ |
| $N_D^+$ | Ionized donors | $\frac{N_D}{1 + 2\exp\left(\frac{E_F - E_D}{k_B T}\right)}$ | cm⁻³ |
| $\rho$ | Resistivity | $\frac{1}{q(n\mu_n + p\mu_p)}$ | Ω·cm |
| $\mu$ | Mobility | $\mu_L \left[1 + \left(\frac{N_D}{N_{ref}}\right)^{0.5}\right]^{-1}$ (empirical) | cm²/(V·s) |

## 2.6 Mobility — Temperature Dependence

Carrier mobility in compound semiconductors is limited by **three primary scattering mechanisms**:

| Mechanism | Temperature Law | Physical Origin |
|:---|:---|:---|
| **Acoustic phonon** | $\mu_{ac} \propto T^{-3/2}$ | Lattice vibrations (deformation potential) |
| **Polar optical phonon** | $\mu_{pop} \propto T^{-1/2}$ (high $T$) | Dominant in **polar III-V** (GaAs, InP) |
| **Ionized impurity** | $\mu_{ii} \propto T^{3/2}$ | Coulomb scattering from $N_D^+$, $N_A^-$ |

The **net mobility** follows **Matthiessen's rule**:

$$\frac{1}{\mu} = \frac{1}{\mu_{ac}} + \frac{1}{\mu_{pop}} + \frac{1}{\mu_{ii}}$$

## 2.7 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **GaN HEMTs** in 5G base stations operate at 200°C junction temperature — knowing $\mu(T)$ prevents thermal runaway.
- **HgCdTe infrared detectors** are cooled to 77 K to suppress intrinsic generation that would otherwise swamp the photoresponse.
- **SiC power devices** in EVs leverage the wide $E_g$ to operate at junction temperatures up to 200°C without intrinsic takeover.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

# 📕 SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Carrier Concentration in the Freeze-Out Region

**Starting Premise:** At very low $T$, $n \ll N_D$ and $p \ll n$, so the CNC reduces to:

$$n \approx N_D^+$$

The number of ionized donors is:

$$N_D^+ = \frac{N_D}{1 + 2\exp\left(\frac{E_F - E_D}{k_B T}\right)}$$

For low temperature, the exponential term dominates, and $E_F$ lies **midway between $E_D$ and $E_C$** (a self-consistent result). Setting $f_D = 1/2$ gives $E_F \approx (E_C + E_D)/2$ when $n = N_D/2$.

**Step 1:** Equate $n$ and $N_D^+$:

$$n = \frac{N_D}{1 + 2\exp\left(\frac{E_F - E_D}{k_B T}\right)}$$

**Step 2:** Express $n$ in terms of $E_F$:

$$n = N_C \exp\left(-\frac{E_C - E_F}{k_B T}\right)$$

**Step 3:** Eliminate $E_F$ by combining. Define $E_D^* = E_C - E_D$ (ionization energy):

$$\exp\left(\frac{E_F - E_D}{k_B T}\right) = \exp\left(\frac{E_F - E_C + E_D^*}{k_B T}\right) = \frac{n}{N_C} \exp\left(\frac{E_D^*}{k_B T}\right)$$

**Step 4:** Substitute back into the ionization expression:

$$n = \frac{N_D}{1 + 2 \cdot \frac{n}{N_C} \exp\left(\frac{E_D^*}{k_B T}\right)}$$

**Step 5:** At low $T$, the exponential is huge, so $2n/N_C \cdot \exp(E_D^*/k_B T) \gg 1$, and the "1" in the denominator is negligible:

$$n \approx \frac{N_D \cdot N_C}{2 n} \cdot \exp\left(-\frac{E_D^*}{k_B T}\right)$$

**Step 6:** Multiply both sides by $n$:

$$n^2 = \frac{N_C N_D}{2} \exp\left(-\frac{E_D^*}{k_B T}\right)$$

**Step 7:** Take the square root:

$$\boxed{n_{freeze-out} = \sqrt{\frac{N_C N_D}{2}} \cdot \exp\left(-\frac{E_D^*}{2 k_B T}\right)}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Physical Interpretation:** The activation energy appears as $E_D^*/2$ (not $E_D^*$) because the freeze-out process is a **two-particle exchange**: the carrier must be thermally freed **and** a donor must be ionized. The exponent effectively reflects the **mid-gap position** of the Fermi level.

## 3.2 Derivation: Intrinsic Carrier Concentration

**Starting Premise:** In an intrinsic semiconductor, $n = p = n_i$. Mass action law gives $np = n_i^2$.

**Step 1:** Express $n$ and $p$:

$$n = N_C \exp\left(-\frac{E_C - E_F}{k_B T}\right), \quad p = N_V \exp\left(-\frac{E_F - E_V}{k_B T}\right)$$

**Step 2:** Form the product:

$$np = N_C N_V \exp\left(-\frac{E_C - E_V}{k_B T}\right) = N_C N_V \exp\left(-\frac{E_g}{k_B T}\right)$$

**Step 3:** By the mass action law, $np = n_i^2$:

$$n_i^2 = N_C N_V \exp\left(-\frac{E_g}{k_B T}\right)$$

**Step 4:** Take the square root:

$$\boxed{n_i = \sqrt{N_C N_V} \cdot \exp\left(-\frac{E_g}{2 k_B T}\right)}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why the $E_g/2$ exponent?** Because $n_i$ represents the **geometric mean** energy between $E_C$ and $E_V$, and the exponential activation is symmetric about the midgap. This is why the Fermi level in an intrinsic semiconductor lies at $E_i \approx E_g/2$ (modified slightly by the $\frac{3}{4} k_B T \ln(m_h^*/m_e^*)$ term).

## 3.3 Worked Numerical Example — Compound Semiconductor (GaAs)

**Problem:** A GaAs sample is doped with $N_D = 10^{15}$ cm⁻³ (Si donors). Calculate $n$ at $T = 300$ K, 77 K, and 600 K. Given: $E_g = 1.42$ eV, $E_D^* = 5.8$ meV, $N_C = 4.7 \times 10^{17}$ cm⁻³, $N_V = 7.0 \times 10^{18}$ cm⁻³.

### **Case 1: T = 300 K (Extrinsic Region)**

Since $k_B T = 0.0259$ eV $\gg E_D^* = 0.0058$ eV, donors are fully ionized.

$$n \approx N_D = 10^{15} \text{ cm}^{-3}$$

Verify intrinsic concentration is negligible:

$$n_i = \sqrt{(4.7 \times 10^{17})(7.0 \times 10^{18})} \exp\left(-\frac{1.42}{2 \times 0.0259}\right)$$

$$n_i = \sqrt{3.29 \times 10^{36}} \cdot \exp(-27.41) = 1.81 \times 10^{18} \cdot 1.16 \times 10^{-12}$$

$$n_i \approx 2.1 \times 10^{6} \text{ cm}^{-3} \ll N_D \;\; \checkmark$$

### **Case 2: T = 77 K (Freeze-Out Region)**

$$k_B T = 77 \times 8.617 \times 10^{-5} = 6.64 \times 10^{-3} \text{ eV}$$

$$n = \sqrt{\frac{(4.7 \times 10^{17})(10^{15})}{2}} \cdot \exp\left(-\frac{0.0058}{2 \times 0.00664}\right)$$

$$n = \sqrt{2.35 \times 10^{32}} \cdot \exp(-0.437) = 1.53 \times 10^{16} \cdot 0.646$$

$$\boxed{n \approx 9.9 \times 10^{15} \text{ cm}^{-3}}$$

**Interpretation:** Even at liquid nitrogen temperature, GaAs is *near* full ionization due to its small $E_D^*$.

### **Case 3: T = 600 K (Approaching Intrinsic)**

$$k_B T = 600 \times 8.617 \times 10^{-5} = 0.0517 \text{ eV}$$

Recalculate $N_C(600K) = N_C(300K) \cdot (600/300)^{3/2} = 4.7 \times 10^{17} \cdot 2.83 = 1.33 \times 10^{18}$ cm⁻³.

$$n_i = \sqrt{(1.33 \times 10^{18})(1.98 \times 10^{19})} \cdot \exp\left(-\frac{1.42}{2 \times 0.0517}\right)$$

$$n_i = \sqrt{2.63 \times 10^{37}} \cdot \exp(-13.73) = 5.13 \times 10^{18} \cdot 1.09 \times 10^{-6}$$

$$n_i \approx 5.6 \times 10^{12} \text{ cm}^{-3} \ll N_D \;\; \text{still extrinsic!}$$

**This demonstrates why GaAs is superior to Si for high-temperature operation:** Even at 600 K, it remains extrinsic.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

# 📒 SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Flowchart: Temperature Regime Decision Logic

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Start: Given T and N_D] --> B{T < T_freeze?}
    B -- Yes --> C[Freeze-Out Regime]
    C --> C1[Use n = sqrt N_C N_D / 2 exp -E_D / 2kT]
    C1 --> Z[Compute n freeze]
    B -- No --> D{T < T_intrinsic?}
    D -- Yes --> E[Extrinsic Saturation Regime]
    E --> E1[Use n approx N_D]
    E1 --> Y[Compute n = N_D]
    D -- No --> F[Intrinsic Regime]
    F --> F1[Compute n_i = sqrt N_C N_V exp -E_g / 2kT]
    F1 --> X[n = p = n_i]
```

## 4.2 Block Diagram: Carrier Concentration vs Temperature (n-type)

```mermaid
graph LR
    subgraph LOW[Low T]
        A1[Freeze-Out: n rises as exp -E_D / 2kT]
    end
    subgraph MID[Mid T - 100K to 500K]
        A2[Extrinsic Plateau: n = N_D constant]
    end
    subgraph HIGH[High T]
        A3[Intrinsic Rise: n_i grows as exp -E_g / 2kT]
    end
    LOW --> MID
    MID --> HIGH
    A1 --> A2
    A2 --> A3
```

## 4.3 Schematic: Energy Band Diagram with Dopant Ionization

```mermaid
graph TB
    subgraph CB[Conduction Band E_C]
        EC[E_C - empty states]
    end
    subgraph DL[Donor Level]
        ED[E_D - 5 to 50 meV below E_C]
    end
    subgraph VB[Valence Band E_V]
        EV[E_V - filled states]
    end
    subgraph FERMI[E_F Position vs T]
        EF1[Low T: E_F near E_D]
        EF2[Mid T: E_F near E_C]
        EF3[High T: E_F at midgap]
    end
    ED -.ionization.-> EC
    EV -.thermal generation.-> EC
```

## 4.4 Block Architecture: Compensation Doping in Compound Semiconductors

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph HOST[Compound Semiconductor Host]
        H[III-V or II-VI Lattice]
    end
    subgraph DONORS[Donor Dopants n-type]
        D1[Group IV on III-site Si on Ga]
        D2[Group VI on V-site Te on As]
    end
    subgraph ACCEPTORS[Acceptor Dopants p-type]
        A1[Group II on III-site Zn on Ga]
        A2[Group IV on V-site Si on As]
    end
    subgraph AMPH[Amphoteric Behavior]
        AM1[Si on Ga: donor]
        AM2[Si on As: acceptor]
    end
    H --> DONORS
    H --> ACCEPTORS
    H --> AMPH
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Insight:** The amphoteric nature of **Si in GaAs** is a critical problem — depending on whether Si occupies a Ga site (donor) or As site (acceptor), the net doping can flip. Crystal growers use **LPE, MBE, or MOCVD** with careful stoichiometry control to favor one site.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->

# 📙 SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 📝 Part A: Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Question 1** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Module 2]`
**Q: Define the freeze-out and extrinsic regions in a doped semiconductor. What distinguishes them physically?**

**Model Answer:**

The **freeze-out region** occurs at very low temperatures where thermal energy $k_B T$ is insufficient to ionize all donor (or acceptor) impurities. The carrier concentration is given by:

$$n = \sqrt{\frac{N_C N_D}{2}} \exp\left(-\frac{E_D}{2k_B T}\right)$$

In this region, $n$ is **exponentially dependent** on temperature and $n \ll N_D$.

The **extrinsic (saturation) region** occurs at moderate temperatures where $k_B T \gg E_D$. All dopants are fully ionized, and the carrier concentration saturates at the doping level: $n \approx N_D$. This region is **temperature-insensitive** and is where most devices operate.

**Physical distinction:** In freeze-out, dopants are *partially* ionized (bound to their parent atoms); in extrinsic, dopants are *fully* ionized (each has donated its electron to the conduction band).

---

### **Question 2** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024, Module 2]`
**Q: Why do wide-bandgap compound semiconductors remain extrinsic at higher temperatures than silicon?**

**Model Answer:**

Wide-bandgap materials like **GaN ($E_g = 3.4$ eV)** and **SiC ($E_g = 3.26$ eV)** have a much larger energy gap than Si ($E_g = 1.12$ eV). The intrinsic carrier concentration depends on temperature as:

$$n_i \propto \exp\left(-\frac{E_g}{2k_B T}\right)$$

For $n_i$ to equal the doping concentration $N_D$ (the transition to intrinsic behavior), the temperature must satisfy:

$$k_B T \approx \frac{E_g}{2 \ln(N_C N_V / N_D^2)}$$

Since $E_g$ is 3× larger in GaN than in Si, the **transition temperature is shifted to much higher values** (~1000 K for GaN vs ~570 K for Si). This makes wide-bandgap compound semiconductors ideal for **high-temperature, high-power electronics** (e.g., EV inverters, aerospace systems).

---

## 5.2 📝 Part B: Long-Answer Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

### ⭐ **Question A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2022, Module 2]`

**(a)** Derive the expression for the intrinsic carrier concentration $n_i$ in a semiconductor. Show that the Fermi level in an intrinsic semiconductor lies at the midgap, modified by the effective mass ratio. **[7 Marks]**

**(b)** A GaAs sample is doped with $N_D = 5 \times 10^{15}$ cm⁻³. Given: $E_g = 1.42$ eV, $m_e^* = 0.067 m_0$, $m_h^* = 0.45 m_0$, $m_0 = 9.11 \times 10^{-31}$ kg. Calculate (i) $N_C$ and $N_V$ at 300 K, (ii) $n_i$ at 300 K, and (iii) the position of the Fermi level at 300 K. **[7 Marks]**

#### **Model Solution:**

**(a) Derivation of $n_i$:**

In an intrinsic semiconductor, $n = p = n_i$. Using the mass action law:

$$np = n_i^2$$

Substituting the Boltzmann expressions:

$$N_C \exp\left(-\frac{E_C - E_F}{k_B T}\right) \cdot N_V \exp\left(-\frac{E_F - E_V}{k_B T}\right) = n_i^2$$

$$N_C N_V \exp\left(-\frac{E_g}{k_B T}\right) = n_i^2$$

$$\boxed{n_i = \sqrt{N_C N_V} \exp\left(-\frac{E_g}{2k_B T}\right)}$$

Setting $n = p$:

$$N_C \exp\left(-\frac{E_C - E_F}{k_B T}\right) = N_V \exp\left(-\frac{E_F - E_V}{k_B T}\right)$$

Taking the natural log and rearranging:

$$E_F = \frac{E_C + E_V}{2} + \frac{1}{2} k_B T \ln\left(\frac{N_V}{N_C}\right)$$

$$\boxed{E_i = \frac{E_g}{2} + \frac{3}{4} k_B T \ln\left(\frac{m_h^*}{m_e^*}\right)}$$

**(b) Numerical Calculation:**

**(i)** Using $N_C = 2\left(\frac{2\pi m_e^* k_B T}{h^2}\right)^{3/2}$:

- $m_e^* = 0.067 \times 9.11 \times 10^{-31} = 6.10 \times 10^{-32}$ kg
- $k_B T = 0.0259$ eV $= 4.14 \times 10^{-21}$ J
- $h = 6.626 \times 10^{-34}$ J·s

$$\frac{2\pi m_e^* k_B T}{h^2} = \frac{2\pi (6.10 \times 10^{-32})(4.14 \times 10^{-21})}{(6.626 \times 10^{-34})^2} = 3.55 \times 10^{15} \text{ m}^{-2}$$

$$N_C = 2 (3.55 \times 10^{15})^{3/2} = 2 (2.11 \times 10^{23}) = 4.23 \times 10^{23} \text{ m}^{-3}$$

$$\boxed{N_C \approx 4.2 \times 10^{17} \text{ cm}^{-3}}$$

Similarly for $N_V$ with $m_h^* = 0.45 m_0$:

$$\boxed{N_V \approx 7.0 \times 10^{18} \text{ cm}^{-3}}$$

**(ii)** Intrinsic carrier concentration:

$$n_i = \sqrt{(4.2 \times 10^{17})(7.0 \times 10^{18})} \exp\left(-\frac{1.42}{2 \times 0.0259}\right)$$

$$n_i = \sqrt{2.94 \times 10^{36}} \cdot \exp(-27.41) = 1.71 \times 10^{18} \cdot 1.16 \times 10^{-12}$$

$$\boxed{n_i \approx 2.0 \times 10^{6} \text{ cm}^{-3}}$$

**(iii)** Fermi level position (n-type, $n \approx N_D = 5 \times 10^{15}$ cm⁻³):

$$E_C - E_F = k_B T \ln\left(\frac{N_C}{N_D}\right) = 0.0259 \cdot \ln\left(\frac{4.2 \times 10^{17}}{5 \times 10^{15}}\right)$$

$$E_C - E_F = 0.0259 \cdot \ln(84) = 0.0259 \cdot 4.43 = 0.115 \text{ eV}$$

$$\boxed{E_F \text{ is } 0.115 \text{ eV below } E_C}$$

---

### ⭐ **Question B (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - May 2023, Module 2]`

**(a)** With a neat sketch, explain the variation of carrier concentration $\log n$ versus $1/T$ for an n-type semiconductor showing the freeze-out, extrinsic, and intrinsic regions. **[7 Marks]**

**(b)** Discuss the temperature dependence of carrier mobility in compound semiconductors. Explain why polar optical phonon scattering dominates in III-V materials like GaAs at high temperatures. **[7 Marks]**

#### **Model Solution:**

**(a) The $\log n$ vs $1/T$ Plot:**

The carrier concentration in the three regimes yields three distinct slopes on an Arrhenius-type plot:

| Region | Slope of $\log n$ vs $1/T$ | Activation Energy |
|:---|:---:|:---:|
| Freeze-out | $-\frac{E_D}{2 k_B}$ | $E_D/2$ |
| Extrinsic | $\approx 0$ (flat plateau) | — |
| Intrinsic | $-\frac{E_g}{2 k_B}$ | $E_g/2$ |

```
   log n  |                  /
          |                 /  (Intrinsic: slope = -E_g/2k)
          |                /
          |---------------/---------------- N_D (extrinsic plateau)
          |              /
          |             / (Freeze-out: slope = -E_D/2k)
          |____________/_________________
                            1/T →
   (low T)                          (high T)
```

**Key transitions:**
- **Freeze-out → Extrinsic:** Occurs near $T \approx E_D/k_B$
- **Extrinsic → Intrinsic:** Occurs when $n_i(T) = N_D$, giving:

$$T_{crit} \approx \frac{E_g}{2 k_B \ln(N_C N_V / N_D^2)}$$

**(b) Mobility Temperature Dependence:**

The three dominant scattering mechanisms combine via Matthiessen's rule:

$$\frac{1}{\mu} = \frac{1}{\mu_{ac}} + \frac{1}{\mu_{pop}} + \frac{1}{\mu_{ii}}$$

- **Acoustic phonon scattering** ($\mu_{ac} \propto T^{-3/2}$): Caused by lattice vibrations modulating the bandgap.
- **Ionized impurity scattering** ($\mu_{ii} \propto T^{3/2}$): Coulomb interaction with $N_D^+$, $N_A^-$. Dominant at **low T**.
- **Polar optical phonon (POP) scattering** ($\mu_{pop} \propto T^{-1/2}$ at high $T$): Dominant in **polar III-V semiconductors** (GaAs, InP, GaN) at high T.

**Why POP dominates in GaAs:**

In III-V compounds, the **two atoms in the unit cell have different electronegativities**, creating a permanent **electric dipole moment** (Born effective charge). When the lattice vibrates (optical phonons), this dipole oscillates and scatters carriers via long-range Coulomb interaction. Since GaAs has a strong bond polarity (electronegativity difference $\sim 0.4$), POP scattering is the **primary high-T mobility limiter**, giving GaAs a peak mobility of ~8500 cm²/(V·s) at 300 K, dropping rapidly with T.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **🚨 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Forgetting the factor of 2 in the freeze-out formula:** Many students write $n = \sqrt{N_C N_D} \exp(-E_D/2k_B T)$ without the $\sqrt{2}$ in the denominator. The 2 arises from spin degeneracy — **lose 1 mark** if missed.
> 2. **Confusing $E_D$ with $E_C - E_D$:** Always define $E_D^* = E_C - E_D$ explicitly to avoid sign errors.
> 3. **Units inconsistency:** $k_B T$ must be in **eV** when used with $E_g$ in eV, but in **Joules** when using the DOS formula. Mixing units costs 1–2 marks.
> 4. **Neglecting $N_V$ in the intrinsic formula:** $n_i = \sqrt{N_C N_V}$ is correct; the common wrong form $n_i = N_C \exp(-E_g/2k_B T)$ is missing the $\sqrt{N_V/N_C}$ factor.
> 5. **Not stating "non-degenerate" assumption:** The Boltzmann approximation is used — explicitly mention $E_C - E_F \gg k_B T$ in your solution.

---

## 5.3 🎯 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- ✅ **Three temperature regimes** — Freeze-out, Extrinsic, Intrinsic — are the backbone of this topic.
- ✅ **Activation energy in freeze-out** is $E_D/2$, **not** $E_D$ (charge exchange symmetry).
- ✅ **Activation energy in intrinsic** is $E_g/2$, **not** $E_g$ (midgap Fermi level).
- ✅ **Mass action law** $np = n_i^2$ is always valid in non-degenerate thermal equilibrium.
- ✅ **Charge neutrality condition** $n + N_A^- = p + N_D^+$ is the master equation — write it first in every problem.
- ✅ **Wide-bandgap compound semiconductors** (GaN, SiC) have a much higher intrinsic onset temperature than Si.
- ✅ **Amphoteric dopants** (Si in GaAs) can occupy both sublattices — stoichiometry control is critical.
- ✅ **Matthiessen's rule** combines scattering mechanisms: $1/\mu = \sum 1/\mu_i$.
- ✅ **Polar optical phonon scattering** is unique to polar III-V materials and dominates at high T.
- ✅ **Effective density of states** $N_C, N_V$ scales as $T^{3/2}$ — always recalculate when T changes.
- ✅ **Self-compensation** in wide-bandgap compounds limits maximum achievable free carrier density.
- ✅ **Fermi level position** moves from near $E_D$ (freeze-out) → near band edge (extrinsic) → midgap (intrinsic) as T increases.

> [!TIP]
> **Last-Minute KTU Mnemonic — "FED-ICE":** **F**reeze-out → **E**xtrinsic → **D**eep states → **I**ntrinsic → **C**ompensation → **E**xhaustion. This is the full sequence of regimes in order of increasing T for an n-type compound semiconductor.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Heterojunction Bipolar Transistors: Principle of Operation
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEEVT413 - COMPOUND SEMICONDUCTORS
> - **Module:** Module Module 2
> - **Topic:** Heterojunction Bipolar Transistors: Principle of Operation

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

A **Heterojunction Bipolar Transistor (HBT)** is a three-terminal semiconductor amplifying device belonging to the bipolar transistor family, in which **at least one of the two p-n junctions is a heterojunction** — that is, a junction formed between two dissimilar semiconductor materials having different energy bandgaps. The most widely used configuration is the **N-p-N single heterojunction bipolar transistor (SHBT)**, in which the **emitter-base (E-B) junction is a heterojunction** and the **base-collector (B-C) junction is a homojunction**. A double heterojunction bipolar transistor (DHBT) employs wide-bandgap materials on both emitter and collector sides.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** The HBT principle of operation rests on the engineering of the **wide-bandgap emitter**. By choosing a wider-bandgap emitter (e.g., AlₓGa₁₋ₓAs) and a narrower-bandgap base (e.g., GaAs), the transistor decouples the trade-off between **emitter injection efficiency** and **base series resistance**, enabling simultaneous high doping in the base and very high current gain — something fundamentally impossible in a homojunction BJT.

The HBT exploits the **discontinuities in the conduction band (ΔE_C)** and **valence band (ΔE_V)** that arise at the heterointerface. These discontinuities are governed by the **Anderson's affinity rule**:

$$\Delta E_C = \chi_{\text{base}} - \chi_{\text{emitter}}$$

$$\Delta E_V = \Delta E_G - \Delta E_C$$

where χ is the **electron affinity** and ΔE_G is the total bandgap difference between the two semiconductors.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy: The "One-Way Turnstile" at a Metro Station**
> Imagine a metro station turnstile. In a normal homojunction BJT, the turnstile is symmetric — both directions (electrons forward, holes backward) are equally easy to pass. To stop the reverse leakage, the station master (engineer) must slow down *everyone*, which restricts the main passenger flow. The result: a sluggish system with low current gain.
>
> In an **HBT**, the engineer installs a **taller, heavier barrier on one side only** (the valence-band offset in the emitter). Passengers (electrons) coming from the wider-bandgap emitter can still cross easily because the conduction band step is small, but anyone trying to sneak back the other way (holes from base to emitter) is blocked. This asymmetric "one-way turnstile" lets the engineer pack *more passengers into the base* (heavy base doping) without losing the forward flow, yielding **very high current gain and ultra-high-speed operation**.

### 1.3 Key Physical Constants and Metrics

- **Thermal voltage (V_T)** at 300 K: **V_T = kT/q ≈ 25.85 mV**
- **Bandgap of GaAs (300 K): E_G = 1.42 eV**
- **Bandgap of Al₀.₃Ga₀.₇As (300 K): E_G ≈ 1.81 eV**
- **Electron affinity of GaAs: χ ≈ 4.07 eV**
- **Electron affinity of Al₀.₃Ga₀.₇As: χ ≈ 3.72 eV**
- **Effective density of states in GaAs conduction band (N_C) ≈ 4.7 × 10¹⁷ cm⁻³**
- **Effective density of states in GaAs valence band (N_V) ≈ 7.0 × 10¹⁸ cm⁻³**
- **Intrinsic carrier concentration of GaAs at 300 K: n_i ≈ 1.79 × 10⁶ cm⁻³**

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Structural Composition of an N-p-N HBT

The standard **N-p-N AlGaAs/GaAs HBT** consists of the following epitaxial layers grown on a semi-insulating (S.I.) GaAs substrate:

| Layer | Material | Doping (cm⁻³) | Thickness | Function |
|-------|----------|---------------|-----------|----------|
| Emitter Cap | n⁺-GaAs | ~10¹⁹ | ~0.2 µm | Ohmic contact layer |
| Emitter | n-Al₀.₃Ga₀₇As | ~5×10¹⁷ | ~0.1 µm | Wide-bandgap emitter |
| Base | p⁺-GaAs | ~10¹⁹ – 10²⁰ | ~0.05–0.1 µm | Thin, heavily doped base |
| Collector | n-GaAs | ~10¹⁶ | ~0.5–1 µm | Drift region |
| Sub-collector | n⁺-GaAs | ~10¹⁹ | ~0.5 µm | Ohmic contact layer |
| Substrate | S.I. GaAs | — | ~400 µm | Mechanical support |

> [!NOTE]
> **Why is the base heavily doped in an HBT?**
> In a homojunction BJT, raising the base doping forces the emitter doping to rise correspondingly to maintain injection efficiency — a self-defeating cycle. In an HBT, the wide-bandgap emitter *isolates* the back-injection of holes, so the base can be doped to **10¹⁹ – 10²⁰ cm⁻³** while the emitter is only moderately doped (~10¹⁷ cm⁻³). This gives a **very low base sheet resistance (R_b)** of **100–500 Ω/□**, directly improving the **maximum oscillation frequency (f_max)**.

### 2.2 Energy Band Diagram at Equilibrium

At thermal equilibrium, the Fermi level (E_F) must be constant across the entire structure. Because the bandgaps of AlGaAs and GaAs differ, the **conduction and valence bands bend discontinuously** at the heterointerface:

- The conduction band shows a **spike + notch** feature: a small spike of height **ΔE_C** followed by a notch of depth ΔE_C on the GaAs side.
- The valence band shows a step discontinuity of **ΔE_V** that acts as a **barrier to holes** trying to move from the p-type base into the n-type wide-bandgap emitter.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU High-Yield Concept:** The valence-band discontinuity **ΔE_V** is the *physical origin* of the HBT's superior injection efficiency. The larger ΔE_V is (for a given ΔE_G), the more effectively holes are blocked from re-entering the emitter.

### 2.3 Operating Bias Conditions (Active Region)

| Junction | Bias | Carrier Action |
|----------|------|----------------|
| E-B Junction | **Forward biased** (~1.2–1.4 V for AlGaAs/GaAs) | Electrons injected from wide-E_g emitter into narrow-E_g base |
| B-C Junction | **Reverse biased** (~2–5 V) | Electrons swept from base into collector; depletion region widens in collector |

### 2.4 Current Components in an HBT

Four terminal currents exist in an HBT:

- **I_nE** — electron current injected from emitter into base (the *useful* signal)
- **I_pE** — hole current injected from base into emitter (the *parasitic* back-injection; suppressed by ΔE_V)
- **I_nC** — electron current at the collector terminal (≈ I_nE × α_T, where α_T is the base transport factor)
- **I_pC** — hole reverse saturation current at the B-C junction (very small)

### 2.5 Emitter Injection Efficiency (γ)

The emitter injection efficiency is defined as:

$$\gamma = \frac{I_{nE}}{I_{nE} + I_{pE}}$$

In a homojunction BJT:

$$\gamma_{\text{homo}} = \frac{D_n N_D L_p}{D_p N_A L_n}$$

This forces the constraint **N_D >> N_A**, limiting the base doping.

In an HBT, the ratio of electron to hole injection is modified by the **heterojunction factors** H_n and H_p:

$$\gamma_{\text{HBT}} = \frac{H_n \, D_n N_D}{H_n \, D_n N_D + H_p \, D_p n_{i,B}^2 / n_{i,E}^2 \cdot (N_A / W_B)}$$

The key is that the term **n_{i,B}² / n_{i,E}²** is *exponentially* suppressed by the bandgap difference:

$$\frac{n_{i,B}^2}{n_{i,E}^2} \propto \exp\left(\frac{\Delta E_G}{kT}\right)$$

For AlGaAs/GaAs with ΔE_G ≈ 0.39 eV at 300 K, this factor is ≈ **3 × 10⁻⁷**, which means γ can be **> 0.99** even with the base doped **two orders of magnitude higher** than the emitter.

### 2.6 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following table consolidates every high-yield formula required for the KTU End-Semester Examination (ESE) on the HBT principle of operation.

| # | Quantity | Formula | Variables / Units | Physical Meaning |
|---|----------|---------|-------------------|------------------|
| 1 | Anderson's Rule (Conduction band offset) | $\Delta E_C = \chi_B - \chi_E$ | eV | Discontinuity at heterointerface |
| 2 | Anderson's Rule (Valence band offset) | $\Delta E_V = E_{G,B} - E_{G,E} - \Delta E_C$ | eV | Hole barrier at heterointerface |
| 3 | Total bandgap difference | $\Delta E_G = \Delta E_C + \Delta E_V$ | eV | Energy step |
| 4 | Intrinsic carrier density ratio | $n_{i,E}^2 / n_{i,B}^2 = \exp[(E_{G,B} - E_{G,E})/kT]$ | dimensionless | Origin of high γ |
| 5 | Emitter injection efficiency | $\gamma = I_{nE} / (I_{nE} + I_{pE})$ | dimensionless | Fraction of useful emitter current |
| 6 | Base transport factor | $\alpha_T \approx 1 - W_B^2 / (2 L_n^2)$ | dimensionless | Recombination loss in base |
| 7 | Common-base current gain | $\alpha = \gamma \cdot \alpha_T$ | dimensionless | — |
| 8 | Common-emitter current gain | $\beta = \alpha / (1 - \alpha)$ | dimensionless | Amplification factor |
| 9 | Emitter current | $I_E = I_{nE} + I_{pE} = I_{ES} \left[\exp(V_{BE}/V_T) - 1\right]$ | A | Forward-bias E-B current |
| 10 | Collector current | $I_C = \alpha I_E + I_{CBO}$ | A | Output current |
| 11 | Base current | $I_B = (1 - \alpha) I_E - I_{CBO}$ | A | Control terminal current |
| 12 | Base sheet resistance | $R_{b,\text{sheet}} = 1 / (q \mu_p N_A W_B)$ | Ω/□ | Determines f_max |
| 13 | Transit-time (base) | $\tau_B = W_B^2 / (2 D_n)$ | s | Diffusion transit time |
| 14 | Cutoff frequency (approx.) | $f_T \approx 1 / (2\pi \tau_{EC})$ | Hz | Maximum useful frequency |
| 15 | Emitter Bandgap (Al_xGa_{1-x}As) | $E_G(x) = 1.424 + 1.247x$ (eV, x ≤ 0.45) | eV | Composition-dependent bandgap |
| 16 | Graded-emitter spike elimination | $E_C \text{ must begraded over L_G \sim 30 nm}$ | nm | Avoids current-blocking spike |

> **Notation used in table:** V_T = thermal voltage (≈ 25.85 mV at 300 K); D_n, D_p = minority carrier diffusion coefficients; N_A, N_D = acceptor/donor densities; W_B = base width; L_n = electron diffusion length; I_{CBO} = reverse leakage current.

### 2.7 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Wireless Power Amplifiers (PAs):** GaAs HBTs dominate cellular handset PAs (2G/3G/4G/5G) due to high power-added efficiency (PAE > 65%), high linearity, and single-supply operation.
- **High-Speed Digital-to-Analog Converters (DACs):** InP HBTs are used in 100+ Gs/s DACs for optical communications.
- **SiGe HBTs in BiCMOS:** Integrated with CMOS for mm-wave transceivers (24–77 GHz automotive radar, 5G FR2).
- **Optoelectronic Integrated Circuits (OEICs):** InP/InGaAs HBTs co-integrated with photodetectors/lasers for 100 GbE/400 GbE transceivers.

## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation I: Anderson's Affinity Rule for an AlGaAs/GaAs HBT

The goal is to compute **ΔE_C** and **ΔE_V** at the emitter-base heterojunction.

**Step 1: Define the energy reference.**

The vacuum level E_vac is the universal reference. The electron affinity χ is the energy difference between E_vac and the conduction band edge E_C at the surface of a semiconductor:

$$\chi = E_{\text{vac}} - E_C$$

**Step 2: Apply the alignment condition.**

At thermodynamic equilibrium, the vacuum level is continuous across the heterointerface (assuming no interface dipoles). Therefore the conduction band edge in the emitter (E_C^E) and base (E_C^B) are related by:

$$E_C^E - E_C^B = \chi_B - \chi_E$$

Rearranging, the conduction-band offset is:

$$\Delta E_C = E_C^B - E_C^E = \chi_B - \chi_E$$

**Step 3: Compute the valence-band offset.**

The valence band edge in any semiconductor is offset from the conduction band by the bandgap E_G. Thus:

$$\Delta E_V = (E_V^B - E_V^E) = (E_C^B - E_C^E) + (E_G^E - E_G^B)$$

$$\Delta E_V = \Delta E_C - \Delta E_G$$

Since ΔE_G = E_G^B − E_G^E for an N-emitter/P-base HBT, we obtain:

$$\Delta E_V = \Delta E_C - \Delta E_G \quad \text{or equivalently} \quad \Delta E_V = E_G^E - E_G^B - (\chi_B - \chi_E)$$

**Step 4: Numerical evaluation for Al₀.₃Ga₀.₇As / GaAs.**

- For GaAs: χ_GaAs = 4.07 eV, E_G,GaAs = 1.42 eV
- For Al₀.₃Ga₀.₇As: χ_AlGaAs ≈ 3.72 eV (decreases with x), E_G,AlGaAs ≈ 1.81 eV

$$\Delta E_C = 4.07 - 3.72 = 0.35 \text{ eV}$$

$$\Delta E_V = (1.81 - 1.42) - 0.35 = 0.04 \text{ eV}$$

This shows that **most of the bandgap difference appears in the conduction band** for the (Al,Ga)As system — a fact exploited for **graded-emitter** designs.

### 3.2 Derivation II: Emitter Injection Efficiency of an HBT

**Step 1: Write electron current density injected from emitter into base.**

Under forward bias V_BE, the electron diffusion current density in the base at the edge x = 0 (just inside the base) is:

$$J_{nE} = q D_n \frac{n_{i,B}^2}{N_A W_B} \left[\exp\left(\frac{V_{BE}}{V_T}\right) - 1\right]$$

**Step 2: Write hole current density injected from base into emitter.**

$$J_{pE} = q D_p \frac{n_{i,E}^2}{N_D L_p} \left[\exp\left(\frac{V_{BE}}{V_T}\right) - 1\right]$$

**Step 3: Form the ratio J_nE / J_pE.**

$$\frac{J_{nE}}{J_{pE}} = \frac{D_n N_D L_p}{D_p N_A W_B} \cdot \frac{n_{i,B}^2}{n_{i,E}^2}$$

**Step 4: Substitute the bandgap-dependent ratio of intrinsic carrier densities.**

Using $n_i^2 \propto T^3 \exp(-E_G/kT)$, we get:

$$\frac{n_{i,B}^2}{n_{i,E}^2} = \frac{N_{C,B} N_{V,B}}{N_{C,E} N_{V,E}} \exp\left(\frac{\Delta E_G}{kT}\right)$$

For the AlGaAs/GaAs system, the pre-exponential ratio is close to unity, so the exponential term dominates.

**Step 5: Determine γ.**

$$\gamma = \frac{1}{1 + (J_{pE}/J_{nE})}$$

Using ΔE_G = 0.39 eV (for x = 0.3) and kT = 0.0259 eV:

$$\exp(\Delta E_G / kT) = \exp(0.39/0.0259) = \exp(15.06) \approx 3.4 \times 10^6$$

Therefore, even if the base is doped **100× higher** than the emitter, the heterojunction term overwhelms the doping ratio, giving γ ≈ 0.9997.

### 3.3 Derivation III: Base Transit Time and Cutoff Frequency

**Step 1: Solve the minority-electron continuity equation in the base.**

For a uniformly doped p-type base with electron diffusion only:

$$D_n \frac{\partial^2 \Delta n}{\partial x^2} - \frac{\Delta n}{\tau_n} + G = \frac{\partial \Delta n}{\partial t}$$

In the small-signal regime with no generation, the steady-state solution across a base of width W_B with boundary conditions Δn(0) = n_{p0} exp(V_BE/V_T) and Δn(W_B) = 0 is parabolic:

$$\Delta n(x) = \Delta n(0) \left(1 - \frac{x}{W_B}\right)$$

**Step 2: Compute the stored minority-electron charge in the base.**

$$Q_n = q A \int_0^{W_B} \Delta n(x)\, dx = \frac{1}{2} q A W_B \Delta n(0)$$

**Step 3: Compute the base transit time τ_B.**

By definition, τ_B = Q_n / I_{nE}:

$$I_{nE} = q A D_n \frac{d\Delta n}{dx}\bigg|_{x=0} = \frac{q A D_n \Delta n(0)}{W_B}$$

$$\tau_B = \frac{Q_n}{I_{nE}} = \frac{\frac{1}{2} q A W_B \Delta n(0)}{q A D_n \Delta n(0)/W_B} = \frac{W_B^2}{2 D_n}$$

**Step 4: Total emitter-to-collector delay.**

The total delay τ_EC includes the emitter charging time (τ_E = r_E C_jE), the base transit time τ_B, and the collector depletion-region transit time τ_C = W_C / (2 v_sat):

$$\tau_{EC} = \tau_E + \tau_B + \tau_C$$

**Step 5: Cutoff frequency.**

$$f_T = \frac{1}{2 \pi \tau_{EC}}$$

For an AlGaAs/GaAs HBT with W_B = 50 nm, D_n = 200 cm²/s:
- τ_B = (50×10⁻⁷)² / (2 × 200) = 6.25 ps
- With τ_E ≈ 2 ps, τ_C ≈ 3 ps → τ_EC ≈ 11.25 ps → **f_T ≈ 14 GHz**

This is a *textbook* example value; production HBTs exceed **f_T = 500 GHz** (InP-based).

### 3.4 Derivation IV: Kirk Effect / Base Push-Out in HBTs

At high collector current densities, the mobile electron charge in the collector depletion region becomes comparable to the ionized donor density N_D^C, shifting the maximum electric field from the B-C metallurgical junction into the collector. The **effective base width** then grows by an amount:

$$\Delta W_B^{\text{Kirk}} = W_C \left(1 - \sqrt{1 - \frac{J_C}{q N_D^C v_{\text{sat}}}}\right)$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU High-Yield:** The Kirk effect limits the maximum usable current density before β and f_T degrade. HBTs with graded or doped collectors are designed to push this limit upward.

### 3.5 Analytical Matrix: Performance Comparison HBT vs. BJT vs. HEMT

| Parameter | Si BJT | SiGe HBT | GaAs HBT | InP HBT | GaAs HEMT |
|-----------|--------|----------|----------|---------|-----------|
| f_T (GHz) | 30–60 | 300 | 200 | 500+ | 150 |
| f_max (GHz) | 30 | 500 | 300 | 700+ | 250 |
| Breakdown voltage | High | Medium | High | Low | Medium |
| Base sheet resistance (Ω/□) | 1k–5k | 200 | 150 | 100 | N/A |
| PAE (%) | 50 | 55 | 65 | 60 | 70 |
| 1/f noise corner | Low | Low | Medium | Medium | High |
| Integration with CMOS | Yes | Yes | No | No | No |
| Substrate | Si | Si | GaAs | InP | GaAs/SiC |

## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture of an HBT (Mermaid Block Diagram)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    A[DC Bias Network: V_BE Forward, V_CB Reverse] --> B[Emitter Terminal: N+ AlGaAs]
    B --> C[Emitter-Base Heterojunction: ΔE_C, ΔE_V]
    C --> D[Base Region: P+ GaAs, W_B ~ 50-100 nm]
    D --> E[Base-Collector Homojunction: Reverse Biased]
    E --> F[Collector Region: N- GaAs Drift Layer]
    F --> G[Sub-Collector N+ Contact and Output Terminal]
    H[Base Ohmic Contact: Low R_b] --> D
    I[Input Signal V_in] --> C
    G --> J[Output Signal V_out Amplified]
    D --> K[Recombination Current: I_B Recomb]
    K --> L[Base Current I_B Output]
    C --> M[Heterojunction Spike ΔE_C at Conduction Band]
    M --> N[Spike Mitigation by Graded Emitter over L_G ~ 30 nm]
```

> **Figure interpretation:**
> - The **left-to-right arrow chain** (A → B → C → D → E → F → G) represents the **electron path** from emitter to collector in the active region.
> - **H** injects the base bias, and **K → L** shows the recombination current that defines I_B.
> - **M → N** captures the engineering trick of *grading the Al composition* at the heterointerface to suppress the conduction-band spike that would otherwise block electrons at low V_BE.

### 4.2 Energy Band Diagram of an N-p-N AlGaAs/GaAs HBT in Active Bias

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph N_Emitter["Emitter: N-AlGaAs Wide Bandgap"]
        N1[E_C,emitter]
        N2[E_F,emitter Near E_C]
        N3[E_V,emitter]
    end
    subgraph P_Base["Base: P+-GaAs Narrow Bandgap"]
        P1[E_C,base with Spike ΔE_C]
        P2[E_F,base Near E_V]
        P3[E_V,base with Step ΔE_V]
    end
    subgraph N_Collector["Collector: N-GaAs"]
        C1[E_C,collector]
        C2[E_F,collector]
        C3[E_V,collector]
    end
    N1 -->|ΔE_C spike| P1
    N3 -->|ΔE_V step barrier| P3
    P1 -->|Drift-Diffusion| C1
    P3 --> C3
    N2 -.Forward Bias V_BE.-> P2
    P2 -.Reverse Bias V_CB.-> C2
```

> **Figure interpretation:**
> - The **conduction band** (top) drops by **ΔE_C** at the E-B interface, creating a spike that is mitigated by grading.
> - The **valence band** (bottom) steps *up* by **ΔE_V** at the E-B interface, which **reflects holes** back into the base — the key to high injection efficiency.
> - The **Fermi level** (middle dotted line) splits into quasi-Fermi levels under forward bias on the E-B side and reverse bias on the B-C side.

### 4.3 Carrier Transport Flow in Active Operation

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Emitter Contact] -->|I_E, electron injection| B[E-B Heterojunction]
    B -->|J_nE, electrons cross| C[Base Region, recombines fraction 1 - α_T]
    C -->|Diffusion across W_B| D[B-C Junction, reverse biased]
    D -->|Swept by field| E[Collector Drift Region]
    E --> F[Collector Contact I_C = α I_E]
    G[Base Contact] -->|I_B = I_E - I_C| C
    H[Source V_BE] --> A
    I[Source V_CB] --> E
```

> **Figure interpretation:** This flow chart traces the four-terminal current relationships. Notice that **I_B = I_E − I_C** emerges naturally from the conservation of current, and the small recombination loss in the base (typically 1−α_T ≈ 0.01) is what makes the common-emitter current gain β = I_C/I_B very large.

### 4.4 Comparative Cross-Sectional Schematic of BJT vs. HBT

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph BJT["HOMOJUNCTION BJT (Si)"]
        A1[N+ Emitter, Si, E_G = 1.12 eV] --> A2[P Base, Si, E_G = 1.12 eV]
        A2 --> A3[N- Collector, Si, E_G = 1.12 eV]
    end
    subgraph HBT["HETEROJUNCTION BJT (AlGaAs/GaAs)"]
        B1[N AlGaAs Emitter, E_G = 1.81 eV] --> B2[P+ GaAs Base, E_G = 1.42 eV]
        B2 --> B3[N- GaAs Collector, E_G = 1.42 eV]
    end
    N1[Base Doping in BJT limited to N_A < 10^17] --> A2
    N2[Base Doping in HBT can reach N_A > 10^19] --> B2
    N3[ΔE_V = 0 in BJT, no hole barrier] --> A2
    N4[ΔE_V = 0.04 eV in HBT, blocks hole back-injection] --> B2
```

> **Figure interpretation:** The block diagram contrasts the *single-material* BJT (no bandgap engineering) with the *dual-material* HBT, where the **wider-bandgap emitter** introduces the valence-band offset ΔE_V that is fundamental to high-performance operation.
