## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A: Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 / Model Paper 2024 Scheme]**
*Define a heterojunction. How is it different from a homojunction? Why is a heterojunction preferred for the emitter-base junction in an HBT?*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
1. **Definition (1 mark):** A heterojunction is an interface between two dissimilar semiconductors that have different energy bandgaps (e.g., AlGaAs and GaAs), as opposed to a homojunction which is formed within a single semiconductor material.
2. **Difference (1 mark):** In a homojunction, the conduction and valence bands are continuous, while in a heterojunction, discontinuities ΔE_C (conduction band) and ΔE_V (valence band) appear at the interface. The energy-band structure is asymmetric.
3. **Preference for E-B (1 mark):** A heterojunction at the emitter-base allows a wide-bandgap emitter, which introduces a valence-band step ΔE_V that blocks the back-injection of holes from the base to the emitter. This decouples the injection efficiency from the base doping, enabling heavy base doping and very high current gain.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – June 2024 / Model Paper 2024 Scheme]**
*What are the four current components in an HBT? Write the expression for common-emitter current gain β in terms of the emitter injection efficiency γ and the base transport factor α_T.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
1. **Four current components (2 marks):** I_nE (electron current from emitter into base), I_pE (hole current from base into emitter — the parasitic back-injection), I_nC (electron current reaching the collector), and I_CBO (reverse leakage current at the B-C junction).
2. **β expression (1 mark):** 
$$\beta = \frac{\gamma \alpha_T}{1 - \gamma \alpha_T}$$

---

### 5.2 Part B: Full 14-Mark Questions (Internal Choice)

**Choose either Question A or Question B and answer both sub-parts.**

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)** — *Conceptual + Analytical*

**(a) [7 marks] [KTU University Exam – Dec 2022, Adapted 2024 Scheme]**
*With the help of a clearly labelled energy band diagram, explain the principle of operation of an N-p-N AlGaAs/GaAs heterojunction bipolar transistor in the active region. Discuss the role of ΔE_C and ΔE_V.*

**Model Answer:**

1. **Energy Band Diagram (3 marks):** Draw the band diagram showing three regions — N-AlGaAs emitter (E_G = 1.81 eV), P⁺-GaAs base (E_G = 1.42 eV), and N-GaAs collector (E_G = 1.42 eV). The vacuum level is continuous. The conduction band shows a spike + notch of height ΔE_C ≈ 0.35 eV at the E-B interface, and the valence band steps up by ΔE_V ≈ 0.04 eV. Under active bias, the E-B junction is forward biased (E_F splits: E_F,n closer to E_C in emitter, E_F,p closer to E_V in base) and B-C is reverse biased (E_F,n below E_F,p).

2. **Principle of Operation (2 marks):** With V_BE > 0, electrons are injected from the wide-bandgap N-AlGaAs emitter into the narrow-bandgap P⁺-GaAs base, where they diffuse across the thin base (W_B ≈ 50–100 nm). At the reverse-biased B-C junction, the depletion field sweeps these electrons into the collector, producing I_C. The base current I_B is the small recombination current in the base plus the suppressed back-injection of holes.

3. **Role of ΔE_C (1 mark):** ΔE_C is a potential spike that, if unmitigated, can block electron injection. In practice, the Al composition is graded over 30 nm to remove the spike and provide a smooth conduction-band profile. The electron injection then proceeds essentially without barrier.

4. **Role of ΔE_V (1 mark):** ΔE_V is the **hole-blocking barrier** in the valence band. It strongly suppresses J_pE (the hole current from base into emitter) by a factor of exp(ΔE_G/kT) ≈ 3 × 10⁶ for x = 0.3. This makes γ ≈ 0.9997, enabling heavy base doping (10¹⁹–10²⁰ cm⁻³) without losing current gain.

---

**(b) [7 marks] [KTU University Exam – June 2023, Adapted 2024 Scheme]**
*An Al₀.₃Ga₀.₇As/GaAs HBT has the following parameters: emitter doping N_D = 5 × 10¹⁷ cm⁻³, base doping N_A = 2 × 10¹⁹ cm⁻³, base width W_B = 0.06 µm, electron diffusion coefficient in base D_n = 35 cm²/s, electron diffusion length L_n = 3 µm. The effective bandgap difference ΔE_G = 0.39 eV. Compute (i) the base transit time τ_B, and (ii) the common-emitter current gain β. Assume n_{i,B}/n_{i,E} ratio is dominated by the bandgap term only.*

**Model Solution:**

**(i) Base transit time τ_B (3 marks):**

Using τ_B = W_B² / (2 D_n):
- W_B = 0.06 µm = 0.06 × 10⁻⁴ cm = 6 × 10⁻⁶ cm
- W_B² = 36 × 10⁻¹² cm² = 3.6 × 10⁻¹¹ cm²
- 2 D_n = 2 × 35 = 70 cm²/s

$$\tau_B = \frac{3.6 \times 10^{-11}}{70} = 5.14 \times 10^{-13} \text{ s} = 0.514 \text{ ps}$$

**(ii) Common-emitter current gain β (4 marks):**

Step 1: Base transport factor α_T:
$$\alpha_T \approx 1 - \frac{W_B^2}{2 L_n^2} = 1 - \frac{(0.06)^2}{2 \times (3)^2} = 1 - \frac{0.0036}{18} = 1 - 2 \times 10^{-4} = 0.9998$$

Step 2: Emitter injection efficiency γ.
For a heterojunction, the ratio J_{nE} / J_{pE} contains the factor exp(ΔE_G / kT):
$$\frac{J_{nE}}{J_{pE}} = \frac{D_n N_D L_p}{D_p N_A W_B} \cdot \exp\left(\frac{\Delta E_G}{kT}\right)$$

Using kT = 0.0259 eV at 300 K:
$$\exp(0.39/0.0259) = \exp(15.06) \approx 3.4 \times 10^6$$

Assuming D_n N_D L_p / (D_p N_A W_B) ≈ 1 for simplicity (typical value), the ratio is ≈ 3.4 × 10⁶, so:
$$\gamma = \frac{3.4 \times 10^6}{3.4 \times 10^6 + 1} \approx 0.9999997$$

Step 3: Common-base gain α:
$$\alpha = \gamma \alpha_T \approx 0.9998 \times 0.9999997 \approx 0.9998$$

Step 4: Common-emitter gain β:
$$\beta = \frac{\alpha}{1 - \alpha} = \frac{0.9998}{0.0002} = 4999 \approx 5000$$

> **Conclusion:** β ≈ 5000, demonstrating that even with base doping 40× higher than the emitter, the heterojunction delivers a current gain that would be impossible in a homojunction BJT.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks)** — *Alternative Choice*

**(a) [7 marks] [KTU University Exam – Model 2024 Scheme]**
*Derive Anderson's affinity rule. Apply it to compute the conduction band offset ΔE_C and valence band offset ΔE_V at the Al₀.₃Ga₀.₇As / GaAs heterointerface. Given: χ(GaAs) = 4.07 eV, χ(Al₀.₃Ga₀.₇As) = 3.72 eV, E_G(GaAs) = 1.42 eV, E_G(Al₀.₃Ga₀.₇As) = 1.81 eV.*

**Model Solution:**

1. **Derivation of Anderson's Rule (4 marks):**

- The electron affinity χ is defined as the energy from the vacuum level to the conduction band edge: $\chi = E_{\text{vac}} - E_C$.
- At a heterointerface with no interface dipoles, the vacuum level is continuous. Therefore, the conduction band edges of the two semiconductors differ by their affinity difference:
$$\Delta E_C = E_C^B - E_C^E = \chi_B - \chi_E$$
- The valence band edge of a semiconductor lies at E_G below the conduction band edge. Therefore the valence band offset is:
$$\Delta E_V = (E_V^B - E_V^E) = (E_C^B - E_C^E) - (E_G^B - E_G^E) = \Delta E_C - \Delta E_G$$

Equivalently:
$$\Delta E_G = \Delta E_C + \Delta E_V$$

2. **Numerical Calculation (3 marks):**

$$\Delta E_C = 4.07 - 3.72 = 0.35 \text{ eV}$$

$$\Delta E_G = 1.81 - 1.42 = 0.39 \text{ eV}$$

$$\Delta E_V = \Delta E_G - \Delta E_C = 0.39 - 0.35 = 0.04 \text{ eV}$$

> **Result:** ΔE_C = 0.35 eV, ΔE_V = 0.04 eV. The bandgap difference predominantly appears in the **conduction band** for the AlGaAs/GaAs system.

---

**(b) [7 marks] [KTU University Exam – Model 2024 Scheme]**
*Compare the structures and operating principles of a homojunction BJT and an AlGaAs/GaAs HBT. Why can the HBT achieve a much higher current gain for the same emitter doping? Discuss three advantages of HBTs over conventional BJTs in high-frequency applications.*

**Model Solution:**

1. **Structural Comparison (2 marks):** A BJT has a single semiconductor (Si, Ge, or GaAs) for emitter, base, and collector. An AlGaAs/GaAs HBT uses N-Al₀.₃Ga₀.₇As for the emitter, P⁺-GaAs for the base, and N-GaAs for the collector. Only the E-B interface is a heterojunction; the B-C is a homojunction.

2. **Operating Principle (1 mark):** Both operate in the active region with V_BE > 0 and V_CB < 0. However, in the HBT, the wide-bandgap emitter introduces ΔE_V at the valence band, blocking the back-injection of holes from base to emitter.

3. **Why Higher β for Same Emitter Doping (2 marks):** In a homojunction BJT, the ratio J_{nE}/J_{pE} ∝ N_D/N_A; hence to maintain γ ≈ 1, the emitter must be doped 50–100× higher than the base, capping N_A at ~10¹⁷ cm⁻³. In an HBT, the heterojunction multiplies this ratio by exp(ΔE_G/kT) ≈ 3 × 10⁶, so γ ≈ 1 is achieved even when N_A > N_D. Higher N_A → lower R_b, lower τ_B, and higher f_T — all while preserving β.

4. **Three High-Frequency Advantages (2 marks):**
- **Low base resistance:** Heavy base doping reduces R_b to 100–500 Ω/□, improving f_max.
- **Reduced base transit time:** Thinner W_B with high D_n in GaAs (~200 cm²/s) yields τ_B < 1 ps, supporting f_T > 100 GHz.
- **Higher breakdown voltage:** Wide-bandgap emitter allows higher V_CE without avalanching, important for power amplifiers.
- (Bonus) **Semi-insulating substrate:** Reduces parasitic capacitance between collector and ground, improving high-frequency performance.

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Pitfalls in HBT Questions (Dec 2023 / June 2024 feedback):**
> 1. **Forgetting to grade the emitter:** Students often draw a band diagram with a sharp ΔE_C spike and do not mention that production HBTs **grade the Al composition over ~30 nm** to suppress the current-blocking spike. A 1–2 mark penalty is applied.
> 2. **Misnaming ΔE_C and ΔE_V:** Conduction-band offset ΔE_C is sometimes confused with the conduction-band edge. Be precise: ΔE_C = χ_B − χ_E (eV).
> 3. **Neglecting exp(ΔE_G/kT) factor in γ:** When asked for β, students write the BJT formula and forget the heterojunction factor, giving β ~ 50 instead of β ~ 5000. The exponential term is the *heart* of HBT physics.
> 4. **Wrong sign convention in V_BE:** The base-emitter junction in an N-p-N HBT in the active region is **forward biased** (V_BE > 0, typically 1.2–1.4 V for AlGaAs/GaAs), not reverse biased. The B-C junction is reverse biased.
> 5. **No units in final answers:** KTU examiners expect SI units (s, m, Hz, eV, cm²/V·s, A/cm²). A correct numerical answer without units attracts a 0.5-mark deduction.

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist for KTU ESE — HBT Principle of Operation**

- **Definition:** HBT = BJT with at least one heterojunction (typically the E-B junction).
- **Wide-Bandgap Emitter:** The key engineering feature that makes HBTs work.
- **Anderson's Rule:** ΔE_C = χ_B − χ_E; ΔE_V = ΔE_G − ΔE_C; ΔE_G = ΔE_C + ΔE_V.
- **Conduction-Band Spike:** Must be removed by compositional grading over ~30 nm to avoid blocking electron injection.
- **Valence-Band Offset ΔE_V:** Acts as the hole barrier; responsible for the huge injection efficiency.
- **Injection Efficiency Factor:** exp(ΔE_G/kT) is the *exponential* advantage of the HBT over the BJT.
- **Base Doping:** Can be 10¹⁹–10²⁰ cm⁻³ in HBTs (versus ~10¹⁷ in BJTs), yielding low R_b and high f_max.
- **Active Region Bias:** E-B forward biased (~1.2–1.4 V for AlGaAs/GaAs); B-C reverse biased.
- **Current Gain β:** β = α/(1−α), with α = γ·α_T and γ ≈ 0.9999, α_T ≈ 0.9998 ⇒ β in the range 10³–10⁴.
- **Base Transit Time:** τ_B = W_B² / (2 D_n); smaller W_B → higher f_T.
- **Cutoff Frequency:** f_T = 1/(2π τ_EC); record InP HBTs exceed 500 GHz.
- **Kirk Effect:** At high J_C, the effective base widens into the collector, degrading β and f_T.
- **Al Composition Limit:** x ≤ 0.45 in Al_xGa_{1−x}As to avoid the indirect-bandgap crossover (E_G stops increasing beyond this point).
- **E_G(x) formula:** E_G(x) = 1.424 + 1.247x (eV) for x ≤ 0.45 at 300 K.
- **Real-World Use:** Cell-phone PAs (GaAs HBT), mm-wave radar (SiGe HBT), 100G+ optical links (InP HBT).
- **Common Pitfalls:** Forgetting ΔE_C grading, sign errors in bias, missing the exp(ΔE_G/kT) factor, and omitting units.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Energy Band Diagrams
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEEVT413 - COMPOUND SEMICONDUCTORS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Energy Band Diagrams

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Energy Band Diagram:** An Energy Band Diagram is a graphical representation of the allowed and forbidden electron energy states in a crystalline solid as a function of position (for heterojunctions) or crystal momentum $\vec{k}$ (for bulk E-k dispersion). It depicts the **Conduction Band** ($E_C$), **Valence Band** ($E_V$), and the **Forbidden Energy Gap** ($E_g = E_C - E_V$) that separates them.

In the context of **Compound Semiconductors** (e.g., GaAs, InP, GaN, CdTe, ZnSe), the energy band diagram is a critical tool because it determines:
- Whether the material has a **direct bandgap** (photon-emitting transitions allowed) or **indirect bandgap** (phonon-assisted transitions).
- The **bandgap energy $E_g$** which dictates optical absorption/emission wavelength.
- The position of **donor ($E_D$)** and **acceptor ($E_A$)** levels in doped compound semiconductors.
- The **band offsets** ($\Delta E_C$, $\Delta E_V$) at heterojunctions such as GaAs/AlGaAs.

The fundamental energy-momentum relationship for an electron near the band extrema in a semiconductor is given by:

$$E(\vec{k}) = E_C + \frac{\hbar^2 k^2}{2 m_e^*}$$

for the conduction band, and

$$E(\vec{k}) = E_V - \frac{\hbar^2 k^2}{2 m_h^*}$$

for the valence band, where $m_e^*$ and $m_h^*$ are the **effective masses** of electrons and holes respectively.

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy (Plain English Intuition)

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy — "The Parking Lot and the Staircase"**
>
> Imagine a multi-storey parking building where cars (electrons) can only park on specific floors. The ground floor is the **Valence Band** (where electrons normally sit, bound to atoms). The roof floor is the **Conduction Band** (where electrons roam freely and conduct electricity). The space *between* the floors — the empty air between ground and roof — is the **Forbidden Energy Gap** ($E_g$). 
>
> - In **conductors (metals)**: the staircase is connected, electrons can freely climb.
> - In **insulators**: the gap is so huge (like a 50-floor jump) that electrons cannot leap it.
> - In **semiconductors**: the gap is just a small jump (e.g., 1.42 eV for GaAs), so with a bit of thermal or optical energy, electrons can jump up and conduct.
>
> In **compound semiconductors**, the structure of the "floors" (energy bands) can have valleys at the same horizontal position (**direct bandgap**, like GaAs) or at different positions (**indirect bandgap**, like Si), which fundamentally changes how the material interacts with light.

---

## 1.3 Classification of Compound Semiconductors by Band Structure

| Category | Examples | Bandgap Nature | Typical $E_g$ |
|:---------|:---------|:---------------|:--------------|
| **III-V Binary** | GaAs, InP, InSb, GaN | Direct (except AlAs) | 0.17 – 3.4 eV |
| **III-V Ternary** | AlGaAs, InGaAs, InGaP | Direct / Indirect | Tunable |
| **II-VI Binary** | CdTe, ZnSe, HgCdTe | Direct | 0.0 – 2.7 eV |
| **IV-VI Binary** | PbS, PbTe | Direct (narrow) | 0.29 – 0.41 eV |
| **III-Nitride** | GaN, AlN, InN | Direct (GaN, InN) | 0.7 – 6.2 eV |

---

## 1.4 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Physical Constants Used in Energy Band Analysis:**
> - Reduced Planck's constant: $\hbar = 1.054 \times 10^{-34}$ **J·s**
> - Electron rest mass: $m_0 = 9.109 \times 10^{-31}$ **kg**
> - Boltzmann constant: $k_B = 1.381 \times 10^{-23}$ **J/K** (or **8.617 × 10⁻⁵ eV/K**)
> - Thermal voltage at 300 K: $V_T = 0.02585$ **V**
> - Elementary charge: $q = 1.602 \times 10^{-19}$ **C**
> - 1 eV $= 1.602 \times 10^{-19}$ **J**

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Step-by-Step Theoretical Framework

### Step 1: Origin of Energy Bands (Brillouin Zone Perspective)
When isolated atoms are brought together to form a crystal, the discrete atomic orbitals **split and broaden** into continuous bands due to **Pauli's exclusion principle** and **wavefunction overlap**. The periodicity of the crystal lattice causes the energy dispersion $E(\vec{k})$ to be periodic in **k-space**, with the smallest repeating cell being the **First Brillouin Zone**.

The boundaries of the Brillouin Zone occur at high-symmetry points labelled:
- $\Gamma$ (Gamma) — center of the zone, $\vec{k} = (0,0,0)$
- $X$ — zone boundary along $\langle 100 \rangle$ direction
- $L$ — zone boundary along $\langle 111 \rangle$ direction
- $K, U, W$ — other high-symmetry points

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Memory Trick:** The **conduction band minimum (CBM)** and **valence band maximum (VBM)** positions determine whether a semiconductor is **direct** or **indirect**.

---

### Step 2: Direct vs Indirect Bandgap Semiconductors

**Direct Bandgap:**
- CBM and VBM occur at the **same k-vector** (typically at $\Gamma$).
- Electrons can transition directly from VBM to CBM with only a **photon** (no change in momentum required).
- Result: **Efficient light emission/absorption**.
- Examples: **GaAs, InP, GaN, CdTe, InAs**.

**Indirect Bandgap:**
- CBM and VBM occur at **different k-vectors** (e.g., VBM at $\Gamma$, CBM near $X$ or $L$).
- Electron transition requires a **phonon** to conserve momentum, in addition to a photon.
- Result: **Poor light emission efficiency**, but suitable for some electronic devices.
- Examples: **Si, Ge, AlAs, SiC**.

The energy-momentum relationship for a direct-gap transition is:

$$E_g^{direct} = E_C(\Gamma) - E_V(\Gamma)$$

For an indirect gap:

$$E_g^{indirect} = E_C(X \text{ or } L) - E_V(\Gamma)$$

---

### Step 3: Effective Mass from Band Curvature

The **effective mass** of a carrier near a band extremum is defined by the **second derivative** (curvature) of the energy band:

$$\frac{1}{m^*} = \frac{1}{\hbar^2} \frac{d^2 E}{dk^2}$$

A **sharply curved band** $\Rightarrow$ small $m^*$ $\Rightarrow$ **high mobility** ($\mu \propto 1/m^*$).
A **flat band** $\Rightarrow$ large $m^*$ $\Rightarrow$ **low mobility** (typical of heavy holes).

For the conduction band of a direct-gap III-V compound like GaAs, there is **one isotropic valley** at $\Gamma$. For indirect-gap materials like Si, there are **multiple equivalent conduction band valleys** (6 for Si along $\Delta$ near $X$), leading to **conductivity effective mass** corrections.

---

### Step 4: Intrinsic Carrier Concentration in Compound Semiconductors

For an intrinsic (undoped) compound semiconductor, the carrier concentration in the conduction band is:

$$n_i = \sqrt{N_C N_V} \exp\left(-\frac{E_g}{2 k_B T}\right)$$

where the effective density of states are:

$$N_C = 2 \left(\frac{2\pi m_e^* k_B T}{h^2}\right)^{3/2}$$

$$N_V = 2 \left(\frac{2\pi m_h^* k_B T}{h^2}\right)^{3/2}$$

The factor of **2** accounts for spin degeneracy.

---

### Step 5: Band Diagram of Doped Compound Semiconductors

When donors (e.g., Si substituting As in GaAs) or acceptors (e.g., Zn substituting Ga in GaAs) are introduced:
- **n-type (donor level $E_D$)**: $E_D$ lies just **below $E_C$**, typically at $E_C - 0.001$ to $E_C - 0.05$ eV.
- **p-type (acceptor level $E_A$)**: $E_A$ lies just **above $E_V$**, typically at $E_V + 0.001$ to $E_V + 0.05$ eV.

Electron concentration in n-type (non-degenerate, Boltzmann approximation):

$$n \approx N_D \quad \text{(if fully ionized)}$$

Fermi level position in n-type semiconductor:

$$E_F = E_C - k_B T \ln\left(\frac{N_C}{N_D}\right)$$

Fermi level position in p-type semiconductor:

$$E_F = E_V + k_B T \ln\left(\frac{N_V}{N_A}\right)$$

---

## 2.2 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **CRITICAL FORMULAS — Energy Band Diagrams of Compound Semiconductors**

| # | Formula | Description / Use | Units |
|:-:|:--------|:------------------|:------|
| 1 | $E_g = E_C - E_V$ | Bandgap energy | eV |
| 2 | $E(\vec{k}) = E_C + \frac{\hbar^2 k^2}{2 m_e^*}$ | Conduction band dispersion (parabolic) | eV |
| 3 | $E(\vec{k}) = E_V - \frac{\hbar^2 k^2}{2 m_h^*}$ | Valence band dispersion | eV |
| 4 | $m^* = \hbar^2 \left( \frac{d^2 E}{dk^2} \right)^{-1}$ | Effective mass definition | kg |
| 5 | $N_C = 2 \left( \frac{2\pi m_e^* k_B T}{h^2} \right)^{3/2}$ | Effective density of states (CB) | cm⁻³ |
| 6 | $N_V = 2 \left( \frac{2\pi m_h^* k_B T}{h^2} \right)^{3/2}$ | Effective density of states (VB) | cm⁻³ |
| 7 | $n_i = \sqrt{N_C N_V} \exp\left(-\frac{E_g}{2 k_B T}\right)$ | Intrinsic carrier concentration | cm⁻³ |
| 8 | $E_F - E_i = k_B T \ln\left( \frac{n}{n_i} \right)$ | Fermi level relative to intrinsic | eV |
| 9 | $\lambda (\mu m) = \frac{1.24}{E_g (eV)}$ | Emission/absorption wavelength | $\mu$m |
| 10 | $\mu = \frac{q \tau}{m^*}$ | Carrier mobility | cm²/(V·s) |
| 11 | $\Delta E_C + \Delta E_V = \Delta E_g$ | Heterojunction band offset relation | eV |
| 12 | $\Delta E_C : \Delta E_V \approx 60:40$ (GaAs/AlGaAs) | Empirical band offset ratio | ratio |
| 13 | $n_0 = N_C \exp\left(-\frac{E_C - E_F}{k_B T}\right)$ | Electron density (Boltzmann) | cm⁻³ |
| 14 | $p_0 = N_V \exp\left(-\frac{E_F - E_V}{k_B T}\right)$ | Hole density (Boltzmann) | cm⁻³ |
| 15 | $np = n_i^2$ | Mass-action law (thermal eq.) | cm⁻⁶ |

---

## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

> [!NOTE]
> **Why Energy Band Diagrams Matter in Compound Semiconductor Engineering:**
>
> 1. **Optoelectronic Device Design**: Lasers (GaAs/AlGaAs, InGaN), LEDs, and photodetectors all require knowledge of $E_g$ to determine emission wavelength $\lambda = 1.24/E_g$.
> 2. **High-Electron-Mobility Transistors (HEMTs)**: GaN/AlGaN HEMTs rely on precise band offsets ($\Delta E_C$) to form a 2-D Electron Gas (2DEG).
> 3. **Solar Cells**: Multi-junction cells (GaInP/GaAs/Ge) are designed by matching bandgaps of different compound semiconductors.
> 4. **Quantum Wells and Heterostructure Lasers**: Require flat-band diagrams with proper alignment of $E_C$ and $E_V$ across layers.
> 5. **Terahertz and Infrared Devices**: HgCdTe (mercury cadmium telluride) is tunable across IR by adjusting Cd composition and hence $E_g$.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Effective Density of States in Conduction Band ($N_C$)

We start from the count of available quantum states in k-space. Each k-state occupies a volume $(2\pi/L)^3$ per band, where $L$ is the crystal length.

**Step 1: Density of states in k-space.**
The number of allowed k-states per unit volume in a crystal is:

$$g(\vec{k}) \, d^3k = 2 \times \frac{d^3k}{(2\pi)^3}$$

The factor of **2** accounts for the two spin orientations of an electron.

**Step 2: Transform from k-space to energy.**
For a parabolic conduction band minimum at $\vec{k}=0$:

$$E = E_C + \frac{\hbar^2 k^2}{2 m_e^*}$$

Solving for $k$:

$$k = \sqrt{\frac{2 m_e^* (E - E_C)}{\hbar^2}}$$

In spherical symmetry, the volume element is $d^3k = 4\pi k^2 \, dk$.

**Step 3: Number of states per unit energy interval.**
Differentiating $E$ with respect to $k$:

$$dE = \frac{\hbar^2 k}{m_e^*} \, dk \quad \Rightarrow \quad dk = \frac{m_e^*}{\hbar^2 k} \, dE$$

Therefore, the density of states per unit energy per unit volume is:

$$g(E) \, dE = \frac{2}{(2\pi)^3} \times 4\pi k^2 \, dk = \frac{k^2}{\pi^2} \, dk$$

Substituting $k^2 = \frac{2 m_e^* (E - E_C)}{\hbar^2}$ and $dk = \frac{m_e^*}{\hbar^2 k} dE$:

$$g(E) = \frac{1}{\pi^2} \cdot \frac{2 m_e^* (E - E_C)}{\hbar^2} \cdot \frac{m_e^*}{\hbar^2 k}$$

$$g(E) = \frac{1}{2\pi^2} \left(\frac{2 m_e^*}{\hbar^2}\right)^{3/2} (E - E_C)^{1/2}$$

**Step 4: Compute the effective density of states $N_C$.**
The total electron density in the conduction band is:

$$n_0 = \int_{E_C}^{\infty} g(E) \, f(E) \, dE$$

Using the **Boltzmann approximation** $f(E) \approx \exp[-(E - E_F)/k_B T]$:

$$n_0 = \frac{1}{2\pi^2} \left(\frac{2 m_e^*}{\hbar^2}\right)^{3/2} \int_{E_C}^{\infty} (E - E_C)^{1/2} \exp\left[-\frac{E - E_F}{k_B T}\right] dE$$

Let $u = (E - E_C)/k_B T$:

$$n_0 = \frac{1}{2\pi^2} \left(\frac{2 m_e^*}{\hbar^2}\right)^{3/2} (k_B T)^{3/2} \exp\left[-\frac{E_C - E_F}{k_B T}\right] \int_{0}^{\infty} u^{1/2} e^{-u} du$$

The integral $\int_0^\infty u^{1/2} e^{-u} du = \frac{\sqrt{\pi}}{2}$.

$$\boxed{N_C = 2 \left(\frac{2 \pi m_e^* k_B T}{h^2}\right)^{3/2}}$$

where we converted $\hbar = h/2\pi$ back to $h$ and combined constants.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Intrinsic Fermi Level Position in a Compound Semiconductor

**Step 1: For an intrinsic semiconductor, $n_0 = p_0 = n_i$.**
Setting the electron and hole densities equal:

$$N_C \exp\left[-\frac{E_C - E_F}{k_B T}\right] = N_V \exp\left[-\frac{E_F - E_V}{k_B T}\right]$$

**Step 2: Take natural logarithm of both sides.**
$$\ln N_C - \frac{E_C - E_F}{k_B T} = \ln N_V - \frac{E_F - E_V}{k_B T}$$

**Step 3: Solve for $E_F$ (which is $E_i$ for intrinsic).**

$$\frac{2 E_F}{k_B T} = \ln N_C + \frac{E_C}{k_B T} + \ln N_V + \frac{E_V}{k_B T}$$

Wait, let me redo this carefully:

$$\ln N_C - \frac{E_C - E_F}{k_B T} = \ln N_V - \frac{E_F - E_V}{k_B T}$$

$$\ln N_C - \ln N_V = \frac{E_C - E_F}{k_B T} - \frac{E_F - E_V}{k_B T}$$

$$\ln\left(\frac{N_C}{N_V}\right) = \frac{E_C + E_V - 2 E_F}{k_B T}$$

**Step 4: Rearrange for $E_F = E_i$.**

$$E_i = \frac{E_C + E_V}{2} + \frac{k_B T}{2} \ln\left(\frac{N_V}{N_C}\right)$$

**Step 5: Express in terms of effective mass ratio.**
Substituting the $N_C$ and $N_V$ expressions:

$$\frac{N_V}{N_C} = \left(\frac{m_h^*}{m_e^*}\right)^{3/2}$$

$$\boxed{E_i = \frac{E_C + E_V}{2} + \frac{3 k_B T}{4} \ln\left(\frac{m_h^*}{m_e^*}\right)}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Physical Interpretation:** If $m_h^* = m_e^*$, then $E_i$ lies exactly at the middle of the bandgap. For GaAs, $m_h^* > m_e^*$, so $E_i$ is slightly **above** midgap.

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3: Wavelength of Emitted Photon from Band-to-Band Recombination

**Step 1: Energy conservation at direct transition.**
When an electron in the conduction band minimum recombines with a hole at the valence band maximum, the energy released is:

$$E_{photon} = E_g$$

**Step 2: Convert energy to wavelength using $E = h\nu = hc/\lambda$.**

$$\lambda = \frac{hc}{E_g}$$

**Step 3: Substitute numerical values for $h$, $c$, and convert $E_g$ to eV.**

$$h = 4.1357 \times 10^{-15} \text{ eV·s}, \quad c = 3 \times 10^8 \text{ m/s}$$

$$\lambda = \frac{(4.1357 \times 10^{-15})(3 \times 10^8)}{E_g} \text{ m}$$

$$\lambda = \frac{1.2407 \times 10^{-6}}{E_g (\text{eV})} \text{ m}$$

$$\boxed{\lambda (\mu m) = \frac{1.24}{E_g (\text{eV})}}$$

**Worked Example (KTU Numerical Style):**
For GaAs with $E_g = 1.42$ eV:

$$\lambda = \frac{1.24}{1.42} = 0.873 \, \mu m = 873 \, \text{nm}$$

This is in the **near-infrared** range, explaining why GaAs is used in CD-laser technology (780 nm is achieved with AlGaAs).

For GaN with $E_g = 3.4$ eV:

$$\lambda = \frac{1.24}{3.4} = 0.365 \, \mu m = 365 \, \text{nm (UV)}$$

This is why GaN is the material of choice for **blue and UV LEDs**.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix: Compound Semiconductor Comparison

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Comparison Matrix — Key Compound Semiconductors (KTU 2024 High-Yield Table)**

| Parameter | GaAs | InP | GaN | CdTe | Si (reference) |
|:----------|:----:|:---:|:---:|:----:|:--------------:|
| $E_g$ (eV) at 300 K | **1.42** | **1.35** | **3.4** | **1.45** | 1.12 |
| Bandgap type | Direct | Direct | Direct | Direct | Indirect |
| $m_e^*/m_0$ | 0.067 | 0.077 | 0.20 | 0.11 | 0.26 (cond.) |
| $m_{hh}^*/m_0$ | 0.50 | 0.60 | 1.0 | 0.35 | 0.49 |
| Mobility $\mu_e$ (cm²/V·s) | **8500** | 5400 | 1000 | 1050 | 1400 |
| Mobility $\mu_h$ (cm²/V·s) | 400 | 200 | 30 | 100 | 450 |
| Lattice const. (Å) | 5.653 | 5.869 | 3.189 | 6.482 | 5.431 |
| Primary use | RF, Laser, Solar | Telecom laser | Blue/UV LED | Solar cell, IR | CMOS baseline |

---

## 3.5 Derivation 4: Carrier Concentration in Doped (n-type) Compound Semiconductor

**Step 1: Charge neutrality condition.**
For an n-type compound semiconductor with donor concentration $N_D$ (fully ionized) and no acceptors:

$$n_0 = N_D^+ + p_0 \approx N_D \quad \text{(if } n_0 \gg p_0\text{)}$$

**Step 2: Express $n_0$ in terms of $E_F$.**

$$n_0 = N_C \exp\left[-\frac{E_C - E_F}{k_B T}\right] = N_D$$

**Step 3: Solve for the Fermi level position.**

$$\exp\left[\frac{E_F - E_C}{k_B T}\right] = \frac{N_C}{N_D}$$

$$E_F = E_C - k_B T \ln\left(\frac{N_C}{N_D}\right)$$

**Step 4: Numerical Example.**
For GaAs at 300 K: $N_C = 4.7 \times 10^{17}$ cm⁻³, $N_D = 10^{17}$ cm⁻³, $k_B T = 0.02585$ eV.

$$E_C - E_F = 0.02585 \times \ln(4.7) = 0.02585 \times 1.548 = 0.040 \text{ eV}$$

The Fermi level lies **0.040 eV below $E_C$**, which is typical for moderately doped n-GaAs.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Diagram 1: Classification of Energy Band Structures in Compound Semiconductors

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[CRYSTALLINE COMPOUND SEMICONDUCTOR] --> B{Direct Bandgap?}
    B -->|YES| C[Direct Gap Materials]
    B -->|NO| D[Indirect Gap Materials]
    C --> C1[GaAs - E_g 1.42 eV]
    C --> C2[InP - E_g 1.35 eV]
    C --> C3[GaN - E_g 3.4 eV]
    C --> C4[CdTe - E_g 1.45 eV]
    C --> C5[InAs - E_g 0.36 eV]
    D --> D1[AlAs - E_g 2.16 eV]
    D --> D2[Si - E_g 1.12 eV]
    D --> D3[Ge - E_g 0.66 eV]
    C1 --> E1[Application: Laser LED Solar]
    C2 --> E2[Application: Telecom 1550 nm]
    C3 --> E3[Application: Blue LED HEMT]
    D1 --> E4[Application: HBT with GaAs]
    D2 --> E5[Application: CMOS Microelectronics]
```

---

## 4.2 Mermaid Diagram 2: Block-Level Functional Architecture of an Energy Band Diagram Construction

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A1[Atomic Orbitals] --> A2[Brillouin Zone Definition]
    A2 --> A3[Compute E_k Dispersion]
    A3 --> A4{Identify CBM and VBM k positions}
    A4 -->|Same k| B1[DIRECT BANDGAP]
    A4 -->|Different k| B2[INDIRECT BANDGAP]
    B1 --> C1[Draw E_C and E_V lines]
    B2 --> C2[Draw E_C and E_V lines]
    C1 --> D1[Add E_g label]
    C2 --> D2[Add E_g label]
    C1 --> E1[Add Fermi Level E_F]
    C2 --> E2[Add Fermi Level E_F]
    E1 --> F1[Add Donor or Acceptor Levels]
    E2 --> F2[Add Donor or Acceptor Levels]
    F1 --> G1[FINAL ENERGY BAND DIAGRAM]
    F2 --> G2[FINAL ENERGY BAND DIAGRAM]
```

---

## 4.3 Mermaid Diagram 3: Energy Band Diagram of a p-n Heterojunction (GaAs / AlGaAs)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph LEFT[GaAs p-type]
        L1[E_V line high]
        L2[E_F left side]
        L3[E_C line]
        L4[Acceptor level E_A]
    end
    subgraph RIGHT[AlGaAs n-type wider gap]
        R1[E_V line lower position]
        R2[E_F right side]
        R3[E_C line higher position]
        R4[Donor level E_D]
    end
    L1 -. band offset dEv .-> R1
    L3 -. band offset dEc .-> R3
    L2 -. aligned at equilibrium .-> R2
```

**Caption for the heterojunction diagram:** The above block diagram represents the relative band positions in a GaAs(p) / AlGaAs(n) heterojunction. The conduction band offset $\Delta E_C$ and valence band offset $\Delta E_V$ arise from the difference in electron affinities of the two compound semiconductors.

---

## 4.4 Mermaid Diagram 4: Process Flow — How a Photon Interacts with a Direct Bandgap Compound Semiconductor

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    P1[Photon with energy h nu greater than E_g] --> P2{Photon absorbed by Valence Electron}
    P2 --> P3[Electron Excited from E_V to E_C]
    P3 --> P4[Electron thermalizes to CBM]
    P4 --> P5[Direct Recombination with Hole at VBM]
    P5 --> P6[Photon of wavelength lambda 1.24 over E_g emitted]
    P6 --> P7[LED or LASER light output]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Insight:** This direct recombination process is the foundation of all **optoelectronic devices** (LEDs, laser diodes, photodetectors) made from direct bandgap compound semiconductors like GaAs, InP, and GaN.

---

## 4.5 Schematic Notation of Standard Energy Band Diagram Features

| Symbol | Meaning |
|:------:|:--------|
| $E_C$ | Bottom edge of conduction band |
| $E_V$ | Top edge of valence band |
| $E_g$ | Bandgap = $E_C - E_V$ |
| $E_F$ | Fermi level |
| $E_i$ | Intrinsic Fermi level |
| $E_D$ | Donor energy level (just below $E_C$) |
| $E_A$ | Acceptor energy level (just above $E_V$) |
| $\Gamma$, $X$, $L$ | High-symmetry points in Brillouin zone |
| $\Delta E_C$ | Conduction band offset at heterojunction |
| $\Delta E_V$ | Valence band offset at heterojunction |
| 2DEG | Two-dimensional electron gas (at HEMT interface) |

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **Q1. [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]**
> **Define direct and indirect bandgap semiconductors. Give one example for each from compound semiconductors.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
A **direct bandgap semiconductor** is one in which the conduction band minimum (CBM) and valence band maximum (VBM) occur at the **same value of crystal momentum $\vec{k}$** (typically at the $\Gamma$ point). Electron-hole recombination can therefore occur with emission of a photon alone, without the assistance of a phonon. Example: **GaAs** ($E_g = 1.42$ eV).

An **indirect bandgap semiconductor** is one in which the CBM and VBM occur at **different $\vec{k}$** values (e.g., VBM at $\Gamma$ and CBM near the $X$ point). Recombination requires a phonon to conserve momentum, making light emission inefficient. Example: **AlAs** ($E_g = 2.16$ eV indirect) or **Si** ($E_g = 1.12$ eV indirect).

---

> **Q2. [KTU University Exam - May 2024]**
> **What is effective mass of an electron in a semiconductor? How is it related to the curvature of the energy band?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
The **effective mass** $m^*$ of an electron in a semiconductor is a parameter that accounts for the effect of the crystal lattice potential on the electron's motion, allowing it to be treated as a free particle with mass $m^*$. It is defined as:

$$m^* = \frac{\hbar^2}{\frac{d^2 E}{dk^2}}$$

It is **inversely proportional to the second derivative (curvature)** of the E-k band. A **sharply curved** (concave-up) conduction band gives a **small positive effective mass**, while a **flat band** gives a **large effective mass**. Heavy holes in the valence band have flatter bands and hence larger effective masses than electrons in the conduction band.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Part B Format:** Two alternative questions (Q-A and Q-B) — KTU ESE Internal Choice Pattern. Each has sub-parts (a) 7 marks and (b) 7 marks.

---

### **QUESTION A (14 Marks)**
**[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 Model Paper]**

**(a)** Draw the energy band diagram of an **intrinsic GaAs** at 300 K. Label the conduction band $E_C$, valence band $E_V$, bandgap $E_g$, and intrinsic Fermi level $E_i$. Show numerically the value of $E_g$ and the position of $E_i$ relative to $E_C$. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** With necessary expressions, derive the **intrinsic Fermi level position** in terms of effective masses of electrons and holes. Comment on the case when $m_h^* = m_e^*$. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Part (a):**
- $E_g$(GaAs) = **1.42 eV** at 300 K.
- $m_e^* = 0.067 m_0$, $m_{hh}^* = 0.50 m_0$.
- $E_i$ is **slightly above midgap** (since $m_h^* > m_e^*$).

**Numerical value of $E_C - E_i$:**

$$E_C - E_i = \frac{E_g}{2} + \frac{3 k_B T}{4} \ln\left(\frac{m_e^*}{m_h^*}\right)$$

$$= \frac{1.42}{2} + \frac{3 \times 0.02585}{4} \ln\left(\frac{0.067}{0.50}\right)$$

$$= 0.710 + 0.0194 \times \ln(0.134)$$

$$= 0.710 + 0.0194 \times (-2.010)$$

$$= 0.710 - 0.039 = 0.671 \text{ eV}$$

So $E_i$ lies **0.671 eV below $E_C$** and **0.749 eV above $E_V$**.

**Part (b):**
Starting from $n_0 = p_0$:

$$N_C \exp\left[-\frac{E_C - E_F}{k_B T}\right] = N_V \exp\left[-\frac{E_F - E_V}{k_B T}\right]$$

$$\ln\left(\frac{N_V}{N_C}\right) = \frac{E_C + E_V - 2E_i}{k_B T}$$

$$E_i = \frac{E_C + E_V}{2} + \frac{k_B T}{2} \ln\left(\frac{N_V}{N_C}\right)$$

Substituting $\frac{N_V}{N_C} = \left(\frac{m_h^*}{m_e^*}\right)^{3/2}$:

$$E_i = \frac{E_C + E_V}{2} + \frac{3 k_B T}{4} \ln\left(\frac{m_h^*}{m_e^*}\right)$$

**Case $m_h^* = m_e^*$:** The log term vanishes, and $E_i$ lies exactly at the **midgap**, i.e., $E_i = (E_C + E_V)/2$. This is the case for an idealized symmetric band structure.

---

### **QUESTION B (14 Marks)**
**[KTU University Exam - May 2024 Model Paper]**

**(a)** Compare the **energy band structures** of **GaAs, InP, GaN, and AlAs** in terms of (i) bandgap energy, (ii) direct/indirect nature, and (iii) effective mass of electron. Explain why direct bandgap compound semiconductors are preferred for **LEDs and laser diodes**. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A **GaAs sample** is doped n-type with $N_D = 5 \times 10^{16}$ cm⁻³. Given $N_C = 4.7 \times 10^{17}$ cm⁻³, $N_V = 7.0 \times 10^{18}$ cm⁻³, $E_g = 1.42$ eV, $T = 300$ K, calculate: (i) the electron concentration, (ii) the hole concentration, (iii) the position of the Fermi level $E_F$ relative to $E_C$. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Part (a):**

| Material | $E_g$ (eV) | Type | $m_e^*/m_0$ |
|:---------|:----------:|:----:|:-----------:|
| GaAs | 1.42 | Direct | 0.067 |
| InP | 1.35 | Direct | 0.077 |
| GaN | 3.40 | Direct | 0.20 |
| AlAs | 2.16 | **Indirect** | 0.71 (X-valley) |

Direct bandgap compound semiconductors are preferred for LEDs and laser diodes because the **conduction band minimum and valence band maximum occur at the same k-vector**, allowing **efficient radiative recombination** with photon emission alone, without needing phonon assistance. This yields **high internal quantum efficiency** and **bright electroluminescence**. For example, GaAs (1.42 eV) emits at 873 nm (NIR), InP (1.35 eV) at 920 nm, and GaN (3.4 eV) at 365 nm (UV). In contrast, indirect bandgap AlAs has a much longer radiative lifetime and is unsuitable as an active laser medium.

**Part (b):**
(i) **Electron concentration** (assume full ionization):
$$n_0 \approx N_D = 5 \times 10^{16} \text{ cm}^{-3}$$

(ii) **Hole concentration** using mass-action law:
$$n_i^2 = N_C N_V \exp\left(-\frac{E_g}{k_B T}\right)$$

$$n_i = \sqrt{4.7 \times 10^{17} \times 7.0 \times 10^{18}} \exp\left(-\frac{1.42}{2 \times 0.02585}\right)$$

$$n_i = \sqrt{3.29 \times 10^{36}} \times \exp(-27.466)$$

$$n_i = 1.814 \times 10^{18} \times 1.143 \times 10^{-12}$$

$$n_i = 2.07 \times 10^{6} \text{ cm}^{-3}$$

$$p_0 = \frac{n_i^2}{n_0} = \frac{(2.07 \times 10^6)^2}{5 \times 10^{16}} = \frac{4.28 \times 10^{12}}{5 \times 10^{16}} = 8.57 \times 10^{-5} \text{ cm}^{-3}$$

(iii) **Fermi level position:**
$$E_C - E_F = k_B T \ln\left(\frac{N_C}{N_D}\right) = 0.02585 \times \ln\left(\frac{4.7 \times 10^{17}}{5 \times 10^{16}}\right)$$

$$= 0.02585 \times \ln(9.4) = 0.02585 \times 2.241 = 0.0579 \text{ eV}$$

So the Fermi level is **0.058 eV below $E_C$**.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes that Cost Marks in KTU Energy Band Diagram Questions:**
>
> 1. **Confusing $E_C$ and $E_V$ positions in the diagram.** Always remember $E_C > E_V$ on the energy axis. Many students draw them inverted.
> 2. **Forgetting the spin degeneracy factor of 2** in $N_C$ and $N_V$ formulas. This is a frequent 1-mark deduction.
> 3. **Using $kT$ in joules instead of eV** when computing Fermi level positions. Always convert: $k_B T$ at 300 K = **0.02585 eV** (not $4.14 \times 10^{-21}$ J).
> 4. **Not specifying the k-point** when defining direct vs indirect bandgap. Examiner expects $\Gamma$, $X$, or $L$ notation.
> 5. **Mixing up effective mass of heavy hole vs light hole** in numerical problems. Read the question carefully — if it says $m_{hh}^*$ vs $m_{lh}^*$, use the right one.
> 6. **Drawing the band diagram without specifying the energy scale or numerical values of $E_g$.** Always annotate key energy values directly on the diagram.
> 7. **Skipping the $E_i$ position** in the band diagram of an intrinsic semiconductor — this is a 2-mark item.
> 8. **For heterojunction questions**, students often forget the relation $\Delta E_C + \Delta E_V = \Delta E_g$ — a critical band-offset relation.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!NOTE]
> **High-Density Revision Bullets — Energy Band Diagrams of Compound Semiconductors**

- The **energy band diagram** shows allowed/forbidden electron energies vs position (real space) or momentum (k-space).
- Three key levels: **$E_C$ (conduction band minimum)**, **$E_V$ (valence band maximum)**, and **$E_F$ (Fermi level)**.
- **Bandgap** $E_g = E_C - E_V$; **larger $E_g$** $\Rightarrow$ **shorter emission wavelength** $\lambda = 1.24/E_g$.
- **Direct bandgap** = CBM and VBM at same k (efficient light emission). **Indirect** = at different k (poor light emission).
- Compound semiconductors like **GaAs, InP, GaN, CdTe** are **direct bandgap** — used in LEDs, lasers, solar cells.
- **AlAs and Si** are **indirect** — used in HBT, CMOS, not for light emission.
- **Effective mass** $m^* = \hbar^2 / (d^2E/dk^2)$ — sharper curvature = smaller $m^*$ = higher mobility.
- **Effective density of states**:
  $N_C = 2 (2\pi m_e^* k_B T / h^2)^{3/2}$, similar for $N_V$.
- **Intrinsic carrier concentration**:
  $n_i = \sqrt{N_C N_V} \exp(-E_g / 2k_B T)$.
- **Intrinsic Fermi level**:
  $E_i = (E_C+E_V)/2 + (3k_BT/4)\ln(m_h^*/m_e^*)$.
- **Fermi level in n-type**:
  $E_F = E_C - k_B T \ln(N_C/N_D)$.
- **Fermi level in p-type**:
  $E_F = E_V + k_B T \ln(N_V/N_A)$.
- **Mass-action law**: $np = n_i^2$ (thermal equilibrium).
- **Heterojunction band offsets**: $\Delta E_C + \Delta E_V = \Delta E_g$; for GaAs/AlGaAs, $\Delta E_C : \Delta E_V \approx 60:40$.
- **GaAs**: $E_g = 1.42$ eV, direct, $\lambda = 873$ nm (NIR laser).
- **GaN**: $E_g = 3.4$ eV, direct, $\lambda = 365$ nm (UV/blue LED).
- **InP**: $E_g = 1.35$ eV, direct, $\lambda = 920$ nm (telecom laser at 1.55 $\mu$m requires quaternary InGaAsP).
- **CdTe**: $E_g = 1.45$ eV, direct, used in thin-film solar cells and IR detectors.
- **HgCdTe**: $E_g$ tunable from 0 to 1.5 eV by adjusting Cd fraction — primary IR detector material.
- **High-symmetry points** in the Brillouin zone: $\Gamma$ (center), $X$ (face), $L$ (corner) — used to identify direct/indirect nature.
- **Donor level $E_D$** lies just below $E_C$ (typically 0.001–0.05 eV below for III-V compounds).
- **Acceptor level $E_A$** lies just above $E_V$ (typically 0.001–0.05 eV above for III-V compounds).
- **Wavelength–bandgap relation**: $\lambda(\mu m) = 1.24 / E_g(eV)$ — memorize this formula.
- **Energy conversion**: 1 eV = $1.602 \times 10^{-19}$ J = $1.602 \times 10^{-12}$ erg.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Types of HBTs: III-V Semiconductor
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEEVT413 - COMPOUND SEMICONDUCTORS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Types of HBTs: III-V Semiconductor

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

A **Heterojunction Bipolar Transistor (HBT)** is a specialized bipolar junction transistor (BJT) in which the emitter, base, and collector regions are fabricated from **dissimilar semiconductor materials**, producing one or more *heterojunctions* (interfaces between two different semiconductors with differing bandgap energies). When the constituent materials are drawn from **Group III** (Al, Ga, In) and **Group V** (N, P, As, Sb) of the periodic table, the device is classified as a **III-V Compound Semiconductor HBT**.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Definition (Board-Examiner Standard):**
> An HBT is a three-terminal bipolar device in which the emitter–base junction is a *wide-bandgap emitter / narrow-bandgap base* heterojunction, engineered to provide simultaneous high emitter injection efficiency and low base resistance — a combination that is fundamentally impossible in a homojunction BJT.

### 1.2 Intuitive Analogy — "The Specially Designed Valve"

Imagine a standard water tap (homojunction BJT) where the inlet pipe, valve, and outlet pipe are all made of the **same metal**. The valve (base) cannot be made too thin or it leaks, but if it is too thick, water flow (current) is restricted.

Now imagine a **composite tap**: the inlet pipe is made of *strong reinforced steel* (wide bandgap emitter), the valve is a *thin, highly-conductive brass disc* (narrow bandgap, heavily doped base), and the outlet pipe is regular metal (collector). The reinforced inlet can apply high pressure without leaking, while the thin valve can still be turned with minimal effort and conduct a lot of water.

This is precisely what an HBT achieves in semiconductor physics:

| Component | Homojunction BJT | III-V HBT |
|---|---|---|
| Emitter | Same material, low bandgap | **Wide bandgap** (e.g., AlGaAs) |
| Base | Same material | **Narrow bandgap** (e.g., GaAs) |
| Base doping | Must be low (otherwise injection efficiency drops) | Can be **very high** (low base resistance) |
| Result | Speed vs. gain trade-off | **Simultaneous high gain + high speed** |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Syllabus Highlight (KTU Module 2):**
> The III-V material systems most frequently examined are **(i) AlGaAs/GaAs**, **(ii) InGaP/GaAs**, **(iii) InP/InGaAs**, and **(iv) AlInAs/InGaAs**. Students must know the energy bandgap, lattice constant, and band alignment (Type-I vs. Type-II) of each.

### 1.3 Why Use III-V Materials? — Key Physical Constants

The defining physical constants that govern the choice of a III-V system are:

- **Energy Bandgap ($E_g$):** typically **0.7 eV – 3.4 eV** across the III-V family, allowing *bandgap engineering*.
- **Electron Mobility ($\mu_n$):** in GaAs, $\mu_n \approx \mathbf{8500 \ cm^2/V\cdot s}$ — nearly **6× higher** than Si ($\approx 1350$).
- **Saturation Velocity ($v_{sat}$):** $\approx \mathbf{1.0 \times 10^7 \ cm/s}$ in GaAs versus $1.0 \times 10^7$ for Si, but the *peak* velocity in GaAs is higher.
- **Effective Mass ($m^*$)** of electrons: lighter in direct-gap III-Vs ⇒ higher mobility.

> [!TIP]
> **Why this matters for HBTs:** Lighter electrons in III-Vs mean **faster transit through the base**, and the wide-bandgap emitter suppresses *back-injection of holes* from base to emitter — boosting injection efficiency $\gamma$ toward unity without forcing the base to be lightly doped.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Structural Anatomy of an HBT

A standard N-p-n III-V HBT consists of the following epitaxial layers (grown via MOCVD/MBE):

1. **Substrate:** Semi-insulating GaAs or InP (resistivity $\rho > \mathbf{10^7 \ \Omega\cdot cm}$)
2. **Sub-collector (n⁺):** Heavily doped n-type collector contact layer
3. **Collector (n):** Lightly doped drift region
4. **Base (p⁺):** Narrow bandgap, **heavily doped** (e.g., $N_A = 10^{19} - 10^{20} \ cm^{-3}$)
5. **Emitter (n):** Wide bandgap, moderately doped
6. **Cap layer (n⁺):** Ohmic contact layer

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Bandgap Engineering Rule (KTU Favourite):** Always $E_{g,emitter} > E_{g,base}$. The conduction band offset $\Delta E_C$ should be **large and positive** to confine electrons in the base and prevent back-diffusion; the valence band offset $\Delta E_V$ should be **large** to block hole back-injection.

## 2.2 Operational Principles — Step-by-Step Logic

**Step 1 — Forward bias the emitter–base (E-B) heterojunction.**
Electrons from the wide-gap emitter are injected into the narrow-gap base. Because $\Delta E_C > 0$, there is a *potential spike/step* in the conduction band that aids injection.

**Step 2 — Minority carrier transport across the base.**
Electrons (minority carriers in the p⁺ base) diffuse across the thin base region. The diffusion time is:

$$
\tau_B = \frac{W_B^2}{2 D_n}
$$

where $W_B$ is the base width and $D_n$ is the electron diffusion coefficient in the base.

**Step 3 — Collection at the reverse-biased B-C junction.**
Electrons arriving at the collector depletion edge are swept into the collector by the electric field. Transit time across the depletion region is:

$$
\tau_C = \frac{X_{BC}}{2 v_{sat}}
$$

**Step 4 — Why the heterojunction matters.**
For a homojunction BJT, the emitter injection efficiency is:

$$
\gamma = \frac{D_n N_D X_E}{D_p N_A X_B} \leq 1
$$

This forces the designer to choose a *low $N_A$* in the base ⇒ *high base resistance* ⇒ *low speed*.

For an HBT, the wide-gap emitter provides an **extra potential barrier** $\Delta E_V$ in the valence band that suppresses hole back-injection by a Boltzmann factor $\exp(-\Delta E_V / kT)$. The effective $\gamma$ becomes:

$$
\gamma \approx \frac{1}{1 + \dfrac{D_p N_A X_B}{D_n N_D X_E} \exp\!\left(-\dfrac{\Delta E_V}{kT}\right)}
$$

Because $\Delta E_V$ is typically **0.2 – 0.4 eV**, the exponential suppression factor is $\approx 10^{-4}$, allowing the base to be **heavily doped** without sacrificing $\gamma$.

## 2.3 The Five Primary III-V HBT Material Systems

| System | E-Gap Emitter | E-Gap Base | Lattice-Match Substrate | Band Alignment | Key Application |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| **AlGaAs / GaAs** | $\mathbf{Al_{x}Ga_{1-x}As}$ (1.42–2.16 eV) | GaAs (1.42 eV) | GaAs | **Type-I** (straddling) | RF power amplifiers, mobile handsets |
| **InGaP / GaAs** | In$_{0.5}$Ga$_{0.5}$P (1.86 eV) | GaAs (1.42 eV) | GaAs | **Type-I** | Wireless PA (replaces AlGaAs; better reliability) |
| **InP / InGaAs** | InP (1.35 eV) | In$_{0.53}$Ga$_{0.47}$As (0.75 eV) | InP | **Type-I** | 40–100+ GHz, fibre-optic OEICs |
| **AlInAs / InGaAs** | Al$_{0.48}$In$_{0.52}$As (1.45 eV) | In$_{0.53}$Ga$_{0.47}$As (0.75 eV) | InP | **Type-I** | 77 GHz automotive radar, mm-wave |
| **GaN / InGaN (or AlGaN)** | AlGaN (3.4–6.2 eV) | GaN (3.4 eV) | SiC / Sapphire | Type-I (hetero-polar) | High-temperature, high-power RF |

## 2.4 The Three Heterojunction Band Alignments

The relative positions of $E_C$ and $E_V$ in the two semiconductors define the heterojunction type:

- **Type-I (Straddling):** $E_C$ of the narrow-gap material is *below* $E_C$ of the wide-gap, AND $E_V$ of the narrow-gap is *above* $E_V$ of the wide-gap. Both electrons and holes are confined in the narrow-gap layer. **Most HBTs use Type-I.**
- **Type-II (Staggered):** $E_C$ and $E_V$ of material A are both *below* those of material B. Electrons and holes are spatially separated. Example: InAs/GaSb.
- **Type-III (Broken-Gap):** $E_V$ of material A lies *above* $E_C$ of material B. Used in tunnel diodes rather than HBTs.

## 2.5 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| # | Formula / Concept | Expression | Units / Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | AlGaAs bandgap | $E_g(x) = 1.424 + 1.247x$ (for $0 \leq x \leq 0.45$, direct) | **eV** |
| 2 | InGaAs bandgap | $E_g(y) = 1.425 - 1.501y + 0.176y^2$ (at 300 K) | **eV**, for $0 \leq y \leq 1$ |
| 3 | InGaP bandgap | $E_g(x) = 1.351 + 0.643x + 0.786x^2$ | **eV** |
| 4 | Conduction-band offset (AlGaAs/GaAs) | $\Delta E_C \approx 0.67 \cdot \Delta E_g$ | eV, **65:35 rule** |
| 5 | Valence-band offset (AlGaAs/GaAs) | $\Delta E_V \approx 0.33 \cdot \Delta E_g$ | eV, **65:35 rule** |
| 6 | Vegard's Law (lattice constant) | $a(A_xB_{1-x}C) = x \cdot a(AC) + (1-x) \cdot a(BC)$ | Å |
| 7 | Common-current gain | $\beta = \dfrac{\alpha}{1-\alpha}$ | dimensionless |
| 8 | Emitter injection efficiency | $\gamma = \dfrac{I_{n,E}}{I_{n,E} + I_{p,E}}$ | dimensionless |
| 9 | Base transport factor | $\alpha_T = \dfrac{1}{\cosh(W_B / L_n)}$ | dimensionless |
| 10 | Cut-off frequency | $f_T = \dfrac{1}{2\pi(\tau_E + \tau_B + \tau_C + \tau_{CC})}$ | Hz |
| 11 | Maximum oscillation frequency | $f_{max} = \sqrt{\dfrac{f_T}{8\pi R_B C_{BC}}}$ | Hz |
| 12 | Kirk effect critical current | $I_{Kirk} = q v_{sat} A N_D$ | A |
| 13 | Energy-gap vs. composition (general) | $E_g(x) = E_{g,BC} + (E_{g,AC} - E_{g,BC}) \cdot x + x(1-x)C$ | eV (bowing parameter $C$) |
| 14 | Thermal voltage | $V_T = kT/q \approx \mathbf{25.85 \ mV}$ at 300 K | V |

> [!WARNING]
> **Pipes inside formulas were replaced** with `\vert` or `\mid` (e.g., $\vert x \vert$) — never use the literal `|` character inside a markdown table cell.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: AlGaAs Bandgap as a Function of Al Mole Fraction $x$

This is a **guaranteed 7-mark derivation** in the KTU 2024 ESE. The student must start from the known bandgaps of the *end-point binaries*:

- $E_g(\text{GaAs}) = 1.424 \ \text{eV}$ at 300 K (direct gap)
- $E_g(\text{AlAs}) = 2.168 \ \text{eV}$ at 300 K (indirect for $x > 0.45$)
- Direct-to-indirect crossover: $x_c \approx 0.45$

**Step 1 — Apply a linear (Vegard) interpolation in bandgap:**

$$
E_g^{linear}(x) = (1-x) \cdot E_g(\text{GaAs}) + x \cdot E_g(\text{AlAs})
$$

**Step 2 — Substitute the numerical end-point values:**

$$
E_g^{linear}(x) = (1-x)(1.424) + x(2.168)
$$

**Step 3 — Expand and collect terms in $x$:**

$$
E_g^{linear}(x) = 1.424 - 1.424x + 2.168x
$$

$$
E_g^{linear}(x) = 1.424 + 0.744 x
$$

**Step 4 — Apply the empirical bowing correction.** Experimental data shows a slight downward bow. The standard KTU-approved empirical expression is:

$$
E_g^{\Gamma}(x) = 1.424 + 1.247 x \quad \text{for } 0 \leq x \leq 0.45 \ (\text{direct gap})
$$

The coefficient **1.247** is *not* the linear coefficient 0.744; it is empirically fitted because the bowing parameter $C \approx -0.127$ eV corrects the linear interpolation.

**Step 5 — Verify at the boundary $x = 0.45$:**

$$
E_g(0.45) = 1.424 + 1.247(0.45) = 1.424 + 0.561 = 1.985 \ \text{eV}
$$

This matches the experimentally observed crossover value $\approx \mathbf{1.99 \ eV}$ at $x = 0.45$ (the indirect AlAs-like $X$-valley then takes over).

**Step 6 — For $x > 0.45$ (indirect regime):**

$$
E_g^{X}(x) = 1.900 + 0.143 x + 0.211 x^2
$$

**Engineering meaning:** Devices (lasers, HBTs) are normally restricted to $x \leq 0.30$ so the alloy remains *direct-gap*, which is essential for efficient radiative recombination and high electron mobility.

---

## 3.2 Derivation: Lattice-Matched Condition for InGaAs on InP

**Given:** Substrate = InP, lattice constant $a_{\text{InP}} = 5.8687$ Å.
End-points: $a_{\text{InAs}} = 6.0584$ Å, $a_{\text{GaAs}} = 5.6533$ Å.

**Step 1 — Apply Vegard's law to $\text{In}_{y}\text{Ga}_{1-y}\text{As}$:**

$$
a(y) = y \cdot a_{\text{InAs}} + (1-y) \cdot a_{\text{GaAs}}
$$

**Step 2 — Set $a(y) = a_{\text{InP}}$ for lattice matching:**

$$
y(6.0584) + (1-y)(5.6533) = 5.8687
$$

**Step 3 — Expand the LHS:**

$$
6.0584y + 5.6533 - 5.6533y = 5.8687
$$

**Step 4 — Group the $y$ terms:**

$$
(6.0584 - 5.6533) y + 5.6533 = 5.8687
$$

$$
0.4051 \, y = 5.8687 - 5.6533 = 0.2154
$$

**Step 5 — Solve for $y$:**

$$
y = \frac{0.2154}{0.4051} \approx 0.532
$$

**Step 6 — Round to the conventional value $y = 0.53$.** Thus the **lattice-matched composition** is $\mathbf{In_{0.53}Ga_{0.47}As}$ on InP, with bandgap $E_g = 0.75$ eV.

**Bandgap check using the quadratic formula:**

$$
E_g(0.53) = 1.425 - 1.501(0.53) + 0.176(0.53)^2
$$

$$
= 1.425 - 0.7955 + 0.0495 = 0.679 \ \text{eV}
$$

(The standard literature value is **0.75 eV** at 300 K; the small discrepancy arises from the precision of the bowing coefficient used.)

---

## 3.3 Derivation: HBT Common-Emitter Current Gain $\beta$

**Step 1 — Start from the standard BJT relation $\alpha = \gamma \cdot \alpha_T \cdot \alpha_I$,** where $\gamma$ is the emitter injection efficiency, $\alpha_T$ is the base transport factor, and $\alpha_I$ is the collector multiplication factor ($\approx 1$ in normal operation).

**Step 2 — For an HBT with valence-band offset $\Delta E_V$:**

$$
\gamma = \frac{1}{1 + \dfrac{N_A D_p X_B}{N_D D_n X_E} \exp\!\left(-\dfrac{\Delta E_V}{V_T}\right)}
$$

**Step 3 — For the base transport factor** in the thin-base limit ($W_B \ll L_n$):

$$
\alpha_T \approx 1 - \frac{W_B^2}{2 L_n^2}
$$

**Step 4 — Combine to get $\alpha$ and then $\beta$:**

$$
\beta = \frac{\alpha}{1-\alpha} \approx \frac{1}{\dfrac{1-\gamma}{\gamma} + \dfrac{1-\alpha_T}{\alpha_T}}
$$

**Step 5 — Numerical illustration** for an AlGaAs/GaAs HBT with $x = 0.3$:
- $\Delta E_g = 1.247 \times 0.3 = 0.374$ eV
- $\Delta E_V = 0.33 \times 0.374 = 0.123$ eV
- At 300 K, $V_T = 0.02585$ V ⇒ $\Delta E_V / V_T = 0.123 / 0.02585 = 4.76$
- $\exp(-4.76) = 8.6 \times 10^{-3}$

Even this small valence-band offset gives a factor of $\approx 100$× suppression of hole back-injection — exactly why III-V HBTs can have $N_A \approx 10^{19} \ cm^{-3}$ and still maintain $\beta > 100$.

## 3.4 Analytical Comparison Matrix

| Parameter | AlGaAs/GaAs HBT | InGaP/GaAs HBT | InP/InGaAs HBT | AlInAs/InGaAs HBT |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| $E_{g,emitter}$ (eV) | 1.80 | 1.86 | 1.35 | 1.45 |
| $E_{g,base}$ (eV) | 1.42 | 1.42 | 0.75 | 0.75 |
| $\Delta E_C$ (eV) | 0.25 | 0.10 | 0.20 | 0.50 |
| $\Delta E_V$ (eV) | 0.12 | 0.30 | 0.35 | 0.20 |
| $f_T$ (GHz) | 50–80 | 100 | 200+ | 300+ |
| $f_{max}$ (GHz) | 100 | 150 | 300 | 500 |
| DX centres? | **YES** (problem) | NO | NO | NO |
| Selective etch? | Difficult | **Easy** | Moderate | **Easy** |
| Main use | Mature, low cost | **Wireless PA standard** | Fibre OEICs | mm-wave radar |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Energy-Band Diagram of a Type-I N-p-n HBT (Forward Active)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph E["EMITTER (n-AlGaAs) Eg = 1.80 eV"]
        nodeEc_E["Ec - high"]
        nodeEf["Fermi level EF"]
        nodeEv_E["Ev - high"]
    end

    subgraph B["BASE (p-GaAs) Eg = 1.42 eV"]
        nodeEc_B["Ec - lower (deltaEc step DOWN)"]
        nodeEv_B["Ev - higher (deltaEv step UP)"]
    end

    subgraph C["COLLECTOR (n-GaAs) Eg = 1.42 eV"]
        nodeEc_C["Ec - lower"]
        nodeEv_C["Ev - higher"]
    end

    nodeEc_E --> nodeEc_B
    nodeEv_E --> nodeEv_B
    nodeEc_B --> nodeEc_C
    nodeEv_B --> nodeEv_C
```

> The arrows on the right show the **direction of band bending**. Note the **spike** in $E_C$ at the E-B interface (aids electron injection) and the **notch** in $E_V$ (blocks hole back-injection). This is the *fingerprint* of a Type-I heterojunction.

---

## 4.2 Physical Layer Stack of an AlGaAs/GaAs HBT (N-p-n)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    nodeCap["Cap layer: n+ GaAs (n = 5e18 cm^-3) -- thickness 100 nm -- for Ohmic contact"]
    nodeEmit["Emitter: n Al0.3Ga0.7As (n = 5e17) -- 150 nm -- wide Eg = 1.80 eV"]
    nodeBase["Base: p+ GaAs (p = 2e19) -- 80 nm -- narrow Eg = 1.42 eV -- heavy doping allowed"]
    nodeColl["Collector: n- GaAs (n = 1e16) -- 700 nm -- drift region"]
    nodeSubC["Sub-collector: n+ GaAs (n = 5e18) -- 500 nm -- for Ohmic back contact"]
    nodeSub["Substrate: SI-GaAs (rho > 1e7 Ohm cm) -- 600 micrometre"]

    nodeCap --> nodeEmit
    nodeEmit --> nodeBase
    nodeBase --> nodeColl
    nodeColl --> nodeSubC
    nodeSubC --> nodeSub
```

---

## 4.3 Comparison Block Diagram: HBT vs. Homojunction BJT

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    nodeBJT["HOMOJUNCTION BJT\nAll three regions same Eg\nBase must be lightly doped\nLow speed, high base R"]
    nodeHBT["HETEROJUNCTION HBT III-V\nWide Eg emitter, narrow Eg base\nBase can be p+ 1e19 to 1e20\nHigh speed, low base R"]

    nodeBJT -- "Drawback:\nTrade-off between\ngain and speed" --> nodeHBT
    nodeHBT -- "Solution:\nBandgap engineering\nbreaks the trade-off" --> nodeBJT
```

---

## 4.4 Heterojunction Type Classification Flowchart

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    nodeStart["Two semiconductors A and B with bandgaps EgA and EgB"]
    nodeEg["Which Eg is larger?"]
    nodeAlign["Compare Ec and Ev positions"]
    nodeType1["Type-I Straddling\nEc, Ev of narrow-gap INSIDE wide-gap\nEg EgA EgB\nBoth carriers confined in narrow-gap layer\nMOST HBTs USE THIS"]
    nodeType2["Type-II Staggered\nEc and Ev of A both below B\nCarriers spatially separated\nInAs/GaSb example"]
    nodeType3["Type-III Broken-Gap\nEv of A above Ec of B\nStrong interband tunnelling\nTunnel diodes only"]

    nodeStart --> nodeEg
    nodeEg --> nodeAlign
    nodeAlign --> nodeType1
    nodeAlign --> nodeType2
    nodeAlign --> nodeType3
```

---

## 4.5 Functional Architecture of an HBT in a Wireless PA System

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    nodeRF["RF Input Signal\n1.9 GHz for GSM\nor 2.4 GHz for WiFi"]
    nodeMatch["Input Matching Network\n50 Ohm matching"]
    nodeHBT["HBT Power Stage\nAlGaAs/GaAs or\nInGaP/GaAs HBT\nbeta approx 100\nfT approx 50 GHz"]
    nodeOutMatch["Output Matching Network\nHarmonic filtering"]
    nodeAnt["Antenna\n50 Ohm load"]
    nodeBias["DC Bias Network\nVce approx 3.5 V\nIb approx 1 mA"]

    nodeRF --> nodeMatch --> nodeHBT --> nodeOutMatch --> nodeAnt
    nodeBias --> nodeHBT
```
