topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEEVT413 - COMPOUND SEMICONDUCTORS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** High Electron Mobility Transistors (HEMT): GaAs HEMT - Structure of GaAs HEMT

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# MODULE 2 — HIGH ELECTRON MOBILITY TRANSISTORS (HEMT)

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition
A **High Electron Mobility Transistor (HEMT)** is a field-effect transistor built on a **heterojunction** (interface between two semiconductors of different bandgaps) in which the conducting channel is a **Two-Dimensional Electron Gas (2-DEG)** formed at the hetero-interface. Because the electrons in the 2-DEG reside in an *undoped* wide-bandgap or narrow-bandgap layer while their parent donor atoms are physically separated in a doped layer of a *different* material, **ionized-impurity scattering is dramatically suppressed**, yielding electron mobilities far higher than those achievable in a uniformly doped MESFET channel.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Synonyms recognized by KTU examiners and IEEE literature:**
> - **HEMT** — High Electron Mobility Transistor (most common)
> - **MODFET** — Modulation-Doped Field-Effect Transistor (original Bell Labs / Mimura 1980 name)
> - **HFET** — Heterostructure FET
> - **2-DEG FET** — Two-Dimensional Electron Gas FET
> - **TEGFET** — Two-dimensional Electron Gas Field-Effect Transistor

The canonical **GaAs HEMT** uses the **AlGaAs / GaAs** heterojunction discovered by Mimura, Delagebeaudeuf, and Solomon in **1980–1982**, and is the workhorse of low-noise microwave, mm-wave, and high-speed digital ICs (e.g., DBS LNBs, satellite receivers, automotive radar at 77 GHz).

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — "The Rolling Ball on a Smooth Highway"

> [!NOTE]
> **Plain-English Analogy**
>
> Imagine a billiard table (the doped channel of a MESFET). The balls (electrons) roll across it easily, but the table is studded with bumpers (ionized donor atoms) that constantly knock the balls off course — this is *ionized-impurity scattering*, which limits mobility.
>
> Now imagine a **different** setup: an elevated, perfectly smooth glass floor (an *undoped* GaAs layer) sitting just above a carpet of positively charged bumpers (ionized donors in AlGaAs). Because of electrostatic attraction, electrons fall down and float on the underside of the glass floor — they never touch the bumpers. Result: balls glide nearly frictionlessly, like marbles on a marble countertop.
>
> - The **glass floor** = undoped GaAs (the channel hosting the 2-DEG)
> - The **bumpers below** = ionized Si donors in n-AlGaAs
> - The **floating electrons** = the 2-DEG

This *spatial separation* of electrons from their parent donors is the central physical trick of the HEMT, and is precisely why **mobility reaches ≈ 8500–9500 cm²/V·s at 300 K** (and > 2 × 10⁶ cm²/V·s at 4.2 K in modulation-doped structures) compared to ≈ 5000 cm²/V·s in doped GaAs MESFET channels.

### 1.3 Standard Metrics at a Glance
| Metric | Typical 300 K value | Significance |
|---|---|---|
| **Electron mobility (μₙ) in 2-DEG** | **8000 – 9500 cm²/V·s** | Determines transconductance & speed |
| **2-DEG sheet density (nₛ)** | **1 × 10¹² – 1.5 × 10¹² cm⁻²** | Conduction current capability |
| **Threshold voltage (Vₜₕ)** | typically **−0.5 V to −2.0 V** | Normally-on (depletion-mode) device |
| **Maximum oscillation freq. (f_max)** | up to **~ 600 GHz (InP-based)** | mm-wave suitability |
| **Cut-off freq. (f_T)** | up to **~ 500 GHz (InP-based)** | Digital switching speed |
| **Al mole fraction (x) in Al_xGa₁₋ₓAs** | **0.20 – 0.30** (GaAs HEMT) | Controls bandgap & ΔE_C |
| **Conduction band offset (ΔE_C)** | **≈ 0.65 × ΔE_g** | Drives 2-DEG confinement |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** The 2024 Scheme PEEVT413 Module 2 mandates the *structure*, *operating principle*, and the *role of each layer* in a GaAs HEMT. Mastery of layer thicknesses, doping, and the heterojunction energy-band picture is essential for both Part A (3-mark) and Part B (14-mark) questions.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Why a Heterojunction? — The Thermodynamic Imperative

In a conventional **MESFET**, donors and electrons coexist in the *same* GaAs layer. Every ionized donor (Si⁺) becomes a Coulombic scattering centre. Reducing doping to raise mobility simultaneously starves the channel of carriers — a fundamental trade-off.

A **heterojunction** breaks this trade-off by exploiting the **conduction-band discontinuity (ΔE_C)** between two III–V alloys. The classic pair is:
- **Al_xGa₁₋ₓAs** (wide bandgap, can be heavily doped n-type with Si)
- **GaAs** (narrow bandgap, kept *intrinsic*/undoped)

Because **E_g(AlGaAs) > E_g(GaAs)**, and the discontinuity is apportioned roughly **65% to the conduction band and 35% to the valence band**, the AlGaAs conduction band sits at a *higher* energy than GaAs. Electrons from the donor states in AlGaAs therefore "fall" across the hetero-interface and accumulate in a **quantum well** on the GaAs side, only a few nanometres thick.

## 2.2 The Magic of Modulation Doping

> [!NOTE]
> **Modulation doping** is the *non-uniform* (spatially modulated) placement of dopants: donors are placed in the wide-bandgap material (AlGaAs), while the narrow-bandgap material (GaAs) where the 2-DEG forms is kept **intrinsic**.

**Logical steps of operation:**

1. **Doping of AlGaAs:** Si donors are introduced (typically n = 1 × 10¹⁸ cm⁻³) into AlGaAs. They ionize to Si⁺ + e⁻ at 300 K.
2. **Band bending:** The freed electrons see a lower-energy pocket in the adjacent GaAs and diffuse across the interface, leaving behind a positively charged depletion region in AlGaAs.
3. **Equilibrium:** Diffusion stops when the built-in electric field from the ionized donors exactly opposes further electron motion. A triangular quantum well forms at the GaAs side of the interface.
4. **2-DEG formation:** The well is so narrow (~ 10 nm) in the growth direction that electron energy is quantized in the perpendicular direction (z) but free in the (x, y) plane. Motion in z is frozen into discrete sub-bands (E₀, E₁, E₂, …), so electrons behave as a **2-D sheet** with density n_s (cm⁻²).
5. **High mobility:** Since the GaAs side is undoped, there are **no ionized impurities in the electron path** — the dominant scattering mechanism is *phonon* scattering, giving mobilities of ≈ 9000 cm²/V·s at 300 K.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering significance:** Modulation doping separates the *source of carriers* (AlGaAs) from the *carrier transport region* (GaAs). This is the conceptual ancestor of all modern high-mobility devices — including Si/SiGe modulation-doped channels, p-HEMTs, InGaAs/InP HEMTs, and GaN HEMTs.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula | Description | Typical Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $E_g(x) = 1.424 + 1.247\,x$ (for $0 \le x \le 0.45$, 300 K) | Bandgap of Al_xGa₁₋xAs | eV |
| 2 | $\Delta E_g = 1.247\,x$ | Bandgap difference at the heterojunction | eV |
| 3 | $\Delta E_C \approx 0.65\,\Delta E_g \approx 0.81\,x$ | Conduction-band offset | eV |
| 4 | $\Delta E_V \approx 0.35\,\Delta E_g \approx 0.44\,x$ | Valence-band offset | eV |
| 5 | $n_s = \dfrac{\varepsilon_{AlGaAs}}{q\,(d_d + \Delta d)}\left(V_{GS} - V_{th} - \dfrac{E_F}{q}\right)$ | 2-DEG sheet density under gate | cm⁻² |
| 6 | $n_{s0} = \dfrac{\varepsilon_{AlGaAs}\,N_D\,d_d^2}{2\varepsilon_{AlGaAs}} \cdot \dfrac{1}{(d_d + \Delta d)}$ | Sheet density at V_GS = 0 (depletion approx.) | cm⁻² |
| 7 | $V_{th} = \phi_b - \dfrac{\Delta E_C}{q} - \dfrac{q\,N_D\,d_d^2}{2\varepsilon_{AlGaAs}}$ | Threshold voltage | V |
| 8 | $\phi_b \approx 0.7 - 0.8\ \text{V}$ | Schottky barrier of Ti/Pt/Au on n-AlGaAs | V |
| 9 | $g_m = \dfrac{\partial I_D}{\partial V_{GS}} = \dfrac{\varepsilon_{AlGaAs}\,\mu_n\,W}{L\,(d_d + \Delta d)}\,V_{DS}$ | Intrinsic transconductance (linear region) | S |
| 10 | $I_{D,sat} = \dfrac{\mu_n\,\varepsilon_{AlGaAs}\,W}{2L\,(d_d + \Delta d)}\,\left(V_{GS} - V_{th}\right)^2$ | Saturation drain current | A |
| 11 | $f_T = \dfrac{g_m}{2\pi C_{gs}} \approx \dfrac{\mu_n\,(V_{GS}-V_{th})}{2\pi L^2}$ | Unity-current-gain cut-off freq. | Hz |
| 12 | $\lambda_{de-Broglie} = \dfrac{h}{\sqrt{2 m^{\ast} E}}$ | Justifies quantum confinement in 2-DEG | nm |
| 13 | $\varepsilon_{AlGaAs}(x) = 13.1 - 3.0\,x$ | Static permittivity of Al_xGa₁₋xAs | — |

> [!NOTE]
> In Formula 5, $d_d$ = thickness of the doped AlGaAs donor layer, $\Delta d$ = effective distance of the 2-DEG from the interface (≈ 8 nm, the centroid of the ground-state wavefunction). The presence of an **undoped AlGaAs spacer** of thickness $d_s \approx 2$–$3$ nm between the doped layer and the hetero-interface further reduces remote-ion scattering and is *critical* for achieving μₙ > 8500 cm²/V·s.

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Domain | Why GaAs HEMT? |
|---|---|
| **Direct-broadcast satellite (DBS) LNBs** at 12 GHz | Low noise figure (0.3–0.5 dB) thanks to high μₙ |
| **Automotive 77-GHz radar (Adaptive Cruise Control)** | High f_T and f_max allow mm-wave MMIC integration |
| **Radio-astronomy receivers** (1–100 GHz) | Cryogenic noise performance is exceptional |
| **High-speed A/D converters & clock-distribution** | High g_m/C_gg ratio enables sub-ns switching |
| **GaN-on-SiC HEMT variant** | Later descendant — same principle, larger ΔE_C, high power |
| **5G/6G front-end modules** | Pseudomorphic HEMTs (pHEMTs) with InGaAs channel push μₙ > 10 000 cm²/V·s |

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of 2-DEG Sheet Density $n_s$ (the HEMT heart)

The standard textbook treatment (Sze, *Physics of Semiconductor Devices*; Pierret, *Semiconductor Device Fundamentals*) starts from the **depletion approximation** on the AlGaAs side and the **Fermi-level pinning** at the 2-DEG.

### Step 1 — Band diagram & charge neutrality

Charge neutrality requires that the negative charge in the 2-DEG (sheet density $n_s$, units cm⁻²) equals the total positive charge left behind in the AlGaAs depletion region:

$$q\,n_s = q\,N_D\,d$$

where $N_D$ is the donor concentration in AlGaAs and $d$ is the width of the depletion region that forms in AlGaAs. (We ignore charges in the undoped spacer and undoped AlGaAs Schottky layer.)

### Step 2 — Voltage drop across the AlGaAs layer

The voltage drop across the AlGaAs depletion region is given by the depletion approximation:

$$V_{AlGaAs} = \frac{q\,N_D\,d^2}{2\,\varepsilon_{AlGaAs}}$$

This must be the difference between the gate bias (referred to the 2-DEG) and the built-in potential plus the conduction-band offset.

### Step 3 — Threshold-voltage relation

Applying Kirchhoff's voltage law around the gate–AlGaAs–2-DEG loop, and using the Schottky barrier height $\phi_b$ at the metal/AlGaAs interface, we obtain:

$$V_{GS} = \phi_b - \frac{\Delta E_C}{q} - \frac{q\,N_D\,d^2}{2\,\varepsilon_{AlGaAs}} - \frac{E_F}{q}$$

where the term $-\dfrac{E_F}{q}$ accounts for the fact that the 2-DEG Fermi level lies *above* the GaAs conduction-band minimum at the interface.

### Step 4 — Define the threshold voltage

The **threshold voltage** $V_{th}$ is defined as the gate bias at which $n_s \to 0$ (i.e., the 2-DEG is just depleted). Setting $n_s = 0$ in the expressions above gives:

$$V_{th} = \phi_b - \frac{\Delta E_C}{q} - \frac{q\,N_D\,d_{d0}^{\,2}}{2\,\varepsilon_{AlGaAs}}$$

where $d_{d0}$ is the *maximum* depletion width at pinch-off (essentially the total donor-layer thickness when fully depleted).

### Step 5 — General $n_s(V_{GS})$ expression

Subtracting the threshold expression and inserting the centroid offset $\Delta d$ (distance of the 2-DEG wavefunction centroid from the hetero-interface) yields the standard HEMT charge-control relation:

$$n_s(V_{GS}) = \frac{\varepsilon_{AlGaAs}}{q\,(d_d + \Delta d)}\left(V_{GS} - V_{th}\right)$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering insight:** The denominator $(d_d + \Delta d)$ plays the same role as the oxide thickness $t_{ox}$ does in a MOSFET. The HEMT therefore behaves like a *high-electron-mobility MOSFET* with an "effective oxide" made of doped AlGaAs. The thinner the donor layer, the larger the gate's capacitive control over the 2-DEG, and the higher the transconductance.

### Step 6 — Linearisation for the saturation regime

For an HEMT biased in saturation with drain voltage $V_{DS}$, the drain current is:

$$I_{D,sat} = \frac{\mu_n\,\varepsilon_{AlGaAs}\,W}{2L\,(d_d + \Delta d)}\left(V_{GS} - V_{th}\right)^2$$

This is the same **square-law** dependence as a long-channel MOSFET, but with a *higher* $\mu_n$ (≈ 9000 cm²/V·s vs. 600–1400 cm²/V·s for Si MOSFETs), which is why HEMTs are superior for high-frequency and low-noise applications.

---

## 3.2 Analytical Matrix — Layer-by-Layer Function of the GaAs HEMT

| Layer (top → bottom) | Material | Typical thickness | Doping | Function |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| (L7) Ohmic contact (source/drain) | AuGe/Ni/Au alloyed | ≈ 100 nm | — | Low-resistance contact to 2-DEG |
| (L6) Cap layer | n⁺-GaAs | 30 – 50 nm | $n = 4\!\times\!10^{18}$ cm⁻³ | Low-resistance ohmic sink, prevents AlGaAs oxidation |
| (L5) Schottky layer | **Undoped** AlGaAs | 20 – 30 nm | intrinsic | Sustains gate–Schottky depletion; blocks parasitic MESFET conduction |
| (L4) Donor layer | n-AlGaAs (x ≈ 0.22–0.30) | 30 – 60 nm | $n = 1\!\times\!10^{18}$ cm⁻³ | Source of electrons that populate the 2-DEG |
| (L3) Spacer | **Undoped** AlGaAs | 2 – 5 nm | intrinsic | Reduces remote-ion scattering → high mobility |
| **(L2) Channel / 2-DEG host** | **Intrinsic GaAs** | ≈ 0.5 – 1 μm | $<10^{14}$ cm⁻³ | Hosts the high-mobility 2-DEG (10 nm thick quantum well) |
| (L1) Superlattice buffer | AlAs / GaAs (period 5–10 nm) | 0.5 – 1 μm | intrinsic | Prevents impurities from semi-insulating substrate reaching the 2-DEG |
| (L0) Substrate | **Semi-insulating (SI) GaAs** | 400 – 625 μm | EL2 / Cr compensated | Mechanical support; high resistivity (>10⁷ Ω·cm) for low parasitic capacitance |

> [!NOTE]
> **Why semi-insulating GaAs?** Unlike Si (which is conductive), GaAs can be made semi-insulating by introducing mid-gap deep donors (EL2, an As-antisite defect) or by Cr/O compensation. This **eliminates the conductive substrate parasitics** that plague silicon-on-anything technology, giving clean mm-wave performance.

## 3.3 Quick Calculation — Sheet Density for a Typical GaAs HEMT

**Given:**
- $x = 0.25$ (Al mole fraction)
- $N_D = 1.0 \times 10^{18}$ cm⁻³
- $d_d = 40$ nm = $4 \times 10^{-6}$ cm
- $\Delta d = 8$ nm = $8 \times 10^{-7}$ cm
- $\varepsilon_0 = 8.854 \times 10^{-14}$ F/cm
- $\varepsilon_r(x) = 13.1 - 3.0(0.25) = 12.35$
- $V_{GS} = 0$ V
- $V_{th} = -1.0$ V (assumed)

**Compute sheet density:**

$$n_s = \frac{(12.35)(8.854\times10^{-14})}{(1.6\times10^{-19})(4\times10^{-6}+8\times10^{-7})}\bigl(0 - (-1.0)\bigr)$$

**Step 1 — Numerator:** $(12.35)(8.854\times10^{-14}) = 1.094 \times 10^{-12}$ F/cm

**Step 2 — Denominator:** $(1.6\times10^{-19})(4.8\times10^{-6}) = 7.68 \times 10^{-25}$ F·cm

**Step 3 — Ratio:** $\dfrac{1.094\times10^{-12}}{7.68\times10^{-25}} = 1.425 \times 10^{12}$ cm⁻²·V⁻¹

**Step 4 — Multiply by $(V_{GS}-V_{th}) = 1.0$ V:**

$$n_s \approx 1.43 \times 10^{12}\ \text{cm}^{-2}$$

This is the **canonical value** quoted in every HEMT textbook — a healthy sanity check that the model is correct.

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 5.1 Part A — Short-Answer Questions (3 marks each)

> **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
> **Q1.** What is a High Electron Mobility Transistor (HEMT)? Why is it also called a MODFET?
> *(3 marks)*

**Model Answer:**
A HEMT is a field-effect transistor whose conducting channel is a **two-dimensional electron gas (2-DEG)** formed at the hetero-interface between two semiconductors with different bandgaps (typically AlGaAs and GaAs). The 2-DEG is created by *modulation doping* — Si donors are placed in the wide-bandgap AlGaAs, while the narrow-bandgap GaAs where electrons accumulate is left undoped. This spatial separation of electrons from their parent donor atoms drastically reduces ionized-impurity scattering, resulting in very high electron mobility (≈ 9000 cm²/V·s at 300 K) and superior high-frequency, low-noise performance compared to a MESFET.

It is called a **MODFET (Modulation-Doped FET)** because the donor concentration is *spatially modulated* (non-uniform) across the hetero-interface — heavy doping in AlGaAs, intrinsic in GaAs. *(3 marks)*

---

> **[KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
> **Q2.** List any *three* advantages of a GaAs HEMT over a conventional GaAs MESFET.
> *(3 marks)*

**Model Answer:**
1. **Higher electron mobility** in the 2-DEG (≈ 9000 cm²/V·s) compared to doped-channel MESFET (≈ 5000 cm²/V·s) due to absence of ionized-impurity scattering. *(1 mark)*
2. **Higher transconductance (g_m)** and **higher unity-current-gain frequency (f_T)**, enabling low-noise microwave and mm-wave amplification. *(1 mark)*
3. **Lower noise figure (NF)** at high frequencies (0.3–0.5 dB at 12 GHz) because the 2-DEG channel is intrinsically low-noise and the SI-GaAs substrate minimises parasitic losses. *(1 mark)*

*(Other acceptable answers: better linearity, semi-insulating substrate reduces parasitic capacitance, can be readily extended to AlGaN/GaN for high-power applications.)*

## 5.2 Part B — 14-Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice)

> **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 / Model Paper 2024 Scheme]**

### ⬛ Question A (14 Marks)

**(a)** Draw the epitaxial layer structure of a conventional GaAs HEMT and explain the function of *each* layer in detail. *(7 marks)*

**(b)** With the help of a neat energy-band diagram, explain the formation of the **2-DEG** at the AlGaAs / GaAs hetero-interface. Define *modulation doping* and state why it leads to high electron mobility. *(7 marks)*

#### Model Solution — (a) Layer structure (7 marks)

The GaAs HEMT is grown by **MBE** or **MOCVD** in the following sequence (top → bottom, complete with thicknesses, doping, and roles):

1. **Ohmic contact metallisation (AuGe/Ni/Au)** — alloyed at ≈ 430 °C to form low-resistance source/drain contacts. *(0.5 mark)*
2. **n⁺-GaAs cap layer (30–50 nm, n ≈ 4 × 10¹⁸ cm⁻³)** — provides a low-bandgap surface for non-alloyed or alloyed ohmics, prevents oxidation of the underlying AlGaAs. *(0.5 mark)*
3. **Undoped AlGaAs Schottky layer (20–30 nm)** — supports the gate Schottky depletion, prevents parallel conduction (a parasitic MESFET) directly under the gate. *(1 mark)*
4. **n-AlGaAs donor layer (30–60 nm, n ≈ 1 × 10¹⁸ cm⁻³)** — supplies free electrons that populate the 2-DEG. *(1 mark)*
5. **Undoped AlGaAs spacer (2–5 nm)** — physical barrier that prevents electrons from "seeing" the ionised donors, suppressing remote-ion scattering. **(Crucial for high μₙ.)** *(1 mark)*
6. **Intrinsic GaAs channel (≈ 0.5–1 μm, n < 10¹⁴ cm⁻³)** — host of the 2-DEG quantum well; the 2-DEG itself is ≈ 10 nm thick on the GaAs side of the hetero-interface. *(1 mark)*
7. **Superlattice buffer (AlAs/GaAs, 0.5–1 μm, 5–10 nm period)** — blocks substrate impurities (e.g., Cr, EL2) from diffusing into the channel. *(1 mark)*
8. **Semi-insulating (SI) GaAs substrate (400–625 μm, ρ > 10⁷ Ω·cm)** — mechanical support; high resistivity eliminates conductive-substrate parasitic capacitance, vital for mm-wave operation. *(1 mark)*

> [!NOTE]
> **Examiner's tip:** Always quote typical thicknesses and doping levels — KTU valuators reward numerical specificity.

#### Model Solution — (b) Energy-band diagram and 2-DEG formation (7 marks)

1. **Draw the equilibrium energy-band diagram** (E_C, E_V, E_F) across the AlGaAs / GaAs heterojunction with **ΔE_C ≈ 0.81 x eV** at the conduction band and **ΔE_V ≈ 0.44 x eV** at the valence band (for x = 0.25, ΔE_C ≈ 0.20 eV, ΔE_V ≈ 0.11 eV). *(2 marks)*
2. Show **band bending** in the doped AlGaAs due to ionised Si⁺ donors. The Fermi level E_F is uniform in equilibrium. *(1 mark)*
3. Indicate the **triangular potential well** formed on the GaAs side of the interface — this is where the 2-DEG accumulates. Mark the **ground-state sub-band E₀** ≈ 50–70 meV above the GaAs conduction-band minimum. *(1 mark)*
4. **Definition of modulation doping:** *Modulation doping is the non-uniform (spatially modulated) placement of donor impurities in the wide-bandgap material (AlGaAs) while keeping the narrow-bandgap material (GaAs) where conduction occurs undoped/intrinsic.* *(1 mark)*
5. **Why high mobility?** The electrons in the 2-DEG are physically separated from their parent donor ions by the spacer, so the dominant scattering mechanism (ionised-impurity scattering) is suppressed. At 300 K the residual mobility is set by *phonon* scattering only, giving **μₙ ≈ 9000 cm²/V·s**, much higher than the ≈ 5000 cm²/V·s in a uniformly doped GaAs MESFET channel. *(2 marks)*

### ⬛ Question B (14 Marks) — *Alternative Choice*

**(a)** Derive the expression for the **2-DEG sheet density** $n_s$ as a function of gate voltage $V_{GS}$ for a GaAs HEMT. Define the threshold voltage $V_{th}$ in the process. *(7 marks)*

**(b)** Explain with a sketch why **semi-insulating GaAs** is used as the HEMT substrate. List *four* advantages of the modulation-doping technique in HEMTs. *(7 marks)*

#### Model Solution — (a) Derivation (7 marks)

1. Start with the **charge-neutrality** condition: $q n_s = q N_D d_d$, so $n_s = N_D d_d$. *(1 mark)*
2. Apply the **depletion approximation** in AlGaAs to get the potential drop $V_{AlGaAs} = \dfrac{q N_D d_d^{\,2}}{2\varepsilon_{AlGaAs}}$. *(1 mark)*
3. Write the **Kirchhoff voltage law** around the gate–AlGaAs–2-DEG loop:

$$V_{GS} = \phi_b - \frac{\Delta E_C}{q} - V_{AlGaAs} - \frac{E_F}{q}$$ *(2 marks)*

4. Solve for $n_s$ (using $n_s = N_D d_d$) and rearrange:

$$n_s = \frac{\varepsilon_{AlGaAs}}{q (d_d + \Delta d)} \left(V_{GS} - V_{th}\right)$$ *(2 marks)*

5. **Threshold-voltage definition:** $V_{th}$ is the gate voltage at which $n_s \to 0$ (2-DEG pinched off). It is given by:

$$V_{th} = \phi_b - \frac{\Delta E_C}{q} - \frac{q N_D d_{d0}^{\,2}}{2\varepsilon_{AlGaAs}}$$ *(1 mark)*

#### Model Solution — (b) SI-GaAs substrate and modulation-doping advantages (7 marks)

**SI-GaAs substrate (3 marks):**
1. **Sketch:** Cross-section showing a high-resistivity (ρ > 10⁷ Ω·cm) substrate below the active layers; emphasise that the active 2-DEG is electrically isolated from any conducting substrate, so there are *no substrate-pad parasitic capacitances* and *no substrate leakage currents*. *(1 mark)*
2. SI-GaAs is achieved by **deep-level compensation** — either intrinsic EL2 (As-antisite) defects or intentional Cr/O doping — which pins the Fermi level near mid-gap and raises resistivity by 6–8 orders of magnitude over doped GaAs. *(1 mark)*
3. The **resulting low-loss dielectric environment** is what makes mm-wave and sub-mm-wave HEMT MMICs possible. *(1 mark)*

**Four advantages of modulation doping (4 marks):**
1. **Spatial separation of dopants and electrons** — eliminates ionised-impurity scattering in the channel. *(1 mark)*
2. **High 2-DEG mobility** — up to 9000 cm²/V·s at 300 K, exceeding 2 × 10⁶ cm²/V·s at 4.2 K. *(1 mark)*
3. **High sheet carrier density** (10¹² cm⁻²) with *low* background doping — impossible in a uniformly doped MESFET. *(1 mark)*
4. **Independent optimisation** of carrier density (via donor-layer doping/thickness) and mobility (via spacer thickness) — a flexibility MESFETs do not offer. *(1 mark)*

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls (cost you 2–3 marks per answer):**
> 1. **Don't confuse "MODFET" and "MOSFET."** MODFET = Modulation-Doped FET (no oxide). The Schottky barrier replaces the gate oxide.
> 2. **Don't state "no scattering"** in the 2-DEG. Always say "ionised-impurity scattering is *suppressed*; phonon scattering remains."
> 3. **Don't forget the spacer layer** in the layer diagram — its omission is the single most common reason for losing the "function-of-each-layer" mark.
> 4. **Don't use generic "AlGaAs"** in derivations — *always* state the alloy composition $x$ and the corresponding $\Delta E_C \approx 0.81 x$ eV when numerical accuracy is required.
> 5. **Don't confuse the 2-DEG with 3-D bulk conduction.** The 2-DEG is a sheet (cm⁻²), not a volume density (cm⁻³). Use $n_s$ in cm⁻², *not* $n$ in cm⁻³.
