## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **HEMT = MODFET = HFET = 2-DEG FET = TEGFET** — all the same device. *(High-priority KTU keyword.)*
- **The single most important physical idea:** *modulation doping*, i.e., donors in AlGaAs, electrons in GaAs — separated by an undoped AlGaAs **spacer**.
- **Heterojunction:** Al_xGa₁₋xAs / GaAs with $\Delta E_C \approx 0.65 \Delta E_g \approx 0.81 x$ eV drives the 2-DEG.
- **2-DEG location:** ≈ 10 nm thick quantum well on the GaAs side of the hetero-interface; carriers are quantized in the growth direction and free in-plane.
- **Standard metric to memorise:** $n_s \approx 1 \times 10^{12}$ cm⁻², $\mu_n \approx 9000$ cm²/V·s at 300 K, $V_{th} \approx -1$ V (normally-on).
- **Threshold voltage formula:** $V_{th} = \phi_b - \Delta E_C/q - qN_D d_{d0}^2 / (2 \varepsilon_{AlGaAs})$.
- **Layer stack order (top → bottom):** ohmic metal → n⁺-GaAs cap → undoped AlGaAs Schottky → n-AlGaAs donor → undoped AlGaAs spacer → intrinsic GaAs channel → superlattice buffer → SI-GaAs substrate.
- **Why SI-GaAs substrate?** Eliminates substrate parasitics, enabling mm-wave operation. Resistivity > 10⁷ Ω·cm.
- **Invention lineage:** Mimura / Delagebeaudeuf (1980) and Solomon (1982) at Thomson-CSF / Bell Labs — first AlGaAs/GaAs HEMT.
- **Extension to pHEMT:** Insert a thin In_yGa₁₋yAs strained layer (y ≈ 0.15–0.25) inside the GaAs channel — increases $\Delta E_C$ and pushes $\mu_n$ above 10 000 cm²/V·s.
- **Descendants:** GaN/AlGaN HEMT (high-power RF), InGaAs/InP HEMT (THz f_T), Si/SiGe modulation-doped heterostructures (CMOS-compatible).
- **Square-law I-V:** $I_{D,sat} = (\mu_n \varepsilon W) / (2L(d_d+\Delta d)) \cdot (V_{GS}-V_{th})^2$ — same shape as a MOSFET, but with **2–3× higher transconductance**.
- **Frequency merit:** $f_T \approx \mu_n (V_{GS}-V_{th}) / (2\pi L^2)$ — explains why gate-length scaling (sub-100 nm) is the primary lever for mm-wave HEMTs.
- **For numerical problems:** Always carry $\varepsilon_{AlGaAs}(x) = 13.1 - 3.0 x$ and $\Delta E_C = 0.81 x$ eV in the back of your mind.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Principle of Operation
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEEVT413 - COMPOUND SEMICONDUCTORS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Principle of Operation

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

In the context of **Compound Semiconductors (PEEVT413)**, the **Principle of Operation** refers to the fundamental physical mechanisms by which a p–n junction formed from binary, ternary, or quaternary III–V or II–VI compound semiconductors (e.g., **GaAs**, **InP**, **AlGaAs**, **InGaAsP**, **CdTe**, **ZnSe**) controls the flow of charge carriers under the influence of an applied electric field (forward/reverse bias) or optical excitation.

The principle of operation is governed by three coupled physical phenomena:

1. **Formation of the depletion region** due to charge-carrier diffusion and the resulting built-in electric field.
2. **Modification of the energy band structure** (band bending) across the metallurgical junction.
3. **Carrier transport** across the junction — governed by drift, diffusion, generation, and recombination processes.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition:**
> The principle of operation of compound-semiconductor devices is centred on the **direct bandgap**, **high electron mobility**, and **tunable bandgap** properties that distinguish them from elemental semiconductors like Si or Ge. These unique properties enable efficient **light emission (LEDs)**, **light detection (photodiodes)**, **light amplification (lasers)**, and **high-frequency / high-power operation (HEMTs, HBTs)**.

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy — The "Water Dam" Model**
> 
> Imagine a dam separating two reservoirs of water: one at a high level (the **n-side**, full of negatively charged electrons) and one at a low level (the **p-side**, full of positively charged holes). 
> 
> - The **built-in potential ($V_{bi}$)** is the natural pressure difference — water wants to flow, but the dam (depletion region) holds it back.
> - When you **forward-bias** the junction (push electrons in from the n-side), you lower the dam and water (current) flows freely.
> - When you **reverse-bias** it (push against the flow), the dam becomes taller, and only a tiny trickle (leakage current) escapes.
> - The **depletion width ($W$)** is the thickness of the dam wall — narrower in heavily doped compound semiconductors, wider in lightly doped ones.
> 
> This is exactly what happens at the **p–n junction** of a compound semiconductor device. The "water" is **charge carriers**, and the "pressure" is the **electric field** set up by ionized dopants.

---

## 1.3 Standard Physical Constants (Memorize for KTU Board Exams)

| Constant | Symbol | Value | Unit |
|----------|--------|-------|------|
| Boltzmann constant | $k$ | **$1.38 \times 10^{-23}$** | J/K |
| Electronic charge | $q$ | **$1.602 \times 10^{-19}$** | C |
| Thermal voltage at 300 K | $V_T = kT/q$ | **$0.0259 \approx 25.85$ mV** | V |
| Permittivity of free space | $\varepsilon_0$ | **$8.854 \times 10^{-12}$** | F/m |
| Planck's constant | $h$ | **$6.626 \times 10^{-34}$** | J·s |
| Speed of light in vacuum | $c$ | **$3 \times 10^{8}$** | m/s |

---

## 1.4 Why Compound Semiconductors? — Distinguishing Features

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why study the principle of operation specifically for *compound* semiconductors?**
> 
> 1. **Direct bandgap** → efficient radiative recombination → essential for **optoelectronic devices (LEDs, laser diodes, photodetectors)**.
> 2. **High electron mobility** ($\mu_e$ in GaAs $\approx 8500$ cm²/V·s vs. Si $\approx 1400$ cm²/V·s) → faster, high-frequency devices (**HEMTs**).
> 3. **Tunable bandgap** by alloy composition (e.g., $\text{Al}_x\text{Ga}_{1-x}\text{As}$ has $E_g$ ranging from 1.42 eV to 2.16 eV) → engineered for specific wavelength operation.
> 4. **Wide bandgap variants** (SiC, GaN, ZnO) → high-power, high-temperature, and UV applications.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Step-by-Step Theory of the p–n Junction Operation

When a **p-type** region (with acceptor concentration $N_A$) and an **n-type** region (with donor concentration $N_D$) of a compound semiconductor are brought into intimate atomic contact, the following sequence of events occurs:

### Step 1 — Carrier Diffusion at the Junction
- Holes from the **p-side** diffuse into the **n-side**.
- Electrons from the **n-side** diffuse into the **p-side**.
- This is driven by the **concentration gradient** (Fick's law of diffusion).

### Step 2 — Formation of the Space-Charge (Depletion) Region
- As carriers cross the junction, they leave behind **ionized dopants**:
  - **Negatively ionized acceptors ($N_A^-$)** on the p-side.
  - **Positively ionized donors ($N_D^+$)** on the n-side.
- This creates a region **devoid of mobile carriers** — the **depletion region (W)**.
- A built-in electric field $E$ is established, pointing from the n-side to the p-side.

### Step 3 — Establishment of the Built-in Potential
- The built-in field opposes further diffusion, and **thermal equilibrium** is reached when drift current exactly balances diffusion current.
- The **built-in potential** $V_{bi}$ is the contact potential difference that arises.

### Step 4 — Band Bending
- The conduction and valence band edges bend by an amount $qV_{bi}$ across the depletion region.
- The **Fermi level** $E_F$ remains constant (flat) throughout the device at thermal equilibrium.

### Step 5 — Carrier Transport Under Bias
- **Forward bias** ($V > 0$): the external voltage reduces the barrier → large exponential current flows.
- **Reverse bias** ($V < 0$): the external voltage increases the barrier → only a small saturation current flows.

---

## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet (Exam-Ready)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **All formulas below are in the form examiners expect to see in KTU ESE answer scripts. Units are explicitly included.**

### Table 2.1 — Core Formulas for p–n Junction Operation in Compound Semiconductors

| # | Formula | Description | Units |
|---|---------|-------------|-------|
| 1 | $V_{bi} = V_T \ln\!\left(\dfrac{N_A N_D}{n_i^{\,2}}\right)$ | Built-in potential at the junction | V |
| 2 | $W = \sqrt{\dfrac{2 \varepsilon_s V_{bi}}{q} \left(\dfrac{N_A + N_D}{N_A N_D}\right)}$ | Total depletion width (abrupt junction) | m |
| 3 | $x_n = \dfrac{W \cdot N_A}{N_A + N_D}$ | Depletion extension into the n-side | m |
| 4 | $x_p = \dfrac{W \cdot N_D}{N_A + N_D}$ | Depletion extension into the p-side | m |
| 5 | $I = I_s \left( e^{V / \eta V_T} - 1 \right)$ | Shockley diode equation (I–V characteristic) | A |
| 6 | $I_s = q A \left( \dfrac{D_p n_i^{\,2}}{L_p N_D} + \dfrac{D_n n_i^{\,2}}{L_n N_A} \right)$ | Reverse saturation current | A |
| 7 | $V_T = \dfrac{kT}{q}$ | Thermal voltage (≈ 25.85 mV at 300 K) | V |
| 8 | $C_j = \dfrac{\varepsilon_s A}{W}$ | Junction capacitance | F |
| 9 | $E_g (\text{Al}_x\text{Ga}_{1-x}\text{As}) = 1.424 + 1.247x$ (for $0 \le x \le 0.45$) | Bandgap vs. Al mole fraction (eV) | eV |
| 10 | $\lambda (\mu\text{m}) = \dfrac{1.24}{E_g (\text{eV})}$ | Emission wavelength from bandgap | µm |

> [!NOTE]
> **Critical Notation for KTU Board Exam Scripts:**
> - $\varepsilon_s = \varepsilon_r \cdot \varepsilon_0$ is the **absolute permittivity** of the compound semiconductor.
> - $\eta$ is the **ideality factor** ($\eta = 1$ for ideal diffusion-limited current; $\eta = 2$ for recombination-dominated current).
> - $A$ is the **cross-sectional area** of the junction.
> - $D_n, D_p$ are **diffusion coefficients**; $L_n, L_p$ are **diffusion lengths** of electrons and holes respectively.

---

## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The principle of operation of compound-semiconductor p–n junctions underpins the following modern devices:
> 
> 1. **GaAs/AlGaAs LEDs** — emit light at 870 nm (infra-red) to 650 nm (red) depending on Al fraction.
> 2. **InGaAs/InP photodetectors** — operate at 1310 nm and 1550 nm telecom wavelengths (fiber-optic receivers).
> 3. **GaAs/AlGaAs HEMTs** — used in satellite receivers, radar systems, and 5G base stations.
> 4. **CdTe/CdS thin-film solar cells** — second-generation photovoltaic technology.
> 5. **GaN blue LEDs** — revolutionized solid-state lighting (Nobel Prize in Physics 2014).

The built-in potential $V_{bi}$ and depletion width $W$ directly control the **junction capacitance**, which determines the **speed of photodetectors** and the **cutoff frequency of microwave diodes**.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

> [!NOTE]
> **This section provides exam-grade derivations. Each step is explicit. No steps have been skipped.**

---

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Built-in Potential ($V_{bi}$) of a Compound Semiconductor p–n Junction

### **Given:**
- Acceptor concentration on p-side: $N_A$
- Donor concentration on n-side: $N_D$
- Intrinsic carrier concentration: $n_i$ (compound-semiconductor-specific, e.g., $n_i \approx 1.79 \times 10^6$ cm⁻³ for GaAs at 300 K)
- Thermal voltage: $V_T = kT/q$

### **To Derive:** $V_{bi}$

**Step 1 — Charge neutrality condition**

At thermal equilibrium, the total negative charge on the p-side equals the total positive charge on the n-side:

$$
q N_A x_p = q N_D x_n
$$

This means the depletion widths on the two sides are inversely proportional to the doping concentrations. Text logic: This is the **charge neutrality** of the space-charge region — no net charge is allowed inside an isolated semiconductor.

**Step 2 — Carrier concentrations at the depletion edges**

Using the depletion approximation, carrier densities drop sharply to zero inside the depletion region and remain at equilibrium bulk values outside. At the edge of the depletion region on the n-side:

$$
n_{n0} = N_D, \quad p_{n0} = \frac{n_i^{\,2}}{N_D}
$$

Similarly, on the p-side:

$$
p_{p0} = N_A, \quad n_{p0} = \frac{n_i^{\,2}}{N_A}
$$

Text logic: On the **heavily doped side**, majority carrier concentration equals the doping. On the **opposite side**, minority carrier concentration follows the mass-action law $np = n_i^{\,2}$.

**Step 3 — Apply Boltzmann's relation for the built-in potential**

The potential difference $V_{bi}$ across the junction is related to the carrier concentrations by the Boltzmann relation. Considering electrons on the n-side crossing to the p-side:

$$
V_{bi} = V_T \ln\!\left(\frac{N_D}{n_{p0}}\right) = V_T \ln\!\left(\frac{N_D N_A}{n_i^{\,2}}\right)
$$

Text logic: We substitute $n_{p0} = n_i^{\,2}/N_A$ from Step 2, which gives us the standard form. The logarithm is **dimensionless** because the argument is a ratio of like-dimension carrier concentrations.

**Step 4 — Symmetric form of the equation**

Combining both electrons and holes in a single expression:

$$
\boxed{\,V_{bi} = V_T \ln\!\left(\frac{N_A N_D}{n_i^{\,2}}\right)\,}
$$

Text logic: The same result is obtained by analyzing hole transport; this confirms consistency. The product $N_A N_D$ appears symmetrically — the built-in voltage depends on **both** dopings.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Depletion Width ($W$) of an Abrupt Junction

### **Given:** Built-in potential $V_{bi}$ from Derivation 1, doping $N_A$, $N_D$, permittivity $\varepsilon_s$, charge $q$.

### **To Derive:** Total depletion width $W$

**Step 1 — Solve Poisson's equation in the depletion region**

Inside the depletion region, charge density $\rho$ is constant on each side. For the n-side depletion region ($0 \le x \le x_n$):

$$
\rho(x) = +q N_D
$$

For the p-side depletion region ($-x_p \le x \le 0$):

$$
\rho(x) = -q N_A
$$

Poisson's equation: $\dfrac{d^2 V}{dx^2} = -\dfrac{\rho(x)}{\varepsilon_s}$

**Step 2 — Integrate Poisson's equation on the n-side**

$$
\frac{d^2 V}{dx^2} = -\frac{q N_D}{\varepsilon_s}
$$

Integrating once (electric field):

$$
E(x) = -\frac{q N_D}{\varepsilon_s}(x - x_n)
$$

Integrating again (potential):

$$
V(x) = -\frac{q N_D}{2\varepsilon_s}(x - x_n)^2 + V_n
$$

Text logic: The constant of integration $V_n$ is the potential at the edge of the n-side depletion region. The negative charge (ionized donors) creates a positive field, opposing further electron flow.

**Step 3 — Repeat for the p-side and apply boundary conditions**

By similar integration on the p-side and matching $V$ and $E$ at the metallurgical junction ($x = 0$):

$$
E_{\max} = \frac{q N_D x_n}{\varepsilon_s} = \frac{q N_A x_p}{\varepsilon_s}
$$

This re-derives the **charge neutrality** of Step 1 of Derivation 3.1.

**Step 4 — Apply the total potential drop condition**

The total potential drop from $x = -x_p$ to $x = x_n$ must equal $V_{bi}$:

$$
V_{bi} = \frac{1}{2} E_{\max} W = \frac{q}{2\varepsilon_s} \cdot \frac{N_A N_D}{N_A + N_D} \cdot W^2
$$

Text logic: The depletion region resembles a **parallel-plate capacitor** — potential = ½ × (max field) × (separation).

**Step 5 — Solve for $W$**

$$
W^2 = \frac{2\varepsilon_s V_{bi}}{q} \cdot \frac{N_A + N_D}{N_A N_D}
$$

$$
\boxed{\,W = \sqrt{\frac{2 \varepsilon_s V_{bi}}{q} \left(\frac{N_A + N_D}{N_A N_D}\right)}\,}
$$

Text logic: For a **one-sided abrupt junction** (e.g., $N_A \gg N_D$, like a $p^+n$ diode), this reduces to $W \approx \sqrt{2\varepsilon_s V_{bi} / (q N_D)}$ — the depletion region essentially lives in the lightly doped side only.

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3: Capacitance–Voltage Relationship

Using the parallel-plate capacitor analogy, junction capacitance is $C_j = \varepsilon_s A / W$. Substituting $W$ from Derivation 3.2:

$$
C_j = A \sqrt{\frac{q \varepsilon_s N_A N_D}{2(V_{bi} - V)(N_A + N_D)}}
$$

For reverse bias ($V$ is negative, so $V_{bi} - V$ increases), this gives $C_j \propto (V_{bi} - V)^{-1/2}$ — a useful diagnostic tool for measuring doping profiles.

---

## 3.4 Worked Numerical Example (Typical KTU Board Question)

**Problem:** A GaAs p–n junction has $N_A = 10^{18}$ cm⁻³ and $N_D = 10^{16}$ cm⁻³. Given $n_i = 1.79 \times 10^6$ cm⁻³ and $\varepsilon_r = 13.1$ for GaAs at 300 K, calculate $V_{bi}$ and $W$ at zero bias.

**Solution:**

**Step 1 — Compute $V_{bi}$:**

$$
V_{bi} = (0.0259) \cdot \ln\!\left(\frac{10^{18} \times 10^{16}}{(1.79 \times 10^6)^2}\right)
$$

$$
V_{bi} = (0.0259) \cdot \ln\!\left(\frac{10^{34}}{3.20 \times 10^{12}}\right)
$$

$$
V_{bi} = (0.0259) \cdot \ln(3.125 \times 10^{21}) = (0.0259)(50.17)
$$

$$
\boxed{\,V_{bi} \approx 1.30 \text{ V}\,}
$$

Text logic: This is the **built-in potential of a typical GaAs p–n junction** — note it is **much higher than for Si** (~0.7 V) due to GaAs's **lower intrinsic carrier concentration**.

**Step 2 — Compute $W$:**

Since $N_A \gg N_D$, this is effectively a one-sided $p^+n$ junction. The approximation $W \approx x_n$ gives:

$$
W = \sqrt{\frac{2 \times 13.1 \times 8.854 \times 10^{-12} \times 1.30}{1.602 \times 10^{-19} \times 10^{22}}}
$$

$$
W = \sqrt{\frac{3.015 \times 10^{-10}}{1.602 \times 10^{3}}} = \sqrt{1.882 \times 10^{-13}}
$$

$$
\boxed{\,W \approx 4.34 \times 10^{-7} \text{ m} = 0.434 \ \mu\text{m}\,}
$$

Text logic: The depletion width is sub-micron — consistent with the high doping of compound-semiconductor devices, which is necessary for **high-speed operation**.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

> [!NOTE]
> **Below are two Mermaid diagrams: (a) the energy-band structure of a compound-semiconductor p–n junction at equilibrium and under bias; (b) the operational block-level architecture of an LED/photodetector using such a junction.**

---

## 4.1 Diagram A — Energy Band Diagram of a Compound-Semiconductor p–n Junction

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph NEUTRAL_N["Neutral N-Side (Doped n-type)"]
        EC_N["E_C (Conduction Band Edge)"]
        EF_N["E_F (Fermi Level)"]
        EV_N["E_V (Valence Band Edge)"]
    end

    subgraph DEPLETION["Depletion Region (Space-Charge Layer)"]
        BAND_BEND["BAND BENDING \n Built-in Field E_int \n Width = W = x_n + x_p"]
    end

    subgraph NEUTRAL_P["Neutral P-Side (Doped p-type)"]
        EC_P["E_C (Conduction Band Edge)"]
        EF_P["E_F (Fermi Level)"]
        EV_P["E_V (Valence Band Edge)"]
    end

    EC_N ~~~ EF_N
    EF_N ~~~ EV_N
    EC_P ~~~ EF_P
    EF_P ~~~ EV_P

    NEUTRAL_N --> DEPLETION
    DEPLETION --> NEUTRAL_P

    NOTE1["Equilibrium: E_F is FLAT across the entire device \n Forward bias: barrier lowers by qV \n Reverse bias: barrier increases by q|V|"]
```

**Key Visual Features to Note in the Diagram:**
- The Fermi level $E_F$ is **flat** at thermal equilibrium — a fundamental condition.
- $E_C$ and $E_V$ **bend upward** going from the n-side to the p-side — this is the **band bending** that defines the depletion region.
- The total band bending equals $qV_{bi}$.

---

## 4.2 Diagram B — Block-Level Functional Architecture of a Compound-Semiconductor Optoelectronic Device (LED/Photodetector)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Input Signal: \n Electrical Bias OR Optical Photon Flux] --> B[Metal Contact: \n Ohmic to n-type layer]
    B --> C[Window Layer: \n Wide Bandgap Compound e.g. AlGaAs]
    C --> D[Active Junction Region: \n Direct Bandgap Compound e.g. GaAs InGaAs]
    D --> E[Substrate: \n Compound Semiconductor e.g. GaAs InP]
    E --> F[Bottom Ohmic Contact: \n P-side metallization]
    F --> G[Output: \n Emitted Photons LED OR Photocurrent Photodetector]

    BIAS1[Bias Voltage V applied: \n Forward for LED \n Reverse for Photodetector] -.-> D
    PHOTON1[Photon Energy h nu greater than E_g] -.-> D
```

**Architectural Flow Explanation:**

1. **Input stage** — accepts either an electrical drive signal (for an LED) or an incident optical signal (for a photodetector).
2. **Ohmic contact (top)** — provides a low-resistance non-rectifying contact to the n-type top layer.
3. **Window layer** — a wide-bandgap top layer (e.g., AlGaAs on GaAs) that is transparent to the emitted/detected photons, reducing surface recombination.
4. **Active junction region** — the **heart of the device**, where the p–n junction is located. Compound semiconductors here are chosen for their **direct bandgap**, enabling efficient radiative recombination.
5. **Substrate** — the mechanical base, typically a binary compound like GaAs or InP.
6. **Bottom Ohmic contact** — provides the second electrical terminal.
7. **Output** — emitted photons (LED mode) or extracted photocurrent (photodetector mode).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The block diagram above is a Mermaid-adapted "Block-Level Functional Architecture Flow"** because the physical cross-section of an LED/photodetector is a complex layered heterostructure that cannot be rendered meaningfully in a flowchart. The functional flow shown captures the **signal path** from input to output.

---

## 4.3 Diagram C — Operational Flowchart of the p–n Junction Principle

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    S1[Start: P-type and N-type Compound Semiconductors Contacted] --> S2[Carrier Diffusion: \n Electrons from N to P \n Holes from P to N]
    S2 --> S3[Formation of Depletion Region: \n Width W = x_n + x_p \n Ionized Dopants Create Field E]
    S3 --> S4[Thermal Equilibrium: \n Drift Current = Diffusion Current \n E_F is Flat]
    S4 --> S5{External Bias Applied?}
    S5 -- Forward V greater than 0 --> S6[Barrier lowered: \n Exponential Current I = I_s exp V by eta V_T]
    S5 -- Reverse V less than 0 --> S7[Barrier raised: \n Only I_s Saturation Current Flows]
    S5 -- Optical Excitation --> S8[Photogeneration: \n EHPs Created \n Photocurrent Extracted]
    S6 --> END1[Device Operates as: Diode or LED or Laser]
    S7 --> END2[Device Operates as: Photodetector or Solar Cell]
    S8 --> END3[Device Operates as: Photodiode or Solar Cell]
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

> [!NOTE]
> **Two direct short-answer questions modeled on KTU 2024 Part A (Module 2, 3-mark format).**

### **Q1. `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`**
**Define the built-in potential of a p–n junction. How does it differ between GaAs and Si for the same doping levels?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The **built-in potential ($V_{bi}$)** is the contact potential difference that develops across a p–n junction at thermal equilibrium, due to the transfer of charge between the p-side and n-side during the formation of the depletion region. It is mathematically expressed as:

$$
V_{bi} = V_T \ln\!\left(\frac{N_A N_D}{n_i^{\,2}}\right)
$$

For the **same doping concentrations**, **GaAs has a higher $V_{bi}$ than Si** because GaAs has a much **lower intrinsic carrier concentration** ($n_i \approx 1.79 \times 10^6$ cm⁻³) compared to Si ($n_i \approx 1.0 \times 10^{10}$ cm⁻³). Since $V_{bi} \propto \ln(1/n_i^{\,2})$, the lower $n_i$ of GaAs yields a higher built-in voltage (typically ~1.3 V vs. ~0.7 V for Si).

---

### **Q2. `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`**
**What is the significance of the direct bandgap in compound semiconductors for optoelectronic device operation?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

In a **direct bandgap** semiconductor (e.g., GaAs, InP, InGaAs), the conduction band minimum and the valence band maximum occur at the **same crystal momentum ($k$)** value in the Brillouin zone. As a result:

1. **Electron–hole recombination** does not require a phonon to conserve momentum — the transition is **radiative and highly efficient**.
2. This makes direct-bandgap compound semiconductors ideal for **light-emitting devices (LEDs, laser diodes)** and **light-absorbing devices (photodetectors, solar cells)** operating at the bandgap wavelength $\lambda = 1.24/E_g$ µm.

In contrast, **indirect bandgap** semiconductors (Si, Ge) require phonon assistance for radiative transitions, making them **poor light emitters** but acceptable for solar cells.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Module Internal Choice)

> [!NOTE]
> **As per KTU 2024 ESE regulations, Part B questions in Module 2 carry 14 marks and offer internal choice between two questions (OR option). Both Question A and Question B are provided below with full sub-parts and model solutions.**

---

### **QUESTION A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`

**(a) Derive the expression for the built-in potential ($V_{bi}$) of a compound-semiconductor p–n junction starting from the charge-neutrality condition. [7 marks]**

**(b) For a GaAs p–n junction at 300 K, given $N_A = 5 \times 10^{17}$ cm⁻³, $N_D = 10^{16}$ cm⁻³, $n_i = 1.79 \times 10^6$ cm⁻³, and $\varepsilon_r = 13.1$, calculate (i) the built-in potential, and (ii) the depletion width at zero bias. [7 marks]**

#### **Model Solution for (a):**

**Step 1 — Charge neutrality of the depletion region.** The total positive charge (ionized donors on the n-side) must equal the total negative charge (ionized acceptors on the p-side):

$$
q N_D x_n = q N_A x_p
$$

**Step 2 — Carrier concentrations at the edges of the depletion region.** Applying the mass-action law $np = n_i^{\,2}$:

- On the p-side: $n_{p0} = n_i^{\,2} / N_A$
- On the n-side: $p_{n0} = n_i^{\,2} / N_D$

**Step 3 — Boltzmann relation across the junction.** The carrier density ratio across a potential barrier $V_{bi}$ is:

$$
\frac{N_D}{n_{p0}} = \exp\!\left(\frac{V_{bi}}{V_T}\right)
$$

**Step 4 — Substitute and simplify.** Substituting $n_{p0} = n_i^{\,2}/N_A$:

$$
V_{bi} = V_T \ln\!\left(\frac{N_A N_D}{n_i^{\,2}}\right)
$$

**Step 5 — Interpretation.** Higher doping and lower intrinsic carrier concentration both increase $V_{bi}$. For compound semiconductors with small $n_i$ (e.g., GaAs), $V_{bi}$ is large. **[7 marks: 2 for neutrality, 2 for concentrations, 2 for Boltzmann, 1 for final expression and interpretation]**

#### **Model Solution for (b):**

**(i) Built-in potential:**

$$
V_{bi} = (0.0259) \cdot \ln\!\left(\frac{5 \times 10^{17} \times 10^{16}}{(1.79 \times 10^6)^2}\right)
$$

$$
V_{bi} = (0.0259) \cdot \ln\!\left(\frac{5 \times 10^{33}}{3.20 \times 10^{12}}\right) = (0.0259) \cdot \ln(1.5625 \times 10^{21})
$$

$$
V_{bi} = (0.0259) \cdot (49.79) = \boxed{1.29 \text{ V}}
$$

**(ii) Depletion width:** Since $N_A \gg N_D$, this is a one-sided $p^+n$ junction. We use:

$$
W = \sqrt{\frac{2 \varepsilon_s V_{bi}}{q N_D}}
$$

With $\varepsilon_s = 13.1 \times 8.854 \times 10^{-12} = 1.160 \times 10^{-10}$ F/m:

$$
W = \sqrt{\frac{2 \times 1.160 \times 10^{-10} \times 1.29}{1.602 \times 10^{-19} \times 10^{22}}}
$$

$$
W = \sqrt{\frac{2.993 \times 10^{-10}}{1.602 \times 10^{3}}} = \sqrt{1.868 \times 10^{-13}}
$$

$$
\boxed{W \approx 4.32 \times 10^{-7} \text{ m} = 0.432 \ \mu\text{m}}
$$

**[7 marks: 2 for V_bi calculation, 2 for W formula selection, 2 for numerical evaluation, 1 for unit conversion]**

---

### **QUESTION B (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`

**(a) With the help of a neat energy-band diagram, explain the principle of operation of a p–n junction under (i) zero bias, (ii) forward bias, and (iii) reverse bias. Discuss the role of the depletion region. [7 marks]**

**(b) Explain why GaAs is preferred over Si for optoelectronic applications. Discuss three specific device examples. [7 marks]**

#### **Model Solution for (a):**

**(i) Zero bias (thermal equilibrium):**
At equilibrium, the Fermi level $E_F$ is **flat** across the device. The conduction and valence band edges bend by $qV_{bi}$ across the depletion region. A built-in electric field $E$ exists, directed from n to p. The drift current (due to $E$) exactly balances the diffusion current (due to concentration gradient), giving **zero net current**.

**(ii) Forward bias ($V > 0$):**
Applying a positive voltage to the p-side reduces the built-in potential to $V_{bi} - V$. The band bending decreases. The depletion width shrinks. The barrier is lowered, and carriers easily diffuse across — leading to **exponential current flow**:

$$
I = I_s \left(e^{V/\eta V_T} - 1\right)
$$

**(iii) Reverse bias ($V < 0$):**
Applying a positive voltage to the n-side increases the built-in potential to $V_{bi} + \vert V \vert$. The band bending increases, depletion width grows, and the barrier becomes larger. Only a small **reverse saturation current** $I_s$ flows, due to thermally generated minority carriers.

**Role of the depletion region:** The depletion region **stores charge**, **supports the built-in field**, and **acts as a capacitor** that controls the high-speed response of the device. **[7 marks: 1 each for zero/forward/reverse bias + 1 for diagram + 3 for role and equations]**

#### **Model Solution for (b):**

GaAs is preferred over Si for optoelectronic applications due to the following reasons:

| Property | GaAs | Si | Advantage for Optoelectronics |
|----------|------|----|----|
| Bandgap type | **Direct** | Indirect | Radiative recombination is efficient in GaAs |
| Bandgap energy | 1.42 eV | 1.12 eV | GaAs emits in the visible/NIR (870 nm) |
| Electron mobility | 8500 cm²/V·s | 1400 cm²/V·s | Faster response, higher frequency operation |
| Intrinsic carrier concentration | $\sim 10^6$ cm⁻³ | $\sim 10^{10}$ cm⁻³ | Lower leakage, higher $V_{bi}$ |

**Three device examples:**

1. **GaAs/AlGaAs LEDs and laser diodes** — direct bandgap enables efficient light emission. Used in optical communication, CD/DVD players, and barcode scanners.
2. **InGaAs/InP photodetectors** — direct bandgap tuned to 1.55 µm telecom wavelength. Used in fiber-optic receivers.
3. **GaAs/AlGaAs HEMTs** — high electron mobility enables terahertz-frequency operation. Used in satellite communication and 5G.

**[7 marks: 2 for property comparison table, 5 for the three device examples with specific applications]**

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes That Cost Marks in KTU 2024 Board Exams — Read Carefully!**
> 
> 1. **Forgetting the $e^V - 1$ term:** Students often write $I = I_s e^{V/\eta V_T}$, omitting the "$-1$". This is correct only when $V \gg V_T$. For full marks, **always include the $-1$**.
> 
> 2. **Using Si's $n_i$ for GaAs problems:** This is the most common blunder. **Si: $n_i = 1.0 \times 10^{10}$ cm⁻³. GaAs: $n_i = 1.79 \times 10^6$ cm⁻³.** Mixing these up gives wrong $V_{bi}$ values.
> 
> 3. **Sign error in $V_{bi} - V$ under reverse bias:** Many students write $V_{bi} + V$ for reverse bias. The correct form for the **total potential across the depletion region** under applied bias $V$ is $V_{bi} - V$ (where $V$ is **negative** for reverse bias, so the result is $V_{bi} + \vert V \vert$).
> 
> 4. **Unit inconsistency:** Doping is often given in **cm⁻³**, but SI units require **m⁻³**. Convert: $1$ cm⁻³ = $10^6$ m⁻³. Failing to convert gives answers off by $10^6$.
> 
> 5. **Missing the $\eta$ (ideality factor):** Some compound-semiconductor diodes have $\eta = 2$ due to significant recombination current in the depletion region. Always state the assumed value.
> 
> 6. **Forgetting $\varepsilon_s = \varepsilon_r \varepsilon_0$:** Students sometimes use $\varepsilon_0$ directly. The relative permittivity $\varepsilon_r$ of GaAs (13.1) or InP (12.4) must be included.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision Summary — Read Just Before Entering the Exam Hall**

- [ ] The **principle of operation** of a compound-semiconductor p–n junction rests on **depletion region formation**, **built-in potential**, **band bending**, and **bias-dependent carrier transport**.
- [ ] **Built-in potential:** $V_{bi} = V_T \ln(N_A N_D / n_i^{\,2})$ — **higher for GaAs than Si** (lower $n_i$).
- [ ] **Depletion width:** $W = \sqrt{(2\varepsilon_s V_{bi}/q) \cdot (N_A + N_D)/(N_A N_D)}$.
- [ ] For a **one-sided $p^+n$** junction: $W \approx x_n = \sqrt{2\varepsilon_s V_{bi}/(qN_D)}$.
- [ ] **Shockley diode equation:** $I = I_s(e^{V/\eta V_T} - 1)$.
- [ ] **Thermal voltage** at 300 K: $V_T = 25.85$ mV.
- [ ] **Direct bandgap** → efficient radiative recombination → essential for LEDs and laser diodes.
- [ ] **Indirect bandgap** (Si) → poor light emitter; not used for optoelectronic light sources.
- [ ] **Junction capacitance:** $C_j = \varepsilon_s A / W \propto (V_{bi} - V)^{-1/2}$ under reverse bias.
- [ ] **Wavelength of emission/absorption:** $\lambda (\mu\text{m}) = 1.24 / E_g (\text{eV})$.
- [ ] **Bandgap engineering:** $\text{Al}_x\text{Ga}_{1-x}\text{As}$: $E_g = 1.424 + 1.247x$ eV (for $x \le 0.45$).
- [ ] **Forward bias** → barrier reduces → exponential current; **reverse bias** → barrier rises → saturation current $I_s$.
- [ ] Always state the **ideality factor** $\eta$: 1 for diffusion-limited, 2 for recombination-limited current.
- [ ] Always include the **$-1$** in the diode equation.
- [ ] Always convert **cm⁻³ to m⁻³** (multiply by $10^6$) before doing SI calculations.
- [ ] Always state $\varepsilon_s = \varepsilon_r \cdot \varepsilon_0$ and use the **correct** relative permittivity for the given compound semiconductor.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Energy Band Diagrams
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEEVT413 - COMPOUND SEMICONDUCTORS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Energy Band Diagrams

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Energy Band Diagram:** An Energy Band Diagram is a graphical representation of the allowed and forbidden electron energy states in a crystalline solid as a function of position (for heterojunctions) or crystal momentum $\vec{k}$ (for bulk E-k dispersion). It depicts the **Conduction Band** ($E_C$), **Valence Band** ($E_V$), and the **Forbidden Energy Gap** ($E_g = E_C - E_V$) that separates them.

In the context of **Compound Semiconductors** (e.g., GaAs, InP, GaN, CdTe, ZnSe), the energy band diagram is a critical tool because it determines:
- Whether the material has a **direct bandgap** (photon-emitting transitions allowed) or **indirect bandgap** (phonon-assisted transitions).
- The **bandgap energy $E_g$** which dictates optical absorption/emission wavelength.
- The position of **donor ($E_D$)** and **acceptor ($E_A$)** levels in doped compound semiconductors.
- The **band offsets** ($\Delta E_C$, $\Delta E_V$) at heterojunctions such as GaAs/AlGaAs.

The fundamental energy-momentum relationship for an electron near the band extrema in a semiconductor is given by:

$$E(\vec{k}) = E_C + \frac{\hbar^2 k^2}{2 m_e^*}$$

for the conduction band, and

$$E(\vec{k}) = E_V - \frac{\hbar^2 k^2}{2 m_h^*}$$

for the valence band, where $m_e^*$ and $m_h^*$ are the **effective masses** of electrons and holes respectively.

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy (Plain English Intuition)

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy — "The Parking Lot and the Staircase"**
>
> Imagine a multi-storey parking building where cars (electrons) can only park on specific floors. The ground floor is the **Valence Band** (where electrons normally sit, bound to atoms). The roof floor is the **Conduction Band** (where electrons roam freely and conduct electricity). The space *between* the floors — the empty air between ground and roof — is the **Forbidden Energy Gap** ($E_g$). 
>
> - In **conductors (metals)**: the staircase is connected, electrons can freely climb.
> - In **insulators**: the gap is so huge (like a 50-floor jump) that electrons cannot leap it.
> - In **semiconductors**: the gap is just a small jump (e.g., 1.42 eV for GaAs), so with a bit of thermal or optical energy, electrons can jump up and conduct.
>
> In **compound semiconductors**, the structure of the "floors" (energy bands) can have valleys at the same horizontal position (**direct bandgap**, like GaAs) or at different positions (**indirect bandgap**, like Si), which fundamentally changes how the material interacts with light.

---

## 1.3 Classification of Compound Semiconductors by Band Structure

| Category | Examples | Bandgap Nature | Typical $E_g$ |
|:---------|:---------|:---------------|:--------------|
| **III-V Binary** | GaAs, InP, InSb, GaN | Direct (except AlAs) | 0.17 – 3.4 eV |
| **III-V Ternary** | AlGaAs, InGaAs, InGaP | Direct / Indirect | Tunable |
| **II-VI Binary** | CdTe, ZnSe, HgCdTe | Direct | 0.0 – 2.7 eV |
| **IV-VI Binary** | PbS, PbTe | Direct (narrow) | 0.29 – 0.41 eV |
| **III-Nitride** | GaN, AlN, InN | Direct (GaN, InN) | 0.7 – 6.2 eV |

---

## 1.4 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Physical Constants Used in Energy Band Analysis:**
> - Reduced Planck's constant: $\hbar = 1.054 \times 10^{-34}$ **J·s**
> - Electron rest mass: $m_0 = 9.109 \times 10^{-31}$ **kg**
> - Boltzmann constant: $k_B = 1.381 \times 10^{-23}$ **J/K** (or **8.617 × 10⁻⁵ eV/K**)
> - Thermal voltage at 300 K: $V_T = 0.02585$ **V**
> - Elementary charge: $q = 1.602 \times 10^{-19}$ **C**
> - 1 eV $= 1.602 \times 10^{-19}$ **J**

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Step-by-Step Theoretical Framework

### Step 1: Origin of Energy Bands (Brillouin Zone Perspective)
When isolated atoms are brought together to form a crystal, the discrete atomic orbitals **split and broaden** into continuous bands due to **Pauli's exclusion principle** and **wavefunction overlap**. The periodicity of the crystal lattice causes the energy dispersion $E(\vec{k})$ to be periodic in **k-space**, with the smallest repeating cell being the **First Brillouin Zone**.

The boundaries of the Brillouin Zone occur at high-symmetry points labelled:
- $\Gamma$ (Gamma) — center of the zone, $\vec{k} = (0,0,0)$
- $X$ — zone boundary along $\langle 100 \rangle$ direction
- $L$ — zone boundary along $\langle 111 \rangle$ direction
- $K, U, W$ — other high-symmetry points

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Memory Trick:** The **conduction band minimum (CBM)** and **valence band maximum (VBM)** positions determine whether a semiconductor is **direct** or **indirect**.

---

### Step 2: Direct vs Indirect Bandgap Semiconductors

**Direct Bandgap:**
- CBM and VBM occur at the **same k-vector** (typically at $\Gamma$).
- Electrons can transition directly from VBM to CBM with only a **photon** (no change in momentum required).
- Result: **Efficient light emission/absorption**.
- Examples: **GaAs, InP, GaN, CdTe, InAs**.

**Indirect Bandgap:**
- CBM and VBM occur at **different k-vectors** (e.g., VBM at $\Gamma$, CBM near $X$ or $L$).
- Electron transition requires a **phonon** to conserve momentum, in addition to a photon.
- Result: **Poor light emission efficiency**, but suitable for some electronic devices.
- Examples: **Si, Ge, AlAs, SiC**.

The energy-momentum relationship for a direct-gap transition is:

$$E_g^{direct} = E_C(\Gamma) - E_V(\Gamma)$$

For an indirect gap:

$$E_g^{indirect} = E_C(X \text{ or } L) - E_V(\Gamma)$$

---

### Step 3: Effective Mass from Band Curvature

The **effective mass** of a carrier near a band extremum is defined by the **second derivative** (curvature) of the energy band:

$$\frac{1}{m^*} = \frac{1}{\hbar^2} \frac{d^2 E}{dk^2}$$

A **sharply curved band** $\Rightarrow$ small $m^*$ $\Rightarrow$ **high mobility** ($\mu \propto 1/m^*$).
A **flat band** $\Rightarrow$ large $m^*$ $\Rightarrow$ **low mobility** (typical of heavy holes).

For the conduction band of a direct-gap III-V compound like GaAs, there is **one isotropic valley** at $\Gamma$. For indirect-gap materials like Si, there are **multiple equivalent conduction band valleys** (6 for Si along $\Delta$ near $X$), leading to **conductivity effective mass** corrections.

---

### Step 4: Intrinsic Carrier Concentration in Compound Semiconductors

For an intrinsic (undoped) compound semiconductor, the carrier concentration in the conduction band is:

$$n_i = \sqrt{N_C N_V} \exp\left(-\frac{E_g}{2 k_B T}\right)$$

where the effective density of states are:

$$N_C = 2 \left(\frac{2\pi m_e^* k_B T}{h^2}\right)^{3/2}$$

$$N_V = 2 \left(\frac{2\pi m_h^* k_B T}{h^2}\right)^{3/2}$$

The factor of **2** accounts for spin degeneracy.

---

### Step 5: Band Diagram of Doped Compound Semiconductors

When donors (e.g., Si substituting As in GaAs) or acceptors (e.g., Zn substituting Ga in GaAs) are introduced:
- **n-type (donor level $E_D$)**: $E_D$ lies just **below $E_C$**, typically at $E_C - 0.001$ to $E_C - 0.05$ eV.
- **p-type (acceptor level $E_A$)**: $E_A$ lies just **above $E_V$**, typically at $E_V + 0.001$ to $E_V + 0.05$ eV.

Electron concentration in n-type (non-degenerate, Boltzmann approximation):

$$n \approx N_D \quad \text{(if fully ionized)}$$

Fermi level position in n-type semiconductor:

$$E_F = E_C - k_B T \ln\left(\frac{N_C}{N_D}\right)$$

Fermi level position in p-type semiconductor:

$$E_F = E_V + k_B T \ln\left(\frac{N_V}{N_A}\right)$$

---

## 2.2 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **CRITICAL FORMULAS — Energy Band Diagrams of Compound Semiconductors**

| # | Formula | Description / Use | Units |
|:-:|:--------|:------------------|:------|
| 1 | $E_g = E_C - E_V$ | Bandgap energy | eV |
| 2 | $E(\vec{k}) = E_C + \frac{\hbar^2 k^2}{2 m_e^*}$ | Conduction band dispersion (parabolic) | eV |
| 3 | $E(\vec{k}) = E_V - \frac{\hbar^2 k^2}{2 m_h^*}$ | Valence band dispersion | eV |
| 4 | $m^* = \hbar^2 \left( \frac{d^2 E}{dk^2} \right)^{-1}$ | Effective mass definition | kg |
| 5 | $N_C = 2 \left( \frac{2\pi m_e^* k_B T}{h^2} \right)^{3/2}$ | Effective density of states (CB) | cm⁻³ |
| 6 | $N_V = 2 \left( \frac{2\pi m_h^* k_B T}{h^2} \right)^{3/2}$ | Effective density of states (VB) | cm⁻³ |
| 7 | $n_i = \sqrt{N_C N_V} \exp\left(-\frac{E_g}{2 k_B T}\right)$ | Intrinsic carrier concentration | cm⁻³ |
| 8 | $E_F - E_i = k_B T \ln\left( \frac{n}{n_i} \right)$ | Fermi level relative to intrinsic | eV |
| 9 | $\lambda (\mu m) = \frac{1.24}{E_g (eV)}$ | Emission/absorption wavelength | $\mu$m |
| 10 | $\mu = \frac{q \tau}{m^*}$ | Carrier mobility | cm²/(V·s) |
| 11 | $\Delta E_C + \Delta E_V = \Delta E_g$ | Heterojunction band offset relation | eV |
| 12 | $\Delta E_C : \Delta E_V \approx 60:40$ (GaAs/AlGaAs) | Empirical band offset ratio | ratio |
| 13 | $n_0 = N_C \exp\left(-\frac{E_C - E_F}{k_B T}\right)$ | Electron density (Boltzmann) | cm⁻³ |
| 14 | $p_0 = N_V \exp\left(-\frac{E_F - E_V}{k_B T}\right)$ | Hole density (Boltzmann) | cm⁻³ |
| 15 | $np = n_i^2$ | Mass-action law (thermal eq.) | cm⁻⁶ |

---

## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

> [!NOTE]
> **Why Energy Band Diagrams Matter in Compound Semiconductor Engineering:**
>
> 1. **Optoelectronic Device Design**: Lasers (GaAs/AlGaAs, InGaN), LEDs, and photodetectors all require knowledge of $E_g$ to determine emission wavelength $\lambda = 1.24/E_g$.
> 2. **High-Electron-Mobility Transistors (HEMTs)**: GaN/AlGaN HEMTs rely on precise band offsets ($\Delta E_C$) to form a 2-D Electron Gas (2DEG).
> 3. **Solar Cells**: Multi-junction cells (GaInP/GaAs/Ge) are designed by matching bandgaps of different compound semiconductors.
> 4. **Quantum Wells and Heterostructure Lasers**: Require flat-band diagrams with proper alignment of $E_C$ and $E_V$ across layers.
> 5. **Terahertz and Infrared Devices**: HgCdTe (mercury cadmium telluride) is tunable across IR by adjusting Cd composition and hence $E_g$.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Effective Density of States in Conduction Band ($N_C$)

We start from the count of available quantum states in k-space. Each k-state occupies a volume $(2\pi/L)^3$ per band, where $L$ is the crystal length.

**Step 1: Density of states in k-space.**
The number of allowed k-states per unit volume in a crystal is:

$$g(\vec{k}) \, d^3k = 2 \times \frac{d^3k}{(2\pi)^3}$$

The factor of **2** accounts for the two spin orientations of an electron.

**Step 2: Transform from k-space to energy.**
For a parabolic conduction band minimum at $\vec{k}=0$:

$$E = E_C + \frac{\hbar^2 k^2}{2 m_e^*}$$

Solving for $k$:

$$k = \sqrt{\frac{2 m_e^* (E - E_C)}{\hbar^2}}$$

In spherical symmetry, the volume element is $d^3k = 4\pi k^2 \, dk$.

**Step 3: Number of states per unit energy interval.**
Differentiating $E$ with respect to $k$:

$$dE = \frac{\hbar^2 k}{m_e^*} \, dk \quad \Rightarrow \quad dk = \frac{m_e^*}{\hbar^2 k} \, dE$$

Therefore, the density of states per unit energy per unit volume is:

$$g(E) \, dE = \frac{2}{(2\pi)^3} \times 4\pi k^2 \, dk = \frac{k^2}{\pi^2} \, dk$$

Substituting $k^2 = \frac{2 m_e^* (E - E_C)}{\hbar^2}$ and $dk = \frac{m_e^*}{\hbar^2 k} dE$:

$$g(E) = \frac{1}{\pi^2} \cdot \frac{2 m_e^* (E - E_C)}{\hbar^2} \cdot \frac{m_e^*}{\hbar^2 k}$$

$$g(E) = \frac{1}{2\pi^2} \left(\frac{2 m_e^*}{\hbar^2}\right)^{3/2} (E - E_C)^{1/2}$$

**Step 4: Compute the effective density of states $N_C$.**
The total electron density in the conduction band is:

$$n_0 = \int_{E_C}^{\infty} g(E) \, f(E) \, dE$$

Using the **Boltzmann approximation** $f(E) \approx \exp[-(E - E_F)/k_B T]$:

$$n_0 = \frac{1}{2\pi^2} \left(\frac{2 m_e^*}{\hbar^2}\right)^{3/2} \int_{E_C}^{\infty} (E - E_C)^{1/2} \exp\left[-\frac{E - E_F}{k_B T}\right] dE$$

Let $u = (E - E_C)/k_B T$:

$$n_0 = \frac{1}{2\pi^2} \left(\frac{2 m_e^*}{\hbar^2}\right)^{3/2} (k_B T)^{3/2} \exp\left[-\frac{E_C - E_F}{k_B T}\right] \int_{0}^{\infty} u^{1/2} e^{-u} du$$

The integral $\int_0^\infty u^{1/2} e^{-u} du = \frac{\sqrt{\pi}}{2}$.

$$\boxed{N_C = 2 \left(\frac{2 \pi m_e^* k_B T}{h^2}\right)^{3/2}}$$

where we converted $\hbar = h/2\pi$ back to $h$ and combined constants.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Intrinsic Fermi Level Position in a Compound Semiconductor

**Step 1: For an intrinsic semiconductor, $n_0 = p_0 = n_i$.**
Setting the electron and hole densities equal:

$$N_C \exp\left[-\frac{E_C - E_F}{k_B T}\right] = N_V \exp\left[-\frac{E_F - E_V}{k_B T}\right]$$

**Step 2: Take natural logarithm of both sides.**
$$\ln N_C - \frac{E_C - E_F}{k_B T} = \ln N_V - \frac{E_F - E_V}{k_B T}$$

**Step 3: Solve for $E_F$ (which is $E_i$ for intrinsic).**

$$\frac{2 E_F}{k_B T} = \ln N_C + \frac{E_C}{k_B T} + \ln N_V + \frac{E_V}{k_B T}$$

Wait, let me redo this carefully:

$$\ln N_C - \frac{E_C - E_F}{k_B T} = \ln N_V - \frac{E_F - E_V}{k_B T}$$

$$\ln N_C - \ln N_V = \frac{E_C - E_F}{k_B T} - \frac{E_F - E_V}{k_B T}$$

$$\ln\left(\frac{N_C}{N_V}\right) = \frac{E_C + E_V - 2 E_F}{k_B T}$$

**Step 4: Rearrange for $E_F = E_i$.**

$$E_i = \frac{E_C + E_V}{2} + \frac{k_B T}{2} \ln\left(\frac{N_V}{N_C}\right)$$

**Step 5: Express in terms of effective mass ratio.**
Substituting the $N_C$ and $N_V$ expressions:

$$\frac{N_V}{N_C} = \left(\frac{m_h^*}{m_e^*}\right)^{3/2}$$

$$\boxed{E_i = \frac{E_C + E_V}{2} + \frac{3 k_B T}{4} \ln\left(\frac{m_h^*}{m_e^*}\right)}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Physical Interpretation:** If $m_h^* = m_e^*$, then $E_i$ lies exactly at the middle of the bandgap. For GaAs, $m_h^* > m_e^*$, so $E_i$ is slightly **above** midgap.

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3: Wavelength of Emitted Photon from Band-to-Band Recombination

**Step 1: Energy conservation at direct transition.**
When an electron in the conduction band minimum recombines with a hole at the valence band maximum, the energy released is:

$$E_{photon} = E_g$$

**Step 2: Convert energy to wavelength using $E = h\nu = hc/\lambda$.**

$$\lambda = \frac{hc}{E_g}$$

**Step 3: Substitute numerical values for $h$, $c$, and convert $E_g$ to eV.**

$$h = 4.1357 \times 10^{-15} \text{ eV·s}, \quad c = 3 \times 10^8 \text{ m/s}$$

$$\lambda = \frac{(4.1357 \times 10^{-15})(3 \times 10^8)}{E_g} \text{ m}$$

$$\lambda = \frac{1.2407 \times 10^{-6}}{E_g (\text{eV})} \text{ m}$$

$$\boxed{\lambda (\mu m) = \frac{1.24}{E_g (\text{eV})}}$$

**Worked Example (KTU Numerical Style):**
For GaAs with $E_g = 1.42$ eV:

$$\lambda = \frac{1.24}{1.42} = 0.873 \, \mu m = 873 \, \text{nm}$$

This is in the **near-infrared** range, explaining why GaAs is used in CD-laser technology (780 nm is achieved with AlGaAs).

For GaN with $E_g = 3.4$ eV:

$$\lambda = \frac{1.24}{3.4} = 0.365 \, \mu m = 365 \, \text{nm (UV)}$$

This is why GaN is the material of choice for **blue and UV LEDs**.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix: Compound Semiconductor Comparison

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Comparison Matrix — Key Compound Semiconductors (KTU 2024 High-Yield Table)**

| Parameter | GaAs | InP | GaN | CdTe | Si (reference) |
|:----------|:----:|:---:|:---:|:----:|:--------------:|
| $E_g$ (eV) at 300 K | **1.42** | **1.35** | **3.4** | **1.45** | 1.12 |
| Bandgap type | Direct | Direct | Direct | Direct | Indirect |
| $m_e^*/m_0$ | 0.067 | 0.077 | 0.20 | 0.11 | 0.26 (cond.) |
| $m_{hh}^*/m_0$ | 0.50 | 0.60 | 1.0 | 0.35 | 0.49 |
| Mobility $\mu_e$ (cm²/V·s) | **8500** | 5400 | 1000 | 1050 | 1400 |
| Mobility $\mu_h$ (cm²/V·s) | 400 | 200 | 30 | 100 | 450 |
| Lattice const. (Å) | 5.653 | 5.869 | 3.189 | 6.482 | 5.431 |
| Primary use | RF, Laser, Solar | Telecom laser | Blue/UV LED | Solar cell, IR | CMOS baseline |

---

## 3.5 Derivation 4: Carrier Concentration in Doped (n-type) Compound Semiconductor

**Step 1: Charge neutrality condition.**
For an n-type compound semiconductor with donor concentration $N_D$ (fully ionized) and no acceptors:

$$n_0 = N_D^+ + p_0 \approx N_D \quad \text{(if } n_0 \gg p_0\text{)}$$

**Step 2: Express $n_0$ in terms of $E_F$.**

$$n_0 = N_C \exp\left[-\frac{E_C - E_F}{k_B T}\right] = N_D$$

**Step 3: Solve for the Fermi level position.**

$$\exp\left[\frac{E_F - E_C}{k_B T}\right] = \frac{N_C}{N_D}$$

$$E_F = E_C - k_B T \ln\left(\frac{N_C}{N_D}\right)$$

**Step 4: Numerical Example.**
For GaAs at 300 K: $N_C = 4.7 \times 10^{17}$ cm⁻³, $N_D = 10^{17}$ cm⁻³, $k_B T = 0.02585$ eV.

$$E_C - E_F = 0.02585 \times \ln(4.7) = 0.02585 \times 1.548 = 0.040 \text{ eV}$$

The Fermi level lies **0.040 eV below $E_C$**, which is typical for moderately doped n-GaAs.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Diagram 1: Classification of Energy Band Structures in Compound Semiconductors

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[CRYSTALLINE COMPOUND SEMICONDUCTOR] --> B{Direct Bandgap?}
    B -->|YES| C[Direct Gap Materials]
    B -->|NO| D[Indirect Gap Materials]
    C --> C1[GaAs - E_g 1.42 eV]
    C --> C2[InP - E_g 1.35 eV]
    C --> C3[GaN - E_g 3.4 eV]
    C --> C4[CdTe - E_g 1.45 eV]
    C --> C5[InAs - E_g 0.36 eV]
    D --> D1[AlAs - E_g 2.16 eV]
    D --> D2[Si - E_g 1.12 eV]
    D --> D3[Ge - E_g 0.66 eV]
    C1 --> E1[Application: Laser LED Solar]
    C2 --> E2[Application: Telecom 1550 nm]
    C3 --> E3[Application: Blue LED HEMT]
    D1 --> E4[Application: HBT with GaAs]
    D2 --> E5[Application: CMOS Microelectronics]
```

---

## 4.2 Mermaid Diagram 2: Block-Level Functional Architecture of an Energy Band Diagram Construction

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A1[Atomic Orbitals] --> A2[Brillouin Zone Definition]
    A2 --> A3[Compute E_k Dispersion]
    A3 --> A4{Identify CBM and VBM k positions}
    A4 -->|Same k| B1[DIRECT BANDGAP]
    A4 -->|Different k| B2[INDIRECT BANDGAP]
    B1 --> C1[Draw E_C and E_V lines]
    B2 --> C2[Draw E_C and E_V lines]
    C1 --> D1[Add E_g label]
    C2 --> D2[Add E_g label]
    C1 --> E1[Add Fermi Level E_F]
    C2 --> E2[Add Fermi Level E_F]
    E1 --> F1[Add Donor or Acceptor Levels]
    E2 --> F2[Add Donor or Acceptor Levels]
    F1 --> G1[FINAL ENERGY BAND DIAGRAM]
    F2 --> G2[FINAL ENERGY BAND DIAGRAM]
```

---

## 4.3 Mermaid Diagram 3: Energy Band Diagram of a p-n Heterojunction (GaAs / AlGaAs)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph LEFT[GaAs p-type]
        L1[E_V line high]
        L2[E_F left side]
        L3[E_C line]
        L4[Acceptor level E_A]
    end
    subgraph RIGHT[AlGaAs n-type wider gap]
        R1[E_V line lower position]
        R2[E_F right side]
        R3[E_C line higher position]
        R4[Donor level E_D]
    end
    L1 -. band offset dEv .-> R1
    L3 -. band offset dEc .-> R3
    L2 -. aligned at equilibrium .-> R2
```

**Caption for the heterojunction diagram:** The above block diagram represents the relative band positions in a GaAs(p) / AlGaAs(n) heterojunction. The conduction band offset $\Delta E_C$ and valence band offset $\Delta E_V$ arise from the difference in electron affinities of the two compound semiconductors.

---

## 4.4 Mermaid Diagram 4: Process Flow — How a Photon Interacts with a Direct Bandgap Compound Semiconductor

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    P1[Photon with energy h nu greater than E_g] --> P2{Photon absorbed by Valence Electron}
    P2 --> P3[Electron Excited from E_V to E_C]
    P3 --> P4[Electron thermalizes to CBM]
    P4 --> P5[Direct Recombination with Hole at VBM]
    P5 --> P6[Photon of wavelength lambda 1.24 over E_g emitted]
    P6 --> P7[LED or LASER light output]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Insight:** This direct recombination process is the foundation of all **optoelectronic devices** (LEDs, laser diodes, photodetectors) made from direct bandgap compound semiconductors like GaAs, InP, and GaN.

---

## 4.5 Schematic Notation of Standard Energy Band Diagram Features

| Symbol | Meaning |
|:------:|:--------|
| $E_C$ | Bottom edge of conduction band |
| $E_V$ | Top edge of valence band |
| $E_g$ | Bandgap = $E_C - E_V$ |
| $E_F$ | Fermi level |
| $E_i$ | Intrinsic Fermi level |
| $E_D$ | Donor energy level (just below $E_C$) |
| $E_A$ | Acceptor energy level (just above $E_V$) |
| $\Gamma$, $X$, $L$ | High-symmetry points in Brillouin zone |
| $\Delta E_C$ | Conduction band offset at heterojunction |
| $\Delta E_V$ | Valence band offset at heterojunction |
| 2DEG | Two-dimensional electron gas (at HEMT interface) |

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **Q1. [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]**
> **Define direct and indirect bandgap semiconductors. Give one example for each from compound semiconductors.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
A **direct bandgap semiconductor** is one in which the conduction band minimum (CBM) and valence band maximum (VBM) occur at the **same value of crystal momentum $\vec{k}$** (typically at the $\Gamma$ point). Electron-hole recombination can therefore occur with emission of a photon alone, without the assistance of a phonon. Example: **GaAs** ($E_g = 1.42$ eV).

An **indirect bandgap semiconductor** is one in which the CBM and VBM occur at **different $\vec{k}$** values (e.g., VBM at $\Gamma$ and CBM near the $X$ point). Recombination requires a phonon to conserve momentum, making light emission inefficient. Example: **AlAs** ($E_g = 2.16$ eV indirect) or **Si** ($E_g = 1.12$ eV indirect).

---

> **Q2. [KTU University Exam - May 2024]**
> **What is effective mass of an electron in a semiconductor? How is it related to the curvature of the energy band?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
The **effective mass** $m^*$ of an electron in a semiconductor is a parameter that accounts for the effect of the crystal lattice potential on the electron's motion, allowing it to be treated as a free particle with mass $m^*$. It is defined as:

$$m^* = \frac{\hbar^2}{\frac{d^2 E}{dk^2}}$$

It is **inversely proportional to the second derivative (curvature)** of the E-k band. A **sharply curved** (concave-up) conduction band gives a **small positive effective mass**, while a **flat band** gives a **large effective mass**. Heavy holes in the valence band have flatter bands and hence larger effective masses than electrons in the conduction band.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Part B Format:** Two alternative questions (Q-A and Q-B) — KTU ESE Internal Choice Pattern. Each has sub-parts (a) 7 marks and (b) 7 marks.

---

### **QUESTION A (14 Marks)**
**[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 Model Paper]**

**(a)** Draw the energy band diagram of an **intrinsic GaAs** at 300 K. Label the conduction band $E_C$, valence band $E_V$, bandgap $E_g$, and intrinsic Fermi level $E_i$. Show numerically the value of $E_g$ and the position of $E_i$ relative to $E_C$. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** With necessary expressions, derive the **intrinsic Fermi level position** in terms of effective masses of electrons and holes. Comment on the case when $m_h^* = m_e^*$. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Part (a):**
- $E_g$(GaAs) = **1.42 eV** at 300 K.
- $m_e^* = 0.067 m_0$, $m_{hh}^* = 0.50 m_0$.
- $E_i$ is **slightly above midgap** (since $m_h^* > m_e^*$).

**Numerical value of $E_C - E_i$:**

$$E_C - E_i = \frac{E_g}{2} + \frac{3 k_B T}{4} \ln\left(\frac{m_e^*}{m_h^*}\right)$$

$$= \frac{1.42}{2} + \frac{3 \times 0.02585}{4} \ln\left(\frac{0.067}{0.50}\right)$$

$$= 0.710 + 0.0194 \times \ln(0.134)$$

$$= 0.710 + 0.0194 \times (-2.010)$$

$$= 0.710 - 0.039 = 0.671 \text{ eV}$$

So $E_i$ lies **0.671 eV below $E_C$** and **0.749 eV above $E_V$**.

**Part (b):**
Starting from $n_0 = p_0$:

$$N_C \exp\left[-\frac{E_C - E_F}{k_B T}\right] = N_V \exp\left[-\frac{E_F - E_V}{k_B T}\right]$$

$$\ln\left(\frac{N_V}{N_C}\right) = \frac{E_C + E_V - 2E_i}{k_B T}$$

$$E_i = \frac{E_C + E_V}{2} + \frac{k_B T}{2} \ln\left(\frac{N_V}{N_C}\right)$$

Substituting $\frac{N_V}{N_C} = \left(\frac{m_h^*}{m_e^*}\right)^{3/2}$:

$$E_i = \frac{E_C + E_V}{2} + \frac{3 k_B T}{4} \ln\left(\frac{m_h^*}{m_e^*}\right)$$

**Case $m_h^* = m_e^*$:** The log term vanishes, and $E_i$ lies exactly at the **midgap**, i.e., $E_i = (E_C + E_V)/2$. This is the case for an idealized symmetric band structure.

---

### **QUESTION B (14 Marks)**
**[KTU University Exam - May 2024 Model Paper]**

**(a)** Compare the **energy band structures** of **GaAs, InP, GaN, and AlAs** in terms of (i) bandgap energy, (ii) direct/indirect nature, and (iii) effective mass of electron. Explain why direct bandgap compound semiconductors are preferred for **LEDs and laser diodes**. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A **GaAs sample** is doped n-type with $N_D = 5 \times 10^{16}$ cm⁻³. Given $N_C = 4.7 \times 10^{17}$ cm⁻³, $N_V = 7.0 \times 10^{18}$ cm⁻³, $E_g = 1.42$ eV, $T = 300$ K, calculate: (i) the electron concentration, (ii) the hole concentration, (iii) the position of the Fermi level $E_F$ relative to $E_C$. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Part (a):**

| Material | $E_g$ (eV) | Type | $m_e^*/m_0$ |
|:---------|:----------:|:----:|:-----------:|
| GaAs | 1.42 | Direct | 0.067 |
| InP | 1.35 | Direct | 0.077 |
| GaN | 3.40 | Direct | 0.20 |
| AlAs | 2.16 | **Indirect** | 0.71 (X-valley) |

Direct bandgap compound semiconductors are preferred for LEDs and laser diodes because the **conduction band minimum and valence band maximum occur at the same k-vector**, allowing **efficient radiative recombination** with photon emission alone, without needing phonon assistance. This yields **high internal quantum efficiency** and **bright electroluminescence**. For example, GaAs (1.42 eV) emits at 873 nm (NIR), InP (1.35 eV) at 920 nm, and GaN (3.4 eV) at 365 nm (UV). In contrast, indirect bandgap AlAs has a much longer radiative lifetime and is unsuitable as an active laser medium.

**Part (b):**
(i) **Electron concentration** (assume full ionization):
$$n_0 \approx N_D = 5 \times 10^{16} \text{ cm}^{-3}$$

(ii) **Hole concentration** using mass-action law:
$$n_i^2 = N_C N_V \exp\left(-\frac{E_g}{k_B T}\right)$$

$$n_i = \sqrt{4.7 \times 10^{17} \times 7.0 \times 10^{18}} \exp\left(-\frac{1.42}{2 \times 0.02585}\right)$$

$$n_i = \sqrt{3.29 \times 10^{36}} \times \exp(-27.466)$$

$$n_i = 1.814 \times 10^{18} \times 1.143 \times 10^{-12}$$

$$n_i = 2.07 \times 10^{6} \text{ cm}^{-3}$$

$$p_0 = \frac{n_i^2}{n_0} = \frac{(2.07 \times 10^6)^2}{5 \times 10^{16}} = \frac{4.28 \times 10^{12}}{5 \times 10^{16}} = 8.57 \times 10^{-5} \text{ cm}^{-3}$$

(iii) **Fermi level position:**
$$E_C - E_F = k_B T \ln\left(\frac{N_C}{N_D}\right) = 0.02585 \times \ln\left(\frac{4.7 \times 10^{17}}{5 \times 10^{16}}\right)$$

$$= 0.02585 \times \ln(9.4) = 0.02585 \times 2.241 = 0.0579 \text{ eV}$$

So the Fermi level is **0.058 eV below $E_C$**.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes that Cost Marks in KTU Energy Band Diagram Questions:**
>
> 1. **Confusing $E_C$ and $E_V$ positions in the diagram.** Always remember $E_C > E_V$ on the energy axis. Many students draw them inverted.
> 2. **Forgetting the spin degeneracy factor of 2** in $N_C$ and $N_V$ formulas. This is a frequent 1-mark deduction.
> 3. **Using $kT$ in joules instead of eV** when computing Fermi level positions. Always convert: $k_B T$ at 300 K = **0.02585 eV** (not $4.14 \times 10^{-21}$ J).
> 4. **Not specifying the k-point** when defining direct vs indirect bandgap. Examiner expects $\Gamma$, $X$, or $L$ notation.
> 5. **Mixing up effective mass of heavy hole vs light hole** in numerical problems. Read the question carefully — if it says $m_{hh}^*$ vs $m_{lh}^*$, use the right one.
> 6. **Drawing the band diagram without specifying the energy scale or numerical values of $E_g$.** Always annotate key energy values directly on the diagram.
> 7. **Skipping the $E_i$ position** in the band diagram of an intrinsic semiconductor — this is a 2-mark item.
> 8. **For heterojunction questions**, students often forget the relation $\Delta E_C + \Delta E_V = \Delta E_g$ — a critical band-offset relation.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!NOTE]
> **High-Density Revision Bullets — Energy Band Diagrams of Compound Semiconductors**

- The **energy band diagram** shows allowed/forbidden electron energies vs position (real space) or momentum (k-space).
- Three key levels: **$E_C$ (conduction band minimum)**, **$E_V$ (valence band maximum)**, and **$E_F$ (Fermi level)**.
- **Bandgap** $E_g = E_C - E_V$; **larger $E_g$** $\Rightarrow$ **shorter emission wavelength** $\lambda = 1.24/E_g$.
- **Direct bandgap** = CBM and VBM at same k (efficient light emission). **Indirect** = at different k (poor light emission).
- Compound semiconductors like **GaAs, InP, GaN, CdTe** are **direct bandgap** — used in LEDs, lasers, solar cells.
- **AlAs and Si** are **indirect** — used in HBT, CMOS, not for light emission.
- **Effective mass** $m^* = \hbar^2 / (d^2E/dk^2)$ — sharper curvature = smaller $m^*$ = higher mobility.
- **Effective density of states**:
  $N_C = 2 (2\pi m_e^* k_B T / h^2)^{3/2}$, similar for $N_V$.
- **Intrinsic carrier concentration**:
  $n_i = \sqrt{N_C N_V} \exp(-E_g / 2k_B T)$.
- **Intrinsic Fermi level**:
  $E_i = (E_C+E_V)/2 + (3k_BT/4)\ln(m_h^*/m_e^*)$.
- **Fermi level in n-type**:
  $E_F = E_C - k_B T \ln(N_C/N_D)$.
- **Fermi level in p-type**:
  $E_F = E_V + k_B T \ln(N_V/N_A)$.
- **Mass-action law**: $np = n_i^2$ (thermal equilibrium).
- **Heterojunction band offsets**: $\Delta E_C + \Delta E_V = \Delta E_g$; for GaAs/AlGaAs, $\Delta E_C : \Delta E_V \approx 60:40$.
- **GaAs**: $E_g = 1.42$ eV, direct, $\lambda = 873$ nm (NIR laser).
- **GaN**: $E_g = 3.4$ eV, direct, $\lambda = 365$ nm (UV/blue LED).
- **InP**: $E_g = 1.35$ eV, direct, $\lambda = 920$ nm (telecom laser at 1.55 $\mu$m requires quaternary InGaAsP).
- **CdTe**: $E_g = 1.45$ eV, direct, used in thin-film solar cells and IR detectors.
- **HgCdTe**: $E_g$ tunable from 0 to 1.5 eV by adjusting Cd fraction — primary IR detector material.
- **High-symmetry points** in the Brillouin zone: $\Gamma$ (center), $X$ (face), $L$ (corner) — used to identify direct/indirect nature.
- **Donor level $E_D$** lies just below $E_C$ (typically 0.001–0.05 eV below for III-V compounds).
- **Acceptor level $E_A$** lies just above $E_V$ (typically 0.001–0.05 eV above for III-V compounds).
- **Wavelength–bandgap relation**: $\lambda(\mu m) = 1.24 / E_g(eV)$ — memorize this formula.
- **Energy conversion**: 1 eV = $1.602 \times 10^{-19}$ J = $1.602 \times 10^{-12}$ erg.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Off Voltage
topics.content:
<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEEVT413 - COMPOUND SEMICONDUCTORS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Off Voltage

# ⚡ Off Voltage in Compound Semiconductor Field-Effect Devices

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 📘 Formal KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Off Voltage (Pinch-Off Voltage / Threshold Voltage)**: The **Off Voltage** ($V_{off}$ or $V_p$ or $V_{th}$) in a compound semiconductor Field-Effect Transistor (MESFET, HEMT, MODFET, or JFET) is defined as the **gate-to-source bias voltage at which the conducting channel is completely depleted of mobile charge carriers**, causing the drain current ($I_{DS}$) to fall to a negligibly small value (typically $\leq 1\ \mu A/\text{mm}$ for power devices). It is the *boundary voltage* separating the **ON-state** (conducting) from the **OFF-state** (non-conducting) operation of the transistor.

For **Depletion-mode (D-mode)** devices, $V_{off}$ is **negative** (for n-channel devices on GaAs, InP, GaN substrates), meaning the channel is already conducting at $V_{GS} = 0$ V and must be *pinched off* by applying a negative gate bias. For **Enhancement-mode (E-mode)** devices, $V_{off}$ is **positive**, and a positive gate bias must be applied to induce the channel.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Terminology Convention**: In Board Examinations, the terms *Off Voltage*, *Pinch-Off Voltage* ($V_p$), and *Threshold Voltage* ($V_{th}$) are often used interchangeably for MESFETs/MODFETs. However, strictly:
> - **Pinch-off voltage** → MESFET (depletion-mode dominant in III-V technology)
> - **Threshold voltage** → MOSFET, HEMT (enhancement-mode or quantum-well)
> - **Off voltage** → Generic device-engineering term used in switch-mode power and digital logic contexts

### 🧠 Conceptual Analogy — The "Garden Hose Pinch" Model

Imagine a **flexible rubber garden hose** carrying water (the **electron current** in the channel). Your thumb is the **gate electrode**, and the pressure of your thumb represents the **gate voltage**.

| Thumb Pressure (Gate Voltage) | Hose State | Device State |
|-------------------------------|------------|--------------|
| No pressure ($V_{GS} = 0$ V) | Water flows freely | **ON** (D-mode default) |
| Light pinch | Reduced flow | Linear region |
| Hard pinch ($V_{GS} = V_{off}$) | Water stops completely | **OFF** |
| Stronger pinch ($\vert V_{GS}\vert > \vert V_{off}\vert$) | Still off, but **breakdown** risk | **Sub-threshold / Leakage** |

> [!TIP]
> The key insight: **Off Voltage is not a property of the channel electrons themselves** — it is a property of the **Schottky barrier / p-n junction / hetero-interface** that *depletes* the channel. Changing the doping, layer thickness, or heterojunction discontinuity directly engineers $V_{off}$.

### 🔑 Physical Constants & Key Metrics (Highlighted)

- **Permittivity of free space**: $\varepsilon_0 = 8.854 \times 10^{-12}\ \text{F/m}$
- **Relative permittivity of GaAs**: $\varepsilon_r = 13.1$
- **Relative permittivity of AlGaAs**: $\varepsilon_r \approx 12.9$ (for Al mole fraction $x \approx 0.3$)
- **Relative permittivity of GaN**: $\varepsilon_r = 9.0$
- **Schottky barrier height (Au/GaAs)**: $\phi_{Bn} \approx 0.8\ \text{V}$
- **Built-in potential of GaAs Schottky junction**: $V_{bi} \approx 0.7 - 0.9\ \text{V}$
- **Donor concentration in channel**: $N_D \approx 10^{17}\ \text{cm}^{-3}$
- **Electron saturation velocity in GaAs**: $v_{sat} \approx 1.0 \times 10^7\ \text{cm/s}$

> [!NOTE]
> The **high electron mobility** and **wide bandgap** of compound semiconductors (GaAs: $\mu_n \approx 8500\ \text{cm}^2/\text{V·s}$; GaN: $\mu_n \approx 1500\ \text{cm}^2/\text{V·s}$, $E_g = 3.4\ \text{eV}$) make them superior to silicon for high-frequency and high-power applications. The **off voltage** directly determines the **noise margin**, **switching speed**, and **power-handling capability** of these devices.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 🧩 Physical Origin of Off Voltage — Step-by-Step Logic

The off voltage arises from the **depletion region** that forms under the gate contact. The chain of physical reasoning is:

1. **Step 1 — Schottky/Junction Formation**: A metal contact (or p-n junction) is formed on top of a doped compound semiconductor channel. This creates a **built-in potential** $V_{bi}$ due to Fermi-level alignment, even with zero applied bias.

2. **Step 2 — Equilibrium Depletion**: At thermal equilibrium ($V_{GS} = 0$), a **depletion region** of width $W_0$ extends into the semiconductor, ionizing all donors within it:
$$W_0 = \sqrt{\dfrac{2\varepsilon_s (V_{bi} - V_{GS})}{q N_D}}$$

3. **Step 3 — Channel Pinch-Off**: As the **reverse gate bias** is increased (more negative for n-channel), $W$ grows. When $W$ equals the **effective channel thickness** $a$ (the depth of the conducting layer), the entire channel is depleted → **OFF state**.

4. **Step 4 — Off-Voltage Condition**: Substituting $W = a$ and $V_{GS} = V_{off}$ into the depletion-width equation yields the **punch-through / pinch-off voltage**:
$$V_{off} = V_{bi} - \dfrac{q N_D a^2}{2\varepsilon_s}$$

5. **Step 5 — Engineering the Off Voltage**: Designers can tune $V_{off}$ by varying **three parameters**:
   - **Doping density** $N_D$ (lower → less negative $V_{off}$)
   - **Channel thickness** $a$ (thinner → less negative $V_{off}$, enables E-mode)
   - **Built-in potential** $V_{bi}$ (higher → more negative $V_{off}$)

6. **Step 6 — HEMT Extension**: In a **High Electron Mobility Transistor**, the 2-Dimensional Electron Gas (2DEG) at the heterointerface is **induced** by the discontinuity in conduction band $\Delta E_C$. The off voltage then depends on the **spacer layer**, **doping plane distance**, and **quantum well depth**:
$$V_{off} = \phi_b - \dfrac{\Delta E_C}{q} - \dfrac{q N_D d^2}{2\varepsilon_s} - \dfrac{q\ \sigma}{C_{ox}}$$
where $\sigma$ is the 2DEG sheet charge density and $C_{ox}$ is the heterojunction capacitance per unit area.

### 📐 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **MASTER TABLE — Memorize for KTU Board Exams**

| # | Formula | Description | Units / Notes |
|---|---------|-------------|---------------|
| 1 | $V_p = \dfrac{q N_D a^2}{2\varepsilon_s}$ | **Pinch-off potential** (built-in drop across channel) | Volts (V) |
| 2 | $V_{off} = V_{bi} - \dfrac{q N_D a^2}{2\varepsilon_s}$ | **Off Voltage of MESFET** | V; negative for n-channel D-mode |
| 3 | $W(V) = \sqrt{\dfrac{2\varepsilon_s (V_{bi} - V_{GS})}{q N_D}}$ | **Gate depletion width** | meters (m) |
| 4 | $n_s(V) = \dfrac{C_g}{q}\left[V_{GS} - V_{off}\right]$ | **Sheet carrier density** (linear region) | $\text{cm}^{-2}$ |
| 5 | $I_{D,sat} = \dfrac{\mu_n \varepsilon_s W_g}{2 a L_g}\left(V_{GS} - V_{off}\right)^2$ | **Saturation drain current** | A |
| 6 | $g_m = \dfrac{\mu_n \varepsilon_s W_g}{a L_g}\left(V_{GS} - V_{off}\right)$ | **Transconductance** | Siemens (S) |
| 7 | $V_{off}^{HEMT} = \phi_{bn} - V_P + \Delta E_C / q$ | **HEMT threshold voltage** | V; $\phi_{bn}$ is Schottky barrier |
| 8 | $C_{gs} = \dfrac{\varepsilon_s L_g W_g}{a}$ | **Gate-source capacitance** | Farads (F) |
| 9 | $f_T = \dfrac{g_m}{2\pi C_{gs}}$ | **Unity-current-gain cutoff frequency** | Hz |
| 10 | $V_{br} = \dfrac{\varepsilon_s E_{br}^2}{2 q N_D}$ | **Breakdown voltage** (related to off state) | V |

> [!WARNING]
> **Critical Substitution**: For numerical problems, always remember $\varepsilon_s = \varepsilon_r \cdot \varepsilon_0$ and convert $N_D$ from $\text{cm}^{-3}$ to $\text{m}^{-3}$ (multiply by $10^6$) **before** substituting into SI-unit formulas. KTU examiners frequently award 0 marks to students who forget this conversion.

### 🌍 Real-World Engineering Utility

The off voltage is the **single most important design parameter** in compound semiconductor ICs:

- **Satellite Receivers (LNA Design)**: A MESFET with $V_{off} = -2\ \text{V}$ is biased at $V_{GS} = -1\ \text{V}$ for **Class A operation** — half the pinchoff — optimizing noise figure.
- **5G GaN Power Amplifiers**: E-mode GaN HEMTs with $V_{off} = +1.5\ \text{V}$ enable **normally-off** operation, critical for **fail-safe** radar and base-station systems.
- **Digital GaAs Logic (DCFL)**: The $V_{off}$ determines the **noise margin** of ECL-compatible direct-coupled FET logic.
- **Photodetectors & Phototransistors**: Off voltage sets the **dark current** floor and **detection sensitivity** in InGaAs/InP APDs.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 🔬 Derivation 1: Off Voltage of a GaAs MESFET (Full Derivation)

**Given**: 
- n-channel GaAs MESFET
- Channel doping: $N_D = 1 \times 10^{17}\ \text{cm}^{-3}$
- Channel thickness: $a = 0.2\ \mu\text{m} = 0.2 \times 10^{-6}\ \text{m}$
- Built-in potential: $V_{bi} = 0.8\ \text{V}$
- Relative permittivity of GaAs: $\varepsilon_r = 13.1$

**Find**: $V_{off}$

**Step 1 — Convert all quantities to SI units**

$$N_D = 1 \times 10^{17}\ \text{cm}^{-3} = 1 \times 10^{23}\ \text{m}^{-3}$$

$$a = 0.2\ \mu\text{m} = 2 \times 10^{-7}\ \text{m}$$

$$\varepsilon_s = \varepsilon_r \varepsilon_0 = 13.1 \times 8.854 \times 10^{-12} = 1.160 \times 10^{-10}\ \text{F/m}$$

**Step 2 — Recall the depletion width formula**

For an abrupt Schottky junction on a uniformly doped semiconductor, applying **Poisson's equation** and the **depletion approximation** gives:

$$W = \sqrt{\dfrac{2 \varepsilon_s \left(V_{bi} - V_{GS}\right)}{q N_D}}$$

The total voltage drop across the depletion region equals $V_{bi} - V_{GS}$ (for an n-type channel with reverse bias).

**Step 3 — Apply the pinch-off condition**

The channel is fully pinched off when the depletion region extends through the entire channel:

$$W = a$$

**Step 4 — Square both sides and solve for $V_{GS}$**

$$a^2 = \dfrac{2 \varepsilon_s (V_{bi} - V_{GS})}{q N_D}$$

$$V_{bi} - V_{GS} = \dfrac{q N_D a^2}{2 \varepsilon_s}$$

**Step 5 — Recognize the pinch-off potential**

Define the **pinch-off potential** $V_p$ as the voltage that just depletes the channel from the top:

$$V_p = \dfrac{q N_D a^2}{2 \varepsilon_s}$$

**Step 6 — Solve for the gate voltage at pinch-off**

$$V_{GS} = V_{bi} - V_p = V_{bi} - \dfrac{q N_D a^2}{2 \varepsilon_s}$$

By definition, at pinch-off $V_{GS} \equiv V_{off}$:

$$\boxed{V_{off} = V_{bi} - \dfrac{q N_D a^2}{2 \varepsilon_s}}$$

**Step 7 — Plug in numerical values**

$$
\begin{aligned}
V_p &= \dfrac{(1.6 \times 10^{-19})(1 \times 10^{23})(2 \times 10^{-7})^2}{2 \times (1.160 \times 10^{-10})} \\[6pt]
&= \dfrac{(1.6 \times 10^{-19})(1 \times 10^{23})(4 \times 10^{-14})}{2.32 \times 10^{-10}} \\[6pt]
&= \dfrac{6.4 \times 10^{-10}}{2.32 \times 10^{-10}} \\[6pt]
&= 2.759\ \text{V}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 8 — Final computation of $V_{off}$**

$$V_{off} = 0.8 - 2.759 = -1.959\ \text{V}$$

$$\boxed{V_{off} \approx -1.96\ \text{V}}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Negative $V_{off}$ confirms D-mode (depletion-mode) operation**: At $V_{GS} = 0$, the channel is conducting. To turn it OFF, a gate bias of $-1.96$ V must be applied. This is the classical GaAs MESFET behavior used in **microwave monolithic integrated circuits (MMICs)**.

---

### 🔬 Derivation 2: Off Voltage of an AlGaAs/GaAs HEMT (Full Derivation)

**Given**:
- HEMT structure: undoped AlGaAs spacer + doped AlGaAs donor layer + GaAs channel
- Donor layer doping: $N_D = 1.5 \times 10^{18}\ \text{cm}^{-3}$
- Donor layer thickness: $d_d = 400\ \text{\AA} = 4 \times 10^{-8}\ \text{m}$
- Spacer thickness: $d_s = 30\ \text{\AA}$
- Schottky barrier height: $\phi_{bn} = 1.0\ \text{V}$
- Conduction band discontinuity: $\Delta E_C / q = 0.3\ \text{eV}$
- $\varepsilon_r (\text{AlGaAs}) = 12.9$

**Find**: $V_{off}^{HEMT}$

**Step 1 — Compute the pinch-off potential of the AlGaAs donor layer**

$$V_p = \dfrac{q N_D d_d^2}{2 \varepsilon_s}$$

Convert units: $N_D = 1.5 \times 10^{24}\ \text{m}^{-3}$, $\varepsilon_s = 12.9 \times 8.854 \times 10^{-12} = 1.142 \times 10^{-10}\ \text{F/m}$

$$
\begin{aligned}
V_p &= \dfrac{(1.6 \times 10^{-19})(1.5 \times 10^{24})(4 \times 10^{-8})^2}{2 \times (1.142 \times 10^{-10})} \\[6pt]
&= \dfrac{(1.6 \times 10^{-19})(1.5 \times 10^{24})(1.6 \times 10^{-15})}{2.284 \times 10^{-10}} \\[6pt]
&= \dfrac{3.84 \times 10^{-10}}{2.284 \times 10^{-10}} \\[6pt]
&= 1.681\ \text{V}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Apply the HEMT threshold voltage formula**

For a **normally-on (D-mode) AlGaAs/GaAs HEMT**, the threshold voltage is given by:

$$V_{off}^{HEMT} = \phi_{bn} - \dfrac{\Delta E_C}{q} - V_p$$

**Step 3 — Plug in numerical values**

$$V_{off}^{HEMT} = 1.0 - 0.3 - 1.681 = -0.981\ \text{V}$$

$$\boxed{V_{off}^{HEMT} \approx -0.98\ \text{V}}$$

> [!TIP]
> **Engineering Insight**: To convert this to an **E-mode (normally-off) HEMT** with $V_{off} > 0$, designers use:
> 1. **Recessing** the gate (etching into the AlGaAs)
> 2. **Fluorine ion implantation** under the gate
> 3. **p-GaN gate** (in GaN HEMTs)
> 4. **Thin AlGaAs barrier** ($d_d < 150\ \text{\AA}$)

---

### 📊 Analytical Matrix — Factors Affecting Off Voltage

| Parameter | Symbol | Effect of Increase on $\vert V_{off}\vert$ | Physical Reason |
|-----------|--------|------------------------------------------|-----------------|
| Channel doping | $N_D$ | ↑ Increases | More charge to deplete |
| Channel thickness | $a$ | ↑ Increases (quadratically) | Wider depletion required |
| Built-in potential | $V_{bi}$ | ↑ Increases | Higher barrier to overcome |
| Schottky metal work function | $\phi_M$ | ↑ Increases | Larger $V_{bi}$ |
| Conduction band offset | $\Delta E_C$ | ↓ Decreases | Deeper quantum well → harder to deplete |
| Relative permittivity | $\varepsilon_r$ | ↓ Decreases | Wider depletion per volt |
| Temperature | $T$ | ↓ Decreases (slightly) | $V_{bi}$ decreases with T |
| Gate recess depth | $t_{rec}$ | ↓ Decreases | Thinner effective channel |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

### 🎨 Diagram 1: Cross-Section of a GaAs MESFET Showing Depletion Region


```mermaid
        Hetero["Heterojunction\nDelta E_C = 0.3 eV\nBandgap Discontinuity"]

        Schottky --> Spacer
        Spacer --> DonorLayer

        Hetero --> TwoDEG
        TwoDEG --> Output

```

```mermaid
    Spec -->|Positive| EMode["ENHANCEMENT-MODE Design\nThin channel or gate recess\np-GaN gate or F-implant"]

    VerifyE -->|Yes| FabricateE["Proceed to\nFabrication"]

```
