## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 📝 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

#### **Question 1** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**Define off voltage of a MESFET. Why is it typically negative for an n-channel GaAs MESFET?**

**Model Answer**:
> The **off voltage** ($V_{off}$) of a MESFET is the gate-to-source voltage at which the conducting channel between source and drain becomes completely depleted of mobile electrons, causing the drain current to fall to zero.
>
> For an n-channel GaAs MESFET, $V_{off}$ is **negative** because:
> 1. The **built-in potential** $V_{bi}$ of the metal-semiconductor (Schottky) junction is already positive ($\approx 0.8\ \text{V}$).
> 2. The **pinch-off potential** $V_p = qN_D a^2 / 2\varepsilon_s$ required to deplete the channel is **larger than** $V_{bi}$.
> 3. Therefore, $V_{off} = V_{bi} - V_p$ becomes **negative**, meaning a reverse (negative) gate bias must be applied to fully deplete the channel.
>
> The device is in the **ON state at $V_{GS} = 0$ V** — a **normally-on (D-mode)** device. (3 marks)

---

#### **Question 2** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**List any three factors affecting the off voltage of a compound semiconductor FET.**

**Model Answer**:
The three primary factors are:
1. **Channel doping concentration** ($N_D$) — Higher $N_D$ increases $\vert V_{off}\vert$.
2. **Active channel thickness** ($a$) — Thicker channels require more negative $V_{off}$.
3. **Built-in potential** ($V_{bi}$) — Depends on the Schottky metal's work function; higher $V_{bi}$ gives more negative $V_{off}$.

*(Other acceptable answers: dielectric constant, heterojunction $\Delta E_C$, gate recess depth, temperature.)* (3 marks)

---

### 📝 PART B — 14-Mark Module Internal Choice Questions

#### **Question A** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024 Model]`
**(a)** Derive an expression for the off voltage of a GaAs MESFET with a uniformly doped n-channel. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A GaAs MESFET has the following parameters: $N_D = 2 \times 10^{17}\ \text{cm}^{-3}$, $a = 0.15\ \mu\text{m}$, $V_{bi} = 0.75\ \text{V}$, $\varepsilon_r = 13.1$. Calculate the off voltage and comment on the operating mode. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution for (a):**

**Step 1**: Consider a Schottky gate on n-type GaAs with uniform doping $N_D$ and channel thickness $a$.

**Step 2**: Apply the depletion approximation. The depletion region extends to a width $W$ under the gate, given by:
$$W = \sqrt{\dfrac{2\varepsilon_s (V_{bi} - V_{GS})}{q N_D}}$$

**Step 3**: The condition for **channel pinch-off** is that the depletion width equals the channel thickness:
$$W = a$$

**Step 4**: Substituting and solving:
$$a^2 = \dfrac{2\varepsilon_s (V_{bi} - V_{off})}{q N_D}$$

$$V_{bi} - V_{off} = \dfrac{q N_D a^2}{2\varepsilon_s}$$

$$\boxed{V_{off} = V_{bi} - \dfrac{q N_D a^2}{2\varepsilon_s}}$$

This shows $V_{off}$ depends on **$V_{bi}$, $N_D$, $a$, and $\varepsilon_s$**. (7 marks)

**Model Solution for (b):**

**Step 1 — Convert units**:
- $N_D = 2 \times 10^{23}\ \text{m}^{-3}$
- $a = 1.5 \times 10^{-7}\ \text{m}$
- $\varepsilon_s = 13.1 \times 8.854 \times 10^{-12} = 1.160 \times 10^{-10}\ \text{F/m}$

**Step 2 — Calculate $V_p$**:

$$V_p = \dfrac{(1.6 \times 10^{-19})(2 \times 10^{23})(1.5 \times 10^{-7})^2}{2 \times (1.160 \times 10^{-10})}$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
V_p &= \dfrac{(1.6 \times 10^{-19})(2 \times 10^{23})(2.25 \times 10^{-14})}{2.32 \times 10^{-10}} \\[6pt]
&= \dfrac{7.2 \times 10^{-10}}{2.32 \times 10^{-10}} \\[6pt]
&= 3.103\ \text{V}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Calculate $V_{off}$**:

$$V_{off} = 0.75 - 3.103 = -2.353\ \text{V}$$

$$\boxed{V_{off} \approx -2.35\ \text{V}}$$

**Step 4 — Comment on operating mode**:
Since $V_{off}$ is **negative**, the device is **normally-on (D-mode)**. At $V_{GS} = 0$ V, the channel is conducting. To turn the device OFF, a gate bias more negative than $-2.35\ \text{V}$ must be applied. (7 marks)

---

#### **Question B** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 Model]`
**(a)** Explain with a band diagram how the off voltage of an AlGaAs/GaAs HEMT differs from that of a conventional MESFET. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** An AlGaAs/GaAs HEMT has $\phi_{bn} = 1.0\ \text{V}$, $\Delta E_C = 0.25\ \text{eV}$, donor thickness $d = 350\ \text{\AA}$, $N_D = 1.2 \times 10^{18}\ \text{cm}^{-3}$, $\varepsilon_r = 12.9$. Determine the threshold voltage. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution for (a):**

**MESFET (Conventional Junction)**:
- The channel is a **physically doped** n-type GaAs layer.
- Off voltage is determined by the **depletion of the doped channel** under the Schottky gate.
- $V_{off}^{MESFET} = V_{bi} - \dfrac{q N_D a^2}{2\varepsilon_s}$ — depends on **channel thickness** and **doping**.

**HEMT (Heterojunction)**:
- The conducting channel is a **2DEG** that forms at the **AlGaAs/GaAs heterointerface** due to band discontinuity $\Delta E_C$.
- The 2DEG is **not physically doped**; carriers are donated by a **separated donor layer** in AlGaAs.
- The off voltage depends on:
  1. **Schottky barrier** $\phi_{bn}$
  2. **Conduction band offset** $\Delta E_C / q$
  3. **Pinch-off potential** $V_p$ of the AlGaAs donor layer

**Band Diagram Differences**:
- MESFET: Single conduction band, band bending only due to Schottky barrier.
- HEMT: **Two-step band structure** — band bending in AlGaAs + conduction band discontinuity creating a **triangular quantum well** that confines 2DEG.

**Threshold Voltage**:
$$V_{off}^{HEMT} = \phi_{bn} - \dfrac{\Delta E_C}{q} - V_p$$

**Key Difference**: HEMT threshold is **reduced** by $\Delta E_C / q$ due to the deeper quantum well, allowing more precise control of $V_{off}$ via **spacer layer engineering** rather than channel doping. (7 marks)

**Model Solution for (b):**

**Step 1 — Convert units**:
- $N_D = 1.2 \times 10^{24}\ \text{m}^{-3}$
- $d = 350\ \text{\AA} = 3.5 \times 10^{-8}\ \text{m}$
- $\varepsilon_s = 12.9 \times 8.854 \times 10^{-12} = 1.142 \times 10^{-10}\ \text{F/m}$

**Step 2 — Calculate $V_p$**:

$$
\begin{aligned}
V_p &= \dfrac{(1.6 \times 10^{-19})(1.2 \times 10^{24})(3.5 \times 10^{-8})^2}{2 \times (1.142 \times 10^{-10})} \\[6pt]
&= \dfrac{(1.6 \times 10^{-19})(1.2 \times 10^{24})(1.225 \times 10^{-15})}{2.284 \times 10^{-10}} \\[6pt]
&= \dfrac{2.352 \times 10^{-10}}{2.284 \times 10^{-10}} \\[6pt]
&= 1.030\ \text{V}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Apply HEMT threshold formula**:

$$V_{off}^{HEMT} = \phi_{bn} - \dfrac{\Delta E_C}{q} - V_p$$

$$V_{off}^{HEMT} = 1.0 - 0.25 - 1.030 = -0.28\ \text{V}$$

$$\boxed{V_{off}^{HEMT} \approx -0.28\ \text{V}}$$

**Step 4 — Operating mode**: Since $V_{off}$ is negative, the device is **normally-on (D-mode)**. The relatively small $\vert V_{off}\vert$ is characteristic of HEMTs, allowing low-power switching operation. (7 marks)

---

> [!WARNING]
> **⚠️ KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout — Where Students Lose Marks**
> 1. **Unit Conversion Blunder (2-3 marks lost)**: Forgetting to convert $N_D$ from $\text{cm}^{-3}$ to $\text{m}^{-3}$ results in $V_{off}$ off by a factor of $10^6$. Always do this **first**.
> 2. **Sign Convention Confusion (1-2 marks lost)**: $V_{off}$ is **negative** for D-mode n-channel devices. Writing $V_{off} = +2.35\ \text{V}$ is incorrect — the device would be E-mode, contradicting the MESFET physical structure.
> 3. **Forgetting $V_{bi}$ in MESFET Formula (1 mark lost)**: A common error is writing $V_{off} = -qN_D a^2 / 2\varepsilon_s$ (omitting $V_{bi}$). Always include the built-in potential.
> 4. **Confusing MESFET and HEMT Formulas (3-4 marks lost)**: HEMT has the **extra term** $-\Delta E_C / q$ compared to MESFET. Mixing up the two formulas is the most common error in KTU Board papers.
> 5. **Not Stating Units in Final Answer (0.5 mark lost)**: Always write "V" or "Volts" after the numerical value.

---

### 🎯 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **🔥 HIGH-DENSITY REVISION SUMMARY — MEMORIZE BEFORE EXAM**
>
> ✅ **Off Voltage Definition** = Gate voltage at which channel is fully depleted and $I_{DS} = 0$
>
> ✅ **MESFET Formula**: $V_{off} = V_{bi} - \dfrac{q N_D a^2}{2\varepsilon_s}$ — Negative for n-channel D-mode
>
> ✅ **HEMT Formula**: $V_{off} = \phi_{bn} - \dfrac{\Delta E_C}{q} - V_p$ — Includes band offset term
>
> ✅ **D-mode (Normally-ON)**: $V_{off} < 0$ — Channel conducts at $V_{GS} = 0$ V
>
> ✅ **E-mode (Normally-OFF)**: $V_{off} > 0$ — Channel pinched off at $V_{GS} = 0$ V
>
> ✅ **Engineering Rule of Thumb**: To make E-mode from D-mode, **recess the gate** or **reduce channel thickness**
>
> ✅ **Pinch-off Potential**: $V_p = \dfrac{q N_D a^2}{2\varepsilon_s}$ — Always positive quantity
>
> ✅ **Unit Conversions**: $1\ \text{cm}^{-3} = 10^6\ \text{m}^{-3}$; $1\ \mu\text{m} = 10^{-6}\ \text{m}$; $1\ \text{\AA} = 10^{-10}\ \text{m}$
>
> ✅ **Compound Semiconductor Properties**:
> - GaAs: $\varepsilon_r = 13.1$, $E_g = 1.42\ \text{eV}$, $\mu_n = 8500\ \text{cm}^2/\text{V·s}$
> - GaN: $\varepsilon_r = 9.0$, $E_g = 3.4\ \text{eV}$, high breakdown field
> - InP: $\varepsilon_r = 12.5$, $E_g = 1.35\ \text{eV}$, used in optical fiber communication
>
> ✅ **Always remember**: Off voltage is **engineered**, not given — it depends on **3 tunable parameters**: $N_D$, $a$, and $V_{bi}$
>
> ✅ **Device Operation Regions**:
> - $V_{GS} > V_{off}$: **ON** (channel conducting)
> - $V_{GS} = V_{off}$: **Pinchoff** (boundary)
> - $V_{GS} < V_{off}$: **OFF** (channel depleted)
>
> ✅ **Relation to I-V Characteristics**: $I_{D,sat} = K(V_{GS} - V_{off})^2$ — Off voltage is the **vertex** of the parabolic transfer curve
>
> ✅ **High-Frequency Implication**: Small $\vert V_{off}\vert$ → higher $g_m$ → higher $f_T$ → better high-frequency performance
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: I-V Characteristics
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEEVT413 - COMPOUND SEMICONDUCTORS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** I-V Characteristics

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **I-V Characteristics** of a compound semiconductor device describe the functional relationship between the current flowing through the device and the voltage applied across its terminals. For a p-n junction diode, heterojunction, or any two-terminal compound semiconductor device, the I-V curve captures the non-linear, rectifying, and (in some cases) negative differential resistance (NDR) behavior arising from carrier transport across the depletion region, band discontinuities, and tunneling mechanisms unique to direct-bandgap materials such as **GaAs**, **InP**, **GaN**, and **SiC**.

The general functional form of the I-V characteristic is:

$$I = f(V)$$

For an ideal p-n junction diode (Shockley's ideal diode model):

$$I = I_0 \left( \exp\!\left(\frac{qV}{k_B T}\right) - 1 \right)$$

where:
- $I_0$ = **reverse saturation current** (A) — typically $10^{-15}$ A for Si, $10^{-20}$ A for GaAs
- $q$ = **elementary charge** = **$1.602 \times 10^{-19}$ C**
- $k_B$ = **Boltzmann constant** = **$1.381 \times 10^{-23}$ J/K**
- $T$ = **absolute temperature** (K)
- $V$ = **applied voltage** (V)

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The One-Way Water Valve

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy:** Imagine a water pipe with a one-way check valve. Water (current) flows easily in the forward direction when you push hard (forward bias), but the valve slams shut when you try to push water backward (reverse bias). However, if you push *really* hard from the wrong side, a tiny leak occurs (reverse saturation current $I_0$), and if you push *extremely* hard, the pipe bursts (breakdown). Compound semiconductor diodes behave similarly, but their valve "leaks" far less than Silicon and "bursts" at precise, tunable voltages — which is exactly why they are chosen for high-frequency, optoelectronic, and power applications.

### 1.3 Why I-V Characteristics Matter in Compound Semiconductors

- **Direct bandgap materials** (GaAs, InP, GaN) have a high radiative recombination efficiency, making their I-V curve intimately coupled with **light emission** (LEDs, laser diodes).
- **Heterojunction band discontinuities** ($\Delta E_C$, $\Delta E_V$) introduce **spikes and notches** in the conduction band profile that strongly modify the I-V curve.
- **Esaki tunnel diodes** (degenerate p-n junctions in heavily doped compound semiconductors) exhibit a **Negative Differential Resistance (NDR)** region — a hallmark feature not seen in ordinary Si diodes.
- **Wide bandgap semiconductors** (GaN, SiC) sustain far higher breakdown voltages and operating currents.

## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Operational Regimes of an I-V Curve

A typical compound semiconductor p-n junction diode exhibits **three distinct operational regimes** when forward-biased:

1. **Low-Injection / Recombination-Dominated Region** (small V):  
   Current is dominated by carrier recombination in the depletion region via defect/trap states (Shockley-Read-Hall recombination). The current follows:

$$I_{\text{rec}} \propto \exp\!\left(\frac{qV}{2k_B T}\right)$$

The factor of **2** in the denominator is the signature of recombination current and gives an **ideality factor $n = 2$**.

2. **Diffusion-Dominated Region** (moderate V):  
   Current flows via minority carrier diffusion across the junction:

$$I_{\text{diff}} = I_0 \left[ \exp\!\left(\frac{qV}{k_B T}\right) - 1 \right]$$

Here, ideality factor $n = 1$ (ideal diffusion-limited behavior).

3. **High-Injection / Series-Resistance-Limited Region** (large V):  
   The series resistance $R_S$ of the neutral bulk and ohmic contacts dominates, and the I-V curve becomes linear:

$$V \approx I \cdot R_S + V_{bi} \quad \text{(at high currents)}$$

### 2.2 Generalized Diode Equation with Ideality Factor

The **practical** I-V relation that KTU examiners love to test is:

$$I = I_0 \left[ \exp\!\left(\frac{qV}{n k_B T}\right) - 1 \right]$$

where $n$ is the **ideality factor** (also called the **quality factor** or **emission coefficient**):
- $n = 1$ → pure diffusion current (ideal diode)
- $n = 2$ → pure recombination current (degraded diode)
- $1 < n < 2$ → mixed mechanism (typical real device)
- $n > 2$ → heavily defective junction

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Exam Tip:** In GaAs and other III-V compound semiconductor diodes, the ideality factor is frequently observed to be close to **2.0** because of the prevalence of **deep-level EL2 defects** in the depletion region. This is a favorite KTU question!

### 2.3 Reverse Bias and Breakdown

Under reverse bias, the current saturates at $I \approx -I_0$. However, at a sufficiently large reverse voltage, **breakdown** occurs via one of two mechanisms:

- **Zener Breakdown** (band-to-band tunneling): Dominant in heavily doped narrow-junction diodes.
- **Avalanche Breakdown**: Multiplication of carriers by impact ionization in the high-field depletion region.

The empirical breakdown voltage for a one-sided abrupt junction is given by:

$$V_{BR} = \frac{\varepsilon_s E_{\text{crit}}^2}{2q N_B}$$

where $E_{\text{crit}}$ is the **critical electric field** and $N_B$ is the background doping on the lightly doped side.

| Material | $E_{\text{crit}}$ (V/cm) | Bandgap $E_g$ (eV) |
|----------|--------------------------|--------------------|
| **Si**   | $3 \times 10^5$          | 1.12               |
| **GaAs** | $4 \times 10^5$          | 1.42               |
| **GaN**  | $3 \times 10^6$          | 3.39               |
| **SiC**  | $2.5 \times 10^6$        | 3.26               |

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Insight:** GaN and SiC can sustain **5–10×** higher breakdown fields than Si, making them the materials of choice for high-power electronics (EV inverters, RF power amplifiers).

### 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| # | Formula | Description | Units |
|---|---------|-------------|-------|
| 1 | $I = I_0 \left[\exp\!\left(\frac{qV}{nk_BT}\right) - 1\right]$ | Generalized diode equation | A |
| 2 | $I_0 = qA \left(\frac{D_p n_{i}^{2}}{L_p N_D} + \frac{D_n n_{i}^{2}}{L_n N_A}\right)$ | Reverse saturation current | A |
| 3 | $V_T = \dfrac{k_B T}{q}$ | Thermal voltage ($\approx 25.85$ mV at 300 K) | V |
| 4 | $R_S = \dfrac{\rho_W}{A} + R_c$ | Series resistance (bulk + contact) | $\Omega$ |
| 5 | $V_{BR} = \dfrac{\varepsilon_s E_{\text{crit}}^{2}}{2q N_B}$ | Avalanche breakdown voltage (one-sided junction) | V |
| 6 | $I_{\text{tunnel}} \propto \exp\!\left(-\frac{E_g}{2\bar{E}}\right)$ | Tunneling current (T-shaped barrier model) | A |
| 7 | $G_{\text{NDR}} = -\dfrac{dI}{dV}\bigg\vert_{\text{NDR region}}$ | Negative differential conductance | S (Siemens) |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **No vertical bars `|x|` inside the table** — the absolute values and derivatives above have been rendered using LaTeX-safe notation (`\vert` implied by positioning within `\dfrac` or natural parentheses). When transcribing, use `\vert` for absolute values.

### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **LEDs (GaAs, AlGaAs, InGaN):** The forward-voltage knee of the I-V curve determines the operating point and color (bandgap energy).
- **Laser Diodes:** Above a threshold current $I_{\text{th}}$, the I-V curve bends sharply, indicating lasing onset.
- **HEMTs (High Electron Mobility Transistors, AlGaAs/GaAs, AlGaN/GaN):** $I_{DS}$–$V_{DS}$ curves define RF power performance.
- **Tunnel/Esaki Diodes:** The NDR region enables high-frequency oscillators (>100 GHz) using GaAs.
- **Solar Cells:** I-V curves under illumination (photocurrent shifted down) define fill factor FF and efficiency $\eta$.

## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of the Ideal Diode Equation (Shockley, 1949)

We start from the **Boltzmann quasi-equilibrium assumption** in the quasi-neutral p and n regions.

**Step 1:** Write the minority carrier concentrations at the edge of the depletion region under applied bias $V$:

$$p_n(0) = p_{n0} \exp\!\left(\frac{qV}{k_B T}\right) \qquad \text{(holes injected into n-side)}$$

$$n_p(0) = n_{p0} \exp\!\left(\frac{qV}{k_B T}\right) \qquad \text{(electrons injected into p-side)}$$

where $p_{n0} = n_i^2 / N_D$ and $n_{p0} = n_i^2 / N_A$ are the equilibrium minority carrier densities.

**Step 2:** Solve the steady-state minority carrier diffusion equation in the n-region:

$$D_p \frac{\partial^2 \delta p_n}{\partial x^2} - \frac{\delta p_n}{\tau_p} = 0$$

with boundary conditions $\delta p_n(x\to\infty) = 0$ and $\delta p_n(x=0) = p_{n0}[\exp(qV/k_BT) - 1]$.

The solution is:

$$\delta p_n(x) = p_{n0} \left[\exp\!\left(\frac{qV}{k_B T}\right) - 1\right] \exp\!\left(-\frac{x}{L_p}\right)$$

where $L_p = \sqrt{D_p \tau_p}$ is the **diffusion length** of holes in the n-region.

**Step 3:** Compute the hole diffusion current density at $x = 0$:

$$J_p = -q D_p \left.\frac{\partial \delta p_n}{\partial x}\right|_{x=0} = \frac{q D_p p_{n0}}{L_p}\left[\exp\!\left(\frac{qV}{k_B T}\right) - 1\right]$$

**Step 4:** By symmetry, the electron current density at the p-side edge:

$$J_n = \frac{q D_n n_{p0}}{L_n}\left[\exp\!\left(\frac{qV}{k_B T}\right) - 1\right]$$

**Step 5:** Total current density is the sum (the depletion-region generation current is neglected here):

$$J = J_p + J_n = q \left(\frac{D_p p_{n0}}{L_p} + \frac{D_n n_{p0}}{L_n}\right) \left[\exp\!\left(\frac{qV}{k_B T}\right) - 1\right]$$

**Step 6:** Recognize the bracketed coefficient as the **reverse saturation current density** $J_0$:

$$J_0 = q n_i^2 \left(\frac{D_p}{L_p N_D} + \frac{D_n}{L_n N_A}\right)$$

**Step 7:** Final Shockley ideal diode equation in terms of total current $I = J \cdot A$:

$$\boxed{\,I = I_0\left[\exp\!\left(\frac{qV}{k_B T}\right) - 1\right]\,}$$

### 3.2 Derivation of Reverse Saturation Current $I_0$ for a GaAs Diode

**Given:** A GaAs p-n junction diode with:
- Cross-sectional area $A = 10^{-4}$ cm²
- $N_D = 10^{16}$ cm$^{-3}$, $N_A = 10^{18}$ cm$^{-3}$ (one-sided abrupt, $n^+p$)
- $D_p = 10$ cm²/s, $L_p = 1 \mu$m
- $n_i(\text{GaAs}) = 2 \times 10^6$ cm$^{-3}$ at 300 K

**Step 1:** Since $N_A \gg N_D$, the electron-injection term $D_n n_{p0}/L_n$ is negligible. The current is dominated by holes injected into the n-side.

**Step 2:** Compute the equilibrium hole density in the n-region:

$$p_{n0} = \frac{n_i^2}{N_D} = \frac{(2\times 10^6)^2}{10^{16}} = 4 \times 10^{-4} \text{ cm}^{-3}$$

**Step 3:** Compute the diffusion length explicitly:

$$L_p = \sqrt{D_p \tau_p} = 10^{-4} \text{ cm} \implies \tau_p = \frac{L_p^2}{D_p} = \frac{10^{-8}}{10} = 10^{-9} \text{ s} = 1 \text{ ns}$$

**Step 4:** Compute the saturation current:

$$I_0 = q A \cdot \frac{D_p p_{n0}}{L_p}$$

Substitute values:

$$I_0 = (1.6 \times 10^{-19})(10^{-4}) \cdot \frac{(10)(4 \times 10^{-4})}{10^{-4}}$$

**Step 5:** Simplify the numerator of the second factor:

$$D_p p_{n0} = 10 \times 4 \times 10^{-4} = 4 \times 10^{-3} \text{ cm}^{-1}\text{s}^{-1}$$

**Step 6:** Divide by $L_p$:

$$\frac{D_p p_{n0}}{L_p} = \frac{4 \times 10^{-3}}{10^{-4}} = 40 \text{ cm}^{-2}\text{s}^{-1}$$

**Step 7:** Final multiplication:

$$I_0 = (1.6 \times 10^{-19})(10^{-4})(40) = 6.4 \times 10^{-22} \text{ A}$$

$$\boxed{\,I_0 \approx 6.4 \times 10^{-22} \text{ A}\,}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Interpretation:** This ultra-low saturation current (six orders of magnitude smaller than a comparable Si diode) is a direct consequence of the **very small intrinsic carrier concentration** of GaAs ($n_i \sim 10^6$ cm$^{-3}$ vs. Si's $10^{10}$ cm$^{-3}$). This is one of the principal reasons GaAs diodes exhibit sharper, more ideal rectifying I-V characteristics.

### 3.3 Derivation of the Turn-On (Knee) Voltage

The knee voltage $V_{\text{knee}}$ is conventionally defined as the forward bias at which the diode current reaches $I = I_{\text{test}}$ (often 1 mA or 10 mA for power diodes). Using the diode equation:

$$I_{\text{test}} = I_0 \exp\!\left(\frac{qV_{\text{knee}}}{k_B T}\right)$$

Solving for $V_{\text{knee}}$:

$$V_{\text{knee}} = \frac{n k_B T}{q} \ln\!\left(\frac{I_{\text{test}}}{I_0}\right)$$

For an ideal GaAs diode at 300 K with $n = 2$, $I_0 = 6.4 \times 10^{-22}$ A, and $I_{\text{test}} = 1$ mA:

$$V_{\text{knee}} = (2)(0.02585) \ln\!\left(\frac{10^{-3}}{6.4 \times 10^{-22}}\right)$$

**Step-by-step calculation:**

$$V_{\text{knee}} = 0.0517 \cdot \ln(1.5625 \times 10^{18})$$

$$\ln(1.5625 \times 10^{18}) = \ln(1.5625) + 18\ln(10) = 0.4463 + 41.447 = 41.893$$

$$V_{\text{knee}} = 0.0517 \times 41.893 = 2.166 \text{ V}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Experimental Match:** This $V_{\text{knee}} \approx 2.16$ V matches the observed turn-on voltage of red GaAs/AlGaAs LEDs! This is not a coincidence — the knee voltage is fundamentally linked to the **bandgap energy** through $V_{\text{knee}} \approx E_g/q$ for direct-bandgap emitters.

### 3.4 Derivation: Tunnel Diode I-V Characteristic (Esaki Diode)

For a heavily doped degenerate p-n junction (e.g., both sides $> 10^{19}$ cm$^{-3}$), the depletion region becomes very narrow ($\sim 10$ nm), enabling **quantum mechanical tunneling**.

The tunneling current density is given by the **Tsu-Esaki formula**:

$$J_T = \frac{q m^*}{18 \hbar^3} \exp\!\left(-\frac{\pi m^{*1/2} E_g^{3/2}}{2\sqrt{2}\, q \hbar F}\right) \cdot F \cdot [f(E) - f(E+qV)]$$

where:
- $F$ = electric field across junction
- $m^*$ = effective tunneling mass
- $E_g$ = bandgap
- $f(E)$ = Fermi-Dirac occupation

A simplified form sufficient for KTU analysis:

$$J_T(V) = J_p \left[ \frac{V}{V_p} \exp\!\left(1 - \frac{V}{V_p}\right) \right] - J_p \left[ \frac{(V-V_v)}{V_p} \exp\!\left(1 - \frac{V - V_v}{V_p}\right) \right]$$

This produces the **characteristic N-shaped I-V curve** with peak current $I_p$ at peak voltage $V_p$ and valley current $I_v$ at valley voltage $V_v$.

## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture: Compound Semiconductor Diode I-V Pipeline

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Input Applied Voltage V] --> B{V Bias Polarity}
    B -- Forward Bias V gt 0 --> C[Carrier Injection Across Junction]
    C --> D[Diffusion plus Recombination in Depletion Region]
    D --> E[Exponential Current Rise]
    E --> F[Series Resistance Limits at High V]
    F --> G[Output Forward Current I]
    B -- Reverse Bias V lt 0 --> H[Minority Carrier Extraction]
    H --> I[Reverse Saturation Current I sub 0]
    I --> J{Breakdown Voltage Reached?}
    J -- No --> K[Constant Reverse Leakage I sub 0]
    J -- Yes --> L[Avalanche or Zener Multiplication]
    L --> M[Sharp Reverse Current Rise]
    K --> G2[Output Reverse Current]
    M --> G2
    G --> OutputNode[Final I-V Characteristic Curve]
    G2 --> OutputNode
    OutputNode --> P[Plot I versus V on Log and Linear Scales]
    P --> Q[Extract Ideality Factor n from Slope]
    P --> R[Extract I sub 0 from y intercept]
    P --> S[Identify Knee Voltage V sub knee]
    P --> T[Identify Breakdown Voltage V sub BR]
```

### 4.2 Schematic: Shockley I-V Curve (Linear & Logarithmic)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph LinearPlot [Linear I-V Plot]
        direction LR
        L1[Reverse Leakage I sub 0] --> L2[Forward Knee near V sub knee]
        L2 --> L3[Steep Exponential Rise]
        L3 --> L4[Series Resistance Linear Region]
        L4 --> L5[Thermal Runaway Limit]
    end
    subgraph LogPlot [Semilog I-V Plot]
        direction LR
        S1[Sub-threshold Region slope q over 2kT] --> S2[Diffusion Region slope q over kT]
        S2 --> S3[Series Resistance Divergence]
    end
```

### 4.3 Schematic: Tunnel Diode I-V Curve (NDR Region)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    T1[V = 0: Zero bias point] --> T2[V increases: Tunnel current rises]
    T2 --> T3[V = V sub p: Peak current I sub p]
    T3 --> T4[NDR region: Current DECREASES with V]
    T4 --> T5[V = V sub v: Valley current I sub v]
    T5 --> T6[Excess current region]
    T6 --> T7[V greater than V sub f: Diffusion current dominates]
    style T3 fill:#ff9,stroke:#333
    style T4 fill:#fbb,stroke:#333
    style T5 fill:#bbf,stroke:#333
```

### 4.4 Heterojunction Band Diagram & I-V Behavior

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    H1[N-side Wide Bandgap Emitter e.g. AlGaAs] --> H2[Band Spike Delta E sub C at Conduction Band Edge]
    H2 --> H3[Narrow Bandgap Base e.g. GaAs]
    H3 --> H4[Band Notch Delta E sub V at Valence Band Edge]
    H4 --> H5[P-side Wide Bandgap Emitter AlGaAs]
    H5 --> H6[Conduction of Electrons Dominates over Holes]
    H6 --> H7[Asymmetric I-V: High Injection Efficiency]
    style H2 fill:#ffd,stroke:#333
    style H4 fill:#ffd,stroke:#333
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Why this matters:** The conduction-band spike $\Delta E_C$ acts as a barrier that can either block or enhance electron flow depending on doping profile. This is the foundational principle behind the **heterojunction bipolar transistor (HBT)** and **double-heterostructure laser diode**, both pivotal compound semiconductor devices.

## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

### 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

#### **Question A1. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Model]**
**Define the ideality factor of a p-n junction diode. What are its typical values for an ideal diffusion-limited current and a recombination-dominated current?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

> The ideality factor $n$ is an empirical parameter in the generalized diode equation that quantifies how closely a real diode's behavior matches the ideal Shockley model:
>
> $$I = I_0\left[\exp\!\left(\frac{qV}{nk_BT}\right) - 1\right]$$
>
> - For **diffusion-limited current** (ideal, minority carrier diffusion in quasi-neutral regions), $n = 1$.
> - For **recombination-dominated current** (carriers recombine via traps in the depletion region via Shockley-Read-Hall process), $n = 2$.
> - In real compound semiconductor diodes, $1 < n < 2$, with $n \approx 2$ common in GaAs due to EL2 deep-level defects.

---

#### **Question A2. [KTU University Exam — May 2024, Model]**
**Why is the reverse saturation current of a GaAs diode several orders of magnitude smaller than that of a Silicon diode of comparable geometry and doping?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

> The reverse saturation current is proportional to $n_i^2$:
>
> $$I_0 \propto n_i^2$$
>
> At 300 K, the intrinsic carrier concentrations are:
> - $n_i(\text{Si}) \approx 1.5 \times 10^{10}$ cm$^{-3}$ → $n_i^2 \approx 2.25 \times 10^{20}$ cm$^{-6}$
> - $n_i(\text{GaAs}) \approx 2 \times 10^{6}$ cm$^{-3}$ → $n_i^2 \approx 4 \times 10^{12}$ cm$^{-6}$
>
> The ratio is $\sim 5.6 \times 10^{7}$, meaning $I_0$ in GaAs is roughly **seven orders of magnitude smaller** than in Si. This arises from GaAs's larger bandgap ($E_g = 1.42$ eV vs. Si's 1.12 eV), which exponentially suppresses thermal carrier generation.

### 5.2 PART B — Full 14-Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice)

---

#### **Question B-A. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Adapted]**
**(a) [7 marks]** Derive the Shockley ideal diode equation starting from the minority carrier diffusion equation. State clearly all assumptions used.

**(b) [7 marks]** A GaAs p-n junction diode has the following parameters at 300 K:
- $N_A = 10^{18}$ cm$^{-3}$, $N_D = 10^{16}$ cm$^{-3}$
- $A = 10^{-4}$ cm², $D_p = 10$ cm²/s, $D_n = 50$ cm²/s
- $L_p = 1$ μm, $L_n = 5$ μm, $n_i = 2 \times 10^6$ cm$^{-3}$
- $V_{\text{applied}} = 1.2$ V forward bias

Compute the forward current, the ideality factor (assume $n = 1.5$), and comment on the dominant conduction mechanism.

---

##### **Solution to (a):**

**Assumptions (1 mark):**
1. Abrupt junction with depletion approximation
2. Boltzmann quasi-equilibrium at depletion edges
3. Low-injection level
4. Minority carrier diffusion in quasi-neutral regions
5. Neglect generation/recombination in depletion region
6. No series resistance or surface leakage

**Diffusion equation (1 mark):**

$$D_p \frac{\partial^2 \delta p_n}{\partial x^2} - \frac{\delta p_n}{\tau_p} = 0$$

**Boundary conditions (1 mark):**
- $x \to \infty$: $\delta p_n \to 0$
- $x = 0$: $\delta p_n = p_{n0}[\exp(qV/k_BT) - 1]$

**Solution (1 mark):**

$$\delta p_n(x) = p_{n0}\left[\exp\!\left(\frac{qV}{k_BT}\right) - 1\right]\exp\!\left(-\frac{x}{L_p}\right)$$

**Current density via Fick's law (1 mark):**

$$J_p = -q D_p \frac{\partial \delta p_n}{\partial x}\bigg\vert_{x=0} = \frac{qD_p p_{n0}}{L_p}\left[\exp\!\left(\frac{qV}{k_BT}\right) - 1\right]$$

**Symmetric electron term + summation (1 mark):**

$$J_n = \frac{qD_n n_{p0}}{L_n}\left[\exp\!\left(\frac{qV}{k_BT}\right) - 1\right]$$

$$J = J_p + J_n = q\left(\frac{D_p p_{n0}}{L_p} + \frac{D_n n_{p0}}{L_n}\right)\left[\exp\!\left(\frac{qV}{k_BT}\right) - 1\right]$$

**Final form (1 mark):**

$$\boxed{\,I = I_0\left[\exp\!\left(\frac{qV}{k_BT}\right) - 1\right]\,}$$

where $I_0 = qAn_i^2\left(\dfrac{D_p}{L_p N_D} + \dfrac{D_n}{L_n N_A}\right)$.

---

##### **Solution to (b):**

**Step 1: Compute $p_{n0}$ and $n_{p0}$ (1 mark):**

$$p_{n0} = \frac{n_i^2}{N_D} = \frac{(2\times 10^6)^2}{10^{16}} = 4 \times 10^{-4} \text{ cm}^{-3}$$

$$n_{p0} = \frac{n_i^2}{N_A} = \frac{4 \times 10^{12}}{10^{18}} = 4 \times 10^{-6} \text{ cm}^{-3}$$

**Step 2: Compute the two contributions to $I_0$ (2 marks):**

Hole contribution:

$$I_{0,p} = qA \frac{D_p p_{n0}}{L_p} = (1.6\times 10^{-19})(10^{-4}) \cdot \frac{(10)(4\times 10^{-4})}{10^{-4}}$$

$$= (1.6\times 10^{-23})(40) = 6.4 \times 10^{-22} \text{ A}$$

Electron contribution:

$$I_{0,n} = qA \frac{D_n n_{p0}}{L_n} = (1.6\times 10^{-19})(10^{-4}) \cdot \frac{(50)(4\times 10^{-6})}{5\times 10^{-4}}$$

$$= (1.6\times 10^{-23})(400) = 6.4 \times 10^{-21} \text{ A}$$

**Step 3: Total saturation current (1 mark):**

$$I_0 = I_{0,p} + I_{0,n} = 6.4 \times 10^{-22} + 6.4 \times 10^{-21} = 7.04 \times 10^{-21} \text{ A}$$

**Step 4: Forward current with $n = 1.5$ (2 marks):**

Thermal voltage: $V_T = 25.85$ mV

Exponent:

$$\frac{qV}{nk_BT} = \frac{1.2}{1.5 \times 0.02585} = \frac{1.2}{0.03878} = 30.94$$

Exponential value: $e^{30.94} \approx 2.83 \times 10^{13}$

Forward current:

$$I = I_0 (e^{30.94} - 1) \approx 7.04 \times 10^{-21} \times 2.83 \times 10^{13}$$

$$\boxed{\,I \approx 1.99 \times 10^{-7} \text{ A} = 0.199 \text{ μA}\,}$$

**Step 5: Comment on dominant mechanism (1 mark):**

> The electron contribution to $I_0$ is **10× larger** than the hole contribution, so electrons injected from the heavily doped p-side into the lightly doped n-side are the dominant carriers. However, since $n = 1.5$ is closer to 1 than to 2, **diffusion current dominates** over recombination, but a significant recombination component is also present (typical of GaAs with EL2 defects).

---

#### **Question B-B. [KTU University Exam — May 2024, Model Alternative]**
**(a) [7 marks]** With the aid of a neat I-V characteristic, explain the operation of an **Esaki tunnel diode** made from a heavily doped compound semiconductor such as GaAs. Clearly identify the peak current, valley current, and the negative differential resistance (NDR) region.

**(b) [7 marks]** Compare the I-V characteristics of Si, GaAs, and GaN p-n junction diodes. Discuss the effect of bandgap, critical electric field, and intrinsic carrier concentration on the turn-on voltage, breakdown voltage, and reverse leakage.

---

##### **Solution to (a):**

**Construction and doping (2 marks):**

> An Esaki tunnel diode is a p-n junction where **both p and n regions are degenerately doped** ($N_A, N_D > 10^{19}$ cm$^{-3}$). This makes the depletion region extremely narrow (typically 5–15 nm), enabling **quantum-mechanical band-to-band tunneling** of carriers. GaAs is the prototypical material because of its small effective tunneling mass and direct bandgap.

**Three regions of the I-V curve (3 marks):**

| Region | Voltage Range | Physical Mechanism |
|--------|---------------|---------------------|
| **Tunneling rise** | $0 < V < V_p$ | Electrons tunnel from filled n-side states to empty p-side states. Current rises rapidly. |
| **NDR region** | $V_p < V < V_v$ | As $V$ increases, filled states on n-side align with **forbidden gap** on p-side. Tunneling probability **decreases** sharply → current drops. |
| **Diffusion rise** | $V > V_v$ | Normal diffusion current takes over; I-V resumes exponential growth. |

**Key parameters (2 marks):**

- **Peak current $I_p$** at **peak voltage $V_p \approx 50$–$100$ mV** (low!)
- **Valley current $I_v$** at **valley voltage $V_v \approx 400$–$600$ mV**
- **Peak-to-valley current ratio (PVCR):** $\text{PVCR} = I_p / I_v$ — figure of merit. For GaAs, PVCR $\approx 15$–$20$ at 300 K.
- **Negative differential resistance:** $R_{\text{NDR}} = -\Delta V / \Delta I < 0$ in the NDR region. Used in high-frequency oscillators up to $\sim 100$ GHz.

**Diagram note (for board work):** Draw an N-shaped curve with $V$ on x-axis and $I$ on y-axis. Mark $(V_p, I_p)$, $(V_v, I_v)$, and shade the NDR region.

---

##### **Solution to (b):**

**Comparison table (4 marks):**

| Property | Si | GaAs | GaN |
|----------|-----|------|-----|
| $E_g$ (eV) | 1.12 | 1.42 | 3.39 |
| $n_i$ (cm$^{-3}$) | $1.5 \times 10^{10}$ | $2 \times 10^{6}$ | $< 10^{-10}$ |
| $E_{\text{crit}}$ (V/cm) | $3 \times 10^{5}$ | $4 \times 10^{5}$ | $3 \times 10^{6}$ |
| Turn-on $V_{\text{knee}}$ (V) | $\sim 0.7$ | $\sim 1.2$ | $\sim 3.0$ |
| $V_{BR}$ (typical, V) | $\sim 50$ | $\sim 100$ | $\sim 1000$ |
| Reverse leakage | High | Low | Extremely low |
| Direct/Indirect gap | Indirect | Direct | Direct |

**Analysis (3 marks):**

1. **Turn-on voltage scales with bandgap:** $V_{\text{knee}} \approx E_g/q$. This is because the diode conducts significantly only when the applied forward bias compensates the built-in potential $V_{bi} \approx E_g/q$. Hence GaN (largest $E_g$) has the highest knee voltage.

2. **Breakdown voltage scales with critical field:** $V_{BR} \propto E_{\text{crit}}^2$. GaN's $E_{\text{crit}}$ is $10\times$ Si's, so its $V_{BR}$ can be $\sim 100\times$ higher at comparable doping — the foundation of GaN power electronics.

3. **Reverse leakage scales with $n_i$:** Since $I_0 \propto n_i^2$ and $n_i$ depends exponentially on $E_g$, the leakage in wide-bandgap GaN is negligibly small — ideal for high-temperature and radiation-hard applications.

4. **Direct vs. indirect bandgap:** Si (indirect) is a poor light emitter. GaAs and GaN (direct) are the materials of choice for LEDs, laser diodes, and solar cells.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. **Do NOT** confuse the ideality factor $n$ with the electron density $n$ — use $\eta$ or $m$ in handwritten scripts.
> 2. **Do NOT** write $V_T = kT/q$ without specifying $T = 300$ K if you then plug in $V_T = 0.0259$ V. Always state the temperature.
> 3. **Do NOT** forget the "-1" in the diode equation — at $V = 0$, $I$ must equal 0. Examiners will deduct **1 mark** for this omission.
> 4. **Do NOT** confuse saturation current direction: in the convention $I = I_0[\exp(qV/nkT) - 1]$, **forward bias gives positive $I$**, and reverse bias saturates at $-I_0$.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- ✅ **Shockley ideal diode equation:** $I = I_0[\exp(qV/nk_BT) - 1]$ — the foundation of all I-V analysis.
- ✅ **Thermal voltage** $V_T = k_BT/q = 25.85$ mV at **300 K**.
- ✅ **Reverse saturation current** $I_0 \propto n_i^2$, so **wider bandgap** → **lower leakage** (huge advantage for GaN/SiC).
- ✅ **Ideality factor** $n$: equals 1 for diffusion, 2 for recombination, 1–2 in real devices.
- ✅ **Series resistance** $R_S$ flattens the high-current region; **shunt resistance** $R_{sh}$ adds leakage in the reverse region.
- ✅ **Breakdown voltage** $V_{BR} \propto E_{\text{crit}}^2$ — GaN and SiC are **5–10× better** than Si.
- ✅ **Tunnel/Esaki diodes** (degenerate GaAs) show **NDR** in the I-V curve, with key parameters $I_p$, $V_p$, $I_v$, $V_v$, and PVCR.
- ✅ **Heterojunction I-V** is asymmetric due to band discontinuities $\Delta E_C$ and $\Delta E_V$.
- ✅ **Knee voltage** $V_{\text{knee}} \approx E_g/q$ for direct-bandgap emitters — explains why red GaAs LEDs switch on near **1.8–2.2 V**.
- ✅ **Light emission** in compound semiconductors is directly tied to the I-V curve above the knee — integral to LED and laser diode operation.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Photodetectors: Basic Principle of Operation, Photodetector Structures, Photodetector Materials, Photodetector Parameters: Responsivity and Efficiency, Photodetector Gain
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEEVT413 - COMPOUND SEMICONDUCTORS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Photodetectors: Basic Principle of Operation, Photodetector Structures, Photodetector Materials, Photodetector Parameters: Responsivity and Efficiency, Photodetector Gain

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition of a Photodetector

A **photodetector** is an optoelectronic device that converts incident optical radiation (photons) into a measurable electrical signal (current or voltage). In the context of **compound semiconductors** (III-V and II-VI alloys), photodetectors exploit the engineered bandgap of ternary and quaternary materials such as **InGaAs**, **InSb**, **HgCdTe**, and **AlGaAs** to achieve selective detection across the ultraviolet, visible, and infrared spectrum.

In KTU 2024 Scheme terminology, a photodetector is formally defined as a **reverse-biased semiconductor p-n junction (or related heterostructure) in which incident photons with energy $h\nu \geq E_g$ generate electron-hole pairs via band-to-band absorption, producing a photocurrent proportional to the incident optical power.**

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight (Module 3):** Photodetectors are the *receiving* counterparts of LEDs and laser diodes. While emitters convert electrical energy to photons, photodetectors perform the inverse energy conversion governed by the principle of *reciprocity* in semiconductor optoelectronics.

## 1.2 Intuitive Analogy: The Solar Rain Bucket

Imagine a **rainwater harvesting bucket with a calibrated measuring tube**:

- **Photons** behave like raindrops falling onto the bucket.
- The **semiconductor absorber** is the bucket itself — it has a *threshold* (its bandgap $E_g$ ) that determines which "raindrops" (photons) it can collect. Photons with energy below the threshold simply pass through without being captured.
- The **photocurrent** is the measured volume of water collected per second.
- **Quantum efficiency** is the fraction of raindrops that *actually* fall into the bucket versus those that splash out or miss.
- **Gain** is like placing a magnifying funnel — for every captured raindrop, multiple water molecules are channeled through the measuring tube.

> [!NOTE]
> **Core Operating Principle:** Absorption → Generation of EHPs → Separation by built-in/reverse-bias field → Collection as photocurrent.

## 1.3 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics (Highlighted)

- **Planck's constant:** $h = 6.626 \times 10^{-34}$ J·s
- **Speed of light:** $c = 3 \times 10^{8}$ m/s
- **Electronic charge:** $q = 1.602 \times 10^{-19}$ C
- **Cut-off wavelength:** $\lambda_c = \dfrac{1.24}{E_g(\text{eV})}$ μm
- **Standard responsivity range:** 0.1 – 1.0 A/W for Si, up to 100+ A/W for APDs

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Basic Principle of Operation — Step-by-Step Physical Mechanism

The operation of any semiconductor photodetector follows a universal **three-stage sequence**:

### Stage 1 — Photon Absorption
An incoming photon of energy $h\nu$ interacts with the semiconductor lattice. If $h\nu \geq E_g$, the photon is absorbed via **band-to-band transition**, promoting a valence-band electron to the conduction band and leaving behind a hole.

$$\text{Photon} \longrightarrow e^-_{(CB)} + h^+_{(VB)}$$

If $h\nu < E_g$, the photon passes through (sub-bandgap transmission), making the device *blind* to long wavelengths.

### Stage 2 — Carrier Separation (Sweep-Out)
The photogenerated electron-hole pair (EHP) is separated by the **internal electric field** of either:
- The depletion region built-in potential of a p-n junction, OR
- An externally applied reverse-bias field.

This separation prevents recombination and forces carriers to drift toward the neutral n and p regions.

### Stage 3 — Collection and Photocurrent Generation
Electrons collected at the n-side and holes at the p-side produce a measurable current in the external circuit. The **primary photocurrent** is:

$$I_{ph} = q \cdot \eta \cdot \frac{P_{opt}}{h\nu}$$

where $P_{opt}$ is the incident optical power and $\eta$ is the **external quantum efficiency**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why reverse bias?** Reverse bias widens the depletion region, increasing the absorption volume, and reduces the junction capacitance, improving high-speed response.

## 2.2 Photodetector Structures

### 2.2.1 p-n Junction Photodiode
The simplest structure. The depletion region acts as the absorption zone. Limited by:
- Narrow depletion width
- Slow diffusion of carriers generated outside depletion region
- Low quantum efficiency at long wavelengths

### 2.2.2 p-i-n Photodiode (PIN)
The industry-standard structure. An **intrinsic (i-) layer** is sandwiched between p and n regions.

**Advantages over simple p-n:**
- Wide depletion region → high absorption volume
- Low capacitance → high bandwidth
- Fast response dominated by *drift* not *diffusion*
- High quantum efficiency across a broad wavelength range

### 2.2.3 Avalanche Photodiode (APD)
Operated at high reverse bias near the breakdown voltage. Photogenerated carriers undergo **impact ionization**, creating secondary EHPs in a multiplicative chain.

**Key feature:** Provides **internal gain** ($M$ = 10 to 100+), making it ideal for detecting very weak optical signals in long-haul fiber-optic communication.

### 2.2.4 Metal-Semiconductor-Metal (MSM) Photodetector
Two interdigitated Schottky contacts on a semiconductor surface. Very low capacitance and ultra-fast response — used in high-speed OEICs (optoelectronic integrated circuits).

### 2.2.5 Heterojunction Photodetector
Uses two different bandgap semiconductors (e.g., InGaAs/InP) to create a **window layer** effect — the wide-bandgap material is transparent to the wavelengths absorbed in the narrow-bandgap active layer, eliminating surface recombination.

## 2.3 Photodetector Materials (Compound Semiconductors)

| Material | Bandgap (eV) | Cut-off λ (μm) | Primary Spectral Region |
|----------|-------------|----------------|------------------------|
| **Silicon (Si)** | 1.12 | 1.11 | Visible & Near-IR |
| **Germanium (Ge)** | 0.67 | 1.85 | Near-IR |
| **GaAs** | 1.42 | 0.87 | Visible & Near-IR |
| **InP** | 1.35 | 0.92 | Near-IR |
| **InGaAs** (lattice-matched to InP) | 0.75 | 1.65 | Telecom (1.3, 1.55 μm) |
| **InSb** | 0.17 | 7.30 | Mid-IR (3–5 μm) |
| **HgCdTe (MCT)** | 0.1 – 1.5 | Tunable | Mid- to Far-IR (thermal imaging) |
| **AlGaAs** | 1.42 – 2.16 | Tunable | Visible (red to green) |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Focus:** Compound semiconductors (III-V alloys) dominate the *1.3 μm* and *1.55 μm* fiber-optic communication windows where Si detectors fail due to their 1.11 μm cut-off.

## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| Symbol / Formula | Definition / Expression | Unit / Condition |
|------------------|-------------------------|------------------|
| $E_g = \dfrac{hc}{\lambda_c}$ | Photon-energy to bandgap relation | $E_g$ in eV, $\lambda_c$ in μm |
| $\lambda_c = \dfrac{1.24}{E_g(eV)}$ | Cut-off wavelength | μm |
| $\eta_{ext} = \dfrac{\text{electrons collected/sec}}{\text{photons incident/sec}}$ | External Quantum Efficiency | dimensionless, 0 ≤ η ≤ 1 |
| $\eta_{int} = \dfrac{\text{electrons collected/sec}}{\text{photons absorbed/sec}}$ | Internal Quantum Efficiency | dimensionless |
| $\eta_{ext} = (1 - R)(1 - e^{-\alpha d})$ | Quantum efficiency with reflection R, absorption α, thickness d | d = absorption length |
| $\mathcal{R} = \dfrac{I_{ph}}{P_{opt}}$ | **Responsivity** | A/W |
| $\mathcal{R} = \dfrac{q\eta\lambda}{hc} = \dfrac{\eta\lambda}{1.24}$ | Responsivity in terms of η and λ (μm) | A/W |
| $\eta = \dfrac{1.24\,\mathcal{R}}{\lambda}$ | Quantum efficiency from responsivity | dimensionless |
| $I_{ph} = q\eta \Phi_{ph}$ | Photocurrent from photon flux $\Phi_{ph}$ | A |
| $I_{ph} = M \cdot I_{ph0}$ | APD multiplied current | A; $M$ = multiplication factor |
| $M = \dfrac{1}{1 - (V/V_{BR})^n}$ | Empirical APD gain (Miller's formula) | dimensionless; $V_{BR}$ = breakdown voltage |
| $F(M) = M^x$ | Excess noise factor (0 < x < 1) | depends on carrier ionization ratio |
| $\tau_{tr} = \dfrac{d}{v_{sat}}$ | Transit time across depletion width d | seconds; $v_{sat} \approx 10^7$ cm/s |
| $BW = \dfrac{1}{2\pi\tau_{tr}}$ | Bandwidth–transit time limit | Hz |
| $NEP = \dfrac{\sqrt{\langle i_n^2 \rangle}}{\mathcal{R}\sqrt{\Delta f}}$ | Noise Equivalent Power | W/√Hz |
| $D^* = \dfrac{\sqrt{A \cdot \Delta f}}{NEP}$ | Specific Detectivity | cm·Hz$^{1/2}$/W (Jones) |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Exam Tip:** The relation $\mathcal{R} = \dfrac{q\eta\lambda}{hc}$ is the single most-tested formula on photodetectors. Memorize the constant $\dfrac{q}{hc} = \dfrac{1}{1.24}$ μm/eV to instantly solve numericals.

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Optical fiber communication (1.55 μm)** → InGaAs PIN and APD receivers
- **Thermal imaging & military IR** → HgCdTe and InSb focal-plane arrays
- **Barcode scanners & CD/DVD pickup** → Si p-n and PIN photodiodes
- **Solar cells** → large-area Si and GaAs photodiodes (zero-bias photovoltaic mode)
- **Quantum cryptography & single-photon counting** → Geiger-mode APDs (SPADs)
- **Astronomy & spectroscopy** → cooled InGaAs and MCT arrays with ultra-low NEP

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation I — Photocurrent from Photon Flux

### Statement
Derive the expression for the primary photocurrent generated by a photodetector when an optical power $P_{opt}$ at wavelength $\lambda$ is incident on it.

### Given
- Incident optical power: $P_{opt}$ (watts)
- Wavelength of incident light: $\lambda$ (meters)
- External quantum efficiency: $\eta_{ext}$
- Electronic charge: $q$

### Derivation

**Step 1 — Energy of a single photon**

The energy carried by one photon of wavelength $\lambda$ is given by the Planck–Einstein relation:

$$E_{ph} = h\nu = \dfrac{hc}{\lambda}$$

**Step 2 — Number of incident photons per second**

If $P_{opt}$ is the total incident optical power, the number of photons striking the detector per second is the total energy divided by the energy per photon:

$$\Phi_{inc} = \dfrac{P_{opt}}{E_{ph}} = \dfrac{P_{opt} \cdot \lambda}{hc}$$

This $\Phi_{inc}$ is the **incident photon flux** (photons/second).

**Step 3 — Number of electrons collected per second**

By definition, the external quantum efficiency $\eta_{ext}$ is the ratio of the number of electrons collected (contributing to photocurrent) to the number of photons incident. Hence the number of collected electrons per second is:

$$n_{e} = \eta_{ext} \cdot \Phi_{inc} = \eta_{ext} \cdot \dfrac{P_{opt} \lambda}{hc}$$

**Step 4 — Convert to current**

Each collected electron carries charge $q$. By the definition of current as charge per unit time, the primary photocurrent is:

$$I_{ph} = q \cdot n_{e} = q \cdot \eta_{ext} \cdot \dfrac{P_{opt} \lambda}{hc}$$

**Step 5 — Final boxed result**

$$\boxed{\,I_{ph} = \dfrac{q\eta_{ext} \lambda}{hc} \cdot P_{opt}\,}$$

> [!NOTE]
> This derivation establishes the **linear relationship** between optical input power and electrical output current — the foundation of all quantitative photodetector analysis.

## 3.2 Derivation II — Responsivity from Quantum Efficiency

### Statement
Show that responsivity $\mathcal{R}$ and quantum efficiency $\eta$ are related by $\mathcal{R} = \dfrac{q\eta\lambda}{hc}$.

### Derivation

**Step 1 — Definition of Responsivity**

Responsivity is defined as the photocurrent produced per unit incident optical power:

$$\mathcal{R} \equiv \dfrac{I_{ph}}{P_{opt}} \quad [\text{A/W}]$$

**Step 2 — Substitute photocurrent expression**

Insert the result from Derivation I:

$$\mathcal{R} = \dfrac{1}{P_{opt}} \cdot \left( \dfrac{q\eta\lambda}{hc} \cdot P_{opt} \right)$$

**Step 3 — Cancel $P_{opt}$**

$$\mathcal{R} = \dfrac{q\eta\lambda}{hc}$$

**Step 4 — Numerical constant evaluation**

Substituting $h = 6.626 \times 10^{-34}$ J·s, $c = 3 \times 10^8$ m/s, and $q = 1.602 \times 10^{-19}$ C:

$$\dfrac{q}{hc} = \dfrac{1.602 \times 10^{-19}}{(6.626 \times 10^{-34})(3 \times 10^8)} = \dfrac{1.602 \times 10^{-19}}{1.9878 \times 10^{-25}}$$

$$\dfrac{q}{hc} = 8.06 \times 10^{5} \text{ (in SI)}$$

**Step 5 — Convert to the practical form using λ in μm**

When $\lambda$ is expressed in micrometers (μm), and using $hc = 1.24$ eV·μm at the bandgap energy scale, the formula simplifies to the KTU board-friendly form:

$$\boxed{\,\mathcal{R}(\text{A/W}) = \dfrac{\eta \cdot \lambda(\mu\text{m})}{1.24}\,}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Physical Insight:** Responsivity *increases linearly with wavelength* for a fixed quantum efficiency. This is why long-wavelength detectors (e.g., InGaAs at 1.55 μm) have intrinsically higher responsivity than silicon at 0.85 μm, even at identical η.

## 3.3 Derivation III — APD Photocurrent with Multiplication Gain

### Statement
Derive the expression for the multiplied photocurrent in an Avalanche Photodiode.

### Derivation

**Step 1 — Primary photocurrent (un-multiplied)**

In the absorption region of an APD, each absorbed photon creates a primary electron-hole pair, giving:

$$I_{ph0} = q \eta \Phi_{ph}$$

This is the *primary* photocurrent that would flow in the absence of avalanche multiplication.

**Step 2 — Impact ionization in the high-field multiplication region**

In the multiplication (avalanche) region, a primary carrier on average travels a distance equal to one **ionization length** before generating a new EHP through impact ionization. Define $\alpha_e$ and $\alpha_h$ as the electron and hole ionization coefficients per unit length.

**Step 3 — Average multiplication factor**

The mean number of secondary EHPs generated per primary carrier is the **multiplication factor** $M$. Empirically (Miller's formula), for an applied reverse bias $V$ approaching breakdown $V_{BR}$:

$$M = \dfrac{1}{1 - \left( \dfrac{V}{V_{BR}} \right)^n}$$

where $n$ is an empirical index (typically 2 to 6 depending on the semiconductor).

**Step 4 — Total multiplied photocurrent**

Each primary electron is effectively multiplied by $M$ before collection. Hence:

$$\boxed{\,I_{ph,APD} = M \cdot q \eta \Phi_{ph} = M \cdot I_{ph0}\,}$$

**Step 5 — APD Responsivity**

Combining with Derivation II:

$$\mathcal{R}_{APD} = M \cdot \mathcal{R}_{PIN} = M \cdot \dfrac{q\eta\lambda}{hc}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **Trade-off:** Increasing $M$ boosts signal but also amplifies noise via the excess noise factor $F(M) = kM + (1-k)(2 - 1/M)$, where $k = \alpha_h/\alpha_e$ is the *ionization ratio*. Low-$k$ materials (e.g., InP, Si) yield low-noise APDs.

## 3.4 Derivation IV — Quantum Efficiency from Absorption Coefficient

### Statement
Show that the internal quantum efficiency of an absorption layer of thickness $d$ and absorption coefficient $\alpha$ is $\eta_{int} = 1 - e^{-\alpha d}$.

### Derivation

**Step 1 — Beer–Lambert absorption law**

If monochromatic light of intensity $I_0$ enters the semiconductor at $x = 0$, the intensity at depth $x$ follows:

$$I(x) = I_0 e^{-\alpha x}$$

**Step 2 — Power absorbed in thickness $dx$**

The optical power absorbed in an infinitesimal slice $dx$ at depth $x$ is:

$$dP_{abs}(x) = -\dfrac{dI}{dx} dx = \alpha I_0 e^{-\alpha x} dx$$

**Step 3 — Total absorbed power over the full thickness d**

Integrate across the entire absorption layer:

$$P_{abs} = \int_{0}^{d} \alpha I_0 e^{-\alpha x} dx = I_0 \left[ -e^{-\alpha x} \right]_0^d = I_0 \left( 1 - e^{-\alpha d} \right)$$

**Step 4 — Internal quantum efficiency**

Internal QE is the ratio of absorbed photons to incident photons (since each absorbed photon of energy $\geq E_g$ generates one EHP):

$$\boxed{\,\eta_{int} = \dfrac{P_{abs}}{P_{inc}} = 1 - e^{-\alpha d}\,}$$

**Step 5 — External quantum efficiency with surface reflection R**

If a fraction $R$ of incident photons is reflected at the surface, only $(1 - R)$ enters the absorber:

$$\eta_{ext} = (1 - R)\left( 1 - e^{-\alpha d} \right)$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Design rule:** To achieve $\eta_{int} > 90\%$, the absorption layer must be thicker than $\dfrac{2.3}{\alpha}$. For InGaAs at 1.55 μm, $\alpha \approx 7000$ cm$^{-1}$, so $d \geq 3.3$ μm suffices.

## 3.5 Analytical Comparison Matrix of Photodetector Structures

| Parameter | p-n Photodiode | PIN Photodiode | APD | MSM |
|-----------|----------------|----------------|-----|-----|
| Depletion width | Narrow | Wide (controlled) | Wide | Surface |
| Speed | Slow (diffusion-limited) | Fast (drift) | Fast | Very fast |
| Gain (M) | 1 | 1 | 10 – 100 | 1 |
| Responsivity (A/W) | 0.3 – 0.5 | 0.5 – 0.9 | 5 – 50 | 0.1 – 0.4 |
| Bias voltage | Low reverse | Moderate reverse | Near $V_{BR}$ | Low |
| Noise | Low | Low | Higher (shot + excess) | Low |
| Best wavelength range | Visible | Visible to NIR | NIR (1.55 μm telecom) | UV to NIR |
| Typical application | Solar cells, light meters | Optical communication | Long-haul fiber links | High-speed OEICs |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Block Diagram — Universal Photodetector Operating Chain

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Optical Source] --> B[Lens / Coupling Optics]
    B --> C[Anti-Reflection Coating]
    C --> D[Absorption Region: p-i-n or APD]
    D --> E{Energy Check: hnu greater than Eg}
    E -- Yes --> F[Band-to-Band Absorption]
    E -- No --> G[Photon Transmitted: No Response]
    F --> H[Electron Hole Pair Generation]
    H --> I[Reverse Bias Field]
    I --> J[Carrier Drift to Contacts]
    J --> K[Photocurrent Iph]
    K --> L[Transimpedance Amplifier]
    L --> M[Output Voltage Signal]
```

## 4.2 Mermaid Schematic — PIN Photodiode Cross-Section

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph PIN[p-i-n Photodiode Cross Section]
        P[Top p-plus Contact Layer]
        I[Intrinsic i-Region: Active Absorber]
        N[Bottom n-plus Contact Layer]
        P --> I --> N
    end
    Light[Incident Photons] --> P
    P -.transparent window.-> I
    I --> Drift[Drift of EHPs under reverse bias]
    Drift --> Output[Photocurrent to External Circuit]
```

## 4.3 Mermaid Schematic — Avalanche Photodiode Multiplication Cascade

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A0[Photon Enters Window Layer] --> A1[Absorbed in InGaAs Active Region]
    A1 --> A2[Primary EHP Generated]
    A2 --> A3[Carrier Drifts into InP Multiplication Region]
    A3 --> A4{High Reverse Bias near VBR}
    A4 --> A5[Impact Ionization Event 1]
    A5 --> A6[Secondary EHPs]
    A6 --> A7[Impact Ionization Event 2]
    A7 --> A8[Additional EHPs]
    A8 --> A9[Multiplication M equals 10 to 100]
    A9 --> A10[Multiplied Photocurrent M times Iph0]
```

## 4.4 Mermaid Decision Tree — Choosing a Photodetector

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Q1{Wavelength of Interest} --> Q1a[Visible 0.4 to 0.7 um]
    Q1 --> Q1b[NIR 0.7 to 1.1 um]
    Q1 --> Q1c[Fiber Telecom 1.3 to 1.55 um]
    Q1 --> Q1d[Mid IR 3 to 12 um]
    Q1a --> R1[Si p-n or PIN]
    Q1b --> R2[Si or Ge PIN]
    Q1c --> R3[InGaAs PIN or APD on InP]
    Q1d --> R4[InSb or HgCdTe cooled detector]
    Q3{Weak Signal} --> S1[Use APD for internal gain]
    Q3 --> S2[Use PIN with low noise TIA]
```

## 4.5 Mermaid Block Architecture — Photodetector Receiver System

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph RX[Optical Receiver Front End]
        PD[Photodetector: PIN or APD]
        TIA[Transimpedance Amplifier]
        LA[Limiting Amplifier]
        CDR[Clock and Data Recovery]
    end
    OpticalIn[Fiber Coupled Optical Signal] --> PD
    PD --> TIA
    TIA --> LA
    LA --> CDR
    CDR --> DigitalOut[Recovered Digital Data]
    Vbias[DC Reverse Bias Supply] -.-> PD
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Mermaid Note for KTU Students:** The diagrams above render correctly in any Markdown viewer that supports Mermaid (GitHub, VS Code, Obsidian). The schematics map the *functional flow* of signal processing — the actual physical cross-sections are typically drawn using standard device-physics software in laboratory sessions.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 [KTU University Exam — May 2024]
**Define quantum efficiency of a photodetector. Distinguish between internal and external quantum efficiency.**

**Model Answer:**

The **quantum efficiency (η)** of a photodetector is defined as the probability that an incident photon will generate a collected electron-hole pair that contributes to the photocurrent. It is a dimensionless quantity between 0 and 1 (or 0% and 100%).

- **Internal Quantum Efficiency (η_int):** Defined as the ratio of the number of electron-hole pairs generated (and collected) to the number of *absorbed* photons in the semiconductor. It excludes surface reflection losses.

$$\eta_{int} = \dfrac{\text{electrons collected}}{\text{photons absorbed}} = 1 - e^{-\alpha d}$$

- **External Quantum Efficiency (η_ext):** Defined as the ratio of the number of electrons collected to the number of *incident* photons on the detector surface. It includes surface reflection losses.

$$\eta_{ext} = (1 - R)(1 - e^{-\alpha d})$$

The external efficiency is always less than or equal to the internal efficiency, with the difference being the surface reflection loss.

---

### Question 2 [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]
**With a neat diagram, explain the principle of operation of a PIN photodiode.**

**Model Answer:**

A **PIN photodiode** consists of a heavily doped p-region, a wide intrinsic (i-) region, and a heavily doped n-region. The intrinsic region is undoped (or very lightly doped) and serves as the primary photon absorption zone.

**Operating Principle:**

1. **Photon absorption:** Photons with energy $h\nu \geq E_g$ enter through the thin p-layer and are absorbed in the wide intrinsic region, generating electron-hole pairs (EHPs).

2. **Reverse bias application:** A reverse bias is applied across the device, which fully depletes the i-region and creates a strong uniform electric field across it.

3. **Carrier separation and drift:** The photogenerated carriers are separated by the high field and *drift* at saturation velocity across the i-region, producing the photocurrent.

4. **Collection:** Carriers are collected at the p and n contacts, producing an external current $I_{ph} = q\eta P_{opt}\lambda / (hc)$.

**Key advantages of the intrinsic layer:**
- Wide absorption volume → high quantum efficiency
- Drift (not diffusion) transport → fast response
- Low junction capacitance → high bandwidth

```
|---p+---||---------- i ----------||---n+---|
              (intrinisic absorber)
              <-- strong E field -->
              <-- EHPs drift here -->
```

---

## PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

### Question A (Choice 1) [KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024]

**(a)** Derive the relationship between responsivity and quantum efficiency of a photodetector. A silicon PIN photodiode has a quantum efficiency of 80% at a wavelength of 900 nm. Calculate the responsivity of the device.

**(b)** What is an Avalanche Photodiode (APD)? Explain the concept of multiplication gain. An InGaAs APD operates at a reverse bias such that its multiplication factor is M = 30. If the primary photocurrent is 50 nA, calculate the multiplied photocurrent and the responsivity at 1.55 μm (assume η = 70%).

---

#### Model Solution to Question A(a) [7 Marks]

**Step 1 — Define responsivity** (1 mark)
Responsivity $\mathcal{R}$ is the ratio of photocurrent to incident optical power:

$$\mathcal{R} = \dfrac{I_{ph}}{P_{opt}} \quad [\text{A/W}]$$

**Step 2 — Express photon flux** (1 mark)
Number of incident photons per second:

$$\Phi_{ph} = \dfrac{P_{opt}}{h\nu} = \dfrac{P_{opt}\lambda}{hc}$$

**Step 3 — Define quantum efficiency** (1 mark)

$$\eta = \dfrac{\text{electrons collected per second}}{\text{photons incident per second}} = \dfrac{I_{ph}/q}{P_{opt}\lambda/(hc)}$$

**Step 4 — Rearrange for $I_{ph}$** (1 mark)

$$I_{ph} = \eta \cdot q \cdot \dfrac{P_{opt}\lambda}{hc} = \dfrac{q\eta\lambda}{hc} P_{opt}$$

**Step 5 — Substitute to get responsivity formula** (1 mark)

$$\mathcal{R} = \dfrac{I_{ph}}{P_{opt}} = \dfrac{q\eta\lambda}{hc}$$

**Step 6 — Substitute numerical values** (1 mark)
Given: $\eta = 0.80$, $\lambda = 900$ nm $= 0.9$ μm

$$\mathcal{R} = \dfrac{(1.602 \times 10^{-19})(0.80)(0.9 \times 10^{-6})}{(6.626 \times 10^{-34})(3 \times 10^8)}$$

$$\mathcal{R} = \dfrac{1.153 \times 10^{-25}}{1.988 \times 10^{-25}} = 0.580 \text{ A/W}$$

**Step 7 — Final boxed answer** (1 mark)

$$\boxed{\mathcal{R} = 0.58 \text{ A/W}}$$

---

#### Model Solution to Question A(b) [7 Marks]

**Step 1 — Define APD** (2 marks)
An **Avalanche Photodiode (APD)** is a reverse-biased p-n junction photodetector designed to operate at a high electric field near the breakdown voltage. In the high-field *multiplication region*, photogenerated carriers gain enough kinetic energy to create *secondary* electron-hole pairs via **impact ionization**. This produces an internal current gain $M$, which amplifies the primary photocurrent.

**Step 2 — Multiplication gain definition** (1 mark)
The multiplication factor $M$ is defined as:

$$M = \dfrac{I_{ph,APD}}{I_{ph,primary}} = \dfrac{I_{ph,APD}}{I_{ph0}}$$

**Step 3 — Compute multiplied photocurrent** (1 mark)
Given $I_{ph0} = 50$ nA and $M = 30$:

$$I_{ph,APD} = M \times I_{ph0} = 30 \times 50 \text{ nA} = 1500 \text{ nA} = 1.5 \text{ μA}$$

**Step 4 — Formula for APD responsivity** (1 mark)

$$\mathcal{R}_{APD} = M \cdot \dfrac{q\eta\lambda}{hc}$$

**Step 5 — Substitute values** (1 mark)
Given: $\eta = 0.70$, $\lambda = 1.55$ μm, $M = 30$:

$$\mathcal{R}_{APD} = 30 \times \dfrac{(1.602 \times 10^{-19})(0.70)(1.55 \times 10^{-6})}{(6.626 \times 10^{-34})(3 \times 10^8)}$$

$$\mathcal{R}_{APD} = 30 \times \dfrac{1.738 \times 10^{-25}}{1.988 \times 10^{-25}}$$

$$\mathcal{R}_{APD} = 30 \times 0.874 = 26.2 \text{ A/W}$$

**Step 6 — Final boxed answer** (1 mark)

$$\boxed{I_{ph,APD} = 1.5 \text{ μA} \quad ; \quad \mathcal{R}_{APD} \approx 26.2 \text{ A/W}}$$

---

### Question B (Choice 2) [KTU University Exam — July 2023]

**(a)** With the help of a block diagram, explain the working principle of a photodetector. Discuss the various structures used in photodetectors.

**(b)** Define responsivity. A GaAs photodetector (E_g = 1.42 eV) is illuminated by 1 mW of optical power at 870 nm. If the measured photocurrent is 0.4 mA, calculate (i) the quantum efficiency, (ii) the responsivity, and (iii) the cut-off wavelength of the detector.

---

#### Model Solution to Question B(a) [7 Marks]

**Step 1 — Working principle** (3 marks)

A photodetector converts incident light into electrical current via the **photovoltaic** or **photoconductive** effect. The working principle involves:

1. **Absorption:** Photons of energy $h\nu \geq E_g$ incident on the depletion region are absorbed, generating EHPs.
2. **Separation:** The junction field (built-in or enhanced by reverse bias) separates electrons and holes before they recombine.
3. **Collection:** Carriers drift to the neutral regions and are collected as photocurrent in the external circuit.

**Block diagram:**

```
Photons → Semiconductor Absorber → EHP Generation → Field Region → Carrier Separation → External Circuit → Photocurrent Output
```

**Step 2 — Photodetector structures** (4 marks)

- **p-n Photodiode:** Simple junction; narrow depletion region; low efficiency.
- **PIN Photodiode:** Intrinsic layer increases absorption volume; faster response.
- **Avalanche Photodiode (APD):** High-field multiplication region provides internal gain $M$.
- **MSM Photodetector:** Two Schottky contacts; very high speed, low capacitance.
- **Heterojunction Photodetector:** Bandgap-engineered (e.g., InGaAs/InP) for selective wavelength detection and reduced surface recombination.

---

#### Model Solution to Question B(b) [7 Marks]

**Step 1 — Define responsivity** (1 mark)

Responsivity is the photocurrent generated per unit incident optical power:

$$\mathcal{R} = \dfrac{I_{ph}}{P_{opt}} \quad [\text{A/W}]$$

**Step 2 — Compute responsivity** (1 mark)
Given $I_{ph} = 0.4$ mA $= 4 \times 10^{-4}$ A and $P_{opt} = 1$ mW $= 10^{-3}$ W:

$$\mathcal{R} = \dfrac{4 \times 10^{-4}}{10^{-3}} = 0.4 \text{ A/W}$$

**Step 3 — Use the responsivity-quantum efficiency relation** (1 mark)

$$\eta = \dfrac{\mathcal{R} \cdot hc}{q\lambda} = \dfrac{1.24 \cdot \mathcal{R}}{\lambda(\mu\text{m})}$$

**Step 4 — Compute quantum efficiency** (1 mark)
Given $\lambda = 870$ nm $= 0.87$ μm:

$$\eta = \dfrac{1.24 \times 0.4}{0.87} = \dfrac{0.496}{0.87} = 0.570$$

$$\eta = 57.0\%$$

**Step 5 — Cut-off wavelength formula** (1 mark)
Cut-off wavelength (the longest wavelength that can be detected):

$$\lambda_c = \dfrac{1.24}{E_g(\text{eV})}$$

**Step 6 — Compute cut-off wavelength** (1 mark)
Given $E_g = 1.42$ eV:

$$\lambda_c = \dfrac{1.24}{1.42} = 0.873 \text{ μm} = 873 \text{ nm}$$

**Step 7 — Final boxed answers** (1 mark)

$$\boxed{\mathcal{R} = 0.4 \text{ A/W} \quad ; \quad \eta = 57\% \quad ; \quad \lambda_c = 873 \text{ nm}}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Unit mismatch in λ:** Always convert wavelength to *micrometers* when using the constant 1.24; using nanometers gives a factor-of-1000 error.
> 2. **Forgetting to convert mW → W:** Photocurrent must be in *amperes* and power in *watts*; mixing mA with mW is a common 1-mark error.
> 3. **PIN vs APD confusion:** Students often write $I_{ph} = q\eta\lambda P_{opt}/hc$ for an APD without multiplying by $M$. Always check if the device is specified as APD.
> 4. **Quantum efficiency bound:** η must lie in [0, 1]. If a calculation yields η > 1, the input data is inconsistent.
> 5. **Confusing $\eta_{int}$ and $\eta_{ext}$:** In problems without reflection data, the formula gives *internal* QE; mention this distinction explicitly in the answer.

---

## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist — Photodetectors (KTU 2024)**

- **Photodetector:** Device that converts optical signal → electrical signal via band-to-band absorption ($h\nu \geq E_g$).
- **Operating principle (3 steps):** Photon absorption → EHP generation → field-driven separation → photocurrent.
- **Cut-off wavelength:** $\lambda_c = 1.24 / E_g(eV)$ in μm.
- **Primary photocurrent:** $I_{ph} = q\eta\lambda P_{opt} / (hc)$.
- **Responsivity:** $\mathcal{R} = I_{ph}/P_{opt} = q\eta\lambda / (hc) = \eta\lambda/1.24$ A/W (with λ in μm).
- **Internal QE:** $\eta_{int} = 1 - e^{-\alpha d}$.
- **External QE:** $\eta_{ext} = (1 - R)(1 - e^{-\alpha d})$.
- **PIN advantage:** Wide intrinsic region → drift transport → high speed + high QE.
- **APD gain:** $I_{ph,APD} = M \cdot I_{ph0}$; Miller formula $M = 1/(1 - (V/V_{BR})^n)$.
- **Excess noise factor:** $F(M) = kM + (1 - k)(2 - 1/M)$, with $k = \alpha_h / \alpha_e$.
- **Transit time:** $\tau_{tr} = d/v_{sat}$; bandwidth limit $BW = 1/(2\pi\tau_{tr})$.
- **Key materials:** Si (visible), Ge (NIR), InGaAs/InP (1.55 μm telecom), InSb (3–5 μm), HgCdTe (tunable IR).
- **Heterojunction benefit:** Window layer suppresses surface recombination.
- **MSM:** Two Schottky contacts; very low capacitance; sub-ns response.
- **APD trade-off:** Higher M → more signal *and* more noise; low-k materials (Si, InP) preferred.
- **NEP & D*:** $NEP = \sqrt{\langle i_n^2 \rangle} / (\mathcal{R}\sqrt{\Delta f})$; $D^* = \sqrt{A\Delta f}/NEP$ (Jones).
- **Numerical constant to memorize:** $q/(hc) = 1/1.24$ μm/eV — use this for instant calculation of $\mathcal{R}$ or $\eta$.
- **Common KTU 14-mark structure:** (a) Derive $\mathcal{R}$–$\eta$ relation + numerical [7 marks], (b) APD/Material concept + numerical [7 marks].
- **Examiner favorite trap:** Confusing $I_{ph}$ with total current; forgetting APD gain factor; unit-mismatch between mW/mA and W/A.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Photodiodes: p-n Photodiodes - Device Structure, Operation, Analysis
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PEEVT413 - COMPOUND SEMICONDUCTORS > - **Module:** Module 3 > - **Topic:** Photodiodes: p-n Photodiodes - Device Structure, Operation, Analysis # SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition A **p-n Photodiode** is a reverse-biased p-n junction semiconductor device specifically engineered and packaged to convert incident optical (photon) energy into a measurable electric current through the **internal photoelectric effect**. In compound semiconductor systems (such as **InGaAs**, **InP**, **GaAs**, **HgCdTe**, and **AlGaAs**), the p-n photodiode is fabricated as a depletion-region optimized heterostructure in which absorbed photons with energy $h\nu \geq E_g$ generate electron-hole pairs that are rapidly separated by the built-in electric field of the space-charge region, producing a photocurrent proportional to the incident optical power. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Syllabus Anchor:** Photodiodes belong to the family of *optoelectronic transducers* in Compound Semiconductors. They form the foundational building block for optical receivers, fiber-optic communication links, LIDAR systems, and infrared imaging arrays. ## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Water Wheel in a Tunnel" Model Imagine a sloped tunnel (the **depletion region**) built into a hillside. Raindrops (the **photons**) fall onto the tunnel entrance. Each raindrop that hits a paddle wheel inside the tunnel (an **absorbed photon generating an e-h pair**) causes the wheel to turn and push water (the **photocurrent**) down the slope, where it is collected at the bottom (the **external circuit**). The faster the rain (brighter light, higher $P_{opt}$), the more water flows. Importantly, a one-way valve at the tunnel exit (the **reverse bias**) ensures water only flows out — never back in — making the device *rectifying* and *unidirectional*. The **bias battery** acts like a pump that widens the tunnel (expands the depletion region) to catch more raindrops. > [!NOTE] > **Key Insight for Students:** A photodiode is fundamentally a *current source* (not a voltage source) controlled by light. Its current direction *opposes* the forward diode current, which is why incident light appears as a downward shift in the I-V curve. ## 1.3 Essential Physical Constants & Material Parameters | Symbol | Quantity | Standard Value (SI) | Compound Semiconductor Context | | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | | $q$ | Elementary charge | $\mathbf{1.602 \times 10^{-19} \ C}$ | Universal | | $h$ | Planck's constant | $\mathbf{6.626 \times 10^{-34} \ J \cdot s}$ | Universal | | $c$ | Speed of light in vacuum | $\mathbf{2.998 \times 10^8 \ m/s}$ | Universal | | $k$ | Boltzmann's constant | $\mathbf{1.381 \times 10^{-23} \ J/K}$ | Universal | | $T$ | Absolute temperature | $\mathbf{300 \ K}$ (room temperature, default) | Default KTU assumption | | $V_T$ | Thermal voltage $kT/q$ | $\mathbf{25.85 \ mV}$ at 300 K | Default KTU assumption | | $\epsilon_0$ | Vacuum permittivity | $\mathbf{8.854 \times 10^{-12} \ F/m}$ | Universal | | $\epsilon_r$ | Relative permittivity | 12.9 (Si), 13.1 (GaAs), 12.4 (InP) | Material dependent | | $E_g$ | Bandgap energy | 0.67 eV (Ge), 1.12 eV (Si), 1.42 eV (GaAs), 0.75 eV (InP) | Material dependent | > [!IMPORTANT] > **Compound Semiconductor Advantage:** Unlike Si (cutoff $\approx 1.1 \ \mu m$), compound alloys like InGaAs extend detection well into the near-infrared ($1.0$–$1.7 \ \mu m$), which is the **telecommunications C-band** (1530–1565 nm) and L-band (1565–1625 nm). This is the dominant reason KTU's Compound Semiconductors course dedicates a full module to photodiodes. # SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Operational Logic — Step-by-Step Photocurrent Generation Mechanism The p-n photodiode operates under **reverse bias** ($V < 0$). The bias expands the depletion width, reduces junction capacitance, and shortens carrier transit time. The complete operational chain is: - **Step 1 — Photon Incidence:** Optical radiation of power $P_{opt}$ and wavelength $\lambda$ strikes the semiconductor surface through an anti-reflection coated optical window. - **Step 2 — Photon Absorption:** A photon with energy $h\nu = hc/\lambda \geq E_g$ is absorbed in the semiconductor, promoting a valence-band electron to the conduction band. This is the **fundamental absorption process** and is governed by the absorption coefficient $\alpha(\lambda)$. - **Step 3 — EHP Generation:** Each absorbed photon creates **one electron-hole pair (EHP)**. The number of photons per second incident on the device is $\Phi = P_{opt} / (h\nu)$. - **Step 4 — Carrier Collection:** Photogenerated carriers *within* or *within one diffusion length* of the depletion region are swept apart by the built-in/reverse-bias field at the saturation drift velocity $v_{sat}$. Electrons go to the n-side, holes to the p-side. - **Step 5 — Photocurrent Formation:** The drift current of photogenerated carriers in the external circuit constitutes the **primary photocurrent** $I_{ph}$, which flows from cathode to anode (i.e., *out* of the anode terminal under reverse bias). - **Step 6 — Quantum Yield:** Only a fraction $\eta$ (the **external quantum efficiency**, $0 \leq \eta \leq 1$) of incident photons ultimately contribute to collected electrons. Therefore, the rate of collected electrons is $\eta \Phi$. - **Step 7 — Terminal I-V Relation:** The total diode current is the dark diode current *minus* the photocurrent: $$I_{total} = I_0 \left( \exp\left( \frac{qV}{kT} \right) - 1 \right) - I_{ph}$$ > [!NOTE] > **Why Reverse Bias?** Forward bias injects large dark current that drowns the weak photocurrent. Reverse bias (a) widens the depletion region (more absorption volume), (b) minimizes capacitance (faster response), and (c) ensures linear, low-noise operation. ## 2.2 Critical Performance Metrics for KTU Problems | Metric | Symbol | Definition | Typical KTU Equation | | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | | Quantum Efficiency | $\eta$ | Collected electrons per incident photon | $\eta = \dfrac{I_{ph}/q}{P_{opt}/(h\nu)}$ | | Responsivity | $\mathcal{R}$ | Photocurrent per unit optical power | $\mathcal{R} = \dfrac{I_{ph}}{P_{opt}} = \dfrac{q\eta\lambda}{hc}$ | | Cutoff Wavelength | $\lambda_c$ | Maximum wavelength that can be detected | $\lambda_c = \dfrac{hc}{E_g} = \dfrac{1.24}{E_g(eV)} \ \mu m$ | | Dark Current | $I_{dark}$ | Reverse leakage with no illumination | $I_0 \left( \exp(qV/kT) - 1 \right)$ | | Junction Capacitance | $C_j$ | Depletion region capacitance | $C_j = \dfrac{\epsilon_s A}{W}$ | | Depletion Width | $W$ | Reverse-bias controlled width | $W = \sqrt{\dfrac{2\epsilon_s(V_{bi}+V_R)}{q}\left(\dfrac{1}{N_A}+\dfrac{1}{N_D}\right)}$ | | Response Time | $t_r$ | Transit time of carriers | $t_r \approx \dfrac{W}{v_{sat}} + R_L C_j$ | | Noise-Equivalent Power | NEP | Optical power giving SNR = 1 in 1 Hz BW | $\text{NEP} = \dfrac{\sqrt{\langle i_n^2 \rangle}}{\mathcal{R}}$ | | Detectivity | $D^*$ | Normalized SNR figure of merit | $D^* = \dfrac{\sqrt{A \cdot \Delta f}}{\text{NEP}}$ | > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Examiner Pattern:** In numerical problems, the relation $\mathcal{R} = q\eta\lambda / (hc)$ is the *most tested* formula. You will almost always be asked to compute $\mathcal{R}$ given $\eta$ and $\lambda$, or solve for $\eta$ given measured $I_{ph}$ and $P_{opt}$. ## 2.3 Engineering Utility — Why This Matters - **Fiber-Optic Communication:** InGaAs p-i-n photodiodes in optical receivers convert 1550 nm light pulses back into electrical data at $> 10 \ Gb/s$. - **Solar Cells:** A p-n photodiode operated at $V = 0$ (photovoltaic mode) is the working principle of every solar cell, including space-grade GaAs and CuInGaSe$_2$ cells. - **LiDAR & Ranging:** Si and InGaAs avalanche photodiodes (APDs) detect pulsed laser returns for autonomous vehicles. - **Medical Imaging:** Compound-semiconductor photodetectors form the active elements in X-ray flat-panel imagers and pulse oximeters. - **Astronomy:** HgCdTe infrared photodiodes (operating at cryogenic temperatures) detect faint infrared sources in space telescopes. > [!NOTE] > **Real-World Engineering Trade-off:** Increasing $\eta$ by making the absorption region thicker *also* increases $C_j$ and transit time, *reducing* bandwidth. This $\eta$–bandwidth trade-off is the central design problem in high-speed photodiode engineering. # SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Photocurrent $I_{ph}$ from Quantum Efficiency **Given:** Optical power $P_{opt}$ is incident on the photodiode at wavelength $\lambda$.
