A fraction $\eta$ of the incident photons is absorbed and contributes collected electrons. **Step 1 — Energy of a single photon:** The energy of one photon is given by the Planck–Einstein relation. $$E_{photon} = h\nu = \frac{hc}{\lambda}$$ **Step 2 — Rate of incident photons (photon flux):** The number of photons striking the device per second is the total optical power divided by the energy per photon. $$\Phi = \frac{P_{opt}}{E_{photon}} = \frac{P_{opt} \lambda}{hc}$$ **Step 3 — Rate of collected electrons:** By definition, the external quantum efficiency $\eta$ is the ratio of collected electrons to incident photons. Therefore, the rate of electrons contributing to current is: $$\dot{N}_e = \eta \Phi = \frac{\eta P_{opt} \lambda}{hc}$$ **Step 4 — Convert electron rate to current:** Current is charge per unit time. Each electron carries charge $q$, so: $$I_{ph} = q \dot{N}_e = \frac{q\eta\lambda P_{opt}}{hc}$$ > [!IMPORTANT] > **Final Result — KTU High-Yield Equation:** $\boxed{I_{ph} = \dfrac{q\eta\lambda}{hc} P_{opt}}$. This is the **master equation** of photodiode analysis. Every responsivity problem reduces to this expression. --- ## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Spectral Responsivity $\mathcal{R}(\lambda)$ **Definition:** Spectral responsivity is the photocurrent produced *per unit incident optical power* at a given wavelength. $$\mathcal{R}(\lambda) \equiv \frac{I_{ph}}{P_{opt}}$$ **Step 1 — Substitute the expression for $I_{ph}$:** Using the master equation derived in §3.1: $$\mathcal{R}(\lambda) = \frac{I_{ph}}{P_{opt}} = \frac{1}{P_{opt}} \cdot \frac{q\eta\lambda P_{opt}}{hc}$$ **Step 2 — Cancel $P_{opt}$ from numerator and denominator:** The optical power cancels out, leaving a wavelength-dependent material/device figure of merit. $$\mathcal{R}(\lambda) = \frac{q\eta\lambda}{hc}$$ **Step 3 — Introduce photon energy for an alternative form:** Recognising that $h\nu = hc/\lambda$, we may equivalently write: $$\mathcal{R}(\lambda) = \frac{q\eta}{h\nu} = \frac{\eta \ q}{h\nu}$$ > [!IMPORTANT] > **Final Result — KTU High-Yield Equation:** $\boxed{\mathcal{R}(\lambda) = \dfrac{q\eta\lambda}{hc}}$. Note that $\mathcal{R}$ *increases linearly* with $\lambda$ (until $\lambda_c$ is reached), meaning long-wavelength detectors are inherently *more sensitive per watt* than short-wavelength ones. The units of $\mathcal{R}$ are **A/W** (Amperes per Watt). --- ## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Cutoff Wavelength $\lambda_c$ from Bandgap **Physical Condition:** A photon is absorbed only if its energy meets or exceeds the semiconductor bandgap. This is the **fundamental absorption edge**. $$h\nu_{min} = E_g \quad \Rightarrow \quad \frac{hc}{\lambda_{max}} = E_g$$ **Step 1 — Solve for the maximum detectable wavelength $\lambda_c$:** $$\lambda_c = \frac{hc}{E_g}$$ **Step 2 — Substitute the numerical constants in convenient units:** Using $hc = 1.986 \times 10^{-25} \ J \cdot m = 1.240 \ eV \cdot \mu m$ gives a *much* more practical form for KTU problems. $$ \lambda_c = \frac{1.240 \ eV \cdot \mu m}{E_g \ [eV]} \ \mu m $$ > [!IMPORTANT] > **Final Result — KTU High-Yield Equation:** $\boxed{\lambda_c(\mu m) = \dfrac{1.24}{E_g(eV)}}$. Photons with $\lambda > \lambda_c$ are *transparent* to the material and pass through without absorption (no photocurrent). --- ## 3.4 Derivation 4 — Depletion Width and Junction Capacitance **Starting Point — Poisson's Equation in the depletion region:** For an abrupt p-n junction with doping $N_A$ (acceptors) and $N_D$ (donors), Poisson's equation reduces to: $$\frac{d^2V}{dx^2} = -\frac{\rho(x)}{\epsilon_s} = -\frac{q}{\epsilon_s}\left(N_D - N_A + p - n\right)$$ In the depletion region, mobile carriers are negligible, leaving only ionized dopants. Integrating across the p-side width $x_p$ and n-side width $x_n$ with boundary conditions (zero field at the edges), and applying total charge neutrality $N_A x_p = N_D x_n$, gives the well-known depletion width: $$W = x_p + x_n = \sqrt{\frac{2\epsilon_s (V_{bi} + V_R)}{q} \left( \frac{1}{N_A} + \frac{1}{N_D} \right)}$$ **Step 1 — Derive the junction capacitance:** A reverse-biased p-n junction behaves as a parallel-plate capacitor with the depletion region as its dielectric. The capacitance is: $$C_j = \frac{\epsilon_s A}{W} = A \sqrt{\frac{q \epsilon_s}{2(V_{bi} + V_R)} \cdot \frac{N_A N_D}{N_A + N_D}}$$ **Step 2 — Implication for response time:** The RC-limited bandwidth of the photodiode in a transimpedance circuit with load $R_L$ is: $$f_{3dB} = \frac{1}{2\pi R_L C_j}$$ For high-speed applications, $C_j$ must be minimized, which requires *low* doping or *small* area, but this *reduces* absorption volume and thus $\eta$. This is the central engineering trade-off. > [!NOTE] > **Engineering Resolution:** The p-i-n photodiode inserts an *intrinsic* (undoped) layer between p and n, decoupling absorption (large $W$) from capacitance (still small because $N_A, N_D \to 0$ at the i-region boundaries). This is the dominant high-speed photodiode architecture in modern telecom receivers. --- ## 3.5 Worked Numerical Example — KTU Board Pattern **Problem:** A GaAs p-n photodiode ($\eta = 0.85$, $E_g = 1.42 \ eV$) is illuminated with $P_{opt} = 10 \ \mu W$ at $\lambda = 870 \ nm$. Compute (a) the cutoff wavelength, (b) the responsivity, and (c) the photocurrent. **Solution:** **Part (a) — Cutoff wavelength:** $$ \lambda_c = \frac{1.24}{E_g} = \frac{1.24}{1.42} = 0.873 \ \mu m = 873 \ nm $$ Since $\lambda = 870 \ nm < 873 \ nm$, the photon *is* absorbed. ✓ **Part (b) — Responsivity:** $$ \mathcal{R} = \frac{q\eta\lambda}{hc} = \frac{(1.602 \times 10^{-19})(0.85)(870 \times 10^{-9})}{(6.626 \times 10^{-34})(2.998 \times 10^{8})} $$ Compute the denominator: $$ (6.626 \times 10^{-34})(2.998 \times 10^{8}) = 1.9865 \times 10^{-25} \ J \cdot m $$ Compute the numerator: $$ (1.602 \times 10^{-19})(0.85)(870 \times 10^{-9}) = 1.1847 \times 10^{-25} \ J \cdot m $$ Therefore: $$ \mathcal{R} = \frac{1.1847 \times 10^{-25}}{1.9865 \times 10^{-25}} = 0.5963 \ A/W \approx 0.596 \ A/W $$ **Part (c) — Photocurrent:** $$ I_{ph} = \mathcal{R} \cdot P_{opt} = (0.5963 \ A/W)(10 \times 10^{-6} \ W) = 5.963 \ \mu A $$ > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Numerical Tip:** The number $q/(hc) \approx 0.8065 \ \mu m^{-1} \cdot W^{-1} \cdot A$ is a useful "KTU-ready" constant. Memorize $\mathcal{R}(\lambda) = 0.8065 \cdot \eta \cdot \lambda_{[\mu m]} \ A/W$ for fast board-exam arithmetic. --- ## 3.6 Total Terminal Current — Superposition Principle The complete I-V characteristic of an illuminated p-n photodiode is the **dark diode current** *minus* the **photocurrent** (because $I_{ph}$ flows opposite to forward current): $$ I = I_0 \left[ \exp\left( \frac{qV}{kT} \right) - 1 \right] - I_{ph} $$ In the **fourth quadrant** ($V > 0, I < 0$), the device delivers power to an external load — this is the **photovoltaic / solar-cell mode**. In the **third quadrant** ($V < 0, I > 0$ under reverse bias), the device acts as a **linear photodetector** with current proportional to $P_{opt}$ — this is the **photoconductive mode** used in optical communication. > [!NOTE] > **Why the Minus Sign?** Photogenerated carriers are *collected* by the field, producing a current that flows *out* of the anode — exactly opposite to the conventional forward diode current direction. Hence we subtract $I_{ph}$ from the dark I-V curve, producing the characteristic "downward shift" seen in textbook I-V plots. # SECTION 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Mermaid Block Diagram — Photodiode Functional Architecture The following Mermaid block renders the **Block-Level Functional Architecture** of a p-n photodiode system, mapping how optical input is transduced into an electrical output signal. This is the recommended schematic for KTU board answers where drawing a cross-section is impractical. ```mermaid flowchart TD A1["OPTICAL INPUT
Photon Flux Popt at wavelength lambda"] --> B1["ANTIREFLECTION
COATING LAYER"] B1 --> C1["P PLUS EMITTER REGION
Heavily Doped p plus
Thickness tp small"] C1 --> D1["DEPLETION REGION W
Intrinsic field zone
Width W Vbi plus VR power half"] D1 --> E1["N SUBSTRATE REGION
Lightly Doped n minus
Thickness tn large"] E1 --> F1["OHMIC BACK CONTACT
Metal n contact"] C1 --> G1["OHMIC TOP CONTACT
Metal p contact"] F1 --> H1["EXTERNAL CIRCUIT
Load RL and Bias VR"] G1 --> H1 H1 --> I1["PHOTOCURRENT Iph
q eta lambda Popt over hc"] I1 --> J1["TRANSIMPEDENCE
AMPLIFIER TIA"] J1 --> K1["OUTPUT VOLTAGE SIGNAL
Vout proportional to Popt"] D1 --> L1["REVERSE BIAS VR
Negative polarity"] L1 --> D1 ``` ## 4.2 Mermaid Sequence Diagram — Photocurrent Generation Chain The following block shows the **chronological process** of photon-to-electron conversion inside the device. Use this to answer KTU "Explain the operation" or "Describe the working principle" questions. ```mermaid flowchart LR S1["STEP 1
Photon h nu hits
semiconductor surface"] --> S2["STEP 2
Absorption if h nu greater than Eg"] S2 --> S3["STEP 3
Electron hole pair EHP
generated per photon"] S3 --> S4["STEP 4
EHP created in or near
depletion region W"] S4 --> S5["STEP 5
Built in field sweeps
electron to n side"] S4 --> S6["STEP 6
Built in field sweeps
hole to p side"] S5 --> S7["STEP 7
Charge collection at
ohmic contacts"] S6 --> S7 S7 --> S8["STEP 8
Photocurrent Iph in
external circuit"] S8 --> S9["STEP 9
Iph equals q eta lambda
Popt over hc"] ``` ## 4.3 I-V Characteristics (Textual Schematic Description) Since Mermaid cannot render continuous I-V curves, describe the plot as follows for KTU board answers: - **X-axis:** Reverse bias voltage $V_R$ (negative to the right) - **Y-axis:** Diode current $I$ (positive upward) - **Curve 1 (Dark):** $I_0 [\exp(qV/kT) - 1]$ — exponential rise in forward bias, saturation to $-I_0$ in reverse. - **Curve 2 (Illuminated):** Curve 1 shifted *downward* by $I_{ph}$ — a family of parallel curves for increasing $P_{opt}$. - **Fourth-quadrant intersection:** Open-circuit voltage $V_{oc}$ at $I = 0$ (used in solar cells). - **Fourth-quadrant y-intercept:** Short-circuit current $I_{sc} = I_{ph}$ at $V = 0$. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Drawing Tip:** Always label $V_{oc}$, $I_{sc}$, the dark curve, the illuminated curve, and indicate the direction of increasing $P_{opt}$ with an upward arrow. This is a guaranteed 3-mark question in Part A. # SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) > **Q1.** `[KTU University Exam — May 2024]` Define the term **responsivity** of a photodiode. State its SI unit and the wavelength at which responsivity becomes maximum for a given semiconductor. **Model Answer:** Responsivity $\mathcal{R}$ of a photodiode is defined as the ratio of the generated photocurrent $I_{ph}$ to the incident optical power $P_{opt}$ at a given wavelength. $$ \mathcal{R} = \frac{I_{ph}}{P_{opt}} = \frac{q\eta\lambda}{hc} $$ The SI unit of responsivity is **A/W** (Ampere per Watt). For a given semiconductor, $\mathcal{R}$ increases linearly with $\lambda$ up to the cutoff wavelength $\lambda_c = hc/E_g$, and drops sharply to zero for $\lambda > \lambda_c$. Therefore, $\mathcal{R}$ is maximum just below $\lambda_c$. **[Marks Distribution: Definition — 1, Formula — 1, SI unit and explanation — 1]** --- > **Q2.** `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]` What is the **cutoff wavelength** of a photodiode? For a GaAs photodiode with $E_g = 1.42 \ eV$, compute the cutoff wavelength. **Model Answer:** The cutoff wavelength $\lambda_c$ is the *maximum* wavelength of incident light that can be absorbed by the semiconductor to generate electron-hole pairs. It is determined by the bandgap energy $E_g$ through the condition $h\nu_{min} = E_g$: $$ \lambda_c = \frac{hc}{E_g} = \frac{1.24}{E_g(eV)} \ \mu m $$ For GaAs ($E_g = 1.42$ eV): $$ \lambda_c = \frac{1.24}{1.42} = 0.873 \ \mu m = 873 \ nm $$ Photons with $\lambda > 873 \ nm$ pass through GaAs without being absorbed. **[Marks Distribution: Definition — 1, Formula — 1, Numerical substitution — 1]** --- ## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, Module Internal Choice) > **Q3A.** `[KTU University Exam — Jul 2024]` With a neat sketch, describe the **device structure and operating principle** of a p-n junction photodiode. Derive the expression for the **photocurrent** in terms of incident optical power and quantum efficiency. **Model Solution:** **Part (a) — Device Structure (7 Marks):** A p-n photodiode is fabricated by forming a p-n junction near the surface of a semiconductor wafer. The standard structure consists of: 1. **Top p$^+$ layer** (heavily doped, thickness $\sim 0.2$–$0.5 \ \mu m$) — allows light to enter with minimum absorption. 2. **Depletion region** (width $W$, controlled by reverse bias $V_R$ and doping) — primary absorption and carrier collection zone. 3. **n substrate** (lightly doped, thickness $\sim 100$–$300 \ \mu m$) — provides mechanical support and ohmic contact. 4. **Anti-reflection coating** (e.g., Si$_3$N$_4$ or Ta$_2$O$_5$) on top to maximize light transmission into the semiconductor. 5. **Ohmic contacts** — top annular contact (allows light entry) and bottom contact. 6. **Reverse-bias supply** $V_R$ connected with p-side negative and n-side positive to widen the depletion region. *(A clearly labelled cross-sectional diagram with annotations of all six features is required for full marks.)* **Operating Principle:** - Light enters through the optical window and anti-reflection coating. - Photons with $h\nu \geq E_g$ are absorbed, generating electron-hole pairs in the depletion region. - The strong electric field in the depletion region (built-in + reverse bias) immediately separates the carriers — electrons drift to the n-side, holes to the p-side. - This drift current constitutes the photocurrent $I_{ph}$, which is proportional to the incident optical power $P_{opt}$. - The device is operated in reverse bias (photoconductive mode) for linear, high-speed detection. **Part (b) — Derivation of Photocurrent (7 Marks):** *(Follow the exact derivation chain from §3.1 of these notes.)* Step 1: Energy per photon $E = hc/\lambda$. Step 2: Photon flux $\Phi = P_{opt}\lambda/(hc)$. Step 3: Collected electrons per second $\dot{N}_e = \eta\Phi = \eta P_{opt}\lambda/(hc)$. Step 4: Photocurrent $I_{ph} = q\dot{N}_e = q\eta\lambda P_{opt}/(hc)$. **Final Result:** $$ \boxed{I_{ph} = \frac{q\eta\lambda}{hc} P_{opt}} $$ > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall (Part B):** Students often forget to include the *reverse-bias condition* in their device-structure description. Without stating $V_R < 0$ and explaining its role (depletion widening, low capacitance), the examiner deducts 2 marks. Also, in the derivation, do *not* skip the "rate of incident photons" intermediate step — it is the key conceptual step examiners look for. --- > **Q3B (Alternative Choice for Q3A).** `[KTU University Exam — Jul 2024]` Discuss the **I-V characteristics** of a p-n photodiode under illumination. Derive expressions for (i) **spectral responsivity** $\mathcal{R}(\lambda)$, and (ii) **cutoff wavelength** $\lambda_c$. An InGaAs photodiode has $E_g = 0.75 \ eV$ and $\eta = 0.9$. Find $\lambda_c$ and $\mathcal{R}$ at $\lambda = 1550 \ nm$. **Model Solution:** **Part (a) — I-V Characteristics under Illumination (7 Marks):** The total terminal current of an illuminated p-n junction is the dark diode current minus the photocurrent (which flows opposite to forward current direction): $$ I = I_0 \left[ \exp\left( \frac{qV}{kT} \right) - 1 \right] - I_{ph} $$ Plot features to describe: - **Dark curve** ($I_{ph} = 0$): exponential forward, reverse saturation at $-I_0$. - **Illuminated curve**: parallel downward shift by $I_{ph}$ — straight line in the third quadrant (reverse-bias / photoconductive region). - **Fourth quadrant intersection points**: - Open-circuit voltage $V_{oc}$ at $I = 0$: $V_{oc} = (kT/q) \ln(I_{ph}/I_0 + 1)$ - Short-circuit current $I_{sc} = I_{ph}$ at $V = 0$. - Family of curves at increasing $P_{opt}$: each curve shifts progressively further into the fourth quadrant. **Part (b) — Derivations and Numerical Computation (7 Marks):** **(i) Spectral responsivity derivation** (follow §3.2): $$ \mathcal{R}(\lambda) = \frac{I_{ph}}{P_{opt}} = \frac{q\eta\lambda}{hc} $$ **(ii) Cutoff wavelength derivation** (follow §3.3): $$ \lambda_c = \frac{hc}{E_g} = \frac{1.24}{E_g(eV)} \ \mu m $$ **Numerical Calculation:** For InGaAs ($E_g = 0.75$ eV, $\eta = 0.9$): Cutoff wavelength: $$ \lambda_c = \frac{1.24}{0.75} = 1.653 \ \mu m = 1653 \ nm $$ Since $1550 \ nm < 1653 \ nm$, the photon *is* absorbed. ✓ Responsivity at $\lambda = 1550 \ nm = 1.55 \ \mu m$: $$ \mathcal{R} = 0.8065 \cdot \eta \cdot \lambda_{[\mu m]} = 0.8065 \times 0.9 \times 1.55 $$ $$ \mathcal{R} = 0.8065 \times 1.395 = 1.125 \ A/W $$ > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall (Part B):** A very common mistake is writing the I-V equation as $I = I_0[\exp(qV/kT) - 1] + I_{ph}$ (with a *plus* sign). The correct sign is *minus* because photocurrent flows in the *reverse* direction. Also, students often forget to convert nm to $\mu m$ before plugging into the "1.24/Eg" shortcut formula, leading to 1000× errors. Always check units first. --- ## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist) - **Master Equation:** $I_{ph} = \dfrac{q\eta\lambda}{hc} P_{opt}$ — the single most important formula in this module. - **Responsivity Identity:** $\mathcal{R} = I_{ph}/P_{opt} = q\eta\lambda/(hc) = 0.8065 \cdot \eta \cdot \lambda_{[\mu m]} \ A/W$. - **Cutoff Wavelength:** $\lambda_c = 1.24/E_g(eV) \ \mu m$. Photons with $\lambda > \lambda_c$ are *not* detected. - **Sign Convention:** $I_{total} = I_0[\exp(qV/kT) - 1] - I_{ph}$ (photocurrent subtracts from dark current). - **Operating Mode:** Reverse bias is *mandatory* for linear, low-noise photodetection (photoconductive mode). - **Depletion Width:** $W \propto \sqrt{V_{bi} + V_R}$ — higher reverse bias widens the absorption zone. - **Capacitance:** $C_j = \epsilon_s A / W$ — higher reverse bias *reduces* $C_j$, increasing bandwidth. - **Junction Capacitance Trade-off:** Larger area $A$ ⇒ higher $\eta$ but higher $C_j$ and lower speed. - **Thermal Voltage:** $V_T = kT/q = 25.85 \ mV$ at $T = 300 \ K$ — default KTU assumption. - **SI Unit Check:** Responsivity in A/W, quantum efficiency dimensionless, $\lambda_c$ in $\mu m$ or nm. - **Compound Semiconductor Advantage:** InGaAs/InP detect up to $\sim 1.7 \ \mu m$ (telecom C/L-band); Si is limited to $\sim 1.1 \ \mu m$. - **Key Performance Trio:** $\eta$, $\mathcal{R}$, $\lambda_c$ — always be ready to compute one from the other two. - **Photovoltaic Mode vs Photoconductive Mode:** $V = 0$ (solar cell) vs $V < 0$ (linear detector). - **KTU Board Drawing Checklist:** Always include (1) p-n layers, (2) depletion region, (3) anti-reflection coating, (4) reverse bias supply, (5) load resistor, (6) optical input arrow, (7) photocurrent direction arrow. - **Memorize Constants:** $q = 1.602 \times 10^{-19}$ C, $h = 6.626 \times 10^{-34}$ J·s, $c = 3 \times 10^8$ m/s, $hc = 1.24$ eV·$\mu m$. > [!IMPORTANT] > **Final KTU Strategy Tip:** The derivation of $I_{ph}$ from $\eta$ and $P_{opt}$ appears in **almost every KTU photodiode question** in some form. Master the three-line derivation (energy of photon → photon flux → collected electrons → current) and you will secure full marks on 70%+ of the problems in this module.

topics.title: p-i-n Photodiodes: Device Structure, Principle of Operation
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEEVT413 - COMPOUND SEMICONDUCTORS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** p-i-n Photodiodes: Device Structure, Principle of Operation

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

A **p-i-n Photodiode** is a specialized semiconductor photodetector in which an intrinsic (undoped, near-pure) semiconductor region is sandwiched between a heavily doped **p-region** (anode side) and a heavily doped **n-region** (cathode side). The presence of this intrinsic (i) layer dramatically widens the depletion region, enabling highly efficient absorption of incident photons, fast carrier transit times, and a low-junction-capacitance response — making it the workhorse detector in high-speed fiber-optic communication and high-sensitivity light-sensing systems.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight (2024 Scheme):** A p-i-n photodiode is *not* a simple p-n junction with light shining on it. The deliberate insertion of the intrinsic layer is what differentiates it, giving it engineered depletion width control and superior quantum efficiency at telecom wavelengths (e.g., **1.3 µm** and **1.55 µm**).

### Symbol Conventions Used Throughout
- **q** = electronic charge = **1.602 × 10⁻¹⁹ C**
- **h** = Planck's constant = **6.626 × 10⁻³⁴ J·s**
- **c** = speed of light in vacuum = **3 × 10⁸ m/s**
- **ε_s** = permittivity of the semiconductor
- **N_A, N_D** = acceptor and donor doping concentrations (cm⁻³)
- **V_bi** = built-in potential
- **V_R** = reverse bias voltage

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Think of a **p-i-n photodiode as a "three-layer sandwich detector"**:

- Imagine a long corridor (the **intrinsic region**) inside a building. Light enters the corridor from one end and gets absorbed along the way as it travels through the corridor walls.
- The **p-layer and n-layer** are the entry doors at each end — they are highly conductive and serve to set up a strong internal electric field.
- The **intrinsic (i) region** is the wide, nearly empty corridor. Because there are very few free carriers here, the electric field is uniform and intense throughout this region — almost like a perfectly smooth slide for the photo-generated electrons and holes to whiz through.
- A **reverse bias voltage** widens the corridor even more (depletion width expansion) and accelerates the carriers — like turning on a powerful fan that pulls the electrons and holes apart instantly.

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Application:** Every long-haul optical fiber link from Kochi to London, every LiDAR in a self-driving car, and every DVD/Blu-ray pickup uses a p-i-n photodiode as the light-to-electrical transducer.

## 1.3 Why a p-n Junction Alone Is Insufficient

In a standard p-n photodiode, the depletion width **W** is typically only **0.1 – 1 µm**. For photons with absorption length much larger than W (e.g., long-wavelength infrared light at 1.55 µm in silicon: absorption length ≈ 15 µm), most photons pass *through* the device without being absorbed. The p-i-n architecture solves this by making the active (absorbing) depletion region **engineerably wide**, typically **1 – 50 µm**.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Layered Structure Breakdown

The p-i-n photodiode consists of three distinct regions grown epitaxially (commonly using **MOCVD** or **MBE** in III-V compound semiconductors such as **InGaAs**, **InP**, **GaAs**):

| Layer | Doping Type | Typical Thickness | Function |
|-------|-------------|-------------------|----------|
| p⁺-region | Heavily p-doped (Zn, Cd acceptors) | ~0.5 – 2 µm | Ohmic contact formation; minority-carrier source |
| i-region | Intrinsic (residual n ≈ 10¹⁴ – 10¹⁵ cm⁻³) | ~1 – 50 µm | **Photon absorption zone** + drift region |
| n⁺-region | Heavily n-doped (Sn, Te, S donors) | ~1 – 5 µm (substrate side) | Ohmic contact formation; wide-bandgap window in heterojunctions |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> In **heterojunction p-i-n photodiodes** (e.g., InP/InGaAs), the wide-bandgap InP "window" layers on either side of the InGaAs absorbing intrinsic layer permit light to enter with negligible surface recombination, while the narrower bandgap InGaAs (E_g ≈ **0.75 eV**) absorbs the long-wavelength light efficiently.

## 2.2 Principle of Operation (Step-by-Step Logic)

**Step 1 — Photon Incidence**
Light of energy **hν > E_g** enters through the anti-reflection (AR) coated top window and penetrates into the intrinsic region.

**Step 2 — Photogeneration**
Each absorbed photon creates **one electron–hole pair (EHP)**. The absorption follows the Beer–Lambert law:

$$I(x) = I_0 \, e^{-\alpha x}$$

where **α** is the absorption coefficient (cm⁻¹), material-dependent. Photogeneration rate at depth x is:

$$G(x) = \alpha \, \eta_{int} \, \Phi_0 \, e^{-\alpha x}$$

**Step 3 — Carrier Separation by Built-in Field**
The EHPs generated *inside the depletion region* (which is essentially the entire i-region under reverse bias) are immediately swept apart by the strong built-in electric field. Electrons drift to the n-side; holes drift to the p-side.

**Step 4 — Drift Transport Across the i-Region**
With reverse bias **V_R**, the electric field is approximately:

$$E = \frac{V_{bi} + V_R}{W}$$

This field is typically **10⁴ – 10⁵ V/cm** — high enough for carriers to reach **saturation velocity** **v_sat ≈ 10⁷ cm/s** in most compound semiconductors.

**Step 5 — Photocurrent Collection**
The collected photocurrent at the external circuit is:

$$I_{ph} = q \, A \, \int_0^W G(x) \, dx$$

After integration and applying the Beer–Lambert law:

$$I_{ph} = q \, A \, \Phi_0 \, (1 - e^{-\alpha W})$$

where A is the active area and Φ₀ is the incident photon flux (photons/s).

**Step 6 — Total Current Under Reverse Bias**
The total diode current follows the Shockley equation modified for photogeneration:

$$I = I_s \left( e^{qV/kT} - 1 \right) - I_{ph}$$

Under sufficient reverse bias, the exponential term → 0, and:

$$I \approx -I_{ph} - I_s$$

i.e., the photocurrent dominates, and the small **dark current I_s** appears as a noise floor.

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> **Critical Examination Formulas — Memorize This Table**

| # | Quantity | Formula | Units / Notes |
|---|----------|---------|---------------|
| 1 | Photon Energy | $E = h\nu = \dfrac{hc}{\lambda}$ | Joules; $\lambda$ in meters |
| 2 | Cutoff Wavelength | $\lambda_c = \dfrac{hc}{E_g} = \dfrac{1.24}{E_g(\text{eV})}$ µm | Longest wavelength absorbed |
| 3 | Absorption Profile | $I(x) = I_0 \, e^{-\alpha x}$ | Beer–Lambert law |
| 4 | Photogeneration Rate | $G(x) = \alpha \, \Phi_0 \, e^{-\alpha x}$ | EHP per cm³ per second |
| 5 | Photocurrent | $I_{ph} = q A \Phi_0 \left(1 - e^{-\alpha W}\right)$ | A; W = depletion width |
| 6 | Quantum Efficiency (η) | $\eta = \dfrac{I_{ph}/q}{P_{opt}/h\nu} = 1 - e^{-\alpha W}$ | Dimensionless (0 ≤ η ≤ 1) |
| 7 | Responsivity (ℛ) | $\mathcal{R} = \dfrac{I_{ph}}{P_{opt}} = \dfrac{\eta \, q}{h\nu} = \dfrac{\eta \, \lambda}{1.24}$ | A/W; λ in µm |
| 8 | Depletion Width (p-i-n) | $W \approx \sqrt{\dfrac{2 \varepsilon_s (V_{bi} + V_R)}{q \, N_{eff}}}$ | For p-i-n, W ≈ i-layer thickness |
| 9 | Transit Time | $t_{tr} = \dfrac{W}{v_{sat}}$ | seconds; sets high-speed limit |
| 10 | 3-dB Bandwidth | $f_{3dB} = \dfrac{0.44}{t_{tr}}$ | Hz; for drift-limited response |
| 11 | Junction Capacitance | $C_j = \dfrac{\varepsilon_s A}{W}$ | Farads; lower → faster RC |
| 12 | RC-Limited Bandwidth | $f_{RC} = \dfrac{1}{2 \pi R_L C_j}$ | Hz; series-R + load-R |
| 13 | Total Dark Current | $I_s = I_{diff} + I_{gen} + I_{tun}$ | A; dominant term depends on V_R |
| 14 | Noise-Equivalent Power | $NEP = \dfrac{h\nu}{\eta} \sqrt{\dfrac{2 I_s}{q}}$ | W/√Hz; minimum detectable power |
| 15 | Detectivity (D*) | $D^* = \dfrac{\sqrt{A}}{NEP}$ | cm·Hz^½/W; figure of merit |

> [!WARNING]
> **Pipes Avoided:** All absolute-value and conditional bars in formula cells use `\vert` or `\mid` to prevent markdown table breakage. Note also that $v_{sat}$ and $V_{bi}$ use different cases to avoid confusion between velocity and voltage.

## 2.4 Engineering Utility — Why This Matters

- **Optical fiber communication (telecom)**: p-i-n photodiodes in InP/InGaAs convert 1.55 µm optical signals to electrical at **>40 Gbps**.
- **LiDAR and 3-D imaging**: Fast response (sub-ns) gives centimeter-level range resolution.
- **Medical pulse oximetry**: Detects red (660 nm) and IR (940 nm) light transmitted through tissue.
- **Astronomical detectors**: High detectivity D* allows single-photon-level detection when cooled.
- **Solar-blind UV sensing**: Wide-bandgap AlGaN p-i-n diodes reject visible/IR background.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of Quantum Efficiency (η)

The **external quantum efficiency (EQE)** is defined as the ratio of collected electron–hole pairs to incident photons.

**Start from the definition:**

$$\eta_{ext} = \frac{\text{Number of carriers collected}}{\text{Number of incident photons}}$$

**Convert each to rates:**

$$\eta_{ext} = \frac{I_{ph}/q}{P_{opt}/h\nu}$$

**The photon flux in terms of incident optical power** $P_{opt}$:

$$\Phi_0 = \frac{P_{opt}}{h\nu} \quad \text{(photons per second)}$$

**Substitute the Beer–Lambert derived photocurrent** $I_{ph} = q A \Phi_0 (1 - e^{-\alpha W})$:

$$\eta_{ext} = \frac{q A \Phi_0 (1 - e^{-\alpha W}) / q}{P_{opt} / h\nu} = \frac{A \Phi_0 h\nu (1 - e^{-\alpha W})}{P_{opt}}$$

**Recognize that the incident power** $P_{opt} = A \Phi_0 h\nu$:

$$\eta_{ext} = \frac{A \Phi_0 h\nu (1 - e^{-\alpha W})}{A \Phi_0 h\nu} = 1 - e^{-\alpha W}$$

**Final boxed result:**

$$\boxed{\eta_{ext} = 1 - e^{-\alpha W}}$$

> The derivation shows that η is purely a geometric/absorption quantity — it depends only on the absorption coefficient α and the depletion width W, *not* on the incident power level. Increasing W (thicker i-layer) or choosing a material with higher α (direct bandgap) both increase η.

## 3.2 Derivation of Responsivity (ℛ)

**Start from the definition:**

$$\mathcal{R} = \frac{I_{ph}}{P_{opt}}$$

**Using η = I_{ph}/q ÷ P_{opt}/hν, solve for I_{ph}:**

$$I_{ph} = \eta \, q \, \Phi_0 = \eta \, q \cdot \frac{P_{opt}}{h\nu}$$

**Therefore:**

$$\mathcal{R} = \frac{\eta \, q \, P_{opt} / h\nu}{P_{opt}} = \frac{\eta \, q}{h\nu}$$

**Substituting hν = hc/λ:**

$$\mathcal{R} = \frac{\eta \, q \, \lambda}{hc}$$

**Using the numerical identity** $hc/q = 1.24 \times 10^{-6}$ V·m = 1.24 µm·V:

$$\boxed{\mathcal{R} = \frac{\eta \, \lambda (\text{in µm})}{1.24} \;\; \text{A/W}}$$

> [!NOTE]
> This is the *most-tested* formula in KTU exams on photodiodes. A typical problem: "Find the responsivity of an InGaAs p-i-n photodiode with η = 0.85 at 1.55 µm." Plug in: ℛ = 0.85 × 1.55 / 1.24 = **1.063 A/W**.

## 3.3 Derivation of Photocurrent from Photogeneration Rate

The continuity equation for minority carriers in steady state under illumination:

$$G(x) = \alpha \, \Phi_0 \, e^{-\alpha x}$$

The photocurrent is the integrated generation rate over the depletion volume, scaled by q and area:

$$I_{ph} = q A \int_0^W G(x) \, dx$$

**Step-by-step integration:**

$$I_{ph} = q A \int_0^W \alpha \, \Phi_0 \, e^{-\alpha x} \, dx$$

$$= q A \alpha \, \Phi_0 \left[ -\frac{1}{\alpha} e^{-\alpha x} \right]_0^W$$

$$= q A \alpha \, \Phi_0 \cdot \left( -\frac{1}{\alpha} \right) \left( e^{-\alpha W} - e^{0} \right)$$

$$= q A \, \Phi_0 \left( 1 - e^{-\alpha W} \right)$$

**Therefore:**

$$\boxed{I_{ph} = q A \Phi_0 \left( 1 - e^{-\alpha W} \right)}$$

## 3.4 Derivation of Transit-Time-Limited 3-dB Bandwidth

The transit time across the intrinsic region of width W at saturation velocity $v_{sat}$:

$$t_{tr} = \frac{W}{v_{sat}}$$

The transit-time limited bandwidth for a uniform generation profile (typical in p-i-n since most absorption is in the i-region) follows from Fourier analysis of the carrier collection impulse:

$$f_{3dB} = \frac{0.44}{t_{tr}} = \frac{0.44 \, v_{sat}}{W}$$

> The 0.44 factor comes from the first zero of the sin(πf·t_tr)/(πf·t_tr) response function at f·t_tr = 0.443.

## 3.5 Analytical Matrix — Common KTU Problem Patterns

| Problem Given | Find | Key Formula Chain |
|---------------|------|------------------|
| λ, η | ℛ | $\mathcal{R} = \eta \lambda / 1.24$ |
| P_opt, λ, η | I_{ph} | $\mathcal{R} = \eta \lambda / 1.24$; then $I_{ph} = \mathcal{R} \cdot P_{opt}$ |
| α, W | η | $\eta = 1 - e^{-\alpha W}$ |
| E_g | λ_c | $\lambda_c = 1.24 / E_g$ (µm, eV) |
| W, v_{sat} | f_{3dB} | $f_{3dB} = 0.44 \, v_{sat} / W$ |
| A, W, ε_s | C_j | $C_j = \varepsilon_s A / W$ |
| Φ_0, α, W, A | I_{ph} | $I_{ph} = q A \Phi_0 (1 - e^{-\alpha W})$ |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Cross-Sectional Mermaid Diagram of a p-i-n Photodiode

> The following Mermaid block uses only safe alphanumeric node IDs (no reserved keywords as labels), and clean uppercase text without markdown formatting inside labels.

```mermaid
graph TD
    A1[ANTIREFLECTION COATING AR LAYER] --> B1[P PLUS REGION HEAVILY P DOPED WIDTH 0.5 TO 2 UM]
    B1 --> C1[I REGION INTRINSIC UNDOPED WIDTH 1 TO 50 UM ACTIVE ABSORPTION ZONE]
    C1 --> D1[N PLUS REGION HEAVILY N DOPED SUBSTRATE WIDTH 1 TO 5 UM]
    D1 --> E1[BOTTOM OHMIC CONTACT METAL]
    B1 --> F1[TOP OHMIC CONTACT METAL P SIDE ANODE]
    E1 --> G1[CATHODETERMINAL N SIDE]
    H1[INCIDENT PHOTON H NU GREATER THAN EG] -.-> A1
    I1[REVERSE BIAS VOLTAGE VR EXTERNAL] -.-> F1
    I1 -.-> G1
    C1 --> J1[DEPLETION REGION VIRTUALLY ENTIRE I LAYER]
    J1 --> K1[UNIFORM ELECTRIC FIELD E APPROX VBI PLUS VR DIVIDED BY W]
    K1 --> L1[ELECTRONS DRIFT TOWARD N SIDE AT VSAT]
    K1 --> M1[HOLES DRIFT TOWARD P SIDE AT VSAT]
    L1 --> N1[COLLECTED PHOTOCURRENT IPH EXTERNAL CIRCUIT]
    M1 --> N1
```

## 4.2 Block-Level Functional Architecture Flow

> Adapted block diagram showing signal-flow and energy-domain conversion in a p-i-n photodiode receiver chain.

```mermaid
graph LR
    Src[OPTICAL SOURCE LASER OR LED] --> AR[AR COATED WINDOW]
    AR --> PPLUS[P PLUS CONTACT LAYER]
    PPLUS --> IZONE[I REGION PHOTON ABSORPTION EHP GENERATION]
    IZONE --> NPLUS[N PLUS SUBSTRATE]
    IZONE -.->.|hv gt Eg| GEN[EHP GENERATION G OF X EQUALS ALPHA PHI0 EXP MINUS ALPHA X]
    GEN -.->.|drift| EFLD[UNIFORM ELECTRIC FIELD E VBI PLUS VR DIVIDED BY W]
    EFLD -.->.|electron path| NCOL[ELECTRON COLLECTION N SIDE]
    EFLD -.->.|hole path| PCOL[HOLE COLLECTION P SIDE]
    NCOL --> TIA[TRANSIMPEDANCE AMPLIFIER TIA]
    PCOL --> TIA
    NPLUS --> TIA
    TIA --> OUT[OUTPUT VOLTAGE OR DIGITAL BITSTREAM]
    BIAS[REVERSE BIAS SUPPLY VR] -.-> PPLUS
    BIAS -.-> NPLUS
```

## 4.3 Energy Band Diagram (Schematic Description)

Under reverse bias $V_R$, the bands tilt downward from p-side to n-side. The intrinsic region appears as a **flat-band region of width W** with a slope equal to $-qE$. Photons absorbed here create carriers that "roll downhill" — electrons to the right (n-side) and holes to the left (p-side). The **quasi-Fermi levels split** by an amount approximately equal to the open-circuit voltage under illumination, but in normal photoconductive operation the device is held at fixed $V_R$ and the photocurrent flows continuously.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Tip:** If asked to "sketch the energy band diagram," always mark: (1) the built-in potential $V_{bi}$, (2) the applied $V_R$ as additional tilt, (3) the flat intrinsic region, and (4) the direction of carrier drift with arrows.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 [KTU University Exam – May 2024]
**Q: With a neat sketch, explain the structure of a p-i-n photodiode. Why is an intrinsic layer introduced between the p and n regions?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- **(1 mark)** A p-i-n photodiode consists of a heavily doped p⁺ region, an intrinsic (i) region of width 1–50 µm, and a heavily doped n⁺ region, sandwiched between two ohmic metal contacts with an anti-reflection coating on the top.
- **(1 mark)** The intrinsic layer acts as the active photon absorption zone where electron–hole pairs are generated and immediately separated by the strong built-in electric field.
- **(1 mark)** Introducing the i-layer widens the depletion region, increases quantum efficiency (since most photons are absorbed within the depletion volume where collection efficiency is nearly 100%), reduces junction capacitance (faster response), and minimizes surface recombination by using heterojunction window layers.

### Question 2 [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]
**Q: Define responsivity of a photodiode. A silicon p-i-n photodiode has a quantum efficiency of 65% at 900 nm. Calculate its responsivity.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- **(1 mark)** Responsivity ℛ is the photocurrent generated per unit incident optical power: $\mathcal{R} = I_{ph} / P_{opt}$, measured in A/W.
- **(1 mark)** Using $\mathcal{R} = \eta \lambda / 1.24$ with η = 0.65 and λ = 0.9 µm:

$$\mathcal{R} = \frac{0.65 \times 0.9}{1.24} = 0.4717 \;\; \text{A/W}$$

- **(1 mark)** Therefore, **ℛ ≈ 0.472 A/W**.

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each) — Internal Choice

### Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024]

**(a)** With the help of a neat cross-sectional diagram, describe the device structure and energy band diagram of a p-i-n photodiode under reverse bias. Discuss the role of the intrinsic region in enhancing the device performance. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** An InGaAs p-i-n photodiode operates at 1.55 µm with quantum efficiency of 85% and active area diameter of 80 µm. If 100 µW of optical power is incident:
   - (i) Calculate the photocurrent generated.
   - (ii) Calculate the responsivity.
   - (iii) If the depletion width is 5 µm and the saturation velocity is $1 \times 10^7$ cm/s, find the transit-time limited 3-dB bandwidth. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution

**Part (a) — 7 marks**
- **(2 marks)** Draw and label the cross-section: AR coating → p⁺ layer (0.5 µm) → intrinsic i-region (5 µm) → n⁺ substrate (1–5 µm) → ohmic contacts. Show incident photon arrow.
- **(2 marks)** Draw energy band diagram under reverse bias: flat intrinsic region, built-in $V_{bi}$ plus applied $V_R$ total band tilt, electron quasi-Fermi level $E_{Fc}$ on n-side, hole quasi-Fermi level $E_{Fv}$ on p-side, separation $qV$ across the device.
- **(3 marks)** Role of i-region: (i) widens the depletion region → higher quantum efficiency since $\eta = 1 - e^{-\alpha W}$, (ii) reduces junction capacitance $C_j = \varepsilon_s A / W$ → faster RC response, (iii) acts as the absorbing volume where drift collection is near-unity (no slow diffusion tails), (iv) enables engineering of W via epitaxy to match the absorption length of the target wavelength.

**Part (b) — 7 marks**

**(i) Photocurrent — 2 marks:**

$$\mathcal{R} = \frac{\eta \lambda}{1.24} = \frac{0.85 \times 1.55}{1.24} = 1.0625 \;\; \text{A/W}$$

$$I_{ph} = \mathcal{R} \times P_{opt} = 1.0625 \times 100 \times 10^{-6} = 106.25 \;\; \mu\text{A}$$

**(ii) Responsivity — 2 marks:** Already computed: $\mathcal{R} = 1.0625$ A/W.

**(iii) 3-dB Bandwidth — 3 marks:**

$$t_{tr} = \frac{W}{v_{sat}} = \frac{5 \times 10^{-4}\;\text{cm}}{1 \times 10^7\;\text{cm/s}} = 5 \times 10^{-11}\;\text{s} = 50\;\text{ps}$$

$$f_{3dB} = \frac{0.44}{t_{tr}} = \frac{0.44}{50 \times 10^{-12}} = 8.8 \times 10^9 \;\; \text{Hz} = 8.8 \;\text{GHz}$$

### Question B (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024]

**(a)** Derive the expression for quantum efficiency and responsivity of a p-i-n photodiode. State clearly all assumptions used. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A GaAs p-i-n photodiode (E_g = 1.42 eV) has an active area of $0.1$ mm² and is illuminated by light at 850 nm with intensity 10 mW/cm². The absorption coefficient is $10^4$ cm⁻¹ and the intrinsic region width is 3 µm. Calculate:
   - (i) The cutoff wavelength.
   - (ii) The quantum efficiency.
   - (iii) The number of photons incident per second.
   - (iv) The generated photocurrent. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution

**Part (a) — 7 marks**
- **(1 mark)** Assumptions: (1) one absorbed photon creates one EHP, (2) every photogenerated carrier in the i-region is collected (collection efficiency = 1), (3) negligible absorption in p⁺ and n⁺ regions, (4) uniform electric field in i-region, (5) no avalanche multiplication.
- **(3 marks)** Derivation of η (see Section 3.1): Start from definition → substitute $I_{ph} = qA\Phi_0(1-e^{-\alpha W})$ → get $\eta = 1 - e^{-\alpha W}$.
- **(3 marks)** Derivation of ℛ (see Section 3.2): Start from $\mathcal{R} = I_{ph}/P_{opt}$ → use $P_{opt} = A\Phi_0 h\nu$ → simplify → use $hc/q = 1.24 \times 10^{-6}$ V·m → final result $\mathcal{R} = \eta \lambda / 1.24$ A/W.

**Part (b) — 7 marks**

**(i) Cutoff wavelength — 1 mark:**

$$\lambda_c = \frac{1.24}{E_g} = \frac{1.24}{1.42} = 0.873 \;\; \mu\text{m} = 873 \;\; \text{nm}$$

Since incident λ = 850 nm < 873 nm, the photons are absorbed (hν > E_g). ✓

**(ii) Quantum efficiency — 2 marks:**

$$\eta = 1 - e^{-\alpha W} = 1 - e^{-(10^4)(3 \times 10^{-4})} = 1 - e^{-3} = 1 - 0.0498 = 0.9502$$

$$\eta \approx 95\%$$

**(iii) Number of photons per second — 2 marks:**

$$P_{opt} = I \times A = 10 \times 10^{-3} \text{ W/cm}^2 \times 0.1 \times 10^{-2} \text{ cm}^2 = 10^{-5} \text{ W}$$

$$\Phi_0 = \frac{P_{opt}}{h\nu} = \frac{P_{opt} \lambda}{hc} = \frac{10^{-5} \times 850 \times 10^{-9}}{6.626 \times 10^{-34} \times 3 \times 10^8}$$

$$\Phi_0 = \frac{8.5 \times 10^{-12}}{1.9878 \times 10^{-25}} = 4.276 \times 10^{13} \;\; \text{photons/s}$$

**(iv) Photocurrent — 2 marks:**

$$I_{ph} = q A \Phi_0 \eta = 1.602 \times 10^{-19} \times 0.1 \times 10^{-2} \times 4.276 \times 10^{13} \times 0.95$$

$$I_{ph} = 6.51 \times 10^{-9} \;\; \text{A} \approx 6.51 \;\; \text{nA}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Unit Mix-ups in ℛ Formula:** The constant is 1.24 *only* when λ is in **µm** and ℛ is in **A/W**. Converting λ to meters without also rescaling the constant costs full marks.
> 2. **Forgetting to Check λ < λ_c:** If the problem gives a wavelength longer than the cutoff, the answer is η = 0 and $I_{ph}$ = 0. Marks are deducted if the student blindly substitutes into the formula.
> 3. **Confusing W (depletion width) with the total device thickness:** The absorption happens only in W. Carriers generated in p⁺ or n⁺ regions contribute negligibly (or recombine).
> 4. **Exponential sign errors in $\eta = 1 - e^{-\alpha W}$:** A frequent careless mistake. Always verify: as W → ∞, η → 1 (asymptote).
> 5. **Forgetting the 0.44 factor in $f_{3dB}$:** Some textbooks use 0.35 or 0.443. KTU expects **0.44** as the standard numerical factor.
> 6. **In Part B (b) numerical problems, not converting mm² to cm²** when working with intensity in mW/cm² is a common error leading to 100× wrong answers.

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist for KTU Module 3 — p-i-n Photodiodes**

- ✅ **Structure:** p⁺ – i – n⁺ sandwich; i-layer is 1–50 µm; AR coating on top window; ohmic metal contacts.
- ✅ **Operation principle:** Photons with hν > E_g absorbed in i-region → EHP generated → swept by built-in + applied field → drift at v_sat → photocurrent.
- ✅ **Key advantage over p-n:** Wider depletion → higher η; lower C_j → faster response; drift-only collection → no slow diffusion tails.
- ✅ **Cutoff wavelength:** $\lambda_c = 1.24 / E_g$ (µm, eV). Above this → no absorption.
- ✅ **Quantum efficiency:** $\eta = 1 - e^{-\alpha W}$. Increases with W and α. Direct-bandgap materials (e.g., GaAs, InGaAs) have much higher α than indirect (Si).
- ✅ **Responsivity:** $\mathcal{R} = \eta \lambda / 1.24$ A/W. Linear in λ below cutoff.
- ✅ **Photocurrent:** $I_{ph} = q A \Phi_0 (1 - e^{-\alpha W}) = \mathcal{R} \cdot P_{opt}$.
- ✅ **Transit time:** $t_{tr} = W / v_{sat}$.
- ✅ **3-dB bandwidth (transit-limited):** $f_{3dB} = 0.44 \, v_{sat} / W$.
- ✅ **Junction capacitance:** $C_j = \varepsilon_s A / W$. Lower W → higher C_j (trade-off with η).
- ✅ **Dark current sources:** $I_{diff}$ (thermal generation in neutral regions), $I_{gen}$ (generation in depletion via SRH), $I_{tun}$ (band-to-band tunneling under high V_R).
- ✅ **Noise-Equivalent Power:** $NEP = (h\nu / \eta) \sqrt{2 I_s / q}$ — minimum detectable power per √Hz bandwidth.
- ✅ **Detectivity:** $D^* = \sqrt{A} / NEP$ — normalized figure of merit in cm·Hz^½/W.
- ✅ **Heterojunction p-i-n (e.g., InP/InGaAs/InP):** Wide-bandgap InP "window" layers prevent surface recombination; narrow-bandgap InGaAs absorbs at 1.3/1.55 µm telecom wavelengths.
- ✅ **Materials comparison:**
  - **Si p-i-n** — visible/NIR (400–1100 nm), cheap, indirect bandgap (lower α).
  - **Ge p-i-n** — NIR/IR (800–1600 nm), higher dark current.
  - **InGaAs p-i-n (on InP)** — Telecom standard 1.3/1.55 µm, low dark current.
  - **GaAs p-i-n** — Visible/red (650–870 nm), direct bandgap, fast.
  - **InSb / HgCdTe p-i-n** — Mid-IR (3–12 µm), needs cryogenic cooling.
- ✅ **Polarization:** p-i-n photodiodes are operated in **reverse bias** for maximum depletion width and linear photoconductive response.
- ✅ **KTU-specific tip:** When asked to "compare p-n and p-i-n photodiodes," always include a quantitative table: depletion width, η, response speed, dark current, and application area.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Avalanche Photodiodes: Device Structure, Operation Principle
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEEVT413 - COMPOUND SEMICONDUCTORS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Avalanche Photodiodes: Device Structure, Operation Principle

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Avalanche Photodiodes (APD): Device Structure & Operation Principle

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!NOTE]
> **Avalanche Photodiode (APD):** A highly sensitive, high-speed semiconductor photodetector in which the photogenerated carriers (electrons and holes) are accelerated through a high electric field region where they undergo **impact ionization**, producing additional electron-hole pairs. This self-multiplying cascade process, known as **avalanche multiplication**, generates an **internal gain (M)** that significantly enhances the device responsivity compared to a conventional PIN photodiode.

In the context of the **KTU 2024 Scheme** syllabus for Compound Semiconductors, an APD is typically realized using **III-V compound semiconductors** such as **InGaAs / InP**, **Si / Ge**, or **InAlAs / InGaAs** heterojunction systems, because their tailored bandgaps and ionization-coefficient ratios are well-suited for low-noise, high-sensitivity detection at fibre-optic communication wavelengths (**1310 nm** and **1550 nm**).

### Key Performance Differentiator

| Device Type | Internal Gain Mechanism | Typical Sensitivity |
|---|---|---|
| PIN Photodiode | None (M = 1) | Moderate |
| Avalanche Photodiode (APD) | Impact Ionization (M = 10–100) | **High** |
| Single-Photon Avalanche Diode (SPAD) | Geiger-mode Avalanche (M → ∞) | Ultra-High |

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Snowball Avalanche" Model

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Imagine a snowball rolling down a snow-covered mountain slope:**
> 1. A single photon (the **initial snowball push**) is absorbed and creates **one** electron-hole pair at the top of the absorption region.
> 2. The electron (the **moving snowball**) drifts into the **high-field multiplication region** (the steeper slope).
> 3. As it accelerates, it gains enough kinetic energy to **knock out** a bound electron from the lattice (impact ionization) — equivalent to the snowball **picking up additional snow**.
> 4. Now **two electrons** continue accelerating; each can create more pairs — the **avalanche cascade** continues.
> 5. The total number of electrons collected (the **final snowball size**) is M times the original photon-generated carrier — this **M** is the **multiplication factor**.

This analogy directly explains **why APDs have internal gain** while PIN diodes do not: PIN diodes lack the high-field multiplication region, so the "snowball" never grows.

## 1.3 Standard Physical Constants Used in APD Analysis

- **Elementary charge** $q = 1.602 \times 10^{-19}\ \text{C}$
- **Planck's constant** $h = 6.626 \times 10^{-34}\ \text{J·s}$
- **Speed of light in vacuum** $c = 3 \times 10^{8}\ \text{m/s}$
- **Permittivity of free space** $\varepsilon_{0} = 8.854 \times 10^{-12}\ \text{F/m}$
- **Boltzmann constant** $k_{B} = 1.381 \times 10^{-23}\ \text{J/K}$
- **Bandgap of InGaAs** $E_{g} \approx 0.75\ \text{eV}$ (at 1550 nm)
- **Bandgap of Si** $E_{g} \approx 1.12\ \text{eV}$ (at 850 nm)
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Fundamental Operating Principle — The Five-Stage Cascade

The operation of an APD can be partitioned into **five sequential physical processes**, each of which the KTU examiner may target individually:

### Stage 1 — **Photon Absorption (Generation)**
An incident photon of energy $h\nu \geq E_{g}$ penetrates the wide-bandgap **window / contact layer** and is absorbed in the narrow-bandgap **absorption region** (typically intrinsic InGaAs), generating a primary electron-hole pair.

### Stage 2 — **Carrier Separation & Drift**
The intrinsic (i) region is fully depleted. The photogenerated carriers are swept out by the internal electric field:
- **Electrons** drift toward the **n-side**.
- **Holes** drift toward the **p-side**.

### Stage 3 — **Injection into the Multiplication Region**
Only **one carrier type** (electrons, for best noise performance) should be injected into the high-field multiplication region to initiate impact ionization. This is the design rationale behind the **SAM (Separate Absorption and Multiplication)** structure.

### Stage 4 — **Impact Ionization (The Avalanche Mechanism)**
The carrier, accelerated by the high field $E$, acquires kinetic energy greater than the **threshold energy** $E_{th} \approx 1.5 E_{g}$. Upon collision with the crystal lattice, it transfers this energy to a valence-band electron, **promoting it to the conduction band** and creating a **new e-h pair**. The process repeats — a chain reaction.

### Stage 5 — **Collection & External Readout**
The multiplied carriers are collected at the terminals, producing a photocurrent that is **M times** larger than the primary photocurrent.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why Compound Semiconductors?** In **Si**, both electrons and holes ionize nearly equally, which is undesirable. In **InP / InGaAs** systems, the **ionization coefficient ratio** $k = \beta / \alpha$ is very small ($\alpha \gg \beta$), meaning electrons dominate the ionization — yielding **lower excess noise** and a cleaner avalanche.

## 2.2 The Ionization Coefficients — α and β

The **impact ionization coefficients** are field-dependent parameters that quantify the probability of ionization per unit length of travel:

$$\alpha(E) = A_{n}\, \exp\!\left(-\frac{B_{n}}{E}\right) \quad \text{(electrons)}$$

$$\beta(E) = A_{p}\, \exp\!\left(-\frac{B_{p}}{E}\right) \quad \text{(holes)}$$

where $A_{n}, B_{n}, A_{p}, B_{p}$ are empirically fitted constants (material-dependent). For **InP**, typical values are $A_{n} \approx 3 \times 10^{6}\ \text{cm}^{-1}$, $B_{n} \approx 2.3 \times 10^{6}\ \text{V/cm}$.

**Critical relationship:**
$$k = \frac{\beta}{\alpha}$$

A **small k** ⇒ electron-dominant ionization ⇒ **low noise**.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet — High-Yield Equations

| # | Formula | Physical Meaning | Typical Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $M = \dfrac{I_{M}}{I_{0}} = \dfrac{1}{1 - \int_{0}^{w} \alpha(x)\, dx}$ | Multiplication factor (electron-initiated) | dimensionless |
| 2 | $k = \beta / \alpha$ | Ionization coefficient ratio | dimensionless |
| 3 | $\int_{0}^{w} \alpha(x)\, dx = 1$ | **Breakdown condition** (M → ∞) | dimensionless |
| 4 | $F(M) = k M + \left(2 - \dfrac{1}{M}\right)(1 - k)$ | McIntyre excess noise factor | dimensionless |
| 5 | $R_{\text{APD}} = \dfrac{q \lambda}{h c}\, \eta\, M$ | APD responsivity (with gain) | A/W |
| 6 | $I_{p} = R_{\text{APD}} \cdot P_{\text{opt}} = \dfrac{q \lambda \eta M P_{\text{opt}}}{h c}$ | Output photocurrent | A |
| 7 | $V_{BR} = \dfrac{\varepsilon_{s} E_{crit}^{2}}{2 q N}$ | Breakdown voltage approximation | V |
| 8 | $f_{3dB} \approx \dfrac{1}{2 \pi \tau_{\text{tr}} M}$ | Bandwidth (gain-dependent) | Hz |
| 9 | $E_{th} \approx 1.5\, E_{g}$ | Threshold kinetic energy for impact ionization | eV |
| 10 | $\text{NEP} = \dfrac{\sqrt{\langle i_{n}^{2}\rangle}}{R_{\text{APD}}}$ | Noise-Equivalent Power | W / $\sqrt{\text{Hz}}$ |

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

APDs are the **workhorse detectors** of long-haul fibre-optic communication links. The internal gain (M ≈ 10) effectively increases the receiver sensitivity by **5–10 dB** compared to PIN-based receivers. They are also central to:
- **LIDAR** systems (autonomous vehicles)
- **Quantum key distribution** (photon counting via SPADs)
- **Positron Emission Tomography (PET)** scintillation detectors
- **Single-photon experiments** in quantum optics
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — The Multiplication Factor (M)

### Starting Point: Carrier Continuity with Ionization

Consider a one-dimensional multiplication region of width $w$. The number of carriers generated per unit length by impact ionization is:

$$\frac{dn(x)}{dx} = \alpha(x)\, n(x) + \beta(x)\, p(x)$$

$$\frac{dp(x)}{dx} = -\alpha(x)\, n(x) - \beta(x)\, p(x)$$

### Assumption — Pure Electron Injection

For the standard SAM-APD, only **electrons** are injected at $x = 0$. Thus $p(0) = 0$ and $n(0) = n_{0}$.

For a **uniform field** in the multiplication region, $\alpha(x) = \alpha = $ constant. The hole current must satisfy:

$$\frac{dp}{dx} = -(\alpha + \beta)\, p(x) - \alpha\, n_{0}\, e^{-\alpha x}$$

Wait — let us solve using a more rigorous route. The boundary conditions are:
- $n(0) = n_{0}$, $p(w) = 0$ (hole-initiated: boundary reversed).

### Solving the Electron-Initiated Case

With only electron injection, by conservation $J_{n} - J_{p} = $ const = $-J_{0}/q$. Defining $M(x) = n(x)/n_{0}$, integration yields:

$$\boxed{\,M = \frac{1}{1 - \int_{0}^{w} \alpha(x)\, dx}\,}$$

### Verification of Limiting Cases

| Condition | Result | Physical Interpretation |
|---|---|---|
| $\int \alpha dx \ll 1$ | $M \to 1$ | No multiplication — PIN-like behaviour |
| $\int \alpha dx \to 1^{-}$ | $M \to \infty$ | **Avalanche breakdown** |
| $\int \alpha dx = 0.5$ | $M = 2$ | Carrier doubles on average |

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — McIntyre's Excess Noise Factor

Excess noise arises because impact ionization is a **statistical (Poisson) process** — each carrier either ionizes or does not, randomly. McIntyre (1966) derived:

### Step A — Define Noise Spectral Density

The variance in multiplied current $i_{M}$ is:

$$\langle i_{M}^{2} \rangle = 2 q I_{0} M^{2} F(M)\, B$$

### Step B — Excess Noise Factor Definition

$$F(M) \equiv \frac{\langle M^{2} \rangle}{\langle M \rangle^{2}}$$

### Step C — Solving the Stochastic Recurrence

Let each incident carrier initiate a random number $N$ of secondary ionizations. The recursion:

$$F(M) = k M + \left(2 - \frac{1}{M}\right)(1 - k)$$

### Step D — Asymptotic Behavior

$$\lim_{M \to \infty} F(M) = k M \quad (\text{for } k \neq 0)$$

This is the **key engineering result**: excess noise grows **linearly with M** and is **proportional to k**. Therefore, choosing a material with **small k** (electron-dominant ionization) is the single most important design decision.

| Material | $k = \beta/\alpha$ | Noise Performance |
|---|---|---|
| **InP** | 0.1 – 0.4 | Excellent |
| **Si** | 0.02 – 0.05 | Outstanding (but indirect bandgap) |
| **InAlAs** | 0.2 – 0.3 | Very Good |
| **Ge** | 0.7 – 1.0 | Poor (used in hybrid Si/Ge APDs) |

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — APD Responsivity with Gain

The primary photocurrent (before multiplication) is:

$$I_{0} = \frac{q \eta P_{\text{opt}}}{h\nu} = \frac{q \eta \lambda P_{\text{opt}}}{h c}$$

Multiplying by M:

$$I_{p} = M \cdot I_{0} = \frac{q \eta \lambda M P_{\text{opt}}}{h c}$$

Defining responsivity:

$$\boxed{\,R_{\text{APD}} = \frac{I_{p}}{P_{\text{opt}}} = \frac{q \eta \lambda}{h c}\, M\,}$$

### Numerical Example (KTU-style)

> **Problem:** An InGaAs/InP APD operates at $\lambda = 1550\ \text{nm}$ with quantum efficiency $\eta = 0.85$ and multiplication factor $M = 12$. Compute the responsivity.
>
> **Solution:**
> $$R_{\text{APD}} = \frac{(1.602 \times 10^{-19})(0.85)(1550 \times 10^{-9})}{(6.626 \times 10^{-34})(3 \times 10^{8})} \times 12$$
> $$R_{\text{APD}} = \frac{2.111 \times 10^{-28}}{1.988 \times 10^{-25}} \times 12 = 1.062 \times 10^{-3} \times 12 \approx 0.0127\ \text{A/W}$$
> $$\boxed{R_{\text{APD}} \approx 12.7\ \text{mA/W}}$$
>
> Without gain (PIN, $M = 1$): $R_{\text{PIN}} \approx 1.06\ \text{mA/W}$ — a **12× improvement**.

## 3.4 Derivation 4 — Breakdown Voltage Approximation

For a one-sided abrupt junction, the breakdown field $E_{crit}$ relates to breakdown voltage via Poisson's equation:

$$\frac{dE}{dx} = \frac{q N}{\varepsilon_{s}}$$

Integrating with $E(0) = 0$:

$$E(x) = \frac{q N x}{\varepsilon_{s}}$$

The breakdown voltage is the area under the $E$–$x$ curve up to $x = w$:

$$V_{BR} = \int_{0}^{w} E\, dx = \frac{1}{2}\, \frac{q N w^{2}}{\varepsilon_{s}}$$

Eliminating $w = \varepsilon_{s} E_{crit}/(q N)$:

$$\boxed{\,V_{BR} = \frac{\varepsilon_{s}\, E_{crit}^{2}}{2 q N}\,}$$
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid — SAM-APD Device Cross-Section (Block-Level Functional Architecture)

```mermaid
graph TD
    A["Optical Fiber Input - Photon h nu"] --> B["P+ Contact Layer - Wide Bandgap InP"]
    B --> C["Multiplication Region - Intrinsic InP - High Field Zone"]
    C --> D["Absorption Region - Intrinsic InGaAs - Eg approx 0.75 eV"]
    D --> E["N+ Buffer Layer - InP Substrate"]
    E --> F["Output Photocurrent Terminal - M times Multiplied"]

    C --> G["Impact Ionization Event 1 - e-h pair created"]
    G --> H["Impact Ionization Event 2 - Secondary e-h pair"]
    H --> I["Impact Ionization Event N - Avalanche Cascade"]
    I --> F

    J["Bias Voltage Source - V reverse bias near VBR"] --> C
    K["Electric Field Profile E x - Peak in Multiplication Region"] --> C
```

## 4.2 Mermaid — Operation Flow (Five-Stage Cascade)

```mermaid
graph LR
    S1["Stage 1 Photon Absorption - Generation of Primary e-h pair"] --> S2["Stage 2 Carrier Drift in Depleted Absorption Region"]
    S2 --> S3["Stage 3 Electron Injection into High-Field Multiplication Zone"]
    S3 --> S4["Stage 4 Impact Ionization Chain Reaction - Avalanche Multiplication"]
    S4 --> S5["Stage 5 Carrier Collection - Output Photocurrent M times I primary"]

    S1 -. "lambda greater than Eg of InGaAs" .- S1
    S4 -. "Integral of alpha dx less than 1 for stable gain" .- S4
```

## 4.3 Mermaid — Energy Band Diagram Schematic (SAM-APD)

```mermaid
graph TB
    P1["P+ InP - Ec high Ev high"] --> P2["Intrinsic InP - Multiplication - Step E field high"]
    P2 --> P3["Heterointerface - Bandgap discontinuity Delta Ec and Delta Ev"]
    P3 --> P4["Intrinsic InGaAs - Absorption - Eg = 0.75 eV"]
    P4 --> P5["N+ InP Buffer - Substrate"]

    P2 -. "Electrons accelerated toward N side" .- P2
    P3 -. "Valence band discontinuity confines holes in InGaAs" .- P3
    P4 -. "Photons absorbed - e-h pairs generated" .- P4
```

## 4.4 APD Structural Variants — Comparative Architecture

```mermaid
graph TD
    APD["Avalanche Photodiode Family"]
    APD --> PIN["PIN APD - Homojunction - Simple structure"]
    APD --> SAM["SAM APD - Separate Absorption and Multiplication"]
    APD --> SAGCM["SAGCM APD - Separate Absorption Grading Charge Multiplication"]
    APD --> SPAD["SPAD - Geiger Mode - Single Photon Detection"]

    SAM --> SAMnote["Best balance of speed and noise - Widely used in 10G 40G optical receivers"]
    SAGCM --> SAGCMnote["Adds grading layer to reduce hole trapping - Used in 100G coherent systems"]
    SPAD --> SPADnote["Operates above breakdown - Requires quenching circuit - Quantum key distribution LIDAR"]
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Examiner Insight:** When asked to draw the structure of an APD, always mention that the **SAM structure** is the *de-facto standard* for InGaAs/InP APDs because it physically **separates** the absorption and multiplication functions, allowing each region to be optimized independently — absorption region for high quantum efficiency (narrow $E_{g}$) and multiplication region for low-noise avalanche (wide $E_{g}$, small $k$).
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Module 3]`
**Define an Avalanche Photodiode. What is the role of impact ionization in its operation?**

**Model Answer:**

> An **Avalanche Photodiode (APD)** is a semiconductor photodetector that employs **impact ionization** within a high-field multiplication region to provide internal gain. The absorbed photon first generates a primary electron-hole pair in the absorption region. The carrier is then injected into the high-field multiplication region, where, upon acquiring kinetic energy greater than the **threshold energy** $E_{th} \approx 1.5 E_{g}$, it collides with the lattice and ionizes a bound electron, creating a **secondary e-h pair**. This process repeats, producing an **avalanche cascade** with multiplication factor $M = 1/(1 - \int \alpha\, dx)$. Thus, impact ionization is the physical mechanism that converts a single photogenerated carrier into M carriers, providing the **internal gain** that distinguishes APDs from PIN photodiodes.

### Q2. `[KTU University Exam – June 2024, Module 3]`
**Explain the term "ionization coefficient ratio k" and state its significance in APD design.**

**Model Answer:**

> The **ionization coefficient ratio** is defined as $k = \beta/\alpha$, where $\alpha$ and $\beta$ are the electron and hole impact ionization coefficients respectively. It quantifies the **asymmetry** between electron- and hole-initiated avalanche multiplication. A **small value of k** (e.g., 0.1 for InP) means that **electrons dominate the ionization process**, leading to a more deterministic and lower-noise avalanche. The **McIntyre excess noise factor** $F(M) = kM + (2 - 1/M)(1-k)$ is directly proportional to k, so choosing a material with low k is the primary design strategy for minimizing excess noise. In Si, $k \approx 0.02$, which is why Si APDs are exceptionally quiet, while Ge APDs ($k \approx 1$) are inherently noisier.

---

## 5.2 Part B — ESE Long Answer Questions (14 Marks — Internal Choice Provided)

### Question A (14 Marks)

**[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Module 3]**

**(a)** With a neat energy-band diagram, describe the **device structure and operation of a SAM-APD** (Separate Absorption and Multiplication Avalanche Photodiode). Explain why III-V compound semiconductors like InGaAs/InP are preferred over silicon for long-haul optical communication APDs. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Derive the expression for the **multiplication factor M** for an electron-initiated avalanche in a uniform-field multiplication region. What is the **breakdown condition** and how does it relate to M? **(7 Marks)**

#### Model Solution to Part (a):

**1. Structure of a SAM-APD (4 marks):**

A SAM-APD consists of four distinct regions grown epitaxially on an **InP substrate**:

| Layer | Material | Function |
|---|---|---|
| P$^{+}$ contact | InP (p-doped) | Ohmic contact, wide $E_{g}$ window |
| Multiplication (i) | InP (undoped) | High-field avalanche region |
| Absorption (i) | InGaAs (undoped) | Photon absorption (narrow $E_{g}$) |
| N$^{+}$ buffer | InP (n-doped) | Substrate / contact |

A **heterojunction** exists between InP and InGaAs. Under reverse bias, the entire i-region is fully depleted, producing a **uniform high field** in the InP multiplication zone and a **lower uniform field** in the InGaAs absorption zone.

**2. Operation (2 marks):**

Photons enter through the wide-bandgap InP window (transparent at 1550 nm) and are absorbed in the **InGaAs** layer, generating e-h pairs. The **electrons** drift into the **InP multiplication region** where the higher electric field initiates the avalanche. Holes are confined to the absorption region by the valence-band discontinuity $\Delta E_{v}$, preventing them from injecting into the multiplication zone and reducing excess noise.

**3. Why InGaAs/InP over Si? (1 mark):**

- **Wavelength match:** InGaAs $E_g = 0.75$ eV is ideal for the **1.3 µm and 1.55 µm** low-loss fibre-optic windows; Si ($E_g = 1.12$ eV) absorbs only below ~1.1 µm.
- **Low ionization ratio k ≈ 0.1** → low excess noise.
- **Heterojunction flexibility** allows independent optimization of absorption and multiplication regions.

#### Model Solution to Part (b):

**1. Derivation (5 marks):**

Consider a uniform-field multiplication region of width $w$. Let $n(x)$ and $p(x)$ be the electron and hole densities, and $J_{n}, J_{p}$ the corresponding current densities. The continuity equations with impact ionization are:

$$\frac{1}{q}\frac{dJ_{n}}{dx} = \alpha J_{n} + \beta J_{p}$$

For pure **electron injection** at $x = 0$: $J_{p}(0) = 0$ and $J_{n}(0) = J_{0}$.

By total current continuity: $J_{n}(x) + J_{p}(x) = J_{T}$ (constant).

Eliminating $J_{p} = J_{T} - J_{n}$:

$$\frac{dJ_{n}}{dx} = (\alpha - \beta) J_{n} + \beta J_{T}$$

This is a first-order linear ODE. With constant $\alpha, \beta$ and defining $\delta = \alpha - \beta$:

$$J_{n}(x) = \frac{\beta J_{T}}{\delta}\left(e^{\delta x} - 1\right) + J_{n}(0)\, e^{\delta x}$$

At $x = w$ (boundary condition $J_{p}(w) = 0$, i.e., $J_{n}(w) = J_{T}$):

$$J_{T} = \frac{\beta J_{T}}{\delta}(e^{\delta w} - 1) + J_{0}\, e^{\delta w}$$

Solving for $M = J_{T}/J_{0}$:

$$M = \frac{e^{\alpha w}}{1 - \int_{0}^{w}\alpha\, dx \cdot \left(\dfrac{\beta}{\alpha}\right)\left(\dfrac{e^{(\alpha - \beta)w} - 1}{\alpha - \beta}\right)/w}$$

For the simpler **single-carrier case** (electron-only ionization, $\beta = 0$):

$$\boxed{\,M = \frac{1}{1 - \int_{0}^{w} \alpha(x)\, dx}\,}$$

**2. Breakdown Condition (2 marks):**

Avalanche **breakdown** occurs when $M \to \infty$, i.e., when the **denominator vanishes**:

$$\boxed{\,\int_{0}^{w} \alpha(x)\, dx = 1\,}$$

Physically, this means that on average, **every electron traversing the multiplication region generates exactly one new electron** — runaway positive feedback.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Internal Choice Alternative

**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Module 3]**

**(a)** What is the **McIntyre excess noise factor** $F(M)$? Derive its dependence on M and the ionization coefficient ratio k. Discuss the implications for material selection in APD design. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** An InGaAs APD operating at **1550 nm** has a quantum efficiency of **80 %** and is illuminated with optical power of **100 nW**. If the multiplication factor M = 15, calculate the **primary photocurrent**, **multiplied photocurrent**, and the **responsivity** of the device. **(7 Marks)**

#### Model Solution to Part (a):

**1. Definition (2 marks):**

The **McIntyre excess noise factor** quantifies the additional noise introduced by the stochastic (random) nature of impact ionization. It is defined as:

$$F(M) = \frac{\langle M^{2} \rangle}{\langle M \rangle^{2}}$$

where the averaging is over the random multiplication events.

**2. Derivation (3 marks):**

Each primary carrier generates a random number of secondary carriers through a branching Poisson process. The recursion for the variance:

$$\langle M^{2} \rangle = M^{2}\!\left[k M + \left(2 - \frac{1}{M}\right)(1-k)\right]$$

Hence:

$$\boxed{\,F(M) = k M + \left(2 - \frac{1}{M}\right)(1-k)\,}$$

For $M \gg 1$: $F(M) \approx k M + 2(1-k) \approx k M$.

**3. Material Implications (2 marks):**

- **Small k (Si: 0.02)** → $F(M)$ grows slowly → **low-noise** APD.
- **Large k (Ge: ~1)** → $F(M) \approx M$ → **high noise** — limits Ge APD gain.
- **Compound semiconductors (InP, InAlAs)** with k ≈ 0.1–0.4 offer the best compromise for fibre-optic APDs.

#### Model Solution to Part (b):

**Given:** $\lambda = 1550\ \text{nm}$, $\eta = 0.80$, $P_{\text{opt}} = 100\ \text{nW} = 10^{-7}\ \text{W}$, $M = 15$.

**Step 1 — Primary photocurrent (3 marks):**

The number of incident photons per second:

$$N_{\text{ph}} = \frac{P_{\text{opt}}}{h\nu} = \frac{P_{\text{opt}} \lambda}{h c} = \frac{(10^{-7})(1550 \times 10^{-9})}{(6.626 \times 10^{-34})(3 \times 10^{8})}$$

$$N_{\text{ph}} = \frac{1.55 \times 10^{-13}}{1.988 \times 10^{-25}} = 7.797 \times 10^{11}\ \text{photons/s}$$

Primary photocurrent:

$$I_{0} = q \eta N_{\text{ph}} = (1.602 \times 10^{-19})(0.80)(7.797 \times 10^{11})$$

$$\boxed{I_{0} \approx 9.99 \times 10^{-8}\ \text{A} \approx 99.9\ \text{nA}}$$

**Step 2 — Multiplied photocurrent (2 marks):**

$$I_{p} = M \cdot I_{0} = 15 \times 99.9\ \text{nA}$$

$$\boxed{I_{p} \approx 1.50\ \mu\text{A}}$$

**Step 3 — Responsivity (2 marks):**

$$R_{\text{APD}} = \frac{I_{p}}{P_{\text{opt}}} = \frac{1.50 \times 10^{-6}}{10^{-7}}$$

$$\boxed{R_{\text{APD}} = 15\ \text{A/W}}$$

**Verification (1 mark):** Without gain ($M=1$), $R_{\text{PIN}} = I_{0}/P_{\text{opt}} = 99.9\ \text{nA}/100\ \text{nW} = 0.999\ \text{A/W}$. The APD provides **15× higher responsivity** than a PIN at the same wavelength — a 11.76 dB sensitivity improvement.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Pitfalls in APD Questions:**
> 1. **Confusing M (multiplication factor) with η (quantum efficiency).** They are entirely different — η relates to photon-to-electron conversion probability; M relates to avalanche gain.
> 2. **Drawing a PIN structure instead of a SAM structure** when asked for an APD diagram. Always include the **heterojunction** and **separate absorption/multiplication regions**.
> 3. **Forgetting the threshold energy condition** $E_{th} \approx 1.5 E_g$ when explaining impact ionization — this is a favourite 1-mark follow-up.
> 4. **Using $F(M) = kM$ without the constant term** $(2 - 1/M)(1-k)$. The full McIntyre expression is expected.
> 5. **Incorrect sign of M in the breakdown expression.** Some students write $M = 1 - \int \alpha dx$, which gives a *negative* M — the correct form is $M = 1/(1 - \int \alpha dx)$.
> 6. **Forgetting units** in the formula sheet — KTU examiners deduct half a mark for missing units like A/W, Hz, or cm$^{-1}$.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Rapid-Revision Checklist

> 📋 **High-Density Revision Points — Avalanche Photodiodes**

- ☐ **APD = PIN + High-Field Multiplication Region.** Internal gain is via **impact ionization**.
- ☐ The **multiplication factor** is $M = 1/(1 - \int_{0}^{w} \alpha(x)\, dx)$.
- ☐ **Breakdown** occurs when $\int_{0}^{w} \alpha\, dx = 1$ (M → ∞).
- ☐ **Impact ionization threshold energy** $E_{th} \approx 1.5 E_{g}$.
- ☐ **Ionization coefficient ratio** $k = \beta/\alpha$ — smaller is better.
- ☐ **McIntyre excess noise factor:** $F(M) = kM + (2 - 1/M)(1-k)$.
- ☐ **Responsivity:** $R_{\text{APD}} = (q \lambda \eta / h c) \cdot M$.
- ☐ **SAM-APD** = Separate Absorption (InGaAs) and Multiplication (InP) — standard for 1310/1550 nm.
- ☐ **SAGCM-APD** adds grading + charge layers for high-speed operation (≥ 10 Gbps).
- ☐ **SPAD** (Geiger mode) operates *above* breakdown — needs quenching circuitry.
- ☐ InP: $k \approx 0.1$ → low noise. Si: $k \approx 0.02$ → ultra-low noise. Ge: $k \approx 1$ → high noise.
- ☐ APDs provide **5–10 dB receiver sensitivity gain** over PIN photodiodes.
- ☐ **Bandwidth–gain trade-off:** $f_{3dB} \propto 1/M$.
- ☐ Always state the **operating wavelength** when quoting responsivity.
- ☐ **Heterojunction band offsets** $\Delta E_c, \Delta E_v$ confine carriers to the correct region.
- ☐ **Breakdown voltage** $V_{BR} = \varepsilon_{s} E_{crit}^{2}/(2qN)$.

> **End of Module 3 Notes — Avalanche Photodiodes**
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Optical Sources: LEDs - Principle of Operation of Homojunction and Heterojunction LEDs, LED Materials
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEEVT413 - COMPOUND SEMICONDUCTORS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Optical Sources: LEDs - Principle of Operation of Homojunction and Heterojunction LEDs, LED Materials

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Light Emitting Diode (LED) — Formal Definition

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Definition (Board-Exam Approved):**
> A **Light Emitting Diode (LED)** is a **p–n junction semiconductor optoelectronic device** that converts **forward-biased electrical current directly into incoherent, narrow-spectrum light** through the process of **spontaneous (radiative) recombination** of injected electrons and holes across the bandgap of the active region.

The fundamental governing relationship is **photon energy equals semiconductor bandgap energy:**

$$E_{photon} = h\nu = \frac{hc}{\lambda} = E_g$$

Solving for the emitted wavelength:

$$\lambda(\mu m) = \frac{1.240}{E_g(eV)}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Highlight:** The wavelength (and therefore color) of light emitted by an LED is **NOT** chosen by doping — it is dictated **exclusively by the bandgap ($E_g$)** of the active-region compound semiconductor. This is why **compound semiconductors** (rather than silicon) are essential for LEDs across the visible spectrum.

## 1.2 Electroluminescence — The Physical Mechanism

**Electroluminescence** is the non-thermal generation of light by a semiconductor under an applied electric field/current. In an LED:

1. **Forward bias** lowers the built-in potential barrier of the p–n junction.
2. **Electrons** from the n-side are injected into the active (depletion) region.
3. **Holes** from the p-side are simultaneously injected.
4. **Radiative recombination** ($e^- + h^+ \rightarrow h\nu$) of electron–hole pairs releases photons whose energy matches $E_g$.

Two recombination pathways compete:

| Recombination Type | Outcome | Effect on Efficiency |
|---|---|---|
| **Radiative (band-to-band)** | Emits photon of energy $E_g$ | Desired — produces light |
| **Non-radiative (SRH / Auger)** | Emits phonons (heat) | Harmful — reduces efficiency |

## 1.3 Intuitive Analogy — The "Ball-in-Hole Waterfall"

> [!TIP]
> **Conceptual Analogy (Waterfall Model):**
> Imagine a **waterfall** between two lakes. The **upper lake** = conduction band (electrons). The **lower lake** = valence band (holes). When the waterfall is "switched on" (forward bias), water (electrons) tumbles down. When a water droplet strikes the lower lake, it produces a **splash of light** (a photon).
> - **Tall waterfall** (large $E_g$) → high-energy (blue/UV) photons.
> - **Short waterfall** (small $E_g$) → low-energy (red/IR) photons.
> - Some splashes are absorbed back into the cliff (re-absorption) — this is the **homojunction** problem.

This single mental picture should guide your understanding of **all** LED physics — homojunction, heterojunction, and material selection.

## 1.4 KTU 2024 Classification of Optical Sources

> [!IMPORTANT]
> LEDs belong to the class of **incoherent optical sources** (as opposed to LASERs, which are coherent). The KTU 2024 syllabus categorizes them under **optoelectronic devices** alongside laser diodes (LDs) and photodetectors, but distinguishes them by the **absence of stimulated emission, optical cavity, and population inversion**.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Detailed Operating Principle of an LED (Step-by-Step Logic)

**Step 1 — Zero-Bias Equilibrium:**
At thermal equilibrium, the **Fermi level ($E_F$)** is constant throughout the p–n junction. A **built-in potential** $V_{bi}$ exists, given by:

$$V_{bi} = \frac{k_BT}{q}\ln\left(\frac{N_A N_D}{n_i^2}\right)$$

where $N_A, N_D$ are acceptor/donor concentrations and $n_i$ is the intrinsic carrier concentration.

**Step 2 — Forward Bias Application:**
An external voltage $V_F$ reduces the barrier from $V_{bi}$ to $V_{bi} - V_F$. This allows:
- Electron injection from n-side (diffusion across the junction).
- Hole injection from p-side (diffusion across the junction).

**Step 3 — Carrier Injection and Spontaneous Recombination:**
The injected minority carrier concentration at the edge of the depletion region is:

$$\Delta n = \frac{n_i^2}{N_D}\left(e^{qV_F/k_BT} - 1\right) \quad \text{(for the n-side)}$$

**Step 4 — Radiative Recombination:**
The radiative recombination rate (per unit volume) is:

$$R_{rad} = B(np - n_i^2)$$

where $B$ is the **radiative recombination coefficient** — a material-dependent constant (high in direct-bandgap materials like GaAs, low in indirect materials like Si).

**Step 5 — Photon Emission and Escape:**
A photon is emitted with probability $h\nu \approx E_g$. The photon must travel to the surface and escape (without total internal reflection).

## 2.2 Internal Quantum Efficiency ($\eta_{int}$)

The fraction of injected carriers that produce photons inside the device:

$$\eta_{int} = \frac{R_{rad}}{R_{rad} + R_{nr}} = \frac{1}{1 + \frac{\tau_{rad}}{\tau_{nr}}}$$

where $\tau_{rad}$ and $\tau_{nr}$ are the radiative and non-radiative carrier lifetimes.

## 2.3 External Quantum Efficiency ($\eta_{ext}$) and Extraction Efficiency

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Critical Concept:** Most photons generated inside the LED are **reabsorbed** or suffer **total internal reflection (TIR)** at the semiconductor–air interface. Only a small fraction escapes.

$$\eta_{ext} = \eta_{int} \times \eta_{extraction}$$

The critical angle for TIR (Snell's Law):

$$\sin\theta_c = \frac{n_{air}}{n_{semiconductor}} = \frac{1}{n}$$

For GaAs ($n \approx 3.5$), only the escape cone of solid angle $\sim 1/(2n^2) \approx 4\%$ of isotropically emitted photons can escape.

## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| # | Quantity | Formula | Units | Key Note |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Photon energy | $E = h\nu = hc/\lambda$ | eV / J | Planck–Einstein relation |
| 2 | Emitted wavelength | $\lambda = 1.240/E_g$ | $\mu$m | $E_g$ in eV |
| 3 | Built-in potential | $V_{bi} = (k_BT/q)\ln(N_A N_D/n_i^2)$ | V | Depends on doping |
| 4 | Minority carrier injection | $\Delta n = (n_i^2/N_D)(e^{qV/k_BT} - 1)$ | cm$^{-3}$ | Law of junction |
| 5 | Radiative recombination rate | $R_{rad} = B(np - n_i^2)$ | cm$^{-3}$s$^{-1}$ | $B$ is material constant |
| 6 | Internal quantum efficiency | $\eta_{int} = \tau_{nr}/(\tau_{rad} + \tau_{nr})$ | dimensionless | Direct gap → high |
| 7 | External efficiency | $\eta_{ext} = \eta_{int} \cdot \eta_{extraction}$ | dimensionless | TIR limits extraction |
| 8 | Critical angle (TIR) | $\sin\theta_c = 1/n$ | dimensionless | $n \approx 3.5$ for GaAs |
| 9 | Total current | $I = I_0(e^{qV/k_BT} - 1)$ | A | Standard diode equation |
| 10 | Output optical power | $P_{opt} = \eta_{ext} \cdot (h\nu/q) \cdot I$ | W | Linear in current |

## 2.5 Direct vs. Indirect Bandgap — Why Compound Semiconductors?

> [!NOTE]
> **Syllabus Anchor (PEEVT413 — Compound Semiconductors):**
> LEDs **require direct bandgap** semiconductors because radiative recombination **conserves momentum** only when the conduction band minimum and valence band maximum occur at the **same $k$-value** in the Brillouin zone.

| Material | Type | Bandgap | Suitable for LED? |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Si** | Indirect | 1.12 eV | ❌ Poor — phonon-assisted |
| **GaAs** | Direct | 1.42 eV | ✅ Excellent — IR LED |
| **InP** | Direct | 1.35 eV | ✅ Excellent — fiber optics |
| **GaP** | Indirect | 2.26 eV | ⚠ With N-doping — green/red |
| **GaN** | Direct | 3.4 eV | ✅ Excellent — blue/UV LED |
| **SiC** | Indirect | 3.26 eV | ⚠ Blue (low efficiency) |

## 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

LEDs are the workhorses of:
