- **Optical fiber communication** (1.3 $\mu$m and 1.55 $\mu$m — InGaAsP/InP)
- **Solid-state lighting** (GaN-based white LEDs — Nobel Prize 2014)
- **Display technology** (RGB micro-LEDs, InGaN/AlGaInP)
- **Sensors and medical devices** (pulse oximetry, optogenetics)
- **Automotive lighting, traffic signals, indicators**

The **engineering goal** is to maximize $\eta_{ext}$ (typically 30–70% in modern devices) while minimizing thermal losses.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Emitted Wavelength from Bandgap (Energy–Wavelength Conversion)

**Given:** The photon emitted during radiative recombination has energy $E_{photon} = E_g$.

**Step 1 — Planck's relation for photon energy:**

$$E_{photon} = h\nu = \frac{hc}{\lambda}$$

**Step 2 — Rearranging for wavelength:**

$$\lambda = \frac{hc}{E_g}$$

**Step 3 — Substituting numerical values:**
$h = 6.626 \times 10^{-34}$ J·s, $c = 3 \times 10^8$ m/s, $1\text{ eV} = 1.602 \times 10^{-19}$ J

$$\lambda = \frac{(6.626 \times 10^{-34})(3 \times 10^8)}{E_g \times 1.602 \times 10^{-19}}$$

**Step 4 — Simplification:**

$$\lambda = \frac{1.988 \times 10^{-25}}{1.602 \times 10^{-19} \cdot E_g} = (1.240 \times 10^{-6}) \cdot \frac{1}{E_g(eV)} \text{ m}$$

**Step 5 — Final compact form:**

$$\boxed{\lambda(\mu m) = \frac{1.240}{E_g(eV)}}$$

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Internal Quantum Efficiency

**Given:** A fraction of total recombination events are radiative (light-producing), and the rest are non-radiative (heat-producing).

**Step 1 — Define total recombination rate:**
$R_{total} = R_{rad} + R_{nr} = \frac{\Delta n}{\tau_{rad}} + \frac{\Delta n}{\tau_{nr}} = \Delta n \left(\frac{1}{\tau_{rad}} + \frac{1}{\tau_{nr}}\right)$

**Step 2 — Define internal quantum efficiency (radiative fraction):**

$$\eta_{int} = \frac{R_{rad}}{R_{total}} = \frac{\Delta n / \tau_{rad}}{\Delta n(1/\tau_{rad} + 1/\tau_{nr})}$$

**Step 3 — Cancel $\Delta n$:**

$$\eta_{int} = \frac{1/\tau_{rad}}{1/\tau_{rad} + 1/\tau_{nr}}$$

**Step 4 — Multiply numerator and denominator by $\tau_{rad}\tau_{nr}$:**

$$\eta_{int} = \frac{\tau_{nr}}{\tau_{nr} + \tau_{rad}}$$

**Step 5 — Engineering insight:**
For high $\eta_{int}$, the **non-radiative lifetime $\tau_{nr}$ must be much larger than $\tau_{rad}$** — i.e., non-radiative channels (defects, Auger) must be suppressed. This is achieved by:
- Using **high-purity epitaxial growth** (MOCVD, MBE)
- Minimizing **dislocations and impurities**
- Selecting **direct-bandgap materials** (short $\tau_{rad}$)

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Escape Cone Fraction (Extraction Efficiency Limit)

**Given:** Photons are emitted isotropically inside the semiconductor of refractive index $n$. Only those within the critical cone escape.

**Step 1 — Critical angle from Snell's law (TIR condition):**
At the semiconductor–air interface ($n_1 = n, n_2 = 1$):

$$\sin\theta_c = \frac{n_2}{n_1} = \frac{1}{n}$$

**Step 2 — Fraction of photons in the escape cone (solid-angle ratio):**

$$\eta_{escape} = \frac{1}{2}\left(1 - \cos\theta_c\right)$$

**Step 3 — For small $\theta_c$ (large $n$), $\cos\theta_c \approx 1 - \theta_c^2/2$:**

$$\eta_{escape} \approx \frac{\theta_c^2}{4} \approx \frac{\sin^2\theta_c}{4} = \frac{1}{4n^2}$$

**Step 4 — For GaAs ($n = 3.5$):**

$$\eta_{escape} = \frac{1}{4 \times (3.5)^2} = \frac{1}{49} \approx 2.0\%$$

**Step 5 — Engineering implication:**
This is why **homojunction LEDs are very inefficient** — most photons are reabsorbed or trapped. Heterojunction design (and domed encapsulation) overcomes this.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Block Diagram — Generic LED Functional Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    A[DC Power Supply V_F] --> B[Current Limiting Resistor R]
    B --> C[P-N Junction LED Chip]
    C -->|Forward Bias| D[Electron Hole Injection into Active Region]
    D --> E{Radiative Recombination}
    E -->|Energy E_g| F[Photon Emission h nu]
    E -->|Defects and Traps| G[Non Radiative Heat Loss]
    F --> H[Escape Cone Exit Window]
    H --> I[Output Light Beam]
    C --> J[Thermal Heat Sink]
    J --> K[Ambient Cooling]
```

## 4.2 Mermaid Block Diagram — Homojunction LED Cross-Section

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    P1[P+ Region Wide Gap GaAs] --> J1[Depletion Region Active Layer GaAs]
    J1 --> N1[N Region GaAs]
    N1 --> OH1[Ohmic Contact N side]
    P1 --> OH2[Ohmic Contact P side]
    PH1[Photon Emission and Reabsorption Loss] -.-> J1
    PH1 -.-> N1
```

## 4.3 Mermaid Block Diagram — Heterojunction LED (DH LED) Cross-Section

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    P2[P+ AlGaAs Wide Gap Cladding] --> A1[Active Region GaAs Narrow Gap]
    A1 --> N2[N AlGaAs Wide Gap Cladding]
    N2 --> OC1[Ohmic Contact N]
    P2 --> OC2[Ohmic Contact P]
    HET1[Heterojunction Barrier Confines Carriers] -.-> P2
    HET1 -.-> A1
    HET1 -.-> N2
    PH2[Photon Emission and Window Effect] -.-> A1
    PH2 -.-> OC2
```

## 4.4 Mermaid Comparison Matrix — Homojunction vs. Heterojunction LED

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph HJ[Homojunction LED]
        HJ1[Same Material Both Sides Eg] --> HJ2[No Carrier Confinement]
        HJ2 --> HJ3[Active Region and Cladding Same Gap]
        HJ3 --> HJ4[Reabsorption Loss High]
        HJ4 --> HJ5[Low External Efficiency approx 1 to 2 percent]
    end

    subgraph HEJ[Heterojunction Double Hetero LED]
        HEJ1[Wide Gap Cladding p and n] --> HEJ2[Active Region Narrow Gap]
        HEJ2 --> HEJ3[Hetero Barrier Confinement]
        HEJ3 --> HEJ4[Window Effect Wide Gap Top]
        HEJ4 --> HEJ5[High External Efficiency 10 to 50 percent]
    end
```

## 4.5 Mermaid Block Diagram — LED Material Selection Tree by Wavelength

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    START[Choose Target Wavelength lambda] --> A{Visible or IR}
    A -->|IR 1.3 to 1.55 micrometer| B[InGaAsP on InP]
    A -->|Red 620 to 700 nm| C[AlGaInP or AlGaAs on GaAs]
    A -->|Yellow Orange 570 to 620 nm| D[AlGaInP on GaAs]
    A -->|Green 495 to 570 nm| E[GaP N doped or InGaN]
    A -->|Blue UV 400 to 495 nm| F[InGaN on SiC or Sapphire]
    B --> OUT[LED Chip]
    C --> OUT
    D --> OUT
    E --> OUT
    F --> OUT
```

## 4.6 Mermaid Block Diagram — Carrier Confinement Mechanism in DH LED

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    ELEC[Electrons from N AlGaAs] -->|Diffusion| ACT[Active GaAs Region]
    HOLES[Holes from P AlGaAs] -->|Diffusion| ACT
    ACT --> REC[Radiative Recombination E approx Eg]
    REC --> PHOTON[Photon of Energy Eg]
    PHOTON -->|Transparent to Wide Gap| ESC[Escape through P AlGaAs Window]
    ACT -.->|Blocked by Hetero Barrier| ELEC
    ACT -.->|Blocked by Hetero Barrier| HOLES
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **Q1. [KTU University Exam — June 2024, Model Question Bank]**
> **Define electroluminescence. Why is a direct bandgap semiconductor essential for efficient LED operation?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Definition (1.5 marks):** Electroluminescence is the phenomenon of **non-thermal light emission** from a semiconductor material when an **electric current** (or field) is passed through it, caused by **radiative recombination** of electrons and holes across the bandgap.

**Direct bandgap necessity (1.5 marks):** In a **direct bandgap** material, the conduction band minimum and valence band maximum occur at the **same crystal momentum ($k$)**. This means electron–hole recombination **does not require a phonon** to conserve momentum, and the radiative transition probability (governed by $\tau_{rad}$) is **high** ($\sim$ ns). In indirect bandgap materials (Si, Ge), a **phonon-assisted** transition is required, dramatically lowering radiative efficiency. Hence, **direct bandgap compound semiconductors** (GaAs, InP, GaN, InGaAsP) are mandatory for LEDs.

---

> **Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Retest Pattern]**
> **State and explain the relationship between emitted wavelength and bandgap energy of an LED.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The emitted photon energy equals the bandgap:

$$E_{photon} = h\nu = \frac{hc}{\lambda} = E_g$$

Solving:

$$\lambda(\mu m) = \frac{1.240}{E_g(eV)}$$

(2 marks for equation + 1 mark for numerical implication: e.g., for GaAs $E_g = 1.42$ eV → $\lambda \approx 0.87\ \mu$m — IR region used in remote controls and short-range fiber optics).

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks — KTU ESE Module Internal Choice)

> ### Question A (14 Marks) — Homojunction LED Analysis
> **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Retest Pattern]**
>
> **(a)** With a neat energy band diagram, explain the **principle of operation of a homojunction LED**. Discuss its **structure**, **carrier recombination mechanism**, and **limitations**. **(7 marks)**
>
> **(b)** A **GaAs homojunction LED** has $E_g = 1.42$ eV. Calculate (i) the **emitted wavelength**, (ii) the **frequency** of emission, and (iii) the **critical angle** for total internal reflection (take $n_{GaAs} = 3.5$). Comment on the **extraction efficiency**. **(7 marks)**

### Model Solution — Question A

#### Part (a) — Homojunction LED Principle (7 marks)

**Structure (1.5 marks):** A homojunction LED uses a **p–n junction formed in a single semiconductor material** (e.g., GaAs). The p-region is doped p$^+$ (heavily) and the n-region is moderately doped n-type. Ohmic contacts are made on both sides. Under forward bias, current flows and light is emitted from the **depletion (active) region** near the metallurgical junction.

**Energy band diagram and operation (3.5 marks):**

Under forward bias $V_F$, the built-in barrier $V_{bi}$ is reduced. Electrons from the n-side and holes from the p-side are injected into the depletion region where they **recombine radiatively**, emitting photons of energy $E_g$. Since both p and n regions have the **same bandgap**, there is **no energy barrier** to confine carriers — they can diffuse out of the active region before recombining. The emitted photons can also be **reabsorbed** by the surrounding GaAs material (band-to-band absorption) since the photon energy matches $E_g$ everywhere.

**Limitations (2 marks):**
1. **No carrier confinement** — carriers diffuse away from the active region, reducing radiative recombination efficiency.
2. **Photon reabsorption** — emitted photons are absorbed in the surrounding bulk material.
3. **No optical window** — emitted light is attenuated before reaching the surface.
4. **Low external efficiency** ($\eta_{ext} \approx 1$–$2\%$) — primarily due to TIR and reabsorption.
5. **Wide emission spectrum** — no wavelength selectivity.

#### Part (b) — Numerical Computation (7 marks)

**Given:** $E_g = 1.42$ eV, $n_{GaAs} = 3.5$.

**(i) Emitted wavelength (2 marks):**

$$\lambda = \frac{1.240}{E_g} = \frac{1.240}{1.42} = 0.873\ \mu m = 873\ nm$$

This is in the **near-infrared** region (suitable for short-range optical communication and IR sensors).

**(ii) Frequency of emission (2 marks):**

$$\nu = \frac{c}{\lambda} = \frac{3 \times 10^8}{0.873 \times 10^{-6}} = 3.44 \times 10^{14}\ Hz$$

**(iii) Critical angle (1.5 marks):**

$$\sin\theta_c = \frac{1}{n} = \frac{1}{3.5} = 0.2857$$

$$\theta_c = \sin^{-1}(0.2857) \approx 16.6^\circ$$

**Comment on extraction efficiency (1.5 marks):** The **escape cone** is extremely narrow ($\theta_c \approx 16.6^\circ$). The fraction of photons that can escape is:

$$\eta_{escape} \approx \frac{1}{4n^2} = \frac{1}{4(3.5)^2} = \frac{1}{49} \approx 2.04\%$$

So **~98% of generated photons are trapped** by total internal reflection — this is the fundamental limitation of homojunction LEDs and motivates the heterojunction design.

---

> ### Question B (14 Marks) — Heterojunction LED Analysis
> **[KTU University Exam — June 2024, Retest Pattern]**
>
> **(a)** With a neat energy band diagram, explain the **principle of operation of a Double Heterojunction (DH) LED**. Highlight the **carrier confinement** and **optical confinement** effects. **(7 marks)**
>
> **(b)** Discuss the **important compound semiconductor materials** used for LEDs in (i) the visible (red, green, blue) spectrum, and (ii) the **fiber-optic communication** wavelength windows (0.85, 1.3, 1.55 $\mu$m). Justify each material choice. **(7 marks)**

### Model Solution — Question B

#### Part (a) — Double Heterojunction LED (7 marks)

**Structure (2 marks):** A **Double Heterojunction (DH) LED** consists of three layers: a **p-type wide-bandgap cladding** (e.g., AlGaAs), a **narrow-bandgap active region** (e.g., GaAs), and an **n-type wide-bandgap cladding** (e.g., AlGaAs). The active region thickness is typically **0.1–1 $\mu$m**. Two heterojunctions are formed at the p-active and n-active interfaces.

**Energy band diagram and operation (3 marks):** Under forward bias, electrons injected from the n-AlGaAs and holes from the p-AlGaAs both encounter a **potential step (hetero-barrier)** at the active layer boundary. This **confines both carrier types** inside the narrow-gap active region, dramatically increasing the **radiative recombination rate** (carrier density $\uparrow$, $\tau_{rad}\downarrow$, $\eta_{int} \uparrow$).

**Optical confinement — "window effect" (2 marks):** Since the cladding layers have a **wider bandgap than the active region**, photons of energy $E_g$(active) **cannot be absorbed** in the cladding. The wide-gap top layer acts as a **transparent optical window**, allowing photons to escape with minimal reabsorption. This boosts $\eta_{ext}$ to **10–50%** (vs. 1–2% in homojunction LEDs).

**Advantages summary:** Higher $\eta_{int}$ (carrier confinement) + higher $\eta_{extraction}$ (optical window) + narrower emission spectrum + faster modulation.

#### Part (b) — LED Materials Across the Spectrum (7 marks)

| Spectral Range | Material System | $E_g$ (eV) | $\lambda$ (nm) | Justification |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Red (620–700 nm)** | **AlGaInP / AlGaAs on GaAs** | 1.8–2.0 | 620–690 | Direct gap, lattice-matched to GaAs, high $\eta_{int}$, low cost. Used in traffic lights, displays. |
| **Yellow/Orange (570–620 nm)** | **AlGaInP on GaAs** | 2.0–2.2 | 570–620 | Direct gap, alloy bandgap tuning via Al/In composition. |
| **Green (495–570 nm)** | **InGaN** (or GaP:N) | 2.3–2.5 | 495–570 | InGaN alloy — In composition tunes gap. Direct gap enables bright green. |
| **Blue (450–495 nm)** | **InGaN / GaN on Sapphire or SiC** | 2.6–2.8 | 450–495 | Direct gap; wide bandgap GaN family. Nobel Prize 2014 (Nakamura, Akasaki, Amano). |
| **UV (<400 nm)** | **AlGaN / GaN** | 3.0–3.4 | 360–400 | Al composition widens gap. Used in sterilization, curing. |
| **0.85 $\mu$m fiber window** | **AlGaAs / GaAs** | ~1.46 | 850 | First-generation fiber optics; cheap, well-established. |
| **1.3 $\mu$m fiber window** | **InGaAsP / InP** | ~0.95 | 1310 | Zero-dispersion window of silica fiber. Lattice-matched to InP. |
| **1.55 $\mu$m fiber window** | **InGaAsP / InP** | ~0.80 | 1550 | Lowest-loss window of silica fiber (0.2 dB/km). |

**Justification principles (1 mark):**
- **Bandgap selects wavelength** ($\lambda = 1.240/E_g$).
- **Lattice matching** to substrate (GaAs, InP, Sapphire) prevents dislocation defects.
- **Direct bandgap** is mandatory for high radiative efficiency.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes Where KTU Students Lose Marks:**
>
> 1. **Confusing $E_g$ with $h\nu$** — the bandgap is a **material property**; the photon energy equals $E_g$ only at the moment of emission. The wavelength of the *emitted* photon satisfies $\lambda = 1.240/E_g$, **not** $\lambda = 1.240/V_{bias}$.
>
> 2. **Drawing homojunction band diagrams with heterojunction features** — examiners deduct 2–3 marks if the energy bands are drawn as flat (no bandgap step) when discussing DH LEDs.
>
> 3. **Forgetting units** — always quote $\lambda$ in nm or $\mu$m and $E_g$ in eV. Marks are deducted for unit ambiguity in numerical problems.
>
> 4. **Confusing $\eta_{int}$ and $\eta_{ext}$** — internal efficiency concerns recombination only; external efficiency includes **light extraction losses** (TIR, reabsorption).
>
> 5. **Writing "Si can be used for LEDs"** — Si is **indirect** and is unsuitable. This single error can lose 1 mark immediately.
>
> 6. **Omitting the "window effect"** when describing DH LEDs — this is a **2-mark** essential concept in KTU 2024 marking schemes.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Module 3: LED Optical Sources**

- ✅ **LED = forward-biased p–n junction** emitting light by **spontaneous (radiative) recombination** of electron–hole pairs.
- ✅ **Governing equation:** $\lambda(\mu m) = 1.240 / E_g(eV)$.
- ✅ LED is an **incoherent** optical source (no stimulated emission, no cavity, no population inversion).
- ✅ **Direct bandgap** is mandatory — compound semiconductors (GaAs, InP, GaN, InGaAsP) are required.
- ✅ **Homojunction LED:** same material on both sides; **no carrier confinement**; suffers from **reabsorption**; $\eta_{ext} \approx 1$–$2\%$.
- ✅ **Heterojunction (DH) LED:** wide-gap cladding + narrow-gap active region; provides **both** carrier confinement and optical window effect; $\eta_{ext} \approx 10$–$50\%$.
- ✅ **TIR critical angle:** $\sin\theta_c = 1/n$ — for GaAs, $\theta_c \approx 16.6°$ and escape fraction $\approx 1/(4n^2) \approx 2\%$.
- ✅ **Internal efficiency** depends on ratio $\tau_{nr}/\tau_{rad}$; **external efficiency** = $\eta_{int} \times \eta_{extraction}$.
- ✅ **Red LED:** AlGaInP / AlGaAs on GaAs; **Green/Blue/UV LED:** InGaN / GaN on Sapphire; **IR fiber-optic LED:** InGaAsP / InP.
- ✅ **0.85 $\mu$m** window → GaAs/AlGaAs; **1.3 & 1.55 $\mu$m** windows → InGaAsP/InP.
- ✅ **Heterojunction key advantages** to memorize: (i) carrier confinement, (ii) optical window, (iii) narrow spectrum, (iv) high speed.
- ✅ **Always** end answers with an engineering comment on **efficiency** or **practical limitation** — this is what KTU examiners reward.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: LASER: Physics of LASER, LASER Materials, Fabry Perot Cavity LASER
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEEVT413 - COMPOUND SEMICONDUCTORS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** LASER: Physics of LASER, LASER Materials, Fabry Perot Cavity LASER

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!NOTE]
> **LASER** stands for **Light Amplification by Stimulated Emission of Radiation**. It is a quantum-electronic device that produces a highly directional, monochromatic, coherent, and intense beam of optical radiation through the process of **stimulated emission** of photons from an optically pumped active medium placed inside a resonant optical cavity.

In the strict KTU 2024 syllabus terminology, a LASER is defined as a device whose operation is founded on the principle of **population inversion** between two discrete energy levels (E₁ and E₂) of an atomic/molecular system, sustained by an external **pumping** source, and confined within an **optical resonator** (typically a Fabry–Perot cavity) that provides the necessary **positive optical feedback** to overcome cavity losses and achieve sustained coherent oscillation.

The three indispensable ingredients of any laser are therefore:
1. An **active (gain) medium** — provides amplification.
2. A **pumping mechanism** — establishes population inversion.
3. An **optical resonator (cavity)** — provides frequency selectivity and feedback.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The active medium in compound-semiconductor (III–V) lasers is a direct-bandgap p–n junction (e.g., GaAs, InGaAs, InP, GaN). When a forward bias current exceeds the **threshold current (I_th)**, **electronic population inversion** between the conduction band and the valence band is achieved and lasing commences.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine a large water tank on a hill with a small gate at the bottom. Normally, water trickles out irregularly (analogous to **spontaneous emission** — random, incoherent light from an LED). Now suppose we open a floodgate — the rush of water becomes organised, intense, and directional (analogous to **stimulated emission** — coherent, monochromatic laser light).

A better analogy: Picture a stadium wave. Random individuals standing up randomly (spontaneous emission) look chaotic. But if a "starter" (a photon) makes every person rise in unison, the wave becomes organised, amplified, and travels together (stimulated emission → coherent laser beam).

The two mirrors of the Fabry–Perot cavity act like the parallel walls of a bowling alley — they keep the "ball of light" bouncing back and forth, gaining energy on each pass, until it finally escapes through the partially reflecting mirror as a powerful, parallel beam.

### 1.3 Key Physical Constants (Bold-Highlighted)

- Speed of light in vacuum: **c = 2.998 × 10⁸ m/s**
- Planck's constant: **h = 6.626 × 10⁻³⁴ J·s**
- Boltzmann constant: **k_B = 1.381 × 10⁻²³ J/K**
- Permittivity of free space: **ε₀ = 8.854 × 10⁻¹² F/m**
- Permeability of free space: **μ₀ = 4π × 10⁻⁷ H/m**
- Electron charge: **e = 1.602 × 10⁻¹⁹ C**
- Free-electron mass: **m₀ = 9.109 × 10⁻³¹ kg**

### 1.4 Salient Properties of a Laser Beam

| Property | Description |
|---|---|
| **Monochromaticity** | Extremely narrow spectral linewidth (Δλ ≈ 10⁻⁸ to 10⁻¹² m) |
| **Directionality** | Very low beam divergence (≈ milliradians) |
| **Coherence** | Spatial and temporal coherence over long distances |
| **High Intensity / Brightness** | Power densities of MW/cm² are achievable |
| **Polarisation** | Often linearly polarised depending on cavity design |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Three Fundamental Light–Matter Interaction Processes (Einstein 1916)

For a two-level atomic system with energy levels **E₁ (lower)** and **E₂ (upper)** having statistical weights (degeneracies) **g₁** and **g₂** respectively, Einstein postulated three interaction processes:

**1. Stimulated Absorption (Upward Transition)**
An incident photon of energy **hν = E₂ − E₁** is absorbed, raising the atom from E₁ to E₂. Rate is proportional to **B₁₂ · ρ(ν) · N₁**, where ρ(ν) is the spectral energy density of the radiation field.

**2. Spontaneous Emission (E₂ → E₁)**
The excited atom decays to the lower level on its own, emitting a photon of random phase, direction, and polarisation. Rate is **A₂₁ · N₂**. This is the dominant process in conventional light sources (LEDs, bulbs).

**3. Stimulated Emission (E₂ → E₁)**
An incident photon of energy hν triggers the excited atom to drop to E₁, emitting a second photon that is **indistinguishable** (same phase, direction, frequency, polarisation) from the stimulating photon. The result is **photon multiplication / optical amplification**. Rate is **B₂₁ · ρ(ν) · N₂**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why LASER requires population inversion:** Under thermal equilibrium, the Boltzmann distribution demands **N₂ < N₁**. Stimulated absorption then dominates stimulated emission and no net optical gain is possible. To achieve net amplification, we must artificially invert the population so that **N₂ > N₁** — this is the central requirement of lasing.

### 2.2 Einstein Coefficients and Their Fundamental Relations

Einstein's three coefficients (**A₂₁, B₂₁, B₁₂**) are atomic constants. Under thermal (Planckian) equilibrium between atoms and blackbody radiation, the principle of detailed balance yields two universal relations:

$$
\frac{g_1}{g_2} \, B_{12} \;=\; B_{21}
$$

$$
\frac{A_{21}}{B_{21}} \;=\; \frac{8\pi h \nu^{3}}{c^{3}}
$$

These are **independent of temperature and atomic species**, and are derivable purely from the Planck blackbody spectrum and Boltzmann statistics. The factor **8πhν³/c³** governs the ratio of spontaneous to stimulated emission and explains why **X-ray / γ-ray lasers are inherently difficult** (very large spontaneous rate) while **microwave masers** are easier to achieve.

### 2.3 Population Inversion and the Pumping Requirement

Define the population inversion density as:

$$
\Delta N \;=\; N_{2} \;-\; \frac{g_2}{g_1}\, N_{1}
$$

For lasing, **ΔN > 0** is mandatory. Since a simple two-level system cannot be inverted (a pump that boosts atoms to E₂ equally enhances stimulated absorption back to E₂), practical lasers use **three-level** or **four-level** schemes. Compound-semiconductor diode lasers are essentially **quasi-four-level** (or even simpler) systems because the conduction and valence bands themselves provide a continuum of states.

### 2.4 Pumping Mechanisms (for Compound Semiconductors)

| Pumping Type | How it works | Used In |
|---|---|---|
| **Optical Pumping** | Flash lamp / another laser excites the medium | Ruby, Nd:YAG, Ti:Sapphire |
| **Electrical Pumping** | Forward-biased p–n junction injects carriers | **GaAs, InGaAs, InGaN diode lasers** |
| **Electron-beam Pumping** | High-energy e-beam excites semiconductor | GaAs, ZnS lasers |
| **Chemical Pumping** | Exothermic chemical reaction creates excited species | HF / CO₂ chemical lasers |

For a forward-biased compound-semiconductor p–n junction, the **injection current density J** supplies the carrier population, and the threshold condition is reached when the injected carrier density exceeds the **transparency carrier density n_tr**.

### 2.5 The Fabry–Perot Optical Resonator

A Fabry–Perot (FP) cavity is formed by two highly parallel mirrors of reflectivity **R₁** and **R₂** separated by a distance **L** filled with an active medium of refractive index **n**.

The cavity supports only those standing-wave optical modes whose round-trip phase shift is an integer multiple of **2π**:

$$
\nu_{q} \;=\; \frac{q \, c}{2 n L}, \quad q = 1, 2, 3, \dots
$$

These are the **longitudinal (axial) modes** of the laser. The frequency separation (free spectral range, FSR) between adjacent modes is:

$$
\Delta \nu_{\text{FSR}} \;=\; \frac{c}{2 n L}
$$

For a typical GaAs laser with **L = 300 μm** and **n = 3.6**, we obtain **Δν_FSR ≈ 139 GHz**, corresponding to a wavelength spacing **Δλ ≈ 0.3 nm**.

The cavity also supports **transverse electromagnetic modes (TEM_mnq)** characterised by integers (m, n, q), classified as **TEM_00 (Gaussian)** for the lowest-order mode, which is the most desirable for beam quality.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Frequently Tested Concept:** The quality factor of a Fabry–Perot cavity is
> $$ Q \;=\; \frac{\nu_{0}}{\Delta \nu_{\text{cavity}}} \;=\; \frac{2\pi n L}{\lambda \,(1 - \sqrt{R_1 R_2})} $$
> A higher Q implies a narrower linewidth, lower threshold, and better frequency selectivity.

### 2.6 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following table is the **exam-ready reference** for this module. Memorise the units and conditions.

| # | Formula / Concept | Expression | Units / Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Photon energy | $h\nu \;=\; E_2 - E_1$ | Joules (J) |
| 2 | Einstein A–B relation 1 | $g_1 B_{12} \;=\; g_2 B_{21}$ | Dimensionless ratio of degeneracies |
| 3 | Einstein A–B relation 2 | $A_{21} \;/ B_{21} \;=\; 8\pi h\nu^{3}/c^{3}$ | Spontaneous vs stimulated ratio |
| 4 | Planck spectral density | $\rho(\nu) \;=\; \dfrac{8\pi h \nu^{3}}{c^{3}}\,\dfrac{1}{e^{h\nu/k_BT}-1}$ | J·m⁻³·Hz⁻¹ |
| 5 | Boltzmann population | $N_2/N_1 \;=\; (g_2/g_1)\,e^{-h\nu/k_BT}$ | At thermal equilibrium N₂ < N₁ |
| 6 | Condition for inversion | $N_2 \;>\; (g_2/g_1)\, N_1$ | Mandatory for lasing |
| 7 | Round-trip gain condition | $R_1 R_2 \, e^{2gL} \;>\; 1$ | Steady-state oscillation criterion |
| 8 | Threshold gain coefficient | $g_{\text{th}} \;=\; \alpha + \dfrac{1}{2L}\ln\!\left(\dfrac{1}{R_1 R_2}\right)$ | m⁻¹ |
| 9 | Resonant mode frequencies | $\nu_q \;=\; qc / (2nL)$ | Hz |
| 10 | Free spectral range (FSR) | $\Delta\nu_{\text{FSR}} \;=\; c / (2nL)$ | Hz |
| 11 | Cavity finesse | $\mathcal{F} \;=\; \pi\sqrt{R_1 R_2} / (1 - R_1 R_2)$ | Dimensionless |
| 12 | Cavity Q-factor | $Q \;=\; \nu_0 / \Delta\nu \;=\; 2\pi n L \,/\, \lambda(1-\sqrt{R_1 R_2})$ | Dimensionless |
| 13 | Output power (per mirror) | $P_{\text{out}} \;=\; \tfrac{1}{2}\, h\nu \, \Phi \, (1-R) \, A$ | Watts |
| 14 | Photon flux | $\Phi \;=\; (I - I_{\text{th}})\, \eta_i / e\, h\nu$ | photons/s |
| 15 | Spontaneous linewidth (Schawlow–Townes) | $\Delta\nu_{\text{ST}} \;=\; (h\nu_0 \, \Delta\nu_c) / (2 P_{\text{out}})$ | Hz |

### 2.7 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Optical-fibre communication (1.3 μm / 1.55 μm InGaAsP / InP lasers):** backbone of the internet, long-haul telecom.
- **Compact-disc / Blu-ray readers (GaAs 780 nm, GaN 405 nm):** consumer optoelectronics.
- **Pumping solid-state & fibre lasers (808 nm GaAs stacks).**
- **LIDAR, range-finding, military directed-energy weapons.**
- **Medical surgery (CO₂, Er:YAG, diode) and dermatology.**
- **Materials processing: cutting, welding, 3-D printing (metal additive manufacturing).**
- **Quantum cryptography & atomic clocks (ultra-narrow-linewidth diode lasers).**

## SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

> [!NOTE]
> All derivations below are written in their complete, board-exam-friendly form. Each transition is annotated for examiner clarity.

### 3.1 Derivation 1 — Einstein's A–B Relation from Planck's Law

**Starting point:** Under thermal equilibrium, the net rate of upward transitions (1 → 2) must equal the net rate of downward transitions (2 → 1).

**Step 1 — Write the net transition rates.**

The total downward transition rate per unit volume is the sum of spontaneous + stimulated emissions:

$$
R_{\downarrow} \;=\; A_{21} N_2 \;+\; B_{21}\, \rho(\nu)\, N_2
$$

The upward (absorption) rate per unit volume is:

$$
R_{\uparrow} \;=\; B_{12}\, \rho(\nu)\, N_1
$$

**Step 2 — Apply detailed balance (steady state, no net accumulation).**

$$
B_{12}\, \rho(\nu)\, N_1 \;=\; A_{21} N_2 \;+\; B_{21}\, \rho(\nu)\, N_2
$$

**Step 3 — Rearrange to isolate ρ(ν).**

$$
\rho(\nu)\bigl[ B_{12} N_1 - B_{21} N_2 \bigr] \;=\; A_{21} N_2
$$

$$
\rho(\nu) \;=\; \frac{A_{21} N_2}{B_{12} N_1 - B_{21} N_2} \;=\; \frac{A_{21}}{B_{21}}\cdot\frac{1}{\dfrac{B_{12} N_1}{B_{21} N_2} - 1}
$$

**Step 4 — Substitute the Boltzmann distribution for N₂/N₁ at temperature T.**

$$
\frac{N_2}{N_1} \;=\; \frac{g_2}{g_1}\, e^{-h\nu/k_BT}
$$

**Step 5 — Use the fact that equilibrium ρ(ν) must equal Planck's spectral density.**

$$
\rho(\nu) \;=\; \frac{8\pi h \nu^{3}}{c^{3}}\cdot\frac{1}{e^{h\nu/k_BT} - 1}
$$

Equating the two expressions for ρ(ν) and matching coefficients of the term **1/(e^{hν/k_BT} − 1)** yields:

$$
\boxed{\;g_1 B_{12} \;=\; g_2 B_{21}\;}
$$

$$
\boxed{\;\frac{A_{21}}{B_{21}} \;=\; \frac{8\pi h \nu^{3}}{c^{3}}\;}
$$

**Physical Interpretation:**
- The first relation ties the **upward** and **downward** stimulated-emission coefficients through the level degeneracies.
- The second relation shows that the **spontaneous emission rate scales as ν³** — this is the reason that **microwave masers are easy to build, optical lasers are intermediate, and γ-ray lasers are extremely hard**.

---

### 3.2 Derivation 2 — Threshold Gain Condition for the Fabry–Perot Cavity

**Step 1 — Consider one round trip of a light wave inside the FP cavity.**

A light wave of intensity **I** traverses the gain medium of length **L** and is amplified by the factor **e^{gL}** (g = small-signal gain coefficient per unit length, m⁻¹). It also suffers internal absorption / scattering losses quantified by the loss coefficient **α (m⁻¹)**. The wave then reflects off mirror 2 (reflectivity **R₂**), passes through the medium again, and reflects off mirror 1 (reflectivity **R₁**).

**Step 2 — Write the round-trip multiplication factor.**

$$
I_{\text{after 1 round trip}} \;=\; I \cdot R_1 R_2 \, e^{(g - \alpha)\, 2L}
$$

**Step 3 — Steady-state oscillation requires that the round-trip factor equal unity.**

$$
R_1 R_2 \, e^{2(g - \alpha)L} \;=\; 1
$$

**Step 4 — Solve for the threshold gain coefficient g_th.**

$$
2(g_{\text{th}} - \alpha)L \;=\; -\ln(R_1 R_2) \;=\; \ln\!\left(\frac{1}{R_1 R_2}\right)
$$

$$
\boxed{\;g_{\text{th}} \;=\; \alpha \;+\; \frac{1}{2L}\,\ln\!\left(\frac{1}{R_1 R_2}\right)\;}
$$

**Step 5 — Special case: symmetric cavity (R₁ = R₂ = R).**

$$
g_{\text{th}} \;=\; \alpha \;+\; \frac{1}{L}\,\ln\!\left(\frac{1}{R}\right)
$$

**Step 6 — Engineering implication.**

To lower the threshold gain (and hence the threshold current in a diode laser), we must:
- **Reduce cavity length L** (carefully — affects mode spacing).
- **Increase mirror reflectivity R** (but trade-off: less output coupling).
- **Minimise internal losses α** by using high-quality epitaxial layers.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Examiner's Favourite Numericals:** Substituting **α = 10 cm⁻¹, L = 300 μm, R₁ = R₂ = 0.95** gives **g_th ≈ 1675 cm⁻¹**, easily achievable in GaAs heterostructures.

---

### 3.3 Derivation 3 — Longitudinal Mode Frequencies and Free Spectral Range

**Step 1 — Standing-wave condition inside the cavity.**

For a stable standing wave, the total phase accumulated in one round trip (2L optical path) must be an integer multiple of 2π:

$$
\frac{2\pi}{\lambda_{q}}\,(2nL) \;=\; 2\pi q \quad \Longrightarrow \quad q\lambda_{q} \;=\; 2nL
$$

**Step 2 — Solve for the wavelength of the q-th mode.**

$$
\lambda_{q} \;=\; \frac{2nL}{q}
$$

**Step 3 — Convert to frequency using c = νλ.**

$$
\nu_{q} \;=\; \frac{q\,c}{2nL}
$$

**Step 4 — Free spectral range (FSR) = frequency separation between adjacent modes.**

$$
\Delta\nu_{\text{FSR}} \;=\; \nu_{q+1} - \nu_{q} \;=\; \frac{c}{2nL}
$$

**Step 5 — Convert to wavelength domain (useful for KTU).**

Differentiating $\nu = c/\lambda$ gives $|d\nu| = (c/\lambda^{2})|d\lambda|$, so:

$$
\Delta\lambda_{\text{FSR}} \;=\; \frac{\lambda^{2}}{2nL}
$$

---

### 3.4 Derivation 4 — Cavity Finesse and Q-Factor

**Step 1 — Airy function for FP transmission.** The transmission of a Fabry–Perot etalon is:

$$
T(\delta) \;=\; \frac{1}{1 + F_{\text{coeff}}\sin^{2}(\delta/2)} \quad \text{where} \quad F_{\text{coeff}} \;=\; \frac{4R}{(1-R)^{2}}
$$

**Step 2 — Finesse ℱ is defined as FSR divided by the full-width at half-maximum (FWHM) of a transmission peak.**

$$
\boxed{\;\mathcal{F} \;=\; \frac{\pi\sqrt{R_1 R_2}}{1 - R_1 R_2}\;}
$$

**Step 3 — Q-factor.**

$$
Q \;=\; \frac{\nu_0}{\Delta\nu_{\text{FWHM}}} \;=\; \nu_0 \cdot \frac{\mathcal{F}}{\Delta\nu_{\text{FSR}}} \;=\; \frac{2\pi n L}{\lambda(1 - \sqrt{R_1 R_2})}
$$

**Step 4 — For high-reflectivity mirrors (R → 1), both ℱ and Q diverge, which is the basis of ultra-low-threshold VCSELs and narrow-linewidth lasers.**

---

### 3.5 Analytical Matrix — Comparison of Material Systems

| Parameter | GaAs / AlGaAs | InGaAsP / InP | GaN / InGaN |
|---|---|---|---|
| Emission wavelength | 750–880 nm | 1.1–1.65 μm | 370–530 nm (UV–blue–green) |
| Bandgap (eV) | 1.42 | 0.75–1.35 | 2.4–3.4 |
| Refractive index n | ≈ 3.6 | ≈ 3.4 | ≈ 2.5 |
| Threshold current density J_th | ≈ 100 A/cm² (DH) | ≈ 1 kA/cm² | ≈ 1–3 kA/cm² (early); < 1 kA/cm² (MOCVD) |
| Internal quantum efficiency η_i | 0.7–0.9 | 0.6–0.8 | 0.3–0.6 |
| Typical cavity length L | 250–500 μm | 200–400 μm | 300–600 μm |
| Applications | CD, telecom pump | Long-haul fibre comm. | Blu-ray, displays, lighting |

> In the **Kerala KTU exam context**, expect short numericals on **(i) FSR calculation, (ii) threshold gain for a given R and L, (iii) Einstein A/B coefficient ratio, (iv) Q-factor estimation**.

### 4.1 Functional Block Architecture of a Compound-Semiconductor Fabry–Perot Laser

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph PUMP[Pumping Subsystem]
        PSU[DC Current Source / Driver Circuit]
        BIAS[Forward Bias Controller]
        PSU --> BIAS
    end

    subgraph JUNCTION[Active P-N Junction Region]
        NLAYER[N-type Cladding Layer]
        ACTIVE[Active Gain Region - Direct Bandgap III-V]
        PLAYER[P-type Cladding Layer]
        NLAYER --> ACTIVE
        ACTIVE --> PLAYER
    end

    subgraph CAVITY[Fabry Perot Optical Resonator]
        MIRROR1[Mirror M1 - HR Coated - R1 near 0.95]
        MEDIUM[Gain Medium of Length L and Refractive Index n]
        MIRROR2[Mirror M2 - Partially Reflecting - R2 near 0.30 to 0.95]
        MIRROR1 --> MEDIUM
        MEDIUM --> MIRROR2
    end

    subgraph OUTPUT[Coherent Output]
        BEAM[Monochromatic Collimated Laser Beam]
        BEAMSPEC[Spectral Width less than 0.1 nm]
    end

    BIAS --> NLAYER
    PLAYER --> BIAS
    ACTIVE -. injected carriers .-> MEDIUM
    MIRROR2 --> BEAM
    BEAM --> BEAMSPEC
```

### 4.2 Energy-Level Schematic for Three-Level and Four-Level Laser Schemes

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph THREEL[Three Level System]
        G1L[Ground Level E0]
        P1L[Pump Level Epump]
        U1L[Upper Laser Level E2]
        G1L -->|optical pump| P1L
        P1L -.non-radiative.-> U1L
        U1L -->|stimulated emission h nu| G1L
    end

    subgraph FOURLEVEL[Four Level System]
        G2L[Ground Level E0]
        P2L[Pump Level Epump]
        U2L[Upper Laser Level E2]
        L2L[Lower Laser Level E1 - rapidly depopulated]
        G2L -->|optical pump| P2L
        P2L -.fast.-> U2L
        U2L -->|stimulated emission h nu| L2L
        L2L -.fast decay.-> G2L
    end
```

### 4.3 Decision Flow for Achieving Lasing — Threshold Logic

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Start - Apply Pump Power] --> B{Population Inversion Achieved}
    B -- NO --> C[No Lasing - LED like Emission]
    B -- YES --> D[Round Trip Gain exceeds Round Trip Loss]
    D -- NO --> E[Subthreshold Amplified Spontaneous Emission ASE]
    D -- YES --> F[Single or Few Longitudinal Modes Survive]
    F --> G{Output Coupling Mirror Transmission Sufficient}
    G -- NO --> H[High internal Power but No Useful Output]
    G -- YES --> I[Stable Coherent Laser Output]
    I --> J[Monitor Power Wavelength Polarisation]
```

### 4.4 Schematic Cross-Section of a Double-Heterojunction (DH) Fabry-Perot Laser

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    TOP[Top Ohmic Contact - P side Metalisation] --> PCON[P+ GaAs Cap Layer]
    PCON --> PCLA[P type AlGaAs Upper Cladding]
    PCLA --> ACT[Active Region - Undoped GaAs Well - thickness about 0.1 to 0.2 micrometer]
    ACT --> NCLA[N type AlGaAs Lower Cladding]
    NCLA --> NSUB[N+ GaAs Substrate]
    NSUB --> BOT[Bottom Ohmic Contact - N side Metalisation]

    LEFT[Mirror M1 - Cleaved Facet - HR coated - left side] -.cleavage.-> ACT
    RIGHT[Mirror M2 - Cleaved Facet - Partially reflecting - right side] -.cleavage.-> ACT
    ACT --> OUT[Laser Output Beam - right side]
```

> [!NOTE]
> The **cleaved facets** of a III–V crystal naturally act as mirrors because of the abrupt refractive-index discontinuity between the semiconductor (n ≈ 3.5) and air (n = 1), giving Fresnel reflectivity **R ≈ 0.32**. One facet is usually coated with a multilayer dielectric stack to push R to ≈ 0.95–0.99.

## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 PART A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Paper]** 
*Define the term "population inversion" and explain why it is essential for lasing action. Why is a simple two-level atomic system not suitable for achieving continuous-wave (CW) lasing?*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Population inversion (1 mark):** Population inversion is a non-equilibrium condition in which the number density of atoms/molecules in an excited upper laser level **N₂** exceeds that in the lower level **N₁**, weighted by the degeneracy ratio, i.e., **N₂ > (g₂/g₁) N₁**.

**Necessity for lasing (1 mark):** Under normal (Boltzmann) thermal equilibrium, the higher-energy level is less populated, so absorption of incident photons dominates stimulated emission. Net optical gain is therefore negative. Only by inverting the populations can stimulated emission exceed absorption, producing optical amplification and hence lasing.

**Why two-level systems fail (1 mark):** In a two-level system, the pump transition (1 → 2) is exactly the same as the lasing transition (1 → 2). A pump that increases N₂ also increases the upward stimulated-absorption rate proportionally. The maximum attainable N₂ equals N₁ (saturation), which gives zero net gain. Hence practical lasers use **three-level** (e.g., Ruby) or **four-level** (e.g., He–Ne, Nd:YAG, diode) schemes.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – May 2024, Model Paper]** 
*List any three essential components of a laser system. With a neat block diagram, explain the function of each.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The three indispensable components of a laser are:

1. **Active (Gain) Medium (1 mark):** A material (solid, liquid, gas or semiconductor) with suitable energy levels that can amplify light by stimulated emission. *Example:* Nd:YAG crystal, He–Ne gas mixture, **GaAs/InGaAs p–n junction** in compound-semiconductor diode lasers.

2. **Pumping Mechanism (1 mark):** The external energy source that creates the population inversion. Optical pumping uses flash lamps or another laser; electrical pumping (used in diode lasers) uses a forward-biased current; chemical and e-beam pumping are also possible.

3. **Optical Resonator / Fabry–Perot Cavity (1 mark):** Two parallel mirrors that reflect light back and forth through the gain medium, providing positive feedback. One mirror is fully reflecting (R ≈ 1) and the other is partially transmitting (R ≈ 0.95) to allow the useful output beam to escape.

A block diagram (to be drawn by the student) showing **Pump → Active Medium ↔ Cavity Mirrors → Output Beam** carries full credit.

---

### 5.2 PART B — Long-Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

---

#### **QUESTION A — **[KTU University Exam – May 2023, Model Paper]****

**(a)** Derive Einstein's relations between the coefficients of stimulated and spontaneous emission. Discuss their physical significance. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** With the help of a suitable diagram, explain the construction and working of a Fabry–Perot cavity laser. Derive the threshold gain condition and obtain the expression for the longitudinal mode frequencies. **(7 marks)**

---

#### SOLUTION TO QUESTION A

**Solution to (a) — Derivation of Einstein's A–B Relations (7 marks):**

Let the two atomic levels be **E₁ (lower, degeneracy g₁)** and **E₂ (upper, degeneracy g₂)**, with **E₂ > E₁** and **hν = E₂ − E₁**. Einstein introduced three probability coefficients:

- **B₁₂** — probability for **stimulated absorption** (1 → 2),
- **A₂₁** — probability for **spontaneous emission** (2 → 1),
- **B₂₁** — probability for **stimulated emission** (2 → 1).

**Step 1 (1 mark):** In thermal equilibrium, the rate of upward transitions equals the rate of downward transitions:

$$
B_{12}\, \rho(\nu)\, N_{1} \;=\; A_{21}\, N_{2} \;+\; B_{21}\, \rho(\nu)\, N_{2}
$$

**Step 2 (1 mark):** Solve for the spectral energy density:

$$
\rho(\nu) \;=\; \frac{A_{21}\, N_{2}}{B_{12}\, N_{1} - B_{21}\, N_{2}}
$$

**Step 3 (1 mark):** Substitute the Boltzmann ratio **N₂/N₁ = (g₂/g₁) exp(−hν/k_BT)**:

$$
\rho(\nu) \;=\; \frac{A_{21}}{B_{21}}\,\frac{1}{\dfrac{B_{12}\,g_1}{B_{21}\,g_2}\,e^{h\nu/k_BT} - 1}
$$

**Step 4 (2 marks):** But in thermal equilibrium, ρ(ν) must be the **Planck blackbody distribution**:

$$
\rho(\nu) \;=\; \frac{8\pi h \nu^{3}}{c^{3}}\cdot\frac{1}{e^{h\nu/k_BT} - 1}
$$

Comparing the two expressions term-by-term:

$$
\frac{A_{21}}{B_{21}} \;=\; \frac{8\pi h \nu^{3}}{c^{3}}, \qquad \frac{B_{12}\,g_1}{B_{21}\,g_2} \;=\; 1
$$

**Step 5 (1 mark):** Hence the two Einstein relations:

$$
\boxed{\;g_{1} B_{12} \;=\; g_{2} B_{21}\;}
$$

$$
\boxed{\;\frac{A_{21}}{B_{21}} \;=\; \frac{8\pi h \nu^{3}}{c^{3}}\;}
$$

**Step 6 (1 mark) — Physical significance:**
- The first relation shows that the upward and downward stimulated-emission probabilities are linked by the ratio of degeneracies.
- The second relation shows that **A₂₁ ∝ ν³**: spontaneous emission rises rapidly with frequency, which is why optical lasers require harder pumping than microwave masers and why γ-ray lasers are impractical.
- These relations are universal — they hold for **every** atom/molecule and do **not** depend on temperature.

---

**Solution to (b) — Fabry–Perot Cavity Laser (7 marks):**

**Construction (2 marks):** A Fabry–Perot laser consists of:
- An **active gain medium** of length **L** and refractive index **n** (in a compound-semiconductor device, this is the p–n junction heterostructure).
- Two **highly parallel mirrors** with reflectivities **R₁** (high, ≈ 0.95–0.99) and **R₂** (lower, ≈ 0.30–0.95) facing each other across the medium.
- An external **pump** (current source for diode lasers) that drives the medium to inversion.

A schematic block diagram should be drawn: **Pump → Active Medium ↔ M₁ / M₂ → Output Beam**.

**Working (1 mark):** The pump creates population inversion. Spontaneously emitted photons travelling along the cavity axis trigger stimulated emission, producing photon multiplication. The mirrors reflect the amplified light back and forth; with each round trip the intensity grows. Once the round-trip gain equals the round-trip loss, a stable, intense, directional beam emerges through the partially transmitting mirror M₂.

**Threshold Gain Derivation (3 marks):** Let **g** be the small-signal gain coefficient (m⁻¹) and **α** the internal loss coefficient (m⁻¹). After one round trip, the wave amplitude scales by:

$$
\exp\!\bigl[(g - \alpha)\, 2L\bigr] \cdot \sqrt{R_{1} R_{2}}
$$

For **steady-state oscillation** the net round-trip factor = 1:

$$
R_{1} R_{2} \, e^{2(g_{\text{th}} - \alpha)L} \;=\; 1
$$

Solving:

$$
\boxed{\;g_{\text{th}} \;=\; \alpha \;+\; \frac{1}{2L}\,\ln\!\left(\frac{1}{R_{1} R_{2}}\right)\;}
$$

For a symmetric cavity (R₁ = R₂ = R): **g_th = α + (1/L) ln(1/R)**.

**Longitudinal Mode Frequencies (1 mark):** Standing-wave condition **qλ_q = 2nL** gives:

$$
\boxed{\;\nu_{q} \;=\; \frac{qc}{2nL}\;}
$$

with mode spacing **Δν = c / (2nL)**.

---

#### **QUESTION B (Alternative Choice) — [KTU University Exam – Dec 2022, Model Paper]**

**(a)** With a neat diagram, explain the different pumping schemes used in lasers. Discuss the conditions under which population inversion can be achieved in a four-level laser system. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** What is a Fabry–Perot etalon? Define finesse and Q-factor. For a GaAs laser with **L = 400 μm**, **n = 3.6**, **λ = 870 nm**, **R₁ = R₂ = 0.95**, calculate (i) the longitudinal mode spacing in GHz, (ii) the threshold gain coefficient, and (iii) the cavity finesse. **(7 marks)**

---

#### SOLUTION TO QUESTION B

**Solution to (a) — Pumping Schemes & Four-Level Inversion (7 marks):**

**Pumping schemes (3 marks):** [Refer to the table in Section 2.4]

- **Optical Pumping:** Flash-lamp or another laser excites the active medium (e.g., Ruby, Nd:YAG).
- **Electrical Pumping:** Forward-biased p–n junction injects electrons into the active region (e.g., **GaAs, InGaAs, GaN diode lasers**).
- **Electron-Beam Pumping:** High-energy e-beam creates electron–hole pairs in direct-gap semiconductors.
- **Chemical Pumping:** Exothermic reaction produces excited species (e.g., HF/DF chemical lasers).

A diagram mapping the four schemes onto their energy sources is recommended.

**Four-level laser and population inversion (4 marks):**

A four-level system has levels **E₀ (ground), E_pump (pump), E₂ (upper laser), E₁ (lower laser)**, with E₁ > E₀.

- Atoms are pumped from E₀ → E_pump → (fast non-radiative decay) → **E₂**.
- The lasing transition is **E₂ → E₁**, emitting a photon of energy **hν = E₂ − E₁**.
- The lower laser level E₁ is **rapidly depopulated** to the ground state E₀ by fast non-radiative relaxation (typical lifetime ≈ 10⁻¹² s, much shorter than the upper level lifetime of 10⁻³ to 10⁻⁶ s).

Because E₁ is essentially empty at equilibrium, **population inversion N₂ > N₁ is achieved even with very modest pump power**. This is why four-level lasers (He–Ne, Nd:YAG, CO₂, diode) have **much lower thresholds** than three-level lasers (Ruby).

---

**Solution to (b) — Numerical on Fabry–Perot Cavity (7 marks):**

**Definitions (2 marks):**

- **Fabry–Perot etalon:** A pair of parallel, partially reflecting mirrors separated by a distance L, used as an optical resonator or spectral filter.
- **Finesse ℱ:** Ratio of the free spectral range (FSR) to the full-width at half-maximum (FWHM) of a transmission peak: **ℱ = π√(R₁R₂) / (1 − R₁R₂)**.
- **Q-factor:** Ratio of resonant frequency to linewidth: **Q = ν₀/Δν_FWHM = 2πnL / [λ(1 − √(R₁R₂))]**.

**Given:** L = 400 μm = 4 × 10⁻⁴ m, n = 3.6, λ = 870 nm = 8.7 × 10⁻⁷ m, R₁ = R₂ = 0.95.

**(i) Longitudinal mode spacing Δν (1.5 marks):**

$$
\Delta \nu \;=\; \frac{c}{2nL} \;=\; \frac{3 \times 10^{8}}{2 \times 3.6 \times 4 \times 10^{-4}}
$$

$$
\Delta \nu \;=\; \frac{3 \times 10^{8}}{2.88 \times 10^{-3}} \;\approx\; 1.042 \times 10^{11}\,\text{Hz} \;\approx\; 104.2\,\text{GHz}
$$

**(ii) Threshold gain coefficient g_th (1.5 marks):**

Assume internal loss α is negligible (a common assumption when not specified, α = 0):

$$
g_{\text{th}} \;=\; \frac{1}{2L}\,\ln\!\left(\frac{1}{R_{1}R_{2}}\right) \;=\; \frac{1}{2 \times 4 \times 10^{-4}}\,\ln\!\left(\frac{1}{0.95 \times 0.95}\right)
$$

$$
g_{\text{th}} \;=\; 1250 \times \ln(1.108) \;\approx\; 1250 \times 0.1026 \;\approx\; 128.3\,\text{m}^{-1} \;\approx\; 1.28\,\text{cm}^{-1}
$$

If α = 10 cm⁻¹ is given, **g_th ≈ 11.28 cm⁻¹**. (Examiner will specify the assumption.)

**(iii) Finesse (2 marks):**

$$
\mathcal{F} \;=\; \frac{\pi\sqrt{R_1 R_2}}{1 - R_1 R_2} \;=\; \frac{\pi \times 0.95}{1 - 0.9025} \;=\; \frac{2.984}{0.0975} \;\approx\; 30.6
$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. **Unit mistakes** — Many students write Δν in Hz and forget to convert to GHz; KTU strictly expects the numerical answer in **GHz** when asked. **Lose 0.5 mark.**
> 2. **Converting μm ↔ m** — Always convert L and λ to **metres** before substitution; mixing units is the single most common numerical error. **Lose 0.5 mark.**
> 3. **Neglecting internal loss α** — If the problem statement does not give α, explicitly state **"assuming α = 0 (negligible)"**. Otherwise you risk losing the 1-mark "assumptions" credit.
> 4. **Forgetting the factor of 2 in g_th formula** — The round-trip length is **2L**, not L. Many students write (1/L) ln(1/R) for an asymmetric cavity, which is wrong. **Lose 1 mark.**
> 5. **Missing diagram in 7-mark sub-parts** — A neat labelled block diagram of the Fabry–Perot cavity (two mirrors, active medium, output beam) is **mandatory** and carries up to 1 mark. Always draw it.

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!TIP]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist (Last-Night Scan)**

- **LASER = Light Amplification by Stimulated Emission of Radiation** (Einstein 1917; first device by Maiman 1960 — Ruby laser).
- **Three essential ingredients:** Active (gain) medium + Pumping source + Optical resonator (Fabry–Perot cavity).
- **Three light–matter processes:** Stimulated absorption (∝ B₁₂), Spontaneous emission (∝ A₂₁), Stimulated emission (∝ B₂₁).
- **Two Einstein relations:** **g₁B₁₂ = g₂B₂₁** and **A₂₁/B₂₁ = 8πhν³/c³**. The second implies **A₂₁ ∝ ν³** (γ-ray lasers hard, masers easy).
- **Population inversion** is a non-equilibrium state requiring **N₂ > (g₂/g₁) N₁** — must be maintained by continuous pumping.
- **Two-level** systems cannot be inverted for CW; **three-level** (Ruby) and **four-level** (He–Ne, Nd:YAG, diode) systems are used. Four-level gives lower threshold.
- **Threshold gain:** **g_th = α + (1/2L) ln(1/R₁R₂)** — reduces with higher R and lower L.
- **Round-trip oscillation criterion:** **R₁R₂ e^{2(g−α)L} = 1**.
- **Longitudinal mode frequencies:** **ν_q = qc/(2nL)**, with FSR **Δν = c/(2nL)**.
- **Wavelength-domain FSR:** **Δλ = λ²/(2nL)** (e.g., ≈ 0.3 nm for typical GaAs FP laser).
- **Cavity finesse:** **ℱ = π√(R₁R₂)/(1 − R₁R₂)** — large for high R.
- **Q-factor:** **Q = 2πnL/[λ(1 − √(R₁R₂))]** — narrow linewidth ⇔ high Q.
- **Compound-semiconductor laser** is electrically pumped; threshold current density J_th is the key figure of merit. Modern quantum-well (QW) and quantum-dot (QD) heterostructures achieve **J_th < 50 A/cm²** at room temperature.
- **Fabry–Perot mirrors in III–V lasers:** natural cleaved facets give R ≈ 0.32 (Fresnel); one facet is HR-coated (R ≈ 0.95–0.99) and the other is AR-coated or partially reflecting for output coupling.
- **Schawlow–Townes linewidth:** **Δν_ST = (hν₀ Δν_cavity)/(2 P_out)** — narrower for higher output power and higher-Q cavities.
- **Material systems to remember:**
  - **AlGaAs / GaAs** → 750–880 nm (CD, telecom pumps)
  - **InGaAsP / InP** → 1.1–1.65 μm (long-haul fibre)
  - **InGaN / GaN** → 370–530 nm (Blu-ray, displays, solid-state lighting)
- **Threshold population-inversion density** for a homogeneously broadened two-level system: **ΔN_th = (8π n² ν² τ_spontaneous h)/(c² · Q · η)**, often simplified in derivations to **ΔN_th ≥ α / (σ · L)** where σ is the stimulated-emission cross-section.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Crystal Growth
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEEVT413 - COMPOUND SEMICONDUCTORS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Crystal Growth

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Crystal Growth (KTU 2024 Definition):** Crystal growth is the process by which a crystalline solid is progressively formed from a disordered phase (vapor, liquid, or solution) through the controlled, thermodynamically-driven addition of atoms, ions, or molecules onto a seed crystal or substrate, resulting in a long-range ordered atomic arrangement.

In the context of **Compound Semiconductors** (e.g., GaAs, InP, CdTe, ZnSe, GaN, AlGaAs), crystal growth is the foundational step that determines the electronic, optical, and structural quality of the final device. Unlike elemental semiconductors (Si, Ge), compound semiconductors require precise stoichiometry control, prevention of phase separation, and minimization of native defects (vacancies, antisites, dislocations).

> [!NOTE]
> **Syllabus Highlight (KTU 2024 Module 4):** This module covers the fundamental thermodynamic driving force for crystallization, the kinetic theories of nucleation and growth, and the principal bulk and epitaxial growth techniques used in III-V and II-VI compound semiconductor technology.

## 1.2 The Two Fundamental Stages of Crystal Formation

Every crystal growth process — whether growing a Si ingot or an MBE-deposited AlGaAs film — proceeds through two universally accepted stages:

1. **Nucleation (Birth of a new phase):** The formation of a stable, sub-microscopic cluster of atoms (the *embryo* or *nucleus*) from a supersaturated parent phase.
2. **Crystal Growth (Propagation of the interface):** The subsequent incorporation of atoms onto the existing nucleus, causing the crystal to enlarge in size.

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!TIP]
> **Real-World Analogy — "Building a Brick Wall in the Fog":**
> Imagine you are trying to build a perfect, perfectly-aligned brick wall during a foggy morning in a quarry.
> - The **fog (supersaturated vapor/melt)** contains millions of loose bricks (atoms) floating randomly.
> - **Nucleation** is the moment when a few bricks accidentally stack together so well that they form a stable little corner that won't collapse. Once this little stable "corner" exists, more bricks will naturally stick to it.
> - **Crystal Growth** is the systematic laying of brick after brick onto this corner, extending the wall.
> - A **seed crystal** is like the first perfectly-laid brick you place deliberately to guarantee the wall grows in the *right* orientation from the start.
> - **Defects (vacancies, dislocations)** are like the crooked bricks that slipped in because the wall was built too fast or in the wrong conditions.

Without nucleation, no growth can begin. Without stable propagation, the crystal becomes defective. KTU questions frequently test both stages.

## 1.4 Why Crystal Growth is Critical in Compound Semiconductors

> [!WARNING]
> **Why this topic is a KTU High-Weightage Area:**
> - Every optoelectronic device (LED, Laser Diode, Photodetector) and every high-speed transistor (HEMT, HBT) **originates from a precisely grown crystal or epitaxial layer**.
> - Device performance metrics — mobility (µ), carrier lifetime (τ), quantum efficiency (η), and breakdown voltage — are **direct functions of crystal quality**.
> - Unlike Si, compound semiconductors do NOT have a stable native oxide, so **the crystal itself is the active medium**. There is no "get out of jail" oxidation step to mask poor crystal quality.

## 1.5 The Driving Force: Why Crystals Grow At All

A crystal grows only when the system can **lower its Gibbs Free Energy (G)** by transitioning from a disordered (vapor/liquid) phase to an ordered (solid) phase. The driving force is the **supersaturation** or **supercooling** of the parent phase.

For a solid forming from its own vapor (a common scenario in MBE):

$$\Delta G = G_{\text{solid}} - G_{\text{vapor}} = -k_B T \ln\left(\frac{P}{P_{eq}}\right)$$

Where:
- $P$ = actual vapor pressure
- $P_{eq}$ = equilibrium vapor pressure at temperature $T$
- $k_B$ = Boltzmann's constant ($1.38 \times 10^{-23}$ J/K)
- The ratio $P/P_{eq} > 1$ defines **supersaturation**, the necessary condition for growth.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Homogeneous Nucleation — The Thermodynamic Foundation

When a spherical solid embryo of radius $r$ forms from a supersaturated vapor (or supercooled melt), the net change in Gibbs free energy is the sum of two competing terms:

1. **Volume Free Energy Reduction (favorable):** Every atom that joins the solid bulk releases the latent heat of fusion/solidification, lowering the system's energy. Scales as $r^3$.
2. **Surface Free Energy Penalty (unfavorable):** Creating a new solid–vapor interface requires energy (surface tension $\gamma$). Scales as $r^2$.

> [!NOTE]
> **Classical Nucleation Theory (CNT) Equation:**
> For a spherical nucleus of radius $r$ forming homogeneously:
> $$\Delta G_{\text{hom}} = -\frac{4}{3}\pi r^3 \Delta G_v + 4\pi r^2 \gamma$$
> Where:
> - $\Delta G_v$ = Gibbs free energy change per unit volume (negative during growth)
> - $\gamma$ = surface free energy per unit area (J/m²)
> - The first term is **negative** (favorable), the second is **positive** (unfavorable).

### 2.1.1 The Critical Radius ($r^*$)

Differentiating $\Delta G_{\text{hom}}$ with respect to $r$ and setting the result to zero yields the **critical radius** — the minimum size a nucleus must reach to be thermodynamically stable.

$$\frac{d(\Delta G_{\text{hom}})}{dr} = -4\pi r^2 \Delta G_v + 8\pi r \gamma = 0$$

Solving:

$$r^* = \frac{2\gamma}{\Delta G_v}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Physical Meaning of $r^*$:** A sub-critical cluster ($r < r^*$) will spontaneously dissolve because adding atoms increases its free energy. A super-critical cluster ($r > r^*$) will grow spontaneously. This is the fundamental "hurdle" every new crystal must overcome.

### 2.1.2 The Critical Free Energy Barrier ($\Delta G^*$)

Substituting $r^*$ back into the $\Delta G_{\text{hom}}$ equation:

$$\Delta G^* = \frac{16\pi \gamma^3}{3(\Delta G_v)^2}$$

This is the **activation energy** for nucleation. A larger $\Delta G^*$ means nucleation is harder; a smaller $\Delta G^*$ means nucleation is easier (more nuclei will form).

## 2.2 Heterogeneous Nucleation

In real crystal growth, nucleation almost never occurs homogeneously in the bulk of the parent phase. Instead, it occurs on a foreign substrate or crucible wall. This is **heterogeneous nucleation**.

> [!TIP]
> **Heterogeneous Nucleation Analogy:** A seed crystal (or substrate) acts like a "scaffold" that reduces the energy barrier. The atoms in the substrate have partial bonds that can be satisfied by the depositing atoms, lowering the surface energy penalty. This is why KTU emphasizes **epitaxial growth on lattice-matched substrates** (e.g., GaAs on GaAs, AlGaAs on GaAs, InGaAs on InP).

The free energy barrier for heterogeneous nucleation is reduced by a **geometric factor** $f(\theta)$:

$$\Delta G_{\text{het}}^* = \Delta G_{\text{hom}}^* \cdot f(\theta)$$

$$f(\theta) = \frac{(2 + \cos\theta)(1 - \cos\theta)^2}{4}$$

Where $\theta$ is the **contact angle** between the nucleus and the substrate.
- When $\theta \to 0$ (perfect wetting, e.g., homoepitaxy), $f(\theta) \to 0$ and $\Delta G_{\text{het}}^* \to 0$. **Growth proceeds almost barrier-free.**
- When $\theta \to 180°$ (no wetting), $f(\theta) \to 1$ and we recover homogeneous nucleation.

## 2.3 Crystal Growth Mechanisms (After Nucleation)

Once a stable interface exists, atoms are incorporated by one or more of the following kinetic mechanisms:

1. **Interface-Controlled (Continuous) Growth:** Atom-by-atom attachment at rough interfaces. Typical of high supercooling. Velocity $v \propto \Delta T$.
2. **Lateral (Layer) Growth:** Atoms attach at the edges of two-dimensional islands (steps) on a smooth (singular) interface. Common in LPE and VPE of III-V compounds.
3. **Spiral Growth (Burton-Cabrera-Frank, BCF Model):** Growth proceeds around screw dislocations, creating a self-perpetuating step source. Important in MBE.

## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Master Reference Table)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Print/Memorize this table for Module 4 Numerical Questions.**

| # | Formula | Meaning / Use | Typical Units |
|---|---------|---------------|---------------|
| 1 | $\Delta G_v = \frac{\Delta H_f \cdot \Delta T}{T_m}$ | Volumetric driving force (supercooling $\Delta T$) | J/m³ |
| 2 | $r^* = \frac{2\gamma}{\Delta G_v}$ | Critical radius of stable nucleus | m |
| 3 | $\Delta G^* = \frac{16\pi \gamma^3}{3(\Delta G_v)^2}$ | Activation barrier for homogeneous nucleation | J |
| 4 | $f(\theta) = \frac{(2+\cos\theta)(1-\cos\theta)^2}{4}$ | Geometric reduction factor (heterogeneous) | dimensionless |
| 5 | $v = K \cdot (P - P_{eq})$ | Vapor-phase growth rate (linear kinetics) | m/s |
| 6 | $v = \mu \cdot \Delta T$ | Melt growth rate (continuous, Wilson-Frenkel) | m/s |
| 7 | $N \propto \exp\left(-\frac{\Delta G^*}{k_B T}\right)$ | Steady-state nucleation rate | nuclei/m³·s |
| 8 | $J = A \cdot \exp\left(-\frac{Q_a}{k_B T}\right)$ | Surface incorporation flux (Arrhenius) | atoms/m²·s |
| 9 | $\alpha_f = \frac{\Delta T}{T_m}$ | Undercooling fraction (used in constitutional supercooling) | dimensionless |
| 10 | $G/R < \frac{m C_0 (1-k)}{k D}$ | Constitutional supercooling stability criterion (Tiller criterion) | dimensionless ratio |

Where $G$ = thermal gradient, $R$ = growth rate, $m$ = liquidus slope, $C_0$ = initial dopant concentration, $k$ = segregation coefficient, $D$ = diffusivity.

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Growth Method | Industry Use Case | Typical Compound |
|---------------|-------------------|------------------|
| Czochralski (CZ) | Bulk Si, semi-insulating GaAs (LEC variant) | GaAs, InP |
| Vertical Gradient Freeze (VGF) | Large-diameter GaAs, InP wafers for integrated circuits | GaAs, InP |
| Bridgman-Stockbarger | II-VI detector crystals (CdZnTe), optoelectronics | CdTe, ZnSe |
| Liquid Phase Epitaxy (LPE) | High-efficiency GaAs solar cells, GaAlAs LEDs (historical) | GaAs, AlGaAs |
| Vapor Phase Epitaxy (VPE) | Thick GaAs, GaP LED layers | GaAs, GaP |
| MOCVD | LED mass production (GaN, AlGaInP), HEMT structures | GaN, InGaN, AlGaInP |
| Molecular Beam Epitaxy (MBE) | Quantum wells, HEMTs, spintronic heterostructures | GaAs/AlGaAs, InGaAs |

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Critical Radius from First Principles

**Starting Premise:** Consider a spherical solid embryo of radius $r$ forming from its own supersaturated vapor at constant temperature $T$ and pressure $P$.

**Step 1 — Write the two contributions to the total Gibbs free energy change.**

The volume term (bulk solid is more stable than bulk vapor) is:

$$\Delta G_{\text{volume}} = \frac{4}{3}\pi r^3 \cdot \Delta G_v$$

The surface term (creating a new solid-vapor interface costs energy) is:

$$\Delta G_{\text{surface}} = 4\pi r^2 \cdot \gamma$$

**Step 2 — Sum the two contributions to obtain the net free energy change.**

$$\Delta G_{\text{hom}} = \frac{4}{3}\pi r^3 \Delta G_v + 4\pi r^2 \gamma$$

Note: $\Delta G_v$ is **negative** during growth (since solid is the lower-energy phase), but we keep the sign explicit.

**Step 3 — Differentiate with respect to $r$ to find the extremum (the maximum of $\Delta G_{\text{hom}}$, which corresponds to the critical nucleus).**

$$\frac{d(\Delta G_{\text{hom}})}{dr} = 4\pi r^2 \Delta G_v + 8\pi r \gamma$$

**Step 4 — Set the derivative to zero and solve for $r$.**

$$4\pi r^2 \Delta G_v + 8\pi r \gamma = 0$$

$$4\pi r (r \Delta G_v + 2\gamma) = 0$$

Discarding the trivial solution $r = 0$:

$$r \Delta G_v + 2\gamma = 0$$

$$r^* = -\frac{2\gamma}{\Delta G_v}$$

Since $\Delta G_v < 0$ during condensation, $r^* > 0$ — a physical, positive critical radius. Dropping the sign by writing $|\Delta G_v|$:

$$\boxed{r^* = \frac{2\gamma}{\vert \Delta G_v \vert}}$$

**Step 5 — Verify this is a maximum by computing the second derivative.**

$$\frac{d^2(\Delta G_{\text{hom}})}{dr^2} = 8\pi r \Delta G_v + 8\pi \gamma$$

At $r = r^* = 2\gamma/|\Delta G_v|$:

$$\frac{d^2(\Delta G_{\text{hom}})}{dr^2}\bigg|_{r=r^*} = 8\pi \left(\frac{2\gamma}{|\Delta G_v|}\right) \Delta G_v + 8\pi \gamma = -16\pi\gamma + 8\pi\gamma = -8\pi\gamma < 0$$

The second derivative is negative, confirming that the extremum is indeed a **maximum**. This is the activation barrier the system must overcome.

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Critical Free Energy Barrier $\Delta G^*$

**Step 1 — Substitute $r^*$ into the expression for $\Delta G_{\text{hom}}$.**

$$\Delta G^* = \frac{4}{3}\pi (r^*)^3 \Delta G_v + 4\pi (r^*)^2 \gamma$$

**Step 2 — Replace $r^*$ with $2\gamma/|\Delta G_v|$.**

$$\Delta G^* = \frac{4}{3}\pi \left(\frac{2\gamma}{|\Delta G_v|}\right)^3 \Delta G_v + 4\pi \left(\frac{2\gamma}{|\Delta G_v|}\right)^2 \gamma$$

**Step 3 — Simplify the volume term.**

$$\frac{4}{3}\pi \cdot \frac{8\gamma^3}{|\Delta G_v|^3} \cdot \Delta G_v = \frac{32\pi \gamma^3}{3|\Delta G_v|^3} \cdot (-|\Delta G_v|) = -\frac{32\pi \gamma^3}{3|\Delta G_v|^2}$$

**Step 4 — Simplify the surface term.**

$$4\pi \cdot \frac{4\gamma^2}{|\Delta G_v|^2} \cdot \gamma = \frac{16\pi \gamma^3}{|\Delta G_v|^2}$$

**Step 5 — Add the two terms.**

$$\Delta G^* = -\frac{32\pi \gamma^3}{3|\Delta G_v|^2} + \frac{16\pi \gamma^3}{|\Delta G_v|^2} = \frac{16\pi \gamma^3}{|\Delta G_v|^2}\left(1 - \frac{2}{3}\right) = \frac{16\pi \gamma^3}{3|\Delta G_v|^2}$$

$$\boxed{\Delta G^* = \frac{16\pi \gamma^3}{3(\Delta G_v)^2}}$$

**Step 6 — Express $\Delta G_v$ in terms of measurable quantities.**

For an ideal vapor at pressure $P$ and equilibrium pressure $P_{eq}$:

$$\Delta G_v = \frac{\rho_s k_B T}{M} \ln\left(\frac{P_{eq}}{P}\right) = -\frac{\rho_s k_B T}{M} \ln\left(\frac{P}{P_{eq}}\right)$$

Where $\rho_s$ is the solid density, $M$ is the molar mass, and the ratio $\sigma = \ln(P/P_{eq})$ is the **supersaturation**.

## 3.3 Derivation 3: Constitutional Supercooling Criterion (Tiller, 1953)

This is a **high-value KTU problem** because it appears in bulk growth of doped compound semiconductors.

**Step 1 — Set up the scenario.** A binary alloy (e.g., Si-doped GaAs) is directionally solidified with growth rate $R$ and temperature gradient $G$ in the liquid ahead of the interface. The dopant segregates with equilibrium partition coefficient $k = C_s/C_l < 1$.

**Step 2 — Solute profile in the liquid ahead of the interface (steady-state, after initial transient).**

By solving Fick's second law with a moving boundary:

$$C_l(z) = C_0 \left[1 + \frac{1-k}{k} \exp\left(-\frac{R z}{D}\right)\right]$$

Where $z$ is distance ahead of the interface into the liquid.

**Step 3 — Constitutional supercooling occurs when the actual temperature gradient in the liquid is less than the liquidus gradient required by the solute profile.**

The liquidus temperature in the liquid is:

$$T_l(z) = T_m + m \cdot C_l(z) = T_m + m C_0 \left[1 + \frac{1-k}{k} \exp\left(-\frac{R z}{D}\right)\right]$$

**Step 4 — Compute the gradient of the liquidus temperature at the interface ($z \to 0$).**

$$\left.\frac{dT_l}{dz}\right|_{z=0} = m C_0 \cdot \frac{1-k}{k} \cdot \left(-\frac{R}{D}\right) = -\frac{m C_0 R (1-k)}{kD}$$

**Step 5 — Apply the constitutional supercooling condition.** Stability requires the actual gradient $G$ to be greater than or equal to the magnitude of the liquidus gradient:

$$G \geq \left\vert \frac{dT_l}{dz} \right\vert_{z=0} = \frac{m C_0 R (1-k)}{kD}$$

Rearranging:

$$\boxed{\frac{G}{R} \geq \frac{m C_0 (1-k)}{kD}}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Interpretation:** If the criterion is violated, the planar solid-liquid interface becomes **morphologically unstable** and breaks down into cells or dendrites, ruining the crystal quality. This is why compound semiconductor growers use **low $R$** (slow pulling) and **high $G$** (strong temperature gradient).

## 3.4 Worked Numerical Example (Model Answer Style)

**Problem:** For GaAs, $\gamma = 1.2 \text{ J/m}^2$ and $\Delta G_v = -2.5 \times 10^8 \text{ J/m}^3$ at the growth temperature. Calculate the critical radius and the activation barrier for homogeneous nucleation. (Take $\pi = 3.1416$.)

**Solution:**

**Step 1 — Critical radius:**

$$r^* = \frac{2\gamma}{\vert \Delta G_v \vert} = \frac{2 \times 1.2}{2.5 \times 10^8} = \frac{2.4}{2.5 \times 10^8} = 9.6 \times 10^{-9} \text{ m} = 9.6 \text{ nm}$$

**Step 2 — Activation barrier:**

$$\Delta G^* = \frac{16\pi \gamma^3}{3(\Delta G_v)^2} = \frac{16 \times 3.1416 \times (1.2)^3}{3 \times (2.5 \times 10^8)^2}$$

Numerator: $16 \times 3.1416 \times 1.728 = 86.86$
Denominator: $3 \times 6.25 \times 10^{16} = 1.875 \times 10^{17}$

$$\Delta G^* = \frac{86.86}{1.875 \times 10^{17}} = 4.63 \times 10^{-16} \text{ J}$$

Converting to eV (divide by $1.602 \times 10^{-19}$):

$$\Delta G^* \approx 2.89 \times 10^3 \text{ eV} = 2.89 \text{ keV}$$

This large barrier confirms why **homogeneous nucleation in bulk melts is essentially impossible** — heterogeneous nucleation on the crucible wall or seed is what actually occurs in practice. **(7 marks standard)**

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Master Architecture: Crystal Growth Process Hierarchy

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["CRYSTAL GROWTH PROCESS"] --> B["BULK GROWTH"]
    A --> C["EPITAXIAL GROWTH"]
    A --> D["NUCLEATION CONTROL"]

    B --> B1["Czochralski CZ"]
    B --> B2["Bridgman Stockbarger"]
    B --> B3["Vertical Gradient Freeze VGF"]
    B --> B4["Liquid Encapsulated Czochralski LEC"]
    B --> B5["Float Zone FZ"]

    C --> C1["Liquid Phase Epitaxy LPE"]
    C --> C2["Vapor Phase Epitaxy VPE"]
    C --> C3["Metalorganic CVD MOCVD"]
    C --> C4["Molecular Beam Epitaxy MBE"]
    C --> C5["Chemical Beam Epitaxy CBE"]

    D --> D1["Homogeneous Nucleation"]
    D --> D2["Heterogeneous Nucleation on Substrate"]
    D --> D3["Critical Radius r star"]
    D --> D4["Gibbs Free Energy Barrier Delta G"]
```

## 4.2 Schematic: Nucleation Energy Landscape

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A["Cluster Size r equals 0"] --> B["Sub critical cluster dissolves"]
    C["Critical Radius r star"] --> D["ACTIVATION BARRIER DELTA G STAR"]
    D --> E["Super critical cluster grows spontaneously"]
    E --> F["Macroscopic Crystal"]

    G["Supersaturated Vapor"] --> A
    F --> H["Solid Crystal Lattice"]
```

## 4.3 Functional Flow: Liquid Phase Epitaxy (LPE) of GaAs / AlGaAs

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["Ga As melt in graphite slider boat"] --> B["Temperature ramped to 750 to 800 C"]
    B --> C["Substrate wafer inserted under melt"]
    C --> D["Solution becomes supersaturated by slow cooling"]
    D --> E["Epitaxial layer nucleates on substrate"]
    E --> F["Lateral layer by layer growth at interface"]
    F --> G["Slide boat to next melt for AlGaAs layer"]
    G --> H["Cool down and remove wafer"]
    H --> I["Inspection: thickness, composition, defects"]
```

## 4.4 Functional Flow: MOCVD Reactor for GaN LED Production

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["TMGa and NH3 precursors"] --> B["H2 and N2 carrier gas mixing manifold"]
    B --> C["Injection into heated reactor 1000 to 1100 C"]
    C --> D["Gas phase reactions form GaN molecular species"]
    D --> E["Diffusion to heated sapphire or SiC substrate"]
    E --> F["Surface adsorption and migration"]
    F --> G["Incorporation into the GaN epitaxial layer"]
    G --> H["By products and unreacted gases pumped to exhaust"]
    H --> I["Scrubber and abatement system"]

    J["In situ monitoring: reflectance pyrometry, RHEED"] -.-> F
```

## 4.5 Functional Flow: Molecular Beam Epitaxy (MBE) Chamber

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["Ultra High Vacuum Chamber 10 to the power minus 10 Torr"] --> B["Effusion Cells for Ga As Al In"]
    B --> C["Heated to produce atomic or molecular beams"]
    C --> D["Shutter control for atomic layer precision"]
    D --> E["Substrate holder with rotating heater"]
    E --> F["RHEED gun for in situ surface monitoring"]
    F --> G["RHEED screen with oscillation pattern"]
    G --> H["Layer by layer deposition on heated substrate"]
    H --> I["Quartz Crystal Monitor for flux calibration"]
    I --> J["Exhaust via ion pumps and cryo panels"]
```

## 4.6 Process Selection Matrix (Concept Map)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A["Material System"] --> B["III V like GaAs InP"]
    A --> C["Wide Bandgap like GaN SiC"]
    A --> D["II VI like CdTe ZnSe"]

    B --> E["LEC for bulk GaAs"]
    B --> F["MBE or MOCVD for heterostructures"]
    C --> G["MOCVD for GaN LEDs"]
    C --> H["HVPE for thick GaN substrates"]
    D --> I["Bridgman for CdZnTe detectors"]
    D --> J["MBE for HgCdTe IR sensors"]
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1
**[KTU University Exam – Model Paper Style 2024 Scheme]**
**Q: Define "Critical Radius" in crystal growth. Why is it important in compound semiconductor crystal growth?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
The **critical radius ($r^*$)** is the minimum size a solid nucleus must reach to be thermodynamically stable against dissolution. It is defined as:

$$r^* = \frac{2\gamma}{\vert \Delta G_v \vert}$$

where $\gamma$ is the surface free energy and $|\Delta G_v|$ is the volumetric driving force.

**Importance in compound semiconductors:** Since compound semiconductors like GaAs, InP, and GaN have high latent heats of formation and complex stoichiometry, controlling the critical radius during nucleation determines whether the grown layer will be a single-crystal epitaxial film (large $r^*$, low nucleation density) or a polycrystalline/amorphous deposit (small $r^*$, high nucleation density). This is critical for device-quality heteroepitaxy (e.g., GaN on sapphire). **(3 marks)**

---

### Question 2
**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Adapted Style]**
**Q: Differentiate between homogeneous and heterogeneous nucleation. Which one is more relevant in real compound semiconductor growth?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Feature | Homogeneous | Heterogeneous |
|---|---|---|
| Location | In the bulk of the parent phase | On a foreign substrate/surface |
| Energy barrier | Very high | Reduced by factor $f(\theta)$ |
| Practical occurrence | Rare | Common |
| Contact angle $\theta$ | Not applicable | $0 < \theta < 180°$ |

**Relevance:** In real growth, **heterogeneous nucleation** is dominant. Epitaxial techniques like MOCVD, MBE, and LPE all rely on heterogeneous nucleation on a substrate. The substrate lowers the energy barrier, and lattice matching between the substrate and the growing film (e.g., AlGaAs on GaAs) ensures single-crystal quality. **(3 marks)**

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each) — Internal Choice Format

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Pattern (14 marks):** Each Part B question has two sub-parts: Part (a) for 7 marks and Part (b) for 7 marks. Students answer **either** full Question A **or** full Question B.

---

### Question A (14 Marks) — **CHOICE 1**

**[KTU University Exam – Model Paper, PEEVT413, Module 4]**

**(a)** With a neat functional diagram, explain the **Czochralski (CZ) method** of bulk crystal growth. Discuss why the **Liquid Encapsulated Czochralski (LEC)** variant is preferred for GaAs. **(7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

The **Czochralski method** is the most widely used bulk crystal growth technique. The procedure is:

1. A polycrystalline charge of the semiconductor (e.g., GaAs) is melted in a **crucible** (usually silica or pyrolytic boron nitride, pBN) inside a furnace.
2. A **seed crystal** of known orientation (e.g., $\langle 100 \rangle$) is lowered from above until it just touches the melt surface.
3. The seed is slowly pulled upward (typical rate: 5–20 mm/hr) and rotated (5–30 rpm) simultaneously.
4. As the seed is withdrawn, the melt that adheres to it **solidifies** by losing heat to the surroundings, continuing the crystal lattice of the seed.
5. The result is a **cylindrical single-crystal ingot** (boule), typically 2–8 inches in diameter.

**Why LEC is preferred for GaAs:**
GaAs has a high equilibrium vapor pressure of **arsenic** at its melting point (1238 °C). If grown by conventional CZ, As would evaporate from the melt, leaving Ga-rich non-stoichiometric material. **LEC (Liquid Encapsulated Czochralski)** solves this by placing a layer of inert, transparent molten **boric oxide (B₂O₃)** on top of the GaAs melt. The B₂O₃ layer:
- Mechanically seals the melt from the atmosphere.
- Suppresses arsenic evaporation (acts as a diffusion barrier).
- Allows the use of high ambient pressure (inert N₂ at ~20–60 atm) to further suppress As loss.

LEC also enables doping with carbon (for semi-insulating GaAs substrates) and growth of large-diameter wafers. **(7 marks)**

---

**(b)** Derive the expression for the **critical radius $r^*$** and the **activation free energy barrier $\Delta G^*$** for homogeneous nucleation. State the physical significance of each. **(7 marks)**

**Model Answer:** (See SECTION 3.1 and 3.2 for the full derivation.)

**Step 1 — Total free energy change for a spherical nucleus of radius $r$:**

$$\Delta G_{\text{hom}} = \frac{4}{3}\pi r^3 \Delta G_v + 4\pi r^2 \gamma$$

**Step 2 — Differentiate and set to zero:**

$$\frac{d(\Delta G_{\text{hom}})}{dr} = 4\pi r^2 \Delta G_v + 8\pi r \gamma = 0$$

**Step 3 — Critical radius:**

$$r^* = -\frac{2\gamma}{\Delta G_v} = \frac{2\gamma}{\vert \Delta G_v \vert}$$

**Step 4 — Substitute back to obtain activation barrier:**

$$\Delta G^* = \frac{16\pi \gamma^3}{3(\Delta G_v)^2}$$

**Physical Significance:**
- **$r^*$:** Sub-critical clusters ($r < r^*$) will spontaneously redissolve because adding atoms increases free energy. Super-critical clusters ($r > r^*$) grow spontaneously into macroscopic crystals. $r^*$ is therefore the **"nucleation threshold."**
- **$\Delta G^*$:** This is the **activation energy** the parent phase must overcome to form a stable nucleus. A higher $\Delta G^*$ means fewer nuclei form (good for single-crystal growth); a lower $\Delta G^*$ means many nuclei form (leads to polycrystalline material). **(7 marks)**

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — **CHOICE 2**
