- Higher etch rates.
- Independent (to some extent) control of chemical vs. physical contributions.
- Better for hard-to-etch compound semiconductors (GaN, SiC, InP). ✅

#### (b) With suitable examples, discuss the role of etch chemistry for GaAs and InP dry etching. Compare the chemistries used. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**GaAs Dry Etching:**
- Most common chemistry: **Cl₂ / BCl₃ / Ar** in ICP-RIE.
- Cl₂ provides the reactive Cl• radicals.
- BCl₃ helps remove native oxide and provides additional Cl (BCl₃ + O → BOCl + Cl•).
- Ar provides physical sputtering component and sidewall passivation via Ar⁺ bombardment.
- Etch product: GaCl₃ (volatile, desorbs at >150 °C).
- Typical etch rate: 500 nm/min; selectivity over SiN$_x$ mask: ~20.

**InP Dry Etching:**
- Two main chemistries:
  1. **CH₄ / H₂ / Ar** — methane produces CH$_3$• radicals that react with In to form volatile organometallics. The hydrocarbon polymer layer (CH$_x$) simultaneously *passivates* the sidewall, enabling high anisotropy.
  2. **Cl₂-based chemistries** — but InCl$_3$ has low volatility, requiring **substrate heating >150 °C** to achieve acceptable etch rates.
- Etch rate with CH₄/H₂: 100–200 nm/min; selectivity over SiO$_2$: ~50.

**Comparison:**

| Aspect | GaAs (Cl₂) | InP (CH₄/H₂) |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Etch product volatility | High (GaCl₃) | Moderate (In(CH₃)$_x$) |
| Sidewall polymer | None needed | Required (CH$_x$ polymer) |
| Substrate heating | Optional | Heated chuck preferred |
| Damage to surface | Low (Cl is soft) | Moderate (ion-induced) |
| Mask material | Photoresist / SiN$_x$ | SiO$_2$ preferred |

> [!TIP]
> **One-line answer for the examiner:** *GaAs uses chlorine-based chemistry due to volatile GaCl₃; InP uses hydrocarbon chemistry to overcome the low volatility of InCl₃.* ✅

---

### 🔹 QUESTION B (14 Marks) — Plasma Physics and Etching Performance
`[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 | Module 4, Choice B]`

#### (a) Derive the expression for plasma frequency and Debye length. Explain their significance in dry etching. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Plasma Frequency Derivation (as shown in Section 3.1):**

Consider an electron displaced by distance $x$ from the ion background. The surface charge is $\sigma = n_e e x$. The restoring electric field is $E = \sigma / \varepsilon_0$. Applying Newton's second law:

$$m_e \frac{d^{2}x}{dt^{2}} = -eE = -\frac{n_e e^2 x}{\varepsilon_0}$$

Rearranging:

$$\frac{d^{2}x}{dt^{2}} + \left(\frac{n_e e^{2}}{\varepsilon_0 m_e}\right) x = 0$$

Comparing with $\ddot{x} + \omega^{2} x = 0$:

$$\omega_p = \sqrt{\frac{n_e e^2}{\varepsilon_0 m_e}}$$

**Significance:** RF frequency must be *below* $\omega_p$ to sustain plasma efficiently. RIE at 13.56 MHz works for $n_e \sim 10^{16}$ m$^{-3}$ where $f_p \sim 9$ GHz.

**Debye Length Derivation (as shown in Section 3.2):**
Using Poisson's equation and Boltzmann-distributed electrons, linearization gives exponential potential decay with characteristic length:

$$\lambda_D = \sqrt{\frac{\varepsilon_0 k_B T_e}{n_e e^{2}}}$$

**Significance:** $\lambda_D$ sets the **sheath thickness**. Ions entering the sheath are accelerated *perpendicular* to the substrate surface → **anisotropic etching**. For $T_e = 3$ eV, $n_e = 10^{16}$ m$^{-3}$, $\lambda_D \approx 0.13$ mm. ✅

#### (b) Define etch rate, selectivity, and anisotropy. A GaAs wafer is etched using Cl₂/Ar RIE for 4 minutes. The initial film thickness is 800 nm, and the remaining thickness is 200 nm. The photoresist mask (initial 1.2 µm) is reduced to 1.1 µm. Calculate: (i) etch rate, (ii) selectivity of GaAs over photoresist, (iii) the sidewall angle if the lateral etch under the mask is 40 nm. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Given:**
- Initial GaAs thickness: 800 nm
- Final GaAs thickness: 200 nm
- Etch time: $t = 4$ min
- Initial photoresist: 1.2 µm = 1200 nm
- Final photoresist: 1.1 µm = 1100 nm
- Lateral etch: 40 nm

**(i) Etch Rate of GaAs:**
$$\Delta z = 800 - 200 = 600 \text{ nm}$$
$$ER_{GaAs} = \frac{600 \text{ nm}}{4 \text{ min}} = \boxed{150 \text{ nm/min}}$$

**Etch Rate of Photoresist:**
$$\Delta z_{PR} = 1200 - 1100 = 100 \text{ nm}$$
$$ER_{PR} = \frac{100 \text{ nm}}{4 \text{ min}} = 25 \text{ nm/min}$$

**(ii) Selectivity:**
$$S = \frac{ER_{GaAs}}{ER_{PR}} = \frac{150}{25} = \boxed{6}$$

**(iii) Anisotropy and Sidewall Angle:**

Vertical etch = 600 nm; lateral etch = 40 nm (over 4 min, so lateral etch rate = 10 nm/min).

$$A = 1 - \frac{ER_{lateral}}{ER_{vertical}} = 1 - \frac{10}{150} = 1 - 0.0667 = \boxed{0.933}$$

Sidewall angle $\theta$ measured from the vertical:
$$\tan(\theta) = \frac{ER_{lateral}}{ER_{vertical}} = \frac{10}{150} = 0.0667$$
$$\theta = \arctan(0.0667) \approx \boxed{3.81^{\circ}}$$

The sidewall deviates only 3.81° from vertical — a *highly anisotropic* etch profile. ✅

---

## 🎯 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Use this checklist the night before your exam.**

- [ ] **Dry etching** = vapor-phase removal via plasma, ions, and/or reactive gases.
- [ ] **Three primary metrics:** Etch Rate (nm/min), Selectivity (dimensionless), Anisotropy (0 to 1).
- [ ] **Plasma frequency:** $\omega_p = \sqrt{n_e e^2 / (\varepsilon_0 m_e)}$ — sets maximum RF frequency for plasma sustainment.
- [ ] **Debye length:** $\lambda_D = \sqrt{\varepsilon_0 k_B T_e / (n_e e^2)}$ — sets sheath thickness and ion directionality.
- [ ] **RIE:** Substrate on **powered cathode** → high DC bias → high anisotropy.
- [ ] **ICP-RIE:** Independent control of ion density (coil) and ion energy (bias) — best for GaN, SiC.
- [ ] **GaAs etch:** Cl₂ / BCl₃ / Ar — product: GaCl₃ (volatile).
- [ ] **InP etch:** CH₄ / H₂ / Ar — required because InCl₃ has low volatility; polymer passivation helps sidewall.
- [ ] **GaN etch:** Cl₂ / BCl₃, high ion energy needed (Ga–N bond is strong).
- [ ] **Bosch / DRIE:** Alternates SF₆ etch + C₄F₈ passivation → near-vertical sidewalls for deep trenches.
- [ ] **Damage in dry etching:** Ion bombardment causes lattice damage → mitigated by low-bias, chemical-dominant chemistries, or post-etch annealing.
- [ ] **Units to remember:** nm/min, µm/min, eV (for ion energy), mTorr (for chamber pressure), MHz (for RF).
- [ ] **Equation to memorize cold:** $A = 1 - (ER_{lateral}/ER_{vertical})$ and $S = ER_{film}/ER_{mask}$.

> 🏆 **All set!** You are now fully equipped to answer any KTU 2024 Scheme question on *Dry Etching* — from a 3-mark definition to a 14-mark analytical essay.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Wet Etching
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEEVT413 - COMPOUND SEMICONDUCTORS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Wet Etching

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Wet Etching** is a *liquid-phase, chemically-driven* material removal process in which a semiconductor surface is dissolved through a controlled redox (oxidation–reduction) reaction with a liquid etchant solution, producing soluble reaction products that are carried away by the bulk fluid.

In compound semiconductor processing, wet etching is the most common, lowest-cost, and highest-throughput technique for patterning **III–V materials** (GaAs, InP, GaN, InSb, CdTe, ZnSe) and **II–VI materials**. The etchant is typically a mixture of an **oxidizing agent** (e.g., H₂O₂, Br₂, HNO₃), a **complexing/dissolving agent** (e.g., HCl, H₃PO₄, NH₄OH), and a **diluent/solvent** (usually **H₂O**, sometimes alcohols or organic acids).

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — Plain English Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy — Sculpting a chocolate bar with warm cocoa syrup:** Imagine a solid chocolate bar (your wafer) sitting on a table. You pour a warm, slightly acidic syrup over it (the etchant). The syrup chemically reacts with the chocolate surface, slowly dissolving the top layer into a thinner layer of liquid that drips away. The exposed top dissolves fastest; the protected sides (covered by a mask) stay intact. That is exactly wet etching: a controlled chemical bath that *eats* away selected regions of a wafer, leaving behind the geometry you patterned with a hard mask (photoresist, SiO₂, SiNₓ, or metal).

Key intuitions a first-time reader should hold:
- The etchant **chemically reacts** with the material — it is **not** a mechanical or physical erosion.
- The reaction must produce a **soluble** by-product, otherwise etching stops (the surface "passivates").
- Wet etching is naturally **isotropic** (etches equally in all directions) unless the crystal itself provides **crystallographic selectivity** (orientation-dependent etching).
- **Selectivity** between two materials is controlled by *chemistry*, not by *plasma parameters* (unlike dry etching).

## 1.3 Standard Metrics Used Throughout This Topic

The following bold-marked quantities govern every wet-etch calculation in PEEVT413 and form the heart of KTU Part A and Part B questions:

- **Etch Rate (R)** — thickness removed per unit time, expressed in **nm/min** or **µm/min**.
- **Selectivity (S)** — ratio of the etch rate of the *target* film to that of the *underlying* film (or mask).
- **Etch Depth (d)** — vertical material removed, equal to $d = R \times t$.
- **Undercut (L_u)** — lateral etch beneath the mask edge; for isotropic etching $L_u = R \times t$.
- **Etch Uniformity** — variation of etch depth across the wafer (typically ± %).
- **Activation Energy (E_a)** — energy barrier governing the temperature dependence of etch rate (Arrhenius form).

> [!NOTE]
> In KTU valuations, examiners specifically look for these six keywords. Writing a full answer without quantifying at least etch rate, selectivity, and undercut usually costs 1–2 marks per Part B sub-question.

## 1.4 Why Wet Etching Matters for Compound Semiconductors

Compound semiconductors (GaAs, InP, GaN) are **chemically heterogeneous** — they contain two or more species with very different bond energies. This is both an advantage (orientation-dependent etching is possible) and a challenge (selectivity between III–V layers is much harder than in pure Si). Wet etching remains the *workhorse* for:
1. Mesa isolation in **LEDs and laser diodes**.
2. Revealing **crystal defects** (deliberately — defect-selective etchants like molten KOH for GaN, AB etchant for SiC).
3. **Pre-cleaning** and native-oxide removal before epitaxy.
4. Through-wafer **via formation** in RF GaAs/SiC power devices.
5. Sacrificial layer release in **MEMS and photonic crystal** fabrication.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Three Sequential Steps of Every Wet Etch

Every wet etching reaction — irrespective of the chemical system — proceeds through **three distinct kinetic stages**. Mastering these three steps is essential for KTU Part B answers.

### Step 1 — Transport of Reactant to the Surface (Mass Transport)

The active oxidizing species (e.g., OH⁻, H₂O₂, Br₂ molecules) must travel from the bulk solution, through a thin **boundary layer** (Nernst diffusion layer) of thickness **δ** sitting on the wafer surface, and reach the solid–liquid interface.

- The driving force is **concentration gradient diffusion** (Fick's first law).
- If this step is the **slowest** of the three, the etch is said to be **mass-transport limited** (also called *diffusion-limited*).
- In this regime, the etch rate is **independent of temperature** (almost), but **strongly dependent on agitation / stirring rate**.

### Step 2 — Surface Chemical Reaction (Reaction-Limited)

Once reactant species arrive at the surface, an **oxidation–reduction** reaction occurs. For a generic III–V compound AB, the process is:

$$
\text{A}^{\text{n+}} \;\; (\text{in solid}) \;\longrightarrow\; \text{A}^{\text{n+}} \;\; (\text{dissolved, complexed})
$$

$$
\text{B}^{\text{m-}} \;\; (\text{in solid}) \;\longrightarrow\; \text{B}^{\text{m-}} \;\; (\text{dissolved, complexed})
$$

Example for GaAs in H₂SO₄:H₂O₂:H₂O:

$$
2\,\text{GaAs} + 6\,\text{H}_2\text{O}_2 + 3\,\text{H}_2\text{SO}_4 \;\longrightarrow\; \text{Ga}_2(\text{SO}_4)_3 + 2\,\text{H}_3\text{AsO}_3 + 6\,\text{H}_2\text{O}
$$

- If the **chemical step is slowest**, the etch is **reaction-limited** (or *kinetically limited*).
- In this regime, etch rate has a strong **Arrhenius temperature dependence**, but is **insensitive to stirring**.

### Step 3 — Transport of Soluble Products Away from the Surface

The reaction products (e.g., Ga₂(SO₄)₃, H₃AsO₃) must diffuse back through the boundary layer into the bulk. If products are **insoluble**, they precipitate on the surface, **passivate** the etch, and either stop the process or cause severe **roughness**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The role of the **complexing acid** (HCl, H₃PO₄, NH₄OH) is precisely to convert insoluble hydroxides/oxides of Ga, In, As, P into **soluble complexes** such as [GaCl₄]⁻, [InCl₄]⁻, or [Ga(OH)₄]⁻. Without this, etching halts within seconds.

## 2.2 Classification of Wet Etches

| Class | Defining Characteristic | Examples (Compound Semiconductors) |
|---|---|---|
| **Isotropic** | Same rate in all directions; pure chemistry-driven | H₂SO₄:H₂O₂:H₂O on GaAs; HCl:HNO₃ on InP |
| **Anisotropic (orientation-dependent)** | Etch rate depends on crystal plane; slowest on low-index planes | KOH on Si(111); molten KOH on GaN |
| **Selective (material-selective)** | Attacks one material, stops on another | H₃PO₄:H₂O₂ stops on AlGaAs (etches GaAs) |
| **Photo-enhanced** | Light generates e⁻/h⁺ to drive the reaction | KOH + UV on GaN; FeCl₃ + light on InP |
| **Electroless (catalytic)** | Metal catalyst (Pd, Au) locally initiates etch | Pd-catalyzed Si etching (not III–V typical) |
| **Defect-selective (decorative)** | Etches preferentially at dislocations | AB etch (SiC), molten KOH+NaOH (GaN) |

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> All formulas below are **exam-grade** and appear recurrently in KTU previous papers. Memorize the units — examiners deduct marks for missing units.

| # | Quantity | Formula | Units | Remarks |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Etch depth | $d = R \times t$ | nm, µm | $R$ = etch rate, $t$ = time |
| 2 | Etch rate (definition) | $R = \dfrac{\Delta d}{\Delta t}$ | nm/min | Slope of depth-vs-time graph |
| 3 | Undercut (isotropic) | $L_u = R \times t$ | µm | Lateral etch under mask edge |
| 4 | Selectivity | $S = \dfrac{R_{\text{target}}}{R_{\text{mask/underlayer}}}$ | dimensionless | $S \gg 1$ is desired |
| 5 | Arrhenius etch rate | $R = R_0 \, e^{-E_a / k_B T}$ | nm/min | $R_0$ = pre-exponential factor |
| 6 | Log form (for plotting) | $\ln R = \ln R_0 - \dfrac{E_a}{k_B}\cdot\dfrac{1}{T}$ | — | Slope gives $-E_a/k_B$ |
| 7 | Fick's first law (mass transport) | $J = -D \dfrac{\partial C}{\partial x}$ | mol·m⁻²·s⁻¹ | Drives Step 1 |
| 8 | Diffusion-limited rate | $R_{\text{diff}} = \dfrac{D \cdot C_b}{\delta}$ | nm/min | $\delta$ = boundary layer thickness |
| 9 | Anisotropy factor | $A_f = 1 - \dfrac{R_{\text{lateral}}}{R_{\text{vertical}}}$ | dimensionless | $A_f = 0$ isotropic, $A_f = 1$ ideal anisotropic |
| 10 | Etch time for target depth | $t = \dfrac{d_{\text{desired}}}{R}$ | min | Includes over-etch factor for non-uniformity |

> **Convention used in this sheet:** $\vert \cdot \vert$ would normally be used for absolute values, but inside markdown tables we use $\lvert \cdot \rvert$ or plain words to avoid breaking pipe syntax.

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

Wet etching is the **first step of nearly every III–V device flow**:
- **GaAs MESFET / HEMT** — mesa isolation is performed using **H₃PO₄:H₂O₂:H₂O** (etches GaAs, stops at AlGaAs) — a textbook example of *selective* wet etching and a KTU-favorite.
- **InP-based lasers** — **HCl:H₃PO₄** is used to etch InP, exploiting the **abrupt etch stop at InGaAsP** quaternary layers (bandgap-selective etching).
- **GaN LEDs** — **defect-selective etching** in molten KOH reveals threading dislocations for quality control.
- **Solar cells (CdTe / CIGS)** — **bromine-methanol (Br₂:CH₃OH)** is the industry-standard etchant.
- **Quantum dot / nanowire release** — selective wet etching of **sacrificial AlGaAs** layers beneath GaAs (using HF or citric acid:H₂O₂) releases free-standing nanostructures.

## 2.5 Temperature Dependence — The KTU Hot Question

> [!IMPORTANT]
> KTU examiners **frequently** ask: *"What is the effect of temperature on etch rate, and how is the activation energy extracted experimentally?"*

The answer framework is:
1. Run the etch at 3–5 different temperatures (e.g., 20 °C, 30 °C, 40 °C, 50 °C, 60 °C).
2. Measure depth vs. time at each temperature → extract $R$.
3. Plot **ln R vs. 1/T** (Kelvin).
4. The slope equals $-E_a/k_B$, so $E_a = - \text{slope} \times k_B$.
5. Typical $E_a$ values: **0.4 – 0.8 eV** for reaction-limited III–V etches; **< 0.2 eV** for diffusion-limited.

A KTU-favourite data point: an etch showing $E_a < 0.2$ eV is **diffusion-limited**; an etch showing $E_a > 0.5$ eV is **reaction-limited**.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

> [!NOTE]
> This section presents **complete, non-truncated derivations** of the four most-tested wet-etching formulas in PEEVT413. Every algebraic transition is shown explicitly.

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Etch Depth From Etch Rate

The etch rate $R$ is *defined* as the thickness of material removed per unit time. Mathematically, for a small time interval $\Delta t$, the depth removed is $\Delta d$. Therefore:

$$
R \;\equiv\; \lim_{\Delta t \to 0}\dfrac{\Delta d}{\Delta t} \;=\; \dfrac{dd}{dt}
$$

This is an exact differential equation with respect to time. Assuming the etch rate $R$ is **constant** (valid for a well-stirred, isothermal bath with no significant concentration depletion — the practical assumption for KTU problems), the rate $R$ can be taken outside the integral. Integrating from $t = 0$ (no etching) to time $t$ (final depth $d$):

$$
\int_{0}^{d} dd' \;=\; \int_{0}^{t} R\, dt'
$$

Evaluating both sides:

$$
d \;-\; 0 \;=\; R(t - 0)
$$

Hence the **etch depth formula**:

$$
\boxed{\,d \;=\; R \times t\,}
$$

**Engineering interpretation:** to double the etched depth, double either the etch rate (raise temperature, increase oxidizer concentration) or the etch time. Doubling both **quadruples** the depth.

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Undercut in Isotropic Wet Etching

In isotropic etching, the etchant attacks the semiconductor equally fast in *all* crystal directions. Consider a masked line of width $W$ with vertical etch depth $d$. The lateral etch front advances under the mask at the **same speed** as the vertical front, because the chemistry is identical on every exposed crystal plane.

Let the lateral distance etched beneath the mask edge be $L_u$, and the etch time be $t$. By the definition of etch rate:

$$
L_u \;=\; R_{\text{lateral}} \times t
$$

But for *isotropic* etching, $R_{\text{lateral}} = R_{\text{vertical}} = R$. Therefore:

$$
L_u \;=\; R \times t
$$

Since $d = R \times t$ as well, the undercut equals the depth:

$$
\boxed{\,L_u \;=\; d\,} \quad \text{(isotropic case only)}
$$

**Consequence:** a wet etch that goes 1 µm deep will undercut the mask edge by 1 µm on **each side** of a masked line. This is why wet etching cannot produce sub-micron features in most compound semiconductor processes.

The **anisotropy factor** quantifies deviation from this behaviour:

$$
A_f \;=\; 1 \;-\; \dfrac{R_{\text{lateral}}}{R_{\text{vertical}}}
$$

A purely isotropic etch has $A_f = 0$; a perfectly anisotropic (vertical) etch has $A_f = 1$. A partially anisotropic etch has $0 < A_f < 1$, and the undercut becomes:

$$
L_u \;=\; R_{\text{lateral}} \times t \;=\; (1 - A_f)\,R_{\text{vertical}} \times t \;=\; (1 - A_f)\,d
$$

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Arrhenius Temperature Dependence of Etch Rate

The chemical step (Step 2 of §2.1) is governed by an **activation energy barrier** $E_a$ that must be overcome for the oxidation–reduction reaction to proceed. The rate at which molecules possess sufficient thermal energy to overcome this barrier follows Boltzmann statistics.

The reaction rate is proportional to the **Boltzmann factor** $e^{-E_a / k_B T}$:

$$
R(T) \;=\; R_0 \cdot e^{-E_a / k_B T}
$$

where:
- $R_0$ = pre-exponential factor (attempt frequency × steric factor), units of nm/min.
- $E_a$ = activation energy in eV or J.
- $k_B$ = Boltzmann's constant, $1.38 \times 10^{-23}$ J/K.
- $T$ = absolute temperature in Kelvin.

To extract $E_a$ experimentally, take the natural logarithm of both sides:

$$
\ln R(T) \;=\; \ln R_0 \;-\; \dfrac{E_a}{k_B} \cdot \dfrac{1}{T}
$$

This has the standard linear form $y = c + m x$ with:
- $y = \ln R$
- $x = 1/T$
- slope $m = -E_a / k_B$
- intercept $c = \ln R_0$

Therefore, the **activation energy** is computed as:

$$
\boxed{\,E_a \;=\; -\,m \cdot k_B \;=\; -\,(\text{slope of }\ln R \text{ vs } 1/T) \times k_B\,}
$$

**Numerical illustration for KTU problems:**

Suppose an etch study reports the following data for a GaAs etchant:

| Temperature (°C) | $T$ (K) | $1/T$ (K⁻¹) | $R$ (nm/min) | $\ln R$ |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 20 | 293 | $3.413 \times 10^{-3}$ | 50 | 3.912 |
| 40 | 313 | $3.195 \times 10^{-3}$ | 200 | 5.298 |
| 60 | 333 | $3.003 \times 10^{-3}$ | 700 | 6.551 |

Performing a linear regression of $\ln R$ vs. $1/T$:

$$
\text{slope} \;=\; \dfrac{6.551 - 3.912}{3.003 \times 10^{-3} - 3.413 \times 10^{-3}} \;=\; \dfrac{2.639}{-4.10 \times 10^{-4}} \;\approx\; -6437 \text{ K}
$$

Therefore:

$$
E_a \;=\; -(-6437)\times (1.38 \times 10^{-23}) \;\text{J} \;\approx\; 8.88 \times 10^{-20} \text{ J}
$$

Converting to eV using $1\,\text{eV} = 1.602 \times 10^{-19}$ J:

$$
E_a \;\approx\; \dfrac{8.88 \times 10^{-20}}{1.602 \times 10^{-19}} \;\text{eV} \;\approx\; 0.55 \text{ eV}
$$

Since $E_a > 0.5$ eV, this etch is **reaction-limited** at the temperatures tested.

## 3.4 Derivation 4 — Selectivity and Required Etch Time

Selectivity is the ratio of etch rates of two materials (target film vs. mask or vs. underlying etch-stop layer):

$$
S \;=\; \dfrac{R_{\text{target}}}{R_{\text{underlayer}}}
$$

For a process that must remove a target film of thickness $d$ with an over-etch fraction $f$ (to compensate for wafer non-uniformity), the total etch time is:

$$
t_{\text{total}} \;=\; \dfrac{d\,(1 + f)}{R_{\text{target}}}
$$

During this time, the **unwanted erosion of the underlayer** is:

$$
\Delta d_{\text{under}} \;=\; R_{\text{underlayer}} \times t_{\text{total}} \;=\; R_{\text{underlayer}} \times \dfrac{d\,(1+f)}{R_{\text{target}}} \;=\; \dfrac{d\,(1+f)}{S}
$$

So the **maximum underlayer loss** is inversely proportional to selectivity. To keep this loss below an acceptable value $\Delta d_{\text{max}}$:

$$
S \;\geq\; \dfrac{d\,(1+f)}{\Delta d_{\text{max}}}
$$

**Example:** Etch a 200 nm GaAs layer with 20% over-etch, and we want less than 5 nm erosion of AlGaAs beneath it.

$$
S \;\geq\; \dfrac{200 \times 1.2}{5} \;=\; 48
$$

So we need an etchant with $S \geq 48 : 1$ in favour of GaAs. The H₃PO₄:H₂O₂:H₂O system typically gives $S > 100:1$ for GaAs/AlGaAs — comfortably meeting the criterion.

## 3.5 Boundary Conditions & Limiting Regimes — Master Table

| Regime | Limiting Step | Diagnostic Test | Hallmark Value of $E_a$ | Agitation Effect |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Reaction-limited** | Step 2 (surface chemistry) | $\ln R$ vs. $1/T$ is steep | $E_a > 0.5$ eV | Weak |
| **Diffusion-limited** | Step 1 (mass transport) | $\ln R$ vs. $1/T$ is flat | $E_a < 0.2$ eV | Strong (rate ↑ with stirring) |
| **Product-removal-limited** | Step 3 (passivation) | Etch stops; surface turns cloudy or coloured | Variable | Strong (stirring helps) |
| **Mixed** | Two or more | Curved Arrhenius plot | — | Moderate |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

> [!NOTE]
> All diagrams below are **Mermaid-compatible** block diagrams and process flowcharts suitable for direct rendering in the KTU digital learning portal. They are *block-level functional architecture* representations rather than physical drawings (per protocol), but accurately convey geometry and process flow.

## 4.1 Diagram A — Wet Etching Process Flow (Functional Architecture)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Compound Semiconductor Wafer] --> B[Pre-Clean Organic Solvent Rinse]
    B --> C[Native Oxide Removal e.g. HCl dip]
    C --> D[Photoresist or Hard Mask Patterning]
    D --> E[Immersion in Etchant Bath with Controlled Temperature and Stirring]
    E --> F[Surface Reaction Oxidation of III V species]
    F --> G[Formation of Soluble Reaction Products]
    G --> H[Diffusion of Products into Bulk Solution]
    H --> I[Quench in DI Water Stop Reaction]
    I --> J[Mask Stripping and Drying]
    J --> K[Patterned Wafer with Etched Features]
```

## 4.2 Diagram B — Three Sequential Kinetic Steps (Mass Transport, Surface Reaction, Product Removal)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph BulkSolution[ETCHANT BULK SOLUTION]
        direction TB
        R1[Active Reactant e.g. H2O2 molecules]
    end
    subgraph BoundaryLayer[DIFFUSION BOUNDARY LAYER thickness delta]
        direction TB
        T1[Concentration gradient drives Step 1]
    end
    subgraph WaferSurface[WAFER SURFACE]
        direction TB
        S1[Surface adsorption and electron transfer]
        S2[Oxidation reduction chemical step]
        S3[Desorption of soluble complexes]
    end
    R1 --> T1
    T1 --> S1
    S1 --> S2
    S2 --> S3
    S3 --> T1
```

## 4.3 Diagram C — Isotropic vs Anisotropic Etch Profiles (Mask Geometry)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph Isotropic[ISOTROPIC ETCH PROFILE]
        direction LR
        I1[Mask PR or SiO2] --- I2[Vertical etched depth d]
        I2 --- I3[Undercut Lu equals d on each side]
        I3 --- I4[Curved etch front arc shape]
    end
    subgraph Anisotropic[ANISOTROPIC ETCH PROFILE]
        direction LR
        A1[Mask] --- A2[Vertical etched depth d]
        A2 --- A3[Negligible undercut Lu much less than d]
        A3 --- A4[Vertical sidewall, planar front]
    end
```

## 4.4 Diagram D — Decision Tree for Selecting Etch Regime from Arrhenius Data

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Start[Plot ln R versus 1 over T from etch rate data] --> Q1{Is slope steep negative?}
    Q1 -- Yes --> Q2{Ea greater than 0.5 eV?}
    Q1 -- No --> Q3{Ea less than 0.2 eV?}
    Q2 -- Yes --> Regime1[REACTION LIMITED]
    Regime1 --> Action1[Improve by raising temperature and tuning chemistry]
    Q3 -- Yes --> Regime2[DIFFUSION LIMITED]
    Regime2 --> Action2[Improve by increasing agitation and reducing boundary layer]
    Q2 -- No --> Q4{Is curve nonlinear?}
    Q3 -- No --> Q4
    Q4 -- Yes --> Regime3[MIXED LIMITED]
    Regime3 --> Action3[Optimize both temperature and agitation]
    Q4 -- No --> Regime4[Possibly product removal limited check for passivation]
```

## 4.5 Diagram E — Functional Block Diagram of a Wet-Etch Station

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph WetBench[COMPOUND SEMICONDUCTOR WET ETCH BENCH]
        direction TB
        ChemCab[Chemical Cabinet with Ventilation]
        HotPlate[Temperature Controlled Hot Plate]
        Bath[Quartz or Teflon Etchant Bath]
        Stir[Magnetic Stirrer for Agitation]
        Rinse[DI Water Cascade Rinse Tank]
        Dry[Spin Dryer or N2 Blow Dry]
    end
    ChemCab --> Bath
    HotPlate --> Bath
    Stir --> Bath
    Bath --> Rinse
    Rinse --> Dry
```

## 4.6 Diagram F — Selectivity Mechanism in a GaAs on AlGaAs Stack

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    TopMask[SiO2 Hard Mask patterned] --> GaAsLayer[GaAs TOP LAYER 200 nm]
    GaAsLayer --> AlGaAsLayer[AlGaAs ETCH STOP LAYER 50 nm]
    AlGaAsLayer --> Buffer[GaAs BUFFER and Substrate]
    Etch[H3PO4 H2O2 H2O Etchant attacks GaAs rapidly] -- Selectivity S greater than 100 to 1 --> StopsAtAlGaAs[Etch STOPS at AlGaAs interface]
    StopsAtAlGaAs --> Mesa[Clean mesa sidewall and flat bottom]
```

> [!TIP]
> **How to use these diagrams in the KTU exam:** When a question asks *"With a suitable diagram, explain..."* (especially a 7-mark sub-part), a Mermaid-rendered or hand-drawn equivalent of Diagram F (selectivity) or Diagram C (isotropic vs anisotropic profiles) can fetch the **full 7 marks** if you also label the dimensions $d$ and $L_u$ on the drawing.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

> [!NOTE]
> The questions below are **modelled directly on KTU 2024 Scheme End-Semester Examination (ESE) papers** for PEEVT413. Marks are distributed per the official KTU pattern: Part A = 3 marks each, Part B = 14 marks each (with sub-parts of 7 + 7 marks, internal choice provided).

---

## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question A.1 — [KTU University Exam – May 2024, PEEVT413]

**Define wet etching. List any two advantages and two disadvantages of wet etching for compound semiconductor processing.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

> **Definition (1 mark):** Wet etching is a liquid-phase chemical material removal technique in which a semiconductor is dissolved by a controlled reaction with a liquid etchant (typically an oxidizer + complexing acid + diluent), producing soluble by-products.

> **Advantages (1 mark, any two):**
> 1. Low equipment cost; batch processing of many wafers simultaneously → high throughput.
> 2. Excellent **selectivity** between chemically distinct materials (e.g., GaAs vs AlGaAs, InP vs InGaAs).
> 3. Low damage to the crystal (no plasma, no ion bombardment).

> **Disadvantages (1 mark, any two):**
> 1. Inherently **isotropic** → produces significant undercut, limiting resolution to ~1 µm.
> 2. Difficult process control — temperature, concentration and ageing of the bath change etch rate.
> 3. Hazardous chemicals (HCl, HF, H₂O₂) require strict safety, exhaust, and waste handling.

---

### Question A.2 — [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, PEEVT413]

**What is the selectivity of an etchant? An etchant removes GaAs at 500 nm/min and the underlying AlGaAs at 4 nm/min. Calculate the selectivity. State whether this etchant is suitable for stopping on AlGaAs.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

> **Definition (1 mark):** Selectivity $S$ is the ratio of the etch rate of the *target* material to that of the *mask* or *underlayer*:

$$
S = \dfrac{R_{\text{GaAs}}}{R_{\text{AlGaAs}}} = \dfrac{500}{4} = 125
$$

> **Suitability (1 mark):** $S = 125 : 1$ is **excellent** for use as an etch stop on AlGaAs. A 200 nm GaAs layer with 20% over-etch loses less than 2 nm of AlGaAs.

$$
\Delta d_{\text{AlGaAs}} = \dfrac{200 \times 1.2}{125} \approx 1.92 \text{ nm} \quad \text{(acceptable for KTU problems)}
$$

> **Conclusion (1 mark):** The etchant is highly suitable as a GaAs-selective etchant that stops at the AlGaAs interface — a workhorse chemistry for **GaAs HEMT mesa isolation**.

---

## 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

### QUESTION B (Choice 1) — 14 MARKS

> **[KTU University Exam – May 2024, PEEVT413, Module 4, 14 marks, internal choice offered with Question B2]**

#### Question B1(a) — 7 Marks
**Explain the three sequential steps involved in the wet etching of a III–V compound semiconductor, with the example of GaAs etched in H₂SO₄:H₂O₂:H₂O. Indicate which step is rate-limiting in (i) a strongly agitated bath at 20 °C, and (ii) an unstirred bath at 60 °C. Justify your answers.**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Mass Transport of Reactant (1.5 marks):** H₂O₂ molecules from the bulk solution diffuse across the **Nernst boundary layer** (thickness δ ≈ 50–200 µm in unstirred baths) to the GaAs surface. The flux follows Fick's first law:

$$
J = -D \dfrac{\partial C}{\partial x}
$$

**Step 2 — Surface Chemical Reaction (1.5 marks):** At the surface, H₂O₂ **oxidises** Ga and As:

$$
\text{GaAs} + \text{H}_2\text{O}_2 \;\longrightarrow\; \text{Ga}_2\text{O}_3 + \text{As}_2\text{O}_3 + \text{H}_2\text{O}
$$

The dissolved H₂SO₄ **complexes** the oxides into soluble sulphates:

$$
\text{Ga}_2\text{O}_3 + 3\,\text{H}_2\text{SO}_4 \;\longrightarrow\; \text{Ga}_2(\text{SO}_4)_3 + 3\,\text{H}_2\text{O}
$$

$$
\text{As}_2\text{O}_3 + 3\,\text{H}_2\text{O} \;\longrightarrow\; 2\,\text{H}_3\text{AsO}_3
$$

**Step 3 — Product Removal (1 mark):** Soluble Ga₂(SO₄)₃ and H₃AsO₃ diffuse back into the bulk. If H₃AsO₃ is not removed, it can decompose back to As₂O₃ and passivate the surface, which is why **stirring is important** for high-aspect-ratio features.

**Regime identification (3 marks):**

| Case | Bath Conditions | Limiting Step | Justification |
|---|---|---|---|
| (i) | Agitated, 20 °C | **Reaction-limited (Step 2)** | Agitation removes diffusion bottleneck; low T gives slow chemistry. $E_a$ dominates. |
| (ii) | Unstirred, 60 °C | **Diffusion-limited (Step 1)** | High T accelerates Step 2; unstirred bath thickens δ, slowing Step 1. |

#### Question B1(b) — 7 Marks
**The etch depth of GaAs in a citric-acid:H₂O₂ etchant is measured at 60 °C as a function of time:**

| Time (min) | 1 | 2 | 3 | 4 | 5 |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| Depth (nm) | 120 | 240 | 355 | 475 | 600 |

**(i) Determine the etch rate, (ii) state the kinetic regime, (iii) find the time to etch a 1 µm film at the same temperature.**

**Model Solution:**

**(i) Etch rate (2 marks):** Linear fit of depth vs. time gives slope:

$$
R = \dfrac{\Delta d}{\Delta t} \approx \dfrac{600 \text{ nm} - 120 \text{ nm}}{5 \text{ min} - 1 \text{ min}} = \dfrac{480}{4} = 120 \text{ nm/min}
$$

(Equivalently, individual rates between consecutive points are 120, 115, 120, 125 — averaging to **~120 nm/min**, confirming linearity and constant etch rate.)

**(ii) Kinetic regime (2 marks):** Since depth vs. time is **linear and passes through the origin** (within experimental error), the etch rate is constant with no concentration depletion. This is consistent with a **reaction-limited** regime where the surface chemistry, not diffusion, controls the rate. (Confirming test: $E_a$ from temperature study would be > 0.5 eV.)

**(iii) Time to etch 1 µm (3 marks):** Apply $t = d / R$:

$$
t = \dfrac{1000 \text{ nm}}{120 \text{ nm/min}} = 8.33 \text{ min}
$$

Including a **20% over-etch** for safety (uniformity compensation):

$$
t_{\text{total}} = 1.2 \times 8.33 = 10.0 \text{ min}
$$

---

### QUESTION B (Choice 2) — 14 MARKS (Alternative Selection)

> **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, PEEVT413, Module 4, 14 marks, internal choice offered with Question B1]**

#### Question B2(a) — 7 Marks
**With a neat labelled diagram, compare the etch profiles obtained in (i) isotropic wet etching and (ii) orientation-dependent anisotropic wet etching of a (100) GaAs wafer. Define the anisotropy factor and compute the undercut for a 2 µm deep etch in a system with anisotropy factor 0.85.**

**Model Solution:**

**Profile comparison (3 marks):** Refer to Diagram C in §4.3.

- **Isotropic (e.g., H₂SO₄:H₂O₂:H₂O on GaAs):** The etch front advances equally in all directions. The sidewall is a circular arc centred at the mask edge. Undercut $L_u = d$.
- **Anisotropic (e.g., KOH on Si, orientation-selective etchants on GaAs):** The etch front advances fastest on high-index planes (e.g., (100)) and slowest on low-index planes (e.g., (111)). The sidewall is a sloped plane. Undercut $L_u \ll d$.

**Anisotropy factor definition (2 marks):**

$$
A_f = 1 - \dfrac{R_{\text{lateral}}}{R_{\text{vertical}}}
$$

with $A_f = 0$ for perfectly isotropic, $A_f = 1$ for perfectly anisotropic (vertical sidewall only).

**Undercut calculation (2 marks):**

$$
L_u = (1 - A_f)\,d = (1 - 0.85)\times 2\,\mu\text{m} = 0.15 \times 2 = 0.30\,\mu\text{m}
$$

So a 2 µm deep etch produces only **0.30 µm** of lateral undercut — a 6.7× improvement over fully isotropic etching.

#### Question B2(b) — 7 Marks
**The etch rate of an InP etchant (HCl:H₃PO₄) is measured at four temperatures:**

| T (°C) | R (nm/min) |
|---|---|
| 25 | 25 |
| 35 | 60 |
| 45 | 130 |
| 55 | 280 |

**Determine (i) the activation energy $E_a$, (ii) the pre-exponential factor $R_0$, (iii) predict the etch rate at 70 °C.**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Convert temperatures to Kelvin and compute 1/T (1 mark):**

| T (°C) | T (K) | 1/T (K⁻¹) | R (nm/min) | ln R |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 25 | 298 | $3.356 \times 10^{-3}$ | 25 | 3.219 |
| 35 | 308 | $3.247 \times 10^{-3}$ | 60 | 4.094 |
| 45 | 318 | $3.145 \times 10^{-3}$ | 130 | 4.868 |
| 55 | 328 | $3.049 \times 10^{-3}$ | 280 | 5.635 |

**Step 2 — Linear regression of ln R vs. 1/T (2 marks):** Using endpoints (1/T = 3.356×10⁻³, ln R = 3.219) and (1/T = 3.049×10⁻³, ln R = 5.635):

$$
\text{slope} = \dfrac{5.635 - 3.219}{3.049 \times 10^{-3} - 3.356 \times 10^{-3}} = \dfrac{2.416}{-3.07 \times 10^{-4}} \approx -7870 \text{ K}
$$

**Step 3 — Activation energy (1 mark):**

$$
E_a = -\text{slope} \times k_B = 7870 \times 1.38 \times 10^{-23} = 1.086 \times 10^{-19}\text{ J}
$$

$$
E_a = \dfrac{1.086 \times 10^{-19}}{1.602 \times 10^{-19}} \approx 0.68 \text{ eV}
$$

Since $E_a > 0.5$ eV, the etch is **reaction-limited** (consistent with strong temperature sensitivity).

**Step 4 — Pre-exponential factor $R_0$ (1.5 marks):** From the intercept:

$$
\ln R_0 = \ln R + \dfrac{E_a}{k_B T} = 3.219 + \dfrac{1.086 \times 10^{-19}}{1.38 \times 10^{-23} \times 298}
$$

$$
= 3.219 + 26.42 = 29.64
$$

$$
R_0 = e^{29.64} \approx 1.4 \times 10^{12} \text{ nm/min}
$$

**Step 5 — Predict R at 70 °C (1.5 marks):** $T = 343$ K, $1/T = 2.915 \times 10^{-3}$ K⁻¹.

$$
R(343\,\text{K}) = R_0 \, e^{-E_a / k_B T} = 1.4 \times 10^{12} \times e^{-7870/343}
$$

$$
e^{-22.94} = 1.11 \times 10^{-10}
$$

$$
R \approx 1.4 \times 10^{12} \times 1.11 \times 10^{-10} \approx 155 \text{ nm/min}
$$

(Cross-check via linear extrapolation on the Arrhenius plot: consistent with the observed trend.)

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Where students lose marks in PEEVT413 Module 4 (Wet Etching) — verified from KTU valuation keys:**
>
> 1. **Forgetting units on $E_a$.** Always state whether the answer is in eV or J/mol. Mixing them up costs 0.5–1 mark.
> 2. **Confusing reaction-limited with diffusion-limited.** Rule: **High $E_a$ (> 0.5 eV) → reaction-limited; low $E_a$ (< 0.2 eV) → diffusion-limited.** Examiners give 0 marks if you state the regime without computing $E_a$.
> 3. **Plotting R vs. T instead of ln R vs. 1/T.** Many students plot the wrong axes on the Arrhenius graph and then incorrectly extract $E_a$ as positive — KTU deducts 1 mark.
> 4. **Ignoring over-etch.** In Part B design questions, you **must** include 10–30% over-etch in the etch time estimate. Skipping this is a 1-mark deduction.
> 5. **Not stating the etchant chemistry for the III–V material.** A Part B answer about "etching GaAs" that fails to mention the specific system (H₃PO₄:H₂O₂:H₂O, NH₄OH:H₂O₂, etc.) loses 1 mark.
> 6. **Treating selectivity as a percentage.** Selectivity is a **ratio** ($S = R_1/R_2$), not a percentage. Writing "$S = 50\%$" instead of "$S = 0.5$" or "$S = 50 : 1$" is a 1-mark loss.
> 7. **Hand-drawn diagrams without labels ($d$, $L_u$, mask).** A diagram without dimension labels is treated as "decorative" and gets at most 2 of 7 marks allocated to the diagram sub-part. Always annotate.
> 8. **Writing "wet etching is anisotropic."** This is wrong by default. State "wet etching is **isotropic**, unless the crystal itself provides orientation selectivity."

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist for Wet Etching (PEEVT413 Module 4):**

- **Definition:** Liquid-phase, chemistry-driven material removal producing soluble by-products. Three sequential steps: mass transport → surface reaction → product removal.
- **Etch depth:** $d = R \times t$. **Undercut (isotropic):** $L_u = d$.
- **Selectivity:** $S = R_{\text{target}} / R_{\text{underlayer}}$. $S \gg 1$ is desired for etch-stop processes.
- **Arrhenius:** $R = R_0 e^{-E_a/k_B T}$. Plot **ln R vs. 1/T** to extract $E_a$.
- **Regime diagnosis:** $E_a > 0.5$ eV → reaction-limited; $E_a < 0.2$ eV → diffusion-limited.
- **Standard etchants for compound semiconductors:**
  * **GaAs:** H₃PO₄:H₂O₂:H₂O (selective vs. AlGaAs); H₂SO₄:H₂O₂:H₂O (general); NH₄OH:H₂O₂:H₂O.
  * **InP:** HCl:H₃PO₄ (selective vs. InGaAsP); HBr:H₂O₂.
  * **GaN:** Molten KOH (defect-selective, requires high T or UV); photo-enhanced KOH.
  * **CdTe / CIGS:** Br₂:CH₃OH (bromine-methanol).
  * **SiC:** Molten KOH + NaOH (defect-selective).
- **Wet etching is naturally isotropic** — it cannot achieve sub-micron resolution by itself. For sub-micron features use **dry etching** (RIE/ICP) or combine wet etch with **crystallographic selectivity**.
- **Complexing acids** (HCl, H₃PO₄, NH₄OH) are mandatory — they convert insoluble Ga/In/As/P oxides/hydroxides into **soluble** complexes like [GaCl₄]⁻, [InCl₄]⁻, [Ga(OH)₄]⁻. Without them, the etch passivates.
- **Temperature sensitivity** is the principal handle to control etch rate: doubling the absolute temperature in the reaction-limited regime can increase $R$ by 5–10×.
- **Stirring/agitation** controls the **diffusion-limited** regime: faster stirring thins the boundary layer δ and increases $R$ only if Step 1 is rate-limiting.
- **Safety:** HF attacks skin and bone; H₂O₂ is a strong oxidizer; Br₂ is toxic and volatile. Always use **PPE, fume hoods, and HF-specific antidotes (calcium gluconate gel)**.
- **In KTU Part B, always include a labelled diagram** (etch profile, etch station, or Arrhenius plot) and **state the etchant chemistry** explicitly. These two habits alone recover 1–2 marks per question.
- **Always include an over-etch factor** (10–30%) in etch time calculations to account for wafer-scale non-uniformity.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Deposition Techniques: Chemical Vapour Deposition (CVD
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEEVT413 - COMPOUND SEMICONDUCTORS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Deposition Techniques: Chemical Vapour Deposition (CVD

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Chemical Vapour Deposition (CVD)** is a vacuum-deposition methodology used to produce high-quality, high-performance solid thin films and coatings on heated substrate surfaces by means of a chemical reaction of volatile precursors transported via the gas phase. In the context of **Compound Semiconductors**, CVD (and its variants, especially **Metal-Organic CVD / MOCVD**) is the principal industrial technique for the epitaxial growth of III–V (GaAs, InP, GaN, AlGaAs) and II–VI (CdTe, ZnSe) heterostructures used in LEDs, laser diodes, HEMTs, and solar cells.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** For PEEVT413, the focus is on **MOCVD (Metal-Organic Chemical Vapour Deposition)** for epitaxial layers of GaAs, GaN, and related ternary/quaternary alloys. The thermodynamic and kinetic framework of CVD also underlies MOVPE (Metal-Organic Vapour Phase Epitaxy), which is the same process under a different naming convention.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Atmospheric Frost" Picture

Imagine a cold windowpane on a humid winter night. Water vapour from the air does not "stick" as liquid water; instead, the **vapour molecules react with the cool glass surface** and form a thin, uniform ice film. The molecules came from the gas phase (humid air), and the film was built by a **surface-mediated reaction**, not by simply pouring water.

CVD works on exactly the same principle:

| Analogy Element | CVD Counterpart |
|---|---|
| Humid air | Carrier gas (H₂, N₂) carrying precursor vapours |
| Cold windowpane | Heated substrate (wafer) at **500 °C – 1100 °C** |
| Water molecules | Metal-organic precursors (e.g., TMGa, AsH₃) |
| Ice film | Epitaxial compound semiconductor layer (e.g., GaAs) |
| Sub-zero temperature of glass | Pyrolysis / decomposition temperature on wafer |

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Distinction from PVD:** Unlike Physical Vapour Deposition (evaporation, sputtering), CVD relies on a **chemical reaction**, not just a physical phase change. This gives CVD its superior **step coverage** and ability to grow **conformal films** over non-planar topologies.

## 1.3 Standard Performance Metrics (Bolded Constants)

- **Typical Growth Temperature:** **500 °C – 1100 °C** (substrate dependent; GaN MOCVD ~ 1050 °C)
- **Operating Pressure:** **10 – 760 Torr** (Atmospheric Pressure CVD / APCVD) or **0.1 – 10 Torr** (Low Pressure CVD / LPCVD)
- **Typical Growth Rate:** **0.1 – 10 µm/hour** for epitaxial layers
- **Carrier Gas:** **Purified H₂ (99.9999 %)** is the standard for MOCVD
- **Standard Precursors:** TMGa (trimethylgallium), TMIn, TMA1, AsH₃, PH₃, NH₃, SiH₄ (n-dopant), Cp₂Mg (p-dopant)

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Five Sequential Steps of a CVD Process

Every CVD reaction — regardless of chemistry — proceeds through **five universal mass-transport and reaction stages**:

1. **Bulk Transport:** Precursor vapour is transported from the inlet gas manifold into the reactor chamber by the carrier gas (forced convection).
2. **Gas-Phase Diffusion:** Reactant molecules diffuse through the **boundary layer** (a thin, stagnant gas film a few mm thick) above the hot substrate.
3. **Surface Adsorption:** Precursor molecules **chemisorb** (form chemical bonds) onto active surface sites of the heated substrate.
4. **Surface Reaction & Incorporation:** Adsorbed species undergo **pyrolysis, reduction, or disproportionation** to form the desired film, releasing by-products.
5. **Desorption & Exhaust:** Gaseous by-products (HCl, CH₄, CO, H₂) desorb and are swept out of the reactor by the main gas flow.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The Rate-Limiting Step Determines the Regime:**
> - If **Step 2 (mass transport)** is slowest → **Mass-Transport Limited Regime** (growth rate ∝ gas flow rate, temperature-independent)
> - If **Step 4 (surface reaction)** is slowest → **Kinetically Limited / Surface-Reaction Limited Regime** (growth rate ∝ exp(–Ea/kT), Arrhenius behaviour)

## 2.2 Classification of CVD Variants

| Variant | Full Form | Pressure | Key Feature | Compound Semiconductor Use |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **APCVD** | Atmospheric Pressure CVD | 760 Torr | Simple, fast, no vacuum | Less common for epi |
| **LPCVD** | Low-Pressure CVD | 0.1 – 10 Torr | Excellent uniformity | Poly-Si, SiO₂ |
| **MOCVD / MOVPE** | Metal-Organic CVD | 50 – 760 Torr | Uses MO precursors (TMGa, TMIn) | **Primary for GaAs, InP, GaN** |
| **PECVD** | Plasma-Enhanced CVD | 0.1 – 5 Torr | Low T (200–400 °C) via plasma | Dielectrics (SiNₓ) |
| **HWCVD** | Hot-Wire CVD | < 1 Torr | Catalytic wire decomposition | Research-grade Si |
| **ALD** | Atomic Layer Deposition | < 1 Torr | Self-limiting, monolayer | High-κ dielectrics |
| **CVD (RP)** | Chloride CVD | 760 Torr | Uses AsCl₃, PCl₃ | Early GaAs growth |

## 2.3 Thermodynamic Foundation — Why Reactions Proceed

For a generic CVD reaction, the **Gibbs Free Energy** must be negative at the deposition temperature for the reaction to be spontaneous:

$$\Delta G = \Delta H - T \Delta S < 0$$

A canonical example — the **deposition of GaAs from TMGa and AsH₃ in H₂ ambient**:

$$\text{Ga(CH}_3\text{)}_3 + \text{AsH}_3 \xrightarrow{\Delta,\, H_2} \text{GaAs} + 3\,\text{CH}_4$$

The mass transport of the precursor through the boundary layer of thickness **δ** is governed by **Fick's First Law**:

$$J = -D \frac{\partial C}{\partial x} \approx \frac{D (C_g - C_s)}{\delta}$$

Where:
- $J$ = molar flux (mol·cm⁻²·s⁻¹)
- $D$ = gas-phase diffusion coefficient (cm²/s)
- $C_g$ = precursor concentration in bulk gas
- $C_s$ = precursor concentration at the surface
- $\delta$ = boundary layer thickness (cm)

## 2.4 The Arrhenius Growth-Rate Equation

When the process is **kinetically limited**, the deposition rate $R$ follows Arrhenius kinetics:

$$R = R_0 \exp\!\left(-\frac{E_a}{k_B T}\right)$$

Where:
- $R_0$ = pre-exponential factor
- $E_a$ = activation energy of the surface reaction (typically **1.0 – 2.0 eV** for MOCVD of III–V)
- $k_B$ = Boltzmann constant = **1.38 × 10⁻²³ J/K**
- $T$ = substrate temperature (K)

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Utility:** The Arrhenius plot (ln R vs. 1/T) is **the** diagnostic graph used by KTU examiners and process engineers to identify the operating regime. A steep negative slope → kinetic regime; a flat line → mass-transport regime.

## 2.5 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet)

| # | Formula / Law | Meaning | Typical KTU Use |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $J = \dfrac{D(C_g - C_s)}{\delta}$ | Fickian mass transport through BL | Boundary-layer analysis |
| 2 | $R = R_0 \exp(-E_a / k_B T)$ | Arrhenius growth rate | Regime identification |
| 3 | $\Delta G = \Delta H - T\Delta S < 0$ | Spontaneity criterion | Predicting feasibility of reaction |
| 4 | $\text{V/III ratio} = \dfrac{P_{AsH_3}}{P_{TMGa}}$ | MOCVD molar flow ratio | Stoichiometry control |
| 5 | $v_{gas} = \dfrac{Q}{A}$ | Mean gas velocity in reactor | Residence time calculation |
| 6 | $\tau_{res} = \dfrac{L}{v_{gas}}$ | Residence time | Reactor design |
| 7 | $\eta_{incorp} = \dfrac{N_{film}}{N_{inlet}}$ | Precursor incorporation efficiency | Cost & safety analysis |
| 8 | $d_{film} = \int_0^t R(t)\, dt$ | Layer thickness by rate integration | Thickness control |

> [!WARNING]
> **Pipe Symbol Alert:** In the KTU exam answer sheet, **never** write absolute values using `|` inside markdown tables (e.g., in the formula $\vert C_g - C_s \vert$). Use the symbols `\vert` or `\mid` so the LaTeX renders correctly and the examiner's auto-grader is not confused.

## 2.6 The V/III Ratio — The Most-Cited MOCVD Parameter

For a III–V compound such as GaAs:

$$\text{V/III ratio} = \frac{\text{Molar flow of Group V precursor (AsH}_3\text{)}}{\text{Molar flow of Group III precursor (TMGa)}}$$

- **GaAs MOCVD typical V/III:** **40 – 200**
- **GaN MOCVD typical V/III:** **1000 – 10000** (very high, because NH₃ has low cracking efficiency)
- **InP MOCVD typical V/III:** **50 – 300**

A high V/III ratio prevents the formation of Group-III-rich droplets (e.g., Ga droplets on GaAs) and ensures stoichiometric epitaxy.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation I: Boundary-Layer Thickness for a Horizontal CVD Reactor

Consider a horizontal, cold-wall CVD reactor with **incompressible laminar flow** of carrier gas (H₂) over a heated susceptor. We derive the **boundary-layer thickness** $\delta(x)$ at a longitudinal position $x$ from the leading edge of the susceptor.

**Step 1 — Starting Equation: Continuity for 2-D Flow**
The continuity equation in 2-D Cartesian form is:

$$\frac{\partial u}{\partial x} + \frac{\partial v}{\partial y} = 0$$

**Step 2 — Blasius Boundary-Layer Momentum Equation**
For a flat plate, the steady-state momentum equation in the boundary layer reduces to:

$$u \frac{\partial u}{\partial x} + v \frac{\partial u}{\partial y} = \nu \frac{\partial^2 u}{\partial y^2}$$

**Step 3 — Apply Similarity Transformation**
Introduce the similarity variable $\eta = y \sqrt{\dfrac{u_\infty}{2 \nu x}}$ and the stream function $\psi = \sqrt{2 \nu x u_\infty}\, f(\eta)$.

**Step 4 — Obtain the Blasius ODE**
The transformation converts the PDE to the famous Blasius ODE:

$$f''' + f\, f'' = 0$$

with boundary conditions $f(0)=0$, $f'(0)=0$, $f'(\infty)=1$.

**Step 5 — Numerical Solution & Definition of $\delta$**
Numerically, $f'(\eta)=0.99$ at $\eta \approx 5.0$. By definition, $\eta = 5$ corresponds to:

$$y = \delta(x) = 5.0 \sqrt{\frac{2 \nu x}{u_\infty}}$$

**Step 6 — Final Closed-Form Expression**
Therefore the boundary-layer thickness grows as $\sqrt{x}$:

$$\delta(x) \approx 5.0 \sqrt{\frac{2 \nu x}{u_\infty}}$$

**Conversion logic:** The derivation shows that $\delta$ is *larger* at higher $x$ (downstream), *larger* for *higher* kinematic viscosity $\nu$ (which is strongly T-dependent), and *smaller* for *higher* mainstream velocity $u_\infty$. This directly impacts the growth-rate uniformity across the wafer — a critical KTU viva question.

## 3.2 Derivation II: From Fick's Law to the CVD Growth Rate

Starting with Fick's first law at the substrate surface:

$$J = -D \left.\frac{\partial C}{\partial y}\right\vert_{y=0}$$

**Step 1 — Assume Linear Concentration Profile Across BL**
If the bulk gas concentration is $C_g$ and the surface concentration is $C_s$, and the profile across $\delta$ is linear:

$$\frac{\partial C}{\partial y} \approx -\frac{C_g - C_s}{\delta}$$

**Step 2 — Substitute into Fick's Law**

$$J = \frac{D (C_g - C_s)}{\delta}$$

**Step 3 — Relate Flux to Growth Rate**
The number of moles of GaAs deposited per unit area per unit time is the flux $J$. Multiplying by the molar volume $V_m$ of the solid film gives the linear growth rate:

$$R = J \cdot V_m = \frac{D\, V_m\, (C_g - C_s)}{\delta}$$

**Step 4 — Introduce Mass-Transfer Coefficient $h_g$**
Define the gas-phase mass-transfer coefficient:

$$h_g = \frac{D}{\delta}$$

**Step 5 — Final Compact Form**

$$\boxed{\,R = h_g\, V_m\, (C_g - C_s)\,}$$

**Conversion logic:** This equation is the **design equation** for industrial MOCVD reactors. The engineer controls $C_g$ (via bubbler temperature and carrier flow), $h_g$ (via total flow and pressure), and $T_{sub}$ (which sets $V_m$ and $C_s$).

## 3.3 Derivation III: Temperature Dependence of Growth Rate

Starting with the Arrhenius equation and substituting the gas-kinetic expression for $D$:

$$D = D_0 \left(\frac{T}{T_0}\right)^{1.75} \frac{P_0}{P}$$

**Step 1 — Surface-Reaction Limited Regime**
Here $C_s \to 0$ and the rate is set by surface kinetics:

$$R_{kinetic} = k_s C_g = k_0 C_g \exp(-E_a / k_B T)$$

**Step 2 — Mass-Transport Limited Regime**
Here $C_s \to 0$ is replaced by the gas-phase flux, and $D \propto T^{1.75}$:

$$R_{transport} = \frac{D_0 (T/T_0)^{1.75} (P_0/P)\, V_m\, C_g}{\delta}$$

**Step 3 — Combined Picture**
On a log-linear plot of $\ln R$ vs. $1/T$:
- **Low T** → exponential rise (kinetic, slope = $-E_a/k_B$)
- **High T** → mild $T^{1.75}$ increase (transport, slope is gentle)
- **Transition** at $T_{trans}$ where both rates are equal

$$\boxed{\,T_{trans} \approx \frac{E_a}{k_B \ln(R_0 / h_g V_m C_g)}\,}$$

**Conversion logic:** This is exactly the "S-shaped" Arrhenius curve drawn in every MOCVD textbook. The KTU examiner expects the student to **label the three regions** (kinetic, transition, mass-transport) and identify the operating point.

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Comparison of Deposition Techniques

| Parameter | MOCVD | MBE | LPE | Sputtering |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Growth Pressure | 50 – 760 Torr | < 10⁻¹⁰ Torr | 760 Torr | 1 – 10 mTorr |
| Growth Temperature | 500 – 1100 °C | 400 – 700 °C | 600 – 900 °C | RT – 500 °C |
| Growth Rate | 1 – 10 µm/hr | 0.1 – 1 µm/hr | 10 – 100 µm/hr | 0.01 – 1 µm/hr |
| Interface Abruptness | ~ 1 nm | ~ 0.5 nm | ~ 5 nm | ~ 2 nm |
| Production Scale | **High (industry standard)** | Medium (R&D) | Low (bulk only) | Medium |
| Materials | III–V, II–VI, oxides | III–V, oxides | III–V phosphates, GaAs | Metals, oxides |
| In-situ Monitoring | Limited | **RHEED** | None | Plasma diagnostics |

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Conclusion:** MOCVD is the **only** technique offering both **high production throughput** *and* **atomic-scale control of heterostructures**, making it the dominant industrial technique for blue/green LEDs, HEMTs, and multi-junction solar cells.

---

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Block Diagram: Overall CVD Process Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    nodeA[Carrier Gas H2 Cylinder] --> nodeB[Mass Flow Controller MFC]
    nodeC[MO Precursor Bubbler TMGa] --> nodeB
    nodeD[Hydride Gas AsH3 Cylinder] --> nodeB
    nodeE[Dopant Source SiH4 or Cp2Mg] --> nodeB
    nodeB --> nodeF[Gas Mixing Manifold]
    nodeF --> nodeG[CVD Reactor Chamber]
    nodeG --> nodeH[Heated Susceptor and Substrate]
    nodeH --> nodeI[Epitaxial Film Growth]
    nodeI --> nodeJ[Exhaust and Scrubber System]
    nodeK[RF or Resistive Heater] --> nodeH
    nodeL[Pyrometer Temperature Sensor] --> nodeK
    nodeM[Pressure Controller] --> nodeG
```

## 4.2 Mermaid Flowchart: Five Sequential CVD Steps

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    stepA[Step 1 Bulk Transport via Carrier Gas] --> stepB[Step 2 Gas Phase Diffusion through Boundary Layer]
    stepB --> stepC[Step 3 Surface Adsorption on Hot Substrate]
    stepC --> stepD[Step 4 Surface Reaction and Film Growth]
    stepD --> stepE[Step 5 Desorption of By products and Exhaust]
    stepE --> stepF[Continuous Epitaxial Layer]
```

