# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Set B]

**Q: Define Chemical Vapour Deposition. List any four differences between CVD and PVD.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- **Definition (1 mark):** CVD is a thin-film deposition process in which volatile precursors react chemically on or near a heated substrate surface to form a non-volatile solid film, with by-products removed by the gas flow.
- **Differences (½ mark each, any four):**

| # | CVD | PVD |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | Chemical reaction involved | Purely physical (evaporation/sputtering) |
| 2 | Conformal step coverage | Poor step coverage (line-of-sight) |
| 3 | Lower vacuum or atmospheric | High vacuum (< 10⁻⁶ Torr) |
| 4 | High temperature (500 – 1100 °C) | Can be room temperature |
| 5 | Uses toxic gases (AsH₃, PH₃) | Generally safer materials |
| 6 | Example: MOCVD for GaAs | Example: Thermal evaporation of Al |

### Question 2 [KTU University Exam – June 2024, Model Paper]

**Q: What is V/III ratio in MOCVD? Why is a high V/III ratio essential for GaN growth?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- **V/III ratio definition (1 mark):** $\text{V/III} = P_{V-precursor} / P_{III-precursor}$, the molar flow ratio of Group-V to Group-III precursors in the gas phase.
- **Importance for GaN (2 marks):**
  - NH₃ has a very low pyrolysis efficiency below 1000 °C, so a high V/III ratio (often > 2000) compensates for the low effective nitrogen supply.
  - It suppresses formation of Ga droplets on the surface, ensuring stoichiometric GaN films.
  - It maintains smooth surface morphology and reduces nitrogen vacancies (which act as shallow donors, degrading optical quality).

---

## 5.2 Part B — Full 14-Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice)

### Question A — 14 Marks [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Set A]

**Q: (a) With a neat schematic, explain the working of a Metal-Organic Chemical Vapour Deposition (MOCVD) system for the growth of GaAs epitaxial layers. (7 marks)**
**(b) Derive the expression for the growth rate of an epitaxial layer in the mass-transport limited regime, starting from Fick's First Law. (7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) MOCVD System for GaAs (7 marks):**
1. **Gas Source Section (1 mark):** Purified H₂ carrier gas is metered through MFCs. TMGa (or TEGa) is held in a stainless-steel bubbler at sub-zero temperature, and H₂ is bubbled through to pick up MO vapour. AsH₃ is supplied as a high-purity gas from a cylinder, also metered by MFC.
2. **Gas Mixing Manifold (1 mark):** The MO vapour and hydride are mixed with the main H₂ line. Switching between vent and run lines enables **abrupt interface** formation (essential for quantum wells and HEMT channels).
3. **Reactor Chamber (2 marks):** A horizontal quartz chamber with a rotating graphite susceptor (rotation gives uniform thickness on large wafers). RF coils (or resistive heaters) heat the susceptor to 600 – 700 °C for GaAs.
4. **Chemical Reaction at Substrate (2 marks):** $\text{Ga(CH}_3\text{)}_3 + \text{AsH}_3 \xrightarrow{650\,^\circ\text{C},\,H_2} \text{GaAs} + 3\,\text{CH}_4$. Epitaxial GaAs grows on the GaAs substrate; lattice-matched film is achieved.
5. **Exhaust & Safety (1 mark):** Unreacted precursors and by-products (CH₄, HCl traces) pass through a **scrubber** that neutralises toxic AsH₃ and PH₃ residues using NaOH or activated carbon.

**(b) Derivation of Mass-Transport Limited Growth Rate (7 marks):**
1. **Fick's First Law (1 mark):** $J = -D \dfrac{dC}{dy}$ at the substrate.
2. **Linear Approximation Across Boundary Layer (1 mark):** $J \approx \dfrac{D (C_g - C_s)}{\delta}$.
3. **Mass-Transport Limit (1 mark):** When surface reaction is fast, $C_s \to 0$ and $J_{max} = \dfrac{D\, C_g}{\delta}$.
4. **Diffusion Coefficient (1 mark):** $D \propto T^{1.75} / P$ (gas kinetic theory).
5. **Growth Rate in Linear Units (1 mark):** $R = J \cdot V_m = \dfrac{D\, V_m\, C_g}{\delta}$.
6. **Effect of Pressure (1 mark):** $R \propto 1/P$ (lowering pressure reduces $\delta$ and increases $D/P$, but throughput also drops).
7. **Final Expression (1 mark):** $\boxed{R = \dfrac{D_0 V_m C_g}{\delta (T/P)^{1.75}}}$.

---

### Question B — 14 Marks [KTU University Exam – June 2024, Model Paper]

**Q: (a) Discuss the various thermodynamic and kinetic regimes of CVD with the help of an Arrhenius plot. (7 marks)**
**(b) Compare MOCVD with MBE for the growth of III–V compound semiconductors. State two applications of MOCVD-grown layers. (7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Thermodynamic vs. Kinetic Regimes in CVD (7 marks):**
1. **Thermodynamic Feasibility (1 mark):** A reaction is possible only if $\Delta G < 0$. For GaAs from TMGa + AsH₃, $\Delta G < 0$ above ~ 400 °C.
2. **Three Regimes from Arrhenius Plot (3 marks):**
   - **Low T, Kinetic regime:** $R = R_0 \exp(-E_a / k_B T)$ — strong T-dependence, slope gives $E_a$.
   - **Intermediate T, Transition:** Curved region; rate is sensitive to both T and gas flow.
   - **High T, Mass-transport regime:** $R \propto T^{1.75}$ — weak T-dependence; rate controlled by gas flow rate.
3. **Operating Point Selection (1 mark):** Industrial MOCVD operates just **below** the mass-transport plateau to balance uniformity and abrupt interface control.
4. **Effect of V/III Ratio (1 mark):** Higher V/III ratio shifts the transition temperature; for GaN, very high V/III is needed to maintain stoichiometry.
5. **Practical Importance (1 mark):** Selecting the right regime ensures reproducible film thickness across the wafer — a key KTU industrial-process question.

**(b) MOCVD vs. MBE & Applications (7 marks):**
1. **Comparison Table (4 marks):** Refer to Section 3.4 Analytical Matrix.
2. **Two Applications of MOCVD (2 marks each, total 4 marks — but capped at 2 marks total for this sub-part to sum to 7 with the table):**
   - **Blue / Green / White LEDs:** GaN/InGaN multi-quantum-well active regions grown by MOCVD on sapphire or SiC substrates.
   - **HEMTs for 5G / Radar:** AlGaN/GaN high-electron-mobility transistors grown on SiC or Si substrates.
   - **Multi-junction Solar Cells:** GaInP/GaAs/Ge tandem cells for space photovoltaics.
   - **Laser Diodes:** GaAs/AlGaAs quantum-well lasers for CD/DVD/Blu-ray and datacoms.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Where students commonly lose marks in CVD questions:**
> 1. **Forgetting units in Fick's Law derivation** — write $D$ in cm²/s, $C$ in mol/cm³, $J$ in mol/(cm²·s).
> 2. **Confusing MOCVD with MOVPE** — they are the same process; "MOCVD" is more common in the US, "MOVPE" in Europe. Don't write them as two different things.
> 3. **Saying "CVD is done at room temperature"** — this is the MOCVD/MOVPE misconception; standard CVD needs 500–1100 °C. Only PECVD operates at low T.
> 4. **Skipping the safety discussion** — AsH₃ and PH₃ are lethal; examiners specifically award 1 mark for mentioning scrubbers and gas handling.
> 5. **Drawing the Arrhenius plot without labelling axes** — the slope is $-E_a/k_B$; examiners will not award the full 2 marks for the curve if axes and slope are missing.
> 6. **Mixing up "boundary layer" with "depletion layer"** — boundary layer is a gas-phase concept (mm thick); depletion layer is a semiconductor junction concept (nm thick).

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

- **CVD = Chemical reaction on a heated substrate from gas-phase precursors** (NOT a physical process).
- **Five universal steps:** Bulk transport → Gas-phase diffusion → Adsorption → Surface reaction → Desorption.
- **MOCVD / MOVPE = the industrial workhorse for III–V (GaAs, GaN, InP).**
- **V/III ratio** is the most-cited MOCVD parameter; for GaN it must be > 1000 due to NH₃'s poor cracking.
- **Arrhenius plot** has three regions: kinetic (steep), transition (curved), mass-transport (flat). The operating point should be in the **transition / just-below-plateau region**.
- **Fick's Law** drives mass transport: $J = D(C_g - C_s)/\delta$; growth rate $R = h_g V_m (C_g - C_s)$.
- **Boundary layer thickness** $\delta \propto \sqrt{\nu x / u_\infty}$ — thicker downstream and at higher viscosity.
- **Mass-transport regime:** $R \propto 1/P$, $R \propto T^{1.75}$.
- **Kinetic regime:** $R \propto \exp(-E_a / k_B T)$.
- **MOCVD > MBE** for production scale; **MBE > MOCVD** for ultra-sharp interfaces (RHEED monitored).
- **Safety:** AsH₃, PH₃, TMIn are toxic; reactor must have **emergency scrubbers** and leak detectors.
- **LPCVD** = better uniformity; **APCVD** = simpler; **PECVD** = lower T (for dielectrics); **MOCVD** = best for compound semiconductors.
- **Standard precursor for Ga:** TMGa (liquid, $\sim -10\,^\circ\text{C}$ bubbler) or TEGa.
- **Standard precursor for As:** AsH₃ (gas, 100 % dilution in H₂ for safety).
- **Standard precursor for N:** NH₃ (gas; very high flow needed because of low cracking efficiency).
- **Standard dopants:** SiH₄ (n-type), Cp₂Mg (p-type), CCl₄ (semi-insulating GaAs).
- **Growth temperature order (descending):** GaN (~1050 °C) > GaAs (~650 °C) > InP (~600 °C) > InGaAs (~550 °C).
- **KTU-favourite trick question:** "Why is MOCVD preferred for GaN over MBE?" — Answer: Higher growth rate and lower cost for thick layers; better suits high V/III requirement of NH₃.
- **Key equations to memorize:** Fick's Law, Arrhenius growth rate, Blasius boundary-layer thickness, V/III ratio.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Metallization Techniques: Evaporation and Sputtering
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEEVT413 - COMPOUND SEMICONDUCTORS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Metallization Techniques: Evaporation and Sputtering

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 What is Metallization in Compound Semiconductor Technology?

**Metallization** is the controlled deposition of a thin metallic film onto a compound semiconductor wafer (e.g., GaAs, InP, SiC, GaN, ZnSe) to form low-resistance **ohmic contacts**, rectifying **Schottky barrier contacts**, gate electrodes, and on-chip **interconnects**. In the context of compound semiconductors, metallization is particularly challenging because the surface chemistry is non-trivial — native oxides, Fermi-level pinning, and out-diffusion of volatile group-V elements (As, P) make contact formation fundamentally different from silicon.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** In compound semiconductors, metallization is the final physical step that "activates" the device. Without proper metal–semiconductor interface engineering, even a perfect epitaxial layer produces a poor, high-resistance, or unstable contact.

The two **Physical Vapour Deposition (PVD)** techniques that dominate metallization in compound semiconductor fabs are:
1. **Evaporation** (thermal, electron-beam)
2. **Sputtering** (DC, RF, magnetron)

---

## 1.2 Intuitive Analogies

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy 1 — Evaporation: The Boiling Pot**
> Imagine heating a kettle of water on a stove. Molecules at the surface gain enough kinetic energy to escape into the air as steam, then condense on a cold lid held above. In **thermal evaporation**, a metal is the "water," a tungsten filament or e-beam is the "stove," and the cold wafer is the "lid." The metal atoms travel ballistically (in straight lines, essentially in vacuum) and stick where they land. This is why evaporation gives excellent **directional deposition** but poor **step coverage** on non-planar surfaces.

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy 2 — Sputtering: The Sandblaster**
> Imagine firing a high-pressure stream of Argon ions (Ar⁺) — like a sandblaster of atomic particles — at a metal target. Atoms knocked off the target fly in *all* directions and land on the wafer. Because atoms are scattered in many directions, they can wrap around corners and coat the sidewalls of deep trenches or vias. This is why sputtering gives far superior **step coverage** than evaporation.

---

## 1.3 Key Physical Constants and Standard Metrics

| Quantity | Symbol | Typical Value | Unit |
|----------|--------|---------------|------|
| Boltzmann's constant | $k$ | **$1.38 \times 10^{-23}$** | J/K |
| Avogadro's number | $N_A$ | **$6.022 \times 10^{23}$** | mol⁻¹ |
| Atomic mass unit | $u$ | **$1.66 \times 10^{-27}$** | kg |
| Mean free path of Al atom in 10⁻⁶ Torr | $\lambda$ | **~ 5000** | cm |
| Typical working pressure (evap.) | $P$ | $10^{-6} - 10^{-7}$ | Torr |
| Typical working pressure (sputter) | $P$ | $1 - 100$ | mTorr |
| Self-bias in RF sputtering | $V_{dc}$ | **-300 to -1000** | V |
| Sputter yield of Ar⁺ on Al (500 eV) | $S$ | **~ 1.0 – 1.5** | atoms/ion |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Thermal Evaporation — Step-by-Step Logic

Thermal evaporation proceeds in **four sequential physical steps**:

1. **Phase transition (Solid → Vapor):** The source material is heated above its *vapor pressure threshold*. For Aluminum, this is reached around **~ 1100 °C** at a vapor pressure of $\sim 10^{-2}$ Torr.
2. **Vapor transport (Ballistic flight):** Metal atoms travel in straight lines from source to substrate because the mean free path $\lambda \gg$ source-to-substrate distance ($d$). This is ensured by **high vacuum** ($< 10^{-6}$ Torr).
3. **Condensation (Vapor → Solid):** Atoms arriving at the cooler wafer surface lose kinetic energy and form a thin film via nucleation and growth (Volmer–Weber, Frank–van der Merwe, or Stranski–Krastanov modes).
4. **Film thickening:** Continuous arrival produces a uniform layer whose thickness is monitored using a **quartz crystal microbalance (QCM)**.

### 2.1.1 Why "Why and How" — Engineering Utility
- The "why" of needing a *vacuum* is to avoid gas-phase collisions that would scatter the metal flux and contaminate the film.
- The "how" of directional deposition enables **lift-off lithography** (a key compound semiconductor patterning technique), but limits conformal coverage.

### 2.1.2 Variants
- **Resistive Evaporation:** A refractory metal boat (W, Mo, Ta) is Joule-heated. Limited to low-melting metals (Al, Au, Ag).
- **Electron-Beam (e-beam) Evaporation:** A focused electron beam (5–10 kV) melts the source from a water-cooled copper crucible. Allows very high temperatures, used for **Ti, Pt, W, Mo, refractory silicides**.

---

## 2.2 Sputtering — Step-by-Step Logic

Sputtering is a **momentum-transfer** process, not a thermal one. It has **five steps**:

1. **Plasma generation:** A低压 (1–100 mTorr) Ar atmosphere is ionized by a high DC or RF voltage applied between two electrodes. Free electrons are accelerated and collide with Ar atoms:
$$Ar + e^{-} \rightarrow Ar^{+} + 2e^{-}$$
2. **Ion bombardment:** Positive Ar⁺ ions are accelerated toward the **cathode (target)** — the metal to be deposited.
3. **Momentum transfer (Knock-on):** The energetic Ar⁺ ion transfers momentum to a surface atom of the target. If the transferred energy exceeds the *surface binding energy* ($\sim$ 1–10 eV for metals), the target atom is ejected — this is the **sputtered atom**.
4. **Transport through plasma:** Sputtered atoms undergo many collisions with the Ar gas (working pressure is much higher than evaporation), so they arrive at the substrate from *all* directions.
5. **Deposition & film growth:** Atoms condense on the substrate. Some energetic Ar ions and secondary electrons also bombard the growing film, which can affect morphology.

### 2.2.1 The Critical "Why" — Why Sputtering Has Better Step Coverage
Because the working pressure is $\sim$ 100–1000× higher than evaporation, atoms undergo many gas-phase collisions and arrive at the substrate with a **nearly isotropic angular distribution**. This is precisely what allows the metal to coat the **sidewalls and bottoms of high-aspect-ratio trenches**.

### 2.2.2 Variants
- **DC Diode Sputtering:** Used for *conducting* targets. Target = cathode.
- **RF Sputtering:** Uses 13.56 MHz AC voltage. Capacitively couples through insulating targets (e.g., SiO₂, Al₂O₃). Develops a self-bias $V_{dc}$.
- **Magnetron Sputtering:** Magnets behind the target confine secondary electrons near the cathode, dramatically increasing plasma density and deposition rate.

---

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> The following table is the *complete* set of high-yield equations for this topic. Memorize the symbols, not just the formulas.

| # | Concept | Formula | Meaning of Symbols | Units |
|---|---------|---------|--------------------|-------|
| 1 | Mean Free Path (evaporation condition) | $\lambda = \dfrac{kT}{\sqrt{2}\,\pi d^{2} P}$ | $k$ = Boltzmann const, $T$ = absolute temp, $d$ = molecular diameter, $P$ = pressure | m |
| 2 | Hertz–Knudsen evaporation rate | $\Phi_{evap} = \dfrac{P_{v}}{\sqrt{2\pi m k T}}$ | $P_{v}$ = vapor pressure of metal, $m$ = atomic mass, $T$ = source temp | atoms/(m²·s) |
| 3 | Deposition rate (mass) | $R_{m} = \dfrac{\Phi_{evap} \cdot A_{src}}{\pi r^{2}} \cdot m$ | $A_{src}$ = source area, $r$ = source-to-substrate distance | kg/s |
| 4 | Film thickness per unit time | $\dot{t} = \dfrac{R_{m}}{\rho A_{sub}}$ | $\rho$ = metal density, $A_{sub}$ = substrate area | m/s |
| 5 | Sputter Yield | $S = \dfrac{\text{Number of ejected atoms}}{\text{Number of incident ions}}$ | Dimensionless ratio | atoms/ion |
| 6 | Momentum threshold (sputtering) | $E_{th} \approx \dfrac{4 M_s M_i}{(M_s + M_i)^{2}} \cdot U_{sb}$ | $M_s, M_i$ = masses of target atom & ion, $U_{sb}$ = surface binding energy | eV |
| 7 | Sputtered atom energy (Thompson distribution) | $f(E) \propto \dfrac{1 - \sqrt{E_{sb}/E}}{E^{2}(1 + E_{sb}/E)^{3}}$ | $E$ = ejection energy, $E_{sb}$ = surface binding energy | — |
| 8 | Self-bias voltage (RF) | $V_{dc} \approx \dfrac{V_{rf}}{2} \cdot \left( \dfrac{A_{anode}}{A_{cathode}} \right)$ | $V_{rf}$ = peak RF voltage | V |
| 9 | Step coverage | $S_{cov} = \dfrac{t_{min}}{t_{nominal}}$ | $t_{min}$ = thinnest sidewall, $t_{nominal}$ = planar thickness | unitless (×100 = %) |
| 10 | Clausius–Clapeyron (vapor pressure vs. T) | $\ln P_{v} = -\dfrac{\Delta H_{vap}}{R T} + C$ | $\Delta H_{vap}$ = enthalpy of vaporization | Pa, K |

---

## 2.4 Engineering Utility — Where These Techniques Are Used in Industry

- **GaAs MESFET / HEMT gate metallization:** Ti/Pt/Au or Ti/Al/Ni/Au **alloyed ohmic contacts** are deposited by e-beam evaporation because the step coverage requirement is minimal (planar surface).
- **GaN HEMT Schottky gate:** Ni/Au deposited by **e-beam evaporation** to avoid plasma-induced damage to the 2DEG (two-dimensional electron gas) channel.
- **InP laser ridge sidewall metallization:** **Sputtering** is mandatory because the ridge is a deep, narrow mesa — evaporation cannot coat the sidewalls.
- **Back-side metallization of SiC power devices:** **Sputtered Ti/Ni/Ag** provides uniform, low-stress coverage.
- **VCSEL Bragg mirror DBR:** **Electron-beam evaporation** of SiO₂/TiO₂ or sputter deposition of amorphous Si for distributed Bragg reflectors.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Mean Free Path of a Metal Atom in a Vacuum Chamber

### 3.1.1 Problem Setup
A student wants to evaporate Aluminum at a chamber pressure of $1 \times 10^{-6}$ Torr at room temperature $T = 300$ K. The effective molecular diameter of air (residual gas) is $d = 3.7 \times 10^{-10}$ m. Determine the mean free path $\lambda$ and discuss whether the assumption of *ballistic transport* holds for a typical source-to-substrate distance $r = 30$ cm.

### 3.1.2 Step-by-Step Derivation

**Step 1 — Convert pressure to SI units (Pascals).**

$$
\begin{aligned}
1 \text{ Torr} &= 133.322 \text{ Pa} \\
P &= 1 \times 10^{-6} \times 133.322 \\
P &= 1.333 \times 10^{-4} \text{ Pa}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Apply the kinetic theory mean-free-path formula.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\lambda &= \frac{kT}{\sqrt{2} \, \pi d^{2} P}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Substitute numerical values.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
k &= 1.38 \times 10^{-23} \text{ J/K} \\
T &= 300 \text{ K} \\
\sqrt{2} &= 1.414 \\
\pi &= 3.1416 \\
d^{2} &= (3.7 \times 10^{-10})^{2} = 1.369 \times 10^{-19} \text{ m}^{2} \\
\lambda &= \frac{(1.38 \times 10^{-23})(300)}{(1.414)(3.1416)(1.369 \times 10^{-19})(1.333 \times 10^{-4})}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Evaluate numerator and denominator.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Numerator} &= 1.38 \times 300 \times 10^{-23} = 4.14 \times 10^{-21} \text{ J} \\
\text{Denominator} &= 1.414 \times 3.1416 \times 1.369 \times 10^{-19} \times 1.333 \times 10^{-4} \\
&= 1.414 \times 3.1416 \times 1.369 \times 1.333 \times 10^{-23} \\
&= 8.105 \times 10^{-23}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5 — Final division.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\lambda &= \frac{4.14 \times 10^{-21}}{8.105 \times 10^{-23}} \\
\lambda &\approx 51.08 \text{ m} \\
\lambda &\approx 5108 \text{ cm}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 6 — Compare with source-to-substrate distance.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
r &= 30 \text{ cm} = 0.30 \text{ m} \\
\frac{\lambda}{r} &= \frac{51.08}{0.30} \approx 170
\end{aligned}
$$

**Conclusion (KTU-style interpretation):**
Since $\lambda \gg r$ (by a factor of ~170), the Al atoms will make, on average, essentially *zero* collisions on their way to the wafer. This justifies treating the evaporation as **ballistic**, which is the very property that makes evaporation highly *directional* and ideal for lift-off patterning in compound semiconductor processing.

> [!NOTE]
> **Cross-check (sputtering comparison):** If we were to do the same calculation at sputtering pressure $P = 10$ mTorr, the mean free path drops to $\lambda \approx 0.5$ cm — meaning atoms undergo *hundreds* of collisions before reaching the substrate. This directly explains the isotropic arrival and superior step coverage of sputtering.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Sputter Yield of Ar⁺ on Aluminum

### 3.2.1 Problem Setup
A 500 eV Ar⁺ ion bombards an Aluminum target. The surface binding energy of Al is $U_{sb} = 3.39$ eV, and the atomic masses are $M_i$ (Ar) = 39.95 u and $M_s$ (Al) = 26.98 u. Estimate the *threshold energy* below which no sputtering occurs.

### 3.2.2 Step-by-Step Derivation

**Step 1 — Apply the threshold energy formula (momentum-transfer condition).**

$$
\begin{aligned}
E_{th} &\approx \frac{4 M_s M_i}{(M_s + M_i)^{2}} \cdot U_{sb}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Compute the mass ratio term.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
M_s + M_i &= 26.98 + 39.95 = 66.93 \text{ u} \\
(M_s + M_i)^{2} &= (66.93)^{2} = 4479.5 \\
4 M_s M_i &= 4 \times 26.98 \times 39.95 \\
&= 4 \times 1077.85 \\
&= 4311.4
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Compute the coefficient.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\frac{4 M_s M_i}{(M_s + M_i)^{2}} &= \frac{4311.4}{4479.5} \\
&\approx 0.9624
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Multiply by surface binding energy.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
E_{th} &= 0.9624 \times 3.39 \\
E_{th} &\approx 3.26 \text{ eV}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5 — Interpret the result.**

Since the incident ion energy (500 eV) is more than 150× the threshold, sputtering will be vigorous. The sputter yield $S$ (atoms ejected per incident ion) at 500 eV for Ar⁺ on Al is experimentally $S \approx 1.0 - 1.5$, which agrees with empirical Sigmund curves.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering takeaway:** Choosing an ion energy only slightly above $E_{th}$ maximizes yield efficiency (less energy wasted in subsurface damage), while very high energies (> 5 keV) embed Ar ions in the target and degrade film purity.

---

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Comparison of the Two Techniques

| Property | Thermal Evaporation | Sputtering |
|----------|--------------------|-----------|
| Driving physics | Thermal (vapor pressure) | Momentum transfer |
| Working pressure | $10^{-6} - 10^{-7}$ Torr | $1 - 100$ mTorr |
| Mean free path $\lambda$ | >> chamber size | << chamber size |
| Atom arrival direction | Highly directional | Isotropic / scattered |
| Step coverage | Poor (0 – 30%) | Good (50 – 100%) |
| Deposition rate | High (10 nm/s – µm/s) | Lower (1 – 10 nm/s) |
| Plasma damage to substrate | None (no plasma) | Possible (Ar bombardment) |
| Compound semiconductor compatibility | Excellent (gentle) | Can damage InP, GaAs surfaces |
| Film purity | Very high | Lower (Ar incorporation) |
| Stoichiometry preservation | Good for e-beam | Risk of preferential sputtering |
| Lift-off compatibility | Excellent | Difficult (conformal) |
| Alloy/refractory metals | e-beam can do both | Easy for most metals |
| Cost & complexity | Lower | Higher (RF generators, magnetrons) |

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture of a Thermal Evaporation System

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    vac[High Vacuum Pump Stack - Rotary plus Diffusion - 10^-6 Torr]
    cru[Crucible - W boat or E-beam pocket]
    src[Source Metal - Al Au Ti Pt]
    fil[Filament Heater - 100 A at 5 V]
    eb[Electron Beam Gun - 8 kV at 200 mA]
    shu[Mechanical Shutter - Timing Control]
    mask[Shadow Mask or Photoresist Pattern]
    sub[Compound Semiconductor Wafer - GaAs InP GaN]
    qcm[Quartz Crystal Monitor - Rate and Thickness]
    ctrl[Thickness Controller - TTL feedback to shutter]
    vac --> cru
    cru --> src
    fil --> cru
    eb --> cru
    src -.evaporant vapor.-> shu
    shu --> mask
    mask --> sub
    sub --> qcm
    qcm --> ctrl
    ctrl --> shu
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Read the diagram top-to-bottom as the actual physical layout of the chamber:** the source is at the bottom, vapor travels upward through the shutter and mask, and condenses on the wafer above. The QCM sits beside the wafer and sends a feedback signal that auto-closes the shutter when the target thickness is reached.

## 4.2 Block-Level Functional Architecture of a Sputtering System

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    ar[Ar Gas Inlet - Mass Flow Controller - 10 to 100 sccm]
    pmp[Turbo plus Rough Pump - 1 to 100 mTorr]
    rf[RF Generator - 13.56 MHz - or DC Power Supply]
    match[Matching Network - L type - Minimizes reflected power]
    cath[Target Cathode - Ti Pt Au Ni]
    mag[Permanent Magnets - behind target - Magnetron only]
    plas[Argon Plasma - glow region - Ar plus e minus]
    sub2[Substrate Anode - heated or water cooled - 200 to 400 C]
    sub3[Compound Semiconductor Wafer - holds face down to target]
    coll[Collimator - Optional - for high aspect ratio trenches]
    ar --> plas
    pmp --> plas
    rf --> match
    match --> cath
    mag --> cath
    plas --> cath
    cath -.sputtered atoms.-> sub3
    sub3 --> sub2
    sub3 -.conformal coating.-> coll
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Notice the key inversion vs. evaporation:** the *target* is the cathode (high-energy surface being eroded), the *wafer* is the anode. In evaporation, the source is *passive* (just hot); in sputtering, the source is *actively bombarded* by Ar⁺ ions.

## 4.3 Side-by-Side Process Flow Comparison

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Substrate Preparation - Solvent Clean plus HF native oxide etch] --> B[Load into PVD Chamber]
    B --> C{Tech choice}
    C -->|Evaporation| D[Heat source to vapor pressure]
    D --> E[Ballistic travel to wafer]
    E --> F[Condensation and film growth]
    C -->|Sputtering| G[Ignite Ar plasma]
    G --> H[Ar plus bombards target]
    H --> I[Momentum transfer - target atoms ejected]
    I --> J[Diffuse through Ar - isotropic arrival]
    J --> F
    F --> K[In-situ or ex-situ anneal - alloy formation]
    K --> L[Final Ohmic or Schottky Contact]
```

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1
**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Set B]**
*Define metallization in compound semiconductor device fabrication. List any two contact schemes (ohmic and Schottky) where metallization is essential.*

**Model Answer:**

**Metallization** is the process of depositing a thin metallic film on a compound semiconductor surface to provide a low-resistance electrical pathway to the active device region. It is the final physical fabrication step that creates the **ohmic contacts** (non-rectifying, low-resistance current injection — e.g., source/drain contacts in MESFETs) and the **Schottky barrier contacts** (rectifying, gate-like behaviour — e.g., the gate electrode of a GaAs MESFET or AlGaN/GaN HEMT). Without metallization, the device terminals cannot be probed, wire-bonded, or connected to the external circuit. Two contact schemes requiring metallization: (i) **AuGe/Ni/Au alloyed ohmic contact** on n-GaAs, (ii) **Ti/Pt/Au Schottky gate** on AlGaAs/GaAs HEMT.

---

### Question 2
**[KTU University Exam – June 2024]**
*State the basic physical principle that distinguishes sputtering from thermal evaporation. What is the practical consequence of this difference for step coverage?*

**Model Answer:**

The basic physical principle is that **thermal evaporation** is a *thermal* process where atoms leave the source by gaining enough energy to overcome surface binding and enter the vapor phase, while **sputtering** is a *momentum-transfer* process where energetic Ar⁺ ions physically knock target atoms off the surface. The practical consequence is that evaporation gives **highly directional, line-of-sight** deposition and therefore **poor step coverage**, while sputtering, because of the many gas-phase collisions in the mTorr-pressure plasma, delivers atoms from **many angles** and gives **excellent conformal step coverage** on trenches, vias, and ridges.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Module Internal Choice)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Rule:** Part B questions carry internal choice. You will be given EITHER Question A OR Question B, not both. Both options below are at the same cognitive level and revised-Bloom mapping.

---

### Question A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Module 4]

**(a) [7 Marks]** With a neat block diagram, describe the working of an **electron-beam (e-beam) evaporation system** for metallization. Mention the role of the water-cooled copper crucible, electron gun, magnetic deflection coil, and quartz crystal monitor.

**(b) [7 Marks)** A student wants to deposit a 200 nm thick Al film on a GaAs wafer by e-beam evaporation. The source-to-substrate distance is 25 cm, the Al vapor pressure at the source is $1 \times 10^{-2}$ Torr at the operating temperature, and the source is a small area of effective size $0.5$ cm². Given the Al atomic mass $m = 4.48 \times 10^{-26}$ kg and density $\rho = 2700$ kg/m³, calculate the **deposition time** required.

**Model Solution to (a):**

**Working of an e-beam evaporation system (block diagram drawn — see SECTION 4.1):**

1. **Filament (Cathode):** A tungsten filament is heated to ~ 2800 K and emits thermionic electrons.
2. **Magnetic deflection coil (Wehnelt + bending magnet):** The emitted electrons are accelerated to 5–10 kV and bent by a magnetic field (typically 270° or 180°) so that they strike the metal source *indirectly*, keeping the filament away from the molten pool.
3. **Water-cooled copper crucible:** Holds the metal charge. Water cooling keeps the crucible cold enough to be in thermal contact with the molten metal only at the top surface, so 90% of the e-beam power goes into heating the metal and not the boat.
4. **Molten pool / vapor source:** The Al is locally melted; its vapor pressure rises to $10^{-2}$ Torr, and Al atoms leave the surface.
5. **Quartz Crystal Monitor (QCM):** A small piezo crystal oscillating at ~ 6 MHz is exposed to the vapor. The mass loading on the crystal lowers its resonant frequency; the controller converts this to a film thickness and closes a mechanical **shutter** when the target thickness is reached.
6. **Wafer holder / substrate heater:** Holds the GaAs wafer face-down, often with planetary rotation for uniformity.

**Model Solution to (b):**

**Step 1 — Write the Hertz–Knudsen flux equation.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\Phi_{evap} &= \frac{P_{v}}{\sqrt{2 \pi m k T}}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Convert vapor pressure to Pa.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
P_v &= 1 \times 10^{-2} \text{ Torr} \times 133.322 \\
P_v &= 1.333 \text{ Pa}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Assume a typical source temperature $T = 1500$ K (Al is molten and evaporating).**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\Phi_{evap} &= \frac{1.333}{\sqrt{2 \pi (4.48 \times 10^{-26})(1.38 \times 10^{-23})(1500)}}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Evaluate the denominator.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
2 \pi &= 6.283 \\
6.283 \times 4.48 \times 10^{-26} \times 1.38 \times 10^{-23} \times 1500 \\
&= 6.283 \times 4.48 \times 1.38 \times 1500 \times 10^{-49} \\
&= 5.832 \times 10^{-45}
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
\sqrt{5.832 \times 10^{-45}} &= 7.636 \times 10^{-23}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5 — Calculate flux.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\Phi_{evap} &= \frac{1.333}{7.636 \times 10^{-23}} \\
&\approx 1.745 \times 10^{22} \text{ atoms/(m}^{2}\cdot\text{s)}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 6 — Use a cosine-distribution correction to get the flux at the substrate, with a small-area source on a large hemispherical area.**

For a small-area source emitting isotropically, the rate at which mass arrives at a small planar substrate at distance $r$ is:

$$
\begin{aligned}
R_m &= \frac{\Phi_{evap} \cdot m \cdot A_{src}}{\pi r^{2}} \\
&= \frac{(1.745 \times 10^{22})(4.48 \times 10^{-26})(0.5 \times 10^{-4})}{\pi (0.25)^{2}}
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Numerator} &= 1.745 \times 10^{22} \times 4.48 \times 10^{-26} \times 5 \times 10^{-5} \\
&= 3.91 \times 10^{-8} \text{ kg/s} \\
\text{Denominator} &= \pi \times 0.0625 = 0.1963 \\
R_m &= \frac{3.91 \times 10^{-8}}{0.1963} \\
&\approx 1.99 \times 10^{-7} \text{ kg/s}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 7 — Convert to deposition rate (thickness per unit time) for a 4-inch wafer ($A_{sub} = 8.11 \times 10^{-3}$ m²).**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\dot{t} &= \frac{R_m}{\rho \, A_{sub}} \\
&= \frac{1.99 \times 10^{-7}}{2700 \times 8.11 \times 10^{-3}} \\
&= \frac{1.99 \times 10^{-7}}{21.90} \\
&\approx 9.09 \times 10^{-9} \text{ m/s} \\
&\approx 9.09 \text{ nm/s}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 8 — Compute the deposition time for 200 nm.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
t &= \frac{200 \text{ nm}}{9.09 \text{ nm/s}} \\
&\approx 22 \text{ s}
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall — Question A(b):**
> The most common mistake students make is to *omit the cosine factor $\frac{1}{\pi r^{2}}$* that converts the source-area flux to the substrate-area flux. Many students also forget to **convert Torr to Pa** and end up with a flux that is off by a factor of 133.322. Always state the units *inside the calculation* to earn the method marks.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam – June 2024, Module 4]

**(a) [7 Marks]** With a neat block diagram, describe the working of a **DC magnetron sputtering system**. Explain the role of permanent magnets, the Ar plasma, target, and self-bias.

**(b) [7 Marks]** Calculate the **threshold energy** for sputtering of a Copper target by Ar⁺ ions, given $M_s$(Cu) = 63.55 u, $M_i$(Ar) = 39.95 u, and surface binding energy $U_{sb}$(Cu) = 3.52 eV. Comment on whether sputtering will occur if the incident ion energy is 100 eV. State **two methods to increase the sputter yield** in practical operation.

**Model Solution to (a):**

**Working of a DC magnetron sputtering system (see SECTION 4.2):**

1. **Chamber pump-down to base vacuum** (~ $10^{-7}$ Torr) to remove residual water vapour and oxygen.
2. **Ar gas inlet** through a mass flow controller. Working pressure stabilizes at 1–10 mTorr.
3. **DC power supply (–300 to –1000 V)** is applied between the cathode (target, e.g., Cu) and the anode (chamber walls / substrate stage).
4. **Plasma ignition:** A high-voltage spike ionizes Ar; subsequently, the self-sustaining discharge produces Ar⁺ ions and free electrons.
5. **Permanent magnets** are placed *behind* the target. Their field lines are parallel to the target surface. The Lorentz force traps secondary electrons in a **closed loop (race track)** close to the target.
6. **Effect of magnetic confinement:** The trapped electrons ionize more Ar atoms per unit length, *increasing plasma density* near the target, *increasing ion current* on the target, and *increasing deposition rate* (10–100× over a plain diode).
7. **Sputtering:** Ar⁺ ions accelerated across the cathode dark space hit the Cu target with energies of a few hundred eV. Cu atoms are ejected.
8. **Transport & deposition:** Cu atoms undergo collisions with Ar and deposit on the wafer. The wafer sits on the anode and can be heated, biased, or rotated.
9. **Self-bias:** In RF sputtering, the DC self-bias develops naturally because electrons (mobile) reach the target faster than ions, leaving the target with a net negative potential. In DC sputtering, this self-bias equals the applied DC voltage.

**Model Solution to (b):**

**Step 1 — Apply the threshold energy formula.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
E_{th} &\approx \frac{4 M_s M_i}{(M_s + M_i)^{2}} \cdot U_{sb}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Substitute masses.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
M_s + M_i &= 63.55 + 39.95 = 103.50 \\
(M_s + M_i)^2 &= 10712.25 \\
4 M_s M_i &= 4 \times 63.55 \times 39.95 = 10155.39
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Compute the mass coefficient.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\frac{4 M_s M_i}{(M_s + M_i)^2} &= \frac{10155.39}{10712.25} \approx 0.948
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Multiply by $U_{sb}$.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
E_{th} &= 0.948 \times 3.52 \text{ eV} \\
&\approx 3.34 \text{ eV}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5 — Comment on 100 eV ions.**

Since $100 \text{ eV} \gg 3.34 \text{ eV}$, the threshold is exceeded by a factor of ~30. Sputtering will definitely occur with a respectable yield ($S \approx 1 - 2$ atoms/ion for Cu at 100 eV Ar⁺).

**Step 6 — Two methods to increase the sputter yield:**

1. **Increase the incident ion energy** (e.g., raise the cathode voltage from 300 V to 800 V) — this raises $S$ until the embedding regime.
2. **Use a heavier noble gas** such as Kr⁺ or Xe⁺ instead of Ar⁺, which has a more favourable mass-ratio with Cu and transfers more momentum per collision.
3. (Bonus) **Use magnetron confinement** to raise the ion current density at the target, increasing atoms ejected per unit time.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall — Question B(b):**
> The most common mistake is forgetting that the **threshold energy is a function of the mass ratio**, not just $U_{sb}$. Students who simply write "$E_{th} \approx U_{sb}$" lose 2 marks. Also, students often confuse **sputter yield** with **deposition rate** — they are proportional but not the same quantity.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Metallization** is the *thin-film deposition of metals* on a compound semiconductor to form ohmic / Schottky / interconnect contacts.
- **Thermal Evaporation** is *thermal*: heat the source → atoms evaporate → travel ballistically (line-of-sight) → condense on the substrate. Requires **high vacuum** ($10^{-6}$ Torr) so that $\lambda \gg$ source–substrate distance.
- **Sputtering** is *momentum transfer*: Ar⁺ ions from a plasma physically knock atoms out of the target. Operates at **higher pressure** (1–100 mTorr) where atoms undergo many collisions, giving **isotropic, conformal** arrival.
- **Step coverage:** Sputtering >> Evaporation. Use sputtering for deep trenches, ridge sidewalls, and vias.
- **Plasma damage:** Sputtering can damage sensitive compound semiconductor surfaces (InP, GaN). Use e-beam evaporation for damage-free gate metallization.
- **e-beam evaporation** can deposit refractory metals (W, Mo, Ti, Pt) that resistive evaporation cannot.
- **Magnetron sputtering** uses permanent magnets to confine secondary electrons, increasing plasma density and deposition rate by 10–100× over plain diode sputtering.
- **Self-bias in RF sputtering** arises because electrons are more mobile than ions; the target acquires a DC offset of $\vert V_{dc} \vert \approx 0.5 V_{rf} \times (A_{anode}/A_{cathode})$.
- **Sputter yield formula:** $S$ = (atoms ejected) / (incident ions). $E_{th} \approx \dfrac{4 M_s M_i}{(M_s + M_i)^2} U_{sb}$.
- **Threshold energy** for Ar⁺ on most metals is in the range **10 – 50 eV**; practical sputtering uses 300 – 1000 eV.
- **Mean free path** at $10^{-6}$ Torr is ~ 50 m (ballistic); at 10 mTorr is ~ 0.5 cm (diffusive). This single fact explains *every* observable difference between the two techniques.
- **Lift-off patterning** works only with evaporation (directional flux) — not with sputtering (conformal flux that traps under the resist).
- **Quartz Crystal Monitor (QCM)** is the standard *in-situ* thickness sensor for both techniques.
- **Preferentially sputtered compounds:** For compound semiconductors, sputtering can change the surface stoichiometry (e.g., preferential loss of As from GaAs). Annealing after deposition often restores stoichiometry via solid-phase regrowth.
- **Final exam checklist:** Know (i) the four steps of evaporation, (ii) the five steps of sputtering, (iii) the mean-free-path formula and one worked numerical, (iv) the threshold-energy formula and one worked numerical, (v) the step-coverage comparison, (vi) at least one example of a compound-semiconductor metallization stack (AuGe/Ni/Au for GaAs ohmic, Ti/Pt/Au for GaAs Schottky, Ni/Au for GaN gate).
